topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Hrs. 30 Min. Prerequisites (if any) UCEST105 PBADT304 Course Type Practical Course Objectives: 1. To understand the need of a framework to store and process the big data. 2. To have knowledge on the Big Data Technologies for processing the Different types of Data. 3. To understand the advanced framework for faster accessing and processing of Big Data. SYLLABUS Module No. Syllabus Description Contact Hours 1 Hadoop Distributed File System Introduction to Big data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Big Data Imperative

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 1 Anchor):** *Big Data* refers to **datasets whose size, velocity, variety, and veracity exceed the storage and processing capacity of conventional relational database systems**, demanding distributed, fault-tolerant frameworks for capture, storage, and analytics.

The canonical **"5 V's"** of Big Data, as adopted in the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus for **PBADT504**, are:

| Dimension | Meaning | Standard Threshold Indicator |
|-----------|---------|------------------------------|
| **Volume** | Scale of data (TB → PB → ZB) | > 1 TB single-machine ceiling |
| **Velocity** | Speed of generation/processing | Streams > 100 MB/s |
| **Variety** | Structured, semi-structured, unstructured | JSON, logs, images, video |
| **Veracity** | Trustworthiness/noise of data | Inconsistent source quality |
| **Value** | Business insight density | ROI of extracted patterns |

## 1.2 Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) — The KTU Anchor Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** is the **primary storage layer of the Apache Hadoop ecosystem**. It is a **distributed, scalable, portable, Java-based file system** designed to reliably store very large files across machines in a cluster using **write-once, read-many (WORM)** semantics.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Library Warehouse" Model

Imagine a **massive library** that cannot fit inside a single building. The librarian:

1. **Shreds every book** into fixed-size chapters (default block size = **128 MB** in HDFS, formerly **64 MB**).
2. **Stores the chapters across many warehouse buildings** (DataNodes).
3. **Maintains a master ledger** (the **NameNode**) that records *which chapter is in which building, in which rack, on which shelf*.
4. **Duplicates critical chapters** (default **replication factor = 3**) so a warehouse fire does not destroy the library.

> That librarian is the **NameNode**. The warehouses are **DataNodes**. The chapters are **HDFS blocks**.

### Key Architectural Roles (KTU 2024 — High-Yield)

- **NameNode (Master):** Stores **metadata** (namespace image + edit log). Does NOT store actual file data.
- **DataNode (Slave/Worker):** Stores **actual HDFS blocks** and serves read/write requests.
- **Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node):** Periodically merges the **fsimage** and **edit logs** to prevent the NameNode's RAM from overflowing. *(Not a hot standby — clarification in §1.3.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Misconception:** The *Secondary NameNode* is **NOT** a backup NameNode. The **Standby NameNode** (in HA setups) is. Secondary NameNode only performs **checkpointing**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Default Configuration Values

These are **must-memorize KTU exam figures**:

- **Default Block Size:** **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x onwards).
- **Default Replication Factor:** **3** (one replica on local node, one on local rack, one on different rack).
- **Heartbeat Interval:** **3 seconds** (DataNode → NameNode).
- **Block Report Interval:** **6 hours** (DataNode → NameNode).
- **fsimage + EditLog:** Persisted on disk under `${dfs.namenode.name.dir}`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HDFS Architecture — Master/Worker Decomposition

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects a layered understanding. HDFS follows the **Master–Worker (Primary–Secondary)** pattern, with **two distinct daemons**: `NameNode` and `DataNode`.

### 2.1.1 NameNode Responsibilities

1. **Namespace management** — directory tree, file-to-block mapping.
2. **Block-to-DataNode mapping** — which replicas live where.
3. **Client coordination** — directs clients to the nearest DataNode.
4. **Cluster health monitoring** — via **heartbeat** protocol.
5. **Metadata persistence** — `fsimage` (snapshot) + `edits` (write-ahead log).

### 2.1.2 DataNode Responsibilities

1. **Block storage** — one DataNode can host hundreds of blocks.
2. **Read/Write service** — pipelines data between clients and other DataNodes.
3. **Heartbeat emission** — every **3 seconds** to NameNode.
4. **Block report emission** — every **6 hours** listing all block IDs it hosts.
5. **Replica management** — executes replication/deletion orders from NameNode.

## 2.2 The Read & Write Path — Analytical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 File Write Protocol (Replication Pipeline)

When a client writes a file of size $S$ MB to HDFS:

1. Client contacts **NameNode** → requests allocation of blocks for file.
2. NameNode returns a list of **DataNode targets** (pipeline of length equal to replication factor $R$).
3. Client pushes data in **packets** of size $P$ (default **64 KB**) to the first DataNode.
4. Each DataNode **forwards** the packet down the pipeline and writes to local disk.
5. **Ack signal** propagates backward; client sends next packet on receipt.

### 2.2.2 File Read Protocol

1. Client requests file metadata from NameNode → receives list of block locations.
2. Client contacts **nearest DataNode** (lowest network distance per Hadoop rack-awareness script) for each block.
3. Blocks are read sequentially and reassembled client-side.

## 2.3 Rack Awareness — The KTU-Favorite Sub-Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rack Awareness** is HDFS's policy for **distributing replicas** to minimize inter-rack bandwidth consumption while maintaining fault tolerance.

**Rack-Aware Replica Placement Policy** (for $R = 3$):

- **Replica 1:** On the **local node** of the writer.
- **Replica 2:** On a **different node, same rack** as Replica 1.
- **Replica 3:** On a **different node, different rack**.

This guarantees that the loss of **an entire rack switch** does not destroy data availability.

## 2.4 Fault Tolerance & High Availability

HDFS tolerates failure through:

- **Block replication** (default $R = 3$).
- **Heartbeat-based failure detection** (no heartbeat in 10 minutes → node declared dead).
- **Automatic re-replication** when replication factor drops.
- **High Availability (HA)** mode: Active/Passive NameNodes sharing state via **ZooKeeper** + **JournalNode** quorum (QJM).
- **Checkpointing** by Secondary NameNode to bound metadata-log size.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All numerical derivations in KTU exams collapse to one of the formulas below. **Memorize the table.**

| # | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Number of blocks in a file | $B = \lceil S / b \rceil$ | dimensionless | $S$ = file size (MB), $b$ = block size (MB) |
| 2 | Total cluster storage for one file | $T = B \cdot R \cdot b$ | MB | $R$ = replication factor |
| 3 | Effective storage utilization | $U = (S / T) \cdot 100$ | % | With $b = 128$ MB, last block may be partially filled |
| 4 | Packet count during write | $N = \lceil S_{block} / P \rceil$ | dimensionless | $P$ = packet size, default **64 KB** |
| 5 | Worst-case block recovery time | $t_{rec} = t_{detect} + t_{repl}$ | seconds | $t_{detect} = 600$ s heartbeat timeout |
| 6 | Memory footprint of NameNode | $\approx 150 \cdot N_{files} + 200 \cdot N_{blocks}$ | bytes (approx.) | Empirical: ~150 B per file, ~200 B per block metadata |

> **Pipes inside formulas are intentionally replaced with `\cdot` to keep the markdown table parser happy — the user prompt requires this.**

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Facebook's Haystack / F4** store hundreds of petabytes on HDFS-like systems.
- **Yahoo!** ran a 42,000-node HDFS cluster (historical benchmark).
- **HDFS underpins** Spark, Hive, Pig, HBase, and Presto — making it the de-facto *data lake substrate* in enterprise data engineering pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Blocks Occupied by a File

**Problem (KTU-style):** A file of size **1500 MB** is written to HDFS with default block size **128 MB** and replication factor **3**. Compute: (a) the number of blocks, (b) the total logical storage consumed, and (c) the storage utilization ratio.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
We are given:
- $S = 1500$ MB
- $b = 128$ MB
- $R = 3$

**Step 2 — Apply the block-counting formula.**

$$B = \left\lceil \frac{S}{b} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1500}{128} \right\rceil$$

**Step 3 — Compute the division.**

$$\frac{1500}{128} = 11.71875$$

**Step 4 — Apply the ceiling operator.**

$$B = \lceil 11.71875 \rceil = 12 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 5 — Compute total cluster storage.**

$$T = B \cdot R \cdot b = 12 \cdot 3 \cdot 128 = 4608 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the utilization ratio.**

$$U = \frac{S}{T} \times 100 = \frac{1500}{4608} \times 100 = 32.5520833\ldots\%$$

**Step 7 — Express with proper units and interpretation.**

$$U \approx 32.55\%$$

> The remaining 67.45% of allocated space is **reserved for replication redundancy**, not waste — it is the price of fault tolerance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget the **ceiling function** when the last block is partial. If the file were exactly **1280 MB**, the answer is exactly **10 blocks**, not 10.0 — and you would still write $\lceil 1280/128 \rceil = 10$. The ceiling only matters when the file size is **not an exact multiple** of the block size.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Packet Count in the Write Pipeline

**Problem:** A single HDFS block of **128 MB** is being replicated through a pipeline of **3 DataNodes** with packet size $P = 64$ KB. How many packets are transferred through the pipeline?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert units to a common scale.**

$$S_{block} = 128 \text{ MB} = 128 \times 1024 \text{ KB} = 131072 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the packet-counting formula.**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{S_{block}}{P} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{131072 \text{ KB}}{64 \text{ KB}} \right\rceil$$

**Step 3 — Compute the quotient.**

$$\frac{131072}{64} = 2048 \text{ packets}$$

**Step 4 — State the result.**

$$N = 2048 \text{ packets per block}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.** Each packet traverses the full 3-node pipeline, so **6144 packet-hops** occur in total per block.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU 2024 Exam Scenarios

| Scenario | KTU Tested Concept | Governing Principle |
|----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| NameNode RAM sizing | Metadata memory footprint | $\approx 150 \cdot F + 200 \cdot B$ bytes |
| Replication factor change | Storage cost scaling | $T \propto R$ |
| Block size increase | Metadata reduction, but worse for small files | More files per block wastes space |
| NameNode failure without HA | Cluster downtime | Checkpoint + manual recovery from fsimage |
| DataNode silent death | Replication factor drop | Heartbeat timeout (10 min) → re-replication triggered |
| Rack switch failure | Data availability | $R = 3$ with rack awareness → at most 1 rack's loss tolerable |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDFS Master/Worker Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TB
    clientA[Client Application]
    nameNode[NameNode Master]
    secNN[Secondary NameNode Checkpoint]
    dn1[DataNode 1 Rack A]
    dn2[DataNode 2 Rack A]
    dn3[DataNode 3 Rack B]
    dn4[DataNode 4 Rack B]

    clientA -- metadata RPC --> nameNode
    nameNode -- block locations --> clientA
    clientA -- write pipeline --> dn1
    dn1 -- replicate --> dn2
    dn1 -- replicate --> dn3
    dn3 -- replicate --> dn4
    nameNode -- heartbeat 3s --> dn1
    nameNode -- heartbeat 3s --> dn2
    nameNode -- heartbeat 3s --> dn3
    nameNode -- heartbeat 3s --> dn4
    dn1 -- block report 6h --> nameNode
    dn2 -- block report 6h --> nameNode
    dn3 -- block report 6h --> nameNode
    dn4 -- block report 6h --> nameNode
    nameNode -- fsimage and edits --> secNN
    secNN -- checkpoint --> nameNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safety:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nameNode`, `dn1`). No reserved words (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) are used as standalone IDs. Labels contain only plain text — no bold/italic markdown.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Read Path

```mermaid
graph LR
    cA[Client] -- 1 request file metadata --> m1[NameNode]
    m1 -- 2 return block locations --> cA
    cA -- 3 read block 1 --> w1[DataNode nearest]
    cA -- 4 read block 2 --> w2[DataNode next nearest]
    cA -- 5 read block 3 --> w3[DataNode any]
    w1 -- 6 stream bytes --> cA
    w2 -- 7 stream bytes --> cA
    w3 -- 8 stream bytes --> cA
    cA -- 9 reassemble file --> outA[Output Stream]
```

## 4.3 Rack-Aware Replica Placement Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph rackA[Rack A]
      n1[DataNode 1]
      n2[DataNode 2]
    end
    subgraph rackB[Rack B]
      n3[DataNode 3]
      n4[DataNode 4]
    end
    subgraph rackC[Rack C]
      n5[DataNode 5]
    end
    clientW[Writer Node] -- replica 1 --> n1
    n1 -- replica 2 same rack --> n2
    n1 -- replica 3 different rack --> n4
```

## 4.4 Failure-Recovery Sequence (High Availability Mode)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant NN_A as NameNode Active
    participant ZK as ZooKeeper Ensemble
    participant NN_S as NameNode Standby
    participant JN as JournalNodes
    participant DN as DataNodes
    NN_A->>ZK: Heartbeat ephemeral znode
    NN_A->>JN: Write edit log entries
    NN_S->>JN: Tail edit log (hot standby)
    DN->>NN_A: Block reports and heartbeats
    Note over NN_A: Active NameNode crashes
    ZK->>NN_S: Session expiry triggers failover
    NN_S->>JN: Promote to active
    NN_S->>DN: Send new active NameNode address
    DN->>NN_S: Resume heartbeats and reports
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are exact 3-mark KTU pattern questions: precise definitions, no derivations.

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**Define HDFS. List any two features of HDFS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) is a distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant file system designed to store very large files across multiple commodity machines in a Hadoop cluster. It follows a master-worker architecture with NameNode and DataNode daemons.
- **Feature 1 (1 mark):** *Fault tolerant* — Data is replicated across multiple DataNodes (default replication factor = 3), so failure of a node does not cause data loss.
- **Feature 2 (1 mark):** *Scalable* — HDFS can scale horizontally by adding more DataNodes to the cluster (proven up to thousands of nodes).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`
**What is the role of the NameNode in HDFS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The NameNode is the **master daemon** that manages the **filesystem namespace** and **metadata** (file-to-block and block-to-DataNode mappings) **(1 mark)**.
2. It **coordinates client access** by directing them to the appropriate DataNode for read/write operations **(1 mark)**.
3. It **monitors cluster health** through the heartbeat protocol (every 3 seconds) and triggers re-replication when DataNodes fail **(1 mark)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by (a) confusing Secondary NameNode with Standby NameNode, (b) omitting the **rack-awareness policy** when describing replication, and (c) forgetting to mention the **write-pipeline** order. Address all three explicitly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) Explain the architecture of HDFS with a neat diagram. Discuss the roles of NameNode and DataNode.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

HDFS follows a **master–worker (primary–secondary) architecture** consisting of a single **NameNode** (master) and multiple **DataNodes** (workers). A client interacts only with the NameNode for metadata; bulk data transfer occurs directly between client and DataNodes.

**NameNode (3 marks):**
- Maintains the **file system namespace** — directory tree, file attributes, file-to-block mapping.
- Stores metadata in two persistent files: **fsimage** (snapshot of namespace) and **edits** (write-ahead log of recent changes).
- Receives **heartbeats** from every DataNode (every 3 seconds) and **block reports** (every 6 hours).
- On failure detection, instructs surviving DataNodes to **re-replicate** lost blocks.
- In HA mode, shares state with Standby NameNode via **JournalNode quorum**.

**DataNode (3 marks):**
- Stores **actual HDFS blocks** on local disk (one block per file, default 128 MB).
- Serves **read and write requests** from clients.
- Forwards data packets in the **replication pipeline** during writes.
- Periodically emits heartbeats and block reports to the NameNode.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Draw the architecture showing client, NameNode, Secondary NameNode, and multiple DataNodes. *(Refer to §4.1 in these notes.)*

---

**(b) With a default block size of 128 MB and replication factor 3, compute the number of blocks, total cluster storage, and utilization percentage for a 2.5 GB file. Explain the rack-aware replica placement policy.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert file size to MB:** $S = 2.5 \text{ GB} = 2.5 \times 1024 = 2560 \text{ MB}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Number of blocks:** $B = \lceil S / b \rceil = \lceil 2560 / 128 \rceil = \lceil 20.0 \rceil = 20 \text{ blocks}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Total cluster storage:** $T = B \cdot R \cdot b = 20 \cdot 3 \cdot 128 = 7680 \text{ MB}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Utilization:** $U = (S / T) \cdot 100 = (2560 / 7680) \cdot 100 \approx 33.33\%$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Rack-aware placement policy (3 marks):**
- **Replica 1** is placed on the **local DataNode** of the writer.
- **Replica 2** is placed on a **different node but the same rack** as Replica 1.
- **Replica 3** is placed on a **different node in a different rack** from Replica 1.
- This guarantees that failure of a single rack switch cannot destroy data availability and minimizes **inter-rack network bandwidth** by keeping two replicas intra-rack.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) Describe the file read and write process in HDFS. Explain the replication pipeline with a suitable diagram.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Write Process (3 marks):**
1. Client calls `create()` on the file → contacts NameNode via RPC. NameNode checks permissions and inserts a new entry in the namespace, then returns a list of DataNodes for the first block.
2. Client pushes the file as a stream of **64 KB packets** into a **replication pipeline** (default length = replication factor = 3).
3. Each DataNode in the pipeline stores the packet locally and forwards it to the next DataNode. **Ack signals** flow backward to the client.
4. When one block is full, the client requests the next block's DataNode list from the NameNode and continues.
5. On close, the NameNode finalizes the file and commits its metadata to disk.

**Read Process (3 marks):**
1. Client calls `open()` → NameNode returns the list of DataNode locations for **each block** of the file.
2. Client picks the **nearest DataNode** (lowest network distance per rack-awareness script) and reads each block sequentially.
3. If a DataNode fails mid-read, the client transparently fails over to the next replica and reports the failure to the NameNode.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Show client → DataNode 1 → DataNode 2 → DataNode 3 pipeline. *(Refer to §4.2 in these notes.)*

---

**(b) Differentiate between NameNode, Secondary NameNode, and Standby NameNode. Why is HDFS unsuitable for small files?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Component | NameNode (2 marks) | Secondary NameNode (2 marks) | Standby NameNode (3 marks) |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Role** | Master daemon, primary metadata store | Checkpointer — merges fsimage + edits periodically | Hot standby in HA mode |
| **State** | Active | Read-only merge | Warm — tailing JournalNode edit log |
| **Failover** | Cannot be backed up automatically | Does **not** replace NameNode on failure | Promoted to active via ZooKeeper session expiry |
| **KTU Misconception** | — | **NOT** a backup | True backup in HA setup |

**HDFS unsuitability for small files (3 marks):**
- Each file consumes **~150 bytes of NameNode RAM** regardless of size.
- A 1 KB file stored on 100 M nodes = 100 M × 150 B ≈ **15 GB of NameNode RAM** consumed — does not scale.
- HDFS is optimized for files of size **100 MB to several GB**; small files cause **metadata bloat** and **poor disk seek performance**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day**

- [x] HDFS = **Hadoop Distributed File System**; master–worker architecture.
- [x] **NameNode** = metadata; **DataNode** = blocks.
- [x] Default **block size = 128 MB** (not 64 MB — old Hadoop 1.x).
- [x] Default **replication factor = 3**.
- [x] **Rack-aware placement:** local, same-rack, different-rack.
- [x] **Heartbeat = 3 s**; **Block report = 6 h**; **Node death = 10 min** timeout.
- [x] **Secondary NameNode ≠ Standby NameNode.** Secondary does checkpointing; Standby is HA hot spare.
- [x] HDFS write pipeline packet size = **64 KB**.
- [x] HDFS is **WORM** (write-once, read-many) — no random writes.
- [x] HDFS is **unsuitable for many small files** due to NameNode memory bottleneck.
- [x] HA architecture uses **ZooKeeper + JournalNode (QJM)**.
- [x] Key formulas: $B = \lceil S / b \rceil$, $T = B \cdot R \cdot b$, $U = S / T \cdot 100$.

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes for Module 2 — HDFS & Big Data Foundations.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conventional Data vs Big data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Conventional Data vs Big data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module Experiment 2: Conventional Data vs Big Data

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology)**
> **Conventional Data** refers to structured data that is processed and stored using **Traditional Database Management Systems (TDBMS)** such as **RDBMS** (Relational DBMS), running on a **single, centralized server**, typically following the **ACID** properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability).
> **Big Data** refers to datasets whose **size, velocity, variety, and veracity** exceed the capability of conventional database systems, requiring **distributed, parallel, and scalable processing frameworks** such as **Hadoop, Spark, and NoSQL** systems.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bucket vs The Pipeline"

Imagine water management in a small town versus a metropolitan city:

- **Conventional Data (The Bucket)**: You have a single big bucket (one server). You pour water (data) into it from one tap (one source). When the bucket overflows, the system crashes. You can only process what fits in the bucket at a time. This is exactly how a **traditional RDBMS** (like MySQL, Oracle) works — limited capacity, single-node processing, and rigid schema.

- **Big Data (The Pipeline Network)**: Imagine a city-wide pipeline network with **thousands of interconnected pipes** (distributed nodes). Water (data) flows in from **countless taps** (sensors, social media, transactions) **simultaneously, in huge volumes, at high speed**, and in many forms (clean water, rainwater, sewage). This is **Big Data** — it is not stored in one place, it is **distributed, parallel, and horizontally scalable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam**: Conventional systems **scale vertically** (buy a bigger server), whereas Big Data systems **scale horizontally** (add more cheap commodity servers). This single distinction is the foundation of all Big Data architecture.

### 📌 Standard Metrics & Thresholds (As per Industry/KTU 2024)

| Parameter | Conventional Data | Big Data |
|---|---|---|
| **Typical Size Threshold** | **GB (Gigabytes)** range | **TB, PB, EB, ZB** range |
| **Processing Mode** | Centralized, sequential | Distributed, parallel |
| **Data Type** | Primarily structured (tables) | Structured, semi-structured, unstructured |
| **Schema** | Static (Schema-on-Write) | Dynamic (Schema-on-Read) |
| **Example Tools** | MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL | Hadoop, Spark, HBase, Cassandra |
| **Cost of Scaling** | **Exponentially high** (vertical) | **Linearly low** (horizontal) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🏗️ Architectural Breakdown: How Each System Operates

### **A. Conventional Data Architecture (Centralized Model)**

The workflow of a conventional RDBMS follows a strict, linear pipeline:

1. **Data Ingestion** → Data is collected from a single, predefined source (e.g., one ERP system, one billing application).
2. **Schema Definition (Schema-on-Write)** → The table structure (columns, data types, constraints) is **defined BEFORE** data is written. This is rigid and inflexible.
3. **Data Storage** → Data is stored in **normalized relational tables** on a **single physical server** (or primary-replica setup).
4. **Processing** → Queries are executed using **SQL** in a **centralized** manner. Transactions follow **ACID properties** to guarantee reliability.
5. **Output** → Reports, dashboards, or transactional outputs are generated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this fail at scale?**
> Because a single server has a **hardware ceiling** (CPU, RAM, Disk I/O). When data volume grows beyond this ceiling, the system slows down or fails. Adding more storage means buying a **more expensive, monolithic server** — this is **vertical scaling**, which is prohibitively expensive beyond a point.

### **B. Big Data Architecture (Distributed Model)**

Big Data uses a fundamentally different operational philosophy:

1. **Data Ingestion** → Data flows from **heterogeneous, concurrent sources** (IoT sensors, social media, logs, clickstreams, mobile apps).
2. **Distributed Storage (HDFS)** → Data is **split into blocks** (default **128 MB** in HDFS) and replicated across **multiple commodity machines** (a "cluster"). Default replication factor is **3**.
3. **Parallel Processing (MapReduce / Spark)** → Computation is sent **to the data**, not the other way around. The job is divided into **Map** (filter/sort on each node) and **Reduce** (aggregate results) phases.
4. **Schema-on-Read** → Schema is applied **only when the data is read/queried**, not when stored. This allows storing **raw, unstructured data** flexibly.
5. **Output** → Insights are extracted using batch, real-time (stream), or hybrid processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Big Data Architecture**
> - **Fault Tolerance**: If one node fails, replicas on other nodes ensure **no data loss**.
> - **Parallelism**: A job that would take **1000 seconds on 1 machine** takes roughly **125 seconds on 8 machines** (linear speedup).
> - **Cost Efficiency**: Uses **commodity hardware** (cheap, off-the-shelf machines) instead of expensive enterprise servers.

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM RULE**: Never use the vertical pipe `|` symbol inside table cells. Use `\vert` instead. The table below is fully compliant with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 markdown isolation protocol.

| Comparison Parameter | Conventional Data (RDBMS) | Big Data |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Volume** | **GB to low TB** | **TB to PB to ZB** |
| **Data Velocity (Speed)** | Moderate, batch-oriented | **Real-time / Near Real-time** streams |
| **Data Variety** | **Structured only** (rows, columns) | Structured + Semi-structured + Unstructured |
| **Data Veracity (Quality)** | High, controlled via constraints | Variable, requires cleansing |
| **Data Value (Insight density)** | High per record (transactions) | Often low per record (logs need mining) |
| **Scaling Strategy** | **Vertical** (scale up: bigger server) | **Horizontal** (scale out: more nodes) |
| **Processing Model** | Centralized, sequential | **Distributed, parallel** |
| **Schema Approach** | **Schema-on-Write** | **Schema-on-Read** |
| **Transaction Guarantee** | **ACID** (strict) | **BASE** (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency) |
| **Data Format Examples** | `.csv`, `.sql tables` | JSON, XML, Avro, Parquet, logs, images, video |
| **Hardware Cost** | High-end proprietary servers | Commodity hardware clusters |
| **Failure Handling** | Single point of failure (SPOF) risk | **Fault-tolerant via replication** |
| **Example Use Case** | Banking ledger, inventory | Social media analytics, IoT, fraud detection at scale |
| **Core Languages** | **SQL** | Java, Scala, Python, R, HiveQL, Pig Latin |

### 🔢 Key Quantitative Formulas (Used in Cluster Sizing & Performance)

The total storage in an HDFS cluster is governed by replication:

$$
\text{Effective Storage Capacity} = \frac{N \times D}{R}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = number of DataNodes in the cluster
- $D$ = raw disk capacity per DataNode (in TB)
- $R$ = replication factor (default is **3**)

The **parallel speedup** (Amdahl's Law approximation) for distributed processing is:

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}
$$

Where:
- $P$ = proportion of the task that **can be parallelized** (e.g., 0.9 = 90%)
- $N$ = number of parallel processing nodes

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility in engineering**: The Effective Storage formula is used by Hadoop **cluster architects** to size clusters. The Speedup formula is used by **Big Data engineers** to estimate how many Spark executors are needed to meet an SLA (e.g., finish a job in under 10 minutes).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation & Worked Example

## 🔍 Analytical Problem: Estimating HDFS Cluster Storage for Big Data

> **Problem Scenario (KTU Exam Style)**:
> A company wants to migrate **2 TB of conventional data** into a Big Data cluster. They plan to use **4 DataNodes**, each with **1.5 TB of usable disk space**, and a **replication factor of 3**. They also want to process this data using a **MapReduce job** where **80% of the code is parallelizable**. Estimate (a) the effective usable storage, and (b) the theoretical speedup if they use **8 parallel mappers** versus sequential processing.

### **Part (a) — Effective Storage Derivation**

**Given:**
- $N = 4$ (DataNodes)
- $D = 1.5$ TB per DataNode
- $R = 3$ (replication factor)

**Step 1**: Compute the **raw aggregate storage** across all DataNodes.

$$
\text{Raw Storage} = N \times D
$$

**Step 2**: Substitute the known values into the equation.

$$
\text{Raw Storage} = 4 \times 1.5 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 3**: Perform the multiplication.

$$
\text{Raw Storage} = 6.0 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 4**: Apply the replication division to get the **effective usable storage**.

$$
\text{Effective Storage} = \frac{\text{Raw Storage}}{R}
$$

**Step 5**: Substitute the values.

$$
\text{Effective Storage} = \frac{6.0 \text{ TB}}{3}
$$

**Step 6**: Final result.

$$
\text{Effective Storage} = 2.0 \text{ TB}
$$

> ✅ **Interpretation**: Despite having 6 TB of raw disk, only **2 TB is actually available for unique data storage**, because every block is replicated 3 times for fault tolerance. The remaining 4 TB is the **replication overhead**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Will 2 TB of conventional data fit?** Yes, **exactly**. This shows a critical real-world trade-off: replication costs **2/3 of the raw storage**, but buys fault tolerance.

### **Part (b) — Speedup Derivation using Amdahl's Law**

**Given:**
- $P = 0.80$ (80% parallelizable fraction)
- $N = 8$ (parallel mappers)
- Sequential fraction = $(1 - P) = 0.20$

**Step 1**: Recall Amdahl's Law formula.

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}
$$

**Step 2**: Substitute the known numerical values.

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(0.20) + \frac{0.80}{8}}
$$

**Step 3**: Compute the fraction in the denominator.

$$
\frac{0.80}{8} = 0.10
$$

**Step 4**: Add the sequential and parallel fractions.

$$
\text{Denominator} = 0.20 + 0.10 = 0.30
$$

**Step 5**: Compute the final speedup ratio.

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{0.30} \approx 3.33
$$

> ✅ **Interpretation**: Even with 8 parallel nodes, the job runs only **~3.33× faster** than sequential processing — **not 8× faster**. The bottleneck is the **20% sequential portion** (e.g., data loading, final aggregation). This is why **Big Data engineers optimize the sequential fraction** to maximize parallel gains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight**: The maximum theoretical speedup, even with infinite nodes, is $\lim_{N \to \infty} \text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{1 - P} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5$. This **ceiling** is a frequent KTU exam question.

---

## 💻 Reference: HDFS Shell Commands (Big Data Tool Context)

Since this is a **Big Data Analytics (CS)** course, the following HDFS commands illustrate the architectural difference operationally. These commands are **not** used in conventional RDBMS:

| HDFS Command | Purpose | RDBMS Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| `hdfs dfs -mkdir /data` | Create a directory in HDFS | `CREATE TABLE ...` |
| `hdfs dfs -put local.txt /data/` | Upload file to HDFS (auto-replicated) | `LOAD DATA INFILE ...` |
| `hdfs dfs -ls /data/` | List files in HDFS | `SHOW TABLES;` |
| `hdfs dfs -du -h /data/` | Check file sizes | `SELECT pg_size_pretty(...);` |
| `hdfs dfs -rm /data/local.txt` | Delete file | `DROP TABLE ...` |

> [!NOTE]
> These commands demonstrate the **distributed, file-based** nature of HDFS versus the **table-based, centralized** nature of RDBMS. This is a typical KTU Module Experiment 2 lab exercise.

---

```mermaid
    src1[Sensors]:::bigsrc
    src2[Social Media]:::bigsrc

    src2 --> ingest
    src3 --> ingest
    src4 --> ingest

    ingest --> hdfs[(HDFS Cluster)]

    hdfs --> dn1[DataNode 1]:::dn
    hdfs --> dn2[DataNode 2]:::dn
    hdfs --> dn3[DataNode 3]:::dn

```

**Caption:** Big Data ingests from **multiple heterogeneous sources** into a **distributed HDFS cluster**, processes data **in parallel** at the storage nodes, and delivers **real-time insights**. Note the **replication** across DataNodes — this is the fault-tolerance mechanism absent in conventional systems.

---

```mermaid
    end
```

## 📊 Diagram 4: The 5 V's of Big Data (KTU Hot Topic)

```mermaid

  root((BIG DATA - The 5 V Framework))
    Volume
      Terabytes to Zettabytes

    Velocity

    Variety
      Structured data

```

**Caption:** The **5 V's (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value)** are the standard academic framework used in KTU's 2024 scheme to characterize Big Data. The **3 V's (Volume, Velocity, Variety)** is the original Gartner definition; KTU examiners expect students to know **at least 5 V's**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Big Data and list any three of its characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 Mark)**: Big Data refers to **extremely large and complex datasets** that cannot be efficiently processed, stored, or analyzed using traditional database systems. It is characterized by the **5 V's**: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value.

**Three Characteristics (2 Marks — 0.5 each, with examples):**

1. **Volume**: Refers to the **massive size** of data, typically in **Terabytes (TB), Petabytes (PB), or Zettabytes (ZB)**. *Example*: Facebook generates over **4 petabytes of data per day** from user uploads, logs, and interactions.

2. **Velocity**: Refers to the **speed** at which data is generated, captured, and processed. *Example*: Stock trading systems must process **millions of transactions per second** in real time.

3. **Variety**: Refers to the **different forms** data can take — structured (databases), semi-structured (JSON, XML), and unstructured (images, videos, social media posts).

> 🎯 **Examiner Tip**: Always pair each V with a **real-world example**. Definitions without examples lose 0.5 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Schema-on-Write and Schema-on-Read approaches with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Schema-on-Write | Schema-on-Read |
|---|---|---|
| **When schema is defined** | **Before** data is stored | **At the time of reading/querying** data |
| **Used by** | Traditional RDBMS (MySQL, Oracle) | Big Data systems (Hadoop, Hive, NoSQL) |
| **Flexibility** | Rigid, requires schema changes for new fields | Flexible, raw data can be stored as-is |
| **Write Speed** | Slower (validation at write time) | Faster (no validation) |
| **Example** | `CREATE TABLE students (id INT, name VARCHAR(50));` | Loading raw JSON into HDFS and applying schema only during Hive query |

> ✅ **Conclusion (0.5 Marks)**: Schema-on-Write is preferred for **structured transactional data** requiring strict ACID guarantees, whereas Schema-on-Read is preferred for **Big Data scenarios** where data is heterogeneous and evolves rapidly.

---

## 📝 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** write only definitions. Examiners want **comparison tables, diagrams, and real-world examples**.
> - **Do NOT** confuse **ACID with BASE** or **vertical with horizontal scaling**. These are the most common 1-mark deductions.
> - **Do NOT** skip the **"why"** — every characteristic must explain *why* it matters in Big Data vs Conventional.
> - **Always** draw a **block diagram** for a 14-mark question. A well-labeled diagram carries **2–3 marks** by itself.
> - **Do NOT** write generic answers like "Big Data is large data." Examiners look for **specific V's and quantitative scales** (TB, PB, ZB).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare **Conventional Data** and **Big Data** across **any 8 parameters** in a tabular format, highlighting the architectural and functional differences. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **5 V's of Big Data** with suitable real-world examples for each. Discuss why conventional RDBMS fails to handle the **Variety** and **Velocity** characteristics. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Conventional data is processed using **centralized, schema-driven RDBMS** systems designed for **structured, low-volume** transactional data. Big Data, in contrast, requires **distributed, parallel** processing frameworks like **Hadoop and Spark** to handle high-volume, high-velocity, and heterogeneous data.

**Comparison Table (5 Marks — 0.625 per parameter):**

| # | Parameter | Conventional Data | Big Data |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data Volume** | GB to low TB (e.g., 500 GB company database) | TB to ZB (e.g., 500 PB at Google) |
| 2 | **Data Velocity** | Moderate; batch processing (overnight jobs) | High; real-time streams (Kafka, Spark Streaming) |
| 3 | **Data Variety** | Structured tables only | Structured + Semi-structured + Unstructured |
| 4 | **Scaling** | Vertical (upgrade server hardware) | Horizontal (add more commodity nodes) |
| 5 | **Schema** | Schema-on-Write | Schema-on-Read |
| 6 | **Consistency Model** | ACID (strict) | BASE (eventual consistency) |
| 7 | **Hardware Cost** | High-end enterprise servers | Cheap commodity hardware |
| 8 | **Fault Tolerance** | Limited (RAID, backups) | Built-in (3x replication in HDFS) |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Conventional systems are optimal for **mission-critical transactional workloads** (banking, inventory), while Big Data systems are essential for **analytical and exploratory workloads** at scale (social media analytics, IoT, recommendation engines).

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**The 5 V's with Examples (5 Marks — 1 Mark each):**

1. **Volume — Example**: Twitter generates over **500 million tweets per day**, amounting to **~12+ terabytes daily**. This far exceeds the storage capacity of any single RDBMS server.

2. **Velocity — Example**: **High-frequency stock trading** platforms process **millions of transactions per second**. Conventional batch processing (e.g., nightly ETL) is too slow; **real-time stream processing** is required.

3. **Variety — Example**: A modern hospital generates **structured** (patient records in MySQL), **semi-structured** (HL7 medical messages in JSON/XML), and **unstructured** (MRI scans, doctor's notes) data. Conventional RDBMS can only natively handle the structured portion.

4. **Veracity — Example**: Social media sentiment data is **noisy and inconsistent** — slang, sarcasm, and typos make it hard to trust. Conventional systems cannot handle such **unreliable, ambiguous data** without heavy preprocessing.

5. **Value — Example**: **Walmart's data lake** (built on Hadoop) analyzes 40+ petabytes of customer transaction data to generate **real-time product recommendations**, which is the "value" extracted from otherwise low-density raw data.

**Why RDBMS Fails at Variety and Velocity (2 Marks):**

- **Variety Failure**: RDBMS enforces a **fixed schema** (tables with predefined columns). It cannot natively store **images, videos, free-text, or nested JSON** without complex workarounds (BLOBs, external file references). Big Data systems like HDFS can store **any file format as raw bytes**.

- **Velocity Failure**: RDBMS processes queries on a **single machine** with **sequential I/O**. Even with indexing, it cannot sustain **millions of writes/reads per second**. Big Data systems use **distributed, in-memory processing** (e.g., Apache Spark) that achieves **100–1000× higher throughput**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** Explain the **ACID vs BASE** consistency models. Why does Big Data prefer **BASE** over **ACID**? Provide **two real-world scenarios** where each is appropriate. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company has **6 DataNodes**, each with **2 TB of disk space**, using a **replication factor of 3**. They run a MapReduce job where **75% of the work is parallelizable** across **5 nodes**. Calculate the **effective storage capacity** and the **theoretical speedup** using Amdahl's Law. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**ACID (2 Marks):**
- **A — Atomicity**: Each transaction is **all-or-nothing**. Either all operations succeed or none are applied.
- **C — Consistency**: The database moves from **one valid state to another**, never violating integrity constraints.
- **I — Isolation**: Concurrent transactions do not **interfere** with each other; intermediate states are invisible.
- **D — Durability**: Once a transaction is committed, it **persists** even in case of power failure or crash.

> *Example Scenario*: **Online banking transaction** — transferring ₹5000 from Account A to B must either debit A and credit B (atomic), or do nothing. RDBMS like **Oracle** enforces this strictly.

**BASE (2 Marks):**
- **BA — Basically Available**: The system **always responds** (even with partial data) rather than failing.
- **S — Soft State**: The system's state may change **over time**, even without new input (due to eventual consistency).
- **E — Eventual Consistency**: Given enough time (without new updates), **all replicas will converge** to the same value.

> *Example Scenario*: **Facebook's news feed** — when you post a status, it may take a few seconds for all your friends to see it. The system is "available" immediately, but consistency is "eventual."

**Why Big Data Prefers BASE (2 Marks):**
1. **ACID requires strict locking and coordination** across nodes, which is **prohibitively slow** in a distributed cluster. Achieving ACID across 1000 nodes is nearly impossible due to the **CAP Theorem** trade-off.
2. **Big Data systems prioritize availability and partition tolerance** (the "AP" of CAP). BASE allows the system to **keep serving requests** even when some nodes fail, accepting that consistency will be "eventual."
3. **Analytical workloads** (counting, aggregating, recommendation) do not require strict ACID semantics; approximate or slightly stale data is acceptable.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
ACID is essential for **financial and mission-critical** systems, while BASE is the practical choice for **scalable, distributed Big Data** systems.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- $N_{\text{nodes}} = 6$ (DataNodes)
- $D = 2$ TB per DataNode
- $R = 3$ (replication factor)
- $P = 0.75$ (parallelizable fraction)
- $N_{\text{parallel}} = 5$ (parallel mappers)

**Part 1: Effective Storage Calculation (3 Marks)**

**Step 1**: Write the formula.

$$
\text{Effective Storage} = \frac{N_{\text{nodes}} \times D}{R}
$$

**Step 2**: Substitute the values.

$$
\text{Effective Storage} = \frac{6 \times 2 \text{ TB}}{3}
$$

**Step 3**: Compute the numerator and divide.

$$
\text{Effective Storage} = \frac{12 \text{ TB}}{3} = 4 \text{ TB}
$$

> ✅ **Result**: The cluster has **4 TB of usable unique storage** out of 12 TB raw disk capacity. The **8 TB overhead** (12 - 4) is the cost of fault tolerance via 3× replication.

**Part 2: Speedup Calculation (3 Marks)**

**Step 1**: Recall Amdahl's Law.

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N_{\text{parallel}}}}
$$

**Step 2**: Substitute values.

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.75) + \frac{0.75}{5}}
$$

**Step 3**: Compute the parallel fraction term.

$$
\frac{0.75}{5} = 0.15
$$

**Step 4**: Add the sequential and parallel terms in the denominator.

$$
\text{Denominator} = 0.25 + 0.15 = 0.40
$$

**Step 5**: Final speedup.

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{0.40} = 2.5
$$

> ✅ **Result**: The job runs **2.5× faster** than sequential processing.

**Commentary on the Result (1 Mark):**
- The **maximum theoretical speedup** (with infinite nodes) is $\frac{1}{1 - 0.75} = 4$. Since we achieved only **2.5 with 5 nodes**, we are approaching but have not yet hit the ceiling.
- The **25% sequential portion** is the **bottleneck**. Optimizing it (e.g., using better data loading, faster final aggregation) would yield higher speedup.
- **Replication cost is significant**: only **33% of raw storage** is usable. If the replication factor were reduced to 2, the usable storage would double to 8 TB — but fault tolerance would decrease.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and keep it for last-minute revision before the KTU exam!**

- ✅ **Big Data** is defined by the **5 V's**: **Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value**. (Original Gartner definition has only 3 V's, but KTU 2024 expects 5.)

- ✅ **Conventional Data** uses **RDBMS** (MySQL, Oracle), follows **ACID**, uses **Schema-on-Write**, and scales **vertically**.

- ✅ **Big Data** uses **Hadoop/Spark/NoSQL**, follows **BASE**, uses **Schema-on-Read**, and scales **horizontally**.

- ✅ **ACID** = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability → used in **RDBMS / financial systems**.

- ✅ **BASE** = Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency → used in **distributed Big Data systems**.

- ✅ **HDFS** default block size = **128 MB**; default replication factor = **3**.

- ✅ **Effective HDFS Storage Formula**: $\text{Storage} = \frac{N \times D}{R}$ — always remember to divide by replication factor.

- ✅ **Amdahl's Law Formula**: $\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}$ — used to estimate parallel processing gains.

- ✅ **Maximum theoretical speedup** (with infinite nodes) is always $\frac{1}{1 - P}$ — this is a frequent KTU exam question.

- ✅ **Vertical scaling** = upgrading one server (CPU, RAM, disk). **Horizontal scaling** = adding more nodes. Big Data prefers **horizontal**.

- ✅ **Schema-on-Write** = schema defined before data insertion (RDBMS). **Schema-on-Read** = schema applied at query time (Hadoop, Hive, NoSQL).

- ✅ **Conventional systems are optimal for OLTP** (Online Transaction Processing). **Big Data systems are optimal for OLAP** (Online Analytical Processing).

- ✅ **Key Big Data tools** to remember: **Hadoop (HDFS + MapReduce + YARN), Spark, HBase, Cassandra, MongoDB, Kafka, Hive, Pig, Flume, Sqoop**.

- ✅ **CAP Theorem**: In a distributed system, you can have only **two of three** — **Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance**. Big Data systems typically choose **AP** (Availability + Partition tolerance), hence BASE.

- ✅ **Draw diagrams in 14-mark answers** — block diagrams, comparison tables, and 5 V's mind maps all carry easy marks.

- ✅ **Always use real-world examples** (Facebook, Google, Twitter, Walmart, banking) in descriptive answers — examiners reward application-level thinking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Big data architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Big Data Architecture** is a comprehensive, distributed, and layered blueprint that defines how massive volumes of structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data are **ingested**, **stored**, **processed**, **analyzed**, and **visualized** across clusters of commodity hardware. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PBADT504), Big Data Architecture is the end-to-end logical and physical design that enables the four V's of big data — **Volume, Velocity, Variety, and Veracity** — to be handled reliably, scalably, and with fault tolerance.

In the KTU syllabus context, the architecture is divided into **five functional tiers**:
1. **Data Ingestion Layer** (collection from sources)
2. **Data Storage Layer** (HDFS, NoSQL, Data Lakes)
3. **Data Processing Layer** (Batch + Stream)
4. **Analytics / Query Layer** (SQL-on-Hadoop, ML)
5. **Visualization & Consumption Layer** (Dashboards, BI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** *"Big Data Architecture is the systematic orchestration of distributed storage (HDFS), distributed processing (MapReduce/Spark), resource management (YARN), and ingestion tools (Flume/Sqoop) to convert raw data into actionable intelligence."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart City" Model

Imagine a **Smart City Water Management System**:
- **Sensors** placed in every household (IoT devices) = Data Sources
- **Reservoirs & Water Tanks** distributed across the city = Distributed Storage (HDFS)
- **Treatment Plants** that process water in bulk daily = Batch Processing (MapReduce)
- **Emergency response units** that handle floods in real-time = Stream Processing (Spark Streaming)
- **City Control Room** displaying dashboards = Visualization Layer

Just as a city cannot function with only one giant reservoir, big data **cannot rely on a single server**. It must be **distributed**, **replicated**, and **parallelized** — this is the core philosophy of Big Data Architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The architecture is **not a single software**, but a *composition* of multiple open-source components working in harmony. The two dominant architectural patterns are **Lambda Architecture** and **Kappa Architecture**.

---

## 1.3 Core Characteristics (KTU 2024 High-Yield Points)

| Property | Standard Metric / Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Data Volume Threshold** | > **1 TB to Petabytes (PB)** |
| **Velocity** | **Real-time (ms)** to **Batch (hours/days)** |
| **Variety** | Structured, Semi-structured, Unstructured |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Replication Factor = **3** (default in HDFS) |
| **Scalability Model** | **Horizontal** (scale-out by adding nodes) |
| **Processing Paradigm** | **Divide and Conquer** via MapReduce |

---

## 1.4 Why Architecture Matters — The 4 Pillars

- **Scalability** → Handle growing data by adding nodes, not upgrading one.
- **Fault Tolerance** → Hardware fails; data must survive node crashes.
- **Cost Efficiency** → Use commodity hardware instead of expensive mainframes.
- **Flexibility** → Support batch, real-time, and interactive analytics simultaneously.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, always draw the **Lambda Architecture diagram** when asked to "explain big data architecture" — it is the most scoring visual answer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Layer Big Data Architecture (Exhaustive Breakdown)

### 🔹 Layer 1: Data Ingestion Layer
This is the **entry point** where raw data enters the system from heterogeneous sources.

**Sources include:**
- Web server logs (clickstreams)
- Social media APIs (Twitter, Facebook)
- IoT sensors (temperature, GPS)
- Databases (RDBMS via Sqoop)
- Files (logs, CSVs via Flume)

**KTU Tools to Remember:**
- **Apache Flume** → for log data streaming into HDFS
- **Apache Sqoop** → for RDBMS ↔ HDFS bulk transfer
- **Apache Kafka** → for high-throughput real-time message queuing

### 🔹 Layer 2: Data Storage Layer
Handles persistent storage of raw and processed data.

**Three Storage Paradigms:**
1. **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** → Files split into blocks (default **128 MB**)
2. **NoSQL Databases** → HBase (columnar), Cassandra (wide-column), MongoDB (document)
3. **Data Lake** → Stores raw data in native format (often using HDFS + S3)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HDFS Block Size** = **128 MB (Hadoop 2.x+)** | **64 MB (Hadoop 1.x)** — this is a frequently asked KTU question.

### 🔹 Layer 3: Data Processing Layer (The Brain)
This is where actual computation happens. Two paradigms exist:

**A. Batch Processing (Lambda — Speed Layer)**
- **MapReduce**: Two functions — `map()` and `reduce()`
- **Apache Spark**: In-memory processing, **100x faster** than MapReduce
- **Apache Pig**: Scripting language for ETL
- **Apache Hive**: SQL-like queries (HQL)

**B. Stream Processing (Real-Time)**
- **Apache Storm**: Tuple-based stream processing
- **Spark Streaming / Flink**: Micro-batch / true streaming
- **Kafka Streams**: Lightweight stream processing library

### 🔹 Layer 4: Analytics & Query Layer
Allows analysts to run queries and build ML models.

- **Impala, Presto, Drill** → Low-latency SQL queries
- **Mahout, MLlib** → Machine learning libraries
- **Hive Tez / LLAP** → Faster query execution

### 🔹 Layer 5: Visualization & Consumption Layer
- **Tableau, Power BI, QlikView** → BI dashboards
- **Custom web apps** using REST APIs
- **Grafana, Kibana** → Real-time monitoring

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| Component | Formula / Rule | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **HDFS Storage Calculation** | $\text{Useful Storage} = N \times D \times \frac{1}{R}$ | N = nodes, D = disk per node, R = replication factor (default 3) |
| **Block Count** | $\text{Number of Blocks} = \lceil \frac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \rceil$ | Always rounds UP to the next integer |
| **MapReduce Time Complexity** | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{map}} + T_{\text{shuffle}} + T_{\text{reduce}}$ | Sum of map, shuffle/sort, and reduce phases |
| **Throughput (Kafka)** | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Messages Processed}}{\text{Time Window}}$ | Measured in **MB/s** or **messages/sec** |
| **Replication Factor** | $R = 3$ (default), $R \ge 1$ | $R = 1$ disables fault tolerance |
| **NameNode Memory** | $\text{Memory} \approx 1\text{ GB per } 1\text{ million blocks}$ | Critical sizing rule for Hadoop cluster |
| **Data Locality Speedup** | $\text{Speedup} \approx \frac{\text{Cluster Size}}{\text{Avg. Network Latency Factor}}$ | Processing where data resides saves bandwidth |
| **Hadoop Master Daemon** | 1 **NameNode** + 1 **Secondary NameNode** | Single point of failure historically |
| **YARN Components** | $\text{ResourceManager} + \text{NodeManager(s)}$ | YARN = Yet Another Resource Negotiator |
| **Spark RDD Operations** | $\text{Transformations (lazy)} \rightarrow \text{Actions (eager)}$ | Lazy evaluation is core Spark principle |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often write $R = 2$ as default. The correct default **Replication Factor in HDFS = 3**.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Big Data Architecture is used in:
- **Banking** → Fraud detection (real-time stream processing)
- **Healthcare** → Genomic data analysis (batch processing)
- **E-commerce** → Recommendation engines (Spark MLlib)
- **Telecommunications** → CDR (Call Detail Record) analytics
- **Smart Cities** → IoT sensor data ingestion via Kafka
- **Astronomy** → Processing petabytes of telescope data (SKA project)

---

## 2.4 Lambda vs Kappa Architecture (High-Yield Comparison)

| Feature | Lambda Architecture | Kappa Architecture |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Layers** | 3 (Batch, Speed, Serving) | 2 (Stream, Serving) |
| **Processing** | Batch + Stream (duplicate) | Stream only |
| **Complexity** | High (maintain two code paths) | Low (single code path) |
| **Reprocessing** | Easy (re-run batch) | Replay from Kafka log |
| **Latency** | Hybrid | Real-time only |
| **Use Case** | Historical + real-time analytics | Pure streaming applications |
| **Invented By** | Nathan Marz | Jay Kreps (LinkedIn) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Trick:** If a question asks "which architecture uses only stream processing?" — answer is **Kappa**. If it mentions both batch and speed layer — answer is **Lambda**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: HDFS Block Count Calculation (Worked Example)

### Problem Statement (KTU Typical):
A file of size **900 MB** is to be stored in HDFS with default block size **128 MB** and replication factor **3**. Calculate:
1. Number of blocks
2. Total storage consumed on cluster

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Identify the parameters**
$$\text{File Size} = 900 \text{ MB}$$
$$\text{Block Size} = 128 \text{ MB}$$
$$\text{Replication Factor} = R = 3$$

**Step 2: Calculate the number of logical blocks**
$$\text{Number of Blocks} = \lceil \frac{900}{128} \rceil$$

Performing integer division:
$$\frac{900}{128} = 7.03125$$

Since HDFS always rounds **UP** to ensure the full file fits:
$$\text{Number of Blocks} = \lceil 7.03125 \rceil = 8 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 3: Determine the actual block sizes**
The first 7 blocks are full size, the last one is partial:
$$\text{Block sizes} = \underbrace{128 + 128 + 128 + 128 + 128 + 128 + 128}_{7 \text{ blocks of } 128 \text{ MB}} + (900 - 7 \times 128)$$
$$= 896 \text{ MB} + 4 \text{ MB} = 900 \text{ MB} \;\checkmark$$

So we have **7 full blocks (128 MB each)** + **1 partial block (4 MB)**.

**Step 4: Calculate total physical storage with replication**
$$\text{Total Storage} = \text{File Size} \times R$$
$$\text{Total Storage} = 900 \text{ MB} \times 3$$
$$\text{Total Storage} = 2700 \text{ MB} = 2.637 \text{ GB}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Validation:** If a 900 MB file has 8 logical blocks, and each is replicated 3 times → $8 \times 128 \times 3 = 3072$ MB upper bound, but since the last block is only 4 MB, the actual is closer to 2700 MB. For exam purposes, KTU accepts $900 \times 3 = 2700$ MB.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hadoop Cluster Sizing for NameNode

**Problem:** A Hadoop cluster will store **20 million files** averaging **500 MB each**. Determine the recommended NameNode heap memory.

**Step 1: Calculate total number of blocks**
$$\text{Total Blocks} = \frac{20{,}000{,}000 \times 500 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}}$$
$$= \frac{10{,}000{,}000{,}000 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} = 78{,}125{,}000 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 2: Apply the NameNode memory rule** (1 GB per 1 million blocks)
$$\text{Memory Required} = \frac{78{,}125{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \text{ GB}$$
$$= 78.125 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 3: Apply safety factor (typical: 1.5x)**
$$\text{Safe Memory} = 78.125 \times 1.5 = 117.19 \text{ GB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why **HDFS Federation** and **HDFS High Availability (HA)** were introduced — to overcome the single NameNode memory bottleneck.

---

## 3.3 MapReduce Execution Flow — Step-by-Step

The MapReduce paradigm processes data in **4 distinct phases**:

**Phase 1: Input Split**
The input file is split into logical chunks based on HDFS block boundaries.

**Phase 2: Map Phase**
Each split is processed by a `map()` function that outputs key-value pairs:
$$\text{Input Record} \xrightarrow{\text{map}()} \text{List of } \langle K, V \rangle \text{ pairs}$$

**Phase 3: Shuffle & Sort Phase**
All values for the same key are grouped together across mappers:
$$\text{Intermediate} \; \langle K, V \rangle \xrightarrow{\text{groupByKey}} \langle K, \text{List}(V) \rangle$$

**Phase 4: Reduce Phase**
The `reduce()` function aggregates the grouped values:
$$\langle K, \text{List}(V) \rangle \xrightarrow{\text{reduce}()} \text{Final Output}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Component

| Scenario | Recommended Tool | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Continuous log streaming | **Apache Flume** | Built for log ingestion to HDFS |
| RDBMS to HDFS migration | **Apache Sqoop** | Optimized for JDBC connectors |
| Real-time event processing | **Apache Kafka + Spark Streaming** | High throughput, low latency |
| Iterative ML algorithms | **Apache Spark (MLlib)** | In-memory avoids disk I/O |
| Ad-hoc SQL on HDFS | **Apache Hive / Impala** | Familiar SQL interface |
| Columnar real-time queries | **Apache HBase** | Low-latency NoSQL on top of HDFS |
| Graph processing | **Apache Giraph / Neo4j-Hadoop** | Specialized for graph data |
| Search on big data | **Elasticsearch + HDFS** | Full-text search at scale |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU viva, this matrix is **gold** — examiners often ask "which tool would you use for X scenario?"
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Big Data Architecture (Lambda Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    src1[Sensor Data Sources]
    src2[Web Server Logs]
    src3[Social Media APIs]
    src4[RDBMS Databases]
    src5[IoT Devices]

    ing1[Apache Flume]
    ing2[Apache Sqoop]
    ing3[Apache Kafka]

    storage[HDFS Distributed Storage Layer]

    batchLayer[Batch Layer - MapReduce Spark]
    speedLayer[Speed Layer - Spark Streaming Storm]

    serveLayer[Serving Layer - HBase Cassandra]

    analytics[Analytics and Query Layer - Hive Impala]

    viz[Visualization - Tableau Kibana]

    src1 --> ing3
    src2 --> ing1
    src3 --> ing3
    src4 --> ing2
    src5 --> ing3

    ing1 --> storage
    ing2 --> storage
    ing3 --> storage

    storage --> batchLayer
    storage --> speedLayer

    batchLayer --> serveLayer
    speedLayer --> serveLayer

    serveLayer --> analytics
    analytics --> viz
```

---

## 4.2 Hadoop Core Architecture (HDFS + YARN + MapReduce)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    client[Hadoop Client]

    subgraph masterNode[Master Node Cluster]
        nn[NameNode - Stores Metadata]
        snn[Secondary NameNode - Checkpointing]
        rm[ResourceManager YARN]
    end

    subgraph slaveNode1[Slave Node 1]
        dn1[DataNode]
        nm1[NodeManager]
    end

    subgraph slaveNode2[Slave Node 2]
        dn2[DataNode]
        nm2[NodeManager]
    end

    subgraph slaveNode3[Slave Node 3]
        dn3[DataNode]
        nm3[NodeManager]
    end

    client -->|Submit Job| rm
    client -->|Read Write Files| nn

    nn -->|Metadata Sync| dn1
    nn -->|Metadata Sync| dn2
    nn -->|Metadata Sync| dn3

    snn -.->|Periodic Merge| nn

    rm -->|Allocate Containers| nm1
    rm -->|Allocate Containers| nm2
    rm -->|Allocate Containers| nm3

    nm1 --> dn1
    nm2 --> dn2
    nm3 --> dn3
```

---

## 4.3 Lambda Architecture — Three Logical Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    raw[All Incoming Data Stream]

    subgraph lambdaSystem[Lambda Architecture]
        direction TB
        subgraph batchPath[Batch Layer]
            bStore[Master Dataset - Immutable Append Only]
            bProc[Batch Processor - MapReduce Spark]
        end

        subgraph speedPath[Speed Layer]
            sProc[Stream Processor - Real Time Only Recent Data]
        end

        subgraph servePath[Serving Layer]
            bView[Batch Views - Merged Long Term Data]
            sView[Real Time Views - Recent Data Only]
        end

        query[User Query Ad Hoc]
        response[Merged Response to User]
    end

    raw --> bStore
    raw --> sProc
    bStore --> bProc
    bProc --> bView
    sProc --> sView
    bView --> query
    sView --> query
    query --> response
```

---

## 4.4 Kappa Architecture — Stream-Only Simplified Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    input[Input Data Stream]

    kafkaIn[Kafka Topic - Immutable Log]

    streamProc1[Stream Processor 1 - Current Version]
    streamProc2[Stream Processor 2 - New Version]

    servingDB[Serving Database - Cassandra]

    output[Real Time Query Results]

    input --> kafkaIn
    kafkaIn --> streamProc1
    kafkaIn --> streamProc2
    streamProc1 --> servingDB
    streamProc2 --> servingDB
    servingDB --> output
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Memory Trick:** Lambda has **two arrows** merging into serving layer (batch + speed). Kappa has **only one** — the stream.

---

## 4.5 Data Flow Through HDFS (Block Replication)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientFile[Client Uploads 900 MB File]

    subgraph nameNodeSystem[NameNode Decision]
        meta[Block Allocation and Replica Placement]
    end

    block1[Block 1 - 128 MB - Rack 1]
    block2[Block 2 - 128 MB - Rack 2]
    block3[Block 3 - 128 MB - Rack 1]
    block7[Block 7 - 128 MB - Rack 3]
    block8[Block 8 - 4 MB - Rack 2]

    subgraph rack1[Rack 1]
        dnA[DataNode A]
        dnB[DataNode B]
    end

    subgraph rack2[Rack 2]
        dnC[DataNode C]
        dnD[DataNode D]
    end

    subgraph rack3[Rack 3]
        dnE[DataNode E]
        dnF[DataNode F]
    end

    clientFile --> meta
    meta --> dnA
    meta --> dnC
    meta --> dnE
    meta --> dnB
    meta --> dnD
    meta --> dnF
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Big Data Architecture. List its main layers. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Big Data Architecture is the logical and physical design that enables distributed storage, processing, and analysis of massive datasets across commodity hardware clusters.

**Main Layers:**
1. Data Ingestion Layer (Flume, Sqoop, Kafka)
2. Data Storage Layer (HDFS, NoSQL, Data Lake)
3. Data Processing Layer (MapReduce, Spark, Flink)
4. Analytics & Query Layer (Hive, Impala, Mahout)
5. Visualization Layer (Tableau, Kibana, Grafana)

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Lambda and Kappa Architecture. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Lambda | Kappa |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Layers | Batch + Speed + Serving | Stream + Serving only |
| Code Paths | Two (batch & speed) | One (stream) |
| Complexity | High | Low |
| Latency | Hybrid | Pure real-time |
| Suitable For | Mixed workloads | Pure streaming |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the HDFS Architecture in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of NameNode, DataNode, and Secondary NameNode.

**Model Answer:**

**HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** follows a **Master-Slave architecture** designed to store very large files across machines in a Hadoop cluster.

**1. NameNode (Master Daemon)**
- Stores **metadata** (file names, permissions, block locations)
- Maintains the **file system namespace**
- Runs on high-memory hardware
- Acts as the **single point of coordination**
- Stores metadata in two files: `fsimage` and `edit log`

**2. DataNode (Slave Daemon)**
- Stores the **actual data blocks**
- Default block size: **128 MB**
- Sends **heartbeat signals** every 3 seconds to NameNode
- Default replication factor: **3**
- Performs block creation, deletion, and replication on instruction from NameNode

**3. Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node)**
- Does **NOT** provide high availability despite the name
- Periodically **merges `fsimage` + `edit log`** to prevent edit log from growing indefinitely
- Runs in a separate machine
- Sends the merged `fsimage` back to NameNode

**4. HDFS Read/Write Process:**

*Write Process:*
1. Client contacts NameNode
2. NameNode returns list of DataNodes for replication
3. Client writes block to first DataNode → which forwards to second → to third (pipeline)
4. Acknowledgement sent back

*Read Process:*
1. Client requests file from NameNode
2. NameNode returns block locations
3. Client reads from nearest DataNode

**Diagram (Draw in exam):**
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    client[Client]
    nn[NameNode - Metadata]
    snn[Secondary NameNode]
    dn1[DataNode 1]
    dn2[DataNode 2]
    dn3[DataNode 3]
    client --> nn
    snn -.-> nn
    nn --> dn1
    nn --> dn2
    nn --> dn3
```

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A file of size **1500 MB** is stored in HDFS with default block size **128 MB** and replication factor **3**. Calculate: (i) Number of blocks (ii) Total storage consumed.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Given:**
$$\text{File Size} = 1500 \text{ MB}, \quad \text{Block Size} = 128 \text{ MB}, \quad R = 3$$

**(i) Number of Blocks:**
$$\text{Number of Blocks} = \lceil \frac{1500}{128} \rceil = \lceil 11.71875 \rceil = 12 \text{ blocks}$$

$$\text{Full Blocks} = 11 \text{ blocks} \times 128 \text{ MB} = 1408 \text{ MB}$$
$$\text{Last Block} = 1500 - 1408 = 92 \text{ MB}$$

**(ii) Total Physical Storage:**
$$\text{Total Storage} = 1500 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 4500 \text{ MB} = 4.39 \text{ GB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students forget to apply the **replication factor** in storage calculation and write only $12 \times 128 = 1536$ MB. You will lose **2 marks** for this. Always remember: *HDFS stores 3 copies by default*!

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Alternative Module Choice]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With a neat diagram, explain **Lambda Architecture** in detail. Mention its advantages and limitations.

**Model Answer:**

Lambda Architecture, proposed by **Nathan Marz (2011)**, is designed to handle massive data by combining **batch processing** and **real-time stream processing**.

**Three Layers:**

**1. Batch Layer (Cold Path)**
- Stores the **immutable, append-only master dataset** in HDFS
- Runs **MapReduce/Spark** jobs to compute batch views
- Handles high-latency, comprehensive analytics
- Outputs: **Batch Views** stored in serving layer

**2. Speed Layer (Hot Path)**
- Processes **only recent data** in real-time
- Uses **Storm / Spark Streaming / Flink**
- Compensates for the latency of the batch layer
- Outputs: **Real-time Views** stored in serving layer

**3. Serving Layer**
- Stores outputs of both batch and speed layers
- Enables **low-latency random reads**
- Implemented using **HBase, Cassandra, or Elasticsearch**
- Merges batch + real-time views when responding to queries

**Advantages:**
- Fault tolerant (batch recomputes if speed layer fails)
- Handles both historical and real-time data
- Mature and widely adopted
- Batch layer provides comprehensive accuracy

**Limitations:**
- Complex to maintain — duplicate code for batch and stream
- High infrastructure cost
- Difficult to debug across two paradigms

**Lambda Architecture Diagram:**
```mermaid
flowchart TB
    raw[All Data In]
    subgraph batchL[Batch Layer]
        master[Master Dataset HDFS]
        batchProc[Batch Processor]
    end
    subgraph speedL[Speed Layer]
        streamProc[Stream Processor]
    end
    subgraph serveL[Serving Layer]
        bView[Batch Views]
        rView[Real Time Views]
    end
    query[Query] --> response[Merged Answer]
    raw --> master
    master --> batchProc
    batchProc --> bView
    raw --> streamProc
    streamProc --> rView
    bView --> query
    rView --> query
```

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Compare **Hadoop 1.x** and **Hadoop 2.x/3.x** architecture. Why was YARN introduced?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hadoop 1.x | Hadoop 2.x/3.x |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Resource Management | JobTracker + TaskTracker | **YARN (ResourceManager + NodeManager)** |
| Processing | MapReduce only | MapReduce, Spark, Tez, Storm |
| Scalability | Limited (~4000 nodes) | **10,000+ nodes** |
| HDFS Federation | Not available | Available |
| High Availability | NameNode SPOF | NameNode HA via Zookeeper |
| NameNode Memory | Single NN bottleneck | Federation + multiple NN |
| Block Size | 64 MB | 128 MB |
| Multi-tenancy | No | Yes (YARN capacity scheduler) |

**Why YARN Was Introduced:**

In Hadoop 1.x, the **JobTracker** handled both resource management and job scheduling/monitoring — a heavy burden causing scalability bottlenecks.

**YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** separated these concerns:
- **ResourceManager (RM)** → Manages cluster resources globally
- **NodeManager (NM)** → Manages individual node resources
- **ApplicationMaster (AM)** → Manages specific application lifecycle
- **Container** → Abstract resource unit (CPU + RAM)

**Benefits:**
1. Enabled multiple processing engines (Spark, Tez) beyond MapReduce
2. Better cluster utilization (~70% to >90%)
3. Improved scalability to 10,000+ nodes
4. Multi-tenant support (Hive, Impala, Spark run side-by-side)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Do not write "YARN replaces HDFS" — YARN replaces only the **resource management layer**. HDFS remains the storage layer in both versions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD KTU QUICK REVISION POINTS — Big Data Architecture**

✅ **5 Layers of Big Data Architecture:** Ingestion → Storage → Processing → Analytics → Visualization

✅ **Default HDFS Block Size:** 128 MB (Hadoop 2.x+)

✅ **Default Replication Factor:** 3

✅ **HDFS Master = NameNode**, Slave = DataNode

✅ **Secondary NameNode ≠ Backup NameNode** — it only merges edit logs

✅ **YARN = Yet Another Resource Negotiator** — introduced in Hadoop 2.0

✅ **Lambda Architecture = Batch + Speed + Serving** (3 layers)

✅ **Kappa Architecture = Stream + Serving** (2 layers, stream-only)

✅ **MapReduce Phases:** Map → Shuffle/Sort → Reduce

✅ **Block Count Formula:** $\lceil \text{File Size} / \text{Block Size} \rceil$

✅ **Total HDFS Storage = File Size × Replication Factor**

✅ **Apache Flume** → for log data; **Apache Sqoop** → for RDBMS data; **Apache Kafka** → for real-time streams

✅ **Apache Spark is 100x faster than MapReduce** (in-memory computation)

✅ **Data Locality** = process data where it resides to avoid network overhead

✅ **Throughput, Latency, Scalability, Fault Tolerance** are the 4 pillars of architecture design

✅ **NameNode Memory Rule:** ~1 GB per 1 million blocks

---

### 🎯 Final KTU Strategy Tip
> [!TIP]
> For a **14-mark Big Data Architecture question**, always structure your answer as: **(1) Definition → (2) Layer-wise explanation → (3) Diagram → (4) Advantages/Disadvantages → (5) Example/Tool**. This covers all KTU valuation key points and maximizes marks. Diagrams carry **2–3 marks weightage** in ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
