<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big Data Platform:** A Big Data platform is a comprehensive, integrated software framework designed to ingest, store, process, analyze, and visualize massive volumes of structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data (the **5 V's: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value**) that exceed the storage and computational capacity of traditional single-node database systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hadoop Ecosystem:** The Hadoop Ecosystem is an open-source, Java-based, distributed computing framework managed by the **Apache Software Foundation**. It provides a scalable, fault-tolerant platform for **batch processing** and **storage** of Big Data across clusters of commodity hardware. The ecosystem is anchored by two core pillars: **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** for storage and **MapReduce** for processing, with **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** acting as the cluster resource manager.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model)

Imagine a **massive, international restaurant chain** that must serve 10 million meals per day, but the recipes (datasets) are too large to fit in any single kitchen (computer).

| Hadoop Component | Real-World Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **HDFS** | The **Central Warehouse** with multiple cold-storage rooms | Splits huge datasets into blocks and distributes them across machines |
| **NameNode** | The **Warehouse Manager** (master registry) | Keeps the directory tree and tracks which data block is stored where |
| **DataNode** | The **Storage Shelves** in each cold room | Stores the actual data blocks and serves read/write requests |
| **MapReduce** | The **Head Chef + Line Cooks** in parallel kitchens | Processes data in two phases: `Map` (chop ingredients) and `Reduce` (cook the dish) |
| **YARN** | The **Floor Manager** who assigns tasks to cooks | Schedules jobs, allocates CPU/RAM to each task |
| **Hive / Pig** | The **Sous-Chef Translator** who converts chef-language to SQL-like language | Lets analysts query Hadoop data without writing raw Java/MapReduce code |
| **Sqoop / Flume** | The **Loading Dock** (incoming goods) | Imports data from RDBMS (Sqoop) or streams log data (Flume) |
| **HBase** | The **Quick-Service Counter** | Provides real-time, random NoSQL read/write access on top of HDFS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Hadoop is **not** a single program — it is a *collection* of tightly integrated services (an "ecosystem") that work together. A typical experiment demonstrates installation of this ecosystem, configuring HDFS, and running a sample MapReduce word-count job.

## 1.3 Why Hadoop? (Motivating Characteristics)

1. **Scalability** — Add hundreds of commodity nodes (horizontal scaling).
2. **Fault Tolerance** — Data is replicated (default **replication factor = 3**); if one node fails, copies are available on other nodes.
3. **Cost-Effective** — Uses cheap commodity hardware instead of expensive mainframes.
4. **Flexibility** — Handles structured, semi-structured (JSON, XML), and unstructured (logs, images, videos) data.
5. **Parallelism** — Distributes work across the cluster for high throughput.
6. **Open Source** — Apache License 2.0; no vendor lock-in.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Block Size:** HDFS uses a default block size of **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x and 3.x) — significantly larger than the 4–64 KB blocks of traditional file systems. This minimizes NameNode metadata overhead.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Architecture of Hadoop

Hadoop's architecture is conventionally divided into three logical layers, each with a distinct responsibility:

### **Layer 1 — Storage Layer: HDFS**
- A **write-once, read-many (WORM)** distributed file system.
- Splits files into fixed-size **blocks** (default **128 MB**) and stores them across cluster nodes.
- Maintains a **master/slave topology**:
  - **NameNode (Master):** Manages metadata — file-to-block mapping, namespace tree, DataNode heartbeats.
  - **DataNode (Slave):** Stores actual data blocks; sends periodic heartbeats to the NameNode (default every **3 seconds**).
  - **Secondary NameNode:** *Misnomer* — it does **not** act as a failover. It periodically merges the *fsimage* with *edit logs* to prevent edit-log overflow (checkpointing).

### **Layer 2 — Resource Management Layer: YARN (Since Hadoop 2.x)**
YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) decouples resource management from data processing. It consists of:
- **ResourceManager (RM):** Global authority that arbitrates cluster resources.
- **NodeManager (NM):** Per-node agent that monitors containers (CPU + RAM).
- **ApplicationMaster (AM):** Per-application; negotiates resources with RM and works with NMs.
- **Container:** An abstract unit of allocated resources (RAM in MB + CPU cores).

### **Layer 3 — Processing Layer: MapReduce (and Higher Abstractions)**
- The original programming model for parallel data processing.
- Two user-defined functions: `map(k1, v1) → list(k2, v2)` and `reduce(k2, list(v2)) → list(k3, v3)`.
- Complemented by higher-level languages: **Hive (HQL)**, **Pig (Pig Latin)**, **Spark**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Working of HDFS Write Operation (Exam Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently tests the read/write pipeline.** Memorize the 4-step ACK handshake.

1. **Client → NameNode:** The client requests permission to write file `F`. NameNode checks namespace and returns a list of DataNodes for each block.
2. **Client → DataNode 1:** The client pushes the first block to the first DataNode.
3. **DataNode 1 → DataNode 2 → DataNode 3:** A *pipeline* is formed; blocks are replicated in a chain.
4. **ACK back:** A success acknowledgment propagates back to the client; the next block begins.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Working of HDFS Read Operation

1. Client sends `open(F)` to the NameNode.
2. NameNode returns the **block locations** (DataNode IPs) for each block.
3. Client connects to the *nearest* DataNode and reads the block.
4. If a DataNode fails, the client transparently reads from a replica.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Hadoop Numerical Questions** — These appear in KTU University Exams regularly.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HDFS Block Count | $N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{FileSize}{BlockSize} \right\rceil$ | Rounded **up** to the nearest integer; **Unit:** blocks |
| 2 | Total Storage with Replication | $Storage_{total} = N_{blocks} \times BlockSize \times R$ | $R$ = Replication factor (default **3**); **Unit:** Bytes |
| 3 | Storage Overhead | $Overhead = \dfrac{R - 1}{1} \times 100\% = (R-1) \times 100\%$ | With $R=3$, overhead = **200%** |
| 4 | Effective Storage Capacity | $Capacity_{usable} = \dfrac{Disk_{raw}}{R}$ | **Unit:** Bytes |
| 5 | MapReduce Data Locality Speed Gain | $Speedup \approx \dfrac{T_{remote}}{T_{local}} \approx 10\times$ | Reading local data is ~10× faster than cross-rack |
| 6 | YARN Container Allocation | $Containers_{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{RAM_{node}}{RAM_{container}} \right\rfloor$ | **Unit:** containers |
| 7 | NameNode Heartbeat Interval | $T_{hb} = 3 \text{ seconds}$ | Default; 10× = **30 s** timeout marks a DataNode as dead |
| 8 | Block Report Interval | $T_{br} = 6 \text{ hours}$ | Default |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Yahoo & Facebook** pioneered Hadoop for search-indexing and ad-log analytics.
- **Healthcare:** Processing petabytes of genomic data (DNA sequencing).
- **Finance:** Real-time fraud detection over transaction streams.
- **IoT:** Storing and analyzing sensor telemetry from millions of devices.
- **KTU Context:** This experiment sets up a single-node Hadoop cluster and runs a **word-count MapReduce job** to demonstrate distributed processing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Computing Number of HDFS Blocks

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU-style):** A file of size **1.2 GB** is stored in HDFS with the default block size of **128 MB** and a replication factor of **3**. Calculate: (a) the number of blocks, (b) total cluster storage consumed, and (c) the storage overhead percentage.

### Step 1 — Convert all units to a common base (MB)
We have:
- File size: $S = 1.2 \text{ GB} = 1.2 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 1228.8 \text{ MB}$
- Block size: $B = 128 \text{ MB}$

### Step 2 — Apply the block-count formula
$$N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{S}{B} \right\rceil$$

$$N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{1228.8}{128} \right\rceil$$

$$N_{blocks} = \left\lceil 9.6 \right\rceil$$

$$N_{blocks} = 10 \text{ blocks}$$

**Logic:** The 10th block contains only $128 \times 0.4 = 51.2 \text{ MB}$ of data (the remaining 76.8 MB is *wasted* padding). This is a hallmark of HDFS — partial blocks are *padded*, not split.

### Step 3 — Compute total storage consumed
$$Storage_{total} = N_{blocks} \times B \times R$$

$$Storage_{total} = 10 \times 128 \times 3$$

$$Storage_{total} = 3840 \text{ MB} = 3.75 \text{ GB}$$

**Logic:** Each of the 10 blocks is replicated 3 times across the cluster, so the actual data physically occupies 3.75 GB even though the logical file is only 1.2 GB.

### Step 4 — Compute storage overhead percentage
$$Overhead \% = \dfrac{Storage_{total} - S}{S} \times 100\%$$

$$Overhead \% = \dfrac{3840 - 1228.8}{1228.8} \times 100\%$$

$$Overhead \% = \dfrac{2611.2}{1228.8} \times 100\%$$

$$Overhead \% \approx 212.5\%$$

**Equivalently**, using the shorthand formula: $Overhead = (R-1) \times 100\% = 2 \times 100\% = 200\%$ (a small discrepancy arises because the last block is padded but the original file is not).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MapReduce Word-Count Numerical Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A text file contains the lines:
> `"big data big data hadoop ecosystem hadoop"`
> Compute the (a) Mapper output, (b) Shuffle & Sort output, (c) Reducer output.

### Step 1 — Mapper Phase
The mapper emits each (word, 1) pair:

| Input Line | Mapper Output |
|---|---|
| big data big data hadoop ecosystem hadoop | (big,1), (data,1), (big,1), (data,1), (hadoop,1), (ecosystem,1), (hadoop,1) |

$$Output_{map} = \{(big,1), (data,1), (big,1), (data,1), (hadoop,1), (ecosystem,1), (hadoop,1)\}$$

### Step 2 — Shuffle & Sort Phase
The framework groups by key:

$$Output_{shuffle} = \{\{big : [1,1]\}, \{data : [1,1]\}, \{ecosystem : [1]\}, \{hadoop : [1,1]\}\}$$

### Step 3 — Reducer Phase
Sums the values per key:

$$Output_{reduce} = \{(big,2), (data,2), (ecosystem,1), (hadoop,2)\}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — YARN Container Allocation

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A DataNode has **64 GB RAM** and **16 CPU cores**. The YARN configuration reserves **8 GB RAM** and **2 cores** for system services and uses container sizes of **4 GB RAM** and **1 core**. Compute the maximum number of containers.

### Step 1 — Compute available resources
$$RAM_{avail} = 64 \text{ GB} - 8 \text{ GB} = 56 \text{ GB}$$
$$Cores_{avail} = 16 - 2 = 14 \text{ cores}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the container-count formula (twice — take the minimum)
$$Containers_{RAM} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{RAM_{avail}}{RAM_{container}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{56}{4} \right\rfloor = 14$$

$$Containers_{Cores} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{Cores_{avail}}{Cores_{container}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{14}{1} \right\rfloor = 14$$

### Step 3 — Final answer
$$Containers_{max} = \min(14, 14) = 14 \text{ containers}$$

**Logic:** YARN considers *both* the RAM constraint and the CPU constraint; the binding constraint limits the cluster.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hadoop Component Roles

| Component | Layer | Type | Master/Slave | Key File / Port |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NameNode | Storage | Master | Master | `fsimage`, `edit logs`; Port **9000/8020** |
| DataNode | Storage | Slave | Slave | Stores blocks; Port **50010** |
| Secondary NameNode | Storage | Helper | — | Checkpointing; Port **50090** |
| ResourceManager | Resource | Master | Master | Port **8032** |
| NodeManager | Resource | Slave | Slave | Port **8042** |
| JobHistoryServer | Processing | Master | Master | Port **10020** |
| HBase Master | NoSQL | Master | Master | Port **60000** |
| Zookeeper | Coordination | Ensemble | — | Port **2181** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Hadoop Ecosystem Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client1[Client Application] --> namenodeA[NameNode]
    client1 --> resourceA[ResourceManager]
    
    subgraph dataLayer[Data Storage Layer HDFS]
        namenodeA -->|manages| datanode1[DataNode 1]
        namenodeA -->|manages| datanode2[DataNode 2]
        namenodeA -->|manages| datanode3[DataNode 3]
    end
    
    subgraph resourceLayer[Resource Management Layer YARN]
        resourceA -->|allocates| nodemanager1[NodeManager 1]
        resourceA -->|allocates| nodemanager2[NodeManager 2]
        resourceA -->|allocates| nodemanager3[NodeManager 3]
    end
    
    subgraph processLayer[Processing Layer]
        mr[MapReduce Engine] --> jobhist[JobHistory Server]
        hiveA[Hive Warehouse] --> mr
        pigA[Pig Latin Script] --> mr
        sparkA[Apache Spark] --> resourceA
    end
    
    subgraph ingestLayer[Ingestion Tools]
        sqoopA[Sqoop RDBMS Import] --> namenodeA
        flumeA[Flume Log Streaming] --> namenodeA
        kafkaA[Kafka Message Queue] --> namenodeA
    end
    
    subgraph nosqlLayer[NoSQL and Query Layer]
        hbaseA[HBase NoSQL] --> namenodeA
        drillA[Apache Drill SQL] --> hbaseA
    end
    
    subgraph coordLayer[Coordination and Monitoring]
        zkA[Zookeeper Coordination] --> namenodeA
        ambariA[Ambari Monitoring] --> resourceA
        oozieA[Oozie Workflow] --> mr
    end
```

## 4.2 HDFS Read Architecture (Data Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant NN as NameNode
    participant DN1 as DataNode 1
    participant DN2 as DataNode 2
    
    C->>NN: 1. open request for file F
    NN-->>C: 2. return block locations (DataNode list)
    C->>DN1: 3. read block B1 (nearest)
    DN1-->>C: 4. stream block bytes
    C->>DN2: 5. read block B2
    DN2-->>C: 6. stream block bytes
    C->>C: 7. assemble final file
```

## 4.3 HDFS Write Architecture (Pipeline Replication)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client] -->|1. write request| B[NameNode]
    B -->|2. block locations| A
    A -->|3. push block| C[DataNode 1]
    C -->|4. replicate| D[DataNode 2]
    D -->|5. replicate| E[DataNode 3]
    E -->|6. ACK| D
    D -->|7. ACK| C
    C -->|8. ACK| A
```

## 4.4 YARN Application Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[Client Submits Application] --> rm1[ResourceManager]
    rm1 -->|allocate container| nm1[NodeManager 1]
    nm1 -->|launch| am[ApplicationMaster]
    am -->|request resources| rm1
    rm1 -->|allocate containers| nm1
    rm1 -->|allocate containers| nm2[NodeManager 2]
    am -->|execute task| t1[Map Task 1]
    am -->|execute task| t2[Map Task 2]
    am -->|execute task| t3[Reduce Task]
    t1 -->|status| am
    t2 -->|status| am
    t3 -->|complete| am
    am -->|finish| rm1
```

## 4.5 Complete MapReduce Word-Count Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input[Input Split 1 and 2] --> mapper1[Mapper 1]
    input --> mapper2[Mapper 2]
    mapper1 -->|intermediate| shuffle[Shuffle and Sort]
    mapper2 -->|intermediate| shuffle
    shuffle -->|group by key| reducer[Reducer]
    reducer --> output[Final Output: word counts]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Guide:** Notice the **fan-out** (one input → many mappers) followed by **fan-in** (many mappers → one reducer). This is the *divide and conquer* essence of MapReduce.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define the Hadoop Ecosystem. List any four of its components with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Hadoop Ecosystem is a suite of open-source Apache projects that together enable distributed storage and processing of Big Data on commodity clusters. The two foundational components are **HDFS** (Hadoop Distributed File System) for storage and **MapReduce** for parallel processing; YARN manages cluster resources. Four other components (any four, 0.5 mark each) are:
1. **Hive** — SQL-like data warehouse for Hadoop.
2. **Pig** — High-level data flow language (Pig Latin).
3. **HBase** — NoSQL columnar database on top of HDFS.
4. **Sqoop** — Tool to import/export data from RDBMS into HDFS.
5. **Flume** — Service to stream log data into HDFS.
6. **Oozie** — Workflow scheduler for Hadoop jobs.
*(Any valid four — full 3 marks)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of the NameNode in HDFS? Why is it called a "single point of failure"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role of NameNode (2 marks):** The NameNode is the master server in HDFS. It stores all **metadata** — the file namespace, the mapping of files to data blocks, block locations across DataNodes, file permissions, and access times. It does *not* store user data; it only tracks where the blocks live. On client read/write requests, it returns the list of DataNodes holding the relevant blocks.
- **Single Point of Failure (1 mark):** Because the NameNode holds the entire directory tree in RAM, its failure halts the cluster — DataNodes cannot be located, and no new read/write can be served. Hadoop 2.x mitigates this via **High-Availability NameNode (stand-by)** using ZooKeeper-based failover.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, every Part B question offers internal choice. Both alternatives carry equal marks. Sub-parts (a) and (b) are usually 7 marks each.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **HDFS architecture** in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the **read** and **write** operations including block replication and the acknowledgement pipeline.

**Model Answer:**

**HDFS Architecture (3 marks):**
HDFS follows a **master/slave** topology.
- **NameNode (Master):** Stores metadata in RAM — file-to-block mapping, block-to-DataNode mapping, file hierarchy, replication factor. Persists state to disk via `fsimage` and `edit logs`.
- **DataNodes (Slaves):** Commodity nodes that store actual data blocks (default 128 MB each) and serve client read/write requests. They send **heartbeats** to the NameNode every 3 seconds; if 10 minutes (10× heartbeat) pass without one, the DataNode is declared dead.
- **Secondary NameNode:** Periodically merges `fsimage` + `edit logs` to prevent edit-log overflow (called **checkpointing**). It is *not* a hot standby.

**HDFS Write Operation (2 marks):**
1. Client requests write permission → NameNode checks namespace and returns a list of DataNodes.
2. Client pushes the first block to the nearest DataNode.
3. The block is replicated along a **pipeline** to the next two DataNodes.
4. ACKs propagate back to the client, who then writes the next block.

**HDFS Read Operation (2 marks):**
1. Client requests the file → NameNode returns block locations.
2. Client connects to the *nearest* DataNode for each block and reads sequentially.
3. On failure, the client transparently reads from a replica.

*(Diagram: see SECTION 4.2 and 4.3.)*

**(b) [7 marks]** A file of size **2.5 GB** is uploaded into HDFS. The default block size is **128 MB** and the replication factor is **3**. Calculate: (i) the number of blocks created, (ii) the total cluster storage consumed, and (iii) the storage overhead.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Convert units**
$$S = 2.5 \text{ GB} = 2.5 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 2560 \text{ MB}$$
$$B = 128 \text{ MB}, \quad R = 3$$

**Step 2 — Number of blocks**
$$N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{2560}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 20 \rceil = 20 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 3 — Total storage consumed**
$$Storage_{total} = 20 \times 128 \times 3 = 7680 \text{ MB} = 7.5 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 4 — Storage overhead**
$$Overhead = \dfrac{7680 - 2560}{2560} \times 100\% = \dfrac{5120}{2560} \times 100\% = 200\%$$

**Final Answers:** (i) **20 blocks**, (ii) **7.5 GB**, (iii) **200%**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **YARN architecture**. Explain the roles of **ResourceManager**, **NodeManager**, and **ApplicationMaster**. Why is YARN called the "operating system of Hadoop"?

**Model Answer:**

**YARN Architecture (3 marks):**
YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) is the cluster resource management layer introduced in Hadoop 2.x. It splits the responsibilities of the old JobTracker into three daemons:
- **ResourceManager (RM):** Global master; arbitrates cluster resources and accepts job submissions.
- **NodeManager (NM):** Per-node slave; launches and monitors containers, reports resource usage.
- **ApplicationMaster (AM):** Per-application; negotiates resources with the RM and coordinates task execution with the NMs.
- **Container:** Abstract allocation unit bundling CPU cores + RAM in MB.

**Roles in detail (2 marks):**
- RM maintains a global scheduler and resource tracker.
- NM registers with the RM, sends heartbeats, and manages container lifecycles on its node.
- AM requests containers from the RM (e.g., one AM container + N task containers) and tells the NMs to launch tasks inside them.

**Why "Operating System of Hadoop" (2 marks):**
YARN *decouples* resource management from data processing, allowing multiple applications (MapReduce, Spark, Tez, Storm) to run *simultaneously* on the same cluster. Just as an OS multiplexes CPU/RAM among many processes, YARN multiplexes cluster resources among many Big Data applications — hence the analogy.

*(Diagram: see SECTION 4.4.)*

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **MapReduce programming model** with a suitable example (word count). List the various phases of MapReduce execution and describe the role of the **Combiner** and **Partitioner**.

**Model Answer:**

**MapReduce Model (2 marks):**
MapReduce is a parallel programming model with two user-defined functions:
$$\text{map}: (k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_2, v_2)$$
$$\text{reduce}: (k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_3, v_3)$$
The framework handles all the *plumbing* — distribution, fault tolerance, shuffling.

**Word-Count Example (2 marks):**
- Input: lines of text.
- Mapper emits `(word, 1)` for every word.
- Reducer sums the 1s per unique word.
*(Full numerical example in SECTION 3.2.)*

**Phases of Execution (2 marks):**
1. **InputFormat / Splitting** — file is divided into InputSplits.
2. **Mapping** — each split processed in parallel.
3. **Combiner (Optional, Local Reduce)** — performs mini-reduce on the *same node* as the mapper to reduce data shuffled over the network.
4. **Shuffle & Sort** — groups intermediate key-value pairs by key.
5. **Partitioning** — assigns keys to specific reducers via a hash function: $P(k) = k.hashCode() \mod R$.
6. **Reducing** — final aggregation per key.
7. **OutputFormat / Write** — final output written to HDFS.

**Role of Combiner vs Partitioner (1 mark):**
- **Combiner** = *optimization*; cuts network traffic.
- **Partitioner** = *distribution*; controls which reducer receives which key range.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions**
> 
> 1. **Confusing NameNode with DataNode** — Students often write "NameNode stores data". It does *not*. It only stores *metadata* (2-mark loss).
> 2. **Forgetting replication in storage calculation** — computing $N \times 128$ MB *without* multiplying by $R=3$ is the #1 numerical mistake (3-mark loss).
> 3. **Using `ceil` instead of `floor` for block count** — the last block is *padded* up to 128 MB, never split. Use **ceiling** (2-mark loss).
> 4. **Calling Secondary NameNode a "backup"** — it is *not* a hot standby; it is a checkpointing helper. KTU explicitly tests this (1–2 marks).
> 5. **YARN diagram without ApplicationMaster** — the AM is the most important *per-application* component; omitting it costs 2 marks.
> 6. **Hadoop version confusion** — block size in Hadoop 1.x was **64 MB**; in Hadoop 2.x/3.x it is **128 MB**. Stating the wrong default is a common slip.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024**

- ☐ **Hadoop = HDFS + YARN + MapReduce + (Hive, Pig, HBase, Sqoop, Flume, Oozie, Zookeeper).**
- ☐ **Default HDFS block size = 128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x/3.x); 64 MB in Hadoop 1.x.
- ☐ **Default replication factor R = 3.**
- ☐ **Storage formula:** $N_{blocks} = \lceil S / B \rceil$; $Storage_{total} = N_{blocks} \times B \times R$.
- ☐ **NameNode = metadata only** (master); **DataNode = data blocks** (slave).
- ☐ **Secondary NameNode ≠ backup** — it is a *checkpointing helper*.
- ☐ **Heartbeat interval = 3 s**; DataNode declared dead after **10 minutes** of silence.
- ☐ **YARN daemons:** ResourceManager (global), NodeManager (per-node), ApplicationMaster (per-app), Container (resource unit).
- ☐ **MapReduce phases:** InputSplit → Map → Combiner → Shuffle & Sort → Partition → Reduce → Output.
- ☐ **Combiner = local reducer** (optimization); **Partitioner = key-to-reducer router** (default: hash mod R).
- ☐ **Hive** translates HQL → MapReduce/Spark/Tez; **Pig** translates Pig Latin → MapReduce.
- ☐ **HBase** is a NoSQL columnar store on HDFS; **Sqoop** = RDBMS ↔ HDFS; **Flume** = streaming logs → HDFS.
- ☐ **Zookeeper** = distributed coordination service; **Oozie** = workflow scheduler.
- ☐ **Replication overhead with R=3 is exactly 200%.**
- ☐ **MapReduce data-locality gives ~10× speedup** vs. cross-rack reads.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Core Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Core Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Core Components** of the Apache Hadoop ecosystem constitute the foundational software layer responsible for **distributed storage**, **parallel computation**, **resource orchestration**, and **common utilities** required to process massive datasets across commodity hardware clusters. In the KTU 2024 scheme for **PBADT504 – Big Data Analytics**, these core components are formally defined as the **Hadoop Core**, which consists of four primary modules:

1. **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** – Distributed, fault-tolerant storage.
2. **MapReduce** – Distributed parallel data processing programming model.
3. **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** – Cluster resource management and job scheduling.
4. **Hadoop Common (Hadoop Core Utilities)** – Libraries and utilities supporting the other modules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The 2024 scheme explicitly requires students to *"illustrate the Hadoop architecture, explain the working of HDFS, MapReduce, and YARN, and demonstrate core component interaction."* Experiment 2 in the lab manual is dedicated to setting up and verifying these components.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – The Warehouse Assembly Line**
> Imagine a massive national courier company:
> - **HDFS** = the **warehouses** scattered across cities where every parcel (data block) is stored in triplicate for safety.
> - **MapReduce** = the **workers** who first sort parcels by city (Map) and then deliver them to specific addresses (Reduce).
> - **YARN** = the **shift supervisor** who decides which worker gets which task and on which warehouse floor.
> - **Hadoop Common** = the **standard toolbox, forklifts, and barcode scanners** used by everyone.

Without any one of these, the system collapses. This mirrors exactly how Hadoop operates: storage (HDFS), compute (MapReduce), coordination (YARN), and utilities (Common) form an indivisible quartet.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Engineering Constants)

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Default Block Size** | **128 MB** (HDFS) | Unit of read/write granularity |
| **Replication Factor** | **3** (default) | Fault tolerance copies |
| **Heartbeat Interval** | **3 seconds** | Node-to-ResourceManager liveness |
| **Safe Mode Threshold** | **0.999** | Minimum % of blocks reported before exiting safe mode |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Component 1 — HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)

**Definition:** A distributed, scalable, and portable file system written in Java, designed to store very large files across machines in a large cluster.

**Architecture – Master/Slave Model:**
- **NameNode (Master):** Maintains metadata (file names, permissions, block locations, replication factor). Stores metadata in memory + `fsimage` and `edits` log files.
- **DataNodes (Slaves):** Store actual data blocks. Send heartbeats to NameNode every 3 seconds.

**Operational Logic – Write Pipeline (Step-by-Step):**
1. Client contacts NameNode requesting to write a file.
2. NameNode returns a list of DataNodes (typically 3) forming a **replication pipeline**.
3. Client splits the file into **128 MB** blocks (last block may be smaller).
4. Data is pushed in packets; each DataNode stores its copy and forwards to the next.
5. Acknowledgement (ACK) propagates back; on failure, the pipeline is reconstructed and the under-replicated block is re-replicated.

**Operational Logic – Read Path:**
1. Client requests a file read.
2. NameNode returns the list of DataNodes containing the closest replicas.
3. Client reads directly from the nearest DataNode.
4. If a block is corrupt, NameNode instructs the client to read from another replica and triggers re-replication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** HDFS follows a **Write Once, Read Many (WORM)** model. Files cannot be modified in place — they can only be appended or deleted and re-created.

### 2.2 Component 2 — MapReduce

**Definition:** A programming model for processing and generating large data sets with a parallel, distributed algorithm on a cluster.

**Two Core Functions:**
- **Map Function:** `(key1, value1) → list[(key2, value2)]` — input transformation and filtering.
- **Reduce Function:** `(key2, list[value2]) → list[(key3, value3)]` — aggregation and summarization.

**Execution Flow (4 Stages):**
1. **Input Split** — File is logically divided; one Mapper per split.
2. **Map Phase** — User-defined `map()` runs on each `(k1, v1)` pair.
3. **Shuffle & Sort Phase** — Framework groups all values by `key2` across all mappers.
4. **Reduce Phase** — User-defined `reduce()` aggregates each group.

### 2.3 Component 3 — YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)

**Definition:** The cluster resource management layer introduced in Hadoop 2.x that decouples resource management from data processing logic.

**Key Daemons:**
- **ResourceManager (RM):** Global arbitrator of resources. Contains the **Scheduler** (capacity/fair) and **ApplicationsManager (AsM)**.
- **NodeManager (NM):** Per-node agent managing containers, monitoring resource usage (CPU, memory, disk, network).
- **ApplicationMaster (AM):** Per-application; negotiates resources with RM and works with NMs to execute tasks.
- **Container:** Abstraction of CPU + memory allocated to a task.

### 2.4 Component 4 — Hadoop Common

**Definition:** The set of shared utilities, libraries, and Java ARchives (JARs) used by HDFS, MapReduce, and YARN. Includes:
- File system APIs
- RPC libraries
- Serialization/deserialization (Avro, Writable)
- Configuration management
- Native IO libraries

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of Blocks | $\vert B \vert = \lceil F / S \rceil$ | blocks | $F$ = file size, $S$ = block size (**128 MB**) |
| Total Storage Used | $T = \vert B \vert \times R \times S$ | MB / GB | $R$ = replication factor (**3**) |
| Storage Overhead | $O = (R - 1) \times 100\%$ | % | For $R=3$, overhead = **200%** |
| Mapper Count | $\vert M \vert = \text{number of InputSplits}$ | mappers | 1 mapper per split by default |
| Reducer Count | $\vert K \vert = \text{partitions}$ | reducers | Configurable via `mapreduce.job.reduces` |
| Data Locality Distance | $d \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ | tiers | 0=node-local, 1=rack-local, 2=off-rack |
| Container Memory | $C_m = \text{mapreduce.map.memory.mb}$ | MB | Default: **1024 MB** |
| Heartbeat Period | $T_h = 3$ | seconds | NM → RM liveness signal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** These four components power enterprises like **Yahoo, Facebook (Meta), Netflix, and LinkedIn** for log analytics, recommendation engines, search indexing, and ETL pipelines — making Hadoop the de facto standard for batch Big Data processing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — HDFS Block Count and Storage Calculation

**Problem:** A log file of size **F = 750 MB** is ingested into HDFS with default block size **S = 128 MB** and replication factor **R = 3**. Calculate (a) the number of blocks, (b) the total physical storage used, and (c) the storage overhead percentage.

**Step 1 — Number of Blocks $\vert B \vert$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert B \vert &= \left\lceil \frac{F}{S} \right\rceil \\[4pt]
&= \left\lceil \frac{750 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil \\[4pt]
&= \left\lceil 5.859375 \right\rceil \\[4pt]
&= 6 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The ceiling function rounds up because the last partial block still requires its own storage slot. Out of 6 blocks, 5 are full (128 MB) and 1 is partial (750 − 5×128 = 110 MB).

**Step 2 — Total Physical Storage $T$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \vert B \vert \times R \times S_{\text{logical}} \\[4pt]
\text{Logical data size} &= 750 \text{ MB} \\[4pt]
T &= 750 \text{ MB} \times R \\[4pt]
&= 750 \times 3 \\[4pt]
&= 2250 \text{ MB} \;(\approx 2.197 \text{ GB})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Storage Overhead $O$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O &= (R - 1) \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= (3 - 1) \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 200\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> This means for every **1 GB** of logical data, HDFS consumes **3 GB** of physical storage — a deliberate trade-off favoring **availability** and **fault tolerance** over storage efficiency.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — MapReduce Data Locality Tier Selection

**Problem:** A mapper is scheduled on Node N1. The required block resides on Node N2. Both N1 and N2 are on Rack R1. Compute the locality tier and the network hops.

**Logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If block on same node} &\Rightarrow d = 0 \text{ (node-local)} \\
\text{Else if block on same rack} &\Rightarrow d = 1 \text{ (rack-local)} \\
\text{Else} &\Rightarrow d = 2 \text{ (off-rack)} \\[6pt]
\therefore \text{Block on different node, same rack} &\Rightarrow d = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> **KTU Insight:** The YARN scheduler **prefers node-local** ($d=0$) assignments to minimize network traffic, falling back to rack-local ($d=1$) only if no node-local slot is free.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component Responsibility Mapping

| Function | HDFS | MapReduce | YARN | Hadoop Common |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stores data | ✅ (persistent) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Runs user logic | ❌ | ✅ (Map/Reduce) | ❌ | ❌ |
| Manages memory/CPU | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (Containers) | ❌ |
| Maintains metadata | ✅ (NameNode) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Provides RPC | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Fault tolerance | ✅ (replication) | ✅ (task re-run) | ✅ (AM restart) | ❌ |
| Heartbeat signaling | ✅ (DN→NN) | ❌ | ✅ (NM→RM) | ❌ |
| Splits input | ✅ (InputSplit) | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ (libraries) |

> This matrix is a **favourite KTU Part-A question pattern** — students must know "who does what" at a glance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HDFS Master–Slave Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client[Client Application] -->|RPC metadata request| namenode[NameNode Master]
    namenode -->|Block locations| client
    client -->|Direct read or write| dn1[DataNode 1]
    client -->|Direct read or write| dn2[DataNode 2]
    client -->|Direct read or write| dn3[DataNode 3]
    dn1 -->|Heartbeat 3s + Block report| namenode
    dn2 -->|Heartbeat 3s + Block report| namenode
    dn3 -->|Heartbeat 3s + Block report| namenode
    namenode -.->|fsimage edits log| fsimage[Disk Metadata Files]
```

### 4.2 YARN Resource Management Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    app[Client Application] -->|Submit| rm[ResourceManager]
    rm -->|Allocate Container| am[ApplicationMaster]
    am -->|Request Resources| rm
    rm -->|Grant Container| am
    am -->|Launch Task| nm1[NodeManager 1]
    am -->|Launch Task| nm2[NodeManager 2]
    nm1 -->|Container Status| am
    nm2 -->|Container Status| am
    am -->|Progress and Status| rm
    am -->|Final Result| app
```

### 4.3 MapReduce Execution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input[HDFS Input File] --> split[InputSplit 128MB]
    split --> m1[Mapper 1]
    split --> m2[Mapper 2]
    split --> m3[Mapper 3]
    m1 -->|k2 v2| shuf[Shuffle and Sort]
    m2 -->|k2 v2| shuf
    m3 -->|k2 v2| shuf
    shuf --> r1[Reducer 1]
    shuf --> r2[Reducer 2]
    r1 --> out1[Output HDFS Part 1]
    r2 --> out2[Output HDFS Part 2]
```

### 4.4 Core Component Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph storage[HDFS Layer]
        nn[NameNode]
        dn1A[DataNode 1]
        dn2A[DataNode 2]
    end
    subgraph compute[MapReduce Engine]
        mapper[Map Task JVM]
        reducer[Reduce Task JVM]
    end
    subgraph orchestration[YARN Layer]
        rmA[ResourceManager]
        amA[ApplicationMaster]
        nmA[NodeManager]
    end
    subgraph common[Hadoop Common]
        rpc[RPC Library]
        io[IO and Serialization]
    end
    nn -->|metadata| mapper
    dn1A -->|read block| mapper
    mapper -->|intermediate| reducer
    dn2A -->|write block| reducer
    rmA -->|allocates| amA
    amA -->|manages| mapper
    amA -->|manages| reducer
    nmA -->|monitors| mapper
    nmA -->|monitors| reducer
    rpc --> nn
    rpc --> rmA
    io --> mapper
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*List the four core components of Apache Hadoop and state the primary function of each in one line.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Component | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **HDFS** | Distributed, fault-tolerant storage of large files via NameNode + DataNodes. |
| 2 | **MapReduce** | Parallel data processing programming model using Map and Reduce phases. |
| 3 | **YARN** | Cluster resource management and job scheduling via ResourceManager and NodeManager. |
| 4 | **Hadoop Common** | Shared utilities, libraries, and JARs that support the other three modules. |

*(1 mark for enumeration, 2 marks for the table of functions.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*What is the default HDFS block size and replication factor? Why is the replication factor set to 3 by default?*

**Model Answer:**

- **Default Block Size:** **128 MB** *(0.5 mark)*
- **Default Replication Factor:** **3** *(0.5 mark)*
- **Justification for R = 3:** *(2 marks)*
  - **Fault tolerance** — tolerates loss of up to 2 simultaneous DataNode failures.
  - **Read parallelism** — allows a client to choose the nearest or least-loaded replica.
  - **Balance of cost vs. availability** — 200% storage overhead is acceptable for the reliability gained.
  - One replica on the local node, one on a different node of the same rack, and one on a different rack — ensures both node-level and rack-level resilience.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of HDFS with a neat diagram. Describe the role of the NameNode and DataNode in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A file of size **2.4 GB** is stored in HDFS. Calculate the number of blocks created, the total physical storage consumed with replication factor **3**, and the storage overhead percentage. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

HDFS follows a **master–slave architecture**:

- **NameNode (Master):** Maintains the entire file system namespace and metadata in memory. It stores:
  - File names, directory tree, permissions, and timestamps.
  - Mapping of file → list of blocks.
  - Mapping of block → DataNode locations.
  - Persisted to disk via `fsimage` (snapshot) and `edits` (transaction log).

- **DataNodes (Slaves):** Store and retrieve blocks as instructed. Periodically (every **3 seconds**) send a **heartbeat** and **block report** to the NameNode. On receiving a write request, they form a **replication pipeline**.

- **Communication:** Client first contacts NameNode for metadata, then communicates **directly** with DataNodes for data transfer — this prevents the NameNode from becoming a bottleneck.

*(Architecture 2 marks, NameNode 2 marks, DataNode 2 marks, Client communication 1 mark = 7 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students wrongly say "NameNode stores the data" — **it stores only metadata**. The actual data resides on DataNodes. Losing this distinction can cost 1–2 marks.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{File size } F &= 2.4 \text{ GB} = 2.4 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 2457.6 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Block size } S &= 128 \text{ MB} \\[6pt]
\vert B \vert &= \left\lceil \frac{2457.6}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 19.2 \right\rceil = 20 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for the formula and substitution, 1 mark for final block count.)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= F \times R = 2457.6 \times 3 = 7372.8 \text{ MB} \approx 7.2 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for total storage calculation.)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
O &= (R - 1) \times 100\% = 200\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for overhead formula and final percentage.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Converting GB → MB incorrectly (using 1000 instead of 1024) loses 1 mark. KTU 2024 expects **1 GB = 1024 MB** unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the working of YARN. Explain the functions of ResourceManager, NodeManager, ApplicationMaster, and Container. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the MapReduce programming model with a Word Count example. Show how the input is split, mapped, shuffled, sorted, and reduced. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) separates **resource management** from **application logic**, enabling multiple data processing engines (MapReduce, Spark, Tez) to share a cluster.

**Workflow:**
1. Client submits an application to the **ResourceManager (RM)**.
2. RM allocates a **Container** and launches an **ApplicationMaster (AM)** on a NodeManager.
3. The AM negotiates further containers with the RM based on application needs.
4. AM coordinates with **NodeManagers (NMs)** to launch **tasks** in allocated containers.
5. Tasks report progress to AM, which in turn reports to RM.
6. On completion, AM releases containers and deregisters from RM.

**Roles:**

| Daemon | Function |
|---|---|
| **ResourceManager** | Global arbiter — Scheduler (allocates resources) + ApplicationsManager (accepts submissions, launches AMs). |
| **NodeManager** | Per-node agent — manages containers, monitors CPU/memory/disk, sends heartbeats. |
| **ApplicationMaster** | Per-application — negotiates resources, monitors task execution, handles task failures. |
| **Container** | Resource bundle (RAM + CPU cores) — the execution unit for tasks. |

*(Workflow 3 marks, Roles table 4 marks = 7 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Confusing **ApplicationMaster** with **ApplicationManager**. The AM is *per application*; the AppsManager is a *component of the ResourceManager*.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Consider input text: `"big data is big and data is huge"`.

**Map Phase — Input Splits & Mapping:**

Split 1: `"big data is"` → Mapper 1 → emits (`big`, 1), (`data`, 1), (`is`, 1)
Split 2: `"big and data"` → Mapper 2 → emits (`big`, 1), (`and`, 1), (`data`, 1)
Split 3: `"is huge"` → Mapper 3 → emits (`is`, 1), (`huge`, 1)

**Shuffle & Sort (Framework-managed):**

All values for each key are grouped:
- `and` → [1]
- `big` → [1, 1]
- `data` → [1, 1]
- `huge` → [1]
- `is` → [1, 1]

**Reduce Phase — Aggregation:**

- `and` → 1
- `big` → 2
- `data` → 2
- `huge` → 1
- `is` → 2

These are written to HDFS as the final output file.

*(Map explanation 3 marks, Shuffle-Sort 2 marks, Reduce + output 2 marks = 7 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention that **Shuffle & Sort is framework-managed**, not user-coded. Also, the output of Map is **NOT immediately** sent to Reduce — it is first partitioned, sorted, and possibly merged.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Core Components**

- ✅ Hadoop Core = **HDFS + MapReduce + YARN + Hadoop Common**.
- ✅ HDFS block size default = **128 MB**; replication = **3**; storage overhead = **200%**.
- ✅ NameNode = metadata (in memory + fsimage/edits); DataNode = actual blocks + heartbeats every **3 s**.
- ✅ HDFS = **Write Once, Read Many (WORM)**; no in-place modification.
- ✅ MapReduce = **Map → Shuffle & Sort → Reduce** (Shuffle is framework-managed).
- ✅ Number of mappers = number of **InputSplits**; number of reducers = configured partitions.
- ✅ YARN components = **ResourceManager, NodeManager, ApplicationMaster, Container**.
- ✅ Container = abstraction of **CPU + memory** for a task.
- ✅ Hadoop Common = shared **RPC, IO, serialization** utilities.
- ✅ Data locality tiers: **0 = node-local, 1 = rack-local, 2 = off-rack** (lower = better).
- ✅ HDFS architecture is **master–slave**; YARN is also **master–slave** with **per-application AM**.
- ✅ Default mapper memory = **1024 MB**; default heartbeat = **3 seconds**.
- ✅ Master–worker pattern is used **twice** — once for storage, once for compute/orchestration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HDFS- Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** HDFS- Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** is a **distributed, scalable, fault-tolerant, and portable file system** written in Java, designed to store *very large* datasets reliably across multiple commodity machines in a Hadoop cluster. It follows the **master/slave (master/worker) architecture**, adheres to the **Write Once, Read Many (WORM)** access model, and implements the **Google File System (GFS)** paper published by Ghemawat, Gobioff, and Leung.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBADT504)**, HDFS is the *primary storage layer* of the Apache Hadoop framework and provides:
- **Streaming access** to filesystem data (high throughput rather than low latency).
- **Horizontal scalability** by simply adding more commodity nodes.
- **Fault tolerance** through block-level replication and self-healing re-replication.

The two principal physical metadata files maintained by the master are:
- **`fsimage`** — A serialized snapshot of the filesystem namespace at a point in time.
- **`edits` (edit log)** — An append-only log of every modification since the last `fsimage` was taken.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Library" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: HDFS as a Nationwide Library System**
>
> Imagine a **nationwide library** that must store **millions of books** (very large files). Instead of one giant building, the government builds **500 small branch libraries** (DataNodes) across the country, each on a different **street block (rack)**. A **chief librarian (NameNode)** sits in the capital and maintains a small register listing *which book* is in *which branch* (metadata). Each book is **photocopied three times (replication factor = 3)** and stored in three different branches. When a **reader (Client)** wants a book, they call the chief librarian, get the branch locations, and walk into the **nearest branch** to read. The chief librarian periodically calls each branch to ask *"Are you still there?"* — that is the **heartbeat**. If a branch is destroyed in a flood, the chief librarian notices the missing heartbeat and asks the remaining branches to **re-photocopy** the lost books from their copies. A **junior librarian (Secondary NameNode)** comes in once an hour to help the chief librarian update the register.

This single analogy encapsulates the **five pillars of HDFS**:
| Library Term | HDFS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Chief Librarian | **NameNode** (Master) |
| Junior Librarian | **Secondary NameNode** (Checkpointer) |
| Branch Library | **DataNode** (Worker/Slave) |
| Photocopied Book | **Replicated Block** |
| Street Block | **Rack** |

## 1.3 The Five Defining Constants of HDFS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU examiner expects the following **standard defaults** to be memorized verbatim (Hadoop 2.x and 3.x):
> - **Default Block Size = 128 MB** (was 64 MB in Hadoop 1.x).
> - **Default Replication Factor = 3**.
> - **Heartbeat Interval = 3 seconds** (DataNode → NameNode).
> - **Block Report Interval = 6 hours** (configurable via `dfs.blockreport.intervalMsec`).
> - **Packet Size = 64 KB** (the unit of streaming transfer inside a pipeline).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components — The Master/Worker Hierarchy

HDFS cluster architecture is a strict **two-tier master/worker design** with an auxiliary checkpointer.

### 2.1.1 NameNode (The Master / Namenode Daemon)
- **Single point of coordination** for the entire cluster.
- Stores **two categories** of metadata in RAM:
  1. **Namespace image** — the entire directory and file tree.
  2. **File-to-block mapping** — which blocks make up each file.
  3. **Block-to-DataNode mapping** — where each replica currently lives.
- Persists metadata to disk as **`fsimage`** + **`edits`**.
- **Never stores user data**, only metadata.
- Receives **heartbeats (every 3 s)** from every DataNode.
- **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** in HDFS v1; solved in v2 via **NameNode HA (High Availability)** with a **Standby NameNode** + **ZooKeeper-based failover** + **Shared NFS/QJM edits directory**.

### 2.1.2 Secondary NameNode (The Checkpointer)
- **NOT a backup/hot-standby** NameNode — this is the *most common* KTU trap.
- Periodically performs the **checkpoint operation**:
  1. Asks the active NameNode to **roll the edits log** (create `edits.new`).
  2. Downloads the latest **`fsimage`** and the rolled **`edits`** to itself.
  3. Merges them into a new **`fsimage.checkpoint`**.
  4. Uploads the new fsimage back to the NameNode.
- The trigger condition is: **whichever happens first** — `dfs.namenode.checkpoint.period` (default **3600 s = 1 hour**) OR `dfs.namenode.checkpoint.txns` (default **1,000,000 transactions**).

### 2.1.3 DataNode (The Worker / Datanode Daemon)
- Stores the **actual data blocks** on its local disk.
- On startup, performs a **handshake** with the NameNode, registers itself, and then enters the main loop:
  - Sends **heartbeats** every 3 s.
  - Sends **block reports** listing all blocks stored on that node.
  - Serves **read/write** requests from clients.
- Maintains **two files per block** on disk: the data itself (`blk_<id>`) and a **checksum file** (`blk_<id>_.meta`) for corruption detection.

### 2.1.4 Client
- The application program (MapReduce job, Spark, HBase, or `hdfs dfs` shell).
- Interacts with NameNode only for **metadata** operations.
- Talks **directly to DataNodes** for all **actual data transfer** — this minimizes NameNode load.

## 2.2 Block Abstraction — The Foundation of HDFS

A *block* is the **smallest unit of read/write** in HDFS. Unlike POSIX file systems, files in HDFS are split into fixed-size blocks, each stored as an independent file on a DataNode.

- **Default Block Size (Hadoop 2.x+):** 128 MB.
- **Minimum Block Size (configurable):** 1 byte (theoretical); `dfs.blocksize` is the parameter.
- A file of 1 byte still consumes **one full block** on each replica (wastage for tiny files).
- **Last block of a file** may be smaller than the block size (no padding).

## 2.3 Rack Awareness & Replica Placement Policy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rack Awareness** = NameNode's intelligence to know the **rack-id** of every DataNode (configured via `net.topology.script.file.name` or core-site.xml topology mapping). It is used to balance **reliability** (loss of an entire rack) against **network bandwidth** (cross-rack switches are slower).

The **default HDFS replica placement policy** (write pipeline of length 3):

| Replica # | Placement Rule | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Replica 1** | On the **local node** of the writer (or a random DataNode on the **same rack**) | Lowest write latency |
| **Replica 2** | On a **different rack**, random DataNode | Survives single-rack failure |
| **Replica 3** | On the **same different rack** as Replica 2, different node | Survives rack failure + adds local-rack read locality |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **every formula** that can be asked in the ESE for HDFS architecture. Memorize the units and the ceiling function.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Standard Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of blocks per file | $N_{blocks} \;=\; \lceil \dfrac{F}{B} \rceil$ | dimensionless | $F$ = file size, $B$ = block size |
| 2 | Logical storage per file | $S_{logical} \;=\; N_{blocks} \times B$ | MB or GB | Includes padding of the last block |
| 3 | Physical storage per file | $S_{physical} \;=\; N_{blocks} \times B \times R$ | MB or GB | $R$ = replication factor |
| 4 | Replication overhead | $\eta \;=\; \dfrac{S_{physical}}{F} \;=\; \dfrac{\lceil F/B \rceil \times B \times R}{F}$ | dimensionless | Approaches $R$ as $F \gg B$ |
| 5 | NameNode RAM (rough) | $M_{NN} \;\approx\; 150 \cdot N_{blocks} \;+\; 50 \cdot N_{files} \;+\; 250 \cdot N_{DN}$ | bytes | $N_{DN}$ = number of DataNodes |
| 6 | Cluster raw capacity | $C \;=\; N_{DN} \times D_{perNode}$ | TB or PB | $D$ = usable disk per node |
| 7 | Block transfer time | $t_{xfer} \;=\; \dfrac{B}{T_{disk} \times N_{par}}$ | seconds | $T_{disk}$ = per-disk throughput, $N_{par}$ = parallel streams |
| 8 | Pipeline write throughput | $T_{pipe} \;=\; \min(T_{DN1},\, T_{DN2},\, \dots,\, T_{DNn})$ | MB/s | Bottleneck = slowest replica in chain |
| 9 | Read throughput (closest replica) | $T_{read} \;=\; \dfrac{B}{t_{seek} \;+\; t_{xfer}}$ | MB/s | $t_{seek}$ = disk seek + DFS lookup |
| 10 | Re-replication trigger delay | $t_{repair} \;\leq\; 10 \cdot t_{heartbeat}$ | seconds | Default $\approx 30$ s with 3-s heartbeat |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** The absolute-value sign `|` is rendered using `\vert` in the table above. In free-standing equations below, the same symbol is written as `\mid` or `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Yahoo!'s original HDFS** deployment scaled to **40,000+ nodes** storing **> 100 PB** of data.
- **Facebook's HDFS** historically stored the entire social graph in a single HDFS namespace (>300 PB).
- Used as the **storage substrate** for **MapReduce, Apache Spark, Apache Hive, Apache HBase, Presto, and Apache Flink** — making HDFS the *de-facto* data lake of the big-data era.
- The block abstraction enables **locality-aware computation**: MapReduce/Spark schedulers place compute tasks on the same node that holds the block, eliminating network transfer — this is the **"move code to data"** principle of Hadoop.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides **exhaustive, exam-ready numerical derivations** for the formulas introduced in §2.4. Each step is shown explicitly — **no shortcuts, no skips**, in line with KTU's step-marking scheme where partial marks are awarded for every transition.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Blocks for an Arbitrary File

**Problem (KTU-style):** A file of size **$F = 1450$ MB** is written to HDFS with default block size **$B = 128$ MB** and replication factor **$R = 3$**. Calculate the number of blocks and the total physical storage consumed.

### Step 1 — Identify the Governing Equation
From the Formula Sheet, the number of blocks is given by the **ceiling function**:

$$
N_{blocks} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Known Values
We substitute $F = 1450$ MB and $B = 128$ MB:

$$
N_{blocks} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{1450}{128} \right\rceil
$$

### Step 3 — Perform the Division (Decimal Form)
Compute the exact decimal quotient to determine the fractional part:

$$
\frac{1450}{128} \;=\; 11.328125
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Ceiling Operator
The ceiling operator $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ rounds **up** to the next integer:

$$
\left\lceil 11.328125 \right\rceil \;=\; 12 \text{ blocks}
$$

### Step 5 — Determine the Last-Block Size
The first 11 blocks are full ($11 \times 128 = 1408$ MB). The 12th block holds the remainder:

$$
B_{last} \;=\; F - (N_{blocks}-1) \times B \;=\; 1450 - 11 \times 128 \;=\; 1450 - 1408 \;=\; 42 \text{ MB}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Logical Storage
Logical storage includes the padding of the last block:

$$
S_{logical} \;=\; N_{blocks} \times B \;=\; 12 \times 128 \;=\; 1536 \text{ MB}
$$

### Step 7 — Compute the Physical Storage
Each of the 12 blocks is replicated 3 times:

$$
S_{physical} \;=\; S_{logical} \times R \;=\; 1536 \times 3 \;=\; 4608 \text{ MB}
$$

### Step 8 — Compute the Replication Overhead
Total wasted space (extra copies) is the physical minus the logical:

$$
\eta_{waste} \;=\; S_{physical} - F \;=\; 4608 - 1450 \;=\; 3158 \text{ MB}
$$

### Step 9 — Compute the Overhead Ratio
The "amplification factor" of the file (how much more space it consumes vs. its true size):

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{S_{physical}}{F} \;=\; \frac{4608}{1450} \;\approx\; 3.178
$$

> **Interpretation:** The overhead is slightly more than $3\times$ because the last block was padded from 42 MB to 128 MB. For a *very large* file (e.g., 1 TB), the ratio approaches exactly **$R = 3$**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — NameNode In-Memory Sizing

**Problem:** A cluster stores **$N_{files} = 10$ million** files (each 1 block) on **$N_{DN} = 100$** DataNodes. Estimate the **minimum NameNode JVM heap** required to hold all in-memory metadata.

### Step 1 — Identify the Formula
From the Formula Sheet, item #5:

$$
M_{NN} \;\approx\; 150 \cdot N_{blocks} \;+\; 50 \cdot N_{files} \;+\; 250 \cdot N_{DN}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Each Term
- Block term: $150 \times 10{,}000{,}000 = 1{,}500{,}000{,}000$ bytes
- File term: $50 \times 10{,}000{,}000 = 500{,}000{,}000$ bytes
- DataNode term: $250 \times 100 = 25{,}000$ bytes

### Step 3 — Sum the Terms

$$
M_{NN} \;\approx\; 1{,}500{,}000{,}000 + 500{,}000{,}000 + 25{,}000
$$

$$
M_{NN} \;\approx\; 2{,}000{,}025{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to GB
Divide by $1024^3$:

$$
M_{NN} \;\approx\; \frac{2{,}000{,}025{,}000}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} \;\approx\; 1.862 \text{ GB}
$$

### Step 5 — Add JVM Overhead
JVM requires overhead for garbage collection, native memory, and replication monitoring. A safe rule of thumb is **2× to 3× the heap**, so:

$$
M_{NN}^{RAM} \;\approx\; 2 \times 1.862 \;\approx\; 3.72 \text{ GB}
$$

> **Insight:** This is precisely why **HDFS Federation** (multiple independent NameNodes, each owning a subset of the namespace) was introduced in Hadoop 2.x — to break the single-NameNode memory bottleneck.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pipeline Write Throughput

**Problem:** A client writes a 1 GB file (8 blocks of 128 MB) to a 3-replica pipeline. The DataNodes have a per-disk write throughput of $T_{disk} = 100$ MB/s. Network switches support $T_{net} = 1$ Gbps. Calculate the **time to write** the file.

### Step 1 — Identify the Bottleneck
The pipeline write rate is governed by the **slowest** link in the chain (Formula Sheet #8):

$$
T_{pipe} \;=\; \min(T_{disk,1}, T_{disk,2}, T_{disk,3}, T_{net,1\rightarrow 2}, T_{net,2\rightarrow 3})
$$

### Step 2 — Convert Network Throughput to MB/s
$$
T_{net} \;=\; \frac{1 \text{ Gbps}}{8} \;=\; 125 \text{ MB/s}
$$

### Step 3 — Select the Bottleneck
The minimum is the disk throughput: $T_{pipe} = 100$ MB/s.

### Step 4 — Compute Total Time

$$
t_{write} \;=\; \frac{S_{logical}}{T_{pipe}} \;=\; \frac{1 \text{ GB} \times 1024 \text{ MB/GB}}{100 \text{ MB/s}}
$$

$$
t_{write} \;=\; \frac{1024}{100} \;\approx\; 10.24 \text{ seconds}
$$

> **Insight:** The 3-replica write does **not** take 3× the time of a single write, because the pipeline transfers data **once** through the network, while the **disk write** is parallelized across the three nodes.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Block-Size Sensitivity ("Small-File Problem")

**Problem:** A dataset consists of **$1 \text{ million}$ files** each of size **1 MB**. Compare the metadata load on the NameNode for $B = 64$ MB vs. $B = 128$ MB.

### Step 1 — Compute Number of Blocks for $B = 64$ MB
Since $1 \text{ MB} < 64 \text{ MB}$, every file occupies **one full block** (padded):

$$
N_{blocks}^{(64)} \;=\; 1 \text{ million blocks}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Number of Blocks for $B = 128$ MB
Identical: $1 \text{ MB} < 128 \text{ MB}$, so:

$$
N_{blocks}^{(128)} \;=\; 1 \text{ million blocks}
$$

### Step 3 — Block Count is Identical
In this case, the block-size parameter has **no effect** because each file is smaller than *both* block sizes. This illustrates the **Small-File Problem** of HDFS — many small files blow up the NameNode memory without utilizing block space efficiently.

### Step 4 — Useful Real Fix
The standard remedy is to **bundle** small files using `HAR` (Hadoop Archive), **SequenceFile**, or **Parquet** to amortize the per-file metadata cost.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Parameter | Hadoop 1.x | Hadoop 2.x | Hadoop 3.x |
|---|---|---|---|
| Default Block Size | **64 MB** | **128 MB** | **128 MB** |
| NameNode SPOF | Yes | Solved via HA | Solved via HA |
| Erasure Coding | No | No | **Yes** (HDFS-7285) |
| Federation | No | **Yes** | Yes (improved) |
| Max NameNode files | $\sim 10^7$ | $\sim 10^8$ | $\sim 10^8+$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Diagram — HDFS Cluster Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    clientApp["Client Application"]
    nameNode["NameNode - Master"]
    secNameNode["Secondary NameNode"]
    dn1["DataNode 1 - Worker"]
    dn2["DataNode 2 - Worker"]
    dn3["DataNode 3 - Worker"]
    dn4["DataNode 4 - Worker"]
    dn5["DataNode 5 - Worker"]
    dn6["DataNode 6 - Worker"]
    rack1["Rack 1"]
    rack2["Rack 2"]
    fsimage["fsimage File - Disk"]
    edits["edits Log - Disk"]

    clientApp -->|1 Metadata Request| nameNode
    nameNode -->|2 Block Locations| clientApp
    clientApp -->|3 Data Transfer Pipeline| dn1
    clientApp -.->|3 Data Transfer Pipeline| dn2
    clientApp -.->|3 Data Transfer Pipeline| dn3

    nameNode --- fsimage
    nameNode --- edits
    secNameNode -->|Checkpoint Merge| nameNode

    nameNode -->|Heartbeat 3s| dn1
    nameNode -->|Heartbeat 3s| dn2
    nameNode -->|Heartbeat 3s| dn3
    nameNode -->|Heartbeat 3s| dn4
    nameNode -->|Heartbeat 3s| dn5
    nameNode -->|Heartbeat 3s| dn6

    dn1 -.-> rack1
    dn2 -.-> rack1
    dn3 -.-> rack1
    dn4 -.-> rack2
    dn5 -.-> rack2
    dn6 -.-> rack2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows represent **active protocol messages**; dashed arrows represent **data-plane operations**. The NameNode sits at the *control-plane centre* and never carries user data.

---

## 4.2 HDFS File Write Operation — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant N as NameNode
    participant D1 as DataNode 1
    participant D2 as DataNode 2
    participant D3 as DataNode 3

    C->>N: RPC create file path
    N-->>C: HDFS File Handle OK
    C->>N: RPC addBlock file
    N-->>C: Pipeline Nodes D1 D2 D3

    loop For each 64KB packet
        C->>D1: Send Packet
        D1->>D2: Forward Packet
        D2->>D3: Forward Packet
        D3-->>C: ACK upstream
    end

    C->>N: RPC complete file
    N-->>C: Close Handle
```

> **Six-step write contract:** (1) `create()` → (2) `addBlock()` → (3) build pipeline → (4) stream packets → (5) pipeline ACK → (6) `complete()`.

---

## 4.3 HDFS File Read Operation — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant N as NameNode
    participant D1 as DataNode 1
    participant D2 as DataNode 2
    participant D3 as DataNode 3

    C->>N: RPC open file path
    N-->>C: Block Locations D1 D2 D3 for each block

    loop For each block
        C->>D1: Read first choice
        alt DataNode 1 alive and has block
            D1-->>C: Stream block data
        else DataNode 1 unreachable
            C->>D2: Read second choice
            D2-->>C: Stream block data
        end
    end

    C->>N: RPC close file
    N-->>C: Release handle
```

> **Read locality heuristic:** The client picks the *first* DataNode in the list, which the NameNode orders by **read locality** (same node > same rack > different rack) to minimize cross-rack bandwidth.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputA["Input File F bytes"]
    splitStep["Block Splitter - 128 MB chunks"]
    blkA["Block 1 - 128 MB"]
    blkB["Block 2 - 128 MB"]
    blkC["Block 3 - 42 MB"]
    repStep["Replicator - Factor 3"]
    r1A["Replica A on DataNode 1"]
    r1B["Replica A on DataNode 4"]
    r1C["Replica A on DataNode 5"]
    r2A["Replica B on DataNode 2"]
    r2B["Replica B on DataNode 5"]
    r2C["Replica B on DataNode 6"]
    r3A["Replica C on DataNode 3"]
    r3B["Replica C on DataNode 6"]
    r3C["Replica C on DataNode 4"]

    inputA --> splitStep
    splitStep --> blkA
    splitStep --> blkB
    splitStep --> blkC

    blkA --> repStep
    blkB --> repStep
    blkC --> repStep

    repStep --> r1A
    repStep --> r1B
    repStep --> r1C
    repStep --> r2A
    repStep --> r2B
    repStep --> r2C
    repStep --> r3A
    repStep --> r3B
    repStep --> r3C
```

> **Reading the block-flow:** Every logical block (left column) is *fanned out* into 3 physical replicas (right columns) by the NameNode-driven placement policy. Rack diversity is enforced by the topology script.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (3-mark vs 14-mark trap)**
> 1. Writing "Secondary NameNode is a **backup** NameNode" — **ZERO marks**. It is a *checkpointer*. The standby NameNode (HDFS HA) is the actual backup.
> 2. Confusing the *replication* with *erasure coding*. With $R=3$ you get **200% overhead**; with RS(6,3) erasure coding (Hadoop 3.x) you get only **50% overhead**.
> 3. Stating that the **NameNode stores the actual data** — **ZERO marks**. It stores *only metadata*.
> 4. Forgetting the **heartbeat** when asked about fault detection. Heartbeat (every 3 s) is the *only* way the NameNode detects a dead DataNode.
> 5. In 14-mark questions, missing the **diagram** — KTU deducts **2–3 marks** for a missing/neat diagram.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between NameNode and DataNode in HDFS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | NameNode | DataNode |
|---|---|---|
| Role | Master (control plane) | Worker (data plane) |
| Stores | **Metadata only** (fsimage, edits, in-memory maps) | **Actual data blocks** on local disk |
| Count per cluster | **1** (in HDFS v1) or 2 (HA) | **Many** (scales horizontally) |
| Heartbeat | **Receives** heartbeats | **Sends** heartbeats (every 3 s) |
| Failure impact | Cluster halts (SPOF in v1) | No impact; data is re-replicated |
| Memory | Holds entire namespace in RAM | Stores blocks on disk only |

*(1 mark for the role identification, 1 mark for the storage contents, 1 mark for the heartbeat direction & count.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is Rack Awareness? Why is it required in HDFS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Rack Awareness** is the NameNode's knowledge of the **physical rack location** (rack-id) of every DataNode, configured using the topology script (`net.topology.script.file.name`). **(1 mark)**
- It is **required** to make a *trade-off* between **reliability** and **network bandwidth**:
  1. **Reliability** — placing replicas on **different racks** ensures data survives the loss of an entire rack (switch failure, power outage). **(1 mark)**
  2. **Bandwidth** — placing one replica on the **same rack** as the writer allows a **local-rack read** with low latency, minimizing cross-rack switch traffic. **(1 mark)**
- Default replica policy: **1 replica on local node/rack + 1 on different rack + 1 on same different rack**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Module Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 14-Mark Format:** Two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Marks are split across concept (3) + diagram (2) + explanation/trade-off (2).

---

### 📘 Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a **neat diagram**, explain the **HDFS architecture**. Describe the responsibilities of NameNode, Secondary NameNode, and DataNode.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **HDFS data write process** with a sequence diagram. Mention the **replica placement policy** for a 3-replica pipeline.

---

#### 📗 Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Architecture Diagram:** (See §4.1 for the full Mermaid block diagram — reproduce the same in the answer script.)

**NameNode Responsibilities:**
1. Manages the **filesystem namespace** (directory tree, file names, permissions). **(1 mark)**
2. Maintains **in-memory metadata**: file-to-block map, block-to-DataNode map. **(1 mark)**
3. Receives **heartbeats** (every 3 s) and **block reports** (every 6 h) from every DataNode. **(1 mark)**
4. Decides **replica placement** using rack-awareness. **(1 mark)**

**Secondary NameNode Responsibilities:**
1. Performs **periodic checkpointing** of the namespace. **(1 mark)**
2. Merges `fsimage` + `edits` → new `fsimage.checkpoint`. **(1 mark)**
3. Triggered every **1 hour** or **1 million transactions**, whichever is earlier. **(1 mark)**
4. **NOT a hot-standby / backup** NameNode.

**DataNode Responsibilities:**
1. Stores **data blocks** on its local disks (one file per block + one `.meta` checksum file). **(1 mark)**
2. Sends **heartbeats** every 3 seconds. **(1 mark)**
3. Serves **read/write requests** from clients. **(1 mark)**
4. Initiates **re-replication** pipeline when instructed by NameNode.

---

#### 📗 Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Write Process — 6 Steps:** (See §4.2 for the sequence diagram.)

1. **Client → NameNode:** `create()` RPC; NameNode validates the path and creates an empty file entry in its namespace. **(1 mark)**
2. **NameNode → Client:** Returns a writable file handle. **(0.5 marks)**
3. **Client → NameNode:** `addBlock()` RPC; NameNode chooses **3 DataNodes** using the **replica placement policy** and returns them as a **pipeline**. **(1 mark)**
4. **Client → DataNode1 → DataNode2 → DataNode3:** Streams data in **64 KB packets** through the pipeline. Each DataNode writes to its local disk and forwards. **(2 marks)**
5. **Pipelined ACK:** The last DataNode sends an **ACK** upstream, which propagates back to the client. **(1 mark)**
6. **Client → NameNode:** `complete()` RPC finalizes the file. **(0.5 marks)**

**Replica Placement Policy (R = 3):**
- **Replica 1** → same node as the writer (or random node on same rack).
- **Replica 2** → different rack, random node.
- **Replica 3** → same different rack as Replica 2, different node.
- **Why?** Guarantees survival of *one full rack failure* while still allowing *local-rack reads*. **(1 mark)**

---

### 📙 Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]` *(Internal Choice)*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **fault-tolerance mechanisms** in HDFS. Discuss how **heartbeat-based failure detection** and **re-replication** work.

**(b) [7 marks]** A file of size **2.5 GB** is stored in HDFS with a block size of **256 MB** and replication factor **3**.
- (i) Calculate the **number of blocks**. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Calculate the **total physical storage** consumed. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) If one DataNode holding 2 blocks fails permanently, what is the **time to re-replicate** if the heartbeat interval is 3 seconds? **(2 marks)**

---

#### 📕 Model Solution for Question B(a)

HDFS achieves **fault tolerance** through *four* inter-locking mechanisms:

1. **Block Replication (R = 3 default):** Every block is stored on 3 different DataNodes, often spread across racks. Any single node/rack loss does not lose data. **(2 marks)**
2. **Heartbeat-Based Failure Detection:** Every DataNode sends a **heartbeat** to the NameNode every **3 seconds**. If the NameNode does **not** receive a heartbeat within **10 × heartbeat interval = 30 seconds** (configurable via `dfs.namenode.heartbeat.recheck-interval`), the DataNode is **declared dead**. **(2 marks)**
3. **Block Re-replication:** When the NameNode detects under-replication (replica count drops below the target), it **schedules** a new replica to be created on a healthy DataNode. The NameNode uses **priority queues** — replicas of *open files* or *blocks with count < 2* are replicated first. **(2 marks)**
4. **Checksums & Self-Healing:** Every block is stored with a **CRC-32 checksum** in a sidecar `.meta` file. On read, if the checksum mismatch occurs, the client transparently **switches to another replica** and reports the corrupt block to the NameNode, which **deletes** the bad copy and creates a new one. **(1 mark)**

---

#### 📕 Model Solution for Question B(b)

**(i) Number of blocks:**
$$
N_{blocks} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{2.5 \times 1024}{256} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{2560}{256} \right\rceil \;=\; 10 \text{ blocks}
$$
*(3 marks: 1 for the formula, 1 for the substitution, 1 for the ceiling evaluation.)*

**(ii) Total physical storage:**
$$
S_{physical} \;=\; N_{blocks} \times B \times R \;=\; 10 \times 256 \times 3 \;=\; 7680 \text{ MB} \;\approx\; 7.5 \text{ GB}
$$
*(2 marks: 1 for the formula, 1 for the numerical answer.)*

**(iii) Time to re-replicate:**
The NameNode waits for **$10 \times$ heartbeat** before declaring the node dead (default `dfs.namenode.heartbeat.recheck-interval` = 300,000 ms; *however* for a *single missed heartbeat threshold* the practical detection latency is **30 seconds** with the 3-s heartbeat):
$$
t_{detect} \;\approx\; 10 \times 3 \;=\; 30 \text{ seconds}
$$
After detection, the re-replication itself depends on network/disk speeds. The **minimum detection delay** is therefore **≈ 30 seconds**.
*(2 marks: 1 for identifying the $10\times$ rule, 1 for the final numerical answer.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this list. Memorize it. Write it on the last page of your answer booklet before the exam.**

- [ ] HDFS = **Hadoop Distributed File System**, Java-based, GFS-inspired, WORM model.
- [ ] **Master/Worker** architecture: NameNode (master) + DataNodes (workers).
- [ ] **Default block size = 128 MB**; default **replication = 3**; **heartbeat = 3 s**.
- [ ] NameNode stores **metadata only** (fsimage + edits + in-memory maps). It does **NOT** store user data.
- [ ] **Secondary NameNode = CHECKPOINTER, NOT a backup.** It rolls edits + merges with fsimage every **1 hour** or **1,000,000 transactions**.
- [ ] Client talks to **NameNode for metadata** but talks **directly to DataNodes for data**.
- [ ] **Rack awareness** places **Replica 1** local/same-rack, **Replica 2** on a different rack, **Replica 3** on the same different rack.
- [ ] Heartbeat *missing* for **10 × 3 s = 30 s** ⇒ DataNode is declared **dead**.
- [ ] Heartbeat (3 s) + Block Report (6 h) are the **two periodic messages** every DataNode sends.
- [ ] HDFS is optimized for **high throughput, batch reads, write-once workloads** — **NOT** for low-latency or random-writes.
- [ ] The **Small-File Problem** arises because each file consumes at least **one full block** of metadata, bloating NameNode RAM.
- [ ] **HDFS Federation** (Hadoop 2.x+) and **Erasure Coding** (Hadoop 3.x) are the two modern extensions that overcome scalability limits of v1.
- [ ] Fault-tolerance relies on **(a) replication, (b) heartbeat failure detection, (c) re-replication, (d) CRC-32 checksums + self-healing reads**.
- [ ] **Pipeline write is NOT 3× slower than a single write** — packets flow through the network only once; disk writes happen in parallel.
- [ ] Formulae to memorize: $N_{blocks}=\lceil F/B\rceil$, $S_{physical}=N_{blocks}\cdot B\cdot R$, $M_{NN}\approx 150\cdot N_{blocks}+50\cdot N_{files}$.
- [ ] **KTU 14-mark blueprint:** Diagram (2) + Component explanation (3) + Operation/process (3) + Trade-off/Policy (2) + Conclusion (1) + Neatness (3) = 14.

topics.title: Using HDFS Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Using HDFS Files

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of HDFS

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** is the primary, fault-tolerant storage layer of the Apache Hadoop ecosystem. It is designed to store very large files across multiple commodity machines in a cluster, following a master/slave (NameNode/DataNode) architecture, with a write-once-read-many (WORM) access model.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBADT504)**, "Using HDFS Files" refers to the practical operations performed by a Big Data practitioner to **interact with the Hadoop Distributed File System through its command-line interface (CLI)**, including file creation, uploading, downloading, inspection, modification, and deletion — all executed from a terminal using the `hdfs dfs` or `hadoop fs` command families.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Library Warehouse" Model**
> Imagine a massive national library warehouse that holds millions of books. Instead of storing all books in one giant building (which would be risky — a fire could destroy everything), the library splits each book into numbered chapters and distributes these chapters across **many smaller branch libraries** in different cities.
> - A **central catalog** (the *NameNode*) remembers which chapter of which book is stored at which branch.
