> - The **branches** (the *DataNodes*) physically hold the chapters.
> - To find a book, you first consult the catalog, then visit the appropriate branches and stitch the chapters back together.

This is **exactly how HDFS works**:
- A large file is split into **blocks** (default size: **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x/3.x).
- Blocks are replicated (default: **3 copies**) across multiple DataNodes.
- The NameNode keeps the **metadata** (file-to-block mapping).

### 1.3 Why HDFS for Big Data?

| Limitation of Traditional File Systems | How HDFS Solves It |
|---|---|
| Single machine = storage limit | Distributes data across cluster |
| Hardware failure = data loss | Replication (default 3x) |
| Sequential read/write only | Streaming access (WORM model) |
| Poor scalability | Horizontal scaling (add nodes) |
| Cannot handle petabytes | Block-based chunking + parallelism |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard HDFS Metrics (Memorize for KTU):**
> - **Block Size:** 128 MB (Hadoop 2.x and later)
> - **Default Replication Factor:** 3
> - **NameNode Memory Rule:** Each file/block/inode consumes **~150–200 bytes** of NameNode RAM
> - **Rack Awareness:** Replicas placed on **2 different racks** (1 on local rack, 1 on remote rack)

### 1.4 HDFS in the KTU 2024 Lab Context (Module 2 Experiment)

For the **Module Experiment 2 — Using HDFS Files**, students are expected to:
1. Start the Hadoop services (`start-dfs.sh`, `start-yarn.sh`).
2. Use the HDFS shell commands to create directories and manipulate files.
3. Transfer data between the **local file system (LFS)** and **HDFS**.
4. Inspect file metadata, block locations, and replication status.
5. Demonstrate fault-tolerance by intentionally deleting a file and recovering it from replicas.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 HDFS Architecture — Operational Logic

HDFS follows a **master-slave architecture** with two critical daemons:

**A. NameNode (Master)**
- Stores **metadata** in memory + on disk (fsimage + edit logs).
- Manages the **file system namespace** (directory tree, file-to-block mapping).
- Does **NOT** store actual file data.
- Single point of failure (mitigated by Secondary NameNode / HA setup in Hadoop 2+).

**B. DataNode (Slave)**
- Stores the **actual data blocks** on local disk.
- Sends **heartbeat signals** (default every **3 seconds**) to NameNode.
- Sends **block reports** periodically (default every **6 hours** or 2 million blocks).
- On instruction from NameNode, performs block creation, deletion, and replication.

### 2.2 Block-Level Read/Write Workflow (Why & How)

#### File Write Operation:
1. Client requests NameNode to create a file → NameNode checks permissions and namespace.
2. NameNode returns the list of DataNodes for the first batch of blocks.
3. Client writes block to first DataNode, which **pipelines** it to the second, which pipelines to the third (replication chain).
4. Acknowledgments flow back to confirm successful write.
5. Client requests next block location; process repeats.

#### File Read Operation:
1. Client requests file from NameNode → NameNode returns **block locations** (DataNode IPs + block IDs).
2. Client reads blocks from the **nearest DataNode** (based on network topology / rack proximity).
3. Blocks are stitched together to reconstruct the file.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — HDFS Capacity Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These formulas are the most frequently tested numerical problems in KTU exams for this module.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of blocks per file | $\displaystyle N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \right\rceil$ | Always round **up** (ceiling) |
| 2 | Total physical storage used | $\displaystyle S_{total} = N_{blocks} \times \text{Block Size} \times R$ | $R$ = replication factor |
| 3 | NameNode memory consumption | $\displaystyle M_{NN} = (\text{no. of files} + \text{no. of blocks}) \times 150 \text{ bytes}$ | Rule of thumb (Hadoop docs) |
| 4 | Logical storage | $\displaystyle S_{logical} = N_{blocks} \times \text{Block Size}$ | Storage user perceives |
| 5 | Storage overhead (wastage) | $\displaystyle O = S_{total} - S_{logical}$ | Extra space due to replication + partial last block |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU table cells above, vertical bars have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 HDFS Shell Command Categories (Exam Essential)

All HDFS operations are accessed through the `hdfs dfs` (or older `hadoop fs`) command family. The two are **functionally identical** in modern Hadoop.

| Category | Commands | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Directory Operations** | `mkdir`, `ls`, `du`, `df` | Create/list/check directories |
| **File Transfer (Upload)** | `put`, `copyFromLocal`, `moveFromLocal` | LFS → HDFS |
| **File Transfer (Download)** | `get`, `copyToLocal`, `moveToLocal` | HDFS → LFS |
| **File Inspection** | `cat`, `text`, `head`, `tail`, `stat` | View file contents/metadata |
| **File Management** | `mv`, `cp`, `rm`, `rmr`, `chmod`, `chown` | Manipulate files |
| **Block & Replication** | `setrep`, `expunge` | Change replication, clear trash |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

HDFS is the **backbone storage** for:
- **Data Lake architectures** in enterprises (Facebook, Yahoo, LinkedIn historically).
- **Log analytics pipelines** (clickstream, IoT sensor data).
- **Machine learning training data storage** (training corpora of TBs).
- **Archival storage** for raw transactional data before ETL.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Problem — HDFS Block Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic KTU numerical problem. Master the step-by-step approach.

**Problem Statement (KTU University Exam Style):**
> A file of size **700 MB** is uploaded to HDFS with a default block size of **128 MB** and a replication factor of **3**.
> Calculate:
> (a) The number of blocks created.
> (b) The total physical storage consumed on the cluster.
> (c) The logical storage perceived by the user.
> (d) The storage overhead.

---

#### Step (a): Number of Blocks

We use the ceiling formula because the last block is **partially filled** but still counts as one full block.

$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \right\rceil
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{700 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil
$$

Computing the division:

$$
\frac{700}{128} = 5.46875
$$

Applying the ceiling function (round up to the next integer):

$$
N_{blocks} = 6 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Block composition breakdown** (for conceptual clarity):
- 5 full blocks × 128 MB = 640 MB
- 1 partial block = 60 MB (uses 128 MB of disk but holds only 60 MB of data)

**∴ Number of blocks = 6**

---

#### Step (b): Total Physical Storage Consumed

The formula accounts for the fact that each block is replicated $R$ times:

$$
S_{total} = N_{blocks} \times \text{Block Size} \times R
$$

Substituting:

$$
S_{total} = 6 \times 128 \text{ MB} \times 3
$$

Multiplying step-by-step:

$$
S_{total} = 768 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 2304 \text{ MB}
$$

Converting to GB for readability:

$$
S_{total} = \frac{2304}{1024} = 2.25 \text{ GB}
$$

**∴ Total physical storage = 2304 MB (2.25 GB)**

---

#### Step (c): Logical Storage Perceived by User

Logical storage is the actual file size — what the user *thinks* they stored:

$$
S_{logical} = \text{File Size} = 700 \text{ MB}
$$

**∴ Logical storage = 700 MB**

---

#### Step (d): Storage Overhead

Overhead is the **extra space** consumed due to replication and partial last-block wastage:

$$
O = S_{total} - S_{logical}
$$

Substituting:

$$
O = 2304 \text{ MB} - 700 \text{ MB} = 1604 \text{ MB}
$$

We can also decompose this:
- **Replication overhead** (extra copies beyond the first): $(R - 1) \times S_{logical} = 2 \times 700 = 1400$ MB
- **Partial block wastage** (unused space in the 6th block): $128 - 60 = 68$ MB
- **Total** = $1400 + 68 = 1468$ MB... *wait, this does not match.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction using the EXACT HDFS accounting model:**
> Each block occupies a full 128 MB on disk (regardless of actual data inside). So overhead = (total disk used) − (actual data) = $2304 - 700 = 1604$ MB, which already includes both the replication cost AND the partial-block wastage. The decomposition above uses a "logical" view; the canonical HDFS formula is the direct subtraction.

**∴ Storage overhead = 1604 MB (≈ 1.567 GB)**

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — HDFS Command Execution Flow

| Step | Command (typed in terminal) | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `hdfs dfs -mkdir /user/ktu/data` | Creates a directory tree in HDFS | Directory `/user/ktu/data` appears |
| 2 | `hdfs dfs -ls /` | Lists root of HDFS | Shows top-level directories |
| 3 | `echo "Hello HDFS" > local.txt` | Creates a file on Local File System (LFS) | `local.txt` exists in `~/` |
| 4 | `hdfs dfs -put local.txt /user/ktu/data/` | Uploads from LFS to HDFS | File now in HDFS |
| 5 | `hdfs dfs -ls /user/ktu/data/` | Verifies upload | `local.txt` is listed |
| 6 | `hdfs dfs -cat /user/ktu/data/local.txt` | Reads file content | Output: `Hello HDFS` |
| 7 | `hdfs dfs -du /user/ktu/data/` | Disk usage | Shows size + replica count |
| 8 | `hdfs dfs -stat "%o %r" /user/ktu/data/local.txt` | Block size + replication | E.g., `134217728 3` |
| 9 | `hdfs dfs -setrep -w 2 /user/ktu/data/local.txt` | Change replication to 2 (with wait flag) | Replicas reduced from 3 → 2 |
| 10 | `hdfs dfs -rm /user/ktu/data/local.txt` | Delete file | File removed (moved to `.Trash`) |

### 3.3 Derivation: NameNode Memory Requirement

**Problem:** A cluster stores **1000 files**, each averaging **500 MB**, with a block size of **128 MB** and replication factor **3**. Estimate the NameNode memory requirement.

**Step 1 — Blocks per file:**

$$
N_{blocks/file} = \left\lceil \frac{500}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.906 \rceil = 4 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 2 — Total blocks across all files:**

$$
N_{blocks/total} = 1000 \times 4 = 4000 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3 — Total entries (files + blocks):**

$$
E_{total} = 1000 + 4000 = 5000 \text{ entries}
$$

**Step 4 — NameNode memory (using 150 bytes/entry rule):**

$$
M_{NN} = 5000 \times 150 \text{ bytes} = 750{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

Converting:

$$
M_{NN} = \frac{750{,}000}{1024 \times 1024} \approx 0.715 \text{ MB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** Even for millions of small files, the NameNode memory grows linearly. This is why Hadoop is **fundamentally designed for large files, not many small files**. A common KTU trick question asks: "Why is HDFS unsuitable for storing millions of small files?" — The answer: **NameNode memory bottleneck**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — HDFS Write Pipeline Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    clientA["HDFS Client Application"]
    nameNodeA["NameNode Master Node"]
    dataNodeA["DataNode 1 First Replica"]
    dataNodeB["DataNode 2 Second Replica"]
    dataNodeC["DataNode 3 Third Replica"]
    diskA["Local Disk Block Storage"]
    diskB["Local Disk Block Storage"]
    diskC["Local Disk Block Storage"]

    clientA -->|"Step 1 Request Create File"| nameNodeA
    nameNodeA -->|"Step 2 Return DataNode Pipeline List"| clientA
    clientA -->|"Step 3 Write Block Packet"| dataNodeA
    dataNodeA -->|"Step 4 Forward to Next DN"| dataNodeB
    dataNodeB -->|"Step 5 Forward to Next DN"| dataNodeC
    dataNodeA -->|"ACK Pipeline Response"| clientA
    dataNodeB -->|"ACK Pipeline Response"| clientA
    dataNodeC -->|"ACK Pipeline Response"| clientA
    dataNodeA --> diskA
    dataNodeB --> diskB
    dataNodeC --> diskC
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — HDFS Read Path Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clientB["HDFS Client"]
    nameNodeB["NameNode"]
    dn1["DataNode 1 Nearest Replica"]
    dn2["DataNode 2 Alternate Replica"]
    dn3["DataNode 3 Alternate Replica"]
    fileResult["Reconstructed File Output"]

    clientB -->|"Step 1 Request File Metadata"| nameNodeB
    nameNodeB -->|"Step 2 Return Block Locations Sorted by Proximity"| clientB
    clientB -->|"Step 3 Read Block Directly"| dn1
    dn1 -.->|"Fallback if DN1 fails"| dn2
    dn2 -.->|"Fallback if DN2 fails"| dn3
    dn1 --> fileResult
    dn2 --> fileResult
    dn3 --> fileResult
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — HDFS File Block Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph clientC["User Perspective"]
        logicalFile["Logical File 700 MB"]
    end

    subgraph nameNodeView["NameNode Metadata View"]
        meta["File: data.txt Size: 700 MB Blocks: 6 Replication: 3"]
    end

    subgraph blockLayout["Physical Block Distribution"]
        b1["Block 1 128 MB"]
        b2["Block 2 128 MB"]
        b3["Block 3 128 MB"]
        b4["Block 4 128 MB"]
        b5["Block 5 128 MB"]
        b6["Block 6 60 MB logical 128 MB physical"]
    end

    logicalFile --> meta
    meta --> b1
    meta --> b2
    meta --> b3
    meta --> b4
    meta --> b5
    meta --> b6
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — LFS to HDFS Data Transfer Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Terminal
    participant H as HDFS Client Library
    participant NN as NameNode
    participant DN as DataNode Cluster

    U->>H: hdfs dfs -put local.txt /data/
    H->>NN: Request file create at /data/local.txt
    NN-->>H: Permission OK, allocate block IDs and DN pipeline
    H->>DN: Write Block 1 to first DataNode
    DN-->>H: ACK block written and replicated
    H->>U: Upload complete
    U->>H: hdfs dfs -ls /data/
    H->>NN: Query directory listing
    NN-->>H: Return file metadata
    H-->>U: Display local.txt with size and replication
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These schematics represent the **functional data flow** rather than physical hardware placement, aligning with the Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow fallback rule for complex distributed systems.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define HDFS. List any four HDFS shell commands with their functions.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** is a distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant file system designed to store very large datasets across multiple commodity machines in a Hadoop cluster. It follows a master-slave architecture with NameNode (master) and DataNodes (slaves), and uses a write-once-read-many access model.

**Four HDFS Shell Commands:**

| Command | Function |
|---|---|
| `hdfs dfs -mkdir /path` | Creates a directory in HDFS |
| `hdfs dfs -put <local> <hdfs>` | Uploads a file from local FS to HDFS |
| `hdfs dfs -cat /hdfs/path` | Displays the contents of an HDFS file |
| `hdfs dfs -rm /hdfs/path` | Deletes a file from HDFS (moves to .Trash) |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**What is the default block size and replication factor in HDFS? Why is HDFS unsuitable for storing many small files?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Default block size:** **128 MB** (in Hadoop 2.x and later versions)
- **Default replication factor:** **3**

**Why HDFS is unsuitable for many small files:**
The NameNode stores all file metadata in RAM, with each file and each block consuming approximately **150 bytes of memory**. When there are millions of small files, the NameNode memory requirement grows enormously, leading to performance degradation and eventually memory exhaustion. Additionally, most small files use only a fraction of a 128 MB block, causing massive **storage wastage**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): HDFS Block Calculation + Architecture**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** A file of size **950 MB** is stored in HDFS with a block size of **128 MB** and replication factor **3**.
Calculate:
(i) Number of blocks
(ii) Total physical storage on the cluster
(iii) Storage overhead
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **HDFS architecture**. List the responsibilities of the NameNode and DataNode.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Given:**
- File size = 950 MB
- Block size = 128 MB
- Replication factor $R$ = 3

**(i) Number of blocks:**

$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{950}{128} \right\rceil
$$

Performing the division:

$$
\frac{950}{128} = 7.421875
$$

Applying the ceiling function:

$$
N_{blocks} = 8 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Block composition:** 7 full blocks × 128 MB = 896 MB + 1 partial block of 54 MB (occupies 128 MB on disk).

**(ii) Total physical storage:**

$$
S_{total} = N_{blocks} \times \text{Block Size} \times R
$$

$$
S_{total} = 8 \times 128 \times 3
$$

$$
S_{total} = 1024 \times 3 = 3072 \text{ MB} = 3 \text{ GB}
$$

**(iii) Storage overhead:**

$$
O = S_{total} - S_{logical} = 3072 - 950 = 2122 \text{ MB} \approx 2.07 \text{ GB}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**HDFS Architecture** consists of:
- **One NameNode (Master)** — manages metadata.
- **Multiple DataNodes (Slaves)** — store actual block data.
- **Client** — interacts with NameNode for metadata, then with DataNodes for data transfer.

**Responsibilities of NameNode:**
1. Manages the file system namespace (directory tree, file/block metadata).
2. Records file-to-block mapping and block-to-DataNode mapping.
3. Receives heartbeat and block reports from all DataNodes.
4. Controls replication and re-replication on DataNode failure.
5. Does **not** store actual file data.

**Responsibilities of DataNode:**
1. Stores actual data blocks on its local disk.
2. Serves read/write requests from clients.
3. Sends **heartbeat** (every 3 sec) to NameNode to signal liveness.
4. Sends **block reports** periodically listing all blocks it stores.
5. Performs block creation, deletion, and replication as instructed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): HDFS Commands + Read/Write Process (Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **HDFS file read process** with a sequence diagram description. Mention the role of rack awareness.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the HDFS shell commands to perform the following operations, and explain each:
1. Create a directory `/user/student/lab2`.
2. Upload `input.txt` from local filesystem to `/user/student/lab2/`.
3. Display the contents of the uploaded file.
4. Check the disk usage of the directory.
5. Change the replication factor to 2.
6. Delete the file from HDFS.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a): HDFS Read Process**

**Step-by-step flow:**
1. The **HDFS client** opens the file by calling `open()` on the FileSystem object, which internally contacts the **NameNode**.
2. The **NameNode** validates the request (permissions, file existence) and returns a list of **block locations** for each block of the file. The list is sorted by **network topology proximity** (closest DataNode first).
3. The **client** then connects directly to the **nearest DataNode** for the first block and begins reading using `read()`. When the block boundary is reached, the client closes the connection to that DataNode and opens a new connection to the next preferred DataNode for the following block.
4. This process continues until all blocks are read. The client then assembles the data into the original file.
5. If the nearest DataNode is **unavailable or fails** mid-read, the client transparently connects to the next DataNode in the list, ensuring **fault tolerance**.

**Role of Rack Awareness:**
Rack awareness is the NameNode's policy of placing **replicas on different physical racks** to survive rack-level failures (e.g., switch failure, power outage). Typically, the policy is: **2 replicas on the same rack as the writer**, and **1 replica on a different rack**. This balances **reliability** (survives rack failure) with **write performance** (minimizes inter-rack network traffic during the write pipeline).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b): HDFS Shell Commands**

```bash
# 1. Create directory in HDFS
hdfs dfs -mkdir -p /user/student/lab2

# 2. Upload file from local filesystem to HDFS
hdfs dfs -put input.txt /user/student/lab2/

# 3. Display contents of the uploaded file
hdfs dfs -cat /user/student/lab2/input.txt

# 4. Check disk usage of the directory
hdfs dfs -du -h /user/student/lab2/

# 5. Change replication factor to 2 (with -w to wait until done)
hdfs dfs -setrep -w 2 /user/student/lab2/input.txt

# 6. Delete the file from HDFS (moves to .Trash by default)
hdfs dfs -rm /user/student/lab2/input.txt
```

**Explanation of each command:**

| # | Command | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `-mkdir -p` | Creates a directory; `-p` creates parent directories if they don't exist |
| 2 | `-put` | Copies a file from the **Local File System (LFS)** to **HDFS** |
| 3 | `-cat` | Prints the file's contents to stdout (terminal) |
| 4 | `-du -h` | Shows **disk usage** in **human-readable** format (KB/MB/GB) |
| 5 | `-setrep -w 2` | Sets replication factor to **2**; `-w` flag waits for the operation to complete |
| 6 | `-rm` | Removes a file; by default moves to `.Trash` (skip trash with `-skipTrash`) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong replication calculation:** Students often forget to **multiply by R** when calculating total physical storage. The file's "real disk usage" is NOT its logical size.
> 2. **Floor vs. Ceiling confusion:** Number of blocks is always **rounded UP (ceiling)**, never down. A 700 MB file with 128 MB blocks gives 6 blocks, not 5.
> 3. **Confusing LFS and HDFS paths:** In `-put` and `-get` commands, the **source path is local** and the **destination path is HDFS** (or vice versa). Reversing these is a frequent error.
> 4. **Forgetting `-p` in mkdir:** Without `-p`, the command fails if the parent directory doesn't exist.
> 5. **Heartbeat vs. Block Report timing confusion:** Heartbeats are every **3 seconds**; block reports are every **6 hours** by default. Examiners love testing this.
> 6. **Saying "NameNode stores data":** A classic trap. The NameNode stores **metadata only**, not the actual file content.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — HDFS Files (Module Experiment 2)**

- [x] **HDFS** = Hadoop Distributed File System; master-slave; fault-tolerant; WORM model.
- [x] **Block size = 128 MB**; **Replication = 3** (default values — must be memorized).
- [x] **NameNode** = master, stores **metadata only** in RAM (~150 bytes per file/block).
- [x] **DataNode** = slave, stores **actual block data** on local disk.
- [x] **Heartbeat** = every **3 seconds**; **Block Report** = every **6 hours**.
- [x] Block count formula: $\displaystyle N_{blocks} = \lceil \text{FileSize} / \text{BlockSize} \rceil$ (always ceiling).
- [x] Total storage formula: $\displaystyle S_{total} = N_{blocks} \times \text{BlockSize} \times R$.
- [x] Storage overhead = $S_{total} - S_{logical}$.
- [x] **Rack Awareness** = 2 replicas on same rack, 1 on different rack (typical policy).
- [x] Write pipeline = Client → DN1 → DN2 → DN3 → ACKs back to client.
- [x] Read process = Client asks NameNode for block locations, reads from **nearest** DataNode.
- [x] **Write Once, Read Many (WORM)** — HDFS files cannot be modified in place; they can only be appended or deleted.
- [x] **Small files problem** = NameNode memory bottleneck + block wastage.
- [x] Essential commands: `mkdir`, `ls`, `put`, `get`, `cat`, `rm`, `cp`, `mv`, `du`, `df`, `setrep`, `stat`, `chmod`, `chown`.
- [x] **Two CLI syntaxes** are equivalent: `hdfs dfs <args>` and `hadoop fs <args>`.
- [x] Starting HDFS: `start-dfs.sh`; stopping: `stop-dfs.sh`; NameNode Web UI: `http://localhost:9870`.
- [x] **`.Trash` checkpoint** — deleted files go to trash unless `-skipTrash` is used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For any HDFS numerical, always **state the formula, substitute values, show intermediate calculation steps, and write the final answer with units**. KTU examiners award partial marks for methodology, not just the final number.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HDFS Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** HDFS Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: HDFS Design — Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** is the primary storage layer of the Apache Hadoop ecosystem, designed to store very large files (terabytes to petabytes) reliably across machines in a commodity cluster. It follows a **master/slave (or master/worker) architecture** in which a single **NameNode (master)** manages the file system namespace and metadata, while multiple **DataNodes (slaves/workers)** store the actual file data as replicated **blocks**.

KTU 2024 Scheme defines HDFS Design under the following characteristics:
- **Streaming data access** (write-once, read-many)
- **Block-structured** file organization
- **Hardware fault tolerance** via software-level replication
- **Portability** across heterogeneous hardware
- **Relaxed POSIX semantics** (trade strict consistency for throughput)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board-Examiner Ready)**
> *"HDFS is a distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant file system written in Java that provides high-throughput access to large datasets by partitioning files into fixed-size blocks, distributing them across cluster nodes, and replicating each block on multiple DataNodes under the supervision of a NameNode."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library System

Imagine a **huge library** that must store a million books:
- The **Librarian's Index Card File** = **NameNode** (does not store books; only knows *where* every book is, on *which shelf* of *which building*).
- The **Actual Shelves in Multiple Buildings** = **DataNodes** (physically hold the books).
- Each book is **torn into chapter-sized chunks** and **photocopied** into 3 different buildings (so a fire in one building does not lose the book). This is **block replication**.
- The librarian writes an **update log** every time a new book arrives, and periodically takes a **snapshot (backup)** of the index file.

That librarian's log is the **EditLog (FsEditLog)**, and the snapshot is the **FsImage**. The backup librarian who takes periodic snapshots is the **Secondary NameNode / Checkpoint Node**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard HDFS Configuration Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard HDFS Default Values (Memorize for KTU Board Exam)**
> - **Block Size:** **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+), earlier **64 MB** (Hadoop 1.x)
> - **Default Replication Factor:** **3**
> - **Heartbeat Interval:** **3 seconds**
> - **Heartbeat Expiry / Stale Interval:** **10 × 3 = 30 seconds** (default multiplier = 10)
> - **Block Report Interval:** **6 hours** (default `dfs.blockreport.intervalMsec = 21600000 ms`)
> - **Replication Pipeline:** Replicas are written in a daisy-chain (tube) fashion.
> - **Safe Mode:** NameNode starts in read-only mode; no block rebalancing or replication on startup.

## 1.4 Why HDFS? — Design Goals

| Design Goal | HDFS Strategy |
|---|---|
| Handle very large files | Large 128 MB blocks reduce metadata overhead |
| Streaming (batch) read access | Write-once-read-many (WORM) model |
| Commodity hardware tolerance | Software-level block replication (default 3x) |
| Portability | Pure Java implementation, hardware-agnostic |
| High throughput | Co-locate computation with data (data locality) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Line:** *"Moving computation is cheaper than moving data."* — This is the foundational philosophy behind HDFS + MapReduce co-location.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HDFS Architecture — Component Breakdown

### A. NameNode (Master)
- Stores **filesystem metadata**: the entire directory tree, file-to-block mapping, block-to-DataNode mapping, file permissions, replication factor per file.
- Metadata is held **in RAM** (for fast lookup) and persisted to disk via:
  - **FsImage** — a checkpoint snapshot of the namespace.
  - **EditLog (FsEditLog)** — an append-only log of every modification.
- The NameNode is a **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** in classical HDFS (Hadoop 1.x); Hadoop 2.x mitigates this with **High Availability (HA)** via a **Standby NameNode** using a **Quorum Journal Manager (ZooKeeper-based failover)**.
- **Does NOT store actual file data.**

### B. DataNode (Slave/Worker)
- Stores **actual data blocks** on local disk under `dfs.datanode.data.dir`.
- Sends a **Heartbeat** to the NameNode every **3 seconds**.
- Sends a **Block Report** (list of all blocks it holds) every **6 hours** by default.
- Performs block **creation, deletion, and replication** on instruction from the NameNode.
- Serves read/write requests directly from clients.

### C. Secondary NameNode / Checkpoint Node (Misnomer)
- Despite the name, it is **NOT a hot standby** for the NameNode.
- Periodically merges `FsImage + EditLog` into a new `FsImage` to prevent the EditLog from growing unboundedly. This is called **checkpointing**.
- In Hadoop 2.x, the **Checkpoint Node** or **Backup Node** replaces this role.

### D. Client
- Talks to NameNode for **metadata** operations (open, close, rename).
- Talks **directly to DataNodes** for **data** operations (read/write).

## 2.2 HDFS File Storage Model — Blocks & Replicas

- A file is split into fixed-size **blocks** (default **128 MB**).
- Each block is stored as a **separate file** on a DataNode's local filesystem.
- Each block is **replicated** across multiple DataNodes (default **3** copies).
- The NameNode decides the **replication placement** using its **rack-awareness** policy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rack Awareness Default Policy (3 Replicas):**
> - Replica 1 → on a node in the **local rack** (where the writer is).
> - Replica 2 → on a **different node** in the **same rack**.
> - Replica 3 → on a **node in a different rack**.
> This gives a balance between **reliability** (rack failure tolerance) and **write bandwidth** (only one inter-rack transfer).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of blocks for a file | $N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{F}{B} \right\rceil$ | blocks | $F$ = file size, $B$ = block size |
| 2 | Last (partial) block size | $B_{last} = F \bmod B$ if not zero, else $B$ | MB | Always padded up to $B$ on disk |
| 3 | Logical storage used (data only) | $S_{logic} = N_{blocks} \times B$ | MB | Counts full allocated blocks |
| 4 | Physical storage with replication | $S_{phys} = S_{logic} \times R = N_{blocks} \times B \times R$ | MB | $R$ = replication factor (default 3) |
| 5 | Wasted storage (replication overhead) | $S_{waste} = S_{phys} - S_{logic} = S_{logic} \times (R-1)$ | MB | |
| 6 | Replication factor (general) | $R = \dfrac{S_{phys}}{S_{logic}}$ | dimensionless | Integer, typically $\geq 1$ |
| 7 | Storage efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{R} \times 100$ | % | $R=3 \Rightarrow \eta \approx 33.33\%$ |
| 8 | Heartbeat expiry time | $T_{expire} = k \times T_{heartbeat}$ | seconds | $k = 10$ (default), $T_{heartbeat} = 3\,s$ ⇒ $T_{expire} = 30\,s$ |
| 9 | Disk bandwidth utilization (pipeline) | $BW_{eff} = \min(BW_{1}, BW_{2}, \ldots, BW_{R})$ | MB/s | Limited by **slowest** replica link |
| 10 | Cluster block capacity | $C_{cluster} = N_{dn} \times D_{avg} \times R$ | MB | $N_{dn}$ = DataNode count, $D_{avg}$ = avg disk per node |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Avoidance Note:** All `|` (absolute value) in this document are rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax. Always inspect your rendered output.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

HDFS design underpins:
- **Search engines** (Yahoo's web index, ~5 PB+).
- **Social media analytics** (Facebook's warehouse).
- **Genomics pipelines** (DNA sequencing data, hundreds of TB).
- **IoT telemetry storage** (sensor streams ingested via Flume/Kafka into HDFS).
- **Log archival** in banking/fintech (e.g., transaction logs at 7-year retention).

The same block + replication design pattern appears in **Google's GFS**, **Facebook's Haystack**, **Amazon S3 (under the hood)**, and **Ceph RADOS**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Storage Capacity with Replication

### Problem Statement
A user uploads **3 files** of sizes **200 MB, 500 MB, and 1500 MB** to an HDFS cluster. HDFS block size is **128 MB**, and replication factor is **3**. Compute:
1. The number of blocks per file.
2. The total number of blocks.
3. Logical storage.
4. Physical storage on disk.
5. Wasted (replication overhead) storage.
6. Storage efficiency.

### Step 1 — Number of Blocks per File

We use the ceiling formula. Recall:

$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil
$$

**File 1: F = 200 MB, B = 128 MB**

$$
N_1 = \left\lceil \frac{200}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 1.5625 \right\rceil = 2 \text{ blocks}
$$

The 2nd block holds only 200 − 128 = 72 MB of actual data but occupies 128 MB on disk.

**File 2: F = 500 MB, B = 128 MB**

$$
N_2 = \left\lceil \frac{500}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 3.90625 \right\rceil = 4 \text{ blocks}
$$

Last block holds 500 − (3 × 128) = 500 − 384 = 116 MB.

**File 3: F = 1500 MB, B = 128 MB**

$$
N_3 = \left\lceil \frac{1500}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 11.71875 \right\rceil = 12 \text{ blocks}
$$

Last block holds 1500 − (11 × 128) = 1500 − 1408 = 92 MB.

### Step 2 — Total Number of Blocks

$$
N_{total} = N_1 + N_2 + N_3 = 2 + 4 + 12 = 18 \text{ blocks}
$$

### Step 3 — Logical Storage (Data + Padding)

$$
S_{logic} = N_{total} \times B = 18 \times 128 = 2304 \text{ MB} \approx 2.25 \text{ GB}
$$

> **Note:** Actual user data = 200 + 500 + 1500 = 2200 MB. The 104 MB difference (2304 − 2200) is **internal fragmentation** from partial last blocks.

### Step 4 — Physical Storage with Replication (R = 3)

$$
S_{phys} = S_{logic} \times R = 2304 \times 3 = 6912 \text{ MB} \approx 6.75 \text{ GB}
$$

### Step 5 — Wasted Storage (Replication Overhead Only)

$$
S_{waste} = S_{phys} - S_{logic} = 6912 - 2304 = 4608 \text{ MB}
$$

(Equivalently, $S_{logic} \times (R - 1) = 2304 \times 2 = 4608$ MB. ✓)

### Step 6 — Storage Efficiency

$$
\eta = \frac{S_{logic}}{S_{phys}} \times 100 = \frac{1}{R} \times 100 = \frac{1}{3} \times 100 \approx 33.33\%
$$

### Final Result Table

| File | Size (MB) | Blocks | Last Block (MB) | Logical (MB) | Physical with R=3 (MB) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200 | 2 | 72 | 256 | 768 |
| 2 | 500 | 4 | 116 | 512 | 1536 |
| 3 | 1500 | 12 | 92 | 1536 | 4608 |
| **Total** | **2200** | **18** | — | **2304** | **6912** |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: HDFS Write Pipeline Mathematics

### Problem Statement
Consider a file write operation with **R = 3** replicas. Each network hop has bandwidth **100 MB/s** but introduces a **20 ms** per-hop propagation/queuing delay. Compute the **effective write throughput** for a **512 MB** file using:
(a) Sequential (one replica at a time) write strategy.
(b) HDFS **pipeline (daisy-chain)** write strategy.

### (a) Sequential Strategy
The client writes each replica one after the other, and each replica's network transfer itself takes time $T_{transfer}$ plus a fixed setup delay $T_{setup}$.

$$
T_{transfer} = \frac{S}{BW} = \frac{512}{100} = 5.12 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{sequential} = R \times (T_{transfer} + T_{setup}) = 3 \times (5.12 + 0.02) = 3 \times 5.14 = 15.42 \text{ s}
$$

$$
BW_{eff}^{seq} = \frac{S}{T_{sequential}} = \frac{512}{15.42} \approx 33.20 \text{ MB/s}
$$

### (b) Pipeline (Daisy-Chain) Strategy
In HDFS, the data is split into **packets** (default 64 KB). While the client streams packet $i$ to DN1, DN1 simultaneously forwards packet $i$ to DN2, and DN2 to DN3. The bottleneck throughput is:

$$
T_{pipeline} = T_{setup} + T_{transfer} = 0.02 + 5.12 = 5.14 \text{ s}
$$

The pipeline adds at most one extra hop of latency in steady state (the pipeline fills), so:

$$
BW_{eff}^{pipeline} = \frac{S}{T_{pipeline}} = \frac{512}{5.14} \approx 99.61 \text{ MB/s}
$$

### Speedup

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{sequential}}{T_{pipeline}} = \frac{15.42}{5.14} = 3.00 \times
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board:** The pipeline approach approximates the bandwidth of a **single link** while ensuring **R-way replication** — the throughput cost of replication is essentially one extra hop, not R-fold. This is why HDFS is "write-once, replicated efficiently."

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Failure & Recovery Scenarios

| Failure Scenario | Detection Mechanism | NameNode Action | Recovery Time Bound |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single DataNode down | Heartbeat missing > 30 s | Mark node *dead*, trigger re-replication of its blocks | $T_{expire} = 30$ s + re-replication time |
| Replica corrupted | Checksum mismatch on read | Schedule deletion, re-replicate to meet R=3 | Upon next block report / client read |
| Block under-replicated (R < 3) | Block report count vs. expected | Issue re-replication command to another DataNode | Next heartbeat cycle (≤ 3 s) |
| Entire rack failure | All DataNodes in rack fail heartbeat | Re-replicate all blocks (racks host only 1 of R=3) | $T_{expire} = 30$ s + transfer |
| NameNode crash (no HA) | ZooKeeper / manual detection | Service is down — cluster inaccessible | Restart + replay EditLog |
| NameNode crash (with HA) | ZooKeeper detects | Standby promoted to Active | ~ seconds (automatic) |
| EditLog corruption | Checksum failure on read | Replay from FsImage + last good EditLog segment | Minutes |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Checkpoint Frequency Trade-off

The Secondary NameNode / Checkpoint Node must merge **FsImage** of size $M$ and **EditLog** of growth rate $r$ (MB/min). If checkpoints happen every $T_{chk}$ minutes:

$$
\text{EditLog size at checkpoint} = r \times T_{chk}
$$

$$
T_{merge} \approx \frac{M + r \cdot T_{chk}}{BW_{merge}}
$$

Where $BW_{merge}$ is the merge throughput (limited by NameNode disk + RAM).

To bound recovery time after a crash, $T_{merge} \leq T_{target}$ (e.g., 1 hour):

$$
T_{chk} \leq \frac{BW_{merge} \cdot T_{target} - M}{r}
$$

> **Engineering trade-off:** Smaller $T_{chk}$ ⇒ smaller recovery time, but more I/O overhead on the NameNode. Hadoop's default in `core-site.xml` is `dfs.namenode.checkpoint.period = 3600 s` (1 hour) or `dfs.namenode.checkpoint.txns = 1,000,000` transactions, whichever first.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDFS Cluster — High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client[Client Application]
    nn[NameNode Master]
    snn[Secondary NameNode Checkpoint]
    dn1[DataNode 1 Rack A]
    dn2[DataNode 2 Rack A]
    dn3[DataNode 3 Rack B]
    dn4[DataNode 4 Rack B]
    dn5[DataNode 5 Rack C]
    dn6[DataNode 6 Rack C]

    client -- metadata RPC open read write close --> nn
    nn -- heartbeat every 3s --> dn1
    nn -- heartbeat every 3s --> dn2
    nn -- heartbeat every 3s --> dn3
    nn -- heartbeat every 3s --> dn4
    nn -- heartbeat every 3s --> dn5
    nn -- heartbeat every 3s --> dn6
    nn -- checkpoint merge --> snn
    client -- direct data stream read write --> dn1
    client -- direct data stream read write --> dn3
    client -- direct data stream read write --> dn5
    dn1 -.replication pipeline.-> dn2
    dn3 -.replication pipeline.-> dn4
    dn5 -.replication pipeline.-> dn6
```

## 4.2 HDFS File Write Flow — Step-by-Step

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant NN as NameNode
    participant DN1 as DataNode 1 Local Rack
    participant DN2 as DataNode 2 Local Rack
    participant DN3 as DataNode 3 Remote Rack

    C->>NN: 1 create file request
    NN-->>C: 2 OK + list of DataNodes for replicas
    C->>DN1: 3 write packet 1
    DN1->>DN2: 4 forward packet 1 pipeline
    DN2->>DN3: 5 forward packet 1 pipeline
    DN3-->>C: 6 ACK packet 1
    DN2-->>DN1: 7 ACK
    DN1-->>C: 8 ACK
    C->>DN1: 9 write packet 2
    DN1->>DN2: 10 forward packet 2
    DN2->>DN3: 11 forward packet 2
    DN3-->>C: 12 ACK packet 2
    DN2-->>DN1: 13 ACK
    DN1-->>C: 14 ACK
    C->>NN: 15 close file
    NN-->>C: 16 commit EditLog entry
```

## 4.3 HDFS File Read Flow — Step-by-Step

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant NN as NameNode
    participant DN1 as DataNode A Block 1
    participant DN2 as DataNode B Block 1
    participant DN3 as DataNode C Block 1

    C->>NN: 1 open file path
    NN-->>C: 2 block locations sorted by proximity
    C->>DN1: 3 read block 1 stream
    DN1-->>C: 4 packet stream
    C->>DN2: 5 read block 2
    DN2-->>C: 6 packet stream
    C->>DN3: 7 read block 3
    DN3-->>C: 8 packet stream
    C->>NN: 9 close file
```

## 4.4 HDFS NameNode Metadata Persistence Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client Write Request] --> B[NameNode]
    B --> C[Update In Memory Namespace]
    C --> D[Append to EditLog on Disk]
    D --> E[EditLog grows over time]
    E --> F[Secondary NameNode / Checkpoint]
    F --> G[Download FsImage + EditLog]
    G --> H[Merge into new FsImage]
    H --> I[Upload new FsImage to NameNode]
    I --> J[NameNode truncates EditLog]
    J --> B
```

## 4.5 Rack-Aware Block Placement (Default 3-Replica Policy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RACKA[Rack A Local]
        DN1[DataNode 1]
        DN2[DataNode 2]
    end
    subgraph RACKB[Rack B Remote]
        DN3[DataNode 3]
        DN4[DataNode 4]
    end
    subgraph RACKC[Rack C Remote]
        DN5[DataNode 5]
        DN6[DataNode 6]
    end

    CLIENT[Client Writer] -- replica 1 on local rack --> DN1
    CLIENT -- replica 2 same rack different node --> DN2
    CLIENT -- replica 3 different rack --> DN3
    DN1 -.replication.-> DN2
    DN1 -.replication.-> DN3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram-to-Concept Mapping:** The HDFS architecture (Section 4.1) shows strict **separation of metadata (NameNode) from data (DataNodes)**. The write flow (Section 4.2) demonstrates **pipeline replication**. The read flow (Section 4.3) shows **client-side proximity sorting** for data locality. Section 4.4 illustrates **EditLog + FsImage checkpointing** for fault recovery. Section 4.5 shows **rack-aware placement** balancing reliability and write bandwidth.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define HDFS. List any four design goals of HDFS.**

**Model Answer (Expected KTU Board Wording):**

**Definition:** HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) is a distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant file system designed to store very large data sets reliably on commodity hardware clusters. It partitions files into fixed-size blocks and replicates them across multiple DataNodes, managed centrally by a NameNode.

**Four Design Goals:**
1. **Handling very large files** — terabytes to petabytes, using large 128 MB blocks.
2. **Streaming data access** — write-once, read-many (WORM) workload pattern.
3. **Commodity hardware tolerance** — software-level replication (default 3x) handles node failures.
4. **Portability** — pure Java, hardware- and OS-agnostic.

*(Other acceptable goals: high throughput, data locality, relaxed POSIX semantics.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between NameNode and DataNode in HDFS architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | NameNode | DataNode |
|---|---|---|
| Role | Master / controller | Slave / worker |
| Stores | Metadata (namespace, file-to-block map) | Actual file data as blocks |
| Count in cluster | 1 (active) + 1 (standby in HA) | Many (typically hundreds) |
| Memory | Keeps metadata in RAM | Stores blocks on local disk |
| Heartbeat | Receives heartbeats | Sends heartbeat every 3 s |
| Block report | Receives block reports | Sends block report every 6 h |
| Failure impact | Cluster metadata unavailable (SPOF) | Only loses blocks on that node; re-replication triggered |
| On-disk artifacts | FsImage, EditLog | Block files under `dfs.datanode.data.dir` |

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **HDFS architecture**. Describe the role of NameNode, DataNode, and Secondary NameNode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **HDFS file write process** with a pipeline replication diagram. Why is the pipeline approach more efficient than sequential writing? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### (a) HDFS Architecture (7 marks)

HDFS follows a **master/slave architecture**.

**NameNode (Master):**
- Maintains the file system **namespace** and **metadata** (file-to-block mapping, block locations, replication factor, permissions, modification times).
- Metadata is held **in RAM** for fast access; persisted to disk via **FsImage** (snapshot) and **EditLog** (append-only modification log).
- Receives **heartbeats** from every DataNode every 3 s; if a heartbeat is missing for 30 s (10 × 3 s), the DataNode is declared **stale/dead**.
- Single Point of Failure in Hadoop 1.x; HA mode (Hadoop 2.x+) uses a **Standby NameNode** + **Quorum Journal Manager**.

**DataNode (Slave):**
- Stores the **actual data blocks** on its local disk.
- Sends **heartbeat (3 s)** and **block report (6 h)** to NameNode.
- Performs block **create, delete, replicate** on NameNode instructions.
- Serves read/write requests directly to clients (after NameNode's metadata hand-off).

**Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node):**
- **Misnomer:** it is NOT a hot backup of the NameNode.
- Periodically performs **checkpointing**: downloads FsImage + EditLog, merges them into a new FsImage, and uploads it back.
- Prevents the EditLog from growing unboundedly and reduces NameNode recovery time.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for architecture illustration.)*

---

#### (b) HDFS Write Process with Pipeline Replication (7 marks)

**Step-by-step flow** (also see Section 4.2 sequence diagram):

1. The **client** calls `create()` on the **NameNode**.
2. The **NameNode** performs a metadata check (no duplicate file, permissions valid) and responds with a list of **DataNodes** that will hold the replicas (chosen via rack-aware policy).
3. The client splits the file into **packets** (default 64 KB).
4. The client establishes a **replication pipeline** — e.g., DN1 → DN2 → DN3.
5. Packet 1 is pushed to **DN1**, which stores and forwards to **DN2**, which stores and forwards to **DN3**.
6. An **ACK** flows back: DN3 → DN2 → DN1 → client. Only after the ACK does the client send the next packet.
7. After all packets are written, the client calls `close()`. The NameNode commits the file in the EditLog.

**Why Pipeline > Sequential:**

In **sequential** writing, the client writes replica 1 completely, then replica 2, then replica 3. Total transfer time = $R \times T_{transfer}$. With $R = 3$ and a 512 MB file on 100 MB/s link, effective throughput drops to ~33 MB/s.

In **pipeline** writing, all replicas receive data concurrently in a daisy-chain; the network behaves like a **single link** for steady-state transfer. Effective throughput ≈ 1 link bandwidth (≈ 100 MB/s), giving an **~R-fold speedup** (≈ 3× here) at the cost of only one extra hop of latency.

**Advantages of Pipeline:**
- **High throughput** (limited by slowest link only).
- **Asynchronous forwarding** (DN2 starts forwarding to DN3 before DN1 finishes sending).
- **Built-in replication** in one network pass.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the **HDFS file read process** with a suitable diagram. How does HDFS handle **fault tolerance** when a DataNode fails during a read? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **rack-aware replica placement policy** in HDFS with default 3-replica configuration. How does this policy balance **reliability, write bandwidth, and read performance**? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### (a) HDFS File Read Process & Fault Tolerance (7 marks)

**Read flow** (also see Section 4.3):

1. The client calls `open(path)` on the **NameNode**.
2. The NameNode returns the **block locations** for each block of the file, **sorted by network proximity** to the client (closest DataNode first).
3. The client connects **directly** to the nearest DataNode and reads the block as a stream of packets.
4. For each subsequent block, the client consults the NameNode again (or uses cached metadata) and connects to the best DataNode.
5. If a checksum mismatch is detected, the client reports the corruption to the NameNode and **fetches the block from a different replica** transparently.
6. After all blocks are read, the client calls `close()`.

**Fault Tolerance during a Read:**
- **Replica redundancy** ensures the same block exists on 2 (or more) other DataNodes; if the first DataNode fails, the client automatically retries with the next replica in the proximity-sorted list.
- The NameNode re-replicates the block to maintain the desired replication factor.
- **Checksum verification** detects silent data corruption (bit-rot); the corrupt replica is scheduled for deletion and replaced.
- The client's **read position** can resume from the last acknowledged packet.

**Reliability through Replication** = $1 - (1-p)^R$ where $p$ is per-replica failure probability and $R$ is the replication factor. With $R=3$ and $p=0.01$, reliability = $1 - 0.99^3 \approx 0.9997$ (i.e., 99.97%).

---

#### (b) Rack-Aware Replica Placement (7 marks)

HDFS uses **rack awareness** to place replicas intelligently. With **R = 3** (default), the policy is:

| Replica # | Placement Rule | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Replica 1 | On a node in the **client's local rack** | Minimize write latency for the user |
| Replica 2 | On a **different node** in the **same rack** | Redundancy within the rack (rack-local) |
| Replica 3 | On a node in a **different rack** | Survive full rack failure (e.g., switch failure, power loss) |

**Balance achieved:**

| Criterion | How the Policy Addresses It |
|---|---|
| **Reliability** | Two of three replicas survive even if one entire rack fails. |
| **Write bandwidth** | Only **one inter-rack transfer** is needed (replica 3); two replicas stay on the local rack. Sequential would need $R-1 = 2$ inter-rack transfers. |
| **Read performance** | Replica 1 is on the local rack, so the most common read (by the writer process or co-located job) is rack-local. MapReduce tasks on the same rack get fast data access. |
| **Aggregate cluster load** | Replication spreads blocks across racks, balancing disk usage. |

> **Engineering trade-off:** Increasing $R$ improves reliability linearly with disk cost, but write throughput drops because the pipeline must span more racks.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in HDFS Design Answers (KTU Board Pattern)**
> 1. **Confusing Secondary NameNode with a Standby NameNode.** The Secondary NameNode only does *checkpointing* — it does **NOT** serve metadata requests. Mark loss: 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention rack-awareness** in the write process. Always describe which rack each replica is placed on. Mark loss: 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Writing a sequential description of the write process** instead of a pipeline. The pipeline / daisy-chain is the *exact* keyword. Mark loss: 2 marks.
> 4. **Saying "NameNode stores file data"** — this is a frequent error. NameNode only stores *metadata*. Mark loss: 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Omitting the heartbeat interval (3 s) and stale interval (30 s)** when describing fault detection. Examiners specifically look for these numbers. Mark loss: 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing block size 64 MB with 128 MB.** Hadoop 2.x+ (and KTU 2024 syllabus context) defaults to **128 MB**. Use 128 MB unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 7. **Not drawing a diagram** in a 7-mark architecture question. KTU valuation key typically allocates **1–2 marks** for a labelled diagram.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HDFS Design — 60-Second KTU Board Revision**

- **Architecture:** NameNode (master/metadata) + DataNodes (slaves/data blocks) + Secondary NameNode (checkpoint only).
- **Block size:** **128 MB** (default, Hadoop 2.x+). Older versions: 64 MB.
- **Replication factor:** **3** (default).
- **Heartbeat:** every **3 s**; **stale timeout:** **30 s** (10× heartbeat).
- **Block report:** every **6 hours**.
- **Two on-disk artifacts on NameNode:** **FsImage** (snapshot) + **EditLog** (append-only log).
- **Checkpointing:** Periodic merge of FsImage + EditLog by Secondary/Checkpoint Node to bound recovery time.
- **Write strategy:** **Pipeline / daisy-chain replication** — packets forwarded in parallel.
- **Read strategy:** Client reads from **nearest replica** (proximity-sorted by NameNode); checksum verifies integrity.
- **Rack awareness (R=3):** local-rack node + same-rack different node + different-rack node.
- **Data locality principle:** "Moving computation is cheaper than moving data." MapReduce tasks are scheduled on nodes holding the relevant blocks.
- **WORM model:** Write Once, Read Many — files are immutable; updates are simulated by creating new files.
- **Failure detection:** Heartbeat missing > 30 s ⇒ DataNode declared dead ⇒ re-replication triggered to maintain R.
- **Storage efficiency** = $1/R$ (e.g., 33.33% for R=3).
- **Wasted storage** = $S_{logic} \times (R-1)$.
- **Internal fragmentation** = last block padding.
- **Reliability formula:** $1 - (1-p)^R$ for independent replica failure probability $p$.
- **HA (Hadoop 2.x+):** Active + Standby NameNode with **ZooKeeper-based failover** + **Quorum Journal Manager** for shared EditLog.
- **Key APIs:** `org.apache.hadoop.fs.FileSystem`, methods `open()`, `create()`, `append()`, `rename()`, `delete()`, `mkdirs()`, `getStatus()`.
- **Single Point of Failure:** NameNode in Hadoop 1.x (mitigated by HA in 2.x+).
- **Commodity hardware assumption:** Designed to tolerate frequent, individual node failures via software replication rather than expensive RAID.
- **Packet size in pipeline:** **64 KB** (default `dfs.write.packet.size`).
- **Cluster block report overhead:** Every DataNode sends a list of all its block hashes; for 10 PB cluster with 1 PB per node, this can be a metadata bottleneck — use larger block reports or compression in production.

---

> **END OF NOTES — HDFS Design | KTU 2024 Scheme | PBADT504 Big Data Analytics | Module Experiment 2**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Blocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Big Data Analytics** and distributed storage, a **Block** is the smallest contiguous unit of data storage in a distributed file system (most notably **HDFS – Hadoop Distributed File System**) that the system reads from or writes to. A file in HDFS is split into one or more fixed-size blocks, which are then distributed, replicated, and processed across multiple machines (DataNodes) in a Hadoop cluster.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A block is a logical abstraction representing a chunk of file data, not a physical disk block. The default HDFS block size is **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x and later) and **64 MB** (Hadoop 1.x). HDFS blocks are transparent to the user — applications interact with logical files, not physical blocks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **500-page novel** that you want to store in a library. Instead of placing the entire book in one shelf (one machine), the librarian tears the novel into **chapters of fixed size** (say, 50 pages each). Each chapter is then placed in a different shelf (DataNode), and **photocopies** of each chapter are made and stored in backup shelves (replicas). 

- The **novel** = the HDFS file
- Each **chapter** = an HDFS block
- The **shelves** = DataNodes
- The **photocopies** = block replicas (replication factor, usually 3)
- The **librarian's index** = the NameNode metadata

This way, if one shelf collapses, you still have the entire novel. Also, multiple readers can read different chapters **in parallel**.

### 1.3 Key Block Metrics (KTU Board-Exam Essentials)

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **HDFS Default Block Size (Hadoop 2.x+)** | **128 MB** | Optimal for TB-PB scale data |
| **HDFS Default Block Size (Hadoop 1.x)** | **64 MB** | Legacy configuration |
| **Default Replication Factor** | **3** | Fault tolerance standard |
| **Minimum Replication Factor** | **1** | No fault tolerance |
| **Block Report Interval** | **6 hours** | DataNode → NameNode heartbeat |
| **Physical Disk Block Size** | **4 KB – 8 KB** | OS-level (NOT HDFS block) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** An **HDFS Block** is a *logical* concept managed by Hadoop. A **Physical Disk Block / Sector** is a hardware-level storage unit managed by the operating system. The two are completely independent. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Why and How Blocks Work

**Why do we need blocks in Big Data?**
1. **Parallelism** — Multiple blocks of a file can be processed simultaneously on different nodes, enabling the **MapReduce** paradigm.
2. **Fault Tolerance** — Replicated blocks survive DataNode failures.
3. **Scalability** — Files larger than a single machine's capacity can be stored across the cluster.
4. **Load Balancing** — Hadoop's balancer redistributes blocks evenly across DataNodes.
5. **Storage Abstraction** — Applications need not know the physical storage location.

**How does HDFS handle a file?**
- Step 1: Client requests file creation from **NameNode**.
- Step 2: NameNode allocates a new file entry in metadata (FSImage + EditLog).
- Step 3: Client writes data as a stream. The stream is **split into packets** of typically 64 KB.
- Step 4: Packets are buffered to a **Block** of 128 MB.
- Step 5: Once a block is full, the client notifies NameNode.
- Step 6: NameNode selects **DataNodes** (typically on different racks) and returns a pipeline.
- Step 7: The block is **replicated** across the pipeline (default: 3 copies).
- Step 8: DataNodes send **block received acknowledgements** up the pipeline.
- Step 9: Block metadata is updated in NameNode once all replicas are confirmed.

### 2.2 Block Placement Policy (Rack Awareness)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The default HDFS block placement strategy places the **first replica** on the **local node** (or a random node if client is external), the **second replica** on a **different rack**, and the **third replica** on the **same rack as the second** but a different node. This balances **reliability** (cross-rack) and **bandwidth** (intra-rack).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Blocks** | $N = \lceil \dfrac{F}{B} \rceil$ | $F$ = File size, $B$ = Block size. Ceiling function ensures the last partial block is counted. |
| **Total Physical Storage** | $S_{total} = F \times R$ | $F$ = File size, $R$ = Replication factor. |
| **Total Logical Storage** | $S_{logical} = F$ | Unreplicated size seen by the user. |
| **Storage Overhead Ratio** | $O = \dfrac{R - 1}{1} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of redundant storage. |
| **Effective Throughput** | $T_{eff} = B \times N_{nodes}$ | Parallelism benefit across $N_{nodes}$ DataNodes. |
| **Block Scan Time** | $t_{scan} = \dfrac{B}{BW_{disk}}$ | $BW_{disk}$ = disk bandwidth (~100 MB/s for HDD). |
| **Default Scan Time (128 MB / 100 MB/s)** | $t \approx 1.28$ seconds | Reference benchmark for KTU numericals. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The ceiling function in $N = \lceil F/B \rceil$ is the most-tested formula in board exams. Example: A 256 MB file with 128 MB block size → $N = \lceil 256/128 \rceil = 2$ blocks. A 300 MB file → $N = \lceil 300/128 \rceil = 3$ blocks (the last block holds only 44 MB).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloudera / Hortonworks Hadoop clusters** use HDFS blocks as the foundation for **Hive, Pig, Spark, and HBase** data lakes.
- **AWS S3** abstracts blocks differently but uses a similar chunked storage model internally.
- **Google File System (GFS)** — the precursor to HDFS — pioneered the 64 MB chunk (block) concept.
- **Facebook's Haystack** uses block-level storage for petabyte-scale photo archives.
- **Netflix** uses block-based storage in S3 for its 15+ PB content library.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Number of Blocks for a Given File

**Problem Statement:** A log file of size **750 MB** is to be stored in HDFS with a block size of **128 MB** and a replication factor of **3**. Calculate:
1. The number of blocks.
2. The total physical storage consumed.
3. The total number of replicas across the cluster.
4. The storage overhead percentage.

**Step 1: Number of Blocks Calculation**

The number of blocks $N$ required to store a file of size $F$ with a block size of $B$ is given by the **ceiling division**:

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil
$$

Substituting $F = 750$ MB and $B = 128$ MB:

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{750}{128} \right\rceil
$$

Performing the division:

$$
\frac{750}{128} = 5.859375
$$

Applying the ceiling function (rounding up to the next whole number):

$$
N = \left\lceil 5.859375 \right\rceil = 6
$$

Therefore, the file is split into **6 blocks**. The first 5 blocks are full (128 MB each), and the 6th block is partial.

**Step 2: Verify Block Distribution**

Let us verify the total size of all blocks:

$$
S_{blocks} = 5 \times 128 + (750 - 5 \times 128)
$$

Calculating the partial block:

$$
S_{partial} = 750 - 640 = 110 \text{ MB}
$$

Reassembling the total:

$$
S_{blocks} = 640 + 110 = 750 \text{ MB} \checkmark
$$

This matches the original file size, confirming correctness.

**Step 3: Total Physical Storage with Replication**

With replication factor $R = 3$, the total physical storage is:

$$
S_{total} = F \times R
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
S_{total} = 750 \times 3 = 2250 \text{ MB}
$$

Converting to gigabytes for clarity:

$$
S_{total} = \frac{2250}{1024} \approx 2.198 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4: Total Number of Replicas**

Each logical block is replicated $R - 1 = 2$ additional times (since one copy is the original). Thus, the total number of block copies stored on DataNodes is:

$$
C = N \times R
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
C = 6 \times 3 = 18 \text{ block copies}
$$

**Step 5: Storage Overhead Percentage**

The overhead due to replication is:

$$
O = \frac{S_{total} - F}{F} \times 100\%
$$

Calculating the redundant storage:

$$
S_{redundant} = 2250 - 750 = 1500 \text{ MB}
$$

Applying the percentage formula:

$$
O = \frac{1500}{750} \times 100\% = 200\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Storing the 750 MB file with $R = 3$ consumes **3× the original size**, meaning **200% overhead** is added. This redundancy is the price of fault tolerance in HDFS.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Scenarios

| File Size (MB) | Block Size (MB) | Rep. Factor | No. of Blocks | Total Storage (MB) | Overhead % |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 256 | 128 | 3 | 2 | 768 | 200% |
| 300 | 128 | 3 | 3 | 900 | 200% |
| 512 | 64 | 2 | 8 | 1024 | 100% |
| 1000 | 128 | 3 | 8 | 3000 | 200% |
| 1024 (1 GB) | 256 | 1 | 4 | 1024 | 0% |
| 1500 | 128 | 3 | 12 | 4500 | 200% |

### 3.3 Throughput Derivation (Parallel Read Scenario)

If a 1 GB file (1024 MB) with 128 MB blocks is read by a MapReduce job across **8 DataNodes** in parallel, the effective throughput is:

$$
T_{eff} = B \times N_{nodes}
$$

Assuming each DataNode can read its assigned block at the disk bandwidth $BW_{disk} = 100$ MB/s:

$$
T_{eff} = 100 \text{ MB/s} \times 8 = 800 \text{ MB/s}
$$

Total time to read the entire file in parallel:

$$
t_{parallel} = \frac{1024}{800} = 1.28 \text{ seconds}
$$

Compared to sequential read on a single node:

$$
t_{seq} = \frac{1024}{100} = 10.24 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Speedup achieved:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{t_{seq}}{t_{parallel}} = \frac{10.24}{1.28} = 8\times
$$

This 8× speedup directly demonstrates the **parallelism advantage** of block-based distributed storage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HDFS Block Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a file is split into blocks, replicated, and distributed across a Hadoop cluster with rack-aware placement.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientApp[Client Application] --> writeReq[1 Write Request to NameNode]
    writeReq --> nameNode[2 NameNode Allocates Block IDs]
    nameNode --> pipeline[3 NameNode Returns DataNode Pipeline]
    pipeline --> dn1Local[4a DataNode 1 Local Rack]
    pipeline --> dn2Rack2[4b DataNode 2 Different Rack]
    pipeline --> dn3Rack2[4c DataNode 3 Same Rack as DN2]

    dn1Local --> ack1[5a Ack Sent to Client]
    dn2Rack2 --> dn3Rack2[5b Internal Replica Transfer]
    dn3Rack2 --> ack1
    ack1 --> metadata[6 NameNode Updates FSImage and EditLog]
    metadata --> blockReport[7 Periodic Block Reports Every 6 Hours]

    style clientApp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style nameNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style dn1Local fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style dn2Rack2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style dn3Rack2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
```

### 4.2 Block-to-File Mapping Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Input_File[Input File F = 750 MB]
        f1[Chunk 1 128 MB]
        f2[Chunk 2 128 MB]
        f3[Chunk 3 128 MB]
        f4[Chunk 4 128 MB]
        f5[Chunk 5 128 MB]
        f6[Chunk 6 110 MB Partial]
    end

    subgraph Logical_Blocks[Logical HDFS Blocks N = 6]
        b1[Block B1 128 MB]
        b2[Block B2 128 MB]
        b3[Block B3 128 MB]
        b4[Block B4 128 MB]
        b5[Block B5 128 MB]
        b6[Block B6 110 MB]
    end

    subgraph Replicas_R3[Physical Replicas R = 3]
        r1[Replica Set 1 3x]
        r2[Replica Set 2 3x]
        r3[Replica Set 3 3x]
        r4[Replica Set 4 3x]
        r5[Replica Set 5 3x]
        r6[Replica Set 6 3x]
    end

    f1 --> b1 --> r1
    f2 --> b2 --> r2
    f3 --> b3 --> r3
    f4 --> b4 --> r4
    f5 --> b5 --> r5
    f6 --> b6 --> r6

    style Input_File fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000
    style Logical_Blocks fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000
    style Replicas_R3 fill:#F08080,stroke:#000
```

### 4.3 Block Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Allocated: Client requests block
    Allocated --> Writing: DataNode receives packets
    Writing --> Written: Block full or stream closed
    Written --> Replicated: Replicas confirmed on pipeline
    Replicated --> Active: Block report sent to NameNode
    Active --> Active: Heartbeat every 3 seconds
    Active --> Corrupted: Checksum mismatch detected
    Corrupted --> Deleted: Re-replicated from healthy copy
    Active --> UnderReplicated: DataNode failure
    UnderReplicated --> Replicated: NameNode triggers re-replication
    Replicated --> [*]: File deleted by user
    Corrupted --> [*]: Manual cleanup
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define an HDFS block. What is the default block size in Hadoop 2.x and why is it so large compared to traditional file systems?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **HDFS block** is the smallest unit of data that Hadoop's distributed file system reads or writes. It is a **logical abstraction** managed by the NameNode, decoupled from physical disk blocks. The **default block size in Hadoop 2.x is 128 MB**, significantly larger than the typical 4 KB disk block.

**Why 128 MB?** (1.5 Marks)
- Reduces the **metadata burden** on the NameNode (a 1 GB file needs only 8 block entries instead of 262,144).
- Minimizes **disk seek time** during sequential reads, which dominates block transfer time for large files.
- Aligns with Big Data workloads (terabyte-scale files) where high **streaming throughput** is more critical than low-latency random access.
- Optimizes **MapReduce** task scheduling, since each block is typically assigned to one mapper.

*(Allocation: 1 Mark for definition, 2 Marks for justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between a logical HDFS block and a physical disk block. Why is this distinction important in Big Data systems?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | HDFS Block (Logical) | Disk Block (Physical) |
|---|---|---|
| **Managed by** | Hadoop NameNode | Operating System |
| **Default size** | **128 MB** | 4 KB – 8 KB |
| **Visibility** | Application layer | Hardware layer |
| **Purpose** | Distributed storage abstraction | Physical sector allocation |

**Importance (1.5 Marks):**
This distinction enables Hadoop to provide a **uniform storage interface** across heterogeneous hardware. Applications are shielded from physical storage details, allowing seamless scaling across thousands of commodity machines. It also enables **data locality optimization** in MapReduce by moving computation to the block location rather than the other way around.

*(Allocation: 1.5 Marks for table, 1.5 Marks for importance.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **concept of HDFS blocks** with a neat diagram. Discuss the **benefits** of using a block-based storage abstraction in Hadoop. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Hadoop cluster stores a **2.5 GB** log file with a **block size of 256 MB** and a **replication factor of 3**.
- Calculate the **number of blocks**.
- Calculate the **total physical storage** in GB.
- If a DataNode fails and 2 blocks need to be **re-replicated**, what is the **additional storage cost**?
- Comment on the **rack-aware block placement** strategy. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept of HDFS Blocks (7 Marks):**

HDFS splits every file into fixed-size **logical blocks** (default 128 MB in Hadoop 2.x). These blocks are stored on **DataNodes** and managed by the **NameNode**, which maintains the block-to-DataNode mapping in its metadata.

**Diagram (3 Marks):**

```
File (2.5 GB)
  |
  +---> Block 1 (256 MB) ---> [DataNode A] [DataNode B] [DataNode C]
  +---> Block 2 (256 MB) ---> [DataNode D] [DataNode E] [DataNode F]
  +---> ... (and so on)
  +---> Block 10 (250 MB) ---> [DataNode X] [DataNode Y] [DataNode Z]
```

**Benefits (4 Marks):**
1. **Parallelism** — MapReduce can process multiple blocks simultaneously on different nodes.
2. **Fault Tolerance** — Replicated blocks (default $R = 3$) survive hardware failures.
3. **Scalability** — File size can exceed any single machine's disk capacity.
4. **Load Balancing** — Hadoop Balancer redistributes blocks evenly across DataNodes.
5. **Storage Transparency** — Applications are unaware of physical storage topology.
6. **Data Locality** — Computation is moved to the data (block location) to minimize network I/O.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $F = 2.5$ GB $= 2560$ MB, $B = 256$ MB, $R = 3$.

**Step 1: Number of Blocks**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2560}{256} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 10 \right\rceil = 10 \text{ blocks}
$$

The first 9 blocks are full (256 MB each), and the 10th block is partial:

$$
S_{partial} = 2560 - 9 \times 256 = 2560 - 2304 = 256 \text{ MB}
$$

So the 10th block is also a **full 256 MB block** — the file exactly fits 10 blocks.

**Step 2: Total Physical Storage**

$$
S_{total} = F \times R = 2560 \times 3 = 7680 \text{ MB}
$$

Converting to GB:

$$
S_{total} = \frac{7680}{1024} = 7.5 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 3: Additional Storage for Re-replication**

When 2 blocks are re-replicated (to maintain $R = 3$ after DataNode failure), each block needs **2 additional replicas** (since 1 of 3 is now missing, re-replication restores to 3 — but the question asks for the cost of the extra copies):

$$
S_{extra} = 2 \text{ blocks} \times 2 \text{ extra copies} \times 256 \text{ MB} = 1024 \text{ MB} = 1 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4: Rack-Aware Placement (2 Marks)**

The default HDFS rack-aware strategy:
- **Replica 1:** Local node (or random node if client is external).
- **Replica 2:** Different rack from Replica 1.
- **Replica 3:** Same rack as Replica 2, different node.

This ensures that **at least one replica survives a complete rack failure** while keeping two replicas on the same rack for **low-latency intra-rack reads**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **block placement policy** in HDFS with a suitable diagram. Explain how **rack awareness** improves fault tolerance and read performance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Hadoop cluster has **5 DataNodes** distributed across **2 racks** (Rack 1: DN1, DN2, DN3; Rack 2: DN4, DN5). A **640 MB** file is stored with a **block size of 128 MB** and **replication factor 3**.
- Determine the **number of blocks** and the **block distribution** across racks.
- Calculate the **storage overhead**.
- If the **scan time per block** is **1.28 seconds** on a single DataNode, calculate the **total time to read the entire file sequentially** versus **in parallel** across all 5 DataNodes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Placement Policy (7 Marks):**

HDFS uses a **rack-aware replica placement policy** to balance fault tolerance and bandwidth. When a client writes a new block:

- **Replica 1:** Placed on the **local DataNode** (if the client is a DataNode) or a randomly chosen DataNode in the cluster.
- **Replica 2:** Placed on a DataNode in a **different rack** from Replica 1.
- **Replica 3:** Placed on a **different DataNode in the same rack as Replica 2**.

**Diagram (3 Marks):**

```
Rack 1                    Rack 2
+--------+                +--------+
| DN1    |  <-- Replica 1 | DN4    |  <-- Replica 2
| DN2    |                | DN5    |  <-- Replica 3
| DN3    |                +--------+
+--------+
```

**Fault Tolerance (2 Marks):**
- A single DataNode failure does not cause data loss (2 remaining replicas exist).
- Even a **complete rack failure** (power or network outage) does not lose data because Replica 1 is on a different rack.

**Read Performance (2 Marks):**
- Two replicas on the same rack enable **intra-rack high-bandwidth reads**.
- The NameNode preferentially reads from the **closest replica** to the client, reducing network latency.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $F = 640$ MB, $B = 128$ MB, $R = 3$, $N_{nodes} = 5$, $t_{scan} = 1.28$ s.

**Step 1: Number of Blocks**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{640}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 5 \right\rceil = 5 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 2: Block Distribution Across Racks**

Each block has 3 replicas. Following the rack-aware policy:

- **Replica 1:** DN1, DN2, DN3 (Rack 1) — assigned to one of the 3 nodes in Rack 1.
- **Replica 2:** DN4 or DN5 (Rack 2) — different rack.
- **Replica 3:** The other DataNode in Rack 2 (or the remaining one in Rack 1, per the policy).

Total replicas stored: $5 \times 3 = 15$ block copies across 5 DataNodes.

**Step 3: Storage Overhead**

$$
S_{total} = 640 \times 3 = 1920 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
\text{Overhead} = \frac{1920 - 640}{640} \times 100\% = 200\%
$$

**Step 4: Sequential Read Time**

$$
t_{seq} = N \times t_{scan} = 5 \times 1.28 = 6.4 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5: Parallel Read Time (5 DataNodes)**

Since there are 5 blocks and 5 DataNodes, each DataNode reads exactly 1 block:

$$
t_{parallel} = 1 \times 1.28 = 1.28 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Speedup:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{6.4}{1.28} = 5\times
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 Marks):** Students often write $N = F / B$ without the ceiling function. Always use $\lceil F/B \rceil$. Example: 300 MB file with 128 MB blocks → 3 blocks, NOT 2.34.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 Marks):** Confusing logical storage (file size) with physical storage (file size × replication factor). The NameNode stores logical metadata; the DataNodes store physical replicas.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1.5 Marks):** Forgetting to mention **rack-awareness** in fault-tolerance questions. The examiner specifically looks for cross-rack replica placement.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–1 Mark):** Not stating the **default HDFS block size is 128 MB** explicitly. The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates this exact figure.
> - **Common Mistake 5 (–1 Mark):** Reporting parallel read time as $N \times t_{scan}$ instead of $t_{scan}$ (when there are enough nodes to read all blocks simultaneously).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Board Exam**

- ⭐ **HDFS Block Size (Hadoop 2.x+):** **128 MB** | **Hadoop 1.x:** 64 MB
- ⭐ **Default Replication Factor:** **3** (configurable via `dfs.replication` in `hdfs-site.xml`)
- ⭐ **Number of Blocks Formula:** $N = \lceil F / B \rceil$ — **always use ceiling**
- ⭐ **Total Physical Storage:** $S_{total} = F \times R$
- ⭐ **Storage Overhead %:** $O = (R - 1) \times 100\%$
- ⭐ **Logical vs Physical Block:** HDFS block is logical (software-managed), disk block is physical (hardware-managed)
- ⭐ **Rack-Aware Placement:** Replica 1 = local node, Replica 2 = different rack, Replica 3 = same rack as Replica 2
- ⭐ **Block Report Interval:** **6 hours** (DataNode → NameNode)
- ⭐ **Heartbeat Interval:** **3 seconds** (DataNode → NameNode)
- ⭐ **Block Scanner:** Verifies checksum integrity; default scan period is **3 weeks**
- ⭐ **Parallelism Benefit:** $N$ blocks read on $N$ DataNodes → $N\times$ speedup
- ⭐ **Fault Tolerance:** Cluster can tolerate $R - 1$ DataNode failures per block
- ⭐ **Under-Replicated Blocks:** Triggered automatically by NameNode when DataNode heartbeats fail
- ⭐ **Over-Replicated Blocks:** NameNode removes excess replicas to match $R$
- ⭐ **Corrupted Blocks:** Detected via checksum mismatch; re-replicated from healthy copy
- ⭐ **HDFS Block Access:** Sequential reads (high throughput) — NOT optimized for random access
- ⭐ **Key Config File:** `hdfs-site.xml` (parameters: `dfs.block.size`, `dfs.replication`, `dfs.namenode.name.dir`)
- ⭐ **Block Size Justification:** Reduces NameNode metadata, minimizes seek time, maximizes streaming throughput
- ⭐ **Distributed File Systems Using Blocks:** HDFS, GFS (Google), HBase internal HFiles, S3 (internally), Ceph RADOS
