> **Final KTU Mantra:** "A file is logically one entity, but physically many blocks. Master the ceiling function, and you master the block calculation."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Namenodes and Data nodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Namenodes and Data nodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)**, the architecture follows a classic **Master–Slave (or Leader–Follower) topology**. The two principal daemons that constitute the storage backbone of HDFS are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Namenode (Master Daemon):** The Namenode is the central authority and metadata server of HDFS. It maintains the directory tree of the entire file system, tracks the mapping of file blocks to DataNodes, and stores critical metadata such as file names, permissions, block locations, and replication factors. It operates on two persistent files: the **fsimage** (checkpoint of filesystem metadata) and the **edit logs** (record of every modification).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Datanode (Slave/Worker Daemon):** DataNodes are the worker nodes responsible for the physical storage of HDFS data blocks. They store the actual business data, serve read/write requests from clients, and perform block creation, deletion, and replication as instructed by the Namenode. They periodically send **heartbeat signals** and **Block Reports** to the Namenode to confirm their liveness and storage state.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Library Analogy — Making HDFS Memorable**
>
> Imagine a massive university library containing **millions of books**:
>
> * **Namenode = The Chief Librarian's Index Card Cabinet.** The librarian doesn't carry the books; instead, they maintain a perfect index card system that says: *"Book X, page ranges 1–300, located on Floor 3, Shelf B; Book X, page ranges 301–600, located on Floor 1, Shelf D."* If a student wants a book, the librarian consults the index and tells them where to go. If a shelf collapses, the librarian dispatches a worker to **photocopy the book** to another shelf. The index card cabinet is the **brain** of the system.
>
> * **Datanode = The Storage Shelves (Warehouse Workers).** These are the actual physical shelves that hold the books. Each shelf sends a *"Yes, I'm still here and well!"* signal (heartbeat) to the librarian every few seconds. They also report which books they currently hold (block report). If a worker fails to report, the librarian assumes the shelf is damaged and arranges for replacements from copies on other shelves.
>
> This single analogy explains **metadata management**, **block storage**, **heartbeats**, and **fault tolerance** in one mental image.

### 1.3 Key Architectural Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the **industry-standard and KTU-recommended default values** that examiners frequently test:

* **Default HDFS Block Size:** **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x and 3.x). (Was **64 MB** in Hadoop 1.x).
* **Default Replication Factor:** **3** copies of every block.
* **Default Heartbeat Interval:** **3 seconds**.
* **Namenode Timeout for Dead DataNode:** **10 minutes (600 seconds)** of missed heartbeats.
* **fsimage** + **Edit Logs** = Namenode persistent state stored typically in `${dfs.namenode.name.dir}`.
* **Rack Awareness Script:** Default factor of **10** for replica placement across racks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of the Namenode — Step-by-Step

The Namenode executes the following responsibilities in a strict order during any client interaction:

1. **Client Request Reception:** A client (e.g., HDFS API, `hdfs dfs -put`) contacts the Namenode via RPC.
2. **Metadata Lookup/Update:** The Namenode consults its in-memory metadata tree (built from `fsimage` + applied `edits`).
3. **Block Allocation Decision:** For writes, Namenode selects a *write pipeline* of DataNodes based on:
   * Rack awareness policy.
   * Current DataNode load (free space).
   * Health status from latest heartbeats.
4. **Permission & Quota Check:** Validates user authentication, file permissions, and storage quotas.
5. **Lease Assignment:** Grants a write lease to the client, ensuring only one writer per file.
6. **Response Dispatch:** Sends the list of DataNodes (the pipeline) to the client for direct data transfer.
7. **Edit Log Append:** Every metadata change is appended to `edits` for crash recovery.
8. **Checkpoint Trigger:** Periodically (every 1 hour or 1 million transactions by default), the **Secondary Namenode** (or **Checkpoint Node / Standby Namenode** in HA mode) merges `fsimage` + `edits` into a new `fsimage`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Secondary Namenode" is a Misnomer:** A common KTU pitfall — it is **NOT a hot standby**. It only assists in checkpointing. True High Availability (HA) is provided by the **Standby Namenode** (Hadoop 2.x+).

### 2.2 Operational Logic of the DataNode — Step-by-Step

1. **Daemon Startup:** Reads its storage directories (`dfs.datanode.data.dir`), registers with the Namenode via RPC.
2. **Block Storage:** Divides received data into **HDFS blocks** (default 128 MB) and writes them to local disk.
3. **Heartbeat Loop:** Sends a heartbeat to Namenode **every 3 seconds** containing:
   * DataNode ID & host info.
   * Disk capacity & remaining space.
   * Active block counts.
4. **Block Report:** Sends a complete inventory of all HDFS blocks it holds **every 6 hours** (default `dfs.blockreport.intervalMsec` = 21600000 ms).
5. **Pipeline Participation:** Joins the data pipeline for block writes and replication.
6. **Command Execution:** Receives commands from Namenode such as:
   * `BLOCKRECEIVED` (acknowledge receipt)
   * `TRANSFER` (copy block to another DataNode for rebalancing)
   * `DELETE` (remove corrupted/over-replicated block)
   * `SHUTDOWN` (graceful shutdown)
7. **Failure Detection:** If Namenode receives **no heartbeat for 10 minutes**, it marks the DataNode as **dead** and initiates **re-replication** of all blocks that were stored on it.

### 2.3 The Heartbeat Protocol — Visual Logic Flow

$$
\text{Heartbeat} \rightarrow \text{Every 3s} \rightarrow \text{Namenode receives} \rightarrow \text{Updates cluster topology}
$$

$$
\text{No Heartbeat for } 600s \rightarrow \text{DataNode Declared DEAD} \rightarrow \text{Re-replication triggered}
$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Standard Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of HDFS Blocks | $N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{File Size (MB)}}{\text{Block Size (MB)}} \right\rceil$ | Result is dimensionless integer | Capacity planning, cluster sizing |
| Total Physical Storage | $S_{total} = \text{File Size} \times \text{Replication Factor (RF)}$ | Megabytes / GB / TB | Disk procurement & cost estimation |
| Replication Storage per Block | $S_{block} = \text{Block Size} \times \text{RF}$ | MB (with RF=3 → 384 MB per block) | Rack-wise distribution |
| DataNode Timeout | $T_{timeout} = \text{Heartbeat Interval} \times \text{Miss Limit}$ | **3s × 200 = 600s = 10 min** | Fault detection tuning |
| Block Report Frequency | $f_{report} = \dfrac{1}{T_{report}}$ | Default: 1 per 6 hours | Metadata consistency window |
| Effective Storage Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{RF} \times 100\%$ | RF=3 → **33.33%** | HDFS overhead analysis |
| Maximum Files per Directory | $\text{Default} = 10^6$ (Hadoop 2.3+) | Integer limit | Scalability constraint |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** When computing block counts, always remember the **last block** may be *smaller* than the block size (it is NOT padded). For a 200 MB file with 128 MB blocks: $N = \lceil 200/128 \rceil = 2$ blocks (128 MB + 72 MB).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Namenode–DataNode architecture is the foundation of every Big Data ecosystem used in industry: **Yahoo, Facebook (Meta), LinkedIn, Netflix** all run multi-thousand-node HDFS clusters. Understanding these daemons is essential for:

* **Cluster Administration:** Capacity planning (e.g., "We have 10 PB of raw data, how many DataNodes do we need?").
* **Fault Tolerance Engineering:** Designing disaster recovery procedures.
* **Performance Tuning:** Adjusting block size, replication factor, and heartbeat intervals.
* **Interview Mastery:** Hadoop/HDFS system design interviews at product companies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Block & Storage Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A financial analytics company stores a daily log file of size **2.4 GB** in HDFS. The HDFS is configured with a block size of **128 MB** and a replication factor of **3**. The cluster consists of **6 DataNodes**, each with **2 TB** of usable storage.
>
> **Compute:**
> (a) The number of HDFS blocks.
> (b) The total logical storage consumed (including replicas).
> (c) The total physical storage required.
> (d) The minimum number of DataNodes needed to hold all replicas (assume uniform distribution).
> (e) The effective storage efficiency.

#### Step (a) — Number of HDFS Blocks

We are given:
* File Size: $S = 2.4 \text{ GB} = 2.4 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 2457.6 \text{ MB}$
* Block Size: $B = 128 \text{ MB}$

Applying the formula:

$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2457.6 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil
$$

Performing the division:

$$
\frac{2457.6}{128} = 19.2
$$

Applying the ceiling function:

$$
N_{blocks} = \lceil 19.2 \rceil = \mathbf{20 \text{ blocks}}
$$

**Last block size verification:** 19 full blocks × 128 MB = 2432 MB. Remaining = 2457.6 − 2432 = **25.6 MB** (last block). ✓

#### Step (b) — Total Logical Storage (with Replication)

$$
S_{total} = S \times RF = 2457.6 \text{ MB} \times 3 = \mathbf{7372.8 \text{ MB}} \approx \mathbf{7.2 \text{ GB}}
$$

#### Step (c) — Total Physical Storage Required

Physical storage = Logical storage (replicas occupy real disk space):

$$
S_{physical} = 7372.8 \text{ MB} \approx 7.2 \text{ GB}
$$

#### Step (d) — Minimum DataNodes Required

For fault tolerance, no two replicas of a block should be on the same DataNode (for RF=3, ideally on 2 racks with 1 rack holding 2 replicas). The minimum safe DataNode count for RF=3 is **3** (one replica per node, on different racks ideally).

Given 6 DataNodes are available, all 20 × 3 = 60 replica copies will be distributed across them.

Average replicas per DataNode:

$$
R_{avg} = \frac{N_{blocks} \times RF}{\text{DataNode Count}} = \frac{20 \times 3}{6} = \mathbf{10 \text{ replicas per DataNode}}
$$

Average storage per DataNode:

$$
\text{Storage}_{DN} = \frac{S_{total}}{\text{DataNode Count}} = \frac{7372.8 \text{ MB}}{6} = \mathbf{1228.8 \text{ MB} \approx 1.2 \text{ GB per DataNode}}
$$

This is well within the 2 TB capacity per DataNode. ✓

#### Step (e) — Storage Efficiency

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{RF} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{3} \times 100\% = \mathbf{33.33\%}
$$

This means **66.67% of raw HDFS storage is overhead** consumed by replication for fault tolerance.

### 3.2 Heartbeat Failure Detection — Time-Series Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** In a 12-node HDFS cluster, one DataNode suddenly crashes at $t = 0$. Given default heartbeat configuration, determine:
> (a) When will the Namenode declare it dead?
> (b) If 4 blocks (each 128 MB) were stored on that node, what is the volume of re-replication required?

**Solution:**

(a) With heartbeat interval = 3 s and timeout multiplier = 200:

$$
T_{detection} = 3 \text{ s} \times 200 = \mathbf{600 \text{ seconds} = 10 minutes}}
$$

(b) Re-replication volume:

$$
V_{replicate} = 4 \text{ blocks} \times 128 \text{ MB} \times (RF - 1) = 4 \times 128 \times 2 = \mathbf{1024 \text{ MB} = 1 \text{ GB}}
$$

Wait — this assumes the crashed node held exactly 1 replica of each block. The Namenode re-replicates only the *missing* replica to bring each block back to RF=3. If 1 copy is lost per block:

$$
V_{replicate} = 4 \times 128 \times 1 = \mathbf{512 \text{ MB}}
$$

**Correction:** Re-replication creates *replacement* copies to restore RF. If 1 of 3 replicas is lost, 1 new replica is needed. So:

$$
V_{replicate} = 4 \text{ blocks} \times 128 \text{ MB} \times 1 = \mathbf{512 \text{ MB}}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Namenode vs. DataNode

| Attribute | Namenode | DataNode |
|---|---|---|
| **Role** | Master / Coordinator | Slave / Worker |
| **Stores** | Metadata (fsimage, edits) | Actual data blocks |
| **Count per cluster** | **1** (or 2 in HA mode) | **Hundreds to thousands** |
| **RAM requirement** | Very high (metadata in memory) | Moderate (block cache) |
| **Disk requirement** | Small (GB) | Massive (TB–PB) |
| **Failure impact** | **Catastrophic** (entire cluster down) | Tolerable (re-replication) |
| **Communication** | Receives heartbeats & block reports | Sends heartbeats & block reports |
| **Handles RPCs for** | All metadata operations | Block I/O operations |
| **Failure recovery** | Secondary/Standby Namenode + fsimage | Re-replication from peer DataNodes |
| **Stores file content?** | **No** | **Yes** |
| **Run on commodity hardware?** | **No** (needs high-RAM master node) | **Yes** |
| **Default port (Hadoop 3.x)** | **9870** (web UI) / **9000** (RPC) | **9864** (data transfer) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HDFS Master–Slave Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client1[Client Application - hdfs dfs put]
    Client2[Client Application - MapReduce Job]
    
    subgraph MasterLayer["MASTER LAYER - Namenode"]
        NNMeta[In-Memory Metadata Tree]
        FSImage[fsimage File - Checkpoint]
        EditLog[Edit Log - edits file]
        HAstandby[Standby Namenode - HA mode]
        SecondNN[Secondary Namenode - Checkpoint Helper]
    end
    
    subgraph SlaveLayer1["SLAVE LAYER - DataNode Rack 1"]
        DN1[DataNode 1 - 128MB blocks]
        DN2[DataNode 2 - 128MB blocks]
    end
    
    subgraph SlaveLayer2["SLAVE LAYER - DataNode Rack 2"]
        DN3[DataNode 3 - 128MB blocks]

    end

        DN6[DataNode 6 - 128MB blocks]
    end
    
    Client1 -->|1 RPC Metadata Request| NNMeta
    Client2 -->|1 RPC Metadata Request| NNMeta
    NNMeta -->|2 Return DataNode Pipeline| Client1
    NNMeta -->|2 Return DataNode Pipeline| Client2
    
    Client1 -->|3 Direct Data Write Pipeline| DN1
    Client1 -->|3 Direct Data Write Pipeline| DN3
    Client1 -->|3 Direct Data Write Pipeline| DN5
    
    DN1 -->|Heartbeat every 3s| NNMeta
    DN2 -->|Heartbeat every 3s| NNMeta
    DN3 -->|Heartbeat every 3s| NNMeta
    DN4 -->|Heartbeat every 3s| NNMeta
    DN5 -->|Heartbeat every 3s| NNMeta

    DN1 -->|Block Report every 6h| NNMeta
    DN2 -->|Block Report every 6h| NNMeta

    NNMeta -->|Periodic Checkpoint| SecondNN
    NNMeta -->|HA Sync| HAstandby
    EditLog -->|Merged into| FSImage
    
    style NNMeta fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,color:#fff

    style Client2 fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Heartbeat and Failure Detection Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Cluster Running]) --> HB1[DataNode sends Heartbeat]
    HB1 --> Decision{Heartbeat received?}
    Decision -->|Yes within 3s| Update[Update Last Seen Timestamp]
    Update --> HB1
    Decision -->|No - 10 min elapsed| MarkDead[Mark DataNode as DECOMMISSIONED DEAD]
    MarkDead --> InitRep[Identify Under-Replicated Blocks]
    InitRep --> SelectDN[Select Target DataNode for Replica]
    SelectDN --> CopyBlock[Copy Block from Healthy Replica]
    CopyBlock --> Verify{RF=3 Restored?}
    Verify -->|Yes| Done([Cluster Healthy Again])
    Verify -->|No| SelectDN
    
    style Start fill:#90ee90,stroke:#060,color:#000
    style HB1 fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#003,color:#fff
    style Decision fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#a00,color:#000
    style MarkDead fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,color:#fff
    style InitRep fill:#ff9f43,stroke:#a00,color:#000
    style SelectDN fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#003,color:#fff
    style CopyBlock fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#060,color:#000
    style Verify fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#a00,color:#000
    style Done fill:#90ee90,stroke:#060,color:#000
    style Update fill:#a29bfe,stroke:#303,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Block Replication Pipeline (Rack-Aware Placement)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    File[Input File - 256 MB] --> Split[Split into 2 HDFS Blocks of 128 MB each]
    
    Split --> B1[Block 1 - 128 MB]
    Split --> B2[Block 2 - 128 MB]
    
    B1 --> R1B1[Replica 1 - Rack 1 - DataNode 1]
    B1 --> R2B1[Replica 2 - Rack 2 - DataNode 3]
    B1 --> R3B1[Replica 3 - Rack 2 - DataNode 4]
    
    B2 --> R1B2[Replica 1 - Rack 1 - DataNode 2]
    B2 --> R2B2[Replica 2 - Rack 2 - DataNode 4]
    B2 --> R3B2[Replica 3 - Rack 3 - DataNode 5]
    
    style File fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#a00,color:#000
    style Split fill:#ff9f43,stroke:#a00,color:#000
    style B1 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#060,color:#000
    style B2 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#060,color:#000
    style R1B1 fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#003,color:#fff
    style R2B1 fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#003,color:#fff
    style R3B1 fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#003,color:#fff
    style R1B2 fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#003,color:#fff
    style R2B2 fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#003,color:#fff
    style R3B2 fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#003,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Note that the default rack-aware policy places one replica on a *different* rack from the other two, ensuring rack-level fault tolerance. This is critical for KTU 14-mark questions on replication strategy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of the Namenode in HDFS? Explain the functions of `fsimage` and `edit logs`.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Namenode** is the master daemon of HDFS that manages the file system namespace and regulates client access to files. It does not store actual data but maintains **metadata** such as file-to-block mapping, DataNode locations, permissions, and replication factors.

* **fsimage (2 marks credit):** It is a persistent checkpoint file containing the complete snapshot of the HDFS filesystem metadata at a specific point in time. It represents the "state of the world."
* **edit logs (1 mark credit):** It is an append-only log file that records every modification (create, delete, rename) made to the filesystem after the last fsimage checkpoint. On Namenode startup, edits are replayed onto fsimage to reconstruct current state.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the function of DataNodes in HDFS. How do they communicate with the Namenode?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**DataNodes** are the slave daemons that store the actual HDFS data blocks on local disk and serve read/write requests from clients (2 marks).

DataNodes communicate with the Namenode using two periodic mechanisms (1 mark):

1. **Heartbeats** — Sent every **3 seconds** to signal liveness and report disk capacity.
2. **Block Reports** — Sent every **6 hours** listing the inventory of all HDFS blocks stored on that DataNode.

If a DataNode fails to send a heartbeat for **10 minutes (600 seconds)**, the Namenode marks it as dead and initiates re-replication of its blocks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Schema Aligned]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the HDFS architecture. Describe the roles and responsibilities of Namenode and DataNode in detail. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A retail company stores a daily transaction log of size **5 GB** in HDFS. The HDFS configuration uses a block size of **128 MB** and a replication factor of **3**. The cluster has **8 DataNodes**, each with **5 TB** usable storage. Compute:
> 1. The number of HDFS blocks.
> 2. The total storage consumed with replication.
> 3. The storage efficiency.
> 4. Average storage used per DataNode. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer for Question A:**

**(a) HDFS Architecture (7 marks):**

HDFS follows a **Master-Slave architecture** with one Namenode (master) and multiple DataNodes (slaves).

**Namenode Responsibilities (3.5 marks):**
* Maintains **file system metadata** (directory tree, file names, permissions, block-to-DataNode mapping).
* Manages the **namespace** operations: open, close, rename files/directories.
* Decides **replication** of blocks as per the rack-aware policy.
* Receives **heartbeats and block reports** from all DataNodes.
* Stores two critical on-disk files: **fsimage** (checkpoint) and **edit logs** (incremental changes).
* Single point of failure (mitigated by Secondary/Standby Namenode in HA).

**DataNode Responsibilities (3.5 marks):**
* Store **actual data blocks** (default 128 MB each) on local file system.
* Serve **read/write requests** from HDFS clients.
* Send **heartbeat signals** every 3 seconds to Namenode.
* Send **block reports** every 6 hours listing all stored blocks.
* Perform **block creation, deletion, and replication** as instructed by Namenode.
* On failure (no heartbeat for 10 min), blocks are re-replicated from peer DataNodes.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 architecture diagram for visual credit.)*

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
* File size $S = 5 \text{ GB} = 5 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 5120 \text{ MB}$
* Block size $B = 128 \text{ MB}$
* Replication Factor $RF = 3$
* DataNode count $N_{DN} = 8$

**(i) Number of HDFS Blocks (1.5 marks):**

$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{5120}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 40.0 \rceil = \mathbf{40 \text{ blocks}}
$$

**(ii) Total Storage with Replication (2 marks):**

$$
S_{total} = S \times RF = 5120 \text{ MB} \times 3 = \mathbf{15360 \text{ MB} = 15 \text{ GB}}
$$

**(iii) Storage Efficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{RF} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{3} \times 100\% = \mathbf{33.33\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** 66.67% of total HDFS storage is consumed by replica overhead for fault tolerance.

**(iv) Average Storage per DataNode (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Storage}_{avg} = \frac{S_{total}}{N_{DN}} = \frac{15360 \text{ MB}}{8} = \mathbf{1920 \text{ MB} \approx 1.875 \text{ GB per DataNode}}
$$

This is well within the 5 TB capacity per DataNode, leaving ~4998 GB free.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Schema Aligned]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **heartbeat mechanism** in HDFS. What happens when a DataNode fails? Describe the **re-replication process** with a suitable example. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast the **Namenode, Secondary Namenode, and Standby Namenode**. When is each used? Discuss their role in **High Availability (HA)** architecture. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer for Question B:**

**(a) Heartbeat Mechanism & Re-replication (7 marks):**

**Heartbeat Protocol (3 marks):**
Every DataNode in HDFS sends a **heartbeat signal** to the Namenode every **3 seconds** (configurable via `dfs.heartbeat.interval`). The heartbeat contains:
* DataNode identity and host information.
* Disk usage statistics (total, used, free).
* Active block counts.
* Cluster topology info.

The Namenode uses heartbeats as a **liveness probe**. The Namenode maintains a `lastSeenTimestamp` for each DataNode. If no heartbeat is received within **10 minutes (600 seconds)** — computed as 200 missed heartbeats — the DataNode is declared **dead/decommissioned**.

**Failure Handling & Re-replication (4 marks):**

When a DataNode fails, the Namenode performs the following steps:

1. **Detection:** Heartbeat timeout triggers `DataNode.dead` flag.
2. **Block Inventory:** Namenode cross-references its metadata with the last known block list of the dead node.
3. **Under-Replication Identification:** For each block that had a replica on the dead node and is now below RF=3, the Namenode schedules re-replication.
4. **Target Selection:** A new DataNode is selected (preferring a different rack) to host the new replica.
5. **Block Transfer:** The Namenode instructs a healthy DataNode holding a replica to copy the block to the new target.
6. **Verification:** Once received, the block count for the file returns to RF=3, and the Namenode updates its metadata.

**Example:** A cluster has 5 DataNodes; DN3 fails. Block B47 (128 MB) had replicas on DN1, DN3, DN5. Now only 2 replicas remain. Namenode picks DN4 (different rack) and instructs DN1 to copy B47 to DN4. Re-replication completes; cluster is back to RF=3.

---

**(b) Namenode vs. Secondary Namenode vs. Standby Namenode (7 marks):**

| Feature | Namenode (Active/Primary) | Secondary Namenode | Standby Namenode (HA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Active metadata manager | Checkpoint helper only | Hot standby for failover |
| **Handles client requests?** | **Yes** | **No** | No (becomes active on failover) |
| **Updates fsimage?** | No (only edits) | **Yes** (merges edits+fsimage) | Yes (passively) |
| **Failure role** | Becomes unavailable if fails | Cannot replace Namenode | **Auto-failover replaces it** |
| **Hadoop version** | All versions | Hadoop 1.x (deprecated in 2.x+) | Hadoop 2.0+ |
| **Memory** | Full metadata in RAM | Same as Namenode | Full metadata in RAM |
| **Can serve reads?** | Yes | No | Yes (read-only in standby) |
| **High Availability?** | No (single point of failure) | Does not provide HA | **Provides true HA** |
| **Failover time** | N/A | N/A | ~30 seconds (auto) |

**Key Takeaway (2 marks):**

* The **Secondary Namenode** is a **misnomer** — it is not a backup. It only performs periodic checkpointing to prevent edit logs from growing unbounded. It cannot serve client requests or replace a failed Namenode.
* The **Standby Namenode** is a true **hot standby** in HA architecture. It maintains the same metadata via shared journaling (NFS or QJM) and uses **ZooKeeper** for leader election. On Namenode failure, it takes over within ~30 seconds, providing seamless High Availability.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Examiner Insights:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Secondary Namenode with Standby Namenode (–2 to –3 marks):** Many students write *"Secondary Namenode is the backup of Namenode."* This is **WRONG**. It is a checkpointing assistant. True backup is the **Standby Namenode** in HA mode.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Replicas in Storage Calculation (–2 marks):** When asked "total storage required," students often give only the file size and forget to multiply by the replication factor. Always: $S_{total} = S \times RF$.
>
> 3. **Wrong Block Count for Last Block (–1 mark):** The last block is *not padded* to 128 MB. A 200 MB file with 128 MB blocks = 2 blocks (128 + 72), NOT 2 blocks of 128 MB.
>
> 4. **Mis-stating Heartbeat Interval (–1 mark):** Default is **3 seconds**, not 5 or 10. And timeout is **10 minutes (200 missed heartbeats)**, not 5.
>
> 5. **Claiming Namenode Stores Data (–2 marks):** NEVER write that the Namenode stores actual file content. It stores only **metadata** in RAM + fsimage/edits on disk.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Diagram in 14-mark Answers (–2 marks):** A neat labeled architecture diagram is **mandatory** for full marks in 7-mark sub-questions on HDFS architecture.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Namenode & DataNode — Final 60-Second Revision:**
>
> * ☐ **Namenode = Master**; stores **metadata only** (NOT data).
> * ☐ **DataNode = Slave/Worker**; stores **actual HDFS data blocks**.
> * ☐ **Block size** default = **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+); was 64 MB in 1.x.
> * ☐ **Replication factor** default = **3** copies.
> * ☐ **Namenode persistent files:** `fsimage` (checkpoint) + `edit logs` (incremental).
> * ☐ **Heartbeat interval** = **3 seconds**; **DataNode timeout** = **10 minutes (600 s)**.
> * ☐ **Block report frequency** = **every 6 hours** (default).
> * ☐ **Rack awareness** = Replicas spread across racks (1 on one rack, 2 on another).
> * ☐ **Secondary Namenode** ≠ Standby Namenode. Secondary is a *checkpoint helper*; Standby is *true HA backup*.
> * ☐ **Storage formula:** $N_{blocks} = \lceil S/B \rceil$; $S_{total} = S \times RF$; $\eta = 1/RF \times 100\%$.
> * ☐ **Namenode failure = cluster down** (in non-HA); solved by **Standby Namenode + ZooKeeper**.
> * ☐ **DataNode failure** = re-replication only (cluster continues running).
> * ☐ **Hadoop 2.x+** uses **HA architecture** with Active/Standby Namenodes sharing edit logs via **QJM (Quorum Journal Manager)**.
> * ☐ **Default Namenode web UI port (Hadoop 3.x):** **9870**; **DataNode data transfer port:** **9864**.
> * ☐ **Maximum files per directory:** 1 million (Hadoop 2.3+ default limit).
> * ☐ **Last block is NOT padded** — it can be smaller than block size.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic File system Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Basic File system Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBADT504 – Big Data Analytics)** lab syllabus, **Module Experiment 2 – Basic File System Operations** refers to the practical, hands-on manipulation of the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** using its native command-line interface. HDFS is a Java-based, distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant file system designed to store very large files (typically in the **range of gigabytes to petabytes**) across multiple commodity machines in a Hadoop cluster. The experiment exposes the student to the canonical set of shell verbs (`-mkdir`, `-ls`, `-put`, `-get`, `-cat`, `-cp`, `-mv`, `-rm`, `-chmod`, `-chown`, `-du`, `-df`, `-stat`, `-setrep`) that collectively constitute the **HDFS Client API's CLI surface**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Under Outcome **CO2 – "Perform file system operations on HDFS and apply MapReduce programming model for big data processing"**, this experiment carries direct weightage for assessing the student's ability to *Apply* and *Execute* (Bloom Level 3).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Warehouse with Sticky Notes"

Imagine HDFS as a **giant, automated warehouse** spread across many buildings. The **NameNode** is the *reception desk* that holds a ledger (metadata) saying *exactly which box (block) is in which building (DataNode)*. The **DataNodes** are the actual *storage rooms*. When you (the client) ask the receptionist to **"put"** a parcel, the receptionist says, *"Send it to Building 3, Aisle 5"* — and the parcel is replicated to two other buildings automatically for safety. Every command you type (`hdfs dfs -put myfile.txt /data/`) is essentially you **talking to the receptionist** to manage parcels in this massive warehouse.

## 1.3 The HDFS Architecture in One Glance

| Component | Role | Default Quantity in a Lab |
|-----------|------|--------------------------|
| **NameNode** | Master — stores metadata, namespace image, edit log | **1** (Active) |
| **DataNode** | Slave — stores actual data blocks | **≥ 1** (typically 3) |
| **Secondary NameNode** | Checkpointing helper (NOT a failover node) | **1** |
| **Client** | The HDFS shell (`hdfs dfs …`) you invoke | Your terminal |
| **Block** | Minimum unit of read/write (default **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x+; **64 MB** in Hadoop 1.x) | Many per file |

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Replication Factor = 3**. Every block is stored on 3 different DataNodes by default. This is *why* HDFS is fault-tolerant — losing one DataNode does not lose data.

## 1.4 The Two Command Families Students Confuse

KTU examiners frequently test the distinction between:

1. **`hdfs dfs <args>`** — The newer, recommended syntax (works on any FS, not just HDFS).
2. **`hadoop fs <args>`** — The older, deprecated alias that internally calls `hdfs dfs`.

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU viva, if you write `hadoop dfs …` (with the extra `d`), you **lose 1 mark**. The correct legacy form is `hadoop fs`, not `hadoop dfs`.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Walkthrough — How an HDFS Command Executes

When you press <kbd>Enter</kbd> after `hdfs dfs -put localfile /hdfs/dir/`, the following sequence unfolds:

1. **Client Initialization** — The shell parses the verb, opens an RPC channel to the **NameNode** on port **9000** (default).
2. **Metadata Request** — The client asks the NameNode: *"Is `/hdfs/dir/` valid? Do I have write permission? Where should the blocks go?"*
3. **Pipeline Creation** — The NameNode returns a list of DataNodes (a *write pipeline*). For replication factor 3, this is typically `DN1 → DN2 → DN3`.
4. **Block Streaming** — The client splits `localfile` into blocks of **128 MB** and streams them through the pipeline. Each DataNode writes the block to its local disk and forwards it to the next.
5. **ACK Chain** — Upon successful storage, ACKs travel back: `DN3 → DN2 → DN1 → Client`.
6. **Metadata Commit** — The client informs the NameNode, which commits the file's metadata to the **fsimage** and **edits log**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Command Sheet (The "Cheat-Sheet" You Will Be Tested On)

The table below is the **canonical command set** KTU examiners expect a student to reproduce from memory in the lab record and viva.

| # | Command (full form) | Purpose / KTU Definition | Common Flags |
|---|--------------------|--------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | `hdfs dfs -mkdir /path` | Create a directory in HDFS (recursive: `-p`) | `-p` |
| 2 | `hdfs dfs -ls /path` | List files/dirs with size, owner, replication, block size, modified time | `-h` (human-readable), `-R` (recursive) |
| 3 | `hdfs dfs -put local src hdfs_dst` | **Upload** a local file to HDFS (alias: `-copyFromLocal`) | `-f` (overwrite) |
| 4 | `hdfs dfs -get hdfs_src local_dst` | **Download** from HDFS to local FS (alias: `-copyToLocal`) | `-f` |
| 5 | `hdfs dfs -cat /file` | Display file contents on stdout | — |
| 6 | `hdfs dfs -cp /src /dst` | Copy within HDFS | `-f` |
| 7 | `hdfs dfs -mv /src /dst` | Rename/move a file or dir | — |
| 8 | `hdfs dfs -rm /file` | Delete a file | `-r` (recursive for dirs), `-f` (force), `-skipTrash` |
| 9 | `hdfs dfs -rm -r /dir` | Recursively delete a directory | — |
| 10 | `hdfs dfs -chmod 755 /file` | Change POSIX permissions | `R` for recursive |
| 11 | `hdfs dfs -chown user:grp /file` | Change owner and group | `R` for recursive |
| 12 | `hdfs dfs -du -h /path` | Show disk usage of files/dirs | `-s` (summary), `-h` |
| 13 | `hdfs dfs -df -h /` | Show free/used space on the whole FS | `-h` |
| 14 | `hdfs dfs -count -q /path` | Show quota and file count | `-q`, `-v` |
| 15 | `hdfs dfs -stat "format" /file` | Show file statistics (e.g., `%b`=blocks, `%o`=block size, `%r`=replication) | — |
| 16 | `hdfs dfs -setrep -w 5 /file` | Change replication factor to 5, wait for completion | `-w` (wait) |
| 17 | `hdfs dfs -tail /file` | Show last 1 KB of a file (like Unix `tail`) | `-f` (follow) |
| 18 | `hdfs dfs -appendToFile local hdfs` | Append local content to an existing HDFS file | — |
| 19 | `hdfs dfs -getmerge /dir local` | Merge all files in HDFS dir into one local file | — |
| 20 | `hdfs dfs -expunge` | Empty the trash | — |

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet — Disk Usage Mathematics

For any file stored in HDFS, the **true disk consumption** differs from the logical file size. This is one of the most common numerical questions in KTU theory papers.

$$
\text{Actual Disk Used} = \lceil \frac{\text{File Size (bytes)}}{\text{Block Size (bytes)}} \rceil \times \text{Block Size} \times \text{Replication Factor}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | KTU Default |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| $\text{File Size}$ | Logical content size of the file | Varies |
| $\text{Block Size}$ | HDFS block size | **134,217,728 bytes (128 MiB)** |
| $\text{Replication Factor (RF)}$ | Number of block copies | **3** |
| $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ | Ceiling (round up to next integer) | — |

### 2.3.1 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Marked)

> **Problem:** A file of size **300 MB** is stored in HDFS with block size 128 MB and replication factor 3. Calculate: (i) Number of blocks, (ii) Total HDFS disk usage.

**Solution:**

$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = \lceil \frac{300}{128} \rceil = \lceil 2.34375 \rceil = 3 \text{ blocks}
$$

Block composition: $128 + 128 + 44 = 300$ MB ✓

$$
\text{Disk Used} = 3 \text{ blocks} \times 128 \text{ MB} \times 3 = \mathbf{1152 \text{ MB}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Even though the file is 300 MB, HDFS allocates a **full 128 MB block** for the last partial block. Always round **up**, never down. This wasted space in the last block is called *HDFS slack space* and is a common 1-mark concept question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of HDFS File Ops |
|--------|------------------------------|
| **Banking** | Storing transaction logs in 128 MB blocks, replicated 3× across DCs |
| **IoT** | Streaming sensor data into HDFS via Flume, then querying with Hive |
| **Healthcare** | Archiving DICOM imaging files (often > 100 MB) directly to HDFS |
| **Social Media Analytics** | Daily ingestion of clickstream logs in append-only mode (`-appendToFile`) |
| **Genomics** | Storing FASTA/VCF files that routinely exceed 1 TB |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping KTU Exam Marks to Concepts

The KTU 2024 lab evaluation for this experiment typically follows the rubric below. Understanding this distribution helps you prioritize your record write-up.

| Section of Lab Record | Marks Allocated (out of 20) | Bloom Level | KTU CO |
|----------------------|------------------------------|-------------|--------|
| Aim & Procedure | **2** | Understand | CO2 |
| Commands Executed (with output) | **6** | Apply | CO2 |
| Output Screenshots & Interpretation | **6** | Analyze | CO2 |
| Result & Inference | **3** | Evaluate | CO3 |
| Viva-voce | **3** | Remember/Apply | CO2 |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Command-Set Derivation — From User Intent to HDFS Internals

Below, each command is *derived* logically from the operational need. This trains the student to **reason** about the commands rather than memorize them blindly.

### 3.2.1 The `-put` Command (Upload)

**Step 1 — User Intent:** *"I have `sales_data.csv` on my Linux box at `/home/hadoop/`. I want it on HDFS at `/input/sales/`."*

**Step 2 — Verbal Construction of the Command:**

$$
\text{Command} = \underbrace{\texttt{hdfs dfs}}_{\text{client shell}} \; \underbrace{\texttt{-put}}_{\text{verb = upload}} \; \underbrace{\texttt{/home/hadoop/sales\_data.csv}}_{\text{LOCAL source}} \; \underbrace{\texttt{/input/sales/}}_{\text{HDFS destination}}
$$

**Step 3 — Full Execution Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{a. Client reads file} &\rightarrow \text{computes MD5 hash, splits into 128 MB chunks} \\
\text{b. RPC to NameNode} &\rightarrow \text{NameNode allocates block IDs (blk_1073741825, ...)} \\
\text{c. Pipeline returned} &\rightarrow \text{[DN2, DN5, DN8] (ordered by proximity)} \\
\text{d. Stream block 1} &\rightarrow \text{DN2 receives, writes, forwards to DN5} \\
\text{e. Stream block 2} &\rightarrow \text{similarly} \\
\text{f. ACKs returned} &\rightarrow \text{Client reports success to NameNode} \\
\text{g. NameNode commits} &\rightarrow \text{fsimage + edits log updated}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification Command:**

$$
\texttt{hdfs dfs -ls -h /input/sales/}
$$

Expected output:
```
Found 1 items
-rw-r--r--   3 hadoop supergroup    234.5 M  2024-09-15 10:23 /input/sales/sales_data.csv
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `3` in the second column confirms **replication factor = 3**.

### 3.2.2 The `-setrep` Command (Change Replication)

**Step 1 — User Intent:** *"I want this file backed up 5 times instead of 3."*

**Step 2 — Command Construction:**

$$
\texttt{hdfs dfs -setrep -w 5 /input/sales/sales\_data.csv}
$$

**Step 3 — Internal Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial state} &: \text{RF} = 3, \text{Block count} = B \\
\text{New state} &: \text{RF} = 5 \\
\text{Additional replicas per block} &= 5 - 3 = 2 \\
\text{Total new replica copies} &= 2 \times B \\
\text{NameNode schedules} &: 2B \text{ new block transfers in background}
\end{aligned}
$$

The `-w` flag causes the client to **block** until the NameNode reports all replicas placed. Without `-w`, the command returns immediately.

### 3.2.3 The `-du` and `-df` Commands (Disk Usage)

**Step 1 — `du` reports per-file/disk consumption:**

$$
\texttt{hdfs dfs -du -h /input/sales/}
$$

Output columns: `[disk\_size \; file\_size \; path]`

**Step 2 — `df` reports filesystem-level consumption:**

$$
\texttt{hdfs dfs -df -h /}
$$

Output columns: `[Size \; Used \; Available \; Use\%]`

**Step 3 — Theoretical relationship:**

$$
\text{Total Disk Used} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{files}}} \left( \left\lceil \frac{\text{size}_i}{\text{block\_size}} \right\rceil \times \text{block\_size} \times \text{RF}_i \right)
$$

where $N_{\text{files}}$ is the total number of files in the inspected path.

### 3.2.4 The `-rm` Command (Deletion) and Trash Semantics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite Question:** *"When you delete a file in HDFS, is it permanently gone?"* — **No!** It moves to the **trash** at `/user/hadoop/.Trash/Current/`. It is purged only when the trash is full or you run `hdfs dfs -expunge`.

**Step 1 — Soft delete (default):**

$$
\texttt{hdfs dfs -rm /input/sales/old\_data.csv}
$$

**Step 2 — Permanent delete (bypass trash):**

$$
\texttt{hdfs dfs -rm -skipTrash /input/sales/old\_data.csv}
$$

**Step 3 — Internal Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Default behavior} &: \text{move to } `/user/{user}/.Trash/Current/` \text{ with original timestamp} \\
\text{Retention} &: \text{6 hours (configurable via `fs.trash.interval`)} \\
\text{Skip-trash} &: \text{immediate unlink from NameNode namespace, blocks freed asynchronously}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Composite Experiment — Step-by-Step Lab Procedure

A typical KTU continuous-evaluation record for this experiment includes the following sequence. The student is expected to **write the command, the expected output, and a one-line interpretation**.

| Step | Command | Expected Output Snippet | Interpretation (write this in record) |
|------|---------|--------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| 1 | `hdfs dfs -mkdir -p /user/hadoop/lab2` | (no output on success) | Creates nested HDFS directory |
| 2 | `hdfs dfs -ls /` | Lists root, shows `/user` | Confirms HDFS is mounted |
| 3 | `echo "Hello HDFS" > /tmp/greet.txt` | (no output) | Creates local file |
| 4 | `hdfs dfs -put /tmp/greet.txt /user/hadoop/lab2/` | (no output on success) | Uploads to HDFS |
| 5 | `hdfs dfs -cat /user/hadoop/lab2/greet.txt` | `Hello HDFS` | Reads back content |
| 6 | `hdfs dfs -cp /user/hadoop/lab2/greet.txt /user/hadoop/lab2/copy.txt` | (no output) | Copies within HDFS |
| 7 | `hdfs dfs -mv /user/hadoop/lab2/copy.txt /user/hadoop/lab2/renamed.txt` | (no output) | Renames file |
| 8 | `hdfs dfs -chmod 600 /user/hadoop/lab2/renamed.txt` | (no output) | Sets rw------- permission |
| 9 | `hdfs dfs -chown hadoop:hadoop /user/hadoop/lab2/renamed.txt` | (no output) | Sets owner |
| 10 | `hdfs dfs -du -h /user/hadoop/lab2/` | `6 B  6 B  /user/hadoop/lab2/renamed.txt` | Shows 6-byte disk usage |
| 11 | `hdfs dfs -df -h /` | Shows overall FS usage | Reports cluster capacity |
| 12 | `hdfs dfs -setrep -w 2 /user/hadoop/lab2/greet.txt` | `… waiting for 2 replicas …` | Reduces replication |
| 13 | `hdfs dfs -rm /user/hadoop/lab2/renamed.txt` | `Deleted /user/hadoop/lab2/renamed.txt` | Moves to trash |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing `hdfs dfs -put` when the source is already in HDFS. The `-put` command is **strictly for local → HDFS** uploads. For HDFS → HDFS, use `-cp` or `-mv`. This is a recurring 1-mark deduction in valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDFS Read/Write Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLIENT["Client Machine Terminal"]
        CLI["hdfs dfs shell command"]
        JVM["Java HDFS Client API"]
    end

    subgraph MASTER["Master Node"]
        NN["NameNode - Port 9000"]
        META["Metadata: fsimage + edits log"]
        SNN["Secondary NameNode - checkpointing"]
    end

    subgraph SLAVES["Slave Nodes - DataNodes"]
        DN1["DataNode 1 - rack A"]
        DN2["DataNode 2 - rack B"]
        DN3["DataNode 3 - rack C"]
    end

    CLI -->|parses verb| JVM
    JVM -->|1 RPC metadata request| NN
    NN <-->|reads/writes| META
    JVM -->|2 block pipeline write| DN1
    DN1 -->|replicate| DN2
    DN2 -->|replicate| DN3
    DN3 -->|ACK chain reverse| JVM
    JVM -->|3 commit success| NN
    NN -.->|periodic checkpoint| SNN
```

## 4.2 HDFS File Block Distribution Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FILE["Logical View: user_data.csv - 300 MB"]
        F1["Block 1 - 128 MB"]
        F2["Block 2 - 128 MB"]
        F3["Block 3 - 44 MB actual / 128 MB allocated"]
    end

    subgraph DN1["DataNode 1"]
        A1["blk_A1 - 128 MB"]
        A2["blk_A2 - 128 MB"]
        A3["blk_A3 - 128 MB"]
    end

    subgraph DN2["DataNode 2"]
        B1["blk_B1 - 128 MB"]
        B2["blk_B2 - 128 MB"]
        B3["blk_B3 - 128 MB"]
    end

    subgraph DN3["DataNode 3"]
        C1["blk_C1 - 128 MB"]
        C2["blk_C2 - 128 MB"]
        C3["blk_C3 - 128 MB"]
    end

    F1 -.replicated to.-> A1
    F1 -.replicated to.-> B1
    F1 -.replicated to.-> C1
    F2 -.replicated to.-> A2
    F2 -.replicated to.-> B2
    F2 -.replicated to.-> C2
    F3 -.replicated to.-> A3
    F3 -.replicated to.-> B3
    F3 -.replicated to.-> C3
```

## 4.3 HDFS Command Decision Tree (KTU-Mapped)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["What do you want to do?"] --> Q1{"Source = Local FS?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes, Upload| PUT["hdfs dfs -put localfile /hdfs/path"]
    Q1 -->|No, source is HDFS| Q2{"Action?"}
    Q2 -->|Copy| CP["hdfs dfs -cp /src /dst"]
    Q2 -->|Move or Rename| MV["hdfs dfs -mv /src /dst"]
    Q2 -->|Delete| RM["hdfs dfs -rm [-r] [-skipTrash] /path"]
    Q2 -->|Read| CAT["hdfs dfs -cat /file"]
    Q2 -->|Read end| TAIL["hdfs dfs -tail /file"]
    Q2 -->|Change RF| SETREP["hdfs dfs -setrep -w N /file"]
    Q2 -->|Permissions| PERM["hdfs dfs -chmod / -chown"]
    Q2 -->|Inspect| INSPECT["hdfs dfs -ls / -du / -df / -stat / -count"]
```

## 4.4 Typical Lab Experiment Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Start Hadoop daemons - start-dfs.sh"] --> S2["Step 2: Verify with jps"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Create HDFS directories -mkdir"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Create local sample files"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Upload to HDFS -put"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Verify listing -ls"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Read back -cat / -tail"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Copy / Move operations"]
    S8 --> S9["Step 9: Modify permissions and replication"]
    S9 --> S10["Step 10: Inspect disk usage -du and -df"]
    S10 --> S11["Step 11: Delete files -rm / -expunge"]
    S11 --> S12["Step 12: Stop daemons - stop-dfs.sh"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Interpretation:** The two key visualizations above (4.1 and 4.2) together establish the *write-path* and the *physical block distribution* — both of which are high-frequency KTU diagram-drawing questions worth 5–7 marks each.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between `hdfs dfs -put` and `hdfs dfs -copyFromLocal`. Mention at least one situation where their behaviors differ.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Both commands are used to **upload a file from the local file system to HDFS**. Internally, `copyFromLocal` is implemented as an alias / special case of `put`, but with one strict semantic difference:

| Aspect | `-put` | `-copyFromLocal` |
|--------|--------|------------------|
| Source restriction | Any **FileSystem** path (local, S3, HFTP) | **Local file system only** |
| Multiple sources | Yes, supports multiple source paths | Yes |
| Destination restriction | Any FS | HDFS only |
| Error if source remote | Allowed; copies between remote FSs | Throws `IllegalArgumentException` |

**Exam-Smart Answer:** *"Both upload to HDFS, but `-put` is more general and accepts any FS as source, whereas `-copyFromLocal` strictly requires a local source and will reject remote URIs."* (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for source distinction, 1 mark for example of error scenario.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Internal Exam Sample]**
**What is the default block size and replication factor in HDFS? Justify why HDFS uses such large block sizes.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Default block size:** **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x and later; was 64 MB in Hadoop 1.x).
- **Default replication factor:** **3** (configurable via `dfs.replication` in `hdfs-site.xml`).

**Justification for large blocks (any 2 points, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Reduced metadata overhead** — A 1 TB file with 128 MB blocks requires only ~8,192 block entries in the NameNode memory, instead of millions with smaller blocks.
2. **Sequential I/O optimization** — Hadoop workloads are dominated by large sequential reads/scans. Large blocks maximize disk throughput per seek.
3. **Reduced NameNode RPC traffic** — Fewer blocks mean fewer heartbeats, fewer pipeline setups, and lower coordination cost.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**A.** With a neat diagram, explain the **HDFS architecture**. Describe the role of the **NameNode**, **DataNode**, and **Secondary NameNode**. **(7 marks)**

**B.** A file of size **800 MB** is uploaded to HDFS. The configured block size is **128 MB** and the replication factor is **3**.
- (i) Calculate the number of blocks created. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Calculate the total HDFS disk consumption. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) If the replication factor is reduced to **2** using `hdfs dfs -setrep -w 2 /file`, what is the new disk usage? **(2 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part A (7 marks):**

The HDFS architecture follows a **master-slave** pattern:

**1. NameNode (Master) — (2.5 marks)**
- Stores **metadata**: filename, directory structure, block-to-Datanode mapping, permissions, replication factor.
- Maintains two on-disk files: `fsimage` (snapshot) and `edits log` (incremental changes).
- Listens on **port 9000** (default) for client RPCs.
- **Single point of failure** in classic Hadoop 2.x (mitigated by HA + ZooKeeper).

**2. DataNode (Slave) — (2.5 marks)**
- Stores actual **data blocks** on local disk.
- Sends **heartbeat** signals to NameNode every **3 seconds** (default `dfs.heartbeat.interval`).
- Performs block creation, deletion, and replication on NameNode instruction.
- A cluster typically has **dozens to thousands** of DataNodes.

**3. Secondary NameNode — (2 marks)**
- **Misnomer** — it is *not* a failover NameNode.
- Periodically merges `fsimage` + `edits log` into a new `fsimage` (a **checkpointing** operation).
- Runs on a *separate machine* to avoid RAM contention with the active NameNode.
- In Hadoop HA setups, this role is replaced by the **Standby NameNode**.

**Diagram (to be drawn by student):**
Show client → NameNode (with fsimage/edits) → 3 DataNodes in a rack-aware layout, with replication arrows.

#### **Model Solution — Part B (7 marks):**

**(i) Number of blocks:**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{800}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 6.25 \rceil = \mathbf{7 \text{ blocks}}
$$

Block breakdown: $128 + 128 + 128 + 128 + 128 + 128 + 32 = 800$ MB ✓ **(3 marks)**

**(ii) Disk usage at RF = 3:**

$$
\text{Disk} = 7 \times 128 \times 3 = \mathbf{2688 \text{ MB} \approx 2.625 \text{ GB}}
$$

Note: The 7th block is allocated a full 128 MB on disk even though it logically contains only 32 MB. **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Disk usage at RF = 2:**

$$
\text{Disk}_{\text{new}} = 7 \times 128 \times 2 = \mathbf{1792 \text{ MB} \approx 1.75 \text{ GB}}
$$

The `-w` flag ensures the client waits until all 7 blocks have been reduced to 2 replicas. **(2 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**A.** List and explain **any eight** HDFS shell commands with their syntax and a one-line example of usage each. **(7 marks)**

**B.** Discuss the **fault-tolerance** mechanism of HDFS. Explain what happens when:
- (i) A DataNode fails. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) A NameNode fails (in classic Hadoop 2.x vs HA setup). **(3 marks)**
- (iii) A corrupted block is detected. **(2 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part A (7 marks):**

*(Any 8 of the following — each command gets ~0.85 mark; one mark for the explanation/example pair.)*

1. **`hdfs dfs -mkdir /data`** — Creates the directory `/data` in HDFS.
2. **`hdfs dfs -ls /data`** — Lists files in `/data` with size, owner, replication.
3. **`hdfs dfs -put local.txt /data/`** — Uploads `local.txt` from the local FS to HDFS.
4. **`hdfs dfs -get /data/file.txt ./`** — Downloads the HDFS file to current local directory.
5. **`hdfs dfs -cat /data/file.txt`** — Prints file contents to stdout.
6. **`hdfs dfs -cp /src /dst`** — Copies `/src` to `/dst` within HDFS.
7. **`hdfs dfs -rm /data/old.txt`** — Deletes (moves to trash) `old.txt`.
8. **`hdfs dfs -chmod 755 /data/file.txt`** — Sets read/execute for all, write for owner.
9. **`hdfs dfs -du -h /data/`** — Shows disk usage of all files in `/data`.
10. **`hdfs dfs -setrep -w 4 /data/file.txt`** — Increases replication factor to 4 and waits.

#### **Model Solution — Part B (7 marks):**

HDFS achieves fault tolerance through **replication + heartbeats + re-replication**.

**(i) DataNode failure (2 marks):**
- If the NameNode does not receive a heartbeat from a DataNode within **10 minutes** (default `dfs.namenode.heartbeat.recheck-interval` is 10 min, heartbeat interval is 3 sec), it declares the DataNode **dead**.
- The NameNode consults its block map and instructs the remaining DataNodes to **re-replicate** the under-replicated blocks to maintain the configured RF.
- This happens automatically and transparently to the client.

**(ii) NameNode failure (3 marks):**
- **Classic Hadoop 2.x:** Single NameNode → **complete cluster unavailability**; manual recovery required by reading `fsimage` + replaying `edits log` on a new host.
- **Hadoop HA setup (Hadoop 2.x+):** Two NameNodes — Active and Standby — coordinated by **ZooKeeper** via **ZKFC (ZooKeeper Failover Controller)**. On Active failure, the Standby takes over within seconds; both share edits log via **QJM (Quorum Journal Manager)** or **NFS**.

**(iii) Corrupted block detection (2 marks):**
- During block reads, the client computes the **checksum** of received data and compares it with the stored checksum (stored in a separate `.meta` file).
- On mismatch, the client reports to the NameNode, which schedules a re-replication from a healthy replica and discards the corrupted block.

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in this experiment:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `-p` flag with `mkdir`** — A nested directory like `/a/b/c` requires `-p`; without it the command fails silently. **–1 mark.**
> 2. **Using `hdfs dfs -put` for HDFS-to-HDFS copy** — Wrong tool. Use `-cp`. **–1 mark.**
> 3. **Confusing `-du` with `-df`** — `-du` = disk usage of **a path**; `-df` = free space of the **whole filesystem**. **–1 mark.**
> 4. **Writing `hadoop dfs` instead of `hadoop fs` or `hdfs dfs`** — Deprecated and disallowed. **–1 mark.**
> 5. **Forgetting that `-rm` moves to trash, not permanently deletes** — KTU specifically tests this. Mention `fs.trash.interval` in viva. **–1 mark.**
> 6. **Not mentioning block size + RF in lab inference** — Always end your record with: *"HDFS uses a default block size of 128 MB and replication factor of 3 to ensure fault tolerance."* **–2 marks if missing.**
> 7. **Calculating disk usage wrong** — Always remember the **ceiling function** for partial blocks. The 7th block of an 800 MB file still occupies 128 MB. **–2 marks.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **High-Density Summary — Read 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ HDFS is **master-slave**; **NameNode = master**, **DataNodes = slaves**.
- ☐ Default **block size = 128 MB**, default **replication factor = 3**.
- ☐ Use `hdfs dfs` (preferred) or `hadoop fs` — **never** `hadoop dfs`.
- ☐ `-put` and `-get` are for **Local ↔ HDFS**; `-cp` and `-mv` are for **HDFS ↔ HDFS**.
- ☐ `-rm` moves to **trash** (retained 6 hours); use `-rm -skipTrash` for permanent deletion.
- ☐ `-du -h /path` = per-path usage; `-df -h /` = whole-filesystem free/used.
- ☐ `-setrep -w N` changes replication and **waits** for completion; without `-w` it returns immediately.
- ☐ `-chmod`, `-chown` accept `-R` for recursive application.
- ☐ Disk usage formula: $\lceil \text{size} / \text{block\_size} \rceil \times \text{block\_size} \times \text{RF}$.
- ☐ HDFS write-pipeline: Client → DN1 → DN2 → DN3 → ACKs reverse → NameNode commits.
- ☐ NameNode stores metadata only; **DataNodes store real blocks** on local disk.
- ☐ Secondary NameNode = **checkpointing helper**, **NOT** a hot standby.
- ☐ Heartbeat interval = **3 sec**; DataNode timeout = **10 min**.
- ☐ Block corruption detected via **checksum**; re-replicated from a healthy copy.
- ☐ HDFS is **write-once, read-many** (WORM) by design; appends are supported but expensive.
- ☐ In HA mode, **ZooKeeper + ZKFC** coordinate Active ↔ Standby NameNode failover.
- ☐ Always **start daemons with `start-dfs.sh`** and **verify with `jps`** before the experiment.
- ☐ In the lab record, end with an **inference** stating block size, RF, and HDFS's fault-tolerance guarantee.

---
