topics.title: Hadoop Specific File Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Hadoop Specific File Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hadoop Specific File Types** refers to the specialized data storage formats engineered to operate natively on the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS). These formats dictate how records are serialized, partitioned, compressed, and indexed across distributed nodes, directly influencing storage footprint, I/O throughput, and query latency within the Hadoop ecosystem (MapReduce, Hive, Spark, Impala, Pig).

The Hadoop ecosystem supports a wide spectrum of file formats, broadly classified as:

1. **Row-Oriented Storage Formats** — Record stored as a contiguous row.
   - `TextFile` (`.txt`, `.csv`, `.json`)
   - `SequenceFile` (binary key-value)
   - `Avro` (schema-based binary row format)

2. **Columnar Storage Formats** — Fields stored column-wise, enabling column-pruning.
   - `RCFile` (Record Columnar File)
   - `ORC` (Optimized Row Columnar)
   - `Parquet` (Dremel-inspired columnar format)

3. **Input/Output Custom Formats** — User-defined `InputFormat` and `OutputFormat` classes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Hadoop file types like different kinds of storage boxes in a giant warehouse:**

Imagine a massive library with millions of books (your data). How you *shelve* the books determines how fast you can find what you need:

- **TextFile (Row-Oriented)**: Books are placed in full on the shelf, exactly as written. To find every mention of "Quantum Physics" across 1 million books, you must physically pull *every single book* off the shelf just to flip through it. Slow, but easy to write.
- **SequenceFile (Binary Row)**: Same as TextFile, but the books are *shrunk* and glued back-to-back in a single bound volume. Saves shelf space, fast to scan, but you still must read the whole book to find one chapter.
- **Columnar (Parquet/ORC)**: Every book is *disassembled* — all Chapter 1s from all books are placed in one aisle, all Chapter 2s in another aisle, etc. To find every "Quantum Physics" reference, you walk *only* to the "Physics chapter" aisle and skip everything else. **Massive speedup** for analytical queries.

## 1.3 Core Metadata Constants & Metrics

The following standard metrics characterize Hadoop file types:

- **Block Size (HDFS default)**: **128 MB** (configurable to 64 MB – 1 GB)
- **Replication Factor (HDFS default)**: **3**
- **Parquet Page Size**: **1 MB** (Row Group default)
- **ORC Stripe Size**: **64 MB** (default)
- **SequenceFile Sync Marker Interval**: **2000 bytes** (default)
- **Avro Container File Block Size**: **64 MB** (default, similar to HDFS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: A frequent 2–3 mark question in PBADT504 asks to differentiate between row-based and columnar storage. Master the *I/O amplification* concept: columnar formats reduce I/O when the query accesses fewer than 50% of the columns in a wide schema.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Row-Based File Types — Operational Logic

### 2.1.1 TextFile Format

- **Definition**: Plain UTF-8 encoded text; each line is a record. Default Hadoop `TextInputFormat`.
- **Operational Steps**:
  1. Record `R` is serialized as a UTF-8 string.
  2. Newline `\n` delimiter separates records.
  3. No schema is embedded — interpretation is left to the processing engine (schema-on-read).
- **Why use it**: Human-readable, simplest to debug, universally compatible.
- **Limitation**: No block-level compression (whole-file split is required), no predicate pushdown.

### 2.1.2 SequenceFile Format

- **Definition**: Hadoop's native binary flat-file format consisting of serialized key-value pairs.
- **Internal Architecture**:
  1. **Header (Magic + Version + KeyClassName + ValueClassName + CompressionCodec + Metadata)**
  2. **Sync Markers** — A small 16-byte marker (`SEQ\x06\x06\x16\xDE\xAD\xBE\xEF\x01\x7F\x7F\x7F\x7F\x7F\x7F\x7F`) inserted every ~2000 bytes.
  3. **Records** — Each record is `<key_length, key_bytes, value_length, value_bytes, sync_marker>`.
- **Why use it**: Splittable even when compressed (block compression mode), intermediate MapReduce output format.

### 2.1.3 Avro Format

- **Definition**: A schema-based, language-neutral, row-oriented binary data format created by Doug Cutting (Hadoop's creator).
- **Schema is embedded inside the file** — supports schema evolution (forward/backward compatibility).
- **Storage Components**:
  1. **File Header** — 4-byte magic `Obj\x01` + Avro schema (JSON) + sync marker.
  2. **Data Blocks** — Serialized records, optionally compressed.
  3. **Sync Marker** — 16-byte random sequence at the end of each block.
- **Why use it**: Schema evolution, cross-language support (Java, C/C++, Python, Ruby), best for streaming ingestion (Kafka).

## 2.2 Columnar File Types — Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 Parquet Format

- **Definition**: A columnar storage format originally built by Twitter and Cloudera, based on Google's Dremel paper.
- **Hierarchical Structure**:
  1. **Row Group** (default 128 MB) — A horizontal partition of the data.
  2. **Column Chunk** — Within a Row Group, all values of one column are stored contiguously.
  3. **Page** (default 1 MB) — Smallest unit; contains values + definition/repetition levels (Dremel encoding) + statistics (min/max/null count).
- **Why use it**: Excellent for analytical workloads, deep nested data support, predicate pushdown, column pruning.

### 2.2.2 ORC (Optimized Row Columnar) Format

- **Definition**: A self-describing, type-aware, columnar file format designed for Hadoop/Hive workloads.
- **Hierarchical Structure**:
  1. **Stripe** (default 64 MB) — Analogous to a Row Group in Parquet.
  2. **Index Data** — Min/max index per column for predicate pushdown.
  3. **Row Data** — Streams of column data, run-length encoded.
  4. **Stripe Footer** — Encoded column encodings and counts.
  5. **File Footer** — Global statistics + schema + stripe locations.
- **Why use it**: Built-in Bloom filters, ACID support in Hive, light-weight index (every 10,000 rows), strong compression (Zlib, Snappy).

### 2.2.3 RCFile Format

- **Definition**: Record Columnar File, the predecessor of ORC, introduced by Facebook.
- **Operational Logic**:
  1. First decomposes rows into a **Key/Value** pair.
  2. Stores columns in `ColumnFamily` style.
- **Limitation**: No index for skip; replaced by ORC in modern pipelines.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Comparison Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE**: In all comparison tables below, the vertical pipe `\vert` is used in place of the markdown table delimiter `|` to preserve table integrity.

| **Parameter** | **TextFile** | **SequenceFile** | **Avro** | **Parquet** | **ORC** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Storage Type** | Row (Text) | Row (Binary) | Row (Binary) | Columnar | Columnar |
| **Splittable when Compressed** | No (whole file) | Yes (block mode) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Schema Embedded** | No (Schema-on-Read) | No | Yes (JSON) | Yes (Thrift) | Yes (Protobuf-like) |
| **Schema Evolution** | Not applicable | Not applicable | Yes (Full) | Limited (add/drop columns) | Limited |
| **Compression Codecs** | Any external | None, Record, Block | Snappy, Deflate, BZip2 | Snappy, Gzip, LZO, Zstd | Zlib, Snappy, LZO |
| **Compression Ratio** | Low (1x) | Medium (~2x) | Medium (~3x) | High (~5–10x) | Very High (~5–10x) |
| **Predicate Pushdown** | No | No | Limited | Yes (Min/Max stats) | Yes (Min/Max + Bloom) |
| **Best Use Case** | Logs, raw ingestion | MapReduce intermediate | Streaming, Kafka | OLAP, nested JSON | Hive warehousing |
| **Read Pattern (Best for)** | Full scan | Full scan | Full scan | Column queries | Column queries + ACID |
| **Write Speed** | Very Fast | Fast | Fast | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Read Speed (Analytical)** | Slow | Slow | Medium | Very Fast | Very Fast |

## 2.4 Key Engineering Derivations

### 2.4.1 I/O Amplification Reduction in Columnar Formats

When a query accesses `k` columns out of a total of `n` columns, the **I/O Reduction Ratio (IRR)** is:

$$IRR = 1 - \frac{k}{n}$$

For example, a query on **3 columns out of 30** in a Parquet file:

$$IRR = 1 - \frac{3}{30} = 0.90 \quad \text{or} \quad 90\% \text{ I/O reduction}$$

### 2.4.2 Storage Size Estimation

The effective on-disk size `S_eff` of a dataset compressed with ratio `C_r` stored with HDFS replication `R` is:

$$S_{eff} = \frac{S_{raw} \times R}{C_r}$$

where:
- $S_{raw}$ = uncompressed logical size
- $R$ = replication factor (typically 3)
- $C_r$ = compression ratio (e.g., 5x for Parquet with Snappy)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Preferred File Type** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| Web Server Log Ingestion | TextFile / Avro | Raw ingestion, streaming, schema flexibility |
| MapReduce Intermediate Output | SequenceFile | Native Hadoop binary, splittable compression |
| Kafka Streaming Pipeline | Avro | Schema evolution, cross-language serialization |
| Data Lakehouse / Lake | Parquet | Industry standard, deep nested support, BI tools |
| Hive Data Warehouse | ORC | Built-in indexes, ACID, light-weight index |
| Log Search / Ad-hoc Exploration | TextFile | Human-readable, grep-friendly |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Compression Efficiency Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example**: Consider a `100 GB` raw log dataset with **30 columns**, where an analytical query reads only **3 specific columns**. Compute the effective HDFS storage and the I/O bytes read for TextFile (Gzip) vs. Parquet (Snappy).

### Given Data

$$S_{raw} = 100 \text{ GB}, \quad n = 30 \text{ columns}, \quad k = 3 \text{ columns accessed}$$

$$R = 3 \text{ (HDFS replication)}, \quad C_{r,Text} = 2\text{x}, \quad C_{r,Parquet} = 5\text{x}$$

### Step 1 — Effective On-Disk Storage for TextFile (Gzip)

The effective storage formula is:

$$S_{eff} = \frac{S_{raw} \times R}{C_r}$$

Substituting values:

$$S_{eff,Text} = \frac{100 \text{ GB} \times 3}{2}$$

$$S_{eff,Text} = \frac{300}{2} = 150 \text{ GB}$$

### Step 2 — Effective On-Disk Storage for Parquet (Snappy)

$$S_{eff,Parquet} = \frac{100 \text{ GB} \times 3}{5}$$

$$S_{eff,Parquet} = \frac{300}{5} = 60 \text{ GB}$$

### Step 3 — I/O Bytes Read for the 3-Column Query (TextFile)

For row-based TextFile, even if only 3 columns are needed, the **entire row** must be deserialized (and decompressed if block compression is used). Hence:

$$I_{Text} = S_{eff,Text} = 150 \text{ GB read}$$

### Step 4 — I/O Bytes Read for the 3-Column Query (Parquet)

For columnar Parquet, only the relevant column chunks within the row groups are read. The I/O reduction ratio is:

$$IRR = 1 - \frac{k}{n}$$

$$IRR = 1 - \frac{3}{30} = 0.90$$

The effective read data:

$$I_{Parquet} = S_{eff,Parquet} \times \frac{k}{n}$$

$$I_{Parquet} = 60 \text{ GB} \times \frac{3}{30} = 6 \text{ GB read}$$

### Step 5 — Net Speedup Factor

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{I_{Text}}{I_{Parquet}} = \frac{150 \text{ GB}}{6 \text{ GB}} = 25\text{x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation**: For this analytical query, Parquet is **25 times faster** in I/O terms and consumes **60% less HDFS storage** than TextFile with Gzip. This single derivation is the *most* high-yield answer KTU examiners expect in 14-mark questions.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Format

> [!NOTE]
> Use this matrix as the **decision rubric** for KTU 14-mark descriptive questions.

| **Workload Characteristic** | **Recommended Format** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| Full table scan, debugging | TextFile | Human-readable, no schema required |
| MapReduce shuffle output | SequenceFile | Native splittable binary intermediate |
| Real-time Kafka streaming | Avro | Schema evolution + cross-language |
| OLAP / BI tool on wide tables | Parquet | Column pruning + nested data |
| Hive warehouse with frequent updates | ORC | ACID transactions + indexes + Bloom filters |
| Compliance archival (read rarely) | ORC w/ Zlib | Maximum compression ratio |
| ML feature pipelines (PySpark) | Parquet | Native Spark support, fast column reads |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Hadoop File Type Ecosystem

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how raw data is ingested, formatted, and consumed by various processing engines in the Hadoop ecosystem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw[Raw Data Sources]:::src
    log[Web / App Logs]
    stream[Kafka Stream]
    rdbms[RDBMS Dump]
    csv[CSV / JSON Files]
    
    raw --> log
    raw --> stream
    raw --> rdbms
    raw --> csv
    
    log --> t1[TextFile Format]
    csv --> t1
    rdbms --> t3[Avro Format]
    stream --> t3
    
    hadoop[HDFS Storage Layer]:::hdfs
    t1 --> hadoop
    t3 --> hadoop
    t4[SequenceFile Format] --> hadoop
    t5[Parquet Format] --> hadoop
    t6[ORC Format] --> hadoop
    
    hadoop --> mr[MapReduce]
    hadoop --> spark[Apache Spark]
    hadoop --> hive[Apache Hive]
    hadoop --> impala[Apache Impala]
    hadoop --> pig[Apache Pig]
    
    t1 --> use1[Full Scan Analytics]
    t4 --> use1
    t3 --> use2[Schema Evolution Use]
    t5 --> use3[OLAP Column Queries]
    t6 --> use4[ACID Warehousing]
    t6 --> use3
    
    classDef src fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#000
    classDef hdfs fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- **Yellow nodes** = Data sources.
- **Blue node (`HDFS Storage Layer`)** = Central storage repository holding all formatted files.
- **Right-side branches** = Processing engines that consume the formats.
- **Bottom labels** = Use-case mapping between format and workload.

## 4.2 Internal Structure: Parquet File Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pfile[Parquet File]:::phead
    pfile --> rg1[Row Group 1 - 128MB]
    pfile --> rg2[Row Group 2 - 128MB]
    pfile --> rg3[Row Group 3 - 128MB]
    pfile --> pfoot[File Footer - Thrift Encoded Schema, Row Group offsets, Column Chunk statistics]
    
    rg1 --> cc1a[Column Chunk - col A]
    rg1 --> cc1b[Column Chunk - col B]
    rg1 --> cc1c[Column Chunk - col C]
    
    cc1a --> pg1a1[Page 1 - 1MB]
    cc1a --> pg1a2[Page 2 - 1MB]
    cc1a --> pg1a3[Page 3 - 1MB]
    
    pg1a1 --> enc[Values + Repetition Levels + Definition Levels + Statistics min max null]
    
    classDef phead fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
```

**Reading the Parquet Block Diagram:**
- A Parquet file is a sequence of **Row Groups** followed by a single **File Footer**.
- Each Row Group contains one **Column Chunk per column**.
- Each Column Chunk is broken into **Pages** (the smallest I/O unit).
- The Footer contains the global schema, the offsets of all row groups, and the column chunk statistics (min/max/null counts) used for **predicate pushdown**.

## 4.3 Internal Structure: ORC File Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ofile[ORC File]:::ohead
    ofile --> bd[Bytes Stream for each Column]
    ofile --> rb[Row Index - 10000 row granularity]
    ofile --> bf[Bloom Filters for nullable columns]
    ofile --> s1[Stripe 1 - 64MB]
    ofile --> s2[Stripe 2 - 64MB]
    ofile --> s3[Stripe 3 - 64MB]
    ofile --> ffoot[File Footer - Type, Row Count, Column Statistics min max sum]
    ofile --> pf[Postscript - File Footer Length, Compression Type, Version]
    
    s1 --> idx[Index Data - min max per 10000 rows]
    s1 --> rd[Row Data - RLE Encoded Column Streams]
    s1 --> sfoot[Stripe Footer - Encoding, Count, Stream locations]
    
    classDef ohead fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
```

**Reading the ORC Block Diagram:**
- ORC files are made of **Stripes** (analogous to Row Groups).
- Each Stripe has **Index Data**, **Row Data** (run-length encoded), and a **Stripe Footer**.
- The **File Footer** holds aggregated column statistics for the whole file.
- The **Postscript** (always at the very end of the file) contains the File Footer length, compression type, and ORC version — used by readers to locate the footer.

## 4.4 Internal Structure: SequenceFile Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sf[SequenceFile]:::shead
    sf --> hdr[Header - Magic SEQ, Version, KeyClass, ValueClass, Compression Codec, Metadata]
    sf --> rec1[Record 1 - key length, key bytes, value length, value bytes]
    sf --> sync1[Sync Marker - 16 bytes]
    sf --> rec2[Record 2]
    sf --> sync2[Sync Marker]
    sf --> recN[Record N ...]
    sf --> syncN[Final Sync Marker]
    
    classDef shead fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca,color:#000
```

**Reading the SequenceFile Diagram:**
- The **Header** identifies the key/value class names and the compression codec.
- **Sync Markers** are inserted at fixed intervals (every ~2000 bytes by default).
- These markers enable a **splittable compressed SequenceFile** — MapReduce uses them as split boundaries so that a single file can be processed by multiple mappers in parallel.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`**

### **Q1. Differentiate between row-based and columnar file formats in Hadoop. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Row-Based Storage** | **Columnar Storage** |
|---|---|---|
| Storage Layout | All fields of a row stored contiguously | All values of a column stored contiguously |
| Best for | Full-row reads, transactional workloads | Analytical column queries, aggregations |
| I/O Behaviour | Read entire row even if one column is needed | Read only the required columns |
| Examples | **TextFile, Avro, SequenceFile** | **Parquet, ORC, RCFile** |

*(Award 1 mark for correct definition of each, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

### **Q2. What is a SequenceFile? List its two main compression modes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **SequenceFile** is Hadoop's native binary flat-file format for storing serialized key-value pairs on HDFS. *(1 mark)*

Its three compression modes are: *(2 marks)*
1. **None** — No compression, raw binary records.
2. **Record Compression** — Each individual value is compressed using a codec like `DefaultCodec` or `GzipCodec`. Splittable only at sync markers.
3. **Block Compression** — Multiple records are grouped into a block, the keys/values compressed together, and the block is bounded by a sync marker. **This mode is fully splittable.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Module Internal Choice

> **`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module Experiment 2]`**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the internal architecture of the ORC file format with a neat diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The **Optimized Row Columnar (ORC)** file format is a self-describing, type-aware columnar storage format designed primarily for Apache Hive and Hadoop ecosystems. Its internal structure consists of the following components from top to bottom of the file: *(1 mark)*

1. **Postscript** *(0.5 mark)* — Located at the very end of the file. Contains file metadata: the length of the file footer, the compression codec used, and the ORC version number. It allows readers to locate the file footer from the end of the file.

2. **File Footer** *(1 mark)* — Stores aggregated information for the entire file: the schema tree, the total row count, and aggregated column-level statistics (min, max, sum, count, hasNull) for every column. These statistics enable **file-level predicate pushdown**, allowing the query engine to skip files entirely if the predicate cannot match.

3. **Bloom Filters** *(0.5 mark)* — Optionally stored for nullable string columns to perform fast membership tests and skip rows whose value cannot match the predicate.

4. **Row Index** *(0.5 mark)* — A lightweight index that stores the min/max values for every **10,000 rows** (configurable). This is used to perform **stripe-level and row-group-level predicate pushdown** before reading the actual data.

5. **Stripes (default 64 MB each)** *(2 marks)* — The main data containers, analogous to Row Groups in Parquet. Each stripe contains:
   - **Index Data** — A min/max index per column (every 10,000 rows).
   - **Row Data** — The actual data, organized as streams of column values. The streams are **Run-Length Encoded (RLE)** for repeated values, with separate streams for present and null values, plus length streams for variable-length types (strings).
   - **Stripe Footer** — Contains the column encodings, the stream locations, the row count, and per-column statistics for that stripe.

6. **Bytes Stream for each Column** *(1 mark)* — Within a stripe, each column is broken into byte streams, allowing the reader to decompress and decode only the columns it needs.

**Diagram of ORC structure:**

```
[ Postscript (compression, footer length, version) ]
[ File Footer (schema, row count, column statistics) ]
[ Bloom Filters ]
[ Row Index (lightweight, 10,000-row granularity) ]
[ Stripe 1 ] -> [ Index Data | Row Data | Stripe Footer ]
[ Stripe 2 ] -> [ Index Data | Row Data | Stripe Footer ]
[ Stripe 3 ] -> [ Index Data | Row Data | Stripe Footer ]
```

*(Award remaining 0.5 mark for diagram clarity.)*

---

**(b) Compare Avro, Parquet, and ORC file formats across any seven parameters. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| **S.No.** | **Parameter** | **Avro** | **Parquet** | **ORC** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Storage Type | Row-oriented (Binary) | Columnar (Dremel) | Columnar (Stripe-based) |
| 2 | Schema Location | Header of every file (JSON) | Footer of every file (Thrift) | Footer of every file (Protobuf-like) |
| 3 | Schema Evolution | Excellent (full forward/backward) | Limited (add/drop columns) | Limited (add columns) |
| 4 | Splittable when Compressed | Yes (sync markers) | Yes (row group boundaries) | Yes (stripe boundaries) |
| 5 | Predicate Pushdown | Limited (projection only) | Yes (min/max stats per column chunk) | Yes (min/max + Bloom filters) |
| 6 | Compression Codecs | Snappy, Deflate, Bzip2 | Snappy, Gzip, LZO, Zstd | Zlib, Snappy, LZO |
| 7 | Nested Data Support | Excellent (Avro records/unions) | Excellent (Dremel rep/def levels) | Limited (lists, structs, maps) |
| 8 | Best Use Case | Kafka streaming, schema evolution | Data Lakehouse, OLAP on nested JSON | Hive Data Warehouse with ACID |
| 9 | Read Speed (Analytical) | Medium | Very Fast | Very Fast |
| 10 | Write Speed | Fast | Moderate | Moderate |

*(Award 1 mark for every 2 correctly explained parameters. Aim for at least 7 parameters explained for full 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) Explain the Hadoop SequenceFile format in detail, including its internal structure, compression modes, and advantages. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition** *(0.5 mark)*: A **SequenceFile** is Hadoop's native binary flat-file format that stores serialized key-value pairs on HDFS. It is the default output format for `MapReduce` jobs when producing binary intermediate data.

**Internal Structure** *(3 marks)*:

1. **Header** *(1 mark)*:
   - Magic bytes `SEQ` (3 bytes)
   - Version number
   - Key Class Name (fully qualified Java class name)
   - Value Class Name
   - Compression Codec Class (e.g., `GzipCodec`, `DefaultCodec`)
   - Metadata (custom key-value pairs for application use)
   - Sync marker

2. **Records** *(1 mark)*: Each record on disk is laid out as:
   - `<key_length><key_bytes><value_length><value_bytes>` (when uncompressed)
   - Followed by a sync marker after every ~2000 bytes of data.

3. **Sync Marker** *(1 mark)*: A 16-byte pseudo-random sequence (`SEQ\x06\x06\x16\xDE\xAD\xBE\xEF\x01\x7F\x7F\x7F\x7F\x7F\x7F\x7F`) that acts as a **resync point** allowing readers to recover from corruption and allowing MapReduce to use it as a **split boundary** for parallel processing.

**Compression Modes** *(2 marks)*:

| **Mode** | **Behaviour** | **Splittable?** |
|---|---|---|
| **None** | No compression applied to records | Yes |
| **Record Compression** | Each individual value compressed with the codec | Only at sync markers |
| **Block Compression** | A block of records compressed together; sync markers bound the block | **Yes** (preferred for large data) |

**Advantages** *(1.5 marks)*:
- Splittable even when compressed (Block mode).
- Compact binary representation — saves storage compared to TextFile.
- Native MapReduce intermediate format — no conversion needed.
- Supports custom metadata.
- Easy to append new records.

---

**(b) What is Parquet? Explain its internal structure, encoding techniques, and advantages over row-based formats. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition** *(0.5 mark)*: **Parquet** is an open-source columnar storage format for Hadoop, originally developed by Twitter and Cloudera, based on Google's Dremel paper. It stores nested data structures in a flat columnar form.

**Internal Structure** *(3 marks)*:

1. **Row Group** *(1 mark)* — A horizontal partition of the data, default size 128 MB. The largest unit that can be loaded into memory for processing.
2. **Column Chunk** *(1 mark)* — Within each row group, all values for one column are stored contiguously. Holds statistics (min, max, null count) for **predicate pushdown**.
3. **Page** *(1 mark)* — The smallest I/O unit within a column chunk, default size 1 MB. Contains encoded values and Dremel metadata.

**Encoding Techniques** *(2 marks)*:
- **Dictionary Encoding** — Repeated values replaced with small integer codes.
- **Run-Length Encoding (RLE)** — Consecutive identical values stored as `<value, count>` pairs.
- **Delta Encoding** — For sorted columns, store the difference between consecutive values.
- **Bit-Packing** — Integer values packed into the minimum number of bits required.
- **Dremel Repetition and Definition Levels** — Enable reconstruction of nested structures (lists, structs, maps) from the columnar representation.

**File Footer** *(0.5 mark)* — A Thrift-encoded block at the end of the file containing the schema, row group offsets, and column chunk statistics.

**Advantages** *(1 mark)*:
- Massive I/O reduction via column pruning.
- Excellent compression ratios (5–10x typical).
- Native support for deeply nested data.
- Compatible with Spark, Hive, Impala, Drill, Presto, and many BI tools.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks (Examiner's Pitfall Guide):**
>
> 1. **Confusing compression ratio with read speed** — A 5x compression ratio does NOT mean queries are 5x faster. Always state both *storage reduction* and *I/O reduction* separately.
> 2. **Forgetting the sync marker in SequenceFile** — It is the reason SequenceFile is splittable. Many students omit this in 7-mark answers and lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Writing "Parquet is faster" without justification** — Examiners want the *I/O amplification reduction* formula or a numeric example.
> 4. **Mixing up ORC components** — The Postscript is at the **end** of the file, not the beginning. The File Footer comes *before* the Postscript.
> 5. **Not mentioning schema evolution in Avro answers** — It is Avro's signature feature; missing it costs at least 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing Row Group (Parquet) with Stripe (ORC)** — They are analogous but use different terminology; examiners penalize interchange of terms.
> 7. **Not drawing a diagram** — For 7-mark sub-questions, a labelled block diagram is mandatory and earns at least 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PBADT504 | Module Experiment 2 | Hadoop Specific File Types**

- **Hadoop file types are classified into Row-Oriented (TextFile, SequenceFile, Avro) and Columnar (Parquet, ORC, RCFile).**
- **TextFile** is human-readable but not splittable when compressed and has no schema.
- **SequenceFile** is a binary key-value format with a 3-byte magic header, optional metadata, and **sync markers** that enable splittable block compression.
- **SequenceFile has three compression modes**: None, Record, and Block. **Block mode is the only fully splittable compressed mode.**
- **Avro** embeds its JSON schema in the file header and supports **full schema evolution** (forward + backward compatibility).
- **Avro is the de-facto standard for Kafka streaming pipelines** because of its schema registry support.
- **Parquet** is a **columnar** format with a hierarchy of **Row Group → Column Chunk → Page**.
- **Parquet supports deep nested data** using Dremel **repetition and definition levels**.
- **Parquet default sizes**: Row Group = 128 MB, Page = 1 MB.
- **ORC** stands for **Optimized Row Columnar**; it is a stripe-based columnar format.
- **ORC default sizes**: Stripe = 64 MB, Index granularity = 10,000 rows.
- **ORC supports ACID transactions** in Hive and has built-in **Bloom filters** for fast lookups.
- **ORC file layout** from top to bottom: Row Index, Bloom Filters, Stripes (Index + Row Data + Stripe Footer), File Footer, Postscript.
- **Columnar formats reduce I/O** by the formula $\vert IRR = 1 - \frac{k}{n} \vert$, where $k$ is columns accessed and $n$ is total columns.
- **Effective HDFS storage** = $\vert \frac{S_{raw} \times R}{C_r} \vert$, where $R$ is replication and $C_r$ is compression ratio.
- **RCFile** is the predecessor of ORC; rarely used in modern pipelines.
- **Predicate pushdown** uses min/max statistics stored in column chunk / stripe footers to **skip entire blocks** of data.
- **Default HDFS block size** = **128 MB**; default replication = **3**.
- **Avro container file default block size** = **64 MB**.
- **For analytical workloads reading <50% of columns, always choose Parquet or ORC over Avro or TextFile.**
- **For MapReduce intermediate output, use SequenceFile** for its native binary and splittable compression.
- **For schema-evolving streaming data, use Avro** with a schema registry.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Anatomy of a file read and file write. Data Processing with MapReduce: Execution Pipeline-Map Reduce: Developing a map-reduce application 12 2 Pig : Introduction to PIG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Anatomy of a File Read in HDFS

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HDFS File Read** is the distributed process by which a client retrieves data stored across multiple DataNodes in a Hadoop cluster, coordinated by the NameNode, using a block-based streaming protocol that involves RPC handshakes, checksum validation, and packet-level data transfer.

**The core components involved are:**
- **NameNode (NN):** The master daemon that stores filesystem metadata (file → block mappings).
- **DataNode (DN):** Slave daemons that store the actual data blocks on local disks.
- **HDFS Client:** The user-facing API (`FileSystem` abstraction) that initiates the read.
- **Block:** The default unit of storage in HDFS, fixed at **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x/3.x.
- **Packet:** A smaller transmission unit (default **64 KB**) used during streaming.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Library Analogy for HDFS Read:**
> Imagine you walk into a massive library (the Hadoop cluster). You ask the librarian (NameNode) for a specific book. The librarian does NOT give you the book directly. Instead, the librarian checks a catalogue and tells you: *"This book is split into 5 chapters. Chapter 1 is in Shelf Rack A, Chapter 2 is in Rack C, Chapter 3 has a duplicate copy in Rack B (use the closest one)."* You then walk to those racks (DataNodes) and read the chapters yourself. The librarian never touched the physical book. This is exactly how HDFS reads work — **metadata is centralized, but data transfer is direct between client and DataNodes.**

### Step-by-Step Intuition of a File Read
1. Client invokes `open()` on `FileSystem` (DFS class) → returns a `DistributedFileSystem` reference.
2. Client calls `getBlockLocations()` via RPC to the **NameNode**.
3. NameNode returns a list of blocks and their **DataNode locations** (sorted by network proximity).
4. Client connects directly to the **nearest DataNode** using FSDataInputStream.
5. Data is streamed as **packets (64 KB each)**, with a checksum verified per packet.
6. Client closes the stream upon completion.

---

## 1.2 Anatomy of a File Write in HDFS

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HDFS File Write** is the distributed process by which a client writes data to the cluster. The file is split into blocks, replicated across multiple DataNodes based on the replication factor (default **3**), and the metadata is committed only after all replicas acknowledge successful storage.

**Pipeline Concept:** The write forms a **replica pipeline** — Data → DN1 → DN2 → DN3 — where each node forwards data to the next, achieving parallel replication.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Photocopying Analogy for HDFS Write:**
> You write a 3-page essay and hand it to a senior clerk (NameNode). The clerk splits it into 3 pages and gives one page each to three typists (DataNodes). Each typist makes 2 extra copies and passes them down a chain so that, in the end, every typist holds a complete set of 3 identical pages. Only after every typist confirms "Done!" does the clerk officially register the document in the central registry. If any typist fails, the senior clerk re-routes the page to a different typist. This is the **HDFS write pipeline with acknowledgment.**

---

## 1.3 MapReduce Execution Pipeline

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MapReduce Execution Pipeline** is the sequential chain of processing stages — InputFormat → RecordReader → Mapper → Combiner → Partitioner → Shuffle & Sort → Reducer → OutputFormat — that transforms raw input data into aggregated, structured output across a distributed cluster.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Voting Count Analogy:**
> Imagine counting votes in a national election. Phase 1 (Map): Each district officer counts votes in their district and produces a list of (candidate, count) pairs. Phase 2 (Combine): Each officer pre-aggregates the local counts to reduce paper. Phase 3 (Shuffle): All lists are routed to a central counting room sorted by candidate name. Phase 4 (Reduce): The final tally for each candidate is summed. This is precisely the **MapReduce paradigm.**

---

## 1.4 Pig: Introduction to PIG

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apache Pig** is a high-level platform for analyzing large data sets. Its principal component, **Pig Latin**, is a scripting language that abstracts MapReduce job complexity, allowing users to write transformations as a data flow sequence that is automatically compiled into optimized MapReduce (or Tez/Spark) jobs by the execution engine.

**Core Components of Pig:**
- **Pig Latin:** The scripting language.
- **Grunt Shell:** The interactive command-line interface.
- **Execution Modes:** Local (single JVM) and MapReduce (distributed).
- **Data Types:** scalar (int, long, float, double, chararray, bytearray) and complex (tuple, bag, map).

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Recipe Analogy for Pig:**
> Writing raw MapReduce is like cooking by manually controlling fire, timing, and ingredients. **Pig Latin** is like writing a recipe: *"Take ingredients → wash → chop → cook for 20 mins → serve."* The Pig engine (the chef) automatically figures out the exact stove settings, pan rotations, and timing. You focus on **what** to cook, not **how** to cook it.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of HDFS File Read — Theoretical Deep Dive

### Phases of a Read Operation
1. **RPC Handshake Phase:** Client → NameNode via Hadoop RPC protocol.
2. **Block Location Resolution:** NameNode returns the ordered DataNode list per block.
3. **Stream Acquisition:** Client receives `DFSInputStream` which manages connections.
4. **Data Streaming Phase:** Packets read sequentially from the closest DataNode.
5. **Checksum Verification:** Each packet's CRC32 checksum is validated.
6. **Block Transition:** If a block ends, the next block's DataNode connection is opened seamlessly.
7. **Stream Closure:** Client calls `close()` → releases file leases and socket connections.

### Key Internal Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **HDFS Read — Standard Tunable Parameters:**
> - **Block Size:** 128 MB (Hadoop 2.x+)
> - **Packet Size:** 64 KB
> - **Replication Factor:** 3 (default)
> - **Checksum Algorithm:** CRC-32C
> - **Socket Buffer Size:** 128 KB (default `io.file.buffer.size`)

### Failure Handling in Reads
- **DataNode Failure During Read:** Client transparently retries with the next DataNode in the block's replica list.
- **Corrupt Packet Detected:** Client fetches the block from a different replica and logs a warning to the NameNode.

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of HDFS File Write — Theoretical Deep Dive

### Pipeline Stages of a Write
1. **File Creation Request:** Client calls `create()` on `FileSystem` → RPC to NameNode.
2. **Lease Acquisition:** NameNode grants a **write lease** (exclusive write lock) to the client.
3. **Data Chunking:** Client splits the input stream into packets (64 KB) inside an output stream.
4. **Replica Pipeline Setup:** A chain of DataNodes (typically 3) is established. Data flows: Client → DN1 → DN2 → DN3.
5. **Acknowledgment Backflow:** ACK packets flow in reverse: DN3 → DN2 → DN1 → Client.
6. **Block Commitment:** Only after all DNs confirm, the block is finalized.
7. **Metadata Update:** NameNode updates the INode with the new block IDs upon `close()`.

### Replication Math

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Replica Pipeline Equation:**
> 
> $$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{client}\to\text{DN1}} + (N_{\text{replicas}} - 1) \times T_{\text{per-hop}} + T_{\text{ack-backflow}}$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{client}\to\text{DN1}}$ = latency from client to first DataNode
- $N_{\text{replicas}}$ = replication factor (typically **3**)
- $T_{\text{per-hop}}$ = inter-DataNode network latency

---

## 2.3 MapReduce Execution Pipeline — Deep Theory

### Stage-by-Stage Analysis

| Stage | Role | Output | Failure Behavior |
|-------|------|--------|------------------|
| **InputFormat** | Validates input & splits file | `InputSplit[]` | Throws `InvalidInputException` |
| **RecordReader** | Converts split into (K,V) pairs | `<K, V>` | Skips corrupt records |
| **Mapper** | User-defined transformation | `Context.write(K2, V2)` | Task retry (up to 4 times) |
| **Combiner** | Local pre-aggregation | Mini-reduce output | Optional, must be commutative/associative |
| **Partitioner** | Routes keys to reducers | `getPartition(K, V, nReducers)` | Default = `HashPartitioner` |
| **Shuffle & Sort** | Groups by key, sorts | Sorted iterator per reducer | Pull-based, on reducer node |
| **Reducer** | Final aggregation | `Context.write(K3, V3)` | Idempotent retry supported |
| **OutputFormat** | Writes final output | HDFS file | Atomic rename on success |

### The Critical Shuffle Phase
> [!NOTE]
> The **Shuffle & Sort** phase is the most network-intensive. It involves:
> - **Map-side**: Spill to local disk → merge sorted segments
> - **Reduce-side**: HTTP fetch from all map task outputs → merge sort into final groups

### Number of Mappers Formula

$$N_{\text{mappers}} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Total Input Size (in bytes)}}{\text{Block Size (in bytes)}} \right\rceil$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication:** A 1 GB file with **128 MB** blocks → exactly **8 mappers** (1 GB = 1024 MB → 1024/128 = 8).

### Number of Reducers
- Default: **1 reducer** (unwise for production).
- Tuned via `mapreduce.job.reduces` property.
- Heuristic: $\approx 0.95 \times N_{\text{slots}} \times N_{\text{nodes}}$ for a balanced cluster.

---

## 2.4 Developing a MapReduce Application — Deep Theory

### Skeleton Components
1. **Driver Class:** Configures `Job`, sets input/output paths, mapper/reducer classes, and submits.
2. **Mapper Class:** Extends `Mapper<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>`; overrides `map()`.
3. **Reducer Class:** Extends `Reducer<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>`; overrides `reduce()`.
4. **Optional Partitioner & Combiner.**

### Generic Dataflow
$$\text{Input} \xrightarrow{\text{RecordReader}} \langle K_1, V_1 \rangle \xrightarrow{\text{Mapper}} \langle K_2, V_2 \rangle$$
$$\xrightarrow{\text{Combiner}} \langle K_2, V_2 \rangle_{\text{local}} \xrightarrow{\text{Partitioner}} \langle K_2, V_2 \rangle_{\text{shard}}$$
$$\xrightarrow{\text{Shuffle\&Sort}} \langle K_2, [V_2, V_2, \ldots] \rangle \xrightarrow{\text{Reducer}} \langle K_3, V_3 \rangle \xrightarrow{\text{OutputFormat}} \text{Output File}$$

### Key Interfaces in Hadoop API

| Interface | Method | Purpose |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| `Writable` | `write()`, `readFields()` | Serialization |
| `WritableComparable` | `compareTo()` | Sorting keys |
| `InputFormat<K, V>` | `getSplits()`, `createRecordReader()` | Input abstraction |
| `OutputFormat<K, V>` | `getRecordWriter()` | Output abstraction |
| `Partitioner<K, V>` | `getPartition()` | Shard routing |

---

## 2.5 Pig: Introduction to PIG — Deep Theory

### Pig Latin Execution Flow
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pig Compilation Pipeline:**
> 
> $$\text{Pig Latin Script} \xrightarrow{\text{Parser}} \text{AST} \xrightarrow{\text{Logical Optimizer}} \text{Logical Plan}$$
> $$\xrightarrow{\text{Physical Optimizer}} \text{Physical Plan (MapReduce / Tez / Spark)} \xrightarrow{\text{Execution Engine}} \text{Result}$$

### Pig Latin Operators (KTU High-Yield)

| Operator | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| `LOAD` | Reads data from filesystem | `LOAD 'data.txt' USING PigStorage(',')` |
| `STORE` | Writes data to filesystem | `STORE result INTO 'out' USING PigStorage('\t')` |
| `FOREACH ... GENERATE` | Projection / transformation | `FOREACH A GENERATE name, age+1;` |
| `FILTER` | Row selection | `FILTER A BY age > 18;` |
| `GROUP` | Groups records by key | `B = GROUP A BY country;` |
| `JOIN` | Joins two relations | `C = JOIN A BY id, B BY id;` |
| `ORDER BY` | Sorts (global) | `D = ORDER A BY age DESC;` |
| `DISTINCT` | Removes duplicates | `E = DISTINCT A;` |

### Pig Data Types

| Type | Example | Notes |
|------|---------|-------|
| `int`, `long` | `42` | 32-bit / 64-bit integer |
| `float`, `double` | `3.14` | Floating point |
| `chararray` | `'hello'` | UTF-8 string |
| `bytearray` | binary blob | Opaque type |
| `tuple` | `('a', 1, 2.0)` | Ordered, fixed-length |
| `bag` | `{('a',1), ('b',2)}` | Unordered collection of tuples |
| `map` | `['name'#'Tom']` | Key-value pairs |

### Pig Execution Modes

| Mode | Use Case | Command Flag |
|------|----------|--------------|
| **Local Mode** | Testing on a single machine | `pig -x local` |
| **MapReduce Mode** | Production cluster (YARN) | `pig` (default) or `pig -x mapreduce` |
| **Tez Mode** | Optimized DAG execution | `pig -x tez` |
| **Spark Mode** | Spark execution backend | `pig -x spark` |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Mappers from File Size

**Problem:** Given a file of size $S$ MB and a block size of $B$ MB, derive the formula for the number of mappers $N_m$ in a MapReduce job.

### Step 1: Recall the Definition of a Split
A `FileInputFormat` (default `TextInputFormat`) divides a file into logical splits. By default in HDFS, **each HDFS block = 1 split = 1 mapper**.

### Step 2: Express File Size in Blocks
The number of full blocks contained in a file of size $S$ is:

$$N_{\text{full}} = \left\lfloor \frac{S}{B} \right\rfloor$$

### Step 3: Account for the Remainder
If $S$ is not an exact multiple of $B$, an additional partial block is created:

$$N_{\text{remainder}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } S \bmod B \neq 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### Step 4: Combine into the Ceiling Formula
Adding the two gives the total number of mappers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_m &= N_{\text{full}} + N_{\text{remainder}} \\
&= \left\lfloor \frac{S}{B} \right\rfloor + \left( S \bmod B \neq 0 \,?\, 1 : 0 \right) \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example
Let $S = 1000$ MB and $B = 128$ MB. Then:

$$N_m = \left\lceil \frac{1000}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 7.8125 \rceil = 8 \text{ mappers}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Hadoop always **over-estimates** slightly. A 1-byte difference can add a full mapper — important for resource planning.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: HDFS Read Throughput

**Problem:** Derive the effective read throughput $R$ for a single client reading a file with $B$ bytes per block, replication factor $r$, and a single-stream packet read.

### Step 1: Total Bytes to Read
The client must read $S$ bytes from the file.

### Step 2: Network Latency Per Block
For each block, the client opens a new TCP connection to the closest DataNode. Let $T_c$ = connection setup time and $T_p$ = per-packet transfer time. With packet size $P$:

$$T_{\text{block}} = T_c + \left\lceil \frac{B}{P} \right\rceil \times T_p$$

### Step 3: Total Read Time
If the file spans $N_b$ blocks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= N_b \times T_{\text{block}} + T_{\text{checksum-verify}} \\
&= N_b \times T_c + \frac{S}{P} \times T_p + N_b \times T_{\text{verify}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Throughput
The effective throughput is:

$$R = \frac{S}{T_{\text{total}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Insight:** The first block suffers a "connection penalty." For large files, the average throughput approaches $\frac{1}{T_p} \times P$ (i.e., the link bandwidth), since connection overhead amortizes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Replica Write Pipeline Latency

**Problem:** Compute the time to write a single block of size $B$ bytes through a 3-node pipeline with $N_r = 3$ replicas.

### Step 1: Pipeline Forwarding Time
Data is forwarded hop-by-hop. With packet size $P$ and per-hop latency $T_h$:

$$T_{\text{forward}} = \frac{B}{P} \times T_h$$

### Step 2: Acknowledgment Backflow Time
ACK packets travel back through the same chain:

$$T_{\text{ack}} = \frac{B}{P} \times T_h$$

### Step 3: Initial Handshake
The client must wait for the full pipeline to be ready:

$$T_{\text{setup}} = (N_r - 1) \times T_{\text{handshake}}$$

### Step 4: Total Write Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{write}} &= T_{\text{setup}} + T_{\text{forward}} + T_{\text{ack}} \\
&= (N_r - 1) \times T_{\text{handshake}} + 2 \times \frac{B}{P} \times T_h
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example
With $N_r = 3$, $B = 128$ MB, $P = 64$ KB, $T_h = 0.1$ ms, $T_{\text{handshake}} = 5$ ms:

$$T_{\text{write}} = 2 \times 5 \text{ ms} + 2 \times 2048 \times 0.1 \text{ ms} = 10 \text{ ms} + 409.6 \text{ ms} \approx 419.6 \text{ ms}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The pipeline is **linear in hops, not in replicas.** That's why increasing replication factor to 5 does NOT 5× the write time — it adds only 4 more hops.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: MapReduce Stage Behaviors

| Stage | Runs On | Input Granularity | Output Granularity | Network Usage | Disk I/O |
|-------|---------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------|----------|
| InputFormat | Client | File | Splits | None | None |
| RecordReader | Mapper Node | Split | Record | None | Read |
| Map | Mapper Node | Record | Key-Value Pairs | None | Write (spill) |
| Combine | Mapper Node | Key-Value Pairs | Aggregated Pairs | None | Read+Write |
| Partition | Mapper Node | Key-Value Pairs | Sharded Pairs | None | None |
| Shuffle | Reducer Node | Sharded Pairs | Sorted Groups | **Heavy HTTP** | Heavy |
| Reduce | Reducer Node | Sorted Groups | Final Pairs | None | Write |
| OutputFormat | Reducer Node | Final Pairs | Output File | None | Write |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Pig Latin vs MapReduce (Word Count)

| Step | MapReduce Code | Pig Latin Code |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | Define `Mapper extends Mapper<...>` | `lines = LOAD 'input.txt' AS (line:chararray);` |
| 2 | Tokenize in `map()` | `words = FOREACH lines GENERATE FLATTEN(TOKENIZE(line)) AS word:chararray;` |
| 3 | Emit `(word, 1)` | `grouped = GROUP words BY word;` |
| 4 | Define `Reducer extends Reducer<...>` | `counts = FOREACH grouped GENERATE group AS word, COUNT(words) AS count;` |
| 5 | Sum in `reduce()` | `STORE counts INTO 'output';` |
| **Lines of Code** | **~50+ lines** | **~5 lines** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pig's value proposition:** The same WordCount that takes ~50 lines in Java MapReduce takes ~5 lines in Pig Latin. Pig automatically handles serialization, partitioning, and job chaining.
