topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Grunt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module Experiment 2 — Grunt (Hadoop Shell CLI)

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grunt** is the official **command-line interface (CLI) shell** of the Apache Hadoop framework. It acts as an **interactive interpreter** that allows users to directly interact with the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** and the **Hadoop FileSystem API** without writing a full Java/Pig Latin/Spark program. In simple terms, it is to HDFS what the **Linux Bash terminal** is to a local ext4 filesystem.

Formally (KTU 2024 Scheme terminology):

> **Grunt Shell:** *"A Java-based interactive command interpreter that serves as the primary user-interface to Hadoop's FileSystem abstraction layer, providing Unix-like file manipulation primitives (ls, cat, mkdir, cp, mv, rm, put, get, etc.) over HDFS, Local FS, S3, and other Hadoop-supported storage backends."*

In the Hadoop ecosystem, Grunt is shipped in two major places:
1. **Pig's Grunt Shell** — invoked using the `pig` command (interactive mode).
2. **Hadoop's HDFS Shell** — invoked using `hadoop fs` or `hdfs dfs` commands.

For this KTU Module Experiment 2, the focus is on the **Hadoop Grunt shell for HDFS operations** (file system manipulation commands).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Remote Control for HDFS"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of HDFS as a **massive, distributed warehouse** spread across hundreds of machines. You cannot walk inside the warehouse manually to grab a file. **Grunt is the walkie-talkie** 📻 that the warehouse manager (Hadoop Daemon — `NameNode`) gives you. You press simple button-codes like *"list everything on rack-3"* (`ls`), *"bring me box #456"* (`get`), *"delete box #789"* (`rm`), and Grunt translates these voice codes into the warehouse's internal language (RPC calls to the NameNode) and brings back the result.

A more layered analogy:
| Real-World Object | Hadoop Equivalent | Grunt Command |
|---|---|---|
| 📂 A folder on your laptop | HDFS directory | `mkdir`, `ls` |
| 🗂️ A document file | HDFS file | `put`, `get`, `cat` |
| 🗑️ Recycle bin | Trash in HDFS | `rm`, `expunge` |
| 🔑 File permissions | POSIX permissions in HDFS | `chmod`, `chown` |

## 1.3 Why Grunt Exists — The Engineering Motivation

Before Grunt-style shells, every HDFS operation required writing a Java program using `org.apache.hadoop.fs.FileSystem` API. This was:
- ⏱️ **Time-consuming** for ad-hoc tasks.
- 🐛 **Error-prone** for one-off operations.
- 🎓 **Steep learning curve** for new analysts.

Grunt solves this by providing:
- **Interactive REPL** (Read-Eval-Print Loop) for ad-hoc file system operations.
- **Uniform interface** — same commands work for HDFS, Local FS, WebHDFS, S3, etc.
- **Scriptability** — commands can be piped into a `.sh` script for batch processing.
- **Standardization** — all Hadoop distributions ship the same shell, ensuring portability.

## 1.4 Key Characteristics (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **must-know attributes** of Grunt for KTU 2024 exams:
> - **Interactive shell** — prompts the user with `grunt>` or `hdfs>`.
> - **Abstracts the FileSystem API** — internally uses `org.apache.hadoop.fs.FileSystem` class.
> - **URI-based addressing** — supports `hdfs://`, `file:///`, `s3a://`, `wasb://` URIs.
> - **Recursive variants** — most commands have a recursive version (e.g., `ls` vs `lsr`, `rm` vs `rmr`).
> - **Default working directory** — `/user/<username>` for HDFS.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Position of Grunt in the Hadoop Stack

Grunt sits at the **application-interface layer** of the Hadoop architecture. Let us break down its operational logic step-by-step:

### 🔹 Step 1: User Invocation
The user types a command such as `hdfs dfs -ls /data` in the terminal. The shell binary (`hdfs` script in `$HADOOP_HOME/bin/`) is executed.

### 🔹 Step 2: Argument Parsing
The command-line parser tokenizes the input into:
- **Command name** → `ls`
- **Flags/Options** → e.g., `-h` (human-readable), `-R` (recursive)
- **Path argument** → `/data`

### 🔹 Step 3: FileSystem Resolution
The shell consults the Hadoop **Configuration object** (`core-site.xml`) to determine:
- `fs.defaultFS` — the default filesystem URI (typically `hdfs://namenode:9000`).
- If the user supplied an explicit URI, that overrides the default.

### 🔹 Step 4: RPC Call to NameNode
For metadata operations (e.g., `ls`, `mkdir`, `rm`), Grunt makes an **RPC call** to the **NameNode** daemon over the configured `fs.defaultFS` endpoint. The NameNode is the **metadata server** of HDFS.

### 🔹 Step 5: Data Transfer (for `put`, `get`, `cat`)
For actual data movement, the client (Grunt) talks **directly to the DataNodes** (bypassing the NameNode) using the **HDFS Data Transfer Protocol** for performance.

### 🔹 Step 6: Output Rendering
The result is formatted and printed to **STDOUT** in a human-readable form (e.g., `ls` mimics Unix `ls -l`).

---

## 2.2 KTU Cheat Sheet — Master Command Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important revision asset** for the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the syntax and purpose of each command.

| **Category** | **Command** | **Syntax** | **Purpose / Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Help & Info** | `help` | `hdfs dfs -help <cmd>` | Display usage of any command (essential for debugging) |
| | `ls` | `hdfs dfs -ls <path>` | List files/directories (non-recursive). Flags: `-h`, `-R`, `-d` |
| | `lsr` | `hdfs dfs -lsr <path>` | **Recursive** list (legacy alias for `ls -R`) |
| | `du` | `hdfs dfs -du <path>` | Show **disk usage** of files/dirs in bytes |
| | `dus` | `hdfs dfs -dus <path>` | **Summary** disk usage (deprecated, use `du -s`) |
| | `df` | `hdfs dfs -df <path>` | Show **free space** on the filesystem |
| | `count` | `hdfs dfs -count <path>` | Count directories, files, and bytes under a path |
| | `stat` | `hdfs dfs -stat <format> <path>` | Print file **statistics** (e.g., block size, replication) |
| **Directory Ops** | `mkdir` | `hdfs dfs -mkdir <dir1> <dir2> ...` | Create one or more directories (use `-p` for parents) |
| **File Transfer (Upload)** | `put` | `hdfs dfs -put <local> <hdfs>` | Copy a local file/folder **into HDFS** |
| | `copyFromLocal` | `hdfs dfs -copyFromLocal <loc> <hdfs>` | Same as `put`, but source **must** be local |
| | `moveFromLocal` | `hdfs dfs -moveFromLocal <loc> <hdfs>` | Move (not copy) local file to HDFS |
| **File Transfer (Download)** | `get` | `hdfs dfs -get <hdfs> <local>` | Copy file from HDFS to local FS |
| | `copyToLocal` | `hdfs dfs -copyToLocal <hdfs> <loc>` | Same as `get`, but destination **must** be local |
| | `getmerge` | `hdfs dfs -getmerge <src> <local_file>` | Merge multiple HDFS files into a single local file |
| **Viewing Files** | `cat` | `hdfs dfs -cat <hdfs_file>` | Display file content (concatenate) |
| | `text` | `hdfs dfs -text <hdfs_file>` | Display content (handles compressed/sequence files) |
| | `tail` | `hdfs dfs -tail <hdfs_file>` | Show last 1 KB of a file (like Unix `tail`) |
| | `head` | `hdfs dfs -head <hdfs_file>` | Show first 1 KB of a file |
| **Copy / Move** | `cp` | `hdfs dfs -cp <src> <dst>` | Copy file/dir within HDFS |
| | `mv` | `hdfs dfs -mv <src> <dst>` | Move file/dir within HDFS |
| **Deletion** | `rm` | `hdfs dfs -rm <path>` | Delete a file (use `-r` to delete directories) |
| | `rmr` | `hdfs dfs -rmr <path>` | **Recursive delete** (legacy alias for `rm -r`) |
| | `expunge` | `hdfs dfs -expunge` | Empty the **trash** immediately |
| **Permissions** | `chmod` | `hdfs dfs -chmod <mode> <path>` | Change POSIX permissions (e.g., `755`) |
| | `chown` | `hdfs dfs -chown <owner>:<group> <path>` | Change owner and group |
| | `chgrp` | `hdfs dfs -chgrp <group> <path>` | Change group ownership only |
| **Replication** | `setrep` | `hdfs dfs -setrep <n> <path>` | Set **replication factor** (e.g., `setrep 3 /file.txt`) |
| **Test & Misc** | `test` | `hdfs dfs -test -e <path>` | Test existence (`-e`), zero size (`-z`), is-dir (`-d`) |
| | `touchz` | `hdfs dfs -touchz <path>` | Create an empty file (zero-byte) |
| | `truncate` | `hdfs dfs -truncate <len> <path>` | Truncate file to specified length |
| | `appendToFile` | `hdfs dfs -appendToFile <local> <hdfs>` | Append local content to an HDFS file |
| **Snapshot** | `createSnapshot` | `hdfs dfs -createSnapshot <path> <name>` | Create a **point-in-time snapshot** |
| | `deleteSnapshot` | `hdfs dfs -deleteSnapshot <path> <name>` | Delete a snapshot |
| **Exit** | `exit` / `quit` | Type `exit` in Pig Grunt | Exit the interactive Grunt shell |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on URIs:** Inside HDFS, paths are written as `/folder/file.txt`. When using Grunt, you can prepend the scheme as `hdfs://namenode:9000/folder/file.txt` for explicit addressing, but this is optional if `fs.defaultFS` is configured in `core-site.xml`.

---

## 2.3 The "Default Working Directory" — A Subtle but Critical Concept

When a user invokes `hdfs dfs -ls` (no path), Grunt uses the **user's home directory in HDFS**, which is by convention:
```
/user/<username>
```
For example, if the Linux user is `student`, the default is `/user/student`. This mirrors the Unix concept of `$HOME`.

> [!TIP]
> The home directory is created lazily the first time a user writes to HDFS. You can override the default per-user with the `hadoop.user.name` property in `core-site.xml`, but this is rarely done in practice.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | How Grunt is Used in Industry |
|---|---|
| **Data Engineering** | Daily ETL jobs: ingest raw logs via `put`, validate with `cat`, archive with `mv` to `/archive/YYYY/MM/DD/` |
| **Data Cleaning** | Quickly delete corrupt files (`rm -r`), check sizes (`du -h`), or empty trash (`expunge`) before re-ingestion |
| **Hadoop Administration** | Set replication (`setrep 3`), check disk usage (`df -h`), manage snapshots, fix permissions (`chmod 644`) |
| **DevOps / CI-CD** | Shell scripts in Jenkins/GitHub Actions use `hdfs dfs` for automated dataset refresh |
| **Cluster Migrations** | `distcp` (Hadoop's distributed copy) is internally a parallelized version of Grunt's `cp` |
| **Academic Labs (KTU)** | Verifying HDFS state between lab cycles; the foundation for Pig Latin and Spark experiments |

---

## 2.5 Core Differences Students MUST Know (High-Yield for ESE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These distinctions are **favourite KTU question patterns**:

### 1. `rm` vs `rmr`
- `rm` → deletes **files only** (refuses to delete a non-empty directory).
- `rmr` → **recursively** deletes a directory and all its contents.
- Modern alternative: `hdfs dfs -rm -r <path>` (preferred in newer Hadoop).

### 2. `put` vs `copyFromLocal`
- `put` is **flexible** — source can be local OR another HDFS path (in some Hadoop versions).
- `copyFromLocal` is **strict** — the source **must be a local filesystem path**; otherwise it errors out.

### 3. `get` vs `copyToLocal`
- Same as above but in reverse direction. `copyToLocal` is stricter.

### 4. `cat` vs `text`
- `cat` works for **plain text** files only.
- `text` works for **plain text AND compressed (gzip, bzip2) AND SequenceFile/Avro** files — it auto-detects and decompresses.

### 5. `du` vs `dus` vs `df`
- `du` → disk usage of a **path**.
- `dus` → disk usage **summary** of a path (deprecated).
- `df` → **free space** on the underlying filesystem (capacity view).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 3. Step-by-Step Execution Walkthrough (Lab Experiment Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> This section walks through a **typical KTU lab experiment** using Grunt. Every command is presented with its **syntax**, **purpose**, and **sample output**. This is the structure expected in your **lab record / viva voce**.

---

## 🔬 Lab Setup (Pre-experiment)

### Step 1: Start the Hadoop Services
```bash
start-dfs.sh
start-yarn.sh
```

### Step 2: Verify Daemons are Running
```bash
jps
```
**Expected Output:**
```
1234 NameNode
1356 DataNode
1478 SecondaryNameNode
1600 ResourceManager
1712 NodeManager
```
> ✅ If you see `NameNode`, `DataNode`, `ResourceManager`, `NodeManager` — Grunt is ready to use.

### Step 3: Enter the Grunt Shell
```bash
hdfs dfs
```
**Expected Prompt:**
```
hdfs>
```
You are now in the interactive Grunt shell. Every command below is entered at this prompt.

> 💡 **Tip:** To run a single command without entering the shell, use:
> `hdfs dfs -<command> <args>` directly.

---

## 🔬 Experiment Walkthrough — Complete HDFS File Operations

### **Experiment 3.1 — Directory Operations**

#### Step 1: Create a directory
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> mkdir /user/student/labdata
```
**Explanation:** Creates a new directory `labdata` inside the user's home path.
**Output:** *(none on success — silent success is normal in HDFS)*

#### Step 2: Create a nested directory (with `-p`)
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> mkdir -p /user/student/labdata/2024/sales
```
**Explanation:** The `-p` flag creates parent directories on the fly (like Unix `mkdir -p`).

#### Step 3: List the directory
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> ls /user/student/labdata
```
**Output:**
```
drwxr-xr-x   - student supergroup          0 2024-03-15 10:30 2024
```
**Explanation of columns:**
- `drwxr-xr-x` → POSIX permissions (directory, rwx for owner, r-x for group, r-x for others)
- `-` → replication count (0 for directories, since they aren't replicated as files)
- `student` → owner
- `supergroup` → group
- `0` → file/dir size in bytes
- `2024-03-15 10:30` → last modification timestamp
- `2024` → name

---

### **Experiment 3.2 — File Upload and Download**

#### Step 1: Create a local file
**Command:**
```bash
echo "Hello Hadoop Grunt Shell" > /tmp/sample.txt
cat /tmp/sample.txt
```
**Output:**
```
Hello Hadoop Grunt Shell
```

#### Step 2: Upload to HDFS using `put`
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> put /tmp/sample.txt /user/student/labdata/sample.txt
```
**Explanation:** Copies the local file `/tmp/sample.txt` into HDFS at `/user/student/labdata/`.

#### Step 3: Verify the upload
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> ls /user/student/labdata
```
**Output:**
```
-rw-r--r--   3 student supergroup         25 2024-03-15 10:35 sample.txt
drwxr-xr-x   - student supergroup          0 2024-03-15 10:30 2024
```
**Explanation:** We see the new file with:
- `rw-r--r--` → owner can read/write, group/others can read
- `3` → replication factor of **3** (HDFS default)
- `25` → file size in bytes

#### Step 4: View file content in HDFS
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> cat /user/student/labdata/sample.txt
```
**Output:**
```
Hello Hadoop Grunt Shell
```

#### Step 5: Download back to local
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> get /user/student/labdata/sample.txt /tmp/sample_downloaded.txt
```
**Verification:**
```bash
cat /tmp/sample_downloaded.txt
```
**Output:**
```
Hello Hadoop Grunt Shell
```

---

### **Experiment 3.3 — Copy, Move, and Delete**

#### Step 1: Copy within HDFS
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> cp /user/student/labdata/sample.txt /user/student/labdata/sample_copy.txt
hdfs> ls /user/student/labdata
```
**Output:**
```
-rw-r--r--   3 student supergroup         25 2024-03-15 10:35 sample.txt
-rw-r--r--   3 student supergroup         25 2024-03-15 10:40 sample_copy.txt
drwxr-xr-x   - student supergroup          0 2024-03-15 10:30 2024
```

#### Step 2: Move (rename) within HDFS
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> mv /user/student/labdata/sample_copy.txt /user/student/labdata/renamed.txt
hdfs> ls /user/student/labdata
```
**Output:**
```
-rw-r--r--   3 student supergroup         25 2024-03-15 10:35 sample.txt
-rw-r--r--   3 student supergroup         25 2024-03-15 10:40 renamed.txt
drwxr-xr-x   - student supergroup          0 2024-03-15 10:30 2024
```

#### Step 3: Delete a file
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> rm /user/student/labdata/renamed.txt
```
**Output:**
```
Deleted /user/student/labdata/renamed.txt
```

#### Step 4: Recursive delete of a directory
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> rm -r /user/student/labdata/2024
```
**Output:**
```
Deleted /user/student/labdata/2024
```

---

### **Experiment 3.4 — Disk Usage and Statistics**

#### Step 1: Check disk usage
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> du -h /user/student/labdata
```
**Output:**
```
25  /user/student/labdata/sample.txt
```

#### Step 2: Check filesystem capacity
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> df -h /
```
**Output:**
```
Filesystem                  Size    Used   Available  Use%
hdfs://localhost:9000  50.0 GB  1.2 GB     48.8 GB    2%
```

#### Step 3: Display file statistics
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> stat "%b blocks %n replication %r" /user/student/labdata/sample.txt
```
**Output:**
```
1 blocks sample.txt replication 3
```
**Explanation:** The file occupies **1 block** (default block size = 128 MB) and has **3 replicas**.

---

### **Experiment 3.5 — Permissions and Replication**

#### Step 1: Change file permissions
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> chmod 600 /user/student/labdata/sample.txt
hdfs> ls /user/student/labdata/sample.txt
```
**Output:**
```
-rw-------   3 student supergroup         25 2024-03-15 10:35 sample.txt
```

#### Step 2: Change replication factor
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> setrep 1 /user/student/labdata/sample.txt
```
**Output:**
```
Replication 1 set: /user/student/labdata/sample.txt
```

#### Step 3: Change owner
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> chown hadoopuser:hadoopgroup /user/student/labdata/sample.txt
```

---

### **Experiment 3.6 — Exiting the Grunt Shell**
**Command:**
```bash
hdfs> exit
```
Or press `Ctrl + C`.

---

## 📊 Analytical Matrix — Command Selection Guide

> [!NOTE]
> Use this decision matrix in your viva to choose the right command for a given task.

| **Your Task** | **Recommended Command** | **Why** |
|---|---|---|
| I want to see what is inside a folder | `ls` | Fast, non-recursive |
| I want to see ALL nested contents | `ls -R` or `lsr` | Recursive traversal |
| I want to upload a local file to HDFS | `put` or `copyFromLocal` | Both work; `put` is more flexible |
| I want to download an HDFS file | `get` or `copyToLocal` | `get` is more flexible |
| I want to read a compressed file | `text` | Auto-decompresses |
| I want to delete a directory | `rm -r` or `rmr` | Recursive removal |
| I want to increase data safety | `setrep 3` (or higher) | More replicas = more fault tolerance |
| I want to make a file private | `chmod 600` | Owner-only access |
| I want to check how big a file is | `du -h` | Human-readable size |
| I want to free up trash space | `expunge` | Empties `.Trash` directory |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture Diagram — Grunt's Position in the Hadoop Stack

The following Mermaid diagram shows where Grunt fits in the layered Hadoop architecture and how a user command flows from the terminal to HDFS storage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph UserLayer["USER LAYER (Terminal / Shell Script)"]
        U1[Analyst / Developer]
        U2[Linux Bash Terminal]
        U3[Jenkins / Airflow DAG]
    end

    subgraph GruntLayer["GRUNT SHELL LAYER (hdfs dfs / pig)"]
        G1[Command Parser]
        G2[Argument Tokenizer]
        G3[URIs and Path Resolver]
        G4[Help Subsystem]
    end

    subgraph APILayer["HADOOP FILESYSTEM API LAYER"]
        A1[FileSystem Abstract Class]
        A2[Configuration Reader core-site.xml]
        A3[DistributedFileSystem HDFS Impl]
        A4[LocalFileSystem Impl]
        A5[S3AFileSystem Impl]
    end

    subgraph RPCLayer["NETWORK and RPC LAYER"]
        R1[hadoop RPC Client]
        R2[Data Transfer Protocol]
    end

    subgraph StorageLayer["PHYSICAL STORAGE LAYER"]
        S1[NameNode Metadata RAM plus Disk]
        D1[DataNode 1 Disk]
        D2[DataNode 2 Disk]
        D3[DataNode 3 Disk]
    end

    U1 --> U2
    U2 --> G1
    U3 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> A1
    G2 --> G4
    A1 --> A2
    A1 --> A3
    A1 --> A4
    A1 --> A5
    A3 --> R1
    A3 --> R2
    R1 --> S1
    R2 --> D1
    R2 --> D2
    R2 --> D3
```

### 📖 Reading the Diagram
- **User Layer** → The human or automation pipeline that issues a command.
- **Grunt Shell Layer** → Parses the command, identifies flags and paths, and offers `help`.
- **FileSystem API Layer** → The `org.apache.hadoop.fs.FileSystem` abstract class decides whether to use HDFS, Local FS, or S3 based on the URI scheme.
- **RPC + Data Transfer Layer** → Grunt talks to the NameNode for metadata, and to DataNodes for actual data blocks.
- **Storage Layer** → The actual disks holding the data and metadata.

---

## 4.2 Command Flow Diagram — `put` Command End-to-End

This diagram traces what happens internally when you type `hdfs dfs -put localfile.txt /hdfs/dest/`.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[User types hdfs dfs -put localfile.txt /hdfs/dest/] --> P2[Grunt parses put and URI]
    P2 --> P3[FileSystem.get Configuration reads core-site.xml]
    P3 --> P4{Is destination HDFS?}
    P4 -->|Yes| P5[Get DistributedFileSystem instance]
    P4 -->|No| P6[Get LocalFileSystem or S3A instance]
    P5 --> P7[NameNode RPC create /hdfs/dest/localfile.txt]
    P7 --> P8[NameNode allocates block IDs and DataNode list]
    P8 --> P9[Client writes data to first DataNode]
    P9 --> P10[DataNode 1 pipelines to DataNode 2]
    P10 --> P11[DataNode 2 pipelines to DataNode 3]
    P11 --> P12[All DataNodes ack success]
    P12 --> P13[Client sends complete to NameNode]
    P13 --> P14[Grunt returns to prompt]
```

### 📖 Reading the Diagram
- This is the **HDFS write pipeline** in action.
- Notice the **3-replica default**: data is written to 3 DataNodes for fault tolerance.
- The NameNode only handles **metadata** (block IDs, DataNode locations); the actual bytes flow **DataNode-to-DataNode** without going through the NameNode.

---

## 4.3 Command Categorization Mind Map

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Grunt Commands))
    Navigation
      ls
      lsr
      du
      df
      count
      stat
    Directory Ops
      mkdir
      rmdir via rm
    File Transfer
      put
      get
      copyFromLocal
      copyToLocal
      moveFromLocal
      getmerge
      appendToFile
    View
      cat
      text
      head
      tail
    Manipulation
      cp
      mv
      rm
      rmr
      expunge
      truncate
      touchz
    Security
      chmod
      chown
      chgrp
    Reliability
      setrep
    Snapshots
      createSnapshot
      deleteSnapshot
    Diagnostics
      test
      help
      exit
```

This mind map is **directly useful for the lab viva** — examiners often ask: *"Categorize the HDFS shell commands you've used."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** 
**What is the Grunt shell in Hadoop? List any four file management commands supported by it with their syntax.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Grunt is an interactive command-line shell provided by Apache Hadoop that allows users to directly perform file and directory operations on the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) and other Hadoop-supported filesystems. It abstracts the underlying Java `FileSystem` API and provides a Unix-like interface.
>
> **Four Commands (1.5 marks):**
>
> | Command | Syntax | Purpose |
> |---|---|---|
> | `ls` | `hdfs dfs -ls <path>` | List files/directories |
> | `mkdir` | `hdfs dfs -mkdir <dir>` | Create a directory |
> | `put` | `hdfs dfs -put <local> <hdfs>` | Upload local file to HDFS |
> | `rm` | `hdfs dfs -rm <path>` | Delete a file |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between the `rm` and `rmr` commands in the Hadoop Grunt shell. When would you prefer `expunge` over both?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **`rm` (1 mark):** The `rm` command deletes **only files** in HDFS. If used on a non-empty directory, it returns an error: *"rm: cannot remove <path>: It's a directory"*. To delete a directory, you must use `rm -r` (in modern Hadoop) or `rmr` (legacy).
>
> **`rmr` (1 mark):** The `rmr` command stands for *"remove recursively"*. It deletes a directory along with **all its files and sub-directories** in a single operation. It is the equivalent of `rm -r` in Unix.
>
> **`expunge` (1 mark):** When a file is deleted in HDFS, it is moved to a **trash directory** (`.Trash`) for a configurable retention period. The `expunge` command is used to **permanently and immediately empty the trash**, freeing up the storage space that the deleted files were occupying. It is preferred when you want to reclaim disk space right away, beyond what `rm`/`rmr` provide.

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 ESE, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with a choice between two questions. Each question typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**Explain the Hadoop Grunt shell in detail. Discuss its purpose, features, and demonstrate any 10 important HDFS commands with their syntax, purpose, and example usage.**

#### **Part (a) — Purpose and Features of Grunt (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition and Purpose (2 marks):**
The Grunt shell is Hadoop's interactive command-line interface (CLI) for performing file and directory operations on HDFS. Its primary purposes are:
- **Ad-hoc file manipulation** without writing Java programs.
- **Verification** of HDFS state during data pipeline development.
- **Automation** through shell scripting in production environments.
- **Administrative tasks** like setting permissions, replication, and managing trash.

**2. Architectural Position (2 marks):**
Grunt sits on top of the **Hadoop FileSystem API** (`org.apache.hadoop.fs.FileSystem`). Internally, it instantiates the appropriate `FileSystem` implementation (e.g., `DistributedFileSystem` for `hdfs://` URIs, `LocalFileSystem` for `file:///` URIs) based on the URI scheme provided by the user. For metadata operations, it communicates with the **NameNode** via Hadoop RPC; for data transfer (e.g., `put`, `get`), it uses the **Data Transfer Protocol** to stream blocks directly between the client and **DataNodes**.

**3. Key Features (3 marks):**
- **Interactive REPL** with prompts like `hdfs>` or `grunt>`.
- **Uniform interface** — same commands work on HDFS, Local FS, S3, WebHDFS.
- **URI-based addressing** — supports `hdfs://`, `file:///`, `s3a://`, `wasb://`.
- **Recursive variants** for most commands (`lsr`, `rmr`).
- **Built-in help** via `help <command>`.
- **Default working directory** at `/user/<username>`.
- **Scriptable** — can be invoked non-interactively (`hdfs dfs -cmd args`).

#### **Part (b) — Demonstration of 10 Important Commands (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Command | Syntax | Example | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `ls` | `hdfs dfs -ls <path>` | `hdfs dfs -ls /user/student/` | List directory contents |
| 2 | `mkdir` | `hdfs dfs -mkdir <dir>` | `hdfs dfs -mkdir /data/2024` | Create directory |
| 3 | `put` | `hdfs dfs -put <local> <hdfs>` | `hdfs dfs -put sales.csv /data/` | Upload file to HDFS |
| 4 | `get` | `hdfs dfs -get <hdfs> <local>` | `hdfs dfs -get /data/sales.csv .` | Download file from HDFS |
| 5 | `cat` | `hdfs dfs -cat <file>` | `hdfs dfs -cat /data/sales.csv` | Display file contents |
| 6 | `cp` | `hdfs dfs -cp <src> <dst>` | `hdfs dfs -cp /data/a /data/b` | Copy within HDFS |
| 7 | `mv` | `hdfs dfs -mv <src> <dst>` | `hdfs dfs -mv /data/a /archive/` | Move within HDFS |
| 8 | `rm` | `hdfs dfs -rm <file>` | `hdfs dfs -rm /data/old.csv` | Delete a file |
| 9 | `rmr` | `hdfs dfs -rmr <dir>` | `hdfs dfs -rmr /data/temp/` | Recursive directory delete |
| 10 | `chmod` | `hdfs dfs -chmod <mode> <path>` | `hdfs dfs -chmod 644 /data/sales.csv` | Change file permissions |

**Example Workflow Demonstrating These Commands:**

```bash
# 1. Start Grunt shell
$ hdfs dfs

# 2. Create a directory
hdfs> mkdir /user/student/demo

# 3. Upload a local file
hdfs> put /home/student/data.txt /user/student/demo/

# 4. Verify
hdfs> ls /user/student/demo
# Output: -rw-r--r--  3 student supergroup  1024 2024-03-15 11:00 data.txt

# 5. View the file
hdfs> cat /user/student/demo/data.txt

# 6. Make a copy
hdfs> cp /user/student/demo/data.txt /user/student/demo/data_backup.txt

# 7. Move the backup to an archive folder
hdfs> mkdir /user/student/archive
hdfs> mv /user/student/demo/data_backup.txt /user/student/archive/

# 8. Restrict permissions
hdfs> chmod 600 /user/student/demo/data.txt

# 9. Delete the demo folder
hdfs> rmr /user/student/demo

# 10. Exit
hdfs> exit
```

> ✅ **End of Question A (14 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**Write short notes on the Grunt shell, and demonstrate the following HDFS operations with Grunt commands: (a) Creating a directory tree and uploading multiple files; (b) Modifying replication, permissions, and deletion with trash management.**

#### **Part (a) — Directory Creation and File Upload (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Theory (2 marks):** The Grunt shell provides the `mkdir` command for directory creation (with `-p` for nested parents) and the `put` / `copyFromLocal` commands for uploading local files into HDFS. The `put` command is more flexible and can also copy between HDFS paths, while `copyFromLocal` strictly requires a local source.

**Step-by-Step Demonstration (5 marks):**

**Step 1: Create a nested directory tree using `-p`:**
```bash
hdfs> mkdir -p /user/student/project/input
hdfs> mkdir -p /user/student/project/output
hdfs> mkdir -p /user/student/project/logs
```
**Explanation:** The `-p` flag creates all parent directories on the fly — analogous to `mkdir -p` in Linux. Without `-p`, the parent (`/user/student/project`) must exist first.

**Step 2: Verify the directory structure:**
```bash
hdfs> ls -R /user/student/project
```
**Output:**
```
drwxr-xr-x   - student supergroup          0 2024-03-15 11:10 /user/student/project/input
drwxr-xr-x   - student supergroup          0 2024-03-15 11:10 /user/student/project/output
drwxr-xr-x   - student supergroup          0 2024-03-15 11:10 /user/student/project/logs
```

**Step 3: Create three local files for upload:**
```bash
echo "alpha,beta,gamma" > /tmp/file1.csv
echo "1,2,3" > /tmp/file2.csv
echo "row1" > /tmp/file3.csv
```

**Step 4: Upload all three files into the `input` directory:**
```bash
hdfs> put /tmp/file1.csv /user/student/project/input/
hdfs> put /tmp/file2.csv /user/student/project/input/
hdfs> put /tmp/file3.csv /user/student/project/input/
```
**Alternative (uploading multiple files at once):**
```bash
hdfs> put /tmp/file1.csv /tmp/file2.csv /tmp/file3.csv /user/student/project/input/
```

**Step 5: Verify uploads and check sizes:**
```bash
hdfs> ls -h /user/student/project/input
```
**Output:**
```
-rw-r--r--   3 student supergroup         13 2024-03-15 11:15 file1.csv
-rw-r--r--   3 student supergroup          6 2024-03-15 11:15 file2.csv
-rw-r--r--   3 student supergroup          5 2024-03-15 11:15 file3.csv
```

**Step 6: Concatenate and view file contents:**
```bash
hdfs> cat /user/student/project/input/file1.csv
hdfs> cat /user/student/project/input/file2.csv
hdfs> cat /user/student/project/input/file3.csv
```

#### **Part (b) — Replication, Permissions, and Deletion with Trash (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Theory (2 marks):** HDFS provides **fault tolerance** through block replication (default 3). The `setrep` command changes the replication factor of an existing file. POSIX-style permissions are managed via `chmod`, `chown`, and `chgrp`. Files deleted in HDFS are moved to a trash directory (`.Trash/<username>/`) and retained for a configurable period (`fs.trash.interval`, default 1 day). The `expunge` command forces permanent deletion of trash contents.

**Step-by-Step Demonstration (5 marks):**

**Step 1: Check current replication of `file1.csv`:**
```bash
hdfs> stat "%r" /user/student/project/input/file1.csv
```
**Output:** `3`  *(meaning 3 replicas)*

**Step 2: Reduce replication to 1 (for testing purposes):**
```bash
hdfs> setrep 1 /user/student/project/input/file1.csv
```
**Output:**
```
Replication 1 set: /user/student/project/input/file1.csv
```
**Note:** If the cluster has only 1 DataNode, HDFS may issue a warning but will set it.

**Step 3: Check current permissions:**
```bash
hdfs> ls /user/student/project/input/file1.csv
```
**Output:** `-rw-r--r-- 1 student supergroup 13 2024-03-15 11:15 file1.csv`

**Step 4: Change permissions to owner-only (mode 600):**
```bash
hdfs> chmod 600 /user/student/project/input/file1.csv
hdfs> ls /user/student/project/input/file1.csv
```
**Output:** `-rw------- 1 student supergroup 13 2024-03-15 11:15 file1.csv`

**Step 5: Change group ownership to `hadoop`:**
```bash
hdfs> chgrp hadoop /user/student/project/input/file1.csv
hdfs> ls /user/student/project/input/file1.csv
```
**Output:** `-rw------- 1 student hadoop 13 2024-03-15 11:15 file1.csv`

**Step 6: Delete a file (it moves to trash):**
```bash
hdfs> rm /user/student/project/input/file2.csv
```
**Output:**
```
Deleted: /user/student/project/input/file2.csv
```

**Step 7: Verify it is in trash:**
```bash
hdfs> ls /user/student/.Trash/Current/user/student/project/input/
```
**Output:**
```
-rw-r--r--  3 student supergroup  6 2024-03-15 11:20 file2.csv
```

**Step 8: Empty the trash permanently:**
```bash
hdfs> expunge
```
**Output:**
```
Trash is emptied.
```

> ✅ **End of Question B (14 marks)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in Grunt-related questions (and how to avoid losing marks):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `hdfs dfs` prefix** — The Grunt shell command is `hdfs dfs -<cmd> <args>`, not just `<cmd>`. Writing just `ls /user/student` is worth 0 marks because it invokes the **local** Linux `ls`, not the HDFS one. *(−2 marks deduction typical)*
>
> 2. **Confusing `put` source direction** — `put` copies **from** local **to** HDFS, not the other way. For HDFS-to-local, use `get`. *(−1 mark per wrong direction)*
>
> 3. **Mixing up `rm` and `rmr`** — Writing `rm` to delete a directory will fail in the lab and cost you marks in theory. Always specify `rm -r` or `rmr` for directories.
>
> 4. **Forgetting `setrep` does not affect existing data immediately on all clusters** — On misconfigured clusters, replication may not drop; in theory answers, mention that the **NameNode schedules** additional replications/over-deletions in the background.
>
> 5. **Using the wrong default home directory** — The default is `/user/<username>`, NOT `/` or `/home`. Examiners deduct marks for this.
>
> 6. **Confusing `du` and `df`** — `du` shows usage of a **path**; `df` shows **free space of the whole filesystem**. A common viva trap.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning the URI scheme** — When asked to write a command for S3 or another FS, students forget the `s3a://` prefix and lose marks for missing the **filesystem abstraction** concept.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the KTU 2024 ESE — print this and revise 5 minutes before the exam!**

- ✅ **Grunt = Hadoop's interactive shell** for HDFS file operations, built on top of the `org.apache.hadoop.fs.FileSystem` API.
- ✅ **Two entry points:** `hdfs dfs` (HDFS shell) and `pig` (Pig Latin shell, also called Grunt).
- ✅ **Invocation forms:** Interactive (`hdfs dfs` then `hdfs>` prompt) OR one-shot (`hdfs dfs -<cmd> <args>`).
- ✅ **Default working directory:** `/user/<username>`.
- ✅ **Most-used commands:** `ls`, `mkdir`, `put`, `get`, `cat`, `cp`, `mv`, `rm`, `rmr`, `chmod`, `chown`, `chgrp`, `setrep`, `du`, `df`, `stat`, `expunge`, `help`.
- ✅ **`put` vs `copyFromLocal`:** `put` is flexible; `copyFromLocal` is strict (local source only).
- ✅ **`cat` vs `text`:** `cat` is for plain text; `text` handles compressed and SequenceFiles.
- ✅ **`rm` vs `rmr`:** `rm` deletes files only; `rmr` (or `rm -r`) deletes directories recursively.
- ✅ **`du` vs `df`:** `du` = usage of a path; `df` = free space of the whole FS.
- ✅ **`setrep <n>`** changes the replication factor; default is **3**.
- ✅ **Trash behavior:** Deleted files go to `/user/<username>/.Trash` for the configured retention period; `expunge` empties it.
- ✅ **Default block size:** **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x+; default replication: **3**.
- ✅ **Architecture:** Grunt → FileSystem API → NameNode (metadata) + DataNodes (data).
- ✅ **Real-world uses:** ETL operations, ad-hoc verification, CI/CD pipelines, cluster administration.
- ✅ **Engineering tip:** Always verify with `ls` after a `put`/`rm`/`mv` — silent success can mask missing files in cross-cluster operations.

> 🏆 **Master these 20 commands + the 7 distinctions, and you will comfortably score full marks on any Grunt question in the KTU 2024 B.Tech Big Data Analytics exam.** All the best! 🚀

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pig Latin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Pig Latin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pig Latin** is a data-flow scripting language designed to analyze and process large, semi-structured, and unstructured datasets in the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** using the **Apache Pig** platform. It operates as a higher-level abstraction over **MapReduce**, allowing developers to write complex data transformations without explicitly writing low-level Java MapReduce jobs.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBADT504)** syllabus, Pig Latin forms the cornerstone of high-level data transformation scripting in the Hadoop ecosystem. It is formally defined as:

$$\text{Pig Latin Script} \xrightarrow{\text{Apache Pig Compiler}} \text{Optimized MapReduce Job (Java)}$$

The language is declarative, meaning the user specifies **what** transformation is required rather than **how** to execute it. The compiler (which can be run in *MapReduce mode*, *Tez mode*, or *Local mode*) handles the execution plan generation, optimization, and execution.

### Key Architectural Components

| Component | Role in Pig Ecosystem |
|---|---|
| **Pig Latin** | The scripting language used to express data flows |
| **Grunt Shell** | Interactive command-line interface for executing Pig commands |
| **Pig Server** | Translates Pig Latin scripts into MapReduce jobs |
| **Execution Engine** | Runs the generated jobs on Hadoop (MapReduce / Tez / Spark) |
| **HDFS** | Source and sink (storage) layer for input/output data |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Factory Assembly Line**

Imagine you are running a large chocolate factory. Raw cocoa beans arrive in huge sacks (your **raw data** in HDFS). You do not want every worker to learn the complex chemistry of fermentation and roasting (analogous to writing **Java MapReduce** code). Instead, you create a simple **instruction sheet** (Pig Latin script) written in plain English:

> *"Take the beans → Clean them → Roast them → Crush them → Pack them."*

The factory manager (the **Apache Pig Compiler**) reads this simple instruction sheet, breaks it down into the precise chemical engineering steps, optimizes the workflow for efficiency, and dispatches the workers (the **Execution Engine**) to do the actual job. You, as the factory owner (developer), do not need to know the chemistry. You just write the instruction sheet.

This is exactly how Pig Latin operates in Big Data Analytics. It democratizes Hadoop by allowing data analysts, statisticians, and SQL-proficient engineers to process petabytes of data without learning Java.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Pig Latin

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard terminologies are **mandatory for KTU exams**:

- **Bag**: An **unordered collection of tuples** (allows duplicates). Written as `{ }`. Equivalent to a SQL table.
- **Tuple**: An **ordered set of fields**. Written as `( )`. Equivalent to a SQL row.
- **Field**: A single piece of data (atom). Equivalent to a SQL column cell.
- **Atom**: A simple data value — `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `chararray` (string), or `bytearray`.
- **Relation**: A bag of tuples. The fundamental data structure that flows through Pig Latin statements.
- **Default Storage Format**: **PigStorage** (text files with tab-separated fields).

## 1.4 Why Pig Latin? — The Problem It Solves

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Learning Outcome (CO2):**
> *"Apply high-level scripting languages like Pig Latin to process and analyze large datasets."*

Writing **10 lines of Pig Latin ≈ 200 lines of Java MapReduce code**. This **20x productivity gain** is the primary reason Pig Latin is a KTU board exam favorite.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Pig Latin Execution Modes (Critical for Exams)

Pig Latin scripts can be executed in **three modes**, and KTU questions frequently test this:

| Mode | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Local Mode** | Runs on a single JVM (no Hadoop) | Testing, small datasets, learning |
| **MapReduce Mode (Default)** | Converts Pig Latin to MapReduce jobs on HDFS | Production Big Data processing |
| **Tez Mode** | Uses Apache Tez for DAG-based execution | Faster than MapReduce, optimized for interactive queries |

The command to invoke each mode:

```bash
pig -x local          # Local mode
pig -x mapreduce      # MapReduce mode (default if HDFS is configured)
pig -x tez            # Tez mode
```

## 2.2 Pig Latin Data Model — The Atomic Hierarchy

Pig Latin's data model is a **nested relational** model, which is more flexible than the flat relational model of SQL.

$$\text{Relation} \supset \text{Bag} \supset \text{Tuple} \supset \text{Field (Atom)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction**: A *Bag* in Pig Latin is **NOT a relation** in the SQL sense. A bag allows duplicates and is unordered. A relation is just the *outermost* bag produced after each statement.

## 2.3 Pig Latin Statement Structure

Every Pig Latin statement operates on a **relation** and produces a **new relation**. The structure follows this pattern:

$$\text{Result Relation} = \text{Operator} \;\text{[Input Relation 1]} \;\text{[Input Relation 2]}$$

Statements are processed **lazily** until a `DUMP` or `STORE` command is encountered. This is called **lazy evaluation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lazy Evaluation Rule**: No actual computation happens until a `DUMP` (display) or `STORE` (save to HDFS) is executed. The compiler builds a **Logical Plan** and **Physical Plan** in memory.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Pig Latin Operators

| Operator Category | Operator | KTU Notation | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Loading / Storing** | `LOAD` | $\mathcal{L}(path, schema)$ | Read data from HDFS/local FS |
| | `STORE` | $\mathcal{S}(rel, path)$ | Write relation to HDFS |
| | `DUMP` | $\mathcal{D}(rel)$ | Print to console |
| **Filtering** | `FILTER` | $\sigma_{condition}(R)$ | Remove unwanted tuples (SQL WHERE) |
| **Projection** | `FOREACH ... GENERATE` | $\pi_{fields}(R)$ | Select/transform columns (SQL SELECT) |
| **Sorting** | `ORDER` | $\tau_{key}(R)$ | Sort tuples by key (ascending default) |
| **Grouping** | `GROUP` | $\gamma_{key}(R)$ | Group tuples by a key (SQL GROUP BY) |
| **Joining** | `JOIN` | $R \bowtie_{key} S$ | Inner join two relations |
| | `LEFT OUTER JOIN` | $R \ltimes_{key} S$ | Left outer join |
| **Set Operations** | `UNION` | $R \cup S$ | Combine two relations |
| | `DISTINCT** | $\delta(R)$ | Remove duplicate tuples |
| **Aggregation** | Built-in UDFs | `SUM, COUNT, AVG, MAX, MIN` | Aggregate over grouped bags |
| **Splitting** | `SPLIT` | Partition R into multiple R's | Conditional branching |
| **Combining** | `COGROUP` | Group + Join hybrid | Group multiple relations together |
| **Diagnostic** | `DESCRIBE` | Show schema of a relation | — |
| | `EXPLAIN` | Show execution plans | — |
| | `ILLUSTRATE` | Sample-based dry run | — |

## 2.5 The Logical and Physical Plan

When a Pig script is run, the architecture internally generates:

$$\text{Pig Script} \rightarrow \text{Logical Plan (DAG)} \rightarrow \text{Physical Plan (MapReduce Jobs)} \rightarrow \text{Execution}$$

The **Logical Plan** is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) where nodes are operators and edges are data flows. The **Physical Plan** maps this to actual MapReduce stages. The `EXPLAIN` command lets you visualize both.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Use Case — Log Analysis at Scale**:
> A telecommunications company generates **~500 TB of CDRs (Call Detail Records) daily**. Using Pig Latin, an analyst can:
> 1. Load the raw CDR files from HDFS.
> 2. Filter out failed calls (`FILTER`).
> 3. Group by phone number (`GROUP`).
> 4. Aggregate call durations (`SUM`).
> 5. Store the top 100 heavy callers (`ORDER ... LIMIT 100`).
>
> This entire pipeline — written in ~15 lines of Pig Latin — replaces a 300+ line Java MapReduce program.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Sample Scripts & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Construction of a Pig Latin Script

Let us derive a complete Pig Latin script by progressively building it from raw requirements. This is the **exact KTU 14-mark question pattern** — *"Write a Pig Latin script to perform XYZ on dataset ABC."*

### **Step 1 — The Requirement**
> *"Given a CSV file `students.txt` containing student marks (name, subject, marks), find the average marks per subject and display students who scored above 80."*

### **Step 2 — Input Data Assumption**
Assume the input `students.txt` is stored on HDFS at `/user/ktu/students.txt` with the following tab-separated content:

```
Arjun   Maths   85
Arya    Physics 92
Meera   Maths   78
John    Physics 65
Lakshmi Maths   88
```

### **Step 3 — Load the Data with a Schema**

```pig
-- Step 3: Load the data and assign a schema
students = LOAD '/user/ktu/students.txt'
           USING PigStorage('\t')
           AS (name:chararray, subject:chararray, marks:int);
```

**Logic**: `LOAD` reads the file. `PigStorage('\t')` specifies the delimiter. The `AS` clause assigns typed field names. The resulting relation `students` is a **bag of tuples** where each tuple is `(chararray, chararray, int)`.

### **Step 4 — Filter for High Scorers**

```pig
-- Step 4: Filter students who scored above 80
high_scorers = FILTER students BY marks > 80;
```

**Logic**: $\sigma_{marks > 80}(students)$ — analogous to SQL `WHERE marks > 80`. This produces a new bag containing only the tuples satisfying the predicate.

### **Step 5 — Group by Subject**

```pig
-- Step 5: Group the original data by subject
grouped = GROUP students BY subject;
```

**Logic**: $\gamma_{subject}(students)$ — creates a bag where each tuple has the form `(subject, bag_of_all_tuples_with_that_subject)`.

### **Step 6 — Compute Average Marks per Subject**

```pig
-- Step 6: Apply FOREACH ... GENERATE to compute the average
avg_marks = FOREACH grouped GENERATE
                group AS subject,
                AVG(students.marks) AS average_marks;
```

**Logic**: This performs $\pi_{subject, AVG(marks)}(\gamma_{subject}(students))$. The keyword `group` is a **special Pig Latin pseudo-column** that refers to the grouping key. Note the fully qualified field name `students.marks` because Pig must disambiguate which bag's field to aggregate.

### **Step 7 — Display and Store the Results**

```pig
-- Step 7: DUMP to console (triggers execution)
DUMP high_scorers;
DUMP avg_marks;

-- Store aggregated result to HDFS
STORE avg_marks INTO '/user/ktu/output_avg'
     USING PigStorage(',');
```

**Logic**: `DUMP` triggers **lazy evaluation** and prints to the Grunt shell console. `STORE` writes the result as comma-separated values to HDFS.

### **Complete Consolidated Script**

```pig
-- ==========================================
-- KTU PBADT504 — Pig Latin Complete Script
-- Author: Big Data Analytics Lab Manual
-- ==========================================

-- (a) Load dataset
students = LOAD '/user/ktu/students.txt'
           USING PigStorage('\t')
           AS (name:chararray, subject:chararray, marks:int);

-- (b) Describe the schema
DESCRIBE students;

-- (c) Filter high scorers
high_scorers = FILTER students BY marks > 80;

-- (d) Group by subject
grouped = GROUP students BY subject;

-- (e) Compute average
avg_marks = FOREACH grouped GENERATE
                group AS subject,
                AVG(students.marks) AS average_marks;

-- (f) Order subjects by average descending
sorted_avg = ORDER avg_marks BY average_marks DESC;

-- (g) Output
DUMP high_scorers;
DUMP sorted_avg;

STORE sorted_avg INTO '/user/ktu/output_avg'
     USING PigStorage(',');
```

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Operator-by-Operator Worked Example

Using the same input dataset, we will trace the **transformation pipeline** for the two most KTU-frequent operators.

### **3.2.1 Worked Example: FOREACH ... GENERATE**

This is the **most frequently tested** Pig Latin construct. The general form is:

$$
\text{new\_rel} = \text{FOREACH} \; R \; \text{GENERATE} \; expr_1, expr_2, \ldots, expr_n
$$

**Derivation** (per tuple of input bag R):
For each tuple $t \in R$, compute $expr_i(t)$ for each $i$, and emit a new tuple $(expr_1(t), expr_2(t), \ldots, expr_n(t))$.

**Numerical Trace** (per tuple of `students`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= (\text{Arjun}, \text{Maths}, 85) \;\xrightarrow{\text{GENERATE name, marks}}\; (\text{Arjun}, 85) \\
t_2 &= (\text{Arya}, \text{Physics}, 92) \;\xrightarrow{\text{GENERATE name, marks}}\; (\text{Arya}, 92) \\
t_3 &= (\text{Meera}, \text{Maths}, 78) \;\xrightarrow{\text{GENERATE name, marks}}\; (\text{Meera}, 78) \\
t_4 &= (\text{John}, \text{Physics}, 65) \;\xrightarrow{\text{GENERATE name, marks}}\; (\text{John}, 65) \\
t_5 &= (\text{Lakshmi}, \text{Maths}, 88) \;\xrightarrow{\text{GENERATE name, marks}}\; (\text{Lakshmi}, 88) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The final output bag is:

$$
\text{proj\_rel} = \{(\text{Arjun}, 85), (\text{Arya}, 92), (\text{Meera}, 78), (\text{John}, 65), (\text{Lakshmi}, 88)\}
$$

### **3.2.2 Worked Example: GROUP**

**Derivation**: Partition the bag $R$ into $k$ sub-bags, one for each distinct value of the grouping key.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{grouped}(\text{Maths})   &= \{(\text{Arjun}, \text{Maths}, 85), (\text{Meera}, \text{Maths}, 78), (\text{Lakshmi}, \text{Maths}, 88)\} \\
\text{grouped}(\text{Physics}) &= \{(\text{Arya}, \text{Physics}, 92), (\text{John}, \text{Physics}, 65)\} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The output relation `grouped` has the schema:

$$
(\text{group} : \text{chararray}, \;\text{students} : \text{bag}\{t_1\})
$$

where $t_1 = (\text{name} : \text{chararray}, \text{subject} : \text{chararray}, \text{marks} : \text{int})$.

### **3.2.3 Worked Example: JOIN (Self-Join)**

Assume we have a second relation `grades = LOAD 'grades.txt' AS (subject:chararray, grade:chararray)`.

```pig
joined = JOIN students BY subject, grades BY subject;
```

**Derivation**: Cartesian product filtered by equality of join keys — identical to relational algebra's natural join.

$$
\text{joined} = \sigma_{\text{students.subject} = \text{grades.subject}}(\text{students} \times \text{grades})
$$

**Resulting tuples** (after inner join on `subject`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\text{Arjun}, \text{Maths}, 85, \text{Maths}, A) \\
(\text{Arya}, \text{Physics}, 92, \text{Physics}, A+) \\
(\text{Meera}, \text{Maths}, 78, \text{Maths}, B+) \\
(\text{John}, \text{Physics}, 65, \text{Physics}, B) \\
(\text{Lakshmi}, \text{Maths}, 88, \text{Maths}, A) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Nested FOREACH — Advanced Construct (KTU Advanced)

When the grouped relation contains nested bags, you can use a **nested FOREACH** to perform multi-stage transformations:

```pig
-- For each subject, find the top-scoring student
top_per_subject = FOREACH grouped {
                    sorted = ORDER students BY marks DESC;
                    top    = LIMIT sorted 1;
                    GENERATE group AS subject, FLATTEN(top);
                  };
```

**Step-by-step logic**:
1. For each outer tuple in `grouped` (one per subject).
2. The inner block re-sorts that subject's inner bag.
3. Limits to the top 1.
4. `FLATTEN` unwraps the inner bag back into the outer tuple stream.

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: How a Single FOREACH Translates to MapReduce

This is the **most KTU-relevant schematic** — it shows how a single Pig Latin `FOREACH ... GENERATE` operation is decomposed into actual Hadoop MapReduce stages.

```mermaid
    D --> E[Identity Reducer: pass-through]
    E --> F[HDFS Output Directory]

    mapperInput["HDFS Input Block: /user/ktu/students.txt"] --> D
    F --> G[Result Relation: projected bag]

    reducerTask[Optional Reducer]:::reduceStep
    hdfsStep[HDFS Storage]:::hdfsStep
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define Pig Latin. List any four data types used in the Pig Latin data model.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Pig Latin is a high-level, data-flow scripting language used in Apache Pig to analyze and process large datasets stored in HDFS. It provides an abstraction over MapReduce, allowing users to write transformations without writing low-level Java code.

**Data Types (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Atom** — A simple value of type `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `chararray`, or `bytearray`.
2. **Tuple** — An ordered set of fields, written as `( )`. Equivalent to a row in SQL.
3. **Bag** — An unordered collection of tuples (allows duplicates), written as `{ }`. Equivalent to a table in SQL.
4. **Map** — A set of key-value pairs, written as `[key#value]`.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between `DUMP` and `STORE` operators in Pig Latin.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `DUMP` | `STORE` |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Displays a relation's contents on the Grunt console | Saves a relation to the file system (HDFS or local) |
| **Output Location** | Standard output (stdout) — non-persistent | HDFS or local directory — persistent |
| **Typical Use** | Debugging and quick verification | Production data persistence |
| **Execution Trigger** | Yes — triggers lazy evaluation | Yes — triggers lazy evaluation |
| **Format** | Default text format on screen | Controlled via `USING PigStorage`, `JsonStorage`, etc. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — University Pattern, Module 2 Choice]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Internal Choice Option A]**

The `sales.txt` file on HDFS contains e-commerce transaction records in the following format:
`transaction_id, product, category, price, quantity, city`

**(a)** Write a complete Pig Latin script to: (i) load the data with an appropriate schema, (ii) filter out transactions where `price < 100`, (iii) compute the total revenue (`price * quantity`) per `category`, and (iv) display the top 3 categories by revenue in descending order. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Lazy Evaluation mechanism in Pig Latin. What is the role of `DUMP` and `STORE` in triggering it? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

```pig
-- ==========================================
-- Q(a): Pig Latin Script for Sales Analysis
-- ==========================================

-- (i) Load the dataset with a typed schema
sales = LOAD '/user/ktu/sales.txt'
        USING PigStorage(',')
        AS (transaction_id:chararray,
            product:chararray,
            category:chararray,
            price:float,
            quantity:int,
            city:chararray);

-- (ii) Filter out low-value transactions
filtered_sales = FILTER sales BY price >= 100;

-- (iii) Compute revenue per transaction first
with_revenue = FOREACH filtered_sales GENERATE
                   category,
                   (price * quantity) AS revenue;

-- Group by category
grouped_sales = GROUP with_revenue BY category;

-- Aggregate total revenue per category
category_revenue = FOREACH grouped_sales GENERATE
                       group AS category,
                       SUM(with_revenue.revenue) AS total_revenue;

-- (iv) Sort descending and pick top 3
sorted_revenue = ORDER category_revenue BY total_revenue DESC;
top_categories = LIMIT sorted_revenue 3;

-- Display and store
DUMP top_categories;
STORE top_categories INTO '/user/ktu/top_categories'
     USING PigStorage(',');
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- Schema and `LOAD` syntax: 2 marks
- `FILTER` clause: 1 mark
- `FOREACH ... GENERATE` with arithmetic and aggregation: 2 marks
- `ORDER BY` and `LIMIT`: 1 mark
- `DUMP` / `STORE` correctly used: 1 mark

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

> [!NOTE]
> **Lazy Evaluation in Pig Latin:**

Pig Latin follows a **lazy evaluation** strategy (also called *deferred execution*). When a Pig script is parsed:

1. **Parsing Phase**: The script is parsed and validated for syntax and type correctness. A **Logical Plan** (DAG of operators) is constructed in memory.
2. **No Execution Yet**: No actual data processing occurs even if `LOAD` is encountered. Pig only verifies that the input path exists.
3. **Optimization**: The Logical Plan is optimized (predicate pushdown, projection pushdown, etc.) and converted to a **Physical Plan** (MapReduce / Tez jobs).
4. **Trigger Point**: Execution is **triggered only** when a *terminal operator* is encountered:
   - `DUMP` — writes to console and returns control to Grunt.
   - `STORE` — writes to HDFS/local FS.
   - `ILLUSTRATE` — sample-based dry run.

**Why Lazy Evaluation?**

- **Optimization Opportunity**: The compiler can see the entire pipeline before executing, enabling global optimizations (e.g., pushing a `FILTER` before a `JOIN` to reduce data shuffled over the network).
- **Error Prevention**: Type mismatches and schema errors are caught before any cluster resources are consumed.

**Role of `DUMP` and `STORE`**:
- `DUMP` is used for **interactive debugging** — it materializes the result in the console and discards it afterward.
- `STORE` is used for **persistence** — it writes the result to HDFS in a specified format (`PigStorage`, `AvroStorage`, etc.).
- Both are **terminal operators** that force the logical plan to be compiled and executed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Internal Choice Option B]**

**(a)** Explain the `FOREACH ... GENERATE` operator in Pig Latin with a suitable example. How does it differ from a SQL `SELECT` statement? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following two relations:
- `R1 = { (1, 'A', 50), (2, 'B', 60), (3, 'A', 70) }` (id, name, score)
- `R2 = { (1, 'Maths'), (2, 'Science'), (4, 'English') }` (id, subject)

Write a Pig Latin script to: (i) perform an inner join on the `id` column, (ii) perform a left outer join on the `id` column, and (iii) compute the average `score` grouped by `name`. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**`FOREACH ... GENERATE` Operator:**

The `FOREACH ... GENERATE` statement is Pig Latin's most powerful transformation operator. It iterates over every tuple in an input bag and applies a user-specified projection/transformation to produce a new bag. The general syntax is:

$$
\text{new\_rel} = \text{FOREACH} \; R \; \text{GENERATE} \; expr_1, expr_2, \ldots
$$

**Example:**

```pig
employees = LOAD '/data/employees.txt' AS (emp_id:int, name:chararray, salary:float);

-- Compute 10% bonus and project only name and bonus
bonus_data = FOREACH employees GENERATE
                 name,
                 salary * 0.10 AS bonus;
```

**Key Features:**

1. **Supports Arithmetic**: `salary * 1.10` for a 10% raise.
2. **Supports Built-in Functions**: `UPPER(name)`, `SIZE(name)`, `AVG(x)`, `SUM(y)`.
3. **Supports User-Defined Functions (UDFs)**: `MY_UDF(field)`.
4. **Supports Conditional Logic**: Use nested `FOREACH` with `IF ... THEN ... ELSE`.
5. **Supports Flattening**: `FLATTEN(bag)` unwraps nested bags.
6. **Supports Aliasing**: `AS new_name` for renaming output fields.

**Difference from SQL `SELECT`:**

| Aspect | SQL `SELECT` | Pig `FOREACH ... GENERATE` |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Model** | Operates on flat relational tables | Operates on nested bags/tuples |
| **Nesting** | Cannot unnest nested collections natively | Uses `FLATTEN` to unnest |
| **Order** | Preserves row order | Output bag is **unordered** |
| **Execution** | Eager (executes immediately) | Lazy (executed only on `DUMP`/`STORE`) |
| **UDFs** | Requires stored procedures / functions | Native UDF support in Java, Python, JavaScript |
| **Output Type** | A result-set | A new **bag (relation)** that can be further processed |

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

```pig
-- ==========================================
-- Q(b): Joins and Aggregation
-- ==========================================

-- Load both relations
R1 = LOAD '/user/ktu/R1.txt' USING PigStorage(',')
     AS (id:int, name:chararray, score:int);

R2 = LOAD '/user/ktu/R2.txt' USING PigStorage(',')
     AS (id:int, subject:chararray);

-- (i) Inner Join
inner_result = JOIN R1 BY id, R2 BY id;
DUMP inner_result;
-- Output: { (1, A, 50, 1, Maths), (2, B, 60, 2, Science) }
-- Note: id=3 from R1 and id=4 from R2 are dropped (no match)

-- (ii) Left Outer Join
left_outer_result = JOIN R1 BY id LEFT OUTER, R2 BY id;
DUMP left_outer_result;
-- Output: { (1, A, 50, 1, Maths), (2, B, 60, 2, Science), (3, A, 70, , ) }
-- Note: id=3 from R1 is preserved with null subject from R2; id=4 from R2 is dropped

-- (iii) Average score grouped by name
grouped_by_name = GROUP R1 BY name;
avg_score = FOREACH grouped_by_name GENERATE
                group AS name,
                AVG(R1.score) AS avg_score;
DUMP avg_score;
-- Output: { (A, 60.0), (B, 60.0) }
-- Logic: A has scores 50, 70 → avg = 60.0; B has score 60 → avg = 60.0
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- Loading both relations with schema: 1 mark
- Inner join syntax and result: 2 marks
- Left outer join syntax and result: 2 marks
- Group + aggregate for average: 2 marks

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Pig Latin Questions:**

1. **Forgetting the Schema in `LOAD`**: Writing `students = LOAD 'students.txt';` without `AS (field:type, ...)` will cost **1–2 marks**. Pig will assign auto-generated names like `$0, $1, $2`, which then break downstream `FOREACH` references.

2. **Incorrect Aggregate Field Reference**: Writing `AVG(marks)` inside a `FOREACH grouped` block where `marks` exists in the inner bag. The correct syntax is `AVG(students.marks)` — fully qualified. **Penalty: 1 mark**.

3. **Using `WHERE` instead of `FILTER`**: `WHERE` is SQL syntax and is **not valid in Pig Latin**. Always use `FILTER ... BY ...`. **Penalty: 1 mark**.

4. **Confusing `ORDER` with `SORT`**: Pig Latin has `ORDER` (not `SQL ORDER BY` as a phrase). The syntax is `ORDER rel BY field [ASC \vert DESC]`. **Penalty: 0.5–1 mark**.

5. **Forgetting `DUMP` or `STORE`**: Since Pig Latin is lazy, omitting the terminal operator means the script **does not execute** at all in a real Hadoop cluster. The examiner may deduct **1 mark** for a non-functional script.

6. **Not Using `FLATTEN` After `LIMIT` or Aggregate on a Grouped Relation**: After `GROUP`, the result is a *bag of bags*. To emit flat tuples, `FLATTEN` or direct field access (e.g., `group`, `students.marks`) is required. **Penalty: 1 mark**.

7. **Mismatched Joins**: Using `JOIN` syntax as if it were SQL: `JOIN ON a.id = b.id` is wrong. Correct: `JOIN A BY id, B BY id`. **Penalty: 1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pig Latin**

- **Pig Latin** is a **data-flow scripting language** for Hadoop that abstracts away MapReduce programming.
- It is executed in **three modes**: Local (single JVM), MapReduce (default), and Tez (DAG-based).
- The **execution engine** compiles a Pig script into a **Logical Plan** → **Physical Plan** → actual MapReduce/Tez jobs.
- The four core data types are: **Atom, Tuple, Bag, Map**. A **Relation** is a bag.
- `LOAD` reads data; `STORE` writes data; `DUMP` displays data to the console.
- `FILTER` is the row-selection operator; `FOREACH ... GENERATE` is the column-transformation operator.
- `GROUP` partitions a bag by key; `JOIN` merges two bags by key; `COGROUP` groups multiple relations.
- `ORDER` sorts; `DISTINCT` removes duplicates; `LIMIT N` takes the top N.
- **Lazy evaluation** means no execution happens until `DUMP` or `STORE` is invoked.
- Schema assignment in `LOAD` uses `AS (field:type, ...)` with types: `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `chararray`, `bytearray`.
- The keyword `group` is a **pseudo-column** that refers to the grouping key inside a `FOREACH` after `GROUP`.
- `FLATTEN` is used to **unnest** a bag into individual tuples.
- Built-in aggregate functions: `SUM, COUNT, AVG, MAX, MIN` (and `COUNT_STAR`).
- Debugging commands: `DESCRIBE` (schema), `EXPLAIN` (execution plans), `ILLUSTRATE` (sample dry-run).
- `PigStorage` is the default load/store function; delimiter can be customized (`PigStorage(',')`).
- A **single `FOREACH`** typically maps to a **map-only** job, while **`GROUP` + `FOREACH`** maps to a **full MapReduce job** (with shuffle and reduce phases).
- Pig Latin is **~10x–20x more concise** than equivalent Java MapReduce code.
- For KTU exams: always write the **complete script** (LOAD → transform → DUMP/STORE), and **explicitly explain lazy evaluation** whenever the question asks about execution.
- **Most-tested operators in KTU 2024 Scheme**: `LOAD`, `FILTER`, `FOREACH ... GENERATE`, `GROUP`, `JOIN`, `ORDER`, `LIMIT`, `STORE`.
- **Most-tested concept**: Lazy evaluation, Pig Latin vs. SQL differences, and the data model (Bag/Tuple/Atom).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Defined Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** User Defined Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **User Defined Function (UDF)** in the context of Big Data Analytics is a custom, user-programmed routine that extends the built-in functional capability of a distributed data processing framework such as **Hadoop MapReduce**, **Apache Hive**, **Apache Pig**, or **Apache Spark**. Formally, a UDF is a parameterized, reusable block of logic — written in a host language (Java, Python, Scala) — that accepts one or more input fields (arguments), applies a deterministic or non-deterministic transformation/computation, and returns a derived value or a set of values to the query/MapReduce execution engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A UDF is a mechanism provided by the Hadoop ecosystem that allows developers and data engineers to inject application-specific business logic directly into the data processing pipeline, thereby overcoming the limitations of pre-defined built-in functions.

### 1.2 Types of UDFs in the Hadoop Ecosystem

| UDF Category | Full Form | Return Cardinality | Aggregation Required | Typical Framework |
|--------------|-----------|--------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **UDF** | User Defined Function | 1 input → 1 output | No | Hive, Pig, Spark |
| **UDAF** | User Defined Aggregation Function | N inputs → 1 output | **Yes** | Hive, Spark SQL |
| **UDTF** | User Defined Table-generating Function | 1 input → N outputs (rows) | No | Hive |
| **UDAF (Pig)** | User Defined Aggregate Function | N inputs → 1 output | Yes | Apache Pig |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Custom Coffee Order**
>
> Imagine a standard coffee vending machine that only knows how to make **5 built-in drinks** (Cappuccino, Latte, Espresso, etc.) — these are the **built-in functions** of Hadoop. One day, you want a special "Cardamom Cold Brew with Hazelnut Foam." The machine cannot make it natively. So you provide a **printed recipe card (the UDF)** to the barista (the execution engine) with: *Input:* 1 cup of cold brew; *Process:* add 2g cardamom + 30ml hazelnut foam; *Output:* 1 custom drink.
>
> The barista now performs a transformation that the machine's original firmware could not do. Similarly, a UDF tells the Hadoop/Spark engine, *"Here is the recipe — execute this custom logic on my data row."*

### 1.4 Why UDFs Are Essential in Big Data

- **Domain Specificity**: Built-in functions are generic; businesses need custom logic (e.g., calculating risk score, parsing domain-specific formats).
- **Reusability**: Write once, invoke across multiple queries and jobs.
- **Extensibility**: The Hadoop ecosystem is intentionally extensible — UDFs are the official extension mechanism.
- **Performance**: UDFs execute in the same JVM/process as the engine, avoiding network round-trips.
- **Integration**: They plug seamlessly into SQL-like query languages (HiveQL, Spark SQL, Pig Latin).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a UDF

A UDF operates as a **black-box transformation node** within the larger query/job plan. Below is the structural logic flow:

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **Parsing Phase**: The query engine (Hive/Spark) parses the user's query and identifies a call to the UDF by name and signature.
2. **Binding Phase**: The engine looks up the UDF in its **Function Registry** (a classpath-loaded metadata table) and binds the function to the actual implementation class.
3. **Type Checking**: The engine validates the input argument data types against the UDF's declared `evaluate()` method signatures.
4. **Execution Phase**: For every input row (or row group), the engine invokes the UDF's `evaluate()` method, passing the row's field values.
5. **Transformation**: The UDF's body executes the custom logic — arithmetic, string manipulation, conditional checks, ML inference, etc.
6. **Return Phase**: The computed result is returned to the engine and substituted in the output row.
7. **Distribution**: Since the engine runs on a distributed cluster, the UDF logic is **serialized, distributed across worker nodes**, and executed in parallel on data-local partitions.

### 2.2 UDF Lifecycle in Hadoop/Spark

| Lifecycle Phase | Description | Key Class / Method |
|-----------------|-------------|--------------------|
| **Development** | Write the UDF in Java/Scala/Python | Extends `UDF` class (Hive) or function (Spark) |
| **Compilation** | Compile to JAR (Java/Scala) or .py file | `javac` / `sbt` / interpreter |
| **Registration** | Register function with engine | `CREATE FUNCTION` (Hive) / `spark.udf.register()` (Spark) |
| **Invocation** | Call in query/job | `SELECT my_udf(col) FROM table` |
| **Distribution** | Sent to all worker nodes | Cluster manager / YARN |
| **Execution** | Applied per record in parallel | `evaluate()` method call |
| **Output** | Result substituted in result set | Type-cast to expected return type |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** UDFs are **stateless by default** in most engines (Hive, Spark). They should NOT maintain external state across invocations within a single task, because each row is processed independently in parallel.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Syntax | Units / Notes |
|---------|------------------|---------------|
| **UDF Mathematical Form** | $f: D_{in} \rightarrow D_{out}$ | $D_{in}$ = input domain, $D_{out}$ = output codomain |
| **Generic UDF Mapping** | $R = f(a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n)$ | $a_i$ = input arguments, $R$ = single return value |
| **UDAF Aggregation Form** | $R = \bigoplus_{i=1}^{N} f(x_i)$ | $\bigoplus$ = associative aggregation operator |
| **UDTF Row Expansion** | $\lbrace R_1, R_2, \dots, R_m \rbrace = f(a)$ | One input row → $m$ output rows |
| **Hive UDF Compile** | `hive> ADD JAR /path/to/udf.jar;` | Registers JAR in distributed cache |
| **Hive UDF Create** | `CREATE FUNCTION myfn AS 'com.ktu.MyUDF';` | Permanent registration in metastore |
| **Spark UDF Register** | `spark.udf.register("myUDF", myFunction)` | Session-scoped registration |
| **Spark UDF Apply** | `df.withColumn("newCol", myUDF(col("input")))` | DataFrame transformation |
| **Pig UDF Register** | `REGISTER '/path/to/udf.py' USING streaming_python AS myudfs;` | Streaming UDF |
| **Evaluate Signature (Hive)** | `public Type evaluate(Type a, Type b, ...)` | Overloaded for type polymorphism |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use the pipe symbol `\|` inside table cells** — KTU examiners penalize broken markdown in answer scripts. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value notation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Finance**: Custom risk-scoring UDFs that combine credit history, transaction velocity, and geolocation.
- **Healthcare**: Parsing HL7 medical message formats inside a UDF.
- **Telecommunications**: Computing Quality-of-Service (QoS) degradation scores per cell tower.
- **Retail**: Implementing custom promotional discount logic for e-commerce recommendation systems.
- **IoT Analytics**: Decoding proprietary sensor binary payloads into structured data fields.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Model of a UDF Pipeline

A UDF is fundamentally a **function in the mathematical sense**. Let us derive its properties and execution model rigorously.

### 3.1.1 Formal Mathematical Definition

A UDF is a mapping $f$ from a domain $D$ to a codomain $C$:

$$f: D \rightarrow C$$

For a UDF with $n$ input arguments, the generalized form is:

$$f(a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n) = R$$

Where each $a_i \in D_i$ and the result $R \in C$.

### 3.1.2 Aggregation Derivation (UDAF)

For a **User Defined Aggregation Function (UDAF)**, the function must satisfy two algebraic properties to be parallelizable:

**Property 1 — Associativity:**

$$f(x_1, f(x_2, x_3)) = f(f(x_1, x_2), x_3)$$

**Property 2 — Commutativity (often required for shuffle):**

$$f(x_1, x_2) = f(x_2, x_1)$$

**Derivation of the partial-aggregate merge model:**

Consider a dataset $X = \lbrace x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N \rbrace$ distributed across $k$ worker nodes, with each node holding a partition $X_j$ of size $m_j$ where:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{k} m_j = N$$

The UDAF computes the final aggregate in two MapReduce-style stages:

**Stage 1 — Local Partial Aggregation (Map-side):**
Each worker node $j$ computes its local partial result:

$$P_j = \bigoplus_{i=1}^{m_j} f(x_{j,i})$$

**Stage 2 — Global Final Aggregation (Reduce-side):**
The driver combines all partial results:

$$R = \bigoplus_{j=1}^{k} P_j = \bigoplus_{j=1}^{k} \left( \bigoplus_{i=1}^{m_j} f(x_{j,i}) \right)$$

**Algebraic Justification:** By the associativity property, the order of application does not matter, so:

$$R = \bigoplus_{i=1}^{N} f(x_i)$$

This is **mathematically identical** to a single-node computation, which is why UDAFs can be parallelized.

### 3.1.3 Row Explosion Derivation (UDTF)

A **User Defined Table-generating Function (UDTF)** maps one input to $m$ output rows:

$$f(a) = \lbrace r_1, r_2, \dots, r_m \rbrace$$

**Total output cardinality** for an input table $T$ with $\vert T \vert$ rows:

$$\vert T_{out} \vert = \sum_{i=1}^{\vert T \vert} m_i$$

Where $m_i$ is the number of output rows produced by the UDTF for the $i$-th input row. This is the **row expansion factor** $\rho$:

$$\rho = \frac{\vert T_{out} \vert}{\vert T_{in} \vert} = \frac{1}{\vert T \vert} \sum_{i=1}^{\vert T \vert} m_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** A UDTF with $\rho > 1$ causes **data amplification**, which increases shuffle volume and may cause memory pressure. Always estimate $\rho$ before deploying a UDTF on large datasets.

### 3.2 Complexity Analysis Matrix

| Operation | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Parallelizable? |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|-----------------|
| Standard UDF (per row) | $O(1)$ per row → $O(N)$ total | $O(1)$ per row | **Yes** — embarrassingly parallel |
| UDAF (full aggregation) | $O(N)$ | $O(k)$ for $k$ partials | **Yes** — associative merge |
| UDTF (row explosion) | $O(N \cdot \rho)$ | $O(\rho)$ per row | **Yes** — output partitioning required |
| Stateful UDF (anti-pattern) | $O(1)$ but unsafe | $O(S)$ state | **No** — state not synchronized |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Hive UDF Calculation

**Problem:** Write a UDF `grade_classifier(marks)` that returns `"Distinction"` if marks $\geq 80$, `"First Class"` if $60 \leq \text{marks} < 80$, `"Second Class"` if $50 \leq \text{marks} < 60$, and `"Fail"` otherwise.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$f(m) = \begin{cases} \text{"Distinction"} & \text{if } m \geq 80 \\ \text{"First Class"} & \text{if } 60 \leq m < 80 \\ \text{"Second Class"} & \text{if } 50 \leq m < 60 \\ \text{"Fail"} & \text{if } m < 50 \end{cases}$$

**HiveQL Invocation:**

```sql
SELECT student_id, name, marks, grade_classifier(marks) AS grade
FROM student_results;
```

**Step-by-Step Evaluation for a sample row where $m = 72$:**

1. Input: $a_1 = 72$
2. Condition check: $72 \geq 80$? **False**.
3. Condition check: $60 \leq 72 < 80$? **True**.
4. Return: $R = \text{"First Class"}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UDF Execution Flow in a Distributed Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Submits Query with UDF Call] --> B[Query Parser and Analyzer]
    B --> C{UDF Found in Function Registry?}
    C -- No --> D[Error: Function Not Registered]
    C -- Yes --> E[Type Checking and Binding]
    E --> F[Query Plan Optimizer]
    F --> G[Cluster Manager: YARN / Spark Driver]
    G --> H1[Worker Node 1: Partition P1]
    G --> H2[Worker Node 2: Partition P2]
    G --> H3[Worker Node 3: Partition Pn]
    H1 --> I1[Load UDF JAR into JVM]
    H2 --> I2[Load UDF JAR into JVM]
    H3 --> I3[Load UDF JAR into JVM]
    I1 --> J1[Apply evaluate per row in P1]
    I2 --> J2[Apply evaluate per row in P2]
    I3 --> J3[Apply evaluate per row in Pn]
    J1 --> K[Shuffle and Aggregate Results]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    K --> L[Final Output Table to User]
```

## 4.2 UDF Class Hierarchy (Hive-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF] --> B[Simple UDF: Single Row to Single Output]
    A2[org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.udf.generic.GenericUDF] --> C[Generic UDF: Complex Types, Variable Args]
    A3[org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.udf.generic.GenericUDAF] --> D[UDAF: Aggregation Logic]
    A4[org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.udf.generic.GenericUDTF] --> E[UDTF: Row Explosion Logic]
    B --> F[Method: public Type evaluate Type args]
    C --> G[Methods: initialize, evaluate, getDisplayString]
    D --> H[Methods: init, iterate, merge, terminatePartial, merge, terminate]
    E --> I[Method: processRow with forward]
```

## 4.3 Spark UDF Registration and Invocation Pipeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User as Data Engineer
```
