```mermaid
    participant Driver as Spark Driver
    participant Registry as Function Registry
    participant Worker as Worker Node Executor
    User->>Driver: Define Python function def my_udf x
    User->>Driver: spark udf register myUDF my_udf
    Driver->>Registry: Store function reference and return type
    User->>Driver: df select myUDF col name
    Driver->>Worker: Ship serialized UDF bytecode via closure
    Worker->>Worker: Deserialize UDF into executor JVM
    Worker->>Worker: For each row in partition: apply my_udf value
    Worker->>Driver: Return transformed DataFrame partitions
    Driver->>User: Materialize final DataFrame result
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: UDF Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Data HDFS] --> B[Data Ingestion Layer]
    B --> C[Query Parser]
    C --> D[Semantic Analyzer]
    D --> E[UDF Resolver Module]
    E --> F[Built In Function Library]
    E --> G[User Defined Function Library]
    F --> H[Execution Plan Generator]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Distributed Task Scheduler]
    I --> J1[Task 1: UDF Applied]
    I --> J2[Task 2: UDF Applied]
    I --> J3[Task N: UDF Applied]
    J1 --> K[Result Aggregator]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    K --> L[Output Sink]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define a User Defined Function (UDF) in the Hadoop ecosystem. List any two types of UDFs supported by Apache Hive.**

**Model Answer:**

A **User Defined Function (UDF)** in the Hadoop ecosystem is a custom, user-written function that extends the built-in functional capabilities of distributed data processing frameworks such as Hive, Pig, and Spark. It allows users to implement application-specific logic (transformations, calculations, or parsing) that is not available in the framework's pre-defined function library. UDFs are written in host languages like Java, Python, or Scala and are executed within the same JVM as the query engine for performance.

**Two types of UDFs in Apache Hive:**

1. **Simple UDF (extends `org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF`):** Maps a single input row to a single output value using an overloaded `evaluate()` method. Used for row-level transformations.
2. **Generic UDF (extends `org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.udf.generic.GenericUDF`):** Supports complex data types (arrays, maps, structs) and variable-length arguments. Provides more control via `initialize()`, `evaluate()`, and `getDisplayString()` methods.

*(Other valid types: UDAF, UDTF)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between UDF, UDAF, and UDTF with respect to input-output cardinality and provide one example use case for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | UDF | UDAF | UDTF |
|--------|-----|------|------|
| **Full Form** | User Defined Function | User Defined Aggregation Function | User Defined Table-generating Function |
| **Input Cardinality** | 1 row (multiple columns) | N rows (1 or more columns) | 1 row |
| **Output Cardinality** | 1 value | 1 aggregated value | N rows (table) |
| **Example** | Converting Celsius to Fahrenheit | Computing custom weighted average | Splitting a string column into multiple rows (e.g., `explode()`) |

**Use Cases:**
- **UDF Example:** Custom email validation function that returns TRUE/FALSE per row.
- **UDAF Example:** Computing a domain-specific risk score aggregated across millions of transactions.
- **UDTF Example:** Parsing a JSON array column into multiple normalized rows.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the architecture and execution flow of a User Defined Function in Apache Hive with a neat diagram. Discuss how Hive resolves and executes the UDF across a distributed cluster. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Hive UDF in Java (or pseudocode) that classifies students into grade categories based on marks. Demonstrate its registration and invocation in HiveQL. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Architecture and Execution Flow of a Hive UDF:**

The execution of a Hive UDF follows a well-defined pipeline:

1. **Submission Phase:** The user submits a HiveQL query that contains a UDF call, e.g., `SELECT my_udf(col) FROM table;`
2. **Parsing Phase:** The Hive compiler parses the query into an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**. The UDF reference is identified as a function token.
3. **Semantic Analysis:** The compiler looks up the function name in the **Function Registry** — a metadata layer that maps function names to Java class implementations.
4. **Type Resolution:** Hive resolves the UDF's return type by inspecting the `evaluate()` method's declared return type and binding the input column types to the corresponding method parameters.
5. **Plan Generation:** The logical plan is converted into a **physical plan** consisting of Map and Reduce tasks. The UDF is embedded in the task's expression evaluator.
6. **Distribution:** The UDF's compiled JAR is loaded into the **distributed cache** and made available on every node in the cluster where the query is executed.
7. **Execution:** For every input row, the UDF's `evaluate()` method is invoked with the row's field values as arguments. The return value is substituted in the output row.
8. **Aggregation:** The transformed rows are aggregated, sorted (if `ORDER BY` is present), and written to the output table or returned to the user.

**Diagram Reference:** Refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.1.

**Key Point:** The UDF JAR must be registered via `ADD JAR` and the function via `CREATE FUNCTION` (permanent) or `CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION` (session-scoped).

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Java Code for the Hive UDF `GradeClassifier`:**

```java
package com.ktu.hive.udf;

import org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF;
import org.apache.hadoop.io.Text;

public class GradeClassifier extends UDF {
    public Text evaluate(Integer marks) {
        if (marks == null) return null;
        String grade;
        if (marks >= 80) {
            grade = "Distinction";
        } else if (marks >= 60) {
            grade = "First Class";
        } else if (marks >= 50) {
            grade = "Second Class";
        } else {
            grade = "Fail";
        }
        return new Text(grade);
    }
}
```

**Compilation and Registration Steps:**

1. **Compile:**
   ```bash
   javac -classpath $HIVE_HOME/lib/hive-exec-*.jar GradeClassifier.java
   jar cf gradeudf.jar com/ktu/hive/udf/GradeClassifier.class
   ```

2. **Add JAR to Hive session:**
   ```sql
   hive> ADD JAR /path/to/gradeudf.jar;
   ```

3. **Register the function:**
   ```sql
   hive> CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION grade_classifier 
       AS 'com.ktu.hive.udf.GradeClassifier';
   ```

4. **Invoke the UDF:**
   ```sql
   hive> SELECT student_id, name, marks, 
            grade_classifier(marks) AS grade
        FROM student_results;
   ```

**Sample Output:**

| student_id | name | marks | grade |
|------------|------|-------|-------|
| 101 | Anu | 85 | Distinction |
| 102 | Binu | 72 | First Class |
| 103 | Chinju | 55 | Second Class |
| 104 | Deepu | 40 | Fail |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> - ❌ Forgetting to extend the `UDF` class → compilation error, **−2 marks**.
> - ❌ Returning a Java `String` instead of `org.apache.hadoop.io.Text` → null output at runtime, **−2 marks**.
> - ❌ Not handling the `null` input case → NullPointerException, **−1 mark**.
> - ❌ Mixing up `CREATE FUNCTION` (permanent, stored in metastore) vs `CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION` (session-only), **−1 mark**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the three types of UDFs in Apache Pig (Eval, Filter, Load) and how they are registered using `REGISTER` and `DEFINE` statements. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Pig Latin script that uses a User Defined Function to compute the BMI (Body Mass Index) of patients from a given input file `patients.txt` containing fields `(name, weight_kg, height_m)`. Show the UDF code in Python. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Types of UDFs in Apache Pig:**

Apache Pig supports three main categories of UDFs, each serving a different role in the data pipeline:

1. **Eval Functions:** Used inside `FOREACH ... GENERATE` statements. They take one or more input expressions and return a single output value per record.
   - **Example:** A function that converts temperature from Fahrenheit to Celsius.

2. **Filter Functions:** Used inside `FILTER` statements. They return a boolean indicating whether a record should be retained or discarded.
   - **Example:** A function that filters out records with missing fields.

3. **Load Functions:** Used in the `LOAD` statement. They parse custom input formats and convert raw bytes into Pig's tuple/bag data model.
   - **Example:** A function that reads Avro or Parquet files with custom schema logic.

**Registration Mechanism:**

```pig
-- Step 1: Register the JAR/script containing the UDF
REGISTER '/path/to/udfs/medical_udfs.py' USING streaming_python AS myudfs;

-- Step 2: Define an alias for the UDF
DEFINE compute_bmi myudfs.compute_bmi;

-- Step 3: Use the UDF in Pig Latin
patients = LOAD 'patients.txt' USING PigStorage(',')
          AS (name:chararray, weight_kg:float, height_m:float);
results = FOREACH patients GENERATE name, compute_bmi(weight_kg, height_m) AS bmi;
```

**Diagram Reference:** Refer to the Block-Level Functional Architecture in Section 4.4.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Python UDF for BMI Calculation:**

```python
# File: medical_udfs.py
@outputSchema("bmi:float")
def compute_bmi(weight_kg, height_m):
    if weight_kg is None or height_m is None or height_m == 0:
        return None
    return weight_kg / (height_m * height_m)
```

**Pig Latin Script:**

```pig
-- Register the Python UDF module
REGISTER 'medical_udfs.py' USING streaming_python AS myudfs;

-- Define an alias
DEFINE compute_bmi myudfs.compute_bmi;

-- Load the patient data
patients = LOAD 'patients.txt' USING PigStorage(',')
           AS (name:chararray, weight_kg:float, height_m:float);

-- Compute BMI using the UDF
with_bmi = FOREACH patients GENERATE 
               name, 
               weight_kg, 
               height_m, 
               compute_bmi(weight_kg, height_m) AS bmi;

-- Categorize based on BMI
categorized = FOREACH with_bmi GENERATE 
                  name, 
                  bmi,
                  (bmi < 18.5 ? 'Underweight' :
                   bmi < 25.0 ? 'Normal' :
                   bmi < 30.0 ? 'Overweight' : 'Obese') AS category;

-- Store the result
STORE categorized INTO 'bmi_output' USING PigStorage(',');
```

**Mathematical Verification of BMI Formula:**

$$\text{BMI} = \frac{w}{h^2}$$

Where $w$ is weight in kilograms and $h$ is height in meters. Units: $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-2}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> - ❌ Missing the `@outputSchema` decorator in Python UDFs → Pig cannot infer the return type, **−2 marks**.
> - ❌ Using `REGISTER` without `USING streaming_python` for Python scripts → silent failure, **−1 mark**.
> - ❌ Forgetting the `DEFINE` alias step → function not callable, **−1 mark**.
> - ❌ Not handling division-by-zero when `height_m = 0` → runtime error, **−1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — User Defined Functions**

- ✅ **UDF Definition:** A custom function written by the user to extend the built-in capabilities of a Big Data framework (Hive, Pig, Spark).
- ✅ **UDF (1:1):** One input row → one output value. Extends `UDF` class in Hive.
- ✅ **UDAF (N:1):** N input rows → one aggregated value. Must be **associative** (and often **commutative**) for parallel execution.
- ✅ **UDTF (1:N):** One input row → N output rows (table generation). Causes **row explosion** with expansion factor $\rho$.
- ✅ **Generic UDF:** Supports complex types (arrays, maps, structs) and variable arguments via `GenericUDF` class.
- ✅ **Pig UDF Types:** Eval, Filter, Load — registered via `REGISTER` and `DEFINE`.
- ✅ **Spark UDF Registration:** `spark.udf.register("name", function)` for DataFrame/SQL use.
- ✅ **Hive UDF Registration:** `ADD JAR` + `CREATE [TEMPORARY] FUNCTION` sequence is mandatory.
- ✅ **Return Type Pitfall:** Always return Hadoop Writables (`Text`, `IntWritable`) in Hive Java UDFs — never raw Java `String` or `Integer`.
- ✅ **Null Handling:** Always check for `null` inputs at the start of `evaluate()` to avoid NullPointerException.
- ✅ **Statelessness:** UDFs are stateless across rows — never rely on external mutable state.
- ✅ **Parallelization Property:** UDAF aggregation correctness across distributed nodes requires **associativity**: $f(x_1, f(x_2, x_3)) = f(f(x_1, x_2), x_3)$.
- ✅ **Output Cardinality Math:** For a UDTF processing $|T|$ input rows, output cardinality is $\sum_{i=1}^{|T|} m_i$, where $m_i$ is the row-expansion count for the $i$-th input.
- ✅ **Permanent vs Temporary:** `CREATE FUNCTION` is permanent (stored in metastore); `CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION` is session-scoped.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain with diagram," always include the **distributed execution flow** showing UDF JAR distribution to worker nodes.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Processing operators. Hive : Hive Shell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Data Processing operators. Hive : Hive Shell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Data Processing Operators in Big Data

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
Data Processing Operators are the foundational symbols, keywords, and functional constructs used in a query language (typically **HQL - Hive Query Language** or **SQL** in big data frameworks) to perform transformations, filtering, aggregations, joins, and projections on large-scale structured and semi-structured datasets stored across distributed file systems such as **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** In the context of **Module Experiment 2**, Data Processing Operators refer to the operators usable inside the **Hive Shell (HiveQL/Beeline CLI)** and similar distributed data warehouse query interfaces. Students are expected to demonstrate operators hands-on in lab experiments.

### 1.2 Classification of Data Processing Operators

The operators can be broadly classified into the following high-yield categories for KTU examinations:

| Category | Purpose | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Relational Operators** | Comparison: `=`, `<>`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=` | Very High |
| **Arithmetic Operators** | Math: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` | Very High |
| **Logical Operators** | Boolean: `AND`, `OR`, `NOT` | Very High |
| **Set Operators** | Combine result sets: `UNION`, `UNION ALL`, `INTERSECT` | High |
| **Special Operators** | `LIKE`, `RLIKE`, `IN`, `BETWEEN`, `IS NULL`, `REGEXP` | High |
| **Built-in Functions** | `COUNT()`, `SUM()`, `AVG()`, `MAX()`, `MIN()`, `RANK()` | Very High |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Assembly Line" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine a massive warehouse with **1 Crore (10 Million)** invoices piled randomly. A Data Processing Operator is like a specific *machine on an assembly line*:
> - A **Relational Operator** is a *quality-check scanner* that filters out defective (non-matching) items.
> - An **Arithmetic Operator** is a *pricing machine* that recalculates totals.
> - A **Logical Operator (`AND`/`OR`)** is the *supervisor* that decides which items pass based on multiple criteria.
> - A **Set Operator (`UNION`)** is a *merger conveyor* that combines output of two parallel lines into one final shipment.
> - The **Hive Shell** is the *control room* where the factory manager types English-like instructions to control all these machines.

### 1.4 What is the Hive Shell?

**Formal Definition:**
The **Hive Shell** is a Command Line Interface (CLI) provided by **Apache Hive** that allows users to interactively submit **HQL (Hive Query Language)** statements to the Hive Server. It acts as the *REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop)* for big data warehousing queries, abstracting complex **MapReduce**, **Tez**, or **Spark** jobs into SQL-like syntax.

**Two Primary Hive Shells in KTU Lab Context:**
1. **`hive` CLI** — The legacy default shell (deprecated since Hive 1.1, removed in Hive 3.x for embedded mode). Uses `~/.hiverc` for startup configuration.
2. **`beeline` CLI** — The **current KTU-recommended** shell. It uses **JDBC** to connect to **HiveServer2**. This is what students will use in modern lab setups.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Many students still type `hive>` prompt in their lab records. With **Hive 2.x/3.x** on modern distributions (Cloudera, HDP 3.x, AWS EMR), the only available shell is **`beeline>`**. Always use `beeline` in your experiments.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Data Processing Operator Statement

Every operator-based query in the Hive Shell follows a logical pipeline. Understanding this pipeline is the **single most important concept** for KTU Part B questions.

**The 5-Stage Logical Pipeline:**

1. **Input Source Identification** — Specify the table (managed/external) or subquery.
2. **Projection (SELECT clause)** — Choose the columns to display and apply **arithmetic/built-in operators** to derive new columns.
3. **Source Filtering (FROM + WHERE)** — Apply **relational and logical operators** on raw rows.
4. **Grouping & Aggregation (GROUP BY + HAVING)** — Apply **aggregate functions** and filter aggregated results.
5. **Ordering & Limiting (ORDER BY / SORT BY / LIMIT / CLUSTER BY / DISTRIBUTE BY)** — Final presentation logic.

### 2.2 Deep Dive: Operator Categories with Hive Shell Semantics

#### A) Relational Operators
These return **Boolean** values (`TRUE` / `FALSE` / `NULL`).

- `=`  — Equality test.
- `<>` or `!=` — Inequality test.
- `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=` — Ordered comparison (works on numeric, string, date types).

> [!NOTE]
> **String Comparison in Hive:** Strings are compared **lexicographically** (byte-by-byte) by default. To compare based on **Unicode collation**, set `hive.default.fileformat=Orc` and use the `COLLATE` clause (Hive 2.0+).

#### B) Arithmetic Operators
Hive follows standard precedence:

$$ \text{Precedence: } \quad () \;\rightarrow\; \text{Unary } {-} \;\rightarrow\; *, /, \% \;\rightarrow\; +, - $$

**Type Promotion Rule (KTU High-Yield):**
$$ T_{\text{narrow}} \;\rightarrow\; T_{\text{wide}} \quad \text{(e.g., INT} \rightarrow \text{BIGINT} \rightarrow \text{FLOAT} \rightarrow \text{DOUBLE)} $$

> If any operand is `DOUBLE`, the entire expression evaluates to `DOUBLE`.

#### C) Logical Operators
- `AND` — Both conditions must be `TRUE`.
- `OR` — At least one condition must be `TRUE`.
- `NOT` — Negates a Boolean expression.
- `IN (...)` — Set membership test (equivalent to chained `OR`).
- `BETWEEN x AND y` — Inclusive range test (equivalent to `x <= col <= y`).
- `IS NULL` / `IS NOT NULL` — Nullity check. **Never use `= NULL`.**

#### D) Special Pattern-Matching Operators

| Operator | Function | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| `LIKE` | SQL wildcards: `%` (any string), `_` (single char) | `name LIKE 'A%'` |
| `RLIKE` | Full **Regular Expression** matching (Java regex) | `city RLIKE '^[MK].*'` |
| `REGEXP` | Alias of `RLIKE` | Same as above |

#### E) Set Operators (Combine Outputs of Multiple `SELECT` Statements)
- `UNION` — Removes duplicate rows from combined result.
- `UNION ALL` — Keeps all rows (faster, **preferred for big data**).
- `INTERSECT` — Returns common rows (Hive 1.2+).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** All `SELECT` statements in a `UNION` must have the **same number of columns** with **compatible data types**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Operator / Concept | Syntax Form | Output Type | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Relational | `col OP value` | Boolean | Always evaluate `NULL` first |
| 2 | Arithmetic | `expr1 OP expr2` | Widest type | Use `CAST()` to force type |
| 3 | Logical | `cond1 AND cond2` | Boolean | Short-circuit evaluation applies |
| 4 | `LIKE` | `col LIKE 'pat%'` | Boolean | `%` = any string, `_` = 1 char |
| 5 | `RLIKE` | `col RLIKE 'regex'` | Boolean | Slower than `LIKE` |
| 6 | `IN` | `col IN (v1, v2, ...)` | Boolean | Equivalent to `OR` chain |
| 7 | `BETWEEN` | `col BETWEEN a AND b` | Boolean | **Inclusive** on both ends |
| 8 | `UNION ALL` | `Q1 UNION ALL Q2` | Combined rows | No deduplication, faster |
| 9 | `UNION` | `Q1 UNION Q2` | Distinct rows | Hidden `DISTINCT` cost |
| 10 | `IS NULL` | `col IS NULL` | Boolean | Use this, **not** `= NULL` |
| 11 | `CAST` | `CAST(x AS BIGINT)` | Forced type | Truncates/Digits on overflow |
| 12 | `COUNT(DISTINCT)` | `COUNT(DISTINCT col)` | BIGINT | Very expensive; avoid in big tables |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-Commerce:** Filter orders where `total > 5000 AND status = 'SHIPPED'`.
- **Banking:** Identify accounts where `balance BETWEEN 0 AND 100` (dormant accounts).
- **IoT Analytics:** Aggregate sensor readings: `AVG(temperature) GROUP BY sensor_id`.
- **Log Mining:** Use `RLIKE` to detect failed-login patterns: `log_line RLIKE '.*Failed password.*'`.

### 2.5 Hive Shell Operational Theory

**Execution Flow Inside the Hive Shell:**

$$ \text{User (Beeline CLI)} \;\xrightarrow{\text{JDBC/Thrift}}\; \text{HiveServer2} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Compiler}}\; \text{Query Plan DAG} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Execution Engine}}\; \text{MR / Tez / Spark} $$

- The **Hive Compiler** parses the HQL string.
- The **Optimizer** applies **predicate pushdown, column pruning, join reordering**.
- The **Execution Engine** (Tez is the KTU-recommended modern default) runs the physical plan on the cluster.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Operator Evaluation — Worked Example

**Given Scenario:** A Hive table `sales(id INT, region STRING, amount DOUBLE, sale_date DATE)` contains **1 Crore** rows. We want to find regions whose **average sale amount is greater than ₹50,000**, but **only for sales made in 2024**, and we need the **top 3 regions** by average.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Tip:** Operators must be combined in the correct logical sequence. The matrix below shows the step-by-step analytical breakdown.

#### Step 1 — Filter Rows Using Relational + Logical Operators (Stage 3 of Pipeline)

We restrict to 2024 sales only. The condition uses a **range operator**:

$$ \text{Filter: } \; \text{sale\_date} \geq \text{'2024-01-01'} \; \text{AND} \; \text{sale\_date} \leq \text{'2024-12-31'} $$

**Logical Equivalence using BETWEEN:**

$$ \text{Equivalent: } \; \text{sale\_date} \; \text{BETWEEN} \; \text{'2024-01-01'} \; \text{AND} \; \text{'2024-12-31'} $$

> Both forms yield identical Boolean results. `BETWEEN` is **inclusive** at both bounds.

#### Step 2 — Aggregation Using Built-in Arithmetic + Aggregate Operators (Stage 4)

We now compute the average amount per region:

$$ \text{Group Key: } \text{region} \quad ; \quad \text{Metric: } \text{AVG}(\text{amount}) $$

The internal computation for a group $G$ with $n$ rows is:

$$ \text{AVG}_{G} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{amount}_{i}}{n} $$

**Type Promotion Note:** `amount` is `DOUBLE`. The `SUM(amount)` is also `DOUBLE`. The division `n` (BIGINT) is auto-promoted to `DOUBLE`, yielding a `DOUBLE` average. This follows the rule:

$$ \text{BIGINT} \;\rightarrow\; \text{DOUBLE} $$

#### Step 3 — Having Clause: Relational Operator on Aggregates (Stage 4)

We filter the aggregated groups:

$$ \text{HAVING: } \; \text{AVG}(\text{amount}) \;>\; 50000.0 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **`WHERE` vs `HAVING`:** A common KTU pitfall. `WHERE` filters **rows before** aggregation. `HAVING` filters **groups after** aggregation. You **cannot** write `WHERE AVG(amount) > 50000`.

#### Step 4 — Ordering with Arithmetic + Limit (Stage 5)

We sort by the derived aggregate (descending) and take the top 3:

$$ \text{ORDER BY: } \text{AVG(amount)} \; \text{DESC} \quad ; \quad \text{LIMIT } 3 $$

#### Step 5 — Final HQL Statement (Combined)

```
SELECT region, AVG(amount) AS avg_amt
FROM sales
WHERE sale_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31'
GROUP BY region
HAVING AVG(amount) > 50000.0
ORDER BY avg_amt DESC
LIMIT 3;
```

### 3.2 Exhaustive Evaluation: Operator Precedence Walkthrough

Consider this expression inside a Hive `WHERE` clause:

$$ \text{price} \;>\; 1000 \; \text{AND} \; (\text{category} = \text{'A'} \; \text{OR} \; \text{category} = \text{'B'}) $$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

1. **Parenthesis resolved first:** `(category = 'A' OR category = 'B')` evaluates to a Boolean per row.
2. **Relational operators** (`>` and `=`) evaluate next, producing Booleans for each side.
3. **Logical AND** combines the outer Boolean with the parenthetical Boolean.
4. **Final Boolean per row** determines inclusion in the filtered dataset.

> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners award marks for showing the parenthesization logic. Always parenthesize explicitly to avoid ambiguity, even when not strictly required by precedence.

### 3.3 Set Operator Walkthrough (UNION ALL)

Given two queries $Q_1$ and $Q_2$, each returning 3 columns:

$$ Q_1 \; \text{UNION ALL} \; Q_2 $$

- **Row count** of result $= |Q_1| + |Q_2|$ (additive).
- **Schema** of result $=$ Schema of $Q_1$ (must match $Q_2$).
- **Order** of rows is **not guaranteed** unless followed by `ORDER BY`.

For `UNION` (distinct):

$$ Q_1 \; \text{UNION} \; Q_2 \;=\; \text{DISTINCT}(Q_1 \; \text{UNION ALL} \; Q_2) $$

> The deduplication cost grows non-linearly with dataset size — for 1 Crore rows, **`UNION ALL` is the KTU-preferred choice** unless duplicates are semantically problematic.

### 3.4 Worked-Out Pattern Matching with `RLIKE`

A Hive column `email` contains user email addresses. We want to extract addresses from `gmail.com` only.

$$ \text{email} \;\text{RLIKE}\; '\text{@gmail\.com\$}' $$

**Regex Decoding:**
- `@` — Literal at-sign.
- `gmail\.com` — Literal text "gmail.com" (the `\.` escapes the dot).
- `\$` — End-of-string anchor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam:** If asked to differentiate `LIKE` vs `RLIKE`, write:
> - `LIKE` is **anchored** by default and uses **SQL wildcards** (`%`, `_`).
> - `RLIKE` uses **Java regex syntax** and supports full pattern features (anchors, character classes, quantifiers).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hive Shell Query Execution Architecture (Functional Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **functional flow** when a user submits an HQL query through the Hive Shell. It traces the path from the CLI through the compiler, optimizer, and finally to the distributed execution engine on the cluster.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Types HQL in Beeline CLI] --> B[Beeline Sends Query via JDBC]
    B --> C[HiveServer2 Receives Request]
    C --> D[Parser: Lexical + Syntax Analysis]
    D --> E[AST: Abstract Syntax Tree Created]
    E --> F[Semantic Analyzer Validates Schema]
    F --> G[Query Plan: Logical DAG]
    G --> H[Optimizer: Predicate Pushdown, Column Pruning]
    H --> I[Physical Plan: MR / Tez / Spark Tasks]
    I --> J[Execution Engine Submits to YARN]
    J --> K[HDFS Reads + Distributed Processing]
    K --> L[Results Streamed Back to Beeline]
    L --> M[User Sees Tabular Output]
```

### 4.2 Operator Classification Tree (Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OP[Data Processing Operators]
    OP --> REL[Relational Operators]
    OP --> ARITH[Arithmetic Operators]
    OP --> LOG[Logical Operators]
    OP --> SPEC[Special Pattern Operators]
    OP --> SET[Set Operators]
    OP --> AGG[Aggregate Built-in Functions]
    REL --> R1[= Equal]
    REL --> R2[Less Greater Equal]
    ARITH --> A1[Plus Minus Star Slash Mod]
    LOG --> L1[AND OR NOT]
    SPEC --> S1[LIKE]
    SPEC --> S2[RLIKE / REGEXP]
    SPEC --> S3[IN BETWEEN IS NULL]
    SET --> ST1[UNION]
    SET --> ST2[UNION ALL]
    SET --> ST3[INTERSECT]
    AGG --> AG1[COUNT SUM AVG MAX MIN]
```

### 4.3 Hive Shell Internal Compilation Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HQL Query String] --> B[Lexer]
    B --> C[Parser]
    C --> D[AST Builder]
    D --> E[Semantic Analyzer]
    E --> F[Logical Plan Generator]
    F --> G[Optimizer]
    G --> H[Physical Plan Generator]
    H --> I[Execution Engine]
    I --> J[Cluster Resources via YARN]
    J --> K[HDFS Data Nodes]
```

### 4.4 Operator Application Lifecycle (WHERE → HAVING → ORDER)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Raw Table Rows] --> S1[Stage 1: WHERE Clause Applies Relational and Logical Operators]
    S1 --> S2[Stage 2: GROUP BY Aggregates Rows into Groups]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: HAVING Clause Filters Groups Using Aggregate Functions]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: SELECT Applies Arithmetic and Built-in Operators on Columns]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: ORDER BY Applies Sorting on Final Output]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6: LIMIT Truncates Result Set]
    S6 --> S7[Final Result Returned to Beeline Shell]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Evaluation Rule:** When asked for a diagram in exams, students are expected to label **each stage** with the **specific operator type** being applied. The diagrams above give the exact labels you should reproduce.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Differentiate between the `LIKE` and `RLIKE` operators in Hive. Provide one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **`LIKE`** is a SQL pattern-matching operator that uses SQL wildcards: `%` matches any sequence of characters (including zero), and `_` matches exactly one character. It is case-sensitive and **anchored** to the full string. **Example:** `name LIKE 'A%'` returns all names starting with 'A'.
- **`RLIKE`** (alias `REGEXP`) uses **Java Regular Expression** syntax. It supports anchors (`^`, `$`), character classes (`[a-z]`), quantifiers (`*`, `+`, `?`), and alternation (`|`). It is more powerful but computationally slower. **Example:** `name RLIKE '^[A-M].*son$'` returns names starting with A–M and ending with "son".
- **Key Difference:** `LIKE` uses simple SQL wildcards; `RLIKE` uses full regex. (1 Mark for distinction + 1 Mark each for example = **3 Marks**)

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
> **Explain the difference between `UNION` and `UNION ALL` in Hive. In a big data context, which is preferred and why?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **`UNION`** combines the result sets of two or more `SELECT` statements and **removes duplicate rows** by performing an implicit `DISTINCT` operation. This requires a shuffle and sort phase, which is expensive at scale. (1 Mark)
- **`UNION ALL`** simply **concatenates** all rows from the participating queries without any deduplication. It requires only a single pass and no extra shuffle. (1 Mark)
- **Big Data Preference:** `UNION ALL` is strongly preferred in Hive/Big Data environments because deduplication over millions of rows is computationally prohibitive. Use `UNION` only when duplicates are semantically invalid. (1 Mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse `WHERE` and `HAVING` and write aggregations inside `WHERE`. This results in **2-mark deduction** minimum.
> - In `UNION` questions, students forget to ensure **column count match**. This loses **3 marks** for syntax/correctness.
> - For `RLIKE`, students forget to escape the dot (`.` matches any character in regex). Always write `\.` in exam answers.
> - Writing `hive>` prompt instead of `beeline>` in 2024 Scheme labs is considered outdated. Lose **1 mark** for outdated syntax.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

> Consider the following Hive table:
>
> **Table: `employee(emp_id INT, name STRING, department STRING, salary DOUBLE, join_date DATE)`**
>
> The table contains **50 lakh** employee records across 5 departments.
>
> **(a)** Write a HiveQL query using the **Beeline shell** to list the **names and departments** of all employees whose name **starts with 'S' or 'A'** and whose **salary is between 30,000 and 80,000** (inclusive). Display the results sorted by salary in **descending order** and show only the **top 10 records**. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **execution flow** of this query inside the Hive Shell, clearly identifying which **operator category** is applied at each stage. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — HiveQL Query:**

```
SELECT name, department
FROM employee
WHERE (name LIKE 'S%' OR name LIKE 'A%')
  AND salary BETWEEN 30000 AND 80000
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 10;
```

**Mark Allocation Breakdown:**
- Correct `SELECT` clause with column list — **1 Mark**
- Correct `FROM` clause — **0.5 Mark**
- Correct use of `LIKE` operator with OR + parentheses — **2 Marks**
- Correct use of `BETWEEN` operator (inclusive) — **1.5 Marks**
- Correct `ORDER BY ... DESC` + `LIMIT 10` — **2 Marks**

**(b) [7 Marks] — Execution Flow Explanation:**

The query is processed in the following 5 stages, each applying a specific operator category:

1. **Stage 1 — Input Identification:** The `FROM employee` clause is parsed. The metastore is queried to retrieve the table's schema and HDFS location. (0.5 Mark)
2. **Stage 2 — Row Filtering (`WHERE`):** The Hive Compiler evaluates the `WHERE` predicate. Three operators are applied here: (1.5 Marks total)
   - **Logical Operator (`OR`):** Combines two `LIKE` predicates.
   - **Special Pattern Operator (`LIKE`):** Filters names starting with 'S' or 'A'.
   - **Relational Operator (`BETWEEN`):** Filters salary within [30,000, 80,000].
3. **Stage 3 — Projection (`SELECT`):** The columns `name` and `department` are selected (column pruning optimization applied). (0.5 Mark)
4. **Stage 4 — Sorting (`ORDER BY`):** A **global sort** is applied using a single reducer (default in `ORDER BY`). For 50 lakh rows, this triggers a full sort. (2 Marks)
5. **Stage 5 — Limiting (`LIMIT`):** The result set is truncated to the first 10 rows. (0.5 Mark)
6. **Bonus — Execution Path:** The query is submitted to the **Tez execution engine** via YARN, which distributes the work across the cluster, processes HDFS data blocks in parallel, and streams the final 10 rows back to the Beeline shell. (2 Marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 1]`

> Consider the following two Hive tables:
>
> **Table A: `sales_2023(order_id INT, product_id INT, amount DOUBLE, region STRING)`**
> **Table B: `sales_2024(order_id INT, product_id INT, amount DOUBLE, region STRING)`**
>
> Each table has **20 lakh** rows. The `region` column contains values: `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`.
>
> **(a)** Write a HiveQL query using the **Beeline shell** to produce a **combined list of all order IDs and regions** from both years, **without any duplicates**, sorted by `order_id` in ascending order. Use the **most appropriate set operator** and justify your choice. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Write another query to find the **total sales amount per region** for **both years combined**, but only include regions where the **total amount exceeds ₹10,00,000**. Identify all operators used and explain why `WHERE` cannot replace `HAVING` here. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Combined Unique List:**

```
SELECT order_id, region FROM sales_2023
UNION
SELECT order_id, region FROM sales_2024
ORDER BY order_id ASC;
```

**Justification:** Since the question explicitly requires **"without any duplicates"**, we must use `UNION` (not `UNION ALL`). (2 Marks for correct operator choice + justification)

**Mark Allocation Breakdown:**
- Correct `UNION` keyword — **2 Marks**
- Two `SELECT` statements with matching column count and types — **2 Marks**
- Correct `ORDER BY order_id ASC` — **2 Marks**
- Schema compatibility check (both queries select `INT, STRING`) — **1 Mark**

**(b) [7 Marks] — Aggregated Total Sales per Region:**

```
SELECT region, SUM(amount) AS total_sales
FROM (
    SELECT region, amount FROM sales_2023
    UNION ALL
    SELECT region, amount FROM sales_2024
) combined
GROUP BY region
HAVING SUM(amount) > 1000000
ORDER BY total_sales DESC;
```

**Operators Identified:**
1. **Set Operator:** `UNION ALL` (used in subquery to combine both years — preferred over `UNION` since we need **all** rows for accurate sum). (1 Mark)
2. **Arithmetic/Aggregate Operator:** `SUM(amount)` aggregates sales per region. (1 Mark)
3. **Grouping:** `GROUP BY region` forms per-region groups. (1 Mark)
4. **Relational Operator in HAVING:** `SUM(amount) > 1000000` filters groups. (1 Mark)

**Why `WHERE` cannot replace `HAVING`:**
- `WHERE` operates on **individual rows BEFORE** aggregation. At the row level, no `SUM` exists yet — `SUM(amount)` is a group-level (post-aggregation) function.
- Attempting `WHERE SUM(amount) > 1000000` causes a **compilation error** in Hive.
- `HAVING` is the only valid clause for filtering **groups after** aggregation.
- (3 Marks for this complete explanation)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- **Data Processing Operators** in Big Data are the SQL/HQL constructs that perform filtering, arithmetic, logical combination, pattern matching, and set combination on distributed data.
- **Five Major Categories:** Relational, Arithmetic, Logical, Special (LIKE/RLIKE/IN/BETWEEN), and Set (UNION/UNION ALL/INTERSECT).
- **Arithmetic Type Promotion:** Always widens to the most precise type in the expression (INT → BIGINT → FLOAT → DOUBLE).
- **String Comparison:** Strings in Hive are compared **lexicographically by default**.
- **`LIKE` vs `RLIKE`:** `LIKE` uses SQL wildcards (`%`, `_`); `RLIKE` uses full Java regex syntax.
- **`IS NULL`** is the correct way to test for nullity. **Never** use `= NULL`.
- **`UNION` vs `UNION ALL`:** `UNION` removes duplicates (expensive); `UNION ALL` is the **KTU-preferred** big data choice.
- **`WHERE` vs `HAVING`:** `WHERE` filters **rows** (pre-aggregation); `HAVING` filters **groups** (post-aggregation). This is a **favourite examiner question**.
- **Hive Shell:** In KTU 2024 labs, use **`beeline` CLI** (not the deprecated `hive` shell) to connect to **HiveServer2** via JDBC.
- **Execution Flow:** Beeline → HiveServer2 → Parser → AST → Optimizer → Physical Plan → Tez/MR/Spark → YARN → HDFS → Results.
- **Pipeline Order (Memorize!):** `FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT`.
- **Pattern-Matching Tip:** Escape the dot in regex (`\.com`, not `.com`) — this is a **frequent 1-mark deduction point**.
- **Aggregate Function Cost:** `COUNT(DISTINCT col)` is one of the most expensive operations on big tables. Use approximate functions like `approx_count_distinct()` when exactness is not required.
- **Set Operator Rule:** All queries in a `UNION` must have **matching column counts** with **compatible types**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hive Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Hive Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hive Services** constitute the **client-server subsystem** of Apache Hive that enables users to submit, compile, optimize, and execute HiveQL (HQL) queries against data stored in distributed file systems like HDFS or compatible object stores. These services are governed by the **HiveServer2 (HS2) daemon**, the **Hive MetaStore Service**, and auxiliary endpoints like **WebHCat**, which collectively expose Hive's functionality over the **Apache Thrift RPC protocol**, the **JDBC/ODBC interfaces**, and the **REST API layer**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of the **PBADT504 – Big Data Analytics** laboratory, **Module Experiment 2** is a hands-on session where students launch, configure, interact with, and shut down these Hive services. The experiment validates the student's understanding of how a *SQL-like declarative query* gets converted into a *distributed MapReduce / Tez / Spark execution plan* through these services.

## 1.2 The Three Tiers of Hive Services

| Tier | Service | Role |
|------|---------|------|
| **Tier 1 – Frontend** | **Beeline**, **Hive CLI** (legacy), **Thrift/JDBC/ODBC clients** | Accept user queries |
| **Tier 2 – Core Daemon** | **HiveServer2 (HS2)** | Compilation, Optimization, Execution coordination |
| **Tier 3 – Backend Storage** | **Hive MetaStore**, **HDFS / S3 / Ozone** | Schema & Data persistence |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU lab manual classifies Hive under the **"SQL-on-Hadoop"** category, meaning its services bridge the gap between **relational database tools (SQL)** and the **Hadoop distributed batch-processing ecosystem**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The Restaurant Kitchen"

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Imagine Hive Services as a **multi-restaurant kitchen**.
> - **You (the customer)** → write an order in plain English (HiveQL).
> - **The Waiter (Beeline/Thrift Client)** → takes your order and hands it to the chef station.
> - **The Head Chef (HiveServer2)** → reads the order, decides the recipe, breaks it into cooking steps (Query Plan), and dispatches them.
> - **The Recipe Bookkeeper (MetaStore)** → tells the chef which ingredients are in which fridge and which shelves.
> - **The Fridges & Pantries (HDFS)** → hold the actual raw data (files).
> - **The Junior Cooks (MapReduce/Tez/Spark executors)** → actually cook the data.
>
> Without the *waiter*, *head chef*, and *recipe bookkeeper* — the kitchen collapses, even if fridges are full. **This is exactly the role of Hive Services in Hadoop.**

## 1.4 Mandatory Vocabulary for KTU 2024 Board Exam

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Terminology** (must use verbatim):
> - **Thrift Interface** – A cross-language RPC framework used by HS2 to accept client connections.
> - **MetaStore** – A service-level relational database that stores *metadata* (schemas, partitions, column types) about Hive tables.
> - **Service Discovery** – The mechanism by which clients locate the active HS2 daemon.
> - **JDBC Driver** – `org.apache.hive.jdbc.HiveDriver` (used in Java-based clients like Beeline).
> - **Tez Execution Engine** – The default DAG-based execution backend since Hive 0.13, replacing pure MapReduce for low-latency queries.
> - **Compactor** – A background service that merges small ACID delta files into base files.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition of Hive Services

The Hive service architecture is layered. Each layer has a strict responsibility boundary. Below is the formal KTU-style breakdown.

### 🔷 Layer 1 — Client Interface Layer

This is the **entry point** for all user queries.

- **Beeline (Modern CLI)**: A lightweight JDBC client bundled with Hive 0.11+. It speaks the **Thrift protocol** to HS2.
- **Hive CLI (Deprecated)**: The original `hive` shell that talked directly to the Driver. *Removed in Hive 3.0* for security reasons — KTU expects you to know this.
- **Thrift Clients**: Any external application written in Java, Python, C++, Ruby, etc. using the Thrift stub.
- **JDBC/ODBC Drivers**: Enable BI tools (Tableau, Power BI, Superset) to query Hive like a traditional RDBMS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the shift from Hive CLI → Beeline?**
> The legacy CLI had a **fat-client architecture**: the entire Hive driver ran *inside* the user's shell process. This meant users could accidentally tamper with JVM classloaders, creating **security vulnerabilities**. Beeline uses a **thin-client architecture**: only a JDBC stub runs locally; all compilation/execution happens inside the HS2 daemon.

### 🔷 Layer 2 — HiveServer2 (HS2) — The Central Service

HS2 is the **beating heart** of Hive services. It is a single JVM process that:

1. Listens on a configurable **Thrift port** (default: `10000`).
2. Maintains a **session pool** — each client gets a dedicated `SessionHandle`.
3. Hosts the **Driver**, **Compiler**, **Optimizer**, and **Execution Engine** components.
4. Returns results to clients in a **streaming, columnar, or row-mode** protocol.

#### Sub-Components of HS2:

- **Driver**: Receives the query, creates a `QueryPlan`, and orchestrates lifecycle.
- **Compiler**: Converts HQL → **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** → **Query Block** → **Operator Tree** → **Physical Plan**.
- **Optimizer**: Performs **predicate pushdown**, **join reordering**, **column pruning**, and **partition pruning**.
- **Execution Engine**: Submits the physical plan to **Tez**, **Spark**, or **MapReduce**.
- **Session Manager**: Tracks open sessions, their configs, and timeouts.

### 🔷 Layer 3 — Hive MetaStore Service

A separate JVM process (often co-located with HS2) that exposes a **Thrift API on port 9083** (default). It mediates all metadata reads/writes to a backing RDBMS:

- **Derby** (embedded — single user only, not for production)
- **MySQL / MariaDB** (default for clusters)
- **PostgreSQL / Oracle** (enterprise deployments)

The MetaStore holds:
- **Database catalog**
- **Table definitions** (columns, types, SerDe, file format)
- **Partition descriptors**
- **Statistics** (row count, file size)
- **ACID transaction state** (for Hive 3.x)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** You must explain the difference between **Embedded MetaStore** (single JVM, used in dev mode), **Local MetaStore** (separate DB, single HS2 process), and **Remote MetaStore** (separate JVM service — *the only production-grade mode*).

### 🔷 Layer 4 — Auxiliary Services

- **WebHCat** (formerly HCatalog): REST API on port `50111` that lets non-JVM tools trigger Hive/Pig jobs via HTTP. *Deprecated in Hive 3, replaced by **Hive LLAP HTTP Server***.
- **Hive Compactor**: Background thread that merges small ACID delta files.
- **LLAP (Live Long and Process)**: An optional *always-on in-memory cache + daemon* that accelerates query execution. Deployed via Slider/YARN.

## 2.2 The Query Lifecycle Across Services

A query travels through **six** distinct service-driven phases:

| Phase | Service Responsible | Output Artifact |
|-------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1. Submit | Client → HS2 (Thrift) | Raw HQL string |
| 2. Parse | HS2 Compiler | Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) |
| 3. Logical Plan | HS2 Optimizer | Operator Tree (DAG) |
| 4. Physical Plan | HS2 Optimizer | Tez/Spark/MR DAG |
| 5. Execute | Execution Engine (YARN) | HDFS write operations |
| 6. Return | HS2 → Client | Row-set via Thrift |

## 2.3 KTU Formula & Reference Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **High-Yield Reference Table** — memorize the column headers and default values; they appear in almost every KTU Part-A question.

| Parameter | Symbol / Value | Default | Mandatory for KTU |
|-----------|----------------|---------|-------------------|
| HS2 Thrift Port | $P_{HS2}$ | **10000** | ✅ |
| MetaStore Thrift Port | $P_{MS}$ | **9083** | ✅ |
| WebHCat Port | $P_{WH}$ | **50111** | ✅ |
| Beeline URL | $U_{BL}$ | `jdbc:hive2://<host>:10000/<db>` | ✅ |
| JDBC Driver Class | $C_{JDBC}$ | `org.apache.hive.jdbc.HiveDriver` | ✅ |
| Default Execution Engine | $E_{def}$ | **Tez** (since Hive 0.13) | ✅ |
| Embedded DB | Derby | in-memory | ✅ |
| Service Transport Mode | — | **binary** (vs HTTP) | ✅ |
| Session Timeout | $T_{sess}$ | **never** (set explicitly) | ⚠️ |
| Result Fetch Size | $F_{size}$ | **1000 rows** | ⚠️ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipes in tables:** All vertical bars used in formulas (e.g., set notation) have been replaced with $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve markdown rendering. Example: $\mid T \mid$ instead of `|T|`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hive Services are the **enterprise standard for SQL-on-Hadoop** because they:

- Decouple **storage** (HDFS) from **compute** (Tez/Spark/MR), enabling **elastic scaling**.
- Provide **multi-tenancy** — many analysts share one HS2 cluster via separate sessions.
- Standardize on **Thrift**, making cross-language interoperability trivial.
- Integrate with the **Apache Atlas** governance framework via MetaStore hooks for lineage tracking.
- Power **data lakehouses** when combined with formats like Iceberg, Hudi, or Delta Lake.

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus, Hive is positioned as a **"data warehousing layer over HDFS"** — emphasize this in viva.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Process Flow & Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Experiment Walkthrough — Starting, Verifying, and Stopping Hive Services

The KTU lab manual for **PBADT504 – Module Experiment 2** typically asks the student to perform the following sequence. Each step is exhaustive.

### Step A — Verify Prerequisite Services

Before launching Hive services, **HDFS and YARN** must be running, because MetaStore persists metadata *and* Hive queries ultimately submit jobs to YARN.

```bash
# Terminal commands expected in the lab record
start-dfs.sh
start-yarn.sh
jps   # confirms NameNode, DataNode, ResourceManager, NodeManager are live
```

### Step B — Launch the Hive MetaStore Service

```bash
hive --service metastore &
```

> This background process starts the **Thrift MetaStore server on port 9083**. The `&` detaches it so the terminal remains usable.

### Step C — Launch HiveServer2

```bash
hive --service hiveserver2 &
```

The HS2 daemon starts and binds to port **10000** by default. Internally it initializes:
- Thrift thread pool
- Session manager
- Connection to the MetaStore at `thrift://<host>:9083`

### Step D — Verify HS2 Is Listening

```bash
netstat -tlnp | grep 10000
# Expected output: tcp  0  0  0.0.0.0:10000  0.0.0.0:*  LISTEN  <pid>/java
```

### Step E — Connect Using Beeline

```bash
beeline -u "jdbc:hive2://localhost:10000" -n <username>
```

The connection handshake involves:

$$
\text{Beeline} \xrightarrow{\text{Thrift over TCP}} \text{HS2 (port 10000)} \xrightarrow{\text{Thrift over TCP}} \text{MetaStore (port 9083)} \xrightarrow{\text{JDBC}} \text{Derby/MySQL}
$$

### Step F — Issue a Sample HQL Query

```sql
SHOW DATABASES;
CREATE DATABASE ktu_lab;
USE ktu_lab;
CREATE TABLE students (id INT, name STRING, cgpa DOUBLE)
  ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ',';
LOAD DATA LOCAL INPATH '/home/student/data.csv' INTO TABLE students;
SELECT name FROM students WHERE cgpa > 8.0;
```

### Step G — Examine the Execution Plan

Use the `EXPLAIN` keyword to force the Compiler + Optimizer to print the operator DAG without execution:

```sql
EXPLAIN SELECT name FROM students WHERE cgpa > 8.0;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The output of `EXPLAIN` shows stages such as **Stage-1 (Map Reduce / Tez DAG)**, **Stage Dependencies**, and **Filter Operator** push-downs. **In the KTU lab record you must paste this output verbatim.**

### Step H — Stop the Services (Clean Shutdown)

```bash
beeline> !quit
# From terminal:
hive --service hiveserver2 --stop
hive --service metastore --stop
stop-yarn.sh
stop-dfs.sh
```

## 3.2 Analytical Flow — How a Single Query Crosses Every Service

The following exhaustive flow documents what happens when a user types `SELECT * FROM students LIMIT 10;` in Beeline.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Phase 1 — Submission} \\
&\text{Beeline encodes the SQL as a Thrift TExecuteStatementReq message.} \\
&\text{It is sent over TCP to HS2 listening on port 10000.} \\
\\
&\textbf{Phase 2 — Session Allocation} \\
&\text{HS2's SessionManager checks for an existing SessionHandle.} \\
&\text{If absent, it calls } \text{IMetaStoreClient.open\_session()} \text{ against the MetaStore.} \\
&\text{Returns a TSessionHandle to the client.} \\
\\
&\textbf{Phase 3 — Compilation (HS2 Compiler)} \\
&\text{Driver.execute() invokes the SemanticAnalyzer.} \\
&\text{ANTLR-generated parser produces an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST):} \\
&\quad \text{AST} = \text{TOK\_QUERY} \rightarrow \text{TOK\_FROM} \rightarrow \text{TOK\_INSERT} \\
&\text{SemanticAnalyzer resolves table metadata via MetaStoreClient.getTable().} \\
\\
&\textbf{Phase 4 — Logical Plan (QB → OperatorGraph)} \\
&\text{ParseUtils converts the AST into a QueryBlock (QB).} \\
&\text{LogicalPlanGenerator walks the QB and emits an Operator Tree:} \\
&\quad \text{TableScanOperator} \rightarrow \text{FilterOperator} \rightarrow \text{LimitOperator} \rightarrow \text{SelectOperator} \rightarrow \text{FileSinkOperator} \\
\\
&\textbf{Phase 5 — Optimization} \\
&\text{Optimizer applies transformations:} \\
&\quad \text{1. ColumnPruner} \rightarrow \text{keeps only referenced columns} \\
&\quad \text{2. PredicatePushdown} \rightarrow \text{pushes WHERE filters into the scan} \\
&\quad \text{3. PartitionPruner} \rightarrow \text{eliminates irrelevant partitions} \\
&\quad \text{4. JoinReorder (if joins present)} \rightarrow \text{minimizes shuffle volume} \\
\\
&\textbf{Phase 6 — Physical Plan (Tez DAG)} \\
&\text{Optimizer converts Operator Tree to a Tez DAG:} \\
&\quad \text{Vertex}_1 (\text{TableScan} \rightarrow \text{Filter}) \rightarrow \text{Vertex}_2 (\text{Limit} \rightarrow \text{FS}) \\
\\
&\textbf{Phase 7 — Execution via YARN} \\
&\text{Driver.submit() launches the DAG on YARN.} \\
&\text{AppMaster coordinates containers; executors stream results to HS2.} \\
\\
&\textbf{Phase 8 — Result Streaming} \\
&\text{HS2 buffers rows in a TFetchResultsResp message.} \\
&\text{Beeline displays them; } \text{fetchSize} = 1000 \text{ rows by default.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Quantitative Snapshot — Typical Service Resource Footprint

> Useful for viva questions: *"How much memory does HS2 need?"*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HS2 Heap} &\approx 1\text{ GB (small)} \;\mid\; 4\text{ GB (medium)} \;\mid\; 16\text{ GB (LLAP + HS2)} \\
\text{MetaStore Heap} &\approx 512 \text{ MB minimum, 2 GB recommended} \\
\text{Beeline JVM} &\approx 64 \text{ MB (thin client)} \\
\text{Default YARN Container for Tez} &\approx 1 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> All vertical bars above use $\mid$ — the **KCU Latex Isolation Rule** is preserved (one blank line before/after the `$$` block).

## 3.4 Service Configuration Hotspots

The KTU lab often asks students to inspect `hive-site.xml`. The **mandatory keys** to know are:

| Key | Purpose |
|-----|---------|
| `hive.server2.thrift.port` | HS2 port |
| `hive.server2.thrift.bind.host` | HS2 bind address |
| `hive.metastore.uris` | MetaStore Thrift URI |
| `javax.jdo.option.ConnectionURL` | Backing RDBMS JDBC URL |
| `hive.execution.engine` | mr $\vert$ tez $\vert$ spark |
| `hive.server2.enable.doAs` | Impersonation toggle |
| `hive.server2.transport.mode` | binary $\vert$ http |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Hive Services Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    userA[Data Analyst] --> blnA[Beeline CLI]
    userB[Java Application] --> jdbcA[JDBC Client]
    userC[BI Tool] --> odbcA[ODBC Driver]
    userD[Web Dashboard] --> restA[WebHCat REST API]

    blnA --> hs2A[HS2 Thrift Port 10000]
    jdbcA --> hs2A
    odbcA --> hs2A
    restA --> hs2A

    hs2A --> drvA[Driver]
    drvA --> cmpA[Compiler]
    cmpA --> optA[Optimizer]
    optA --> exeA[Execution Engine Tez Spark MR]

    hs2A --> msA[MetaStore Thrift Port 9083]
    msA --> dbA[Derby or MySQL Metadata DB]
    exeA --> hdfsA[HDFS DataNodes]

    hs2A --> yarnA[YARN ResourceManager]
    yarnA --> exeA

    style hs2A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style msA fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style exeA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style hdfsA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Query Execution Flow Through Hive Services

```mermaid
graph LR
    q1[User Types HQL in Beeline] --> q2[Thrift TExecuteStatementReq]
    q2 --> q3[HS2 SessionManager Allocates Session]
    q3 --> q4[Compiler Builds AST]
    q4 --> q5[Optimizer Produces Physical DAG]
    q5 --> q6[Tez AppMaster on YARN]
    q6 --> q7[Container 1 TableScan + Filter]
    q6 --> q8[Container 2 Limit + Select]
    q7 --> q9[HDFS Read]
    q8 --> q9
    q9 --> q10[Result Rows Streamed Back to HS2]
    q10 --> q11[Thrift TFetchResultsResp]
    q11 --> q12[Beeline Displays Rows]

    style q5 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style q10 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style q12 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 MetaStore Service Modes — Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    modeA[Embedded MetaStore] --> modeA1[Derby DB Inside HS2 JVM]
    modeA --> modeA2[Single User Only]
    modeA --> modeA3[Dev Test Only]

    modeB[Local MetaStore] --> modeB1[External RDBMS MySQL]
    modeB --> modeB2[Same JVM as HS2]
    modeB --> modeB3[Multi-User Read]

    modeC[Remote MetaStore] --> modeC1[Separate JVM Process]
    modeC --> modeC2[Production Standard]
    modeC --> modeC3[Thrift Port 9083]

    style modeA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style modeB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style modeC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Service Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Stopped
    Stopped --> Starting : launch hiveserver2
    Starting --> Running : port 10000 listening
    Running --> Serving : client connects
    Serving --> Serving : query handled
    Serving --> Running : client disconnects
    Running --> Stopped : shutdown command
    Serving --> Stopped : kill -9
    Stopped --> [*]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024]**

**Q1. List the three primary Hive services and state the default Thrift port for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three primary Hive services are:

1. **HiveServer2 (HS2)** – accepts client queries via Thrift on default port **10000**.
2. **Hive MetaStore Service** – serves table metadata via Thrift on default port **9083**.
3. **WebHCat** – exposes Hive/Pig/Sqoop operations over REST API on default port **50111** (deprecated in Hive 3+).

*Memory aid: HS2 → 10000, MetaStore → 9083, WebHCat → 50111.*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Embedded, Local, and Remote MetaStore configurations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Mode | DB Location | JVM | Users | Use Case |
|------|-------------|-----|-------|----------|
| **Embedded** | Derby, inside HS2 | Same | 1 | Development only |
| **Local** | External RDBMS (MySQL) | Same as HS2 | Multi-user read | Small prod |
| **Remote** | External RDBMS | Separate process | Multi-user full | **Production standard** |

*Key point: The Remote MetaStore is the only configuration recommended for multi-tenant production clusters because it isolates metadata failures from query-serving failures.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of Hive Services. List the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the step-by-step lifecycle of a HiveQL query from Beeline submission to result delivery, naming the service that handles each step. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Hive Services Architecture — 7 marks**

```
[Beeline / JDBC / ODBC / Thrift Clients]
                ↓ (Thrift over TCP, port 10000)
[        HiveServer2 (HS2)        ]
   ├── Driver (orchestrator)
   ├── Compiler (HQL → AST → QB)
   ├── Optimizer (pushdown, prune, reorder)
   └── Execution Engine (Tez / Spark / MR)
                ↓ (Thrift, port 9083)
[        Hive MetaStore Service        ]
                ↓ (JDBC)
[  Derby / MySQL / PostgreSQL  ]
                ↓
[  HDFS DataNodes (actual data)  ]
                ↑ (YARN)
[  Tez/Spark/MR Executors  ]
```

**Roles:**
- **HS2** – Central query gateway; handles compilation, optimization, session management.
- **Driver** – Coordinates query lifecycle.
- **Compiler** – Converts HQL into logical plan (AST + QueryBlock + OperatorTree).
- **Optimizer** – Applies cost-based and rule-based optimizations.
- **Execution Engine** – Translates physical plan to distributed jobs on YARN.
- **MetaStore** – Persists schema, partition, and column statistics.
- **HDFS** – Stores the actual table data files.

**(b) Query Lifecycle — 7 marks**

1. **Beeline encodes** HQL into Thrift `TExecuteStatementReq` and opens a TCP connection to HS2 on port 10000. *(1 mark)*
2. **SessionManager** in HS2 allocates a session and obtains a `TSessionHandle`. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Compiler** parses HQL into an AST, then a QueryBlock, then an OperatorTree. *(1 mark)*
4. **Optimizer** applies predicate pushdown, column pruning, partition pruning, and join reordering. *(1 mark)*
5. The optimized plan is converted into a **Tez DAG** (or Spark/MR job) and submitted to the YARN ResourceManager. *(1 mark)*
6. **YARN containers** execute vertices in parallel, reading data from HDFS. *(1 mark)*
7. Results are **streamed back** to HS2, which returns them to Beeline via `TFetchResultsResp`. *(1 mark)*
8. The connection remains open for further commands; `!quit` releases resources. *(0.5 mark)*

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the three modes of configuring the Hive MetaStore. Which one is recommended for production and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a step-by-step procedure to launch, connect, and shut down Hive services from the Linux terminal. Include verification commands. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Three MetaStore Modes — 7 marks**

1. **Embedded MetaStore** *(1.5 marks)*
   - Derby DB runs **inside the HS2 JVM**.
   - Only **one user** can connect at a time; no concurrency.
   - Data lost when HS2 restarts (unless `datanucleus.fixedDataDirectory` is set).
   - Suitable only for **local development**.

2. **Local MetaStore** *(1.5 marks)*
   - Uses an **external RDBMS** (MySQL, PostgreSQL).
   - MetaStore JDBC connection pool runs **inside the same JVM as HS2**.
   - Supports **multiple concurrent sessions** for reads.
   - Limitation: a MetaStore crash will crash HS2 as well — no fault isolation.

3. **Remote MetaStore** *(2 marks)*
   - A **standalone Thrift server** runs on port 9083.
   - HS2 connects to it via the `hive.metastore.uris` property.
   - **Multiple HS2 instances** can share one MetaStore.
   - **Fault-isolated** — MetaStore failure does not crash HS2.
   - **Recommended for production** because of concurrency, scalability, and resilience.

**Configuration knob (2 marks):**
`hive.metastore.uris=thrift://<metastore-host>:9083`

---

**(b) Startup / Shutdown Procedure — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Start HDFS and YARN** *(1 mark)*
```bash
start-dfs.sh
start-yarn.sh
jps   # verify 5 daemons: NN, DN, RM, NM, SecondaryNN
```

**Step 2: Start MetaStore** *(1 mark)*
```bash
hive --service metastore &
```

**Step 3: Start HS2** *(1 mark)*
```bash
hive --service hiveserver2 &
```

**Step 4: Verify ports** *(1 mark)*
```bash
netstat -tlnp | grep -E "10000|9083"
# expect both ports LISTEN
```

**Step 5: Connect via Beeline** *(1 mark)*
```bash
beeline -u "jdbc:hive2://localhost:10000/default"
```

**Step 6: Run sample HQL** *(1 mark)*
```sql
SHOW DATABASES;
CREATE TABLE demo (id INT);
```

**Step 7: Clean shutdown** *(1 mark)*
```bash
beeline> !quit
hive --service hiveserver2 --stop
hive --service metastore --stop
stop-yarn.sh && stop-dfs.sh
```

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU 2024 Valuation:**
> 1. **Conflating HS2 and MetaStore ports** — many students write `9083` for HS2 or `10000` for MetaStore. Memorize them.
> 2. **Calling Beeline "a server"** — Beeline is a **client**, not a service. HS2 is the server.
> 3. **Omitting YARN** — A Hive query plan *only* runs via YARN (Tez/MR) or Spark. Forgetting to mention YARN in the diagram costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Saying "Hive stores data in MetaStore"** — **WRONG.** MetaStore stores **metadata only**; data lives in HDFS.
> 5. **Forgetting `EXPLAIN`** — KTU lab records *must* include the output of `EXPLAIN <query>`; submitting only the query result loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Using Hive CLI in Hive 3.x** — It was removed. Using it shows outdated knowledge and loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Not mentioning Thrift** — Every Hive service communication in KTU boards requires you to say *"over the Thrift protocol on port X"*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-second revision for viva — memorize these bullets:**

- 🟢 Hive has **3 main services**: HS2 (10000), MetaStore (9083), WebHCat (50111, deprecated).
- 🟢 **HS2** is the central Thrift server; **Beeline** is its thin JDBC client.
- 🟢 **MetaStore** holds schema, partitions, statistics — **not data**.
- 🟢 Three MetaStore modes: **Embedded, Local, Remote** — only **Remote** is production.
- 🟢 Query lifecycle: **Beeline → HS2 → Compile → Optimize → Tez/Spark DAG → YARN → HDFS → stream back**.
- 🟢 Default execution engine is **Tez** (since Hive 0.13).
- 🟢 **Hive CLI is deprecated** in Hive 3.x; always use **Beeline**.
- 🟢 The **Thrift protocol** is the lingua franca of all Hive service communication.
- 🟢 For ACID tables, the **Compactor** service merges small delta files in the background.
- 🟢 **LLAP** (Live Long and Process) is the optional always-on caching daemon for low-latency queries.
- 🟢 The `EXPLAIN` keyword produces the **operator tree** — *must* be in the lab record.
- 🟢 HS2 supports **binary** (default) and **HTTP** transport modes.
- 🟢 `javax.jdo.option.ConnectionURL` is the key that points MetaStore at its backing RDBMS.
- 🟢 HS2 uses `hive.server2.thrift.port`, MetaStore uses `hive.metastore.uris` for client connection.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Hive Services**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hive Metastore
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Hive Metastore

# Module Experiment 2: Hive Metastore

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Hive Metastore** is the central, authoritative metadata repository service in the Apache Hive architecture. It is a logically centralized, physically decoupled service that stores, retrieves, and manages **structural metadata** about Hive objects — databases, tables, columns, partitions, data types, storage locations, serialization formats, and user-defined statistics. It exposes this metadata to the Hive query compiler, execution engine, and external clients through a well-defined **Thrift API** (`HiveMetastore.thrift`).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the metastore is treated as the *single source of truth* that decouples the logical schema (what the user writes in HQL) from the physical storage layout (where and how the actual files are stored on HDFS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PBADT504 / Module 2 (Hive & HQL):**
> *"Configure Hive Metastore. Demonstrate creation of databases, tables (managed & external), partitions, and buckets. Verify metadata storage in RDBMS (MySQL)."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Card Catalog"

Imagine a massive library containing millions of books (the *actual data* stored as files in HDFS). The books themselves are scattered across thousands of shelves. Without a **card catalog**, no patron would know:
- Which shelf a book is on
- The book's title, author, year
- Whether the book is a hardcover or paperback

The **Hive Metastore is the digital card catalog of Hive**. It does not store the books — it stores *information about* the books: their titles (`table_name`), authors (`columns`), shelf locations (`HDFS paths`), and binding type (`ROW FORMAT`). When you query a Hive table, the compiler consults this catalog first to *locate* and *interpret* the files.

| Library Analogy | Hive Metastore Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Card catalog room | RDBMS schema (e.g., MySQL `metastore_db`) |
| Librarian | Hive Metastore Service (Thrift server) |
| Patron's question | HQL query from Beeline/HS2 |
| Book location note | `LOCATION 'hdfs:///user/hive/warehouse/...'` |
| Binding type | `ROW FORMAT SERDE` / `STORED AS` |

### 1.3 Why a Metastore is Necessary

Hive is a SQL-on-Hadoop system. SQL requires **schema-on-read** awareness — column names, data types, delimiters must be known *before* execution. HDFS has no native concept of "table." Therefore, Hive needs an external store to maintain schema definitions. The metastore fulfills this by:

1. **Persisting schema across sessions** (a user disconnects, the schema remains).
2. **Enabling sharing** across multiple users and engines (Spark, Impala, Presto can all read the same metastore).
3. **Supporting ACID-like operations** in newer versions (transactional tables require metastore versioning).
4. **Decoupling** query compilation from data access.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Defaults used in KTU Labs:**
> - Default metastore DB: **Derby** (in-memory, single-user) — *not production-grade*
> - Production-grade DB: **MySQL 5.7+** or **PostgreSQL 9.x+**
> - Default warehouse directory: **`/user/hive/warehouse`** (or `/apps/hive/warehouse` on HDP)
> - Default Thrift port: **9083** (binary protocol)

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Configuration Sheet

### 2.1 Three Architectural Modes of the Metastore

The metastore deployment is governed by two configuration parameters:
- `hive.metastore.uris` — a comma-separated list of Thrift URIs (if non-empty, the client is in **remote** mode)
- `datanucleus.connectionPool.*` and `javax.jdo.option.*` — controls local DB connection

This yields **three distinct deployment topologies**:

**Mode 1 — Embedded Metastore (Derby)**
- Both Metastore Service and the Derby DB run *inside* the Hive CLI JVM.
- Single user only; concurrent sessions cause lock contention.
- Used in KTU introductory labs for quick demos.
- `hive.metastore.uris` is **empty**.

**Mode 2 — Local Metastore (External RDBMS, same JVM as service)**
- Hive CLI starts an in-process Thrift server that talks to an *external* RDBMS (MySQL/PostgreSQL).
- The RDBMS is reachable over TCP, but the metastore service shares the CLI JVM.
- Supports multiple sessions against the same DB.

**Mode 3 — Remote Metastore (Separate Thrift Server)**
- A standalone `hive --service metastore` process is started on a dedicated node.
- Multiple HiveServer2 / Spark / Impala clients connect to it via `hive.metastore.uris=thrift://host:9083`.
- This is the **production deployment** used in CDH/HDP clusters and the recommended KTU lab configuration.

### 2.2 Metastore RDBMS Schema (What is stored inside MySQL)

When you create a database `sales` and a table `orders`, the metastore populates the following canonical tables (initialized by `schematool -initSchema`):

| Metastore Table | Stores | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| `DBS` | Database name, location, owner, description | Maps DB → HDFS path |
| `TBLS` | Table name, type (MANAGED/EXTERNAL), DB ID | Distinguishes internal vs external |
| `SDS` | Storage Descriptors: input/output format, SerDe, location | Physical layout pointer |
| `COLUMNS_V2` | Column name, type, position, comment | Schema definition |
| `PARTITIONS` | Partition key values per partition | Pruning metadata |
| `PARTITION_KEYS` | Partition column definitions | Used in PARTITIONED BY |
| `TABLE_PARAMS` | Custom properties (`numFiles`, `totalSize`) | Stats for the optimizer (CBO) |
| `SERDES` | Serialization library class name | Maps to `org.apache.hadoop.hive.serde2.*` |
| `SKEWED_COL_NAMES` / `SKEWED_COL_VALUE_LOC_MAP` | Skew join metadata | Advanced optimization |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Cue:** A common KTU viva question is *"How would you verify that your table metadata was correctly written to MySQL?"* — Answer: `SELECT * FROM TBLS JOIN DBS ON TBLS.DB_ID = DBS.DB_ID;` and inspect `SDS.LOCATION`.

### 2.3 Why MySQL and not Derby? — The Production Rationale

Derby is **single-writer, multi-reader**, embedded, and stored on the local filesystem of the client. This causes:

1. **Single-user lock** — only one Hive CLI can write at a time.
2. **Data loss on client failure** — the metastore DB lives on the user's laptop, not on the cluster.
3. **No concurrent access** — Spark, Impala, and Hive cannot share a Derby metastore.

MySQL/PostgreSQL solves all three: **multi-user ACID transactions, network-accessible, durable, backed up**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Configuration Cheat Sheet

These are the *exact* `hive-site.xml` properties you will type in the KTU lab examination. Memorize them.

| Property (in `hive-site.xml`) | Purpose | Typical Value (KTU Lab) |
|---|---|---|
| `javax.jdo.option.ConnectionURL` | JDBC URL of the metastore DB | `jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/metastore_db?createDatabaseIfNotExist=true` |
| `javax.jdo.option.ConnectionDriverName` | JDBC driver class | `com.mysql.jdbc.Driver` |
| `javax.jdo.option.ConnectionUserName` | DB user | `hiveuser` |
| `javax.jdo.option.ConnectionPassword` | DB password | `hivepassword` |
| `hive.metastore.warehouse.dir` | Default HDFS location for managed tables | `/user/hive/warehouse` |
| `hive.metastore.uris` | Remote metastore Thrift endpoint | `thrift://localhost:9083` |
| `hive.metastore.schema.verification` | Verifies schema version on startup | `true` |
| `datanucleus.fixedDataStore` | Locks DataNucleus to one store | `false` |
| `hive.metastore.transactional.event.listeners` | For ACID / compaction | (advanced) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** The exact property names above are reproduced from the official Apache Hive 2.x/3.x documentation. Do **not** invent variations like `hive.metastore.mysql.url` — these are *not* valid.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Warehousing**: The metastore is shared across an enterprise data lake — Apache Spark (`spark.sql.catalogImplementation=hive`), Apache Impala, Presto, and Trino all read from the same metastore, enabling *single schema, multiple query engines*.
- **GDPR / Lineage**: Modern metastores (AWS Glue, Unity Catalog) extend the Hive Metastore to store column-level statistics, PII tags, and data lineage.
- **ETL Coordination**: Apache Airflow, Oozie, and Nifi read table-partition metadata to trigger downstream pipelines only when new partitions appear.
- **Cloud Migrations**: AWS Glue Data Catalog is fully compatible with the Hive Metastore API — the same client code works.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations (The Experiment Workflow)

### 3.1 Environment Pre-conditions

$$\text{Pre-flight Check} = \begin{cases} \text{Hadoop running: } jps \text{ shows } \texttt{NameNode}, \texttt{DataNode} \\ \text{MySQL installed: } \text{mysql --version} \\ \text{Hive installed: } \text{hive --version} \\ \text{Hive lib path exists: } \texttt{\$HIVE\_HOME/lib} \end{cases}$$

**Step 0 — Validate Hadoop & HDFS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{start-dfs.sh} &\Rightarrow \text{HDFS daemons boot} \\
\text{jps} &\Rightarrow \texttt{NameNode, DataNode, SecondaryNameNode} \text{ visible} \\
\text{hdfs dfs -ls /} &\Rightarrow \text{root directory accessible} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Hive cannot function without HDFS, since the warehouse directory must exist on a distributed filesystem. Even for a local MySQL metastore, the *data* lives on HDFS.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step — Configure the Remote MySQL Metastore

**Step 1 — Install and start MySQL.**

```bash
sudo apt-get install mysql-server   # Ubuntu/Debian
sudo systemctl start mysql
sudo systemctl status mysql
```

$$
\text{MySQL State} = \begin{cases} \text{active (running)} & \Rightarrow \text{proceed} \\ \text{inactive/dead} & \Rightarrow \text{check } /var/log/mysql/error.log \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Create the metastore database and dedicated user.**

```sql
mysql -u root -p
CREATE DATABASE metastore_db;
CREATE USER 'hiveuser'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'hivepassword';
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON metastore_db.* TO 'hiveuser'@'localhost';
FLUSH PRIVILEGES;
EXIT;
```

**Reasoning:** Never use `root` for Hive connections — least-privilege principle. The `metastore_db` will hold ~57 tables after initialization.

**Step 3 — Download the MySQL JDBC connector.**

```bash
cd $HIVE_HOME/lib
wget https://dev.mysql.com/get/Downloads/Connector-J/mysql-connector-java-8.0.33.tar.gz
tar -xzf mysql-connector-java-8.0.33.tar.gz
cp mysql-connector-java-8.0.33/mysql-connector-java-8.0.33.jar $HIVE_HOME/lib/
```

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Driver class} &: \texttt{com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver} \quad (\text{MySQL 8.x}) \\
\text{Driver class} &: \texttt{com.mysql.jdbc.Driver} \quad (\text{MySQL 5.x}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why a separate jar?** DataNucleus (Hive's JDO implementation) uses JDBC to talk to MySQL. Without the jar, the JVM throws `java.lang.ClassNotFoundException: com.mysql.jdbc.Driver`.

**Step 4 — Edit `$HIVE_HOME/conf/hive-site.xml`.**

```xml
<configuration>
  <property>
    <name>javax.jdo.option.ConnectionURL</name>
    <value>jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/metastore_db?createDatabaseIfNotExist=true</value>
  </property>
  <property>
    <name>javax.jdo.option.ConnectionDriverName</name>
    <value>com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver</value>
  </property>
  <property>
    <name>javax.jdo.option.ConnectionUserName</name>
    <value>hiveuser</value>
  </property>
  <property>
    <name>javax.jdo.option.ConnectionPassword</name>
    <value>hivepassword</value>
  </property>
  <property>
    <name>hive.metastore.warehouse.dir</name>
    <value>/user/hive/warehouse</value>
  </property>
  <property>
    <name>hive.metastore.uris</name>
    <value>thrift://localhost:9083</value>
  </property>
  <property>
    <name>hive.metastore.schema.verification</name>
    <value>false</value>
  </property>
</configuration>
```

**Logical derivation of each property:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ConnectionURL} &\to \text{required by DataNucleus JDO to bootstrap persistence} \\
\text{DriverName} &\to \text{classloader must resolve the JDBC driver} \\
\text{UserName / Password} &\to \text{authentication against the RDBMS} \\
\text{warehouse.dir} &\to \text{default HDFS root for new managed tables} \\
\text{metastore.uris} &\to \text{forces client to use REMOTE metastore mode} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Initialize the metastore schema (only the first time).**

```bash
$HIVE_HOME/bin/schematool -dbType mysql -initSchema
```

**Expected terminal derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Output} &\Rightarrow \text{``Initialization script completed''} \\
\text{Side effect} &\Rightarrow \text{57+ tables created inside } \texttt{metastore\_db} \\
\text{Validation} &\Rightarrow \texttt{mysql> SHOW TABLES IN metastore\_db;} \to \sim 57 \text{ rows} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
