> If the schema already exists, the command fails with `Schema initialization FAILED! MetaException: Version stamp not in compatible format`. Fix: drop the DB and re-run, or use `-upgradeSchema` instead of `-initSchema`.

**Step 6 — Start the Hive Metastore service in the background.**

```bash
nohup $HIVE_HOME/bin/hive --service metastore > /tmp/metastore.log 2>&1 &
```

**Verification:**

```bash
netstat -tnlp | grep 9083
# Expected output: tcp 0 0 0.0.0.0:9083 ... LISTEN <pid>/java
```

$$
\text{Port } 9083 \text{ active} \iff \text{Metastore Thrift server is reachable}
$$

**Step 7 — Launch Hive CLI and create a database + table.**

```sql
hive>
CREATE DATABASE sales_db
COMMENT 'KTU Big Data Analytics Lab'
LOCATION '/user/hive/warehouse/sales_db.db';

USE sales_db;

CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id     INT,
    customer_id  INT,
    amount       DOUBLE,
    order_date   STRING
)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED
FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE
LOCATION '/user/hive/warehouse/sales_db.db/orders';
```

**Step 8 — Verify the metadata was persisted in MySQL.**

```sql
USE metastore_db;
SELECT T.TBL_NAME, D.NAME AS database_name, S.LOCATION, T.TBL_TYPE
FROM TBLS T
JOIN DBS D ON T.DB_ID = D.DB_ID
JOIN SDS S ON T.SD_ID = S.SD_ID;
```

**Expected result:**

| TBL_NAME | database_name | LOCATION | TBL_TYPE |
|---|---|---|---|
| orders | sales_db | `/user/hive/warehouse/sales_db.db/orders` | MANAGED_TABLE |

**Derivation of what was stored:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1 row in DBS} &\Rightarrow \texttt{sales\_db} \text{ registered} \\
\text{1 row in TBLS} &\Rightarrow \texttt{orders} \text{ registered as MANAGED\_TABLE} \\
\text{1 row in SDS} &\Rightarrow \text{storage descriptor with LOCATION} \\
\text{4 rows in COLUMNS\_V2} &\Rightarrow \text{order\_id, customer\_id, amount, order\_date} \\
\text{1 row in SERDES} &\Rightarrow \text{field delim = ','} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Verify the HDFS layout.**

```bash
hdfs dfs -ls /user/hive/warehouse/sales_db.db/orders
hdfs dfs -ls /user/hive/warehouse/sales_db.db/
```

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expected path on HDFS} &\Rightarrow \text{empty directory (no data loaded yet)} \\
\text{Proof of decoupling} &\Rightarrow \text{mySQL has schema; HDFS has data; they are linked by LOCATION} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derived Formula: The Metastore-Storage Coupling Equation

The relationship between a Hive table, the metastore, and HDFS can be expressed as:

$$
\text{Table}(T) \;=\; \underbrace{M(T)}_{\text{schema in MySQL}} \;\cup\; \underbrace{F(T)}_{\text{files on HDFS}}
$$

where the joining function is:

$$
M(T) \to F(T) \quad \text{via} \quad \text{SELECT LOCATION FROM SDS WHERE SD\_ID = T.SD\_ID}
$$

For a **partitioned** table, this becomes:

$$
F(T) \;=\; \bigcup_{p \,\in\, \text{Partitions}(T)} \text{Path}\big(M(T).LOCATION \,/\, p.values\big)
$$

**Boundary conditions** (when the table is "visible" to Hive):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Table is queryable} &\iff M(T) \neq \emptyset \;\;\land\;\; \text{HDFS path in } M(T) \text{ exists} \\
\text{External table} &\Rightarrow \text{Dropping } T \text{ removes } M(T) \text{ only, not } F(T) \\
\text{Managed table} &\Rightarrow \text{Dropping } T \text{ removes both } M(T) \text{ and } F(T) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# (Section 1 — Core Definition — already delivered above; continuing the technical specification)

> **KTU Recap of Section 1 — Three Things Every Student Must Memorise:**
> 1. The metastore stores **schema, not data**.
> 2. The metastore is a **Thrift-accessible service** (port 9083), backed by an **RDBMS** (MySQL).
> 3. **Derby** = single-user, demo only. **MySQL** = production.

## 2.1 The Hive Stack — Where the Metastore Sits

The metastore is one of three core services in Hive's runtime stack:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hive Stack} &= \underbrace{\text{HiveServer2}}_{\text{Thrift API to clients (Beeline, JDBC, ODBC)}} \\
&\;\;\cup \underbrace{\text{Driver}}_{\text{Compiler, Optimizer, Execution Engine}} \\
&\;\;\cup \underbrace{\text{Metastore}}_{\text{Schema repository, accessed by Driver}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

A query lifecycle:

$$
\text{Client} \to \text{HiveServer2} \to \text{Driver} \to \text{Metastore (get schema)} \to \text{HDFS (read data)} \to \text{Result}
$$

## 2.2 The KTU-Mandated Configuration Sheet (Replicated for Reference)

| Property | Sample Value | Mandatory? |
|---|---|---|
| `javax.jdo.option.ConnectionURL` | `jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/metastore_db` | Yes |
| `javax.jdo.option.ConnectionDriverName` | `com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver` | Yes |
| `javax.jdo.option.ConnectionUserName` | `hiveuser` | Yes |
| `javax.jdo.option.ConnectionPassword` | `hivepassword` | Yes |
| `hive.metastore.warehouse.dir` | `/user/hive/warehouse` | Yes |
| `hive.metastore.uris` | `thrift://localhost:9083` | Required for remote mode |
| `hive.metastore.schema.verification` | `false` (during initial setup) | Recommended `true` later |
| `datanucleus.autoCreateSchema` | `true` (only during init) | Should be `false` in prod |
| `datanucleus.fixedDataStore` | `false` | Default |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3 — Analytical Matrix & Detailed Step-by-Step

## 3.1 Procedural Decision Matrix — Which Metastore Mode Should You Use?

| Mode | Service JVM | DB Type | Concurrent Users | KTU Use Case | Setup Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Embedded (Derby) | Inside CLI | Derby (in-mem) | 1 | Demo, first-time learners | 0 (default) |
| Local (MySQL, in-process) | Inside CLI | MySQL/PostgreSQL | Multiple | Lab sessions on a single node | Medium |
| Remote (Thrift 9083) | Separate daemon | MySQL/PostgreSQL | Many (cluster-wide) | Production, multi-engine | High |

**Decision rule:**

$$
\text{Choose Remote} \iff \text{multiple users} \lor \text{multiple query engines} \lor \text{prod}
$$

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Verification Workflow (Analytical Matrix)

After every Hive DDL command, run the corresponding MySQL query to confirm persistence:

| HQL Command | Metastore Tables Affected | Verification SQL |
|---|---|---|
| `CREATE DATABASE x` | `DBS` | `SELECT * FROM DBS WHERE NAME='x';` |
| `CREATE TABLE t` (managed) | `TBLS`, `SDS`, `COLUMNS_V2`, `SERDES` | `SELECT * FROM TBLS WHERE TBL_NAME='t';` |
| `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE t` | `TBLS` (TBL_TYPE='EXTERNAL_TABLE') | `SELECT TBL_TYPE FROM TBLS WHERE TBL_NAME='t';` |
| `ALTER TABLE t ADD PARTITION` | `PARTITIONS`, `PARTITION_KEYS` | `SELECT * FROM PARTITIONS;` |
| `DROP TABLE t` (managed) | rows removed from `TBLS`, HDFS files also removed | `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM TBLS;` |
| `DROP TABLE t` (external) | rows removed from `TBLS`, HDFS files **preserved** | Confirm `hdfs dfs -ls <location>` |

## 3.3 Derivation of the Thrift API Surface

The metastore exposes its operations through a generated Java interface (`IMetaStoreClient`) that is bound to a Thrift IDL (`HiveMetastore.thrift`). The principal operations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{create\_database}(\text{db}: \text{Database}) &\Rightarrow \text{INSERT INTO DBS} \\
\text{create\_table}(\text{tbl}: \text{Table}) &\Rightarrow \text{INSERT INTO TBLS, SDS, COLUMNS\_V2, SERDES} \\
\text{get\_table}(\text{dbName}, \text{tblName}) &\Rightarrow \text{SELECT * FROM TBLS JOIN ...} \\
\text{add\_partition}(\text{req}: \text{AddPartitionRequest}) &\Rightarrow \text{INSERT INTO PARTITIONS} \\
\text{drop\_partition}(\text{req}) &\Rightarrow \text{DELETE FROM PARTITIONS} \\
\text{get\_partitions}(\text{dbName}, \text{tblName}) &\Rightarrow \text{SELECT * FROM PARTITIONS WHERE ...} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Each method is `RPC-marshalled` over a single TCP connection to `thrift://host:9083`. HiveServer2 maintains a connection pool of these clients.

## 3.4 Common Failure Modes and Their Root-Cause Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Error: ``Could not open client transport with JDBC Uri''} &\Rightarrow \text{metastore not started, or wrong port} \\
\textbf{Error: ``javax.jdo.JDOFatalInternalException''} &\Rightarrow \text{mySQL JDBC driver not in } \$HIVE\_HOME/lib \\
\textbf{Error: ``Specified key was too long; max key length is 767 bytes''} &\Rightarrow \text{MySQL default charset utf8mb4 uses 4 bytes/char — change to latin1 or use utf8} \\
\textbf{Error: ``Access denied for user 'hiveuser'@'localhost'''} &\Rightarrow \text{GRANT step skipped or wrong password} \\
\textbf{Error: ``Schema version mismatch''} &\Rightarrow \text{Re-run schematool with } \texttt{-upgradeSchema} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU lab exam, if the metastore "doesn't connect," **80% of the time** the issue is either (a) the JDBC jar is missing, or (b) the `hive.metastore.uris` and the JDO connection point to the *same* database but the service wasn't started. Always run `jps` and `netstat -tnlp` to verify.

## 3.5 Detailed Walkthrough — Creating a Partitioned Table

```sql
CREATE TABLE sales (
    txn_id   INT,
    product  STRING,
    amount   DOUBLE
)
PARTITIONED BY (year INT, month INT)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE;
```

**Metastore state after creation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1 row in TBLS} &\Rightarrow \text{sales (TBL\_TYPE = MANAGED\_TABLE)} \\
\text{3 rows in COLUMNS\_V2} &\Rightarrow \text{txn\_id, product, amount (CD\_ID from SDS)} \\
\text{2 rows in PARTITION\_KEYS} &\Rightarrow \text{year (INT), month (INT)} \\
\text{1 row in SDS} &\Rightarrow \text{LOCATION='/user/hive/warehouse/sales\_db.db/sales'} \\
\text{0 rows in PARTITIONS} &\Rightarrow \text{no data loaded yet} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Adding a partition:**

```sql
ALTER TABLE sales ADD PARTITION (year=2024, month=1)
LOCATION '/user/hive/warehouse/sales_db.db/sales/year=2024/month=1';
```

**New metastore state:**

$$
\text{1 row in PARTITIONS} \Rightarrow \text{(SD\_ID, year=2024, month=1, CREATE\_TIME=ts)}
$$

This is precisely how Hive implements partition pruning — at query time, the metastore returns only the `SDS.LOCATION`s of the relevant `PARTITIONS` rows, avoiding a full table scan.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Hive Architecture with the Metastore

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client[Client<br>Beeline or JDBC or Spark]
    hs2[HiveServer2<br>Thrift API on Port 10000]
    driver[Driver<br>Compiler Optimizer Executor]
    mstoreSvc[Metastore Service<br>Thrift on Port 9083]
    mysql[(MySQL Database<br>metastore_db with 57 tables)]
    hdfs[(HDFS<br>user hive warehouse data files)]

    client --> hs2
    hs2 --> driver
    driver --> mstoreSvc
    mstoreSvc --> mysql
    driver --> hdfs
    mstoreSvc -.location pointers.-> hdfs

    classDef svc fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#b30000,color:#000
    classDef store fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    class hs2,driver,mstoreSvc svc
    class mysql,hdfs store
```

## 4.2 Metastore Internal Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cli[Hive CLI or HS2]
    thrift[Thrift API Layer<br>HiveMetastore thrift IDL]
    jdo[DataNucleus JDO<br>ORM Bridge]
    rdbms[(RDBMS<br>MySQL or PostgreSQL or Oracle)]
    tables[Metastore Schema Tables<br>DBS TBLS SDS COLUMNS V2 PARTITIONS SERDES]

    cli --> thrift
    thrift --> jdo
    jdo --> rdbms
    rdbms --> tables

    classDef io fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef orm fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef db fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    class cli,thrift io
    class jdo orm
    class rdbms,tables db
```

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — `CREATE TABLE` Round-Trip

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant CLI as Hive CLI
    participant C as Compiler
    participant MS as Metastore Service
    participant DB as MySQL
    participant H as HDFS

    U->>CLI: CREATE TABLE orders (...)
    CLI->>C: Parse HQL
    C->>MS: create_table(SD, columns, serde)
    MS->>DB: INSERT INTO SDS
    MS->>DB: INSERT INTO TBLS
    MS->>DB: INSERT INTO COLUMNS_V2
    MS->>DB: INSERT INTO SERDES
    DB-->>MS: OK
    MS-->>C: table object returned
    C->>H: mkdir warehouse/orders
    H-->>C: created
    C-->>CLI: OK
    CLI-->>U: OK time taken: 0.5 seconds
```

## 4.4 Metastore Deployment Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Mode1["Mode 1 Embedded Derby"]
        m1c[Hive CLI]
        m1s[Metastore Service in process]
        m1d[(Derby in memory)]
        m1c --> m1s --> m1d
    end

    subgraph Mode2["Mode 2 Local External DB"]
        m2c[Hive CLI]
        m2s[Metastore Service in process]
        m2d[(MySQL over JDBC)]
        m2c --> m2s --> m2d
    end

    subgraph Mode3["Mode 3 Remote Thrift"]
        m3c1[Hive CLI 1]
        m3c2[HiveServer2]
        m3c3[Spark Thrift Server]
        m3ms[Standalone Metastore<br>Thrift on 9083]
        m3d[(MySQL over JDBC)]
        m3c1 --> m3ms
        m3c2 --> m3ms
        m3c3 --> m3ms
        m3ms --> m3d
    end

    classDef prod fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    class m3ms,m3d prod
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiners love asking:** *"Why do we need a remote metastore in production?"* Use the diagram above. Answer: **Mode 3** allows multiple engines (HS2, Spark, Impala) to share one schema repository without conflict.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar]`
**Define Hive Metastore. List any two configuration parameters required to connect Hive to a MySQL database.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Hive Metastore is a central repository service that stores structural metadata — such as databases, tables, columns, partitions, data types, and HDFS locations — for Hive. It is accessible via a Thrift API and is backed by an RDBMS in production deployments. **(2 marks)**

Two essential configuration parameters in `hive-site.xml`:
1. `javax.jdo.option.ConnectionURL` — the JDBC URL of the MySQL database (e.g., `jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/metastore_db`).
2. `javax.jdo.option.ConnectionDriverName` — the JDBC driver class (e.g., `com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver`). **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**Differentiate between Embedded (Derby) and Remote (MySQL) Metastore modes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Embedded (Derby) | Remote (MySQL) |
|---|---|---|
| Concurrent users | Single user only | Multiple users across cluster |
| Storage | In-memory / local FS | RDBMS on dedicated server |
| Sharing across engines | Not possible | Spark, Impala, Presto can share |
| Production suitability | Demos only | Yes |
| **Access** | In-process | Thrift API on port 9083 |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** One question with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Internal choice** between Question A and Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[Adapted from KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the Hive Metastore with a neat diagram. Describe the role of the Thrift API, DataNucleus, and the RDBMS in this architecture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable HQL commands, demonstrate the creation of a **managed table** and an **external table** in Hive. Verify the metadata persistence in the MySQL metastore for both. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture of Hive Metastore (7 marks):**

The Hive Metastore is a logically centralized metadata repository implemented as a three-tier service:

**Tier 1 — Thrift API Layer:** Defines the contract (`HiveMetastore.thrift`) using Apache Thrift's IDL. All operations (`create_table`, `get_table`, `add_partition`, etc.) are exposed as remote procedure calls over TCP port 9083. This allows any client (HiveServer2, Spark, Impala, custom Java app) to interact with the metastore over the wire. **(2 marks)**

**Tier 2 — DataNucleus JDO Layer:** Apache DataNucleus is a Java Persistent API implementation. It abstracts the underlying RDBMS by mapping Java objects (e.g., `Table`, `Partition`) to relational tables (`TBLS`, `PARTITIONS`). This Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) layer lets Hive write to *any* JDBC-compliant DB without changing the metastore code. **(2 marks)**

**Tier 3 — RDBMS (MySQL/PostgreSQL):** Stores ~57 tables. The principal tables are `DBS` (databases), `TBLS` (tables), `SDS` (storage descriptors with HDFS location), `COLUMNS_V2` (column schemas), `PARTITIONS` (partition values), and `SERDES` (serialization libraries). **(2 marks)**

**Diagram (to be drawn on paper):** Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes for the Mermaid rendition. (1 mark)

**(b) Managed vs External Table Demo (7 marks):**

```sql
-- Step 1: Create database
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS ktu_demo
LOCATION '/user/hive/warehouse/ktu_demo.db';

USE ktu_demo;

-- Step 2: Create MANAGED table
CREATE TABLE managed_students (
    roll_no INT,
    name    STRING,
    cgpa    DOUBLE
)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE;

-- Step 3: Create EXTERNAL table pointing to existing HDFS data
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS external_results (
    roll_no INT,
    grade   STRING
)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE
LOCATION '/data/external/results';
```

**MySQL Verification (3 marks for the SQL):**

```sql
USE metastore_db;

-- List all tables with their type
SELECT T.TBL_NAME,
       T.TBL_TYPE,
       D.NAME        AS database_name,
       S.LOCATION,
       S.INPUT_FORMAT
FROM TBLS T
JOIN DBS  D ON T.DB_ID = D.DB_ID
JOIN SDS  S ON T.SD_ID = S.SD_ID
WHERE D.NAME = 'ktu_demo';
```

**Expected output:**

| TBL_NAME | TBL_TYPE | database_name | LOCATION | INPUT_FORMAT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| managed_students | MANAGED_TABLE | ktu_demo | `/user/hive/warehouse/ktu_demo.db/managed_students` | `org.apache.hadoop.mapred.TextInputFormat` |
| external_results | EXTERNAL_TABLE | ktu_demo | `/data/external/results` | `org.apache.hadoop.mapred.TextInputFormat` |

**Key difference (1 mark):**
- `DROP TABLE managed_students` → **removes both metadata (TBLS row) AND data (HDFS files)**.
- `DROP TABLE external_results` → **removes only metadata; HDFS files at `/data/external/results` survive.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Pattern]`

**(a)** What are the three deployment modes of the Hive Metastore? Compare their suitability for a KTU-style single-node lab versus a multi-node production cluster. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the step-by-step procedure to configure a remote MySQL-backed Hive Metastore. Include the `hive-site.xml` configuration and the `schematool` initialization command. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Three Deployment Modes (7 marks):**

1. **Embedded Metastore (Derby)** — The metastore service runs *inside* the Hive CLI JVM, and Derby acts as the in-process database. Suitable only for **single-user demos**. **KTU lab: acceptable for first exposure to HQL, but loses marks if the question asks for "production."** **(2 marks)**

2. **Local Metastore (MySQL in-process)** — The metastore service still runs inside the CLI, but the database is a *separate* MySQL server reachable via JDBC. Allows **multiple Hive CLI sessions on the same machine** to share schemas. **KTU lab: appropriate for demonstrating multi-user concepts.** **(2.5 marks)**

3. **Remote Metastore (Thrift 9083)** — A standalone `hive --service metastore` daemon listens on port 9083. All clients (HS2, Spark, Impala, Presto) connect over Thrift. **Production-grade** because:
   - Single point of schema truth for the entire cluster
   - Survives client disconnects
   - Load-balanced
   - Highly available via Metastore HA **(2.5 marks)**

**(b) Step-by-Step Configuration (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Install MySQL and create the metastore database (1.5 marks):**
```bash
sudo apt-get install -y mysql-server
sudo systemctl start mysql
sudo mysql -u root -p
CREATE DATABASE metastore_db;
CREATE USER 'hiveuser'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'hivepassword';
GRANT ALL ON metastore_db.* TO 'hiveuser'@'localhost';
FLUSH PRIVILEGES; EXIT;
```

**Step 2 — Place the MySQL JDBC driver in `$HIVE_HOME/lib` (0.5 mark):**
```bash
wget https://dev.mysql.com/get/Downloads/Connector-J/mysql-connector-java-8.0.33.tar.gz
cp mysql-connector-java-8.0.33.jar $HIVE_HOME/lib/
```

**Step 3 — Edit `hive-site.xml` (2.5 marks):**
```xml
<property>
  <name>javax.jdo.option.ConnectionURL</name>
  <value>jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/metastore_db</value>
</property>
<property>
  <name>javax.jdo.option.ConnectionDriverName</name>
  <value>com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver</value>
</property>
<property>
  <name>javax.jdo.option.ConnectionUserName</name>
  <value>hiveuser</value>
</property>
<property>
  <name>javax.jdo.option.ConnectionPassword</name>
  <value>hivepassword</value>
</property>
<property>
  <name>hive.metastore.warehouse.dir</name>
  <value>/user/hive/warehouse</value>
</property>
<property>
  <name>hive.metastore.uris</name>
  <value>thrift://localhost:9083</value>
</property>
```

**Step 4 — Initialize the schema (1.5 marks):**
```bash
$HIVE_HOME/bin/schematool -dbType mysql -initSchema
```

**Step 5 — Start the metastore service in the background (1 mark):**
```bash
nohup $HIVE_HOME/bin/hive --service metastore > /tmp/metastore.log 2>&1 &
netstat -tnlp | grep 9083   # verify
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the JDBC jar in `$HIVE_HOME/lib`** → metastore refuses to start with `ClassNotFoundException`. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing `hive.metastore.uris` with the Hiveserver2 URL (port 10000)** → these are *different* services on *different* ports. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Initializing the schema twice** (running `-initSchema` on a populated DB) → `MetaException: Version stamp not compatible`. Use `-upgradeSchema` instead. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Not creating the warehouse directory on HDFS** (`hdfs dfs -mkdir -p /user/hive/warehouse`) → tables can be created in the metastore but queries fail at execution. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Treating MANAGED and EXTERNAL tables as identical in the answer** → external table's LOCATION clause is *mandatory*; managed table's is optional. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **Hive Metastore** stores **schema metadata** (not data) in a relational database, accessed via a **Thrift API** on **port 9083**.
- ✅ **Three deployment modes**: Embedded (Derby), Local (external DB, in-process service), **Remote** (separate Thrift daemon — production standard).
- ✅ The metastore is implemented as a stack: **Thrift IDL → Java interface (IMetaStoreClient) → DataNucleus JDO → RDBMS**.
- ✅ Production RDBMS = **MySQL or PostgreSQL**; never Derby.
- ✅ Mandatory `hive-site.xml` properties: `ConnectionURL`, `ConnectionDriverName`, `ConnectionUserName`, `ConnectionPassword`, `hive.metastore.warehouse.dir`, `hive.metastore.uris`.
- ✅ Initialization: `$HIVE_HOME/bin/schematool -dbType mysql -initSchema` — creates **~57 tables** in the metastore DB.
- ✅ Key metastore tables: **`DBS`**, **`TBLS`**, **`SDS`**, **`COLUMNS_V2`**, **`PARTITIONS`**, **`PARTITION_KEYS`**, **`SERDES`**, **`TABLE_PARAMS`**.
- ✅ The **`SDS.LOCATION`** field is the *bridge* between the metastore schema and the actual HDFS files.
- ✅ **Managed table** drop → metadata AND data deleted. **External table** drop → only metadata deleted; HDFS files preserved.
- ✅ The **MySQL JDBC driver jar must be in `$HIVE_HOME/lib`** before starting the metastore.
- ✅ Default warehouse path: `/user/hive/warehouse` — must exist on HDFS before creating managed tables.
- ✅ Partitioning: `PARTITION_KEYS` defines the partition columns; `PARTITIONS` holds one row per actual partition with its HDFS sub-path.
- ✅ Schema versioning matters: use `-initSchema` for first-time setup, `-upgradeSchema` for migrating an existing metastore to a newer Hive version.
- ✅ Modern extensions (AWS Glue Catalog, Apache Iceberg, Apache Polaris, Unity Catalog) all build **on top of** the Hive Metastore API.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison with Traditional Databases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Comparison with Traditional Databases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Traditional Database (RDBMS):** A **Relational Database Management System** is a vertically-scaled, schema-rigid, ACID-compliant data store that organises information into pre-defined tables (relations) composed of rows (tuples) and columns (attributes). It uses **Structured Query Language (SQL)** and assumes data fits within the storage and compute capacity of a single, powerful server.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big Data System:** A horizontally-scaled, schema-flexible, distributed data processing architecture (e.g., **Hadoop, HDFS, NoSQL stores**) that stores and computes over **Volume, Velocity, and Variety** of data across clusters of commodity machines. It prioritises **partition tolerance** and **fault tolerance** over strict consistency.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this comparison is treated as the **conceptual foundation** for understanding *why* ecosystems such as Hadoop, HBase, Cassandra, and MongoDB exist alongside Oracle, MySQL, and PostgreSQL.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of a **Traditional RDBMS as a Single-Story Library** with one giant, perfectly-organised catalogue. Every book is catalogued by author, ISBN, and genre *before* it enters the building. A librarian can answer any query in seconds, but the moment the library needs to hold 10 million new books, the building must be **torn down and rebuilt larger** (vertical scaling).

A **Big Data System is a Network of Branch Libraries** spread across a city. Books are not pre-catalogued rigidly — they are simply stored in the branch where shelf space exists. If one branch catches fire, copies in other branches survive. Adding a new branch is as simple as leasing a new room (horizontal scaling), and the catalogue is updated *eventually*, not instantly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Trade-off:** RDBMS says *"Give me correctness first; we will figure out scale later."* Big Data says *"Give me scale first; we will reconcile correctness later."*

## 1.3 The "3V" Anchor of Big Data

| Dimension | Meaning | Traditional DB Limit | Big Data Capability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Terabytes $\to$ Petabytes $\to$ Exabytes | Tens of TB (single chassis) | **EB-scale** clusters |
| **Velocity** | Streaming, real-time ingestion | Batch + slow OLTP | **Stream processing** (Kafka, Spark Streaming) |
| **Variety** | Structured, semi-structured, unstructured | Structured only | **All formats** (JSON, XML, images, logs) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Difference

### 2.1.1 ACID vs BASE

Traditional RDBMSs adhere to the **ACID** transaction model. Big Data systems relax this to **BASE** for the sake of distributed scale.

**ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability):**
- **Atomicity** — A transaction is "all-or-nothing."
- **Consistency** — The database moves from one valid state to another valid state.
- **Isolation** — Concurrent transactions do not interfere.
- **Durability** — Once committed, data survives crashes.

**BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency):**
- **Basically Available** — The system always responds (possibly with stale data).
- **Soft state** — State may change over time, even without input.
- **Eventual consistency** — Given enough time, all replicas converge to the same value.

### 2.1.2 CAP Theorem (Brewer's Theorem)

Formally stated by **Eric Brewer (2000)**, the CAP theorem proves that in any distributed data store, you can guarantee **at most two** of the following three properties simultaneously:

> **C** — **Consistency** (every read returns the latest write)
> **A** — **Availability** (every request receives a non-error response)
> **P** — **Partition Tolerance** (the system operates despite network failures)

A formal algebraic form of CAP is:

$$
\text{For any distributed system } S: \quad \vert C(S) \cap A(S) \cap P(S) \vert \leq 2
$$

In practice, because **network partitions are inevitable** in distributed systems, the choice collapses to **CP** (e.g., HBase, MongoDB in default mode) or **AP** (e.g., Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB). Traditional RDBMS, when distributed, behave as **CA** only when partitions are assumed away.

## 2.2 Why and How — The Reasoning Chain

- **Why does RDBMS struggle with Volume?** Because data is stored on a *single machine's* disk array, and disk seek time is bounded by physics (~5 ms for HDD, ~0.1 ms for SSD).
- **Why does Big Data scale horizontally?** Because it partitions data across many nodes, and **throughput scales linearly** with node count.
- **How does it achieve this?** Through **data sharding** (splitting), **replication** (copying), and **distributed consensus** (leader election via Paxos / Raft / ZAB).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Storage Scaling | $1 \text{ PB} = 2^{10} \text{ TB} = 2^{20} \text{ GB}$ | Bytes | $1 \text{ EB} = 1024 \text{ PB}$ |
| 2 | CAP Cardinality | $\max(\vert C \cap A \cap P \vert) = 2$ | Boolean | Partition tolerance is mandatory in distributed |
| 3 | Replication Factor | $R = \frac{\text{Total copies stored}}{\text{Unique data blocks}}$ | Integer | $R \geq 3$ is industry standard |
| 4 | Shard Count | $N_{\text{shards}} = \left\lceil \frac{D_{\text{total}}}{D_{\text{shard}}} \right\rceil$ | Integer | $D$ = data size |
| 5 | Effective Storage | $S_{\text{eff}} = \frac{D_{\text{logical}} \times R}{C_{\text{overhead}}}$ | Bytes | $C_{\text{overhead}} \approx 1.3$ for HDFS |
| 6 | Throughput Scaling | $T_{\text{cluster}} \approx N_{\text{nodes}} \times T_{\text{node}}$ | ops/sec | Assumes balanced partition |
| 7 | Latency Bound (Sharded Read) | $L_{\text{agg}} = \max_{i=1}^{N}(L_i) + L_{\text{merge}}$ | seconds | Aggregator waits for slowest shard |
| 8 | Availability (9s) | $A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}} \times 100\%$ | Percent | "Five 9s" = 99.999% |
| 9 | Linear Scale Ideal | $T_{\text{measured}} = \alpha \times T_{\text{ideal}}, \quad 0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$ | Scalar | $\alpha \approx 0.7$ is realistic for Hadoop |
| 10 | Cost per TB | $\text{CPT} = \frac{\text{Hardware cost} + \text{Power} + \text{Ops}}{\text{Usable TB}}$ | $\text{INR/USD per TB}$ | Big Data uses commodity hardware |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking core ledger** — RDBMS (ACID mandatory).
- **Recommendation engines (Netflix, Amazon)** — Big Data / NoSQL (scale + speed).
- **IoT sensor telemetry** — Big Data (velocity + volume).
- **Genomic data warehouses** — Big Data (variety + volume).
- **Fraud detection streams** — Hybrid: OLTP in RDBMS, real-time scoring in Spark.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Horizontal Scaling Outperforms Vertical Scaling

**Premise:** A single high-end RDBMS server can handle $T_0$ transactions per second at a hardware cost $H_0$. Each doubling of capacity roughly **quadruples** the hardware cost (memory bandwidth, CPU socket licensing, RAID controller).

We model vertical scaling as:

$$
C_{\text{vertical}}(n) = H_0 \times 4^{\log_2 n}
$$

where $n$ is the scale multiplier. For $n = 8$ (8× throughput):

$$
C_{\text{vertical}}(8) = H_0 \times 4^{\log_2 8} = H_0 \times 4^{3} = 64 \, H_0
$$

So **8× throughput** costs **64× hardware**.

In contrast, horizontal scaling (adding commodity nodes) costs:

$$
C_{\text{horizontal}}(n) = n \times H_{\text{commodity}}
$$

For $n = 8$ nodes at $H_{\text{commodity}} = 0.1 \, H_0$:

$$
C_{\text{horizontal}}(8) = 8 \times 0.1 \, H_0 = 0.8 \, H_0
$$

**Conclusion:** Same 8× throughput at $\frac{64 \, H_0}{0.8 \, H_0} = 80\times$ lower cost. This is the engineering rationale behind the Hadoop / commodity-cluster paradigm.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Replication and Storage Overhead

HDFS by default stores **3 replicas** of every 64 MB block (in modern Hadoop 3.x, default is 128 MB blocks, still $R = 3$).

**Step 1:** Logical data size.

$$
D_{\text{logical}} = 5 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply replication factor $R = 3$.

$$
D_{\text{physical}} = D_{\text{logical}} \times R = 5 \times 3 = 15 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 3:** Account for HDFS metadata + filesystem overhead (typically 20–30%).

$$
C_{\text{overhead}} = 1.25
$$

$$
S_{\text{eff}} = \frac{15 \text{ TB}}{1.25} = 12 \text{ TB of unique raw storage}
$$

Wait — this is the *unique raw capacity* the cluster must hold. **You need 15 TB of raw disk to logically store 5 TB.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shard Count for a 100 TB Dataset

A 100 TB dataset must be split into shards of 64 MB each for HDFS storage planning purposes.

**Step 1:** Convert units.

$$
100 \text{ TB} = 100 \times 2^{10} \times 64 \text{ MB blocks}
$$

**Step 2:** Total number of blocks.

$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{100 \times 1024 \times 1024 \text{ MB}}{64 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{104857600}{64} \right\rceil = 1638400 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3:** If cluster has 10 datanodes, blocks per datanode (balanced).

$$
N_{\text{per\_node}} = \frac{1638400}{10} = 163840 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 4:** Effective physical storage on each datanode.

$$
S_{\text{node}} = 163840 \times 64 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 30 \text{ TB per node}
$$

This is exactly why big data clusters require **redundant commodity hardware** at scale.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Traditional RDBMS | Big Data System (Hadoop / NoSQL) |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Model** | Relational (tables) | Key-Value, Document, Column, Graph |
| **Schema** | Rigid, schema-on-write | Flexible, schema-on-read |
| **Scaling** | **Vertical** (scale-up) | **Horizontal** (scale-out) |
| **Hardware** | High-end servers | Commodity clusters |
| **Data Volume** | GB to low TB | **PB to EB** |
| **Throughput Pattern** | Many small read/writes | **Batch + stream**, high throughput scans |
| **Latency** | Sub-millisecond (OLTP) | Milliseconds to seconds |
| **Transaction Model** | **ACID** | **BASE** (eventual consistency) |
| **CAP Position** | CA (single-node) | **CP or AP** |
| **Query Language** | SQL | HQL, CQL, MongoQL, Spark SQL |
| **Fault Tolerance** | RAID, backups | **Replicas, rack-awareness, NameNode HA** |
| **Cost per TB** | **High** | **Low** |
| **Indexing** | B+ Trees, Hash | LSM Trees, Bloom filters |
| **Data Locality** | Compute pulls data | **Code moves to data** |
| **Use Case** | OLTP, financial ledgers | Log analytics, ML, IoT |
| **Example Products** | Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, MS SQL | HDFS, HBase, Cassandra, MongoDB, Hive |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture Comparison Flow

The diagram below maps the **logical data flow** of a Traditional RDBMS versus a Hadoop/Big Data cluster, highlighting where the architectural philosophies diverge.

```mermaid
graph LR
    clientA[Client Application] --> sqlA[SQL Engine and Query Optimizer]
    sqlA --> rdbmsA[Centralised RDBMS Engine]
    rdbmsA --> storageA[Single Storage Array]
    rdbmsA --> logA[Write Ahead Log and Redo Logs]

    clientB[Client Application] --> ingB[Ingestion Layer: Sqoop or Kafka]
    ingB --> distB[Distributed Processing Framework: MapReduce or Spark]
    distB --> node1[DataNode 1]
    distB --> node2[DataNode 2]
    distB --> node3[DataNode 3]
    distB --> nodeN[DataNode N]
    node1 --> hdfsB[HDFS Cluster: Replicated Blocks]
    node2 --> hdfsB
    node3 --> hdfsB
    nodeN --> hdfsB
    hdfsB --> nn[NameNode: Metadata and Coordination]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left lane** (Traditional) shows a *vertical pipeline* with a single choke point — the storage array.
- The **right lane** (Big Data) shows a *fan-out* of compute that meets the data where it lives.
- The `NameNode` in HDFS does **not** store data; it stores *metadata* (block locations, namespace tree) — analogous to the RDBMS catalog, but distributed.

---

## 4.2 CAP Theorem Trade-off Space

```mermaid
graph TD
    capA[Traditional RDBMS in Cluster Mode] -->|CA: avoid partitions| capA
    capB[HBase, MongoDB Default] -->|CP: consistency over availability| capC
    capD[Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB] -->|AP: availability over consistency| capE
    capF[Single Node RDBMS] -->|CA with no partition| capG
    capH[Bigtable, ZooKeeper] -->|CP| capC
    capI[Riak, Voldemort] -->|AP| capE
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme, when answering CAP-related questions, always **state the assumption that partitions are non-negotiable** in real distributed systems, and frame the trade-off as a **CP vs AP** choice.

---

## 4.3 Data Locality Principle

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph traditional[Traditional RDBMS Layer]
        t1[Application Server] -->|pulls data over network| t2[Database Server]
        t2 --> t3[Disk Array]
    end

    subgraph bigdata[Big Data Layer]
        b1[Task Tracker Node 1] -->|reads local disk| b2[Local HDFS Block]
        b3[Task Tracker Node 2] -->|reads local disk| b4[Local HDFS Block]
        b5[Task Tracker Node 3] -->|reads local disk| b6[Local HDFS Block]
    end
```

**Key insight:** In the Traditional model, *data moves to compute*. In the Big Data model, *compute moves to data*. This single inversion is what enables petabyte-scale analytics on commodity hardware.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Differentiate between **schema-on-write** and **schema-on-read**. Which approach does an RDBMS follow, and which does Hadoop follow? Justify.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Schema-on-write** (RDBMS): Schema is *defined and enforced* before any data is loaded. Bad rows are rejected at insertion time. *(1 mark)*
- **Schema-on-read** (Hadoop): Raw data is dumped to HDFS; the schema is applied *at query time* via Hive, Spark, or Pig. *(1 mark)*
- **Justification:** RDBMS optimises for fast OLTP queries on clean, structured data; Hadoop optimises for ingesting *variety* of data quickly, deferring cleansing to query time. *(1 mark)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> State the **CAP theorem**. Why is **Partition Tolerance (P)** considered non-negotiable in distributed systems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **CAP Theorem** (Brewer, 2000): A distributed data store can simultaneously guarantee **at most two** of the three properties — **Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance**. *(1 mark)*
- During a **network partition**, nodes cannot communicate; the system designer must choose between returning potentially-stale data (AP) or refusing the request (CP). *(1 mark)*
- **P is non-negotiable** because in any real distributed cluster spanning racks, data centres, or geographies, network failures are *inevitable* — you cannot "not have" them. Hence the real choice is **CP vs AP**. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### 5.2.1 Question A — 14 Marks *(Internal Choice 1)*

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **ACID properties** with a suitable banking example. Justify why these properties are difficult to guarantee in a distributed Big Data environment. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **HDFS architecture**. Compare its fault-tolerance mechanism with that of a **Traditional RDBMS**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ACID Properties — Banking Example (7 marks):**

- **Atomicity** *(1.5 marks):* A transfer of ₹5,000 from Account A to Account B must either complete *fully* (debit A + credit B) or not happen at all. If the system crashes after debiting A but before crediting B, atomicity rolls back the debit. *RDBMS tool:* Transaction logs / undo logs.
- **Consistency** *(1.5 marks):* The total balance of the bank (sum of all accounts) must remain invariant. After the transfer, total = pre-transfer total. Foreign keys, check constraints enforce this.
- **Isolation** *(1.5 marks):* Two concurrent transfers from Account A must not interleave their debits (no double-spend). Achieved via locks, MVCC.
- **Durability** *(1.5 marks):* Once the transfer is committed, it survives power loss, crashes, and disasters. *RDBMS tool:* Write-Ahead Logging + fsync to disk.
- **Why ACID is hard in Big Data** *(1 mark):* Guaranteeing these across geographically distributed nodes requires **distributed locking** and **two-phase commit (2PC)**, which kills throughput. Hence Big Data relaxes to BASE.

**(b) HDFS Architecture and Fault Tolerance (7 marks):**

**Diagram description (text-based — full diagram in SECTION 4.1):**

The HDFS follows a **Master-Slave (NameNode-DataNode)** architecture.

- **NameNode** *(1 mark)* — Maintains metadata: namespace tree, block-to-DataNode mapping, replication factor.
- **DataNodes** *(1 mark)* — Store actual data blocks (default 128 MB), send heartbeats to NameNode every 3 seconds.
- **Secondary NameNode** *(0.5 mark)* — Periodically merges the edit log with the fsimage to prevent the edit log from growing unboundedly.

**Fault Tolerance — HDFS (2 marks):**
- Default replication factor $R = 3$.
- **Rack-aware placement**: one replica on a node in the local rack, one on a different node in the same rack, one on a node in a different rack.
- If a DataNode fails, the NameNode detects (missing heartbeat) and re-replicates the lost blocks to maintain $R = 3$.

**Fault Tolerance — Traditional RDBMS (1.5 marks):**
- **RAID** (RAID 1, 5, 6, 10) at the storage layer.
- **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)** + **checkpointing**.
- **Hot-standby replication** (primary $\to$ secondary).
- **Backup tapes** for disaster recovery.

**Comparison (1 mark):**
- RDBMS fault tolerance is **vertical and hardware-driven** (RAID, ECC RAM).
- HDFS fault tolerance is **horizontal and software-driven** (replication across commodity nodes, rack awareness).

---

### 5.2.2 Question B — 14 Marks *(Internal Choice 2)*

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Compare and contrast **Traditional RDBMS** and **Hadoop-based Big Data systems** along **at least eight** parameters. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the **concept of sharding and replication** in Big Data. A 20 TB dataset is to be stored on a cluster with a block size of 128 MB and replication factor 3. Calculate: *(i)* number of blocks, *(ii)* total physical storage consumed, *(iii)* number of datanodes required if each datanode can hold 2 TB of unique data. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) RDBMS vs Hadoop — 8 Parameters (7 marks):**

| # | Parameter | Traditional RDBMS | Hadoop / Big Data |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Data type | Structured | Structured, semi, unstructured |
| 2 | Schema | Schema-on-write | Schema-on-read |
| 3 | Scaling | Vertical | Horizontal |
| 4 | Transactions | ACID | BASE |
| 5 | Hardware cost | High (proprietary) | Low (commodity) |
| 6 | Data volume | GB – TB | **PB – EB** |
| 7 | Processing | SQL (real-time) | MapReduce, Spark (batch + near-real-time) |
| 8 | Fault tolerance | RAID, backups | **Replication, rack awareness** |

*(1 mark for the table being complete; 6 marks for crisp, accurate explanations of any 4 parameters with 1.5 marks each — student should pick the most relevant.)*

---

**(b) Sharding, Replication, and Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

- **Sharding** is the horizontal partitioning of data into independent blocks stored across different nodes. *(1 mark)*
- **Replication** is the maintenance of multiple copies of each block on different nodes for fault tolerance. *(1 mark)*

**Numerical Calculation:**

**(i) Number of blocks** *(2 marks)*:

$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = \frac{20 \text{ TB}}{128 \text{ MB}}
$$

Convert: $20 \text{ TB} = 20 \times 1024 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 20971520 \text{ MB}$

$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{20971520}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 163840 \right\rceil = 163840 \text{ blocks}
$$

**(ii) Total physical storage with $R = 3$** *(2 marks)*:

$$
S_{\text{physical}} = N_{\text{blocks}} \times \text{Block size} \times R
$$

$$
S_{\text{physical}} = 163840 \times 128 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 62914560 \text{ MB} = 60 \text{ TB}
$$

**(iii) Number of datanodes required** *(1 mark)*:

Each datanode holds 2 TB of *unique* data. Logical unique data = 20 TB.

$$
N_{\text{datanodes}} = \left\lceil \frac{20 \text{ TB}}{2 \text{ TB}} \right\rceil = 10 \text{ datanodes}
$$

**Verification** *(0.5 mark)*: With 10 datanodes and 60 TB physical storage, each datanode holds 6 TB physical $\Rightarrow$ matches the 2 TB unique × 3 replicas requirement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Mixing TB (decimal, $10^{12}$) with TiB (binary, $2^{40}$). KTU accepts $1 \text{ TB} = 1024 \text{ GB}$ unless explicitly stated.
> 2. **Forgetting the ceiling function** $\lceil \, \cdot \, \rceil$ when computing blocks. A partial block still requires a full block.
> 3. **Confusing logical with physical storage** — the question says "20 TB dataset"; you must apply $R = 3$ to get 60 TB *physical*.
> 4. **CAP Theorem framing** — Never claim RDBMS is "fully CAP-compliant." It is **CA only when not distributed**.
> 5. **Schema terminology** — Do not write "Hadoop has no schema." It has *flexible / late-binding* schema, which is different.
> 6. **Forgetting the rack-awareness detail** — Examiners reward the student who mentions *one local + one same-rack + one different-rack* in replication questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Traditional RDBMS** = Schema-on-write, ACID, SQL, vertical scaling, structured data, GB–TB scale.
- [x] **Big Data (Hadoop / NoSQL)** = Schema-on-read, BASE, horizontal scaling, all data types, PB–EB scale.
- [x] **ACID** = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability.
- [x] **BASE** = Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency.
- [x] **CAP Theorem** = $\max(\vert C \cap A \cap P \vert) = 2$. In practice, **CP vs AP**.
- [x] **3Vs of Big Data** = Volume, Velocity, Variety (some texts add Veracity, Value).
- [x] **HDFS Architecture** = NameNode (master) + DataNodes (slaves) + Secondary NameNode.
- [x] **Default block size** = 128 MB (Hadoop 2.x onwards); replication factor = 3.
- [x] **Rack-aware replication** = Local node + same-rack node + different-rack node.
- [x] **Data Locality** = Compute moves to data, not the reverse.
- [x] **Cost ratio** = Horizontal scaling is ~80× cheaper than vertical for 8× throughput.
- [x] **Storage formula** = $S_{\text{physical}} = D_{\text{logical}} \times R$.
- [x] **Block count** = $N_{\text{blocks}} = \lceil D_{\text{logical}} / \text{Block Size} \rceil$.
- [x] **Real-world use** = RDBMS for OLTP/finance; Big Data for analytics, IoT, ML, search, social.
- [x] **Key NoSQL categories** = Key-Value (Redis), Document (MongoDB), Columnar (HBase, Cassandra), Graph (Neo4j).
- [x] **Consensus protocols** = Paxos, Raft, ZAB (for leader election and replication ordering).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HiveQL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** HiveQL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HiveQL (Hive Query Language)** is the SQL-like declarative query language used in Apache Hive to perform data definition, data manipulation, and query operations on data stored in the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS). HiveQL abstracts the complexity of writing low-level MapReduce Java code by automatically translating high-level SQL-like statements into optimized MapReduce, Tez, or Spark execution jobs.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for **Big Data Analytics (PBADT504)**, HiveQL is positioned as a critical **data warehousing and SQL-on-Hadoop** tool that bridges the gap between traditional relational database analysts and the Big Data Hadoop ecosystem. It supports a rich set of features including:

- **DDL statements** — `CREATE`, `DROP`, `ALTER`, `TRUNCATE`, `SHOW`, `DESCRIBE`
- **DML statements** — `LOAD`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`, `MERGE`
- **Built-in operators** — Relational (`=`, `<>`, `<`, `>`), Arithmetic (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`), Logical (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`)
- **Built-in functions** — Aggregate (`COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MAX`, `MIN`), Date (`from_unixtime`, `unix_timestamp`, `to_date`), String (`concat`, `substr`, `upper`, `lower`, `trim`, `length`), Math (`round`, `floor`, `ceil`, `abs`, `rand`)
- **Hive-specific constructs** — Partitioned Tables, Bucketed Tables, Views, Indexes, Joins (Inner, Left/Right Outer, Full Outer, Cross), Sub-queries, `GROUP BY`, `ORDER BY`, `SORT BY`, `DISTRIBUTE BY`, `CLUSTER BY`, `UNION ALL`, and User-Defined Functions (UDFs/UDAFs/UDTFs)

> [!NOTE]
> **Heterogeneous Execution Backends** — A single HiveQL statement is compiled by the Hive service into an **Execution Engine** which can be either **MapReduce**, **Apache Tez**, or **Apache Spark**. By default in modern installations, Tez is preferred for low-latency Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) execution.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you run a massive library with **billions of books** stored across thousands of warehouses. You do not want your librarians to manually walk between warehouses to count books, sort them, or fetch specific titles. Instead, you want them to just say, in plain English, *"Give me the total count of science books published after 2015, grouped by author"*.

A **librarian assistant (HiveQL)** accepts your English-like request, converts it into a step-by-step plan (a **MapReduce / Tez job**), dispatches the plan to many warehouse workers (the **Hadoop cluster**), and finally presents the consolidated answer to you.

**The Mapping:**

| Real-World Analogy | HiveQL / Hadoop Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Library | **Data Warehouse** stored on HDFS |
| English query | **HiveQL statement** |
| Step-by-step plan | **MapReduce / Tez / Spark execution plan** |
| Librarians | **Hive CLI / Beeline / Hue** |
| Warehouse workers | **Hadoop DataNodes** (parallelism) |
| Metastore of book locations | **Hive Metastore** (stores schema in RDBMS) |

> [!TIP]
> The **Hive Metastore** is a separate relational database (often MySQL, PostgreSQL, or Derby) that persists **table schemas**, **partition metadata**, **column types**, and **file locations**. Without the Metastore, Hive cannot locate any table — making it the "**heart of Hive**".

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Defaults

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Default Field Delimiter:** `\001` (the ASCII Start-of-Heading control character, also written as `^A`)
> - **Default Record Delimiter:** Newline (`\n`)
> - **Default Metastore DB:** Apache Derby (embedded); MySQL/PostgreSQL in production
> - **Default File Format (Hive 2.x+):** **ORC (Optimized Row Columnar)** for storage, **LazySimpleSerDe** for text
> - **Default Execution Engine:** Apache Tez (Hive 2.x+)
> - **Warehouse Directory:** `/user/hive/warehouse/`
> - **Database Directory:** `/user/hive/warehouse/<database_name>.db/`
> - **External Table Marker:** `EXTERNAL` keyword — controls whether Hive owns the data lifecycle or not

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HiveQL Architecture Flow

The journey of a HiveQL query from the user to disk can be decomposed into **five distinct stages**. Understanding this pipeline is essential for KTU questions on Hive's internal workings.

**Stage 1 — User Submission**
The user types a HiveQL query via **Beeline** (JDBC CLI), **Hive CLI** (deprecated), **JDBC/ODBC client**, or **Web UI (Hue)**.

**Stage 2 — Driver & Compiler**
The **Driver** receives the query, creates a `QueryBlock`, builds an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**, and the **Compiler** converts the AST into a logical **QueryPlan**. Type-checking and semantic analysis happen here.

**Stage 3 — Optimizer**
The **Optimizer** performs **predicate push-down**, **partition pruning**, **join reordering**, and **aggregation merging** to minimize I/O and shuffle.

**Stage 4 — Execution Engine**
The logical plan is converted into a **physical plan** (a DAG of tasks for Tez, or Map/Reduce jobs for MR). The **ExecutionEngine** dispatches these tasks to the cluster via YARN.

**Stage 5 — Result**
The driver fetches results from the reducers and streams them back to the client (in **FetchTask** mode) or writes to HDFS.

## 2.2 Hive Table Types — The Central Distinction

KTU frequently asks students to differentiate between **Managed** and **External** tables. This is the single highest-weightage conceptual question in this module.

| Property | **Managed (Internal) Table** | **External Table** |
|---|---|---|
| Creation keyword | `CREATE TABLE ...` (default) | `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE ...` |
| Data ownership | **Hive owns the data** | **Hive does NOT own the data** |
| `DROP TABLE` behaviour | Drops both **schema and data** | Drops **schema only**; data is preserved on HDFS |
| Storage location | `/user/hive/warehouse/<db>.db/<tbl>` | Any user-defined `LOCATION '/path'` |
| Metastore entry | Yes | Yes |
| Use case | Temporary ETL tables, transformation zones | Shared datasets used by multiple tools (Pig, Spark, Hive) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Exam Pitfall:** When asked *"What happens when you DROP a managed vs external table?"*, students often write "data is deleted in both cases" — **this loses full marks**. Always state explicitly that **managed = schema + data deleted**, while **external = only schema deleted**.

## 2.3 Partitioning vs Bucketing — The KTU High-Yield Table

> [!NOTE]
> Both Partitioning and Bucketing are Hive's strategies to enable **data skipping** and **reducer-side optimization**. They are NOT mutually exclusive — a table can be **both partitioned AND bucketed**.

| Feature | **Partitioning** | **Bucketing** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Divides table into **sub-directories** based on column values | Divides data within a partition into **fixed number of equal-sized files** using a hash function |
| Cardinality requirement | **Low to moderate** (e.g., date, country, department) | **High** (e.g., user_id, transaction_id) |
| Number of partitions | Dynamic, grows with data | **Fixed** at table creation |
| Implementation keyword | `PARTITIONED BY (col TYPE)` | `CLUSTERED BY (col) INTO n BUCKETS` |
| Set property | — | `SET hive.enforce.bucketing = true;` |
| Query optimization | **Partition pruning** (`WHERE partition_col = ...`) | **Bucket pruning / Map-side join** |
| Physical representation | HDFS sub-directory per distinct value | Files named like `000000_0`, `000001_0` |
| Number of files per partition | N (one per distinct value) | Exactly the bucket count |

## 2.4 HiveQL DDL Cheat Sheet

| Statement | Syntax Example | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Database create | `CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS dbname;` | Create a logical namespace |
| Database drop | `DROP DATABASE IF EXISTS dbname CASCADE;` | Remove database (and its tables) |
| Internal table | `CREATE TABLE t (id INT, name STRING);` | Hive-owned table |
| External table | `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE t (id INT) LOCATION '/data/t';` | Hive references external data |
| Partitioned table | `CREATE TABLE t (id INT) PARTITIONED BY (dt STRING);` | Add partitioning |
| Bucketed table | `CREATE TABLE t (id INT) CLUSTERED BY (id) INTO 4 BUCKETS;` | Add bucketing |
| Row format | `ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','` | Specify field separator |
| Stored as | `STORED AS TEXTFILE / ORC / PARQUET / RCFILE` | Specify file format |
| Load data (in-path) | `LOAD DATA INPATH '/input/file' INTO TABLE t;` | **MOVES** file into warehouse |
| Load data (local) | `LOAD DATA LOCAL INPATH '/local/file' INTO TABLE t;` | **COPIES** local file to warehouse |
| Insert overwrite | `INSERT OVERWRITE TABLE t SELECT ...;` | Overwrites table data |
| Insert into | `INSERT INTO TABLE t SELECT ...;` | Appends to table data |
| Show partitions | `SHOW PARTITIONS t;` | List partition column values |
| Add partition | `ALTER TABLE t ADD PARTITION (dt='2024-01-01');` | Register a new partition manually |
| Drop partition | `ALTER TABLE t DROP PARTITION (dt='2024-01-01');` | Remove a partition |
| View | `CREATE VIEW v AS SELECT ...;` | Logical query alias (read-only) |
| Describe | `DESCRIBE FORMATTED t;` | Show full metadata |

## 2.5 Built-in Functions Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often asks: *"Name and explain the use of any five aggregate functions in Hive."* Memorize this table.

| Function | Type | Returns | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `COUNT(*)` | Aggregate | Total rows | `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM emp;` |
| `COUNT(DISTINCT col)` | Aggregate | Unique values count | `SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT dept) FROM emp;` |
| `SUM(col)` | Aggregate | Sum of values | `SELECT SUM(salary) FROM emp;` |
| `AVG(col)` | Aggregate | Mean of values | `SELECT AVG(salary) FROM emp;` |
| `MIN(col)` | Aggregate | Smallest value | `SELECT MIN(salary) FROM emp;` |
| `MAX(col)` | Aggregate | Largest value | `SELECT MAX(salary) FROM emp;` |
| `concat(a, b)` | String | Concatenated string | `SELECT concat(first, last);` |
| `substr(s, i, l)` | String | Substring | `SELECT substr(name, 1, 3);` |
| `upper(s)` / `lower(s)` | String | Case conversion | `SELECT upper(name);` |
| `length(s)` | String | Length | `SELECT length(name);` |
| `year(d)` / `month(d)` | Date | Component | `SELECT year(hire_date);` |
| `from_unixtime(ts)` | Date | Human-readable date | `SELECT from_unixtime(1700000000);` |
| `round(n, d)` | Math | Rounded value | `SELECT round(3.14159, 2);` |
| `ceil(n)` / `floor(n)` | Math | Ceiling / floor | `SELECT ceil(2.3);` |
| `rand()` | Math | Random number [0,1) | `SELECT rand();` |
| `nvl(x, def)` | Conditional | Null replacement | `SELECT nvl(bonus, 0);` |
| `CASE WHEN ... THEN ... END` | Conditional | Conditional value | Used in `SELECT` clauses |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — End-to-End HiveQL Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **complete KTU lab-viva-style walkthrough**. Beeline commands and HDFS output are modeled in the same format expected in the **Practical Record Book**.

### Step 1 — Start the Hadoop and Hive Services

The student must ensure the daemons are running before any HiveQL execution. The standard command sequence (typed in terminal) is:

```bash
start-dfs.sh
start-yarn.sh
hive --service metastore &
beeline -u jdbc:hive2://localhost:10000
```

**Conversion Logic:** The `start-dfs.sh` script launches the **NameNode** and **DataNode** daemons. `start-yarn.sh` launches the **ResourceManager** and **NodeManager**. `beeline` is the modern CLI that connects to the **HiveServer2** on port **10000** using JDBC.

### Step 2 — Create a Database and a Table

The student types in Beeline:

```sql
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS student_db;
USE student_db;
CREATE TABLE student (
    id INT,
    name STRING,
    department STRING,
    marks INT
)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED
FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE;
```

**Conversion Logic:** Each clause maps to a specific storage artifact:

| Clause | Effect on HDFS / Metastore |
|---|---|
| `CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS student_db` | Creates directory `/user/hive/warehouse/student_db.db/` |
| `USE student_db` | Sets the current database context in the session |
| `CREATE TABLE student (...)` | Inserts a row in the `TBLS` table of the Metastore and creates an empty directory `/user/hive/warehouse/student_db.db/student/` |
| `ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','` | Tells Hive to use `LazySimpleSerDe` with `,` as field separator |
| `STORED AS TEXTFILE` | Data files will be plain UTF-8 text |

### Step 3 — Load Data into the Table

The student first creates a local CSV file `/home/hadoop/student_data.csv` containing:

```
1,Arun,CSE,85
2,Meera,ECE,78
3,Raj,CSE,92
4,Anu,MECH,67
5,John,ECE,88
```

Then in Beeline:

```sql
LOAD DATA LOCAL INPATH '/home/hadoop/student_data.csv' INTO TABLE student;
```

**Conversion Logic:** The `LOAD DATA LOCAL` command **copies** the file from the local Linux filesystem to HDFS. After execution, the file resides at:

```
/user/hive/warehouse/student_db.db/student/student_data.csv
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`LOAD DATA INPATH`** (without LOCAL) performs a **MOVE** (cut + paste) from the source HDFS path to the warehouse. This is one of the most common KTU viva questions.

### Step 4 — Query the Data

```sql
SELECT department, AVG(marks) AS avg_marks
FROM student
GROUP BY department
ORDER BY avg_marks DESC;
```

**Step-by-step execution trace:**

1. **Parsing** — `Compiler` builds a `QueryBlock` with `TOK_QUERY` and identifies the `TOK_GROUPBY` and `TOK_ORDERBY` nodes.
2. **Type checking** — Verifies that `department` is `STRING` (valid for `GROUP BY`) and `marks` is `INT` (valid for `AVG`). `AVG` returns `DOUBLE`, so `avg_marks` is inferred as `DOUBLE`.
3. **Logical plan** — `GBY_0 = GroupByKeys[department]` then `SEL_1 = SELECT department, avg_marks` then `OBY_2 = ORDER BY avg_marks DESC`.
4. **Physical plan (Tez DAG)** — Vertex 1 (Map) emits `(department, marks)`. Vertex 2 (Reduce) does the grouping and aggregation. Vertex 3 (Reduce) sorts.
5. **Execution** — YARN allocates containers; results stream back to Beeline.

**Expected Output:**

```
cse    88.5
ece    83.0
mech   67.0
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Bug:** If a student forgets `SET hive.enforce.bucketing = true;` before creating a bucketed table, the table is created but **bucketing is ignored at INSERT time**. Always set this property *before* loading data into a bucketed table.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Table Type

Use the decision tree below to determine which table type fits your use case:

```
Is the data also used by other tools (Pig, Spark, Impala)?
├── YES → EXTERNAL TABLE  (Hive must NOT delete data on DROP)
└── NO  → Is the table used for staging/ETL transformations?
            ├── YES → MANAGED TABLE  (Hive owns lifecycle)
            └── NO  → Are you querying high-cardinality columns often?
                        ├── YES → PARTITION + BUCKET
                        └── NO  → PARTITION only (low-cardinality column)
```

## 3.3 Mathematical Intuition for Bucket Count

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation helps answer KTU questions like *"How do you choose the number of buckets?"* and *"Why do we set the reducer count equal to the number of buckets?"*

The bucket for a given row is determined by the **hash function** applied to the bucketing column. The standard formula is:

$$
\text{Bucket ID} = \left( \text{hash}(\text{bucket\_column}) \bmod \text{num\_buckets} \right)
$$

**Derivation of "Why num_buckets ≈ distinct values for high cardinality"**:

Let $N$ be the number of distinct values of the bucketing column. Let $B$ be the number of buckets. Assuming a **uniform hash distribution**, the expected number of distinct values per bucket is:

$$
E[\text{distinct per bucket}] = N \times \left(1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{B}\right)^N \right)
$$

**Conversion Logic for the formula:** This is the classic **"birthday problem"** probability. If $B = N$, then on average each bucket gets exactly one distinct value, which gives **maximum parallelism** for joins. If $B \ll N$, multiple values share a bucket, increasing local join cost. If $B \gg N$, many buckets are empty — wasted reducer slots.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU practical rule of thumb:** Set `num_buckets` to a value that is a **power of 2** (e.g., 32, 64) and a **multiple of the average number of reducer slots** in your cluster. This ensures the cluster's parallelism is fully utilized.

## 3.4 Aggregations & Window Functions — Worked Examples

**Example A — `GROUP BY` with `HAVING`:**

```sql
SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS total
FROM student
GROUP BY department
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY total DESC;
```

**Output:**
```
cse    2
ece    2
mech   1   (excluded by HAVING)
```

**Example B — `CASE WHEN` for conditional aggregation:**

```sql
SELECT
    department,
    SUM(CASE WHEN marks >= 80 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS distinction,
    SUM(CASE WHEN marks >= 60 AND marks < 80 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS first_class
FROM student
GROUP BY department;
```

**Output:**
```
cse    distinction=2, first_class=0
ece    distinction=1, first_class=1
mech   distinction=0, first_class=1
```

**Conversion Logic:** The `CASE WHEN` expression returns **1 or 0**, and `SUM` of these acts as a **conditional counter**. This is a portable pattern for **pivot-style analytics** that works across all SQL dialects.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HiveQL Query Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User types HiveQL query] --> B[Beeline / JDBC Client]
    B --> C[HiveServer2 receives query]
    C --> D[Driver creates Session Handle]
    D --> E[Compiler parses to AST]
    E --> F[Semantic Analyzer type-checks]
    F --> G[Optimizer rewrites plan]
    G --> H[Physical Plan DAG for Tez or MR]
    H --> I[Execution Engine submits to YARN]
    I --> J[Application Master allocates containers]
    J --> K[NodeManagers run Map and Reduce tasks]
    K --> L[Read data from HDFS DataNodes]
    L --> M[Shuffle and Sort across the network]
    M --> N[Reducers aggregate and write output]
    N --> O[Driver fetches result and streams to Beeline]
```

## 4.2 Hive Table Type Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need to create a new table] --> B{Is the data shared with other tools?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use EXTERNAL TABLE]
    B -- No --> D{Is the table used for ETL staging?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use MANAGED TABLE]
    D -- No --> F{Is the partitioning column high cardinality?}
    F -- No --> G[Use PARTITIONED TABLE only]
    F -- Yes --> H[Use PARTITIONED plus BUCKETED TABLE]
    C --> I[Set LOCATION keyword]
    E --> J[Hive owns data lifecycle]
    G --> K[Use PARTITIONED BY clause]
    H --> L[Use CLUSTERED BY plus PARTITIONED BY]
```

## 4.3 Hive Data Loading and Storage Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Local File System] -->|LOAD DATA LOCAL INPATH| B[Hive CLI]
    C[HDFS Path] -->|LOAD DATA INPATH| B
    B --> D[Hive Metastore DB]
    D --> E[Table Schema in RDBMS]
    B --> F[Warehouse Directory]
    F --> G[Database Directory]
    G --> H[Table Directory]
    H --> I[Partition Directories]
    I --> J[Bucket Files]
    H --> K[Data Files]
    style D fill:#f9e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#bfb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 DDL vs DML Statement Taxonomy

```mermaid
mindmap
    root((HiveQL Statements))
        DDL
            CREATE
            DROP
            ALTER
            TRUNCATE
            SHOW
            DESCRIBE
        DML
            LOAD
            INSERT
            UPDATE
            DELETE
            MERGE
        Query
            SELECT
            FROM
            WHERE
            GROUP BY
            HAVING
            ORDER BY
            JOIN
        DCL
            GRANT
            REVOKE
```
