# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the difference between a Managed table and an External table in Hive.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Managed (Internal) table** is created without the `EXTERNAL` keyword. In this case, **Hive fully owns the data lifecycle** — both the schema and the underlying data files. When a `DROP TABLE` statement is issued on a managed table, Hive deletes **both the metadata entry in the Metastore and the data files in the warehouse directory** (`/user/hive/warehouse/<db>.db/<table>/`).

An **External table** is created using the `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE` statement with an explicit `LOCATION` clause. Here, **Hive only manages the schema**; the data is stored at the user-defined path and is typically shared with other tools like Pig or Spark. When a `DROP TABLE` is issued, **only the schema is deleted from the Metastore; the data files remain intact on HDFS**. This makes external tables ideal for datasets owned by other systems.

> [!WARNING]
> Students often write "External tables cannot be queried" — **this is FALSE**. External tables can be queried exactly like managed tables. Marks will be cut.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any five aggregate functions available in Hive and write their syntax.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Function | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `COUNT(*)` | Counts the number of rows including NULLs | `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM emp;` |
| 2 | `SUM(col)` | Returns the sum of all values in the column | `SELECT SUM(salary) FROM emp;` |
| 3 | `AVG(col)` | Returns the mean of all values in the column | `SELECT AVG(salary) FROM emp;` |
| 4 | `MIN(col)` | Returns the smallest value in the column | `SELECT MIN(salary) FROM emp;` |
| 5 | `MAX(col)` | Returns the largest value in the column | `SELECT MAX(salary) FROM emp;` |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Module-Internal Choice means the student is given TWO full 14-mark questions, both from Module 2, and they must answer ONE. Each question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Module Internal Choice Q1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Hive architecture with a neat diagram. List the main components of Hive.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete HiveQL script to:
1. Create a database `sales_db`
2. Create a partitioned table `orders` with columns `order_id INT, product STRING, amount DOUBLE` partitioned by `order_date STRING`
3. Load local data from `/home/hadoop/orders.csv`
4. Show the partitions of the table
5. Display the top 5 highest-value orders grouped by product

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Hive Architecture [7 marks]:**

Apache Hive is built on top of Hadoop and consists of the following components:

1. **Hive Clients** — Provide interfaces such as **Thrift Client**, **JDBC Driver**, **ODBC Driver**, and **Beeline CLI** to submit queries.
2. **Hive Services** — Includes **HiveServer2** (for client connections), **Driver** (query lifecycle), **Compiler** (HiveQL → execution plan), and **Optimizer** (plan rewriting).
3. **Hive Metastore** — A central **RDBMS** (MySQL/PostgreSQL) that stores **table definitions, column types, partition information, and SerDe properties**.
4. **Hadoop Cluster Interface** — The **Execution Engine** that submits the physical plan to YARN as MapReduce/Tez/Spark jobs.
5. **HDFS / Storage** — The actual data files stored in the Hadoop Distributed File System.

**Conversion Logic (for examiner's expectations):** The student must mention all **five** components and the role of the **Metastore** as the "central schema repository". Marks are split: **2 marks for diagram**, **3 marks for component names**, **2 marks for explaining Metastore role**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT omit the **Metastore** in your diagram. It is a **favourite question** and students who draw only "User → Hive → HDFS" lose 2 full marks.

**Part (b) — Complete HiveQL Script [7 marks]:**

```sql
-- Step 1: Create and use database
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS sales_db;
USE sales_db;

-- Step 2: Create partitioned table
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id   INT,
    product    STRING,
    amount     DOUBLE
)
PARTITIONED BY (order_date STRING)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED
FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE;

-- Step 3: Load local data
LOAD DATA LOCAL INPATH '/home/hadoop/orders.csv' INTO TABLE orders
PARTITION (order_date='2024-01-01');

-- Repeat the above for each date partition, OR use dynamic partitioning:
SET hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode = nonstrict;
INSERT INTO TABLE orders PARTITION (order_date)
SELECT order_id, product, amount, order_date FROM staging_orders;

-- Step 4: Show partitions
SHOW PARTITIONS orders;

-- Step 5: Top 5 highest-value orders per product
SELECT product, order_id, amount
FROM (
    SELECT product, order_id, amount,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY product ORDER BY amount DESC) AS rn
    FROM orders
) ranked
WHERE rn <= 5
ORDER BY product, amount DESC;
```

**Conversion Logic:** The window function `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY product ORDER BY amount DESC)` assigns a unique rank to each row within each `product` group, sorted by `amount` descending. The outer filter `WHERE rn <= 5` keeps only the top 5 per group. The final `ORDER BY` sorts the entire result set for presentation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write `LIMIT 5` instead of the window function — but `LIMIT 5` would return only the **5 globally highest** orders, not **5 per product**. This is a **3-mark deduction** on part (b).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Module Internal Choice Q2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between Partitioning and Bucketing in Hive. Explain with a real-world example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write HiveQL statements to:
1. Create an external table `customer` with columns `cust_id INT, name STRING, city STRING, signup_date DATE`, stored as ORC, located at `/data/customers/`
2. Create a bucketed table `purchase` with `purchase_id INT, cust_id INT, amount DOUBLE` clustered by `cust_id` into 8 buckets
3. Perform an inner join between `customer` and `purchase` to find the total purchase amount per city
4. Create a view `vip_customers` listing customers whose total purchase exceeds ₹50,000

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Partitioning vs Bucketing [7 marks]:**

| Parameter | **Partitioning** | **Bucketing** |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | **Sub-directory** per distinct column value | **File** per bucket within a partition |
| Cardinality | **Low** (e.g., year, month, country) | **High** (e.g., customer_id) |
| Mechanism | `PARTITIONED BY (col TYPE)` | `CLUSTERED BY (col) INTO n BUCKETS` |
| Number of subdivisions | Dynamic, equals distinct values | Fixed at table creation |
| Optimization | **Partition pruning** via `WHERE` filter | **Map-side join** using sorted buckets |
| Data organization | Sub-directories like `/year=2024/` | Files like `000000_0`, `000001_0` |

**Real-world example:** Consider a telecom company's call detail records (CDR).

- **Partitioning** the table by `call_year` (2018, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024) creates **7 sub-directories** — one per year. A query for *"all calls in 2024"* only scans **one partition** (1/7 of the data), giving a **7× speedup**.
- **Bucketing** the same table by `customer_id` into **64 buckets** distributes customers evenly across files. A query joining this CDR table with a **customer dimension table** can use a **map-side join** because both tables are bucketed identically — eliminating the costly shuffle phase entirely.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the "cardinality" recommendation. Partitioning is for **low-cardinality** columns; bucketing is for **high-cardinality** columns. Reversing this loses **2 marks**.

**Part (b) — External + Bucketed + Join + View [7 marks]:**

```sql
-- 1. Create external table stored as ORC
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE customer (
    cust_id     INT,
    name        STRING,
    city        STRING,
    signup_date DATE
)
STORED AS ORC
LOCATION '/data/customers/';

-- 2. Create bucketed table
SET hive.enforce.bucketing = true;
CREATE TABLE purchase (
    purchase_id INT,
    cust_id     INT,
    amount      DOUBLE
)
CLUSTERED BY (cust_id) INTO 8 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC;

-- 3. Inner join with aggregation
SELECT c.city, SUM(p.amount) AS total_purchase
FROM customer c
JOIN purchase p
  ON c.cust_id = p.cust_id
GROUP BY c.city
ORDER BY total_purchase DESC;

-- 4. Create view
CREATE VIEW vip_customers AS
SELECT c.cust_id, c.name, SUM(p.amount) AS total_amount
FROM customer c
JOIN purchase p
  ON c.cust_id = p.cust_id
GROUP BY c.cust_id, c.name
HAVING SUM(p.amount) > 50000;
```

**Conversion Logic:** The `JOIN` uses `cust_id` as the **equi-join key** (required for both **map-side joins** in Tez and **bucket-pruned joins** in Hive 3.x). The `GROUP BY c.city` aggregates purchases per city. The `HAVING` clause in the view filters customers whose `SUM(amount)` exceeds **50,000**. The view is **read-only** — DML operations on it are not permitted in Hive.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Three common mistakes:
> 1. Forgetting `SET hive.enforce.bucketing = true;` **before** creating the bucketed table — bucketing is silently ignored.
> 2. Using `WHERE` instead of `HAVING` in the view's filter — `WHERE` is applied **before** aggregation and cannot reference `SUM()`.
> 3. Writing `STORED AS TEXTFILE` for the external table when ORC is specified in the question — **exact match required**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — HiveQL (Module 2)**

- **HiveQL = SQL for Hadoop** — converts SQL-like queries into MapReduce/Tez/Spark jobs automatically.
- **Metastore is mandatory** — Hive cannot work without it; it stores schema + partition metadata in an RDBMS.
- **Managed table = Hive owns data** (DROP deletes data); **External table = Hive owns only schema** (DROP preserves data).
- **`LOAD DATA LOCAL INPATH`** copies from local FS; **`LOAD DATA INPATH`** moves within HDFS.
- **Partitioning = sub-directories on low-cardinality columns** (e.g., year, country). Enables **partition pruning**.
- **Bucketing = fixed number of files on high-cardinality columns** (e.g., user_id). Enables **map-side joins**.
- **`SET hive.enforce.bucketing = true;`** is required **before** `INSERT` into a bucketed table.
- **Default field separator** for `LazySimpleSerDe` is `,`; for `TEXTFILE` it is the ASCII `\001` (^A).
- **`ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY ...)`** is the standard pattern for "top-N per group" — **not** `LIMIT N`.
- **`WHERE` filters rows before aggregation; `HAVING` filters after aggregation** — frequent viva trap.
- **HiveQL is NOT full SQL** — it lacks `UPDATE`/`DELETE` on transaction tables by default (requires ACID setup with `ORC` and `tblproperties('transactional'='true')`).
- **Views are read-only logical queries** — they do not store data; they re-execute the underlying query each time.
- **CTAS (`CREATE TABLE AS SELECT`)** is a popular single-statement way to create a table and populate it from a query result.
- **High-yield exam keywords**: "Metastore", "SerDe", "partition pruning", "bucket pruning", "map-side join", "execution engine", "warehouse directory", "LazySimpleSerDe".

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Tables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Big Data Analytics**, a **Table** is a logical, structured data abstraction that organises records (rows/tuples) into a defined schema of columns (fields/attributes), physically persisted across a distributed file system such as **HDFS** (Hadoop Distributed File System) and managed by a data warehouse engine such as **Apache Hive** or a NoSQL store such as **Apache HBase**. In Hive, a table is essentially a **metadata descriptor** (stored in the **Metastore**) that maps a directory in HDFS to a schema (column names, data types, row format, storage format), enabling **SQL-like declarative querying** (HiveQL) over petabytes of unstructured and semi-structured data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** *A Hive Table = Schema + Storage Location (HDFS Path) + SerDe + Metastore Entry.* Removing the table does **not** always delete the underlying HDFS data — this single fact distinguishes **Internal (Managed)** from **External** tables.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Excel Sheet Inside a Warehouse"

Imagine a massive, dimly-lit **warehouse** with millions of cardboard boxes scattered across thousands of shelves (this is **HDFS**). Now, imagine an **inventory register** (the **Hive Metastore**) that says:

- *"Box group SHELF-A-12"* contains files shaped like an *employee record* (Name = String, Salary = Double, JoinDate = Date). That register entry **is** the table.

So a **Table in Big Data = a logical rulebook that tells Hive how to read scattered files in HDFS as if they were a single SQL table on your laptop.**

| Concept | Excel Analogy | Hive Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| One spreadsheet | `Employees.xlsx` | Hive Database (schema) |
| One sheet inside it | `Sheet1` | Hive Table |
| Column headers | `A1, B1, C1` | Schema (column names + types) |
| Rows of data | Records | Records in HDFS files |
| File format | `.xlsx` | TextFile / ORC / Parquet / Avro |
| Cell formatting rule | Cell formatter | **SerDe** (Serializer/Deserializer) |

### 1.3 Classification of Tables in the Big Data Stack

```text
Big Data Tables
 ├── Structured Warehouse Tables (Hive)
 │     ├── Internal / Managed Tables
 │     ├── External Tables
 │     ├── Partitioned Tables
 │     ├── Bucketed Tables
 │     └── Temporary Tables
 └── NoSQL Columnar Tables (HBase)
       ├── Wide-column tables
       └── Sparse tables with row keys
```

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 Module Experiment 2, the **primary focus is Hive Tables** (DDL + DML + partitioning + bucketing), with a secondary conceptual touch on **HBase Tables**. HBase is covered for contrast only.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Hive Table — The Four Pillars

Every Hive table, regardless of type, is built on **four immutable pillars**. Examiners love asking which pillar is affected by which operation.

| # | Pillar | Description | What it controls |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Schema** | Column name + Data type | Logical structure of rows |
| 2 | **Storage Location** | HDFS path (e.g. `/user/hive/warehouse/...`) | Physical placement of data |
| 3 | **Row Format / SerDe** | How bytes ↔ records are translated | Parsing & serialization |
| 4 | **Storage Format** | TextFile / ORC / Parquet / Avro / RCFile | Compression & columnar reads |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — "SSRS"** → **S**chema, **S**torage Location, **R**ow Format, **S**torage Format. Memorise this acronym — it appears in almost every KTU Part B question on tables.

### 2.2 Internal (Managed) vs External Tables — The Core Distinction

This is the **single most-tested concept** in KTU's Big Data Module 2. The behavioural difference is captured in the table below.

| Feature | **Internal (Managed) Table** | **External Table** |
|---|---|---|
| Creation keyword | `CREATE TABLE` (default) | `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE` |
| Data ownership | Hive owns the data | User owns the data |
| HDFS default location | `/user/hive/warehouse/<db>.db/<table>` | User-specified `LOCATION '/path/...'` |
| `DROP TABLE` behaviour | **Deletes metadata AND HDFS data** | **Deletes metadata ONLY** |
| Use case | ETL staging, scratch work, transformation outputs | Production shared datasets, source-of-truth files |
| `DESCRIBE FORMATTED` shows | `Table Type: MANAGED_TABLE` | `Table Type: EXTERNAL_TABLE` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Examiner Trap:** Students frequently write *"External table stores data outside Hive"*. This is **wrong** — what it actually means is that the **user retains ownership** of the data, and Hive only holds a pointer (metadata). The data may still be in HDFS.

### 2.3 Partitioned Tables — Divide and Conquer

A **partition** is a way of dividing a large table into **logical segments** based on the value of one or more columns (typically date, region, or category). Physically, **each partition becomes a separate sub-directory** inside the table's HDFS directory.

**Why partition?**
- **Pruning** — Hive skips entire directories during a query if the `WHERE` clause filters on the partition column, reducing I/O drastically.
- **Parallelism** — Each partition can be processed by a separate mapper task.

**Partitioning rules**
- Partition column should be of a **low-cardinality** type (e.g., `country`, `year`, `status`).
- A column used in `WHERE` very frequently is a good partition candidate.
- Avoid high-cardinality columns (e.g., `user_id`, `timestamp`) — they explode directory count and crush the **NameNode** (the HDFS master that holds all file/directory metadata in RAM).

### 2.4 Bucketed Tables — Hash-Based Slicing

**Bucketing** (also called *clustering* in some SQL engines) **hash-partitions** data within a partition (or within a non-partitioned table) into a fixed number of **buckets**, each stored as a **file** in HDFS. Hive uses the formula:

$$
\text{hash}(\text{bucket\_column}) \;\bmod\; \text{num\_buckets} \;=\; \text{bucket\_id}
$$

This makes **sampling**, **map-side joins**, and **skew-resistant joins** extremely fast.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the following table as a one-stop reference. **No `|` is used inside formulas** — only `\vert` for absolute/modulus notation, as required for safe markdown rendering.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bucket Assignment** | $b = h(c) \bmod n$ | $b$ = bucket id, $h$ = hash fn, $c$ = bucket column value, $n$ = `num_buckets` |
| 2 | **Recommended bucket count** | $n = \dfrac{\text{total\_rows}}{\text{average\_rows\_per\_bucket}}$ | Typical target: $\vert 50\,\text{MB to } 256\,\text{MB} \vert$ per bucket file |
| 3 | **Optimal block size for ORC** | $128 \text{ MB} \le \text{stripe\_size} \le 256 \text{ MB}$ | Aligned with HDFS default block of **128 MB** |
| 4 | **Number of mappers spawned** | $\approx \dfrac{\text{total\_input\_size}}{\text{mapreduce.input.fileinputformat.split.maxsize}}$ | Default `split.maxsize` ≈ HDFS block size |
| 5 | **Number of reducers** | $\max\!\left(1,\;\left\lfloor \dfrac{\text{total\_input\_size}}{\text{reducer\_bytes\_per\_reducer}} \right\rfloor\right)$ | Default $\approx 1 \text{ GB}$ input per reducer |
| 6 | **Partition pruning savings** | $\text{files\_scanned} = \text{total\_partitions} - \text{partitions\_matched}$ | Ignored partitions cost 0 I/O |
| 7 | **Compression ratio (ORC)** | $\text{ratio} \approx 3{:}1 \text{ to } 8{:}1$ | Depends on data repetition |
| 8 | **HDFS block size (default)** | $\mathbf{128 \text{ MB}}$ | Configurable via `dfs.blocksize` |
| 9 | **Replication factor (default)** | $\mathbf{3}$ | Configurable via `dfs.replication` |
| 10 | **HBase region split threshold** | $\geq 10 \text{ GB}$ (default) | RegionServer splits hot regions automatically |

### 2.6 Storage Formats — TextFile vs ORC vs Parquet

| Property | **TextFile** | **ORC** (Optimized Row Columnar) | **Parquet** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Row-oriented | Columnar | Columnar |
| Compression | Poor | Excellent (Snappy/Zlib) | Excellent (Snappy/Gzip) |
| Predicate pushdown | ❌ | ✅ (min/max indexes per stripe) | ✅ (page-level stats) |
| Best for | Raw ingest, interoperability | Hive analytical workloads | Spark, multi-engine access |
| ACID support | ❌ | ✅ (since Hive 0.14) | ❌ native, ✅ via Delta/Iceberg |
| Default KTU choice | Loading stage | **Reporting/analytics** | Cross-engine pipelines |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Big Data Tables |
|---|---|
| **Banking** | Partitioned transaction tables (by year/month) for audit and risk analytics |
| **E-Commerce** | External tables over clickstream logs (raw Parquet files); managed tables for derived KPI marts |
| **Telecom** | Bucketed CDR (Call Detail Record) tables for fast sample-based churn modelling |
| **Healthcare** | Partitioned patient records (by hospital ID) for HIPAA-compliant data isolation |
| **IoT / Smart Cities** | HBase tables for high-velocity sensor writes; Hive tables for offline daily aggregations |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Expectation:** When asked *"create a table"* in the exam, you must show: **database creation → table DDL → verification → data loading → query**. Skipping any one step costs 1–2 marks. Below is the **exhaustive analytical walk-through** of a complete Experiment 2 scenario.

### 3.1 Analytical Scenario (End-to-End)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU lab question):**
*"Create a database `sales_db`. Inside it, create an external table `raw_orders` pointing to `/data/orders/`, an internal ORC table `orders_orc` partitioned by `order_year`, and a bucketed internal table `orders_buck` with 8 buckets on `customer_id`. Load CSV data into the external table, then insert it into the partitioned and bucketed tables. Verify with `DESCRIBE FORMATTED` and `SHOW PARTITIONS`."*

---

### Step 1 — Create the Database (Logical Container)

```sql
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS sales_db
COMMENT 'Sales analytics database for Experiment 2'
LOCATION '/user/hive/warehouse/sales_db.db'
WITH DBPROPERTIES ('owner' = 'student', 'dept' = 'BDA');
```

**Logic Behind Each Clause**

| Clause | Purpose | KTU Mark Worth |
|---|---|---|
| `IF NOT EXISTS` | Idempotent — won't fail on re-run | 1 |
| `COMMENT` | Self-documenting | 1 |
| `LOCATION` | Override default path (still recommended) | 1 |
| `WITH DBPROPERTIES` | Stores key-value metadata in Metastore | 1 |

> Default DB path rule: `/user/hive/warehouse/<database_name>.db/`

---

### Step 2 — Create the EXTERNAL Table (Raw Landing Zone)

```sql
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS sales_db.raw_orders (
    order_id      INT,
    customer_id   INT,
    product       STRING,
    quantity      INT,
    price         DOUBLE,
    order_date    STRING
)
COMMENT 'Raw orders landed from upstream system'
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED
    FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
    LINES TERMINATED BY '\n'
STORED AS TEXTFILE
LOCATION '/data/orders/'
TBLPROPERTIES ('skip.header.line.count'='1');
```

**Analytical Explanation**

```text
File on HDFS: /data/orders/orders_2024.csv
                 ↓
         raw_orders table "points" to this directory
                 ↓
   Hive reads bytes → SerDe splits by comma → emits rows
```

- **Why EXTERNAL here?** Because the upstream ETL pipeline writes files into `/data/orders/`. If we used a managed table and a teammate accidentally `DROP TABLE`s it, we'd lose 100 GB of source data. External = safe.
- **Why TEXTFILE?** Interoperable with anything that writes CSV. We will convert to ORC next for performance.

---

### Step 3 — Load Raw CSV Data

```sql
LOAD DATA INPATH '/data/staging/orders_2024.csv'
INTO TABLE sales_db.raw_orders;
```

> **Critical:** `LOAD DATA INPATH` **moves** the file (cut-paste semantics), while `LOAD DATA LOCAL INPATH` **copies** from the local filesystem. This is a famous KTU viva question.

---

### Step 4 — Derive the Partitioning Strategy (Analytical)

Assume the dataset is **$120 \text{ GB}$** spanning **5 years (2020–2024)**, and 80% of queries filter on a single year.

**Mathematical Justification**

$$
\text{partition\_data} = \dfrac{\text{total\_size}}{\text{num\_partitions}} = \dfrac{120 \text{ GB}}{5} = 24 \text{ GB per partition}
$$

A query filtering on `order_year = 2024` will scan only **$24 \text{ GB}$** instead of **$120 \text{ GB}$** — a **5× speedup** at zero extra cost.

**Rule of thumb derived:**

$$
\boxed{\text{ideal\_partition\_size} \in [1 \text{ GB},\; 10 \text{ GB}]}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> If a partition size $\lt 1 \text{ GB}$, you have **over-partitioning** → NameNode memory pressure, small-file problem, increased map-task overhead. If $\gt 10 \text{ GB}$, you have **under-partitioning** → poor pruning benefit.

---

### Step 5 — Create the Partitioned ORC Table

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS sales_db.orders_orc (
    order_id     INT,
    customer_id  INT,
    product      STRING,
    quantity     INT,
    price        DOUBLE
)
PARTITIONED BY (order_year INT)
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES ('orc.compress'='SNAPPY');
```

**Insert with Dynamic Partitioning (the exam favourite)**

```sql
SET hive.exec.dynamic.partition = true;
SET hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode = nonstrict;

INSERT INTO TABLE sales_db.orders_orc
PARTITION (order_year)
SELECT order_id, customer_id, product, quantity, price,
       YEAR(TO_DATE(order_date)) AS order_year
FROM sales_db.raw_orders;
```

**Static vs Dynamic Partitioning**

| Mode | Syntax | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| **Static** | `PARTITION (order_year=2024)` | You know the exact partition value |
| **Dynamic** | `PARTITION (order_year)` (column appears last in `SELECT`) | Unknown values, ETL pipelines |

---

### Step 6 — Derive the Bucketing Strategy

For the `customer_id` bucketed table, we have **$1{,}200{,}000$** rows.

**Choose bucket count**

$$
n = \frac{1{,}200{,}000 \text{ rows}}{150{,}000 \text{ rows/bucket}} = 8 \text{ buckets}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{num\_buckets} = 8}
$$

(Always choose a power of 2 — Hive performs bitwise `AND` instead of modulo, which is faster.)

**Bucket assignment for a sample row**

Assume `customer_id = 17{,}543`, hash function returns $h(17543) = 8{,}741{,}209$.

$$
b = 8{,}741{,}209 \bmod 8 = 1
$$

So this row lands in `bucket_000001` file inside the partition directory.

---

### Step 7 — Create and Populate the Bucketed Table

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS sales_db.orders_buck (
    order_id     INT,
    customer_id  INT,
    product      STRING,
    quantity     INT,
    price        DOUBLE,
    order_year   INT
)
CLUSTERED BY (customer_id) INTO 8 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC;

SET hive.enforce.bucketing = true;

INSERT INTO TABLE sales_db.orders_buck
SELECT * FROM sales_db.orders_orc;
```

---

### Step 8 — Verification Commands (Must Appear in Lab Record)

```sql
DESCRIBE FORMATTED sales_db.raw_orders;     -- shows Table Type: EXTERNAL_TABLE
DESCRIBE FORMATTED sales_db.orders_orc;     -- shows Table Type: MANAGED_TABLE
SHOW PARTITIONS sales_db.orders_orc;        -- lists year=2020, year=2021, ...
SHOW TBLPROPERTIES sales_db.orders_orc;
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM sales_db.orders_orc WHERE order_year = 2024;
```

---

### 3.2 Complete HBase Table Analogy (Brief but Examinable)

```sql
-- In HBase shell
CREATE 'sales_db:hbase_orders',
  {NAME => 'cf1', VERSIONS => 3, COMPRESSION => 'SNAPPY'},
  {NAME => 'cf2', COMPRESSION => 'SNAPPY'}
```

| Hive Term | HBase Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Database | Namespace (e.g., `sales_db:`) |
| Table | Table (one per HBase namespace) |
| Row | Row (identified by **Row Key**) |
| Column | Column Qualifier inside a Column Family |
| Schema | **Schema-less** (columns added on the fly) |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — How a Hive Table is Materialised

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Submits HiveQL Query] --> B[Hive Driver]
    B --> C[Compiler - Parser and Semantic Analyzer]
    C --> D[Query Plan - DAG of MapReduce or Tez Jobs]
    D --> E[Execution Engine - Tez or MR or Spark]
    E --> F[HDFS Read - scans table directory]
    F --> G[SerDe - deserialises bytes to rows]
    G --> H[Filter and Project columns]
    H --> I[Reducer or Aggregation]
    I --> J[SerDe - serialises result rows]
    J --> K[HDFS Write to result directory]
    K --> L[Result Returned to User]

    M[Hive Metastore] -.metadata.-> C
    M -.schema info.-> E
    N[HDFS NameNode] -.block locations.-> E

    style M fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px
    style A fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram (for the lab record explanation):**
- The **Metastore** holds *what the table looks like* (schema, location, SerDe).
- The **NameNode** holds *where the blocks are* in the cluster.
- The **Execution Engine** combines both to actually read/write data on HDFS.

---

### 4.2 Internal vs External Table Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTERNAL ["Internal Managed Table"]
        I1["CREATE TABLE"] --> I2["Data copied or loaded into warehouse path"]
        I2 --> I3["DROP TABLE"]
        I3 --> I4["Metastore entry DELETED"]
        I3 --> I5["HDFS data DELETED"]
    end

    subgraph EXTERNAL ["External Table"]
        E1["CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE LOCATION X"] --> E2["Hive only stores pointer to path X"]
        E2 --> E3["DROP TABLE"]
        E3 --> E4["Metastore entry DELETED"]
        E3 --> E5["HDFS data PRESERVED at path X"]
    end

    style I4 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    style I5 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    style E4 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    style E5 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
```

---

### 4.3 Partition + Bucket Internal Directory Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["/user/hive/warehouse/sales_db.db/orders_orc/"]
    R --> P2020["order_year=2020/"]
    R --> P2021["order_year=2021/"]
    R --> P2022["order_year=2022/"]
    R --> P2023["order_year=2023/"]
    R --> P2024["order_year=2024/"]

    P2024 --> B0["000000_0 (bucket 0)"]
    P2024 --> B1["000001_0 (bucket 1)"]
    P2024 --> B2["000002_0 (bucket 2)"]
    P2024 --> B3["..."]
    P2024 --> B7["000007_0 (bucket 7)"]

    style R fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca
    style P2024 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style B1 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
```

> This single diagram is worth **2–3 marks** in the KTU viva — draw it from memory to demonstrate you understand the **physical storage model**, not just the DDL.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Differentiate between Internal (Managed) and External tables in Hive. What happens to the underlying HDFS data in each case when we execute `DROP TABLE`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Internal (Managed) Table | External Table |
|---|---|---|
| Creation | `CREATE TABLE` (default) | `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE ... LOCATION` |
| Data ownership | Hive owns the data | User owns the data |
| `DROP TABLE` result | Both **metadata** and **HDFS data** are deleted | Only **metadata** is deleted; HDFS data is preserved |

External tables are preferred when multiple tools (e.g., Spark, Pig, Flink) need to read the **same** underlying HDFS files, or when the data must outlive a Hive session.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**What is a partitioned table? Why is partitioning beneficial, and what type of column should be chosen as the partition key?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **partitioned table** logically divides data into sub-directories based on the value(s) of one or more **partition columns**. During a query, Hive uses **partition pruning** to skip entire directories whose partition values do not match the `WHERE` clause, drastically reducing I/O.

The partition key should be a **low-cardinality column** frequently used in filters — e.g., `year`, `month`, `country`, `region`. **High-cardinality columns** (e.g., `user_id`) should never be used, as they create millions of tiny directories and overload the NameNode.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** *Explain the architecture of a Hive table with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the Metastore, SerDe, and the storage formats TextFile, ORC, and Parquet.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Consider a 200 GB log dataset stored at `/logs/app/`. Design and create: (i) an external table over this data, (ii) a partitioned ORC table partitioned by `log_year` and `log_month`, and (iii) load the data and verify partitions. Show all HiveQL statements.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture Explanation — 7 marks**

A Hive table is a **metadata abstraction** over files stored in HDFS. The architecture has three logical layers:

1. **Metastore Layer** — A relational database (usually MySQL/PostgreSQL) that stores:
   - Database and table definitions (schemas)
   - Column names and data types
   - HDFS location of the table
   - SerDe class name
   - Storage format and other TBLPROPERTIES

2. **SerDe Layer (Serializer/Deserializer)** — Converts raw bytes in HDFS files into typed records (deserialise) and back (serialise). Examples:
   - `org.apache.hadoop.hive.serde2.lazy.LazySimpleSerDe` — for CSV
   - `org.apache.hive.hcatalog.data.JsonSerDe` — for JSON
   - `org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.io.orc.OrcSerde` — for ORC

3. **Storage Layer** — The actual file format on HDFS. Comparison:

| Property | TextFile | ORC | Parquet |
|---|---|---|---|
| Layout | Row | Columnar | Columnar |
| Compression | None / Gzip | Snappy / Zlib (high ratio) | Snappy / Gzip |
| Predicate pushdown | No | Yes (stripe-level min/max) | Yes (page-level) |
| Best use | Raw landing, easy debugging | Hive analytics, ACID | Spark + cross-engine |

> **Diagram** — Refer to Section 4.1 above. Marks awarded for showing Metastore → Execution Engine → HDFS data flow.

**(b) Design + Implementation — 7 marks**

```sql
-- Step 1: Switch context
USE sales_db;

-- Step 2: Create EXTERNAL table pointing to raw logs
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS raw_logs (
    log_id        BIGINT,
    user_id       INT,
    event_type    STRING,
    message       STRING,
    log_timestamp STRING
)
ROW FORMAT SERDE 'org.apache.hive.serde2.RegexSerDe'
WITH SERDEPROPERTIES (
    "input.regex" = "(\\d+)\\t(\\d+)\\t(\\w+)\\t(.+)"
)
STORED AS TEXTFILE
LOCATION '/logs/app/'
TBLPROPERTIES ('skip.header.line.count'='1');

-- Step 3: Create the PARTITIONED ORC table
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS logs_partitioned (
    log_id        BIGINT,
    user_id       INT,
    event_type    STRING,
    message       STRING
)
PARTITIONED BY (log_year INT, log_month INT)
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES ('orc.compress'='SNAPPY');

-- Step 4: Enable dynamic partitioning and load
SET hive.exec.dynamic.partition = true;
SET hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode = nonstrict;

INSERT INTO TABLE logs_partitioned
PARTITION (log_year, log_month)
SELECT
    log_id, user_id, event_type, message,
    YEAR(TO_DATE(FROM_UNIXTIME(UNIX_TIMESTAMP(log_timestamp))))  AS log_year,
    MONTH(TO_DATE(FROM_UNIXTIME(UNIX_TIMESTAMP(log_timestamp)))) AS log_month
FROM raw_logs;

-- Step 5: Verify
SHOW PARTITIONS logs_partitioned;
DESCRIBE FORMATTED logs_partitioned;
```

**Step-by-step validation logic (each step worth ~1 mark):**

- **Step 2** demonstrates external table ownership semantics (1 mark)
- **Step 3** demonstrates composite partitioning (1 mark)
- **Step 4** demonstrates dynamic partition settings (1 mark)
- **Step 5** demonstrates verification commands (1 mark)
- Partition pruning benefit explanation (1 mark)
- Correct DDL syntax (1 mark)
- Storage format choice justification (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** *What is bucketing in Hive? How is the bucket number for a row computed? Explain the advantages of bucketing with suitable examples.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Write HiveQL statements to: (i) create a database `exam_db`, (ii) create a bucketed table `student_scores` with 4 buckets on `student_id` stored as ORC, (iii) insert data from a staging table `staging_scores`, and (iv) demonstrate a `TABLESAMPLE` query on the bucketed table.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Bucketing Theory — 7 marks**

**Definition:** Bucketing is a technique of **decomposing a dataset into manageable parts (buckets)** based on the **hash value of a column**. Unlike partitioning (which uses range/categorical splits), bucketing uses a deterministic hash, ensuring that **the same input value always lands in the same bucket** across runs and queries.

**Bucket Number Computation (derivation):**

Let $c$ be the value of the bucketed column for a given row, and $h(c)$ be the hash code (Java-style `hashCode()`). Let $n$ be the total number of buckets declared in the table DDL.

$$
\boxed{b \;=\; h(c) \;\bmod\; n}
$$

When $n$ is a **power of 2** (e.g., 4, 8, 16, 32), Hive replaces the modulo with a bitwise operation:

$$
b \;=\; h(c)\; \&\; (n - 1)
$$

This is significantly faster and is the **default recommended practice** in production.

**Advantages of Bucketing (each worth ~1 mark):**

1. **Efficient Sampling** — `TABLESAMPLE (BUCKET x OUT OF y)` retrieves a uniform random sample without scanning the full table.
2. **Map-Side Joins** — Two tables bucketed on the **same join column** with the **same number of buckets** can perform a **bucket-based join**, avoiding the costly shuffle phase.
3. **Skew Resistance** — Even if `customer_id` has hot keys, each bucket has a bounded size, preventing reducer OOM (out-of-memory) errors.
4. **Faster Aggregation** — `GROUP BY` on the bucketed column can use a partial aggregation at the mapper side.
5. **Predictable File Sizes** — Each bucket file is roughly `total_size / num_buckets`, useful for capacity planning.

**(b) Implementation — 7 marks**

```sql
-- (i) Create database
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS exam_db
LOCATION '/user/hive/warehouse/exam_db.db';

USE exam_db;

-- (ii) Create the bucketed ORC table
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS student_scores (
    student_id   INT,
    subject      STRING,
    marks        INT,
    exam_date    STRING
)
CLUSTERED BY (student_id) INTO 4 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES ('orc.compress'='SNAPPY');

-- (iii) Load from staging table
SET hive.enforce.bucketing = true;

INSERT OVERWRITE TABLE student_scores
SELECT student_id, subject, marks, exam_date
FROM staging_scores;

-- (iv) Sampling query
SELECT * FROM student_scores
TABLESAMPLE (BUCKET 1 OUT OF 4);

-- Equivalent to scanning roughly 25% of rows
```

**Mark Allocation for Part (b):**

| Step | Statement / Concept | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| (i) | `CREATE DATABASE` with location | 1 |
| (ii) | `CLUSTERED BY ... INTO 4 BUCKETS` + `STORED AS ORC` | 2 |
| (iii) | `SET hive.enforce.bucketing=true;` + `INSERT OVERWRITE` | 2 |
| (iv) | `TABLESAMPLE (BUCKET x OUT OF y)` | 2 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Tables**
> 1. **Confusing `LOAD DATA INPATH` (move) with `LOAD DATA LOCAL INPATH` (copy).** Examiners explicitly test this. Lose 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting `SET hive.exec.dynamic.partition = nonstrict`** when using dynamic partitions. The query will fail with `FAILED: SemanticException`.
> 3. **Writing external table DDL without `LOCATION` clause.** Without it, Hive assumes a managed location and the table effectively becomes internal.
> 4. **Confusing `TBLPROPERTIES` with `SERDEPROPERTIES`.** The former is for metadata hints (e.g., `orc.compress`); the latter is for the SerDe library.
> 5. **Drawing the architecture diagram without the Metastore component.** Worth 2 marks — easy to lose, easy to gain.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- [ ] A **Hive table** is a metadata pointer to HDFS data — not the data itself (except for managed tables on `DROP`).
- [ ] **SSRS acronym** → Schema, Storage location, Row format, Storage format. The four pillars of every table.
- [ ] **Internal table `DROP` = data gone. External table `DROP` = data safe.** Memorise this — it is the most-asked question.
- [ ] **Partitioning** = directory-based slicing on a low-cardinality column. Ideal partition size = **1–10 GB**.
- [ ] **Bucketing** = hash-based file slicing. Bucket id = `hash(col) mod num_buckets`. Always use a power of 2.
- [ ] **Dynamic partition** requires `SET hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode = nonstrict;`.
- [ ] **Bucketing** requires `SET hive.enforce.bucketing = true;` (older Hive versions).
- [ ] `LOAD DATA INPATH` **moves**; `LOAD DATA LOCAL INPATH` **copies**.
- [ ] `TABLESAMPLE (BUCKET x OUT OF y)` works **only on bucketed tables**.
- [ ] **ORC** = columnar + Snappy + predicate pushdown → use for analytics.
- [ ] **TextFile** = interoperable, no compression → use for raw landing.
- [ ] **Parquet** = cross-engine (Spark, Hive, Impala, Presto) compatibility.
- [ ] **Partition column** should be **last** in the `SELECT` list for dynamic inserts.
- [ ] HDFS default **block size = 128 MB**; default **replication = 3**.
- [ ] HBase tables are **schema-less wide-column** stores — completely different from Hive's schema-on-write model.
- [ ] **DESCRIBE FORMATTED** is the single most useful command to identify `Table Type` (MANAGED vs EXTERNAL).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Querying Data and User Defined Functions. Hbase : HBasics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Querying Data and User Defined Functions. Hbase : HBasics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Module Experiment 2 — KTU 2024 Scheme**
> This experiment module bridges the gap between **declarative data querying** (HBase shell + API) and **extensible analytical functions** (Hive/Spark UDFs). The acronym "HBasics" in the syllabus refers to the foundational HBase operations: create, read, update, delete, and scan — the "CRUD + Scan" alphabet every Big Data engineer must master.

### 1.1 HBase — Formal Definition

**Apache HBase** is a distributed, scalable, **NoSQL column-oriented database** built on top of the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)**. It is modeled after Google's **Bigtable** paper and provides **real-time, random read/write access** to massive datasets (billions of rows × millions of columns).

| Property | Value (per KTU syllabus) |
|---|---|
| Data Model | Sparse, multi-dimensional sorted map |
| Consistency Model | Strongly consistent (single-row) |
| Storage Backend | HDFS |
| Coordination Service | Apache ZooKeeper |
| Query Language | HBase Shell, Java API, REST, Thrift, Avro, HiveQL-on-HBase |

### 1.2 User Defined Function (UDF) — Formal Definition

A **User Defined Function (UDF)** is a custom, user-written function that extends the built-in function library of a query engine (Hive, Spark SQL, Pig) to perform domain-specific transformations that are not available out-of-the-box.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Library Analogy for HBase**
> Think of HBase as a **giant library**:
> - **HMaster** = the **Librarian-in-Chief** (manages metadata, assigns shelves)
> - **RegionServers** = **Floor Managers** (handle actual book retrieval on their floor)
> - **HFiles on HDFS** = the **book stacks** in the basement (durable, sorted)
> - **MemStore** = the **return cart** on each floor (in-memory, fast)
> - **WAL (Write-Ahead Log)** = the **security camera footage** (replay for crash recovery)
> - **ZooKeeper** = the **central intercom system** (coordination, heartbeats)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kitchen Analogy for UDFs**
> Think of UDFs as **custom recipes** you teach your smart kitchen (Hive/Spark):
> - Built-in functions = recipes from the cookbook (`SUM`, `AVG`, `LOWER`)
> - UDF = your **grandmother's secret pickle recipe** (custom logic, registered into the system)
> - UDAF = recipe that **combines many ingredients** into one jar (aggregate)
> - UDTF = recipe that **splits one fruit** into many slices (table-generating)

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- Default HBase **cell size limit: 10 MB** (per cell, configurable).
- Default **WAL (Write-Ahead Log) replication factor: 3** in production clusters.
- **Region split threshold: 10 GB** (default `hbase.hregion.max.filesize`).
- HBase uses **lexicographic byte-ordering** for Row Keys.
- **Bloom filters** are enabled by default with **~1% false positive rate**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 HBase Data Model — The Anatomy of a Cell

HBase stores data as a **sparse, multi-dimensional, sorted map**. Formally:

$$\text{Data} = (RowKey, ColumnFamily : ColumnQualifier, Timestamp) \rightarrow \text{Value}$$

Every cell is uniquely identified by a **4-tuple** of coordinates.

| Component | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Row Key** | Unique identifier (byte-sorted) | `r001` |
| **Column Family** | Logical grouping (defined at table creation) | `personal`, `professional` |
| **Column Qualifier** | Sub-column within a family (dynamic) | `name`, `email`, `salary` |
| **Timestamp** | Versioning dimension (long integer) | `1700000000000` |
| **Value** | Actual payload (byte array) | `"Rahul"` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule for HBase Schema Design**
> Row keys are the **only indexed access path**. There is no secondary index in vanilla HBase. Always design row keys around your most frequent query pattern. A bad row key = a slow cluster.

### 2.2 HBase Architecture — The Bullet Logic

- **Client → ZooKeeper → HMaster → RegionServer → HDFS**
- **Step 1:** Client contacts **ZooKeeper** (`/hbase/meta`) to locate the `-ROOT-` and `.META.` region servers.
- **Step 2:** Client reads `.META.` table to find the **RegionServer** hosting the target region.
- **Step 3:** Client directly talks to the **RegionServer** for reads/writes (HMaster is *not* in the data path).
- **Step 4:** RegionServer writes first to **WAL** (durability), then to **MemStore** (in-memory buffer).
- **Step 5:** When MemStore fills (default **128 MB**), it **flushes** to a new **HFile** on HDFS.
- **Step 6:** When a region exceeds the split threshold, HMaster splits it into two regions at the **midpoint** of the largest HFile.

### 2.3 HBase Core Components

| Component | Role | Failure Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **HMaster** | Manages schema, region assignment, DDL operations | Cluster still serves reads/writes from existing assignments |
| **RegionServer** | Serves reads/writes for assigned regions | All regions on that server become unavailable until recovery |
| **ZooKeeper** | Coordination, leader election, cluster metadata | Cluster cannot start without quorum (odd number, min 3) |
| **HFile** | Immutable, sorted storage file on HDFS | Naturally fault-tolerant via HDFS replication |
| **MemStore** | In-memory write buffer (per Column Family) | Data loss mitigated by WAL replay |
| **WAL (HLog)** | Sequential log of edits for crash recovery | Replayed on RegionServer restart |
| **BlockCache** | LRU read cache (default 40% of heap) | Cache miss = direct HFile read |

### 2.4 Querying Data in HBase

The **four fundamental operations** (the "HBasics"):

| Operation | Shell Command | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Create** | `create 't1', 'cf1'` | Define table with column families |
| **Put** | `put 't1', 'r1', 'cf1:c1', 'value'` | Insert/Update a cell |
| **Get** | `get 't1', 'r1'` | Retrieve all cells of a row |
| **Scan** | `scan 't1'` | Range scan over multiple rows |
| **Delete** | `delete 't1', 'r1', 'cf1:c1'` | Mark cell for deletion (tombstone) |

### 2.5 User Defined Functions (UDFs) — The Three Families

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UDF Classification — KTU High-Yield**
> 1. **UDF (User Defined Function):** 1 row in → 1 row out. Example: custom `encrypt(string)`.
> 2. **UDAF (User Defined Aggregate Function):** n rows in → 1 row out. Example: custom `geometricMean()`.
> 3. **UDTF (User Defined Table-generating Function):** 1 row in → n rows out. Example: `explode()` in Hive.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Region count | $R = \lceil \text{TableSize} / \text{MaxRegionSize} \rceil$ | Regions, GB |
| HFile Block Size | `hbase.hfile.blocksize` default | **64 KB** (small) or **128 MB** (large) |
| MemStore Flush Size | `hbase.hregion.memstore.flush.size` | **128 MB** default |
| MemStore Flush Threshold | Sum across all stores in region | **0.4 × heap** default |
| BlockCache Fraction | `hfile.block.cache.size` | **0.4** (40% of heap) |
| Cell Version Count | $V = \sum \text{Timestamps per cell}$ | Up to `MAX_VERSIONS` kept |
| ZooKeeper Quorum | $Q = 2k + 1$ for $k$ failures | Minimum $Q = 3$ |
| HBase Data Locality | $L = \frac{\text{HDFS replicas on same node}}{\text{Total replicas}}$ | Target $\geq 80\%$ |
| Bloom Filter FP Rate | $p \approx (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k$ | $m$ bits, $k$ hash funcs, $n$ items |
| UDF Row Mapping | $\text{UDF}: \mathbb{R}^1 \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^1$ | Row-level |
| UDAF Row Mapping | $\text{UDAF}: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^1$ | Aggregate |
| UDTF Row Mapping | $\text{UDTF}: \mathbb{R}^1 \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^n$ | Tabular explode |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HBase** powers Facebook's Messages, LinkedIn's member graph, Pinterest's timeline, and Xiaomi's user data lake — all require **millisecond latency over petabytes**.
- **UDFs** in Hive power domain-specific NLP tokenizers, financial risk formulas, biometric scoring, and geospatial transformations that built-in SQL cannot express.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 HBase Shell — Step-by-Step CRUD + Scan Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the **canonical HBase shell session** expected in KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Examinations for Module Experiment 2.

**Step 1 — Start HBase (Standalone mode for lab):**

$$
\text{\$ start-hbase.sh} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{HMaster + RegionServer + ZooKeeper launched}
$$

The script first starts an embedded ZooKeeper (single-node mode), then HMaster, then a single RegionServer.

**Step 2 — Launch the HBase Shell:**

$$
\text{\$ hbase shell} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{HBase Shell; version 2.x.x}
$$

**Step 3 — Create a Table with Two Column Families:**

$$
\text{hbase> create 'employee', 'personal', 'professional'}
$$

**Logical translation:** Define a table `employee` with two column families: `personal` (e.g., name, email) and `professional` (e.g., designation, salary). Column families are stored as **separate HFiles** and configured independently (compression, TTL, block size).

**Step 4 — List Tables to Verify:**

$$
\text{hbase> list} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{["employee"]}
$$

**Step 5 — Insert Data Using `put`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{hbase> put 'employee', 'e01', 'personal:name', 'Anand Kumar'} \\
\text{hbase> put 'employee', 'e01', 'personal:email', 'anand@ktu.in'} \\
\text{hbase> put 'employee', 'e01', 'professional:role', 'Data Engineer'} \\
\text{hbase> put 'employee', 'e01', 'professional:salary', '85000'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical explanation:** Each `put` creates one cell. Internally, the RegionServer writes to WAL first, then to the MemStore of the relevant column family. The cell is `(rowkey=e01, cf=personal, qual=name, ts=1700000000000, value=Anand Kumar)`.

**Step 6 — Retrieve a Single Row Using `get`:**

$$
\text{hbase> get 'employee', 'e01'}
$$

**Output (logical):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COLUMN} \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{CELL} \\
\text{personal:email} \quad \text{ts=..., value=anand@ktu.in} \\
\text{personal:name} \quad \text{ts=..., value=Anand Kumar} \\
\text{professional:role} \quad \text{ts=..., value=Data Engineer} \\
\text{professional:salary} \quad \text{ts=..., value=85000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Scan a Range of Rows:**

$$
\text{hbase> scan 'employee', \{COLUMNS \Rightarrow ['personal:name'], LIMIT \Rightarrow 10\}}
$$

**Logical explanation:** `scan` performs a lexicographic range scan starting from the smallest row key. The result is sorted by row key, then by column family, then by column qualifier, then by timestamp (descending).

**Step 8 — Update a Cell (HBase is upsert-by-default):**

$$
\text{hbase> put 'employee', 'e01', 'professional:salary', '95000'}
$$

A new version is created. The old cell is preserved (subject to `MAX_VERSIONS`).

**Step 9 — Delete a Cell:**

$$
\text{hbase> delete 'employee', 'e01', 'personal:email'}
$$

**Logical explanation:** HBase writes a **tombstone** marker. The actual HFile compaction physically removes the cell during a major compaction.

**Step 10 — Count and Disable/Drop:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{hbase> count 'employee'} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad N \text{ rows} \\
\text{hbase> disable 'employee'} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{offline state} \\
\text{hbase> drop 'employee'} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{table removed}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 User Defined Function in Hive — Step-by-Step Development

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 expects students to demonstrate **registering a UDF in Hive** (Java or Python). The Python path (using `TRANSFORM` or Hive's Python UDF in newer versions) is the most exam-friendly.

**Step 1 — Write the UDF in Python (e.g., `udf_salary_band.py`):**

```python
# UDF: classify salary into a band
def salary_band(salary):
    try:
        s = float(salary)
    except (ValueError, TypeError):
        return "UNKNOWN"
    if s < 30000:   return "LOW"
    if s < 70000:   return "MID"
    if s < 150000:  return "HIGH"
    return "PREMIUM"
```

**Step 2 — Add the Python File to Hive Session:**

$$
\text{hive> ADD FILE /home/hadoop/udf\_salary\_band.py;}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the UDF via `TRANSFORM`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{hive> SELECT} \quad & \text{TRANSFORM(name, salary)} \\
& \text{USING 'python udf\_salary\_band.py'} \\
& \text{AS name, band} \\
& \text{FROM employee\_hbase;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — (Java alternative for exam write-up):**

```java
package com.ktu.udf;
import org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF;
import org.apache.hadoop.io.Text;

public class SalaryBandUDF extends UDF {
    public Text evaluate(Text salary) {
        if (salary == null) return new Text("UNKNOWN");
        double s = Double.parseDouble(salary.toString());
        if (s < 30000)   return new Text("LOW");
        if (s < 70000)   return new Text("MID");
        return new Text("HIGH");
    }
}
```

Compile: `javac -cp $(hive classpath) SalaryBandUDF.java` → produces `SalaryBandUDF.class`.

**Step 5 — Register and Use:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{hive> ADD JAR /home/hadoop/SalaryBandUDF.jar;} \\
\text{hive> CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION band\_fn AS 'com.ktu.udf.SalaryBandUDF';} \\
\text{hive> SELECT band\_fn(salary) FROM employee\_hbase;}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — HBase vs RDBMS (High-Yield for KTU)

| Dimension | HBase | RDBMS (e.g., MySQL) |
|---|---|---|
| Data Model | Wide-column, sparse | Row-oriented, normalized |
| Schema | Flexible (qualifiers added at runtime) | Fixed (DDL required) |
| Scaling | Horizontal (add RegionServers) | Vertical (bigger box) |
| Transactions | Single-row only | Full ACID |
| Query Language | Shell, API, HiveQL | SQL |
| Indexing | Row key only (+ secondary via Phoenix) | Multi-column B-Tree |
| Best For | Write-heavy, sparse, time-series | OLTP, joins, complex queries |
| Latency | Milliseconds | Sub-millisecond (smaller scale) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HBase Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Client Application] -->|1. Locate -ROOT- and .META.| B[ZooKeeper Quorum]
    B -->|2. Cluster coordination, leader election| C[HMaster]
    C -->|3. Region assignment, DDL| D1[RegionServer 1]
    C -->|3. Region assignment, DDL| D2[RegionServer 2]
    C -->|3. Region assignment, DDL| D3[RegionServer 3]
    A -->|4. Direct read/write| D1
    A -->|4. Direct read/write| D2
    A -->|4. Direct read/write| D3
    D1 -->|5. Append edit| E1[WAL HFile on HDFS]
    D1 -->|6. Buffer writes| F1[MemStore personal]
    D1 -->|6. Buffer writes| F2[MemStore professional]
    D1 -->|7. Flush when full| G1[HFile personal]
    D1 -->|7. Flush when full| G2[HFile professional]
    D2 --> E2[WAL HFile on HDFS]
    D3 --> E3[WAL HFile on HDFS]
    G1 --> H[HDFS DataNodes]
    G2 --> H
    E1 --> H
    E2 --> H
    E3 --> H
    D1 -.->|Read cache| I1[BlockCache]
    D2 -.->|Read cache| I2[BlockCache]
    D3 -.->|Read cache| I3[BlockCache]
```

### 4.2 HBase Data Model — Logical Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Table: employee] --> B[RowKey: e01]
    A --> C[RowKey: e02]
    A --> D[RowKey: e03]
    B --> E[Column Family: personal]
    B --> F[Column Family: professional]
    E --> E1["name = Anand"]
    E --> E2["email = anand@ktu.in"]
    F --> F1["role = Data Engineer"]
    F --> F2["salary = 85000"]
    C --> G[Column Family: personal]
    C --> H[Column Family: professional]
    G --> G1["name = Beena"]
    H --> H1["role = Analyst"]
    H --> H2["salary = 60000"]
    D --> I[Column Family: personal]
    I --> I1["name = Chitra"]
    I --> I2["email = chitra@ktu.in"]
```

### 4.3 HBase Read Path — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Client get request] --> B{BlockCache hit?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Return value from memory]
    B -- No --> D[Check MemStore]
    D -- Found --> E[Return value]
    D -- Not Found --> F[Check Bloom Filter]
    F -- Maybe present --> G[Scan HFiles on HDFS]
    F -- Definitely absent --> H[Return null]
    G --> I[Merge results from all HFiles]
    I --> J[Populate BlockCache]
    J --> K[Return to client]
```

### 4.4 HBase Write Path — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Client put request] --> B[Append to WAL on HDFS]
    B --> C[Write to MemStore of correct CF]
    C --> D{MemStore full?}
    D -- No --> E[Acknowledge write to client]
    D -- Yes --> F[Flush to new HFile on HDFS]
    F --> G[MemStore cleared]
    G --> E
    E --> H{Region size > threshold?}
    H -- No --> I[Done]
    H -- Yes --> J[Request split from HMaster]
    J --> K[Split region at midpoint of largest HFile]
    K --> I
```

### 4.5 UDF Classification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Defined Functions] --> B[UDF]
    A --> C[UDAF]
    A --> D[UDTF]
    B --> B1["1 row to 1 row"]
    B --> B2["Example: salary_band()"]
    C --> C1["n rows to 1 row"]
    C --> C2["Example: geometricMean()"]
    D --> D1["1 row to n rows"]
    D --> D2["Example: explode array column"]
    B --> E[Implementation: extends UDF]
    C --> F[Implementation: extends UDAF]
    D --> G[Implementation: extends GenericUDTF]
```

### 4.6 HBase Region Split — Visual Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Region A: rows a to m] -->|Exceeds 10 GB| B[Split Triggered]
    B --> C[Region A1: rows a to g]
    B --> D[Region A2: rows h to m]
    C --> E[Hosted on RegionServer 1]
    D --> F[Hosted on RegionServer 2]
    D --> G[Load Balanced by HMaster]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**
*Define HBase. List any four HBase shell commands with their syntax.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **HBase** is a distributed, scalable, **NoSQL column-oriented database** running on top of HDFS, modeled after Google's Bigtable. It provides real-time random read/write access to large datasets.
>
> **Four HBase shell commands:**
> 1. `create 'table_name', 'cf1', 'cf2'` — creates a table with column families.
> 2. `put 'table_name', 'rowkey', 'cf:col', 'value'` — inserts/updates a cell.
> 3. `get 'table_name', 'rowkey'` — retrieves all cells of a row.
> 4. `scan 'table_name'` — retrieves all rows in a table.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Expected Pattern)]**
*Differentiate between UDF, UDAF, and UDTF with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Type | Mapping | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **UDF** | 1 row → 1 row | `lower(name)` |
| **UDAF** | n rows → 1 row | `sum(salary)` |
| **UDTF** | 1 row → n rows | `explode(array_col)` |

> UDF performs **row-level** transformations, UDAF performs **aggregation** across many rows, UDTF performs **table generation** by producing multiple rows from a single input.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — ESE Module Internal Choice**
> Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both carry 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat architecture diagram, explain the core components of HBase. Describe the role of HMaster, RegionServer, ZooKeeper, MemStore, WAL, and HFile.

**Model Answer:**

HBase follows a **master-slave architecture** powered by ZooKeeper for coordination.

**HMaster** (1 mark): Acts as the **coordinator node**. Responsibilities:
- Manages region assignment and rebalancing across RegionServers.
- Handles DDL operations (`create`, `disable`, `drop`).
- Performs region transitions during split/merge.
- **Not in the data path** for client reads/writes.

**RegionServer** (1 mark): The **workhorse** that serves actual data:
- Hosts multiple **regions** (contiguous row-key ranges).
- Handles `put`, `get`, `scan`, `delete` requests.
- Maintains MemStore, BlockCache, and WAL per server.

**ZooKeeper** (1 mark): **Distributed coordination service**:
- Maintains cluster state (`/hbase/master`, `/hbase/rs/*`).
- Performs leader election for HMaster failover.
- Quorum of odd number of nodes (minimum 3) required.

**MemStore** (1 mark): **In-memory write buffer** per column family:
- Writes accumulate here for fast inserts.
- Sorted in-memory structure (SkipList / ConcurrentSkipListMap).
- Flushed to HFile when size threshold (default **128 MB**) is reached.

**WAL (Write-Ahead Log)** (1 mark): **Durability log**:
- Every write is appended to WAL on HDFS **before** acknowledgment.
- On RegionServer crash, WAL is replayed to recover MemStore contents.

**HFile** (1 mark): **Immutable, sorted storage file** on HDFS:
- Contains key-value pairs sorted by row key, then column family, qualifier, timestamp.
- Indexed using block-level indexes and optional Bloom filters.
- Compaction merges small HFiles into larger ones.

**Conclusion** (1 mark): The combination of MemStore (fast writes), WAL (durability), HFile (durable sorted storage), and ZooKeeper (coordination) makes HBase a **highly available, strongly consistent, real-time read/write** data store.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write and explain the step-by-step procedure to perform CRUD operations in HBase shell. Create a table `student` with column families `academic` and `contact`, insert three records, and demonstrate a range scan.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Start HBase** (0.5 marks):
```
$ start-hbase.sh
$ hbase shell
```

**Step 2 — Create the table** (1 mark):
```
hbase> create 'student', 'academic', 'contact'
```
This creates a table named `student` with two column families. HBase allocates a region for the table and assigns it to a RegionServer.

**Step 3 — Insert records using `put`** (2 marks):
```
hbase> put 'student', 's01', 'academic:name', 'Anand'
hbase> put 'student', 's01', 'academic:cgpa', '8.7'
hbase> put 'student', 's01', 'contact:phone', '9876543210'

hbase> put 'student', 's02', 'academic:name', 'Beena'
hbase> put 'student', 's02', 'academic:cgpa', '9.1'
hbase> put 'student', 's02', 'contact:email', 'beena@ktu.in'

hbase> put 'student', 's03', 'academic:name', 'Chitra'
hbase> put 'student', 's03', 'contact:phone', '9123456780'
```

**Step 4 — Get a single row** (0.5 marks):
```
hbase> get 'student', 's02'
```
Returns all cells of row `s02`.

**Step 5 — Update a cell** (0.5 marks):
```
hbase> put 'student', 's01', 'academic:cgpa', '9.0'
```
Creates a new version of the cell.

**Step 6 — Range scan** (1.5 marks):
```
hbase> scan 'student', {STARTROW => 's01', STOPROW => 's03', COLUMNS => ['academic:name']}
```
Returns rows from `s01` (inclusive) to `s03` (exclusive) showing only the `academic:name` column.

**Step 7 — Delete a cell and a table** (0.5 marks):
```
hbase> delete 'student', 's03', 'contact:phone'
hbase> count 'student'
hbase> disable 'student'
hbase> drop 'student'
```

**Logical note** (0.5 marks): Each `put` writes to WAL → MemStore → (eventually flush) HFile. Each `scan` walks the MemStore and relevant HFiles in sorted order.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of User Defined Functions (UDFs) in Hive. Differentiate between UDF, UDAF, and UDTF. Write a sample UDF in Java or Python to classify employee salary into bands.

**Model Answer:**

**Concept** (1.5 marks): Hive provides built-in functions for SQL-like operations. When built-ins are insufficient for **domain-specific logic**, users write their own functions, called **UDFs**, package them as JARs or scripts, and register them with the Hive session.

**UDF** (1 mark): **U**ser **D**efined **F**unction — operates on a single row, returns a single value. Mathematical, string, date transformations fall in this category.

**UDAF** (1 mark): **U**ser **D**efined **A**ggregate **F**unction — operates on a group of rows, returns a single value. Examples: custom `geometricMean`, `weightedAverage`, `stringConcatAll`.

**UDTF** (1 mark): **U**ser **D**efined **T**able-generating **F**unction — operates on a single row, returns multiple rows (a table). Example: `explode()` splits an array column into multiple rows.

**Sample Python UDF** (2.5 marks):

```python
# File: salary_band.py
import sys

def salary_band(s):
    try:
        v = float(s)
    except:
        return "UNKNOWN"
    if v < 30000:    return "LOW"
    elif v < 70000:  return "MID"
    elif v < 150000: return "HIGH"
    else:            return "PREMIUM"

for line in sys.stdin:
    parts = line.strip().split('\t')
    name, salary = parts[0], parts[1]
    print(f"{name}\t{salary_band(salary)}")
```

**Registration in Hive** (0.5 marks):
```
hive> ADD FILE /home/hadoop/salary_band.py;
hive> SELECT TRANSFORM(name, salary)
      USING 'python salary_band.py'
      AS name, band
      FROM employee_hbase;
```

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the HBase data model with a real-world example. Explain the roles of Row Key, Column Family, Column Qualifier, Timestamp, and Cell. Why is HBase called a "sparse" multi-dimensional map?

**Model Answer:**

**HBase Data Model** (1.5 marks): HBase organizes data as a **sparse, distributed, persistent, multi-dimensional sorted map**. The map is indexed by a **row key, column family, column qualifier, and timestamp**. Each cell holds an uninterpreted byte array value.

**Real-world example — Library Book Records** (1 mark):
- **Table:** `library`
- **Row Key:** Book ID like `B001`, `B002`
- **Column Family `info`:** contains `title`, `author`, `publisher`
- **Column Family `inventory`:** contains `shelf`, `copies`, `borrowed`
- **Timestamp:** Records each update; multiple versions of `borrowed` count can be retained.
- **Cell:** A single value at a coordinate, e.g., `(B001, info:author, ts=1700000000000) → "Tolkien"`.

**Row Key** (0.5 marks): The **primary identifier**. Sorted lexicographically. Determines the **physical storage order**. Bad design = data skew ("hotspotting") and slow scans.

**Column Family** (0.5 marks): **Logical grouping** of columns defined at table creation. Members of a family are stored together in the same HFile. Different families have different physical properties (compression, TTL).

**Column Qualifier** (0.5 marks): The **sub-column name** within a family. Qualifiers are **dynamic** — you can add a new qualifier at write time without schema change. This is HBase's famous **schema flexibility**.

**Timestamp** (0.5 marks): A **64-bit long** identifying the version of a cell. Every `put` creates a new version. Reads can request a specific timestamp or "latest".

**Cell** (0.5 marks): The atomic unit `(row, family, qualifier, ts) → value`. The only place actual data lives.

**Why "sparse"?** (1 mark): Unlike relational tables where every column has a value (or NULL) for every row, in HBase a column qualifier that has **never been written** simply **does not exist** physically. With millions of possible qualifiers, a row may use only a handful. The empty space is **not stored** — hence "sparse".

**Why "multi-dimensional"?** (0.5 marks): The data is indexed along **four** dimensions (row, family, qualifier, timestamp), unlike a 2D relational table. The timestamp dimension enables **versioned storage** without schema changes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Column Family with Column Qualifier** (loses 1 mark in Q1 of Part B). CF is fixed at table creation; CQ is dynamic. Example: `personal:name` → CF is `personal`, CQ is `name`.
> 2. **Forgetting to disable before drop** in HBase (`drop` requires `disable` first). Direct `drop` on an enabled table throws an error → loses 0.5 mark in lab practical.
> 3. **Wrong UDF class extension** in Java: must extend `org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF` (not `GenericUDF`) for simple cases. The class must be `public`, method `evaluate` must be `public`, return type must be writable (e.g., `Text`, `IntWritable`).
> 4. **Writing `UDF` instead of `UDAF` or `UDTF`** in the classification question (Part A Q2). Examiners specifically look for the correct acronym.
> 5. **HBase is NOT column-oriented storage in the OLAP sense** (like Parquet/ORC). It is a **wide-column store** (column-family store) — a critical distinction worth 1 mark in the theory question.
> 6. **Missing `ADD FILE` or `ADD JAR`** in the UDF registration step. Many students write the SQL but skip the registration step, losing 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme, Module Experiment 2**

- **HBase** is a **distributed, scalable, NoSQL, wide-column store** built on **HDFS**, providing **real-time random read/write** to **petabytes** of data.
- HBase architecture = **HMaster (control) + RegionServers (data) + ZooKeeper (coordination) + HDFS (storage)**.
- **HMaster is NOT in the data path** for client reads/writes — clients go directly to RegionServers after ZooKeeper lookup.
- **Data model = sparse, multi-dimensional, sorted map** indexed by `(RowKey, ColumnFamily:ColumnQualifier, Timestamp)`.
- **Row Key** is the **only natively indexed** access path — design carefully to avoid hotspotting.
- **Column Family** is fixed at table creation; **Column Qualifier** is dynamic.
- **MemStore** = in-memory write buffer (default 128 MB). **WAL** = durability log. **HFile** = immutable sorted file on HDFS.
- **WAL → MemStore → (flush) HFile** is the write path. **BlockCache → MemStore → BloomFilter → HFile** is the read path.
- **Region split** at **10 GB** default (`hbase.hregion.max.filesize`). Splits happen at the **midpoint of the largest HFile**, not the row-count midpoint.
- **HBase shell CRUD:** `create`, `put`, `get`, `scan`, `delete`, `disable`, `drop`, `count`, `list`, `describe`.
- **Scan** returns rows in **lexicographic order of row keys**.
- **Delete** creates a **tombstone** — physical removal happens during **major compaction**.
- **UDF = 1 row → 1 row; UDAF = n rows → 1 row; UDTF = 1 row → n rows**.
- **Hive UDF in Python:** use `ADD FILE` + `TRANSFORM ... USING 'python script.py' AS ...`.
- **Hive UDF in Java:** extend `org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.UDF`, override `evaluate()`, compile with `hive classpath`, register with `ADD JAR` + `CREATE TEMPORARY FUNCTION`.
- **HBase + Hive integration:** Hive external tables can map to HBase tables using `HBaseStorageHandler`.
- **Strong consistency** for single-row operations; **eventual consistency** for cluster-wide region movement.
- **Bloom filters** reduce HFile lookups by ~99% (1% false positive rate).
- **HBase vs RDBMS:** HBase is write-heavy, sparse, schema-flexible, scales horizontally; RDBMS is join-heavy, normalized, ACID, scales vertically.
- **ZooKeeper** quorum must be **odd** (3, 5, 7) for fault tolerance.
- **HBase cell size limit** = **10 MB** (configurable via `hbase.cell.max.value`).
- **HBase 2.x** uses **Procedure V2** for DDL operations (asynchronous, recoverable).
- **Lab practical tip:** Always start with `jps` to verify HMaster and HRegionServer processes are running before the HBase shell session.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Concepts # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > In the context of **PBADT504 – Big Data Analytics (Module Experiment 2)**, the term *"Concepts"* refers to the **foundational architectural and operational principles of the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** and the **MapReduce programming paradigm**. These concepts form the theoretical backbone required to understand, execute, and validate large-scale distributed data storage and processing experiments on a Hadoop cluster (single-node or multi-node). The **two pillars** of Module Experiment 2 are: 1. **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** – a distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant file system designed to store very large files across multiple machines in a Hadoop cluster. 2. **MapReduce** – a parallel programming model for processing massive datasets by breaking the job into independent `Map` and `Reduce` tasks executed across the cluster. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of HDFS as a "Library of Photocopied Books"** > > Imagine you have a **1000-page book** that is too large to fit in a single bookshelf. You split it into chapters (think of these as **blocks**), and instead of keeping one copy, you make **3 photocopies** of each chapter and distribute them across **3 different library branches** (these are **DataNodes**). The **librarian at the central desk** (the **NameNode**) keeps a register noting *which chapter is in which branch*. If one branch burns down, you still have two copies left — that's **fault tolerance via replication**. Similarly, **MapReduce** is like a school exam-checking system: - **Map phase**: Each teacher (Mapper) checks a bundle of answer sheets and assigns preliminary scores. - **Shuffle & Sort phase**: Sheets are grouped by question number. - **Reduce phase**: A senior teacher (Reducer) aggregates and finalizes the marks for each question. --- ## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | One-line Meaning | |---|---| | **HDFS** | Hadoop's primary distributed storage layer | | **NameNode** | Master daemon storing the **file system metadata** | | **DataNode** | Slave daemon storing the **actual data blocks** | | **Block** | Default unit of data storage in HDFS (**128 MB** by default in Hadoop 2.x+) | | **Replication Factor** | Number of copies of each block (**default = 3**) | | **JobTracker / ResourceManager** | Master that schedules and monitors MapReduce jobs | | **TaskTracker / NodeManager** | Slave that executes individual map/reduce tasks | | **Mapper** | Function that processes input key-value pairs and emits intermediate pairs | | **Reducer** | Function that aggregates intermediate values grouped by key | | **Combiner** | Local reducer running on the mapper node to reduce network traffic | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students are expected to *verbally explain* HDFS architecture, the **Read/Write flow**, and trace the **MapReduce data flow** during the lab viva. Marks are often awarded for clear definitions in the record. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 HDFS Architecture – The "Why" and the "How" ### 2.1.1 Why HDFS was Designed Traditional file systems (NTFS, ext4) were built for **single machines**. As data exploded into the **Petabyte** range, the following constraints emerged: - **Storage ceiling** of a single disk - **No inherent fault tolerance** at the disk level for huge files - **Sequential read/write bottleneck** when many users access a giant file HDFS solves these with three design decisions: 1. **Block-based storage** → Files are split into fixed-size blocks (default **128 MB**). 2. **Replication** → Each block is replicated across multiple DataNodes (default **3 copies**). 3. **Master-Slave topology** → A central **NameNode** holds *metadata*; many **DataNodes** hold *actual data*. ### 2.1.2 The Master–Slave Logic - **NameNode (Master)** - Stores metadata in memory: filenames, directory tree, block-to-DataNode mapping, replication details. - Does **NOT** store actual file data. - On startup, it merges `fsimage` (checkpoint) + `edits log` (transaction log) to reconstruct metadata. - **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** in Hadoop 1.x — solved in Hadoop 2.x via **High Availability (HA)** with a **Standby NameNode** using **ZooKeeper**. - **DataNode (Slave)** - Stores actual data blocks on local disk. - Sends **heartbeat signals** to the NameNode every **3 seconds**. - Sends **Block Reports** (list of all blocks it holds) periodically (default every **6 hours**). - Executes read/write commands issued by the NameNode. ### 2.1.3 Block, Replication & Rack Awareness > [!NOTE] > **Why 128 MB?** > A typical seek on a magnetic disk is ~10 ms; to keep seek time at < **1%** of transfer time, the block size is kept large. With a 128 MB block and 100 MB/s transfer rate, transfer time ≈ 1.28 s, so seek overhead is acceptable. - **Rack Awareness Algorithm**: Hadoop assumes the **first replica** is placed on the **local node**, the **second** on a different node on the **same rack**, and the **third** on a **different rack**. This balances **reliability** (rack failure) and **bandwidth** (intra-rack switch is faster). ### 2.1.4 HDFS Read/Write Flow (High-Yield for Viva) **Write Path (4-step):** 1. Client calls `DistributedFileSystem.create()` → contacts **NameNode** for a lease and a list of DataNodes (pipeline). 2. Data is split into packets and pushed to the **first DataNode** in the pipeline. 3. Each DataNode forwards the packet to the **next** in the pipeline (chain replication). 4. After all replicas acknowledge, the **packet** is removed from the buffer; next packet flows. **Read Path (3-step):** 1. Client calls `DistributedFileSystem.open()` → NameNode returns the **closest DataNode** holding the first block. 2. Client reads block directly from the DataNode (NameNode is not in the data path). 3. On block boundary, client contacts NameNode again for the next block's location. --- ## 2.2 MapReduce Programming Model ### 2.2.1 The Core Abstraction MapReduce expresses any distributed computation as **two functions**: - $\text{map}: (k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_2, v_2)$ - $\text{reduce}: (k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_3, v_3)$ > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU 2024 lab exams, the **WordCount** example is the de-facto standard Experiment 2.
