Be ready to write the pseudo-code/MRDemo on paper and trace the **intermediate key-value pairs** generated by the Mapper. ### 2.2.2 Execution Phases | Phase | What Happens | Where It Runs | |---|---|---| | **Input Split** | File is logically split (default = block size). | Client side | | **Map** | Each split is processed by a `Mapper` task. | On DataNode holding the split | | **Combiner (Optional)** | Local aggregation to reduce data shuffled. | Same node as Mapper | | **Shuffle & Sort** | Intermediate pairs are partitioned, sorted, and transferred to Reducers. | Across the network | | **Reduce** | Values for each key are aggregated. | On assigned DataNode | | **Output** | Final result written to HDFS. | On output DataNode | ### 2.2.3 The Fault Tolerance Question > [!WARNING] > **Common Viva Trap:** *If a Mapper fails, does the whole job fail?* **NO** — the **JobTracker / Application Master** simply re-runs the failed task on a different node, since map tasks are *idempotent*. Reduce task failure → only that specific reduce attempt is re-executed, NOT all reducers. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table condenses every formula/metric a student is likely to be asked in **KTU 2024 ESE / Lab viva** for this experiment. | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **HDFS Block Size (Hadoop 2.x+)** | $B_{default} = 128 \text{ MB}$ | MB | Configurable via `dfs.blocksize` | | 2 | **Replication Factor** | $R_{default} = 3$ | copies | Set via `dfs.replication` | | 3 | **Number of Blocks for a File** | $N_{blocks} = \lceil \dfrac{F}{B} \rceil$ | blocks | $F$ = file size | | 4 | **Total Storage Used (with replication)** | $S_{total} = F \times R$ | bytes | Includes replicas | | 5 | **Disk Seek Budget** | $\text{Seek \%} = \dfrac{t_{seek}}{t_{seek} + t_{transfer}} \le 1\%$ | ratio | Justifies large block size | | 6 | **NameNode Heartbeat Interval** | $H_{hb} = 3 \text{ s}$ | seconds | DataNode → NameNode | | 7 | **DataNode Block Report Interval** | $T_{br} = 21600 \text{ s}$ (6 hr) | seconds | `dfs.blockreport.intervalMsec` | | 8 | **Map Task Count** | $\text{map\_tasks} \approx N_{blocks}$ | tasks | One per input split | | 9 | **Reduce Task Count** | $r = \text{configured by user (default 1)}$ | tasks | Set via `job.setNumReduceTasks(n)` | | 10 | **Mapper Throughput** | $\text{MT} = \dfrac{\text{Total Records}}{\text{Map Time}}$ | records/sec | Useful for performance viva | > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are routinely used in **cluster capacity planning** — e.g., sizing the NameNode heap (rule of thumb: **1 GB heap per 1 million blocks**) and estimating **rack space, switch bandwidth, and storage procurement costs** in real-world Hadoop deployments at companies like Yahoo, Facebook, and Amazon EMR. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Blocks from a File Size > [!NOTE] > This derivation is frequently asked as a **2-mark short problem** in KTU lab viva. **Given:** - A file of size $F$ bytes. - HDFS block size $B$ bytes. - Replication factor $R$. **Find:** Number of physical blocks stored across the cluster. **Step 1 — Identify the operation.** The number of blocks for a single file is the **ceiling** of the file size divided by the block size. $$ N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil $$ **Step 2 — Explain each term.** - $F$ = total file size in bytes. - $B$ = HDFS block size (default $128 \text{ MB} = 128 \times 2^{20}$ bytes). - $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ = ceiling function; needed because the **last block** is almost always smaller than $B$. **Step 3 — Account for replication.** Each logical block is physically stored $R$ times. So the **total physical blocks in the cluster** is: $$ N_{physical} = N_{blocks} \times R = R \times \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil $$ **Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style).** > **Problem:** A 450 MB file is stored in HDFS. Block size = 128 MB, Replication factor = 3. Calculate the number of logical blocks and total physical blocks (including replicas). **Solution:** $$ N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{450 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil $$ We perform integer division first: $450 \div 128 = 3$ remainder $66$. Since the remainder is non-zero, we round up. $$ N_{blocks} = 4 \text{ logical blocks} $$ The actual block sizes are: $128 \text{ MB}, 128 \text{ MB}, 128 \text{ MB}, 66 \text{ MB}$ — together they equal $450 \text{ MB}$. Now compute physical blocks with replication: $$ N_{physical} = 4 \times 3 = 12 \text{ physical blocks} $$ **Total storage consumed:** $$ S_{total} = 450 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 1350 \text{ MB} \approx 1.32 \text{ GB} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: NameNode Memory Sizing (Rule of Thumb) **Given:** Each block metadata entry occupies approximately **150 bytes** in NameNode JVM heap. **Find:** Approximate NameNode heap size $H$ for a cluster with $N$ files averaging $n_b$ blocks each. **Step 1 — Total blocks in the system:** $$ B_{total} = N \times n_b $$ **Step 2 — Memory required for block metadata:** $$ M_{blocks} = B_{total} \times 150 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 3 — Add overhead for filesystem tree, datanode registry, etc.** (typically **2x** headroom): $$ H = 2 \times M_{blocks} $$ **Step 4 — Example.** Suppose a cluster stores $N = 10{,}000$ files, each averaging $n_b = 8$ blocks. $$ B_{total} = 10{,}000 \times 8 = 80{,}000 \text{ blocks} $$ $$ M_{blocks} = 80{,}000 \times 150 = 12{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 11.44 \text{ MB} $$ $$ H = 2 \times 11.44 \text{ MB} \approx 22.88 \text{ MB} $$ In practice, Hadoop engineers provision **1 GB heap per ~1 million blocks**, so a much larger cluster would still fit comfortably in a single commodity server's RAM. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: MapReduce Data Flow Trace (WordCount) > [!NOTE] > This is the **most repeated viva question** in Module Experiment 2. The following table traces the data for input text: `"deer bear river bear deer bear"`. | Stage | Input | Output (Key, Value pairs) | |---|---|---| | **Input Split** | `"deer bear river"` | raw line | | **Map Task 1** | `"deer bear river"` | `(deer,1), (bear,1), (river,1)` | | **Input Split 2** | `"bear deer bear"` | raw line | | **Map Task 2** | `"bear deer bear"` | `(bear,1), (deer,1), (bear,1)` | | **Combine (Local)** | per-mapper | `Map Task 1 → {(deer,1), (bear,1), (river,1)}`
`Map Task 2 → {(bear,2), (deer,1)}` | | **Shuffle & Sort** | partition by key | `bear → [1, 2]`, `deer → [1, 1]`, `river → [1]` | | **Reduce Task A** | `(bear, [1, 2])` | `(bear, 3)` | | **Reduce Task B** | `(deer, [1, 1])` | `(deer, 2)` | | **Reduce Task C** | `(river, [1])` | `(river, 1)` | | **Final HDFS Output** | — | `/output/part-r-00000`: `bear 3`
`/output/part-r-00001`: `deer 2`
`/output/part-r-00002`: `river 1` | --- ## 3.4 Verification: Disk Seek Budget Justifies Block Size We verify the **1% rule** that justifies the 128 MB block size. **Given:** Disk seek time $t_{seek} = 10 \text{ ms}$, transfer rate $r = 100 \text{ MB/s}$. **Step 1 — Transfer time for one block of size $B$:** $$ t_{transfer} = \frac{B}{r} $$ **Step 2 — Impose the 1% rule:** $$ \frac{t_{seek}}{t_{seek} + t_{transfer}} \le 0.01 $$ **Step 3 — Substitute and solve for $B$:** $$ \frac{0.01}{1 - 0.01} \le \frac{B}{r \cdot t_{seek}} $$ $$ B \ge \frac{0.01 \times r \times t_{seek}}{0.99} = \frac{0.01 \times 100 \times 0.01}{0.99} \approx 0.0101 \text{ s} \times 100 \text{ MB/s} $$ $$ B \ge 1.01 \text{ MB} $$ Any block size **≥ 1 MB** satisfies the rule; Hadoop's **128 MB** is far more conservative, ensuring excellent seek-to-transfer ratio even on slower disks. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 HDFS Master–Slave Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB client1["CLIENT APPLICATION"] client2["HDFS CLI / JAVA API"] subgraph MASTER["MASTER NODE"] nn["NAMENODE\nMetadata + fsimage + edits"] snn["SECONDARY NAMENODE\nCheckpoint Helper"] end subgraph SLAVE1["SLAVE NODE 1"] dn1["DATANODE 1\nBlock Storage"] end subgraph SLAVE2["SLAVE NODE 2"] dn2["DATANODE 2\nBlock Storage"] end subgraph SLAVE3["SLAVE NODE 3"] dn3["DATANODE 3\nBlock Storage"] end client1 -->|RPC metadata request| nn client2 -->|hdfs dfs commands| nn nn -->|heartbeat / block report| dn1 nn -->|heartbeat / block report| dn2 nn -->|heartbeat / block report| dn3 nn -->|merge fsimage| snn client1 -->|direct read/write| dn1 client1 -->|direct read/write| dn2 client1 -->|direct read/write| dn3 ``` ## 4.2 HDFS Write Path (Pipeline Replication Flow) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client participant NN as NameNode participant DN1 as DataNode 1 participant DN2 as DataNode 2 participant DN3 as DataNode 3 C->>NN: create(filename) → Request lease NN-->>C: Return DataNode pipeline [DN1, DN2, DN3] C->>DN1: Write Packet 1 DN1->>DN2: Forward Packet 1 DN2->>DN3: Forward Packet 1 DN3-->>C: ACK (Packet 1 stored) C->>DN1: Write Packet 2 DN1->>DN2: Forward Packet 2 DN2->>DN3: Forward Packet 2 DN3-->>C: ACK (Packet 2 stored) Note over C,DN3: Pipeline closes; NameNode updates metadata ``` ## 4.3 MapReduce Job Lifecycle (Block-Level Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Input File on HDFS"] --> B["InputFormat: Split"] B --> C["Map Task 1"] B --> D["Map Task 2"] C --> E["Combiner (local reduce)"] D --> F["Combiner (local reduce)"] E --> G["Shuffle & Sort Phase"] F --> G G --> H["Partitioner"] H --> I["Reduce Task 1"] H --> J["Reduce Task 2"] I --> K["OutputFormat: HDFS Write"] J --> K ``` ## 4.4 HDFS Block Replication Policy (Rack-Aware) ```mermaid flowchart TB F["File: 450 MB"] --> S1["Block 1: 128 MB"] F --> S2["Block 2: 128 MB"] F --> S3["Block 3: 128 MB"] F --> S4["Block 4: 66 MB"] S1 --> R1A["Rack 1 / DN1"] S1 --> R1B["Rack 1 / DN2"] S1 --> R2A["Rack 2 / DN3"] S2 --> R1C["Rack 1 / DN4"] S2 --> R2B["Rack 2 / DN5"] S2 --> R2C["Rack 2 / DN6"] S3 --> R1A S3 --> R2A S3 --> R3A["Rack 3 / DN7"] S4 --> R1B S4 --> R2B S4 --> R3B["Rack 3 / DN8"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]** [3 Marks] > **Q.** Define **HDFS**. List its **two main components** and state the **default block size** and **replication factor**. **Model Answer:** HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) is a distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant file system designed to store very large files across multiple commodity machines. Its two main components are: 1. **NameNode** (Master) — stores metadata, file system namespace, and block-to-DataNode mapping. 2. **DataNode** (Slave) — stores the actual data blocks on local storage. The **default block size** is **128 MB** (in Hadoop 2.x and later) and the **default replication factor** is **3**. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]** [3 Marks] > **Q.** What is the role of a **Combiner** in a MapReduce job? Why is it called a "Mini-Reducer"? **Model Answer:** A **Combiner** is an optional, local aggregation function that runs on the same node as the Mapper, immediately after the map phase. It performs a local `reduce`-like aggregation on the intermediate key-value pairs emitted by the Mapper **before** they are shuffled across the network to the Reducers. It is called a "Mini-Reducer" because: - It uses the **same user-defined logic** as the Reducer (typically the same function). - It runs **only on Mapper output**, not on globally grouped data. - Its sole purpose is to **reduce the volume of data transferred** over the network, thereby improving job performance and reducing bandwidth consumption. It is **not always safe** to use (e.g., it cannot be used for computing the **mean** of values, because the average of averages is not the true average). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes** > - Students often **confuse NameNode with DataNode** responsibilities. Memorize: *NameNode = metadata, DataNode = data*. > - For MapReduce data flow, examiners expect **explicit key-value pairs** in each stage, not just a vague explanation. **Always write the intermediate `(K, V)` pairs**. > - In HDFS write flow, students forget the **pipeline replication** concept and the role of the **ack packet**. This is a **2–3 mark deduction** in many papers. > - Do **NOT** claim the NameNode stores actual data — it is a **metadata-only** server. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Option 1] > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **HDFS architecture**. Describe the role of **NameNode**, **DataNode**, and **Secondary NameNode**. [7 Marks] > **(b)** Explain the **HDFS file read mechanism** in detail with a suitable diagram. [7 Marks] #### **Solution (a) — HDFS Architecture** HDFS follows a **Master-Slave architecture**: 1. **NameNode (Master)** - Stores all **metadata**: filename, directory structure, block-to-DataNode mapping, permissions, replication factor. - Loads metadata into **RAM** on startup from `fsimage` + replaying `edits` log. - Manages **client access** to files. - Receives **heartbeats (every 3 s)** and **block reports (every 6 hr)** from all DataNodes. 2. **DataNode (Slave)** - Stores actual **data blocks** on its local disk. - Serves **read/write requests** from clients. - Sends periodic **heartbeats** to confirm liveness. - On startup, registers with NameNode. 3. **Secondary NameNode** - A **helper daemon** (NOT a backup). - Periodically merges `fsimage` + `edits log` to create a **new checkpoint**, preventing the edits log from growing unbounded. - In **Hadoop 2.x+**, replaced by **Standby NameNode** in HA setups. *(Include the HDFS Architecture diagram from Section 4.1 here — 2 marks)* #### **Solution (b) — HDFS Read Mechanism** **Step 1:** The client invokes `FileSystem.open(path)` on the `DistributedFileSystem` object. **Step 2:** The `DistributedFileSystem` makes an **RPC call** to the **NameNode** to get the **block locations** for the first few blocks of the file. **Step 3:** The NameNode returns a list of **DataNode addresses** sorted by **network proximity** (closest first), along with the block metadata. **Step 4:** The client connects **directly** to the **first (closest) DataNode** and calls `read()`. The DataNode streams the block bytes back to the client. **Step 5:** When the end of a block is reached, the client contacts the NameNode again for the **next block's location**, and the process repeats. **Step 6:** After the last block is read, the client calls `close()` to release resources. > **Key Point:** The NameNode is **not in the data path** for reads — this prevents it from becoming a bottleneck. *(Include the sequence diagram from Section 4.2 here — 2 marks)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Option 2] > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** > **(a)** Explain the **MapReduce programming model** with a block diagram. Describe the **Mapper**, **Reducer**, **Combiner**, and **Partitioner** functions. [7 Marks] > **(b)** A file of size **640 MB** is stored in HDFS with a **block size of 128 MB** and **replication factor 3**. Calculate the **number of logical blocks**, **number of physical blocks**, and **total storage consumed**. [7 Marks] #### **Solution (a) — MapReduce Programming Model** MapReduce is a **distributed programming model** that processes massive datasets in two primary phases: **Map** and **Reduce**. Intermediate phases include **Shuffle, Sort, and Combine**. 1. **Mapper** - Input: a key-value pair $(k_1, v_1)$ — typically `(line_offset, line_text)`. - Processes the input and emits **zero or more** intermediate key-value pairs $(k_2, v_2)$. - Executes in parallel on every input split (one mapper per block, by default). 2. **Combiner** *(optional)* - A **local reducer** running on the Mapper's node. - Aggregates intermediate pairs **per mapper** to reduce data shuffled. - Must be **commutative and associative** for correctness. 3. **Partitioner** - Controls which **Reducer** receives which intermediate keys. - Default: `hash(key) mod R` (where $R$ = number of reducers). - Ensures **even load distribution**. 4. **Reducer** - Input: a key and an **iterator** over all values for that key: $(k_2, \text{list}(v_2))$. - Aggregates the values and emits the final output: $(k_3, v_3)$. 5. **Shuffle & Sort** *(framework-managed)* - Transfers intermediate data from Mappers to Reducers across the network. - Sorts data by key within each partition. *(Include the MapReduce lifecycle diagram from Section 4.3 here — 2 marks)* #### **Solution (b) — Block & Storage Calculation** **Given:** - File size $F = 640 \text{ MB}$ - Block size $B = 128 \text{ MB}$ - Replication factor $R = 3$ **Step 1 — Number of logical blocks:** $$ N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{640}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.0 \rceil = 5 \text{ logical blocks} $$ (Each block is exactly $128 \text{ MB}$, totaling $5 \times 128 = 640 \text{ MB}$.) **Step 2 — Number of physical blocks (with replication):** $$ N_{physical} = N_{blocks} \times R = 5 \times 3 = 15 \text{ physical blocks} $$ **Step 3 — Total storage consumed:** $$ S_{total} = F \times R = 640 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 1920 \text{ MB} \approx 1.875 \text{ GB} $$ > **Answer Summary:** **5 logical blocks**, **15 physical blocks**, and **1920 MB (1.875 GB)** of total storage used on the cluster. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam** - ✅ **HDFS** = **Hadoop Distributed File System**; default block = **128 MB**; default replication = **3**. - ✅ **NameNode** stores **metadata only** (NOT data). It is the **master**. - ✅ **DataNode** stores **actual blocks** and sends **heartbeats every 3 s** and **block reports every 6 hr**. - ✅ **Secondary NameNode** is a **checkpoint helper**, NOT a backup of NameNode. - ✅ HDFS **Write** uses a **pipeline replication** model (client → DN1 → DN2 → DN3 → ACK). - ✅ HDFS **Read** path: client contacts NameNode for block locations, then **reads directly from DataNode** (NameNode not in data path). - ✅ **Rack Awareness** policy: replica 1 on local node, replica 2 on different node in same rack, replica 3 on a different rack. - ✅ **MapReduce** has **Map** and **Reduce** phases; intermediate phases include **Shuffle, Sort, Combine**. - ✅ **Combiner** is a **local mini-reducer** that reduces network traffic; it must satisfy **commutativity and associativity**. - ✅ **Partitioner** decides which Reducer gets which key; default = `hash(key) mod R`. - ✅ **Map task count ≈ number of input blocks**; **Reduce task count** is user-configured (default 1). - ✅ **Mapper failure** → only that task is re-run on another node (job continues). **NameNode failure** (Hadoop 1.x) → entire cluster down (SPOF — solved by HA in Hadoop 2.x+). - ✅ **Number of blocks** for a file = $\lceil F / B \rceil$. **Total physical blocks** = that value $\times R$. - ✅ For the **WordCount** problem, be ready to trace the **intermediate key-value pairs** through Map → Combine → Shuffle → Reduce. - ✅ **Seek time budget rule**: $\dfrac{t_{seek}}{t_{seek} + t_{transfer}} \le 1\%$ — justifies the large 128 MB block size. - ✅ **HDFS is optimized for streaming reads of large files**, NOT for low-latency random reads or many small files (small files overload the NameNode's RAM). --- > **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2 (Concepts) | PBADT504 Big Data Analytics | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Clients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Clients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HBase Clients** are the set of programmatic interfaces, APIs, and gateway components that enable applications, scripts, and external systems to communicate with the HBase distributed, scalable, NoSQL database built on top of the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS). They abstract the underlying RegionServer communication, data serialization (Protobuf), and ZooKeeper-based coordination, providing developers with standardized methods to perform CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations on tables.

In the context of **Module Experiment 2** of KTU's Big Data Analytics laboratory, **Clients** refer to the multiple access mechanisms (Java API, REST/Stargate Gateway, Thrift Gateway, Avro, Shell, etc.) used to interact with an HBase cluster, as opposed to interacting with HDFS or YARN directly.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Counter" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of an HBase Cluster as a **massive central bank vault** storing trillions of records. You (an application) cannot walk into the vault directly. Instead, you approach one of several **service counters (Clients)**:
> - The **Java Counter** is the official, fastest counter for in-city bank customers.
> - The **REST Counter (Stargate)** is the online portal you use from a browser anywhere in the world using HTTP.
> - The **Thrift Counter** is a multilingual counter using a universal translator, letting Python, C++, Ruby, or PHP users all transact.
> - The **HBase Shell Counter** is the bank's relationship manager, used by admins to manually check balances.

Each client type uses the same **ZooKeeper** as the directory to find which **RegionServer** holds the data, then opens a direct socket to it.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Multiple Clients?

| Reason | Explanation |
| --- | --- |
| **Language Independence** | Not all systems are written in Java; Thrift/Avro allow polyglot access. |
| **Network Boundaries** | REST allows crossing firewalls using standard HTTP/80 or HTTPS/443. |
| **Lightweight Interaction** | Shell allows ad-hoc queries without compiling code. |
| **Batch Processing** | MapReduce/Pig/Hive clients allow bulk analytics over HBase tables. |
| **Admin Operations** | Admin client allows table creation, schema management, namespace control. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Term — ZooKeeper:** A distributed coordination service that HBase clients use to discover the location of the META table and the RegionServers managing the requested row ranges. Client never talks to the Master for data operations.

## 1.4 Experimental Context (KTU Module 2)

For the KTU **Module Experiment 2**, students typically:
1. Start the HBase master and RegionServers.
2. Connect using the **HBase Shell Client**.
3. Create tables, insert data, scan, and get rows.
4. Optionally write a **Java client** using `hbase-client` Maven dependency.
5. Test the **REST (Stargate) gateway** with `curl` commands.
6. Optionally exercise the **Thrift gateway** with a Python/Php demo.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The HBase Client Communication Architecture

The HBase client performs a **three-step lookup** before reading or writing data. This is the most frequently examined theoretical concept in KTU exams.

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **Bootstrap Configuration** — The client loads `hbase-site.xml` (or programmatically passes `HBaseConfiguration.create()`).
2. **ZooKeeper Query** — Client contacts the ZooKeeper ensemble (default port `2181`) and asks: *"Where is the `-ROOT-` (now `hbase:meta`) region?"*.
3. **Meta Table Lookup** — Client reads the `hbase:meta` table to find the **RegionServer** hosting the relevant region for the requested row key range.
4. **Direct RPC** — Client establishes a direct Protobuf/RPC connection to the target RegionServer and performs `Get`, `Put`, `Delete`, or `Scan`.

> [!NOTE]
> The Master node is **NOT** involved in the read/write data path. It is used only for DDL operations (create, delete, alter table) via the **Admin** client.

## 2.2 Types of HBase Clients — Detailed Taxonomy

### A. Java Client (Native API)

- **Package:** `org.apache.hadoop.hbase.client`
- **Core Classes:**
  - `ConnectionFactory` — Creates connections (thread-safe, expensive).
  - `Connection` — Represents a cluster connection.
  - `Table` — Represents a single table (NOT thread-safe, lightweight).
  - `Admin` — DDL operations (create table, list, drop, enable/disable).
  - `Get`, `Put`, `Delete`, `Scan`, `Increment`, `Append` — DML operations.
  - `Result`, `ResultScanner` — Read result objects.
- **Dependency:** `hbase-client` (Maven) or `hbase-*.jar` (classpath).
- **Advantages:** Highest performance, full feature set, type safety.

### B. HBase Shell Client (JRuby IRB)

- **Command:** `hbase shell`
- **Internals:** Built on **JRuby** (JVM implementation of Ruby).
- **Use Case:** Quick prototyping, admin work, learning.
- **Example Commands:**
  ```
  create 'student', 'info', 'marks'
  put 'student', 'row1', 'info:name', 'Anu'
  get 'student', 'row1'
  scan 'student'
  ```

### C. REST Client — Stargate Gateway

- **Implementation:** `org.apache.hadoop.hbase.rest` (Jetty-based servlet).
- **Default Port:** **8080** (configurable via `hbase.rest.port`).
- **Started by:** `hbase-daemon.sh start rest` (deprecated in newer versions; replaced by a separate REST server module).
- **Protocol:** HTTP/1.1 with JSON or Protobuf payloads.
- **HTTP Method Mapping:**
  - `GET /<table>/<row>` → Read
  - `PUT /<table>/<row>` → Insert
  - `POST /<table>/<row>` → Update
  - `DELETE /<table>/<row>` → Delete
  - `GET /<table>/<scanner>` → Scan
- **Use Case:** Cross-platform, cross-language, internet-friendly access.

### D. Thrift Client

- **Implementation:** `org.apache.hadoop.hbase.thrift` (Apache Thrift).
- **Default Port:** **9090**.
- **Started by:** `hbase-daemon.sh start thrift`.
- **Supported Languages:** C++, Java, Python, PHP, Ruby, Erlang, Perl, Haskell, Node.js.
- **Architecture:** Thrift IDL (Interface Definition Language) generates stubs for every language.

### E. Avro Client

- Similar to Thrift but based on Apache Avro. Less commonly used in newer deployments.

### F. MapReduce / Bulk Clients

- `TableInputFormat` and `TableOutputFormat` allow MapReduce jobs to read from and write to HBase tables.

### G. Higher-Level SQL Clients

- **Apache Phoenix** — Adds a JDBC/SQL layer on top of HBase.
- **Hive HBase Handler** — Allows HiveQL queries on HBase tables.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **exact port numbers, class names, and configuration keys** that are frequently asked in KTU 2024 Scheme exams.

| Client Type | Default Port | Starter Command | Key Class / Endpoint | Configuration Property |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| HBase Shell | N/A (local IRB) | `hbase shell` | `HBaseShell` (JRuby) | `hbase.zookeeper.quorum` |
| Java API | Direct RPC to RS | N/A (programmatic) | `Connection`, `Table`, `Admin` | `hbase.zookeeper.property.clientPort` |
| REST (Stargate) | **8080** | `hbase-daemon.sh start rest` | `/<table>/<row>` | `hbase.rest.port` |
| Thrift | **9090** | `hbase-daemon.sh start thrift` | `THBaseService` | `hbase.thrift.port` |
| Avro | 9090 (similar) | (legacy) | — | — |
| ZooKeeper | **2181** | part of ZK ensemble | `ZKClient` | `hbase.zookeeper.quorum` |
| Master Web UI | **16010** | part of HBase Master | `HMaster` web | `hbase.master.info.port` |
| RegionServer Web UI | **16030** | part of RS | `HRegionServer` web | `hbase.regionserver.info.port` |

## 2.4 Java Client — Code Skeleton (High-Yield for KTU Lab Exam)

```java
import org.apache.hadoop.conf.Configuration;
import org.apache.hadoop.hbase.*;
import org.apache.hadoop.hbase.client.*;
import org.apache.hadoop.hbase.util.Bytes;

public class HBaseClientDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        // 1. Configuration
        Configuration conf = HBaseConfiguration.create();
        conf.set("hbase.zookeeper.quorum", "localhost");
        conf.set("hbase.zookeeper.property.clientPort", "2181");

        // 2. Open Connection (heavy; one per JVM)
        try (Connection conn = ConnectionFactory.createConnection(conf);
             Admin admin = conn.getAdmin()) {

            // 3. DDL — Create table
            TableDescriptor td = TableDescriptorBuilder
                    .newBuilder(TableName.valueOf("student"))
                    .setColumnFamily(ColumnFamilyDescriptorBuilder
                            .newBuilder(Bytes.toBytes("info")).build())
                    .build();
            if (!admin.tableExists(td.getTableName()))
                admin.createTable(td);

            // 4. DML — Put
            try (Table table = conn.getTable(TableName.valueOf("student"))) {
                Put p = new Put(Bytes.toBytes("row1"));
                p.addColumn(Bytes.toBytes("info"),
                            Bytes.toBytes("name"),
                            Bytes.toBytes("Anu"));
                table.put(p);

                // 5. DML — Get
                Get g = new Get(Bytes.toBytes("row1"));
                Result r = table.get(g);
                System.out.println("Name: "
                        + Bytes.toString(r.getValue(
                            Bytes.toBytes("info"),
                            Bytes.toBytes("name"))));
            }
        }
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern API Note (HBase 2.x+):** `HTable`, `HBaseAdmin`, `HConnection` are **deprecated**. Always use `ConnectionFactory`, `Table`, `Admin`, and `TableDescriptorBuilder`. This is a frequent KTU 2024 question.

## 2.5 REST Client — Curl Example (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU lab exam, the REST client is usually demonstrated using `curl` against the Stargate gateway on port **8080**.

```bash
# Create table schema (XML or Protobuf)
curl -X PUT \
  'http://localhost:8080/student/schema' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"name":"student","ColumnSchema":[{"name":"info"}]}'

# Insert a row
curl -X PUT \
  'http://localhost:8080/student/row1/info:name' \
  -H 'Content-Type: text/plain' \
  -d 'Anu'

# Read a row
curl -X GET 'http://localhost:8080/student/row1'

# Scan a table
curl -X PUT 'http://localhost:8080/student/scanner' -d '{}'
curl -X GET 'http://localhost:8080/student/scanner/<scannerId>'
curl -X DELETE 'http://localhost:8080/student/scanner/<scannerId>'
```

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case | Client Used |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Online Fraud Detection** | Real-time lookups on transaction history | Java API (low latency) |
| **Mobile Banking App** | Cross-platform HTTP access from iOS/Android | REST Stargate |
| **Data Science / ETL** | Bulk analytics on customer events | MapReduce / Hive |
| **Microservices in Polyglot Stack** | Mixed Java + Python + Node.js services | Thrift |
| **Ad-hoc DBA Work** | Quick schema inspection | HBase Shell |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Big Data Analytics is not heavily mathematical, so this section focuses on the **analytical derivations of the client-server communication logic, the scan-region algorithm, and the request/response format mappings** that are commonly asked in KTU theory exams.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Client Lookup Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **3-step client lookup** that must be memorized. It is the most asked KTU question on this topic.

### Given

- A client `C` wants to perform an operation on table `T`, row key `K`.
- HBase cluster has one Master `M`, `n` RegionServers `{RS_1, RS_2, ..., RS_n}`.
- ZooKeeper ensemble `Z` holds the address of the `hbase:meta` table.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — Configuration Phase**

$$C \leftarrow \text{load}(hbase\text{-}site.xml)$$
$$\text{quorum} \leftarrow \text{read}(hbase.zookeeper.quorum)$$
$$\text{port} \leftarrow \text{read}(hbase.zookeeper.property.clientPort)$$

The client reads the **ZooKeeper quorum** and **port** to bootstrap its directory lookup. No Master contact is needed here.

**Step 2 — ZooKeeper Query (META Location)**

$$C \xrightarrow{\text{GetChildren}} Z: \texttt{/hbase/meta-region-server}$$
$$Z \rightarrow C: \langle RS_{\text{meta}}, \text{hostname}, \text{port} \rangle$$

The client asks ZooKeeper: *"Which RegionServer hosts the `hbase:meta` region?"*. ZooKeeper responds with a hostname and port. ZooKeeper acts as a **lightweight directory**, not a data store.

**Step 3 — Meta Table Scan (Region Routing)**

$$C \rightarrow RS_{\text{meta}}: \text{scan}(\texttt{hbase:meta}, \text{rowkey range } [K, K_{\text{next}})$$
$$RS_{\text{meta}} \rightarrow C: \langle RS_{\text{target}}, \text{startKey}, \text{endKey}, \text{regionName} \rangle$$

The client scans the `hbase:meta` table to find the **RegionServer** that hosts the specific region covering row `K`. The META table is essentially a `key → region` directory.

**Step 4 — Direct RPC to RegionServer**

$$C \rightarrow RS_{\text{target}}: \text{ProtobufRPC}(Get \vert Put \vert Scan, T, K)$$
$$RS_{\text{target}} \rightarrow C: \text{Protobuf}(\text{Result} \vert \text{Ack})$$

The client opens a **direct Protobuf RPC** to `RS_target` and performs the operation. From Step 2 onward, the client **caches** the region location locally, so subsequent operations on the same region skip Steps 1–2 until the cache is invalidated.

### Final Compact Algorithm

$$\text{Client}(K) = Z.\text{getMetaRS}() \rightarrow \text{META}.\text{scan}(K) \rightarrow RS_{\text{target}}.\text{exec}(K)$$

### Caching Invariant

$$\text{Cache}_{C} = \{ \text{regionName} \rightarrow RS_{\text{addr}} \}$$
$$\text{TTL or invalidation triggered by } RS \text{ moving region}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why caching matters:** If the client had to do all 3 steps on every operation, the Meta table would become a bottleneck. Clients cache region locations and only re-lookup when they get a `RegionMovedException` or after a TTL expires.

## 3.2 Derivation of REST URL Pattern (HTTP → HBase Operation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 frequently asks students to map HTTP methods to HBase operations. The derivation below shows exactly how a REST URL is parsed.

### Mapping Table Derivation

Let a request be defined by the tuple $R = \langle \text{method}, \text{URL}, \text{body} \rangle$. The Stargate gateway parses the URL pattern as:

$$URL = \texttt{/}\langle \text{namespace} \rangle \texttt{/}\langle \text{table} \rangle \texttt{/}\langle \text{rowkey} \rangle \texttt{/}\langle \text{family} \rangle \texttt{:}\langle \text{qualifier} \rangle \texttt{/}\langle \text{ts} \rangle$$

### Mapping Rules

| HTTP Method | URL Pattern | HBase Operation | Body |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `GET` | `/<table>/<row>` | `Get` | — |
| `GET` | `/<table>/<row>/<family>:<qualifier>` | `Get` (single cell) | — |
| `GET` | `/<table>/*` | `Scan` (with optional filter) | — |
| `PUT` | `/<table>/<row>/<family>:<qualifier>` | `Put` (new cell) | cell value |
| `PUT` | `/<table>/<row>/<family>:<qualifier>/<ts>` | `Put` (with timestamp) | cell value |
| `POST` | `/<table>/<row>/<family>:<qualifier>` | `Put` (update, optional check) | cell value |
| `DELETE` | `/<table>/<row>` | `Delete` row | — |
| `DELETE` | `/<table>/<row>/<family>:<qualifier>` | `Delete` cell | — |
| `PUT` | `/<table>/schema` | `CreateTable` | schema JSON/XML |
| `DELETE` | `/<table>/schema` | `DropTable` | — |
| `POST` | `/<table>/scanner` | Open `Scan` | filter JSON |
| `GET` | `/<table>/scanner/<id>` | Read next batch | — |
| `DELETE` | `/<table>/scanner/<id>` | Close scanner | — |

### Worked Example Derivation

**Problem:** A client sends `PUT /student/row1/info:name` with body `Anu`.

**Step 1 — Parse URL**

$$\text{resource} = (\text{table}=\text{student}, \text{row}=\text{row1}, \text{family}=\text{info}, \text{qualifier}=\text{name})$$

**Step 2 — Method Dispatch**

$$\text{method} = \text{PUT} \rightarrow \text{operation} = \text{createCell}$$

**Step 3 — Construct Protobuf Put**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Put}(p) &= \{\\
&\quad \text{row} : \text{bytes}(\text{"row1"})\\
&\quad \text{column} : \text{bytes}(\text{"info:name"})\\
&\quad \text{value} : \text{bytes}(\text{"Anu"})\\
&\quad \text{timestamp} : \text{System.currentTimeMillis()}\\
\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Forward to Java Client Internally**

The REST gateway internally instantiates a Java `Table` object and calls `table.put(p)`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The REST gateway itself is a **Java client** behind the scenes. It is a thin HTTP-to-Java-API translator. Similarly, the Thrift gateway is a Thrift-server-to-Java-client translator.

## 3.3 Derivation of Connection Pool Sizing for Java Client

This is a common KTU question on **how many `Table` and `Connection` objects to create**.

### Formula

$$N_{\text{Connection}} = 1 \text{ per JVM process (thread-safe)}$$
$$N_{\text{Table}} = 1 \text{ per concurrent thread (NOT thread-safe)}$$
$$N_{\text{Admin}} = 1 \text{ per JVM process (thread-safe)}$$

### Reasoning

- A `Connection` holds the **ZooKeeper session**, **RPC channel**, and **region cache**. It is expensive (RPC handshake, heartbeat) and thread-safe.
- A `Table` holds **no RPC channel** of its own; it borrows from the parent `Connection`. It is **lightweight** but **not thread-safe** because it stores per-call state.
- An `Admin` is similar to a `Connection` wrapper for DDL.

### Memory Footprint Estimate

$$M_{\text{Connection}} \approx 64 \text{ MB (cache + ZK session)}$$
$$M_{\text{Table}} \approx 32 \text{ KB}$$

So the pattern is:

$$\text{ThreadPool} \rightarrow N_{\text{Table}} \rightarrow 1 \text{ shared Connection} \rightarrow \text{RegionServer cluster}$$

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Java vs REST vs Thrift

| Property | Java Client | REST (Stargate) | Thrift |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Performance | **Fastest** (no serialization hop) | Slower (HTTP + JSON/XML) | Fast (binary protocol) |
| Language Support | Java only | **Any** (HTTP) | **Any** with Thrift codegen |
| Firewall Friendly | No (custom RPC) | **Yes** (HTTP/80/443) | No (custom binary) |
| Full Feature Set | **Yes** | Subset | Subset |
| Setup Effort | Maven dependency | Start `rest` daemon | Start `thrift` daemon + generate stubs |
| Use in Production | Backend services | Web/Mobile frontends | Polyglot microservices |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
