# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a unit vector. Find the unit vector in the direction of $\vec{v} = (3, 4, 12)$.**

**Model Answer:**

A **unit vector** is a vector whose magnitude is exactly equal to **1**. It represents *pure direction* without any magnitude component.

**Step 1:** Compute the magnitude of $\vec{v}$:

$$\|\vec{v}\| = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13$$

**Step 2:** Divide $\vec{v}$ by its magnitude to get the unit vector:

$$\hat{v} = \frac{\vec{v}}{\|\vec{v}\|} = \frac{1}{13}(3, 4, 12) = \left(\frac{3}{13}, \frac{4}{13}, \frac{12}{13}\right)$$

**Verification:** 

$$\|\hat{v}\| = \sqrt{\left(\tfrac{3}{13}\right)^2 + \left(\tfrac{4}{13}\right)^2 + \left(\tfrac{12}{13}\right)^2} = \frac{1}{13}\sqrt{9+16+144} = \frac{13}{13} = 1 \checkmark$$

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the conditions for two vectors to be (a) parallel and (b) orthogonal. Express both in terms of dot product and cross product.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Parallel Vectors:** Two non-zero vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are **parallel** (collinear) if and only if their **cross product is zero**:

$$\vec{a} \parallel \vec{b} \iff \vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \vec{0}$$

Equivalently, using dot product: $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = \pm\|\vec{a}\|\|\vec{b}\|$ (i.e., $\cos\theta = \pm 1$).

**(b) Orthogonal Vectors:** Two non-zero vectors are **orthogonal** (perpendicular) if and only if their **dot product is zero**:

$$\vec{a} \perp \vec{b} \iff \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 0$$

This corresponds to $\cos\theta = 0$, meaning the angle between them is exactly $90°$.

**Summary Table:**

| Condition | Dot Product | Cross Product |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| Parallel | $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = \pm\|\vec{a}\|\|\vec{b}\|$ | $\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \vec{0}$ |
| Orthogonal | $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 0$ | $\|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}\| = \|\vec{a}\|\|\vec{b}\|$ |

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Choice Pattern:** Each Part B question contains sub-parts (a) worth **7 marks** and (b) worth **7 marks**. Both sub-parts must be answered completely. The student chooses **one** of the two alternatives.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Given two vectors $\vec{a} = (1, 2, 3)$ and $\vec{b} = (4, -1, 2)$:**

**(a)** Compute the dot product $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}$ and find the angle between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the cross product $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$ and verify that it is perpendicular to both $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$. Also find the unit vector in the direction of $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A

**Part (a) — Dot Product and Angle [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Compute the dot product using component form:**

$$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = (1)(4) + (2)(-1) + (3)(2) = 4 - 2 + 6 = 8$$

**Step 2: Compute the magnitudes:**

$$\|\vec{a}\| = \sqrt{1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4 + 9} = \sqrt{14}$$

$$\|\vec{b}\| = \sqrt{4^2 + (-1)^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{16 + 1 + 4} = \sqrt{21}$$

**Step 3: Apply the cosine formula:**

$$\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{\|\vec{a}\| \cdot \|\vec{b}\|} = \frac{8}{\sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{21}} = \frac{8}{\sqrt{294}}$$

Simplify $\sqrt{294} = \sqrt{49 \times 6} = 7\sqrt{6}$:

$$\cos\theta = \frac{8}{7\sqrt{6}} = \frac{8}{7\sqrt{6}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\sqrt{6}} = \frac{8\sqrt{6}}{42} = \frac{4\sqrt{6}}{21}$$

**Step 4: Compute the angle:**

$$\cos\theta = \frac{4\sqrt{6}}{21} \approx \frac{4 \times 2.449}{21} \approx 0.4665$$

$$\theta = \arccos(0.4665) \approx 62.18°$$

The angle is acute, confirming $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} > 0$ implies angle less than $90°$.

---

**Part (b) — Cross Product and Verification [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Compute the cross product using determinant form:**

$$\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & -1 & 2 \end{vmatrix}$$

$$\hat{i} \text{ component: } (2)(2) - (3)(-1) = 4 + 3 = 7$$

$$\hat{j} \text{ component: } -[(1)(2) - (3)(4)] = -[2 - 12] = -(-10) = 10$$

$$\hat{k} \text{ component: } (1)(-1) - (2)(4) = -1 - 8 = -9$$

$$\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = 7\hat{i} + 10\hat{j} - 9\hat{k} = (7, 10, -9)$$

**Step 2: Verify perpendicularity to $\vec{a}$:**

$$\vec{a} \cdot (\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) = (1)(7) + (2)(10) + (3)(-9) = 7 + 20 - 27 = 0 \checkmark$$

This confirms $\vec{a} \perp (\vec{a} \times \vec{b})$.

**Step 3: Verify perpendicularity to $\vec{b}$:**

$$\vec{b} \cdot (\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) = (4)(7) + (-1)(10) + (2)(-9) = 28 - 10 - 18 = 0 \checkmark$$

This confirms $\vec{b} \perp (\vec{a} \times \vec{b})$.

**Step 4: Compute the magnitude of the cross product:**

$$\|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}\| = \sqrt{7^2 + 10^2 + (-9)^2} = \sqrt{49 + 100 + 81} = \sqrt{230}$$

**Step 5: Compute the unit vector in the direction of $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$:**

$$\widehat{\vec{a} \times \vec{b}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{230}}(7, 10, -9) = \left(\frac{7}{\sqrt{230}}, \frac{10}{\sqrt{230}}, \frac{-9}{\sqrt{230}}\right)$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Two data points in a Big Analytics feature space are represented as vectors $\vec{p} = (2, 3, -1, 4)$ and $\vec{q} = (1, -2, 5, 0)$.**

**(a)** Compute the **Euclidean distance** between $\vec{p}$ and $\vec{q}$, and the **cosine similarity** between them. Comment on whether these two data points are "close" or "similar". **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **vector projection** of $\vec{p}$ onto $\vec{q}$ and interpret its meaning in the context of dimensionality reduction. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B

**Part (a) — Euclidean Distance and Cosine Similarity [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Compute the difference vector $\vec{p} - \vec{q}$:**

$$\vec{p} - \vec{q} = (2-1,\ 3-(-2),\ -1-5,\ 4-0) = (1, 5, -6, 4)$$

**Step 2: Apply the Euclidean distance formula (L2 norm of difference):**

$$d(\vec{p}, \vec{q}) = \|\vec{p} - \vec{q}\| = \sqrt{1^2 + 5^2 + (-6)^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{1 + 25 + 36 + 16} = \sqrt{78}$$

$$d(\vec{p}, \vec{q}) = \sqrt{78} \approx 8.832$$

**Step 3: Compute the dot product $\vec{p} \cdot \vec{q}$:**

$$\vec{p} \cdot \vec{q} = (2)(1) + (3)(-2) + (-1)(5) + (4)(0) = 2 - 6 - 5 + 0 = -9$$

**Step 4: Compute individual magnitudes:**

$$\|\vec{p}\| = \sqrt{2^2 + 3^2 + (-1)^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9 + 1 + 16} = \sqrt{30}$$

$$\|\vec{q}\| = \sqrt{1^2 + (-2)^2 + 5^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4 + 25 + 0} = \sqrt{30}$$

**Step 5: Compute the cosine similarity:**

$$\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \vec{q}}{\|\vec{p}\| \cdot \|\vec{q}\|} = \frac{-9}{\sqrt{30} \cdot \sqrt{30}} = \frac{-9}{30} = -0.3$$

**Step 6: Interpretation:**

- The Euclidean distance is $\sqrt{78} \approx 8.83$, indicating the points are **moderately far apart** in the 4D feature space.
- The cosine similarity is $-0.3$, which is **negative** and **far from +1**, indicating the two vectors point in **opposing directions** in the feature space.
- Despite having equal magnitudes ($\sqrt{30}$ each), the vectors are **not similar** — they have low cosine similarity, which means they would not be clustered together in similarity-based analytics (e.g., document clustering, KNN).

**Big Data Insight:** This demonstrates the **fundamental difference between distance and similarity metrics**. Two vectors can have equal magnitudes yet be highly dissimilar. Cosine similarity captures **directional** alignment, while Euclidean distance captures **positional** closeness.

---

**Part (b) — Vector Projection [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Recall the projection formula:**

$$\text{proj}_{\vec{q}}\vec{p} = \left(\frac{\vec{p} \cdot \vec{q}}{\|\vec{q}\|^2}\right)\vec{q}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values computed in Part (a):**

- $\vec{p} \cdot \vec{q} = -9$
- $\|\vec{q}\|^2 = 30$

$$\text{proj}_{\vec{q}}\vec{p} = \left(\frac{-9}{30}\right)\vec{q} = -0.3 \cdot (1, -2, 5, 0)$$

**Step 3: Compute the projection vector:**

$$\text{proj}_{\vec{q}}\vec{p} = (-0.3 \cdot 1,\ -0.3 \cdot (-2),\ -0.3 \cdot 5,\ -0.3 \cdot 0)$$

$$= (-0.3, 0.6, -1.5, 0)$$

**Step 4: Interpretation in Dimensionality Reduction Context:**

In **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**, we project high-dimensional data onto a lower-dimensional subspace spanned by principal components (eigenvectors). The projection:

- **Sign $(-)$** confirms $\vec{p}$ and $\vec{q}$ are anti-aligned (angle $> 90°$), which is consistent with our earlier cosine similarity of $-0.3$.
- **Magnitude of projection** = $\left|\frac{\vec{p} \cdot \vec{q}}{\|\vec{q}\|}\right| = \frac{9}{\sqrt{30}} \approx 1.643$ — this is the *scalar component* of $\vec{p}$ along $\vec{q}$.

**Big Data Meaning:** If $\vec{q}$ were a principal component direction (an eigenvector of the covariance matrix), then $\text{proj}_{\vec{q}}\vec{p}$ represents the *reduced-dimension representation* of data point $\vec{p}$. By projecting all data points onto the top-$k$ principal components, we reduce dimensionality from $n$ to $k$ dimensions while preserving maximum variance.

**Step 5: Scalar (Component) Projection:**

$$\text{comp}_{\vec{q}}\vec{p} = \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \vec{q}}{\|\vec{q}\|} = \frac{-9}{\sqrt{30}} = \frac{-9\sqrt{30}}{30} = \frac{-3\sqrt{30}}{10} \approx -1.643$$

The negative scalar confirms the component is in the **opposite direction** of $\vec{q}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Cross Product:** The $\hat{j}$ component has a **negative sign** in the determinant expansion. Students frequently forget this and write $+(a_1 b_3 - a_3 b_1)$ instead of $-(a_1 b_3 - a_3 b_1)$. This is a **2-mark deduction** in standard valuation.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\|\vec{a}\|^2$ with $\|\vec{a}\|$:** The formula $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a} = \|\vec{a}\|^2$ (squared magnitude) is often misapplied as $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a} = \|\vec{a}\|$. Always take the **square root** at the end.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Normalize in Cosine Similarity:** Computing $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}$ alone gives the dot product, **not** the cosine similarity. The denominator $\|\vec{a}\|\|\vec{b}\|$ is mandatory. Missing this loses **3 marks**.
>
> 4. **Unit Vector Computation:** Many students compute $\|\vec{v}\|$ but then forget to **divide** $\vec{v}$ by it. The unit vector is $\hat{v} = \vec{v} / \|\vec{v}\|$, not just $\|\vec{v}\|$.
>
> 5. **Cross Product Applied to 2D Vectors:** The cross product is **defined only in 3D** (and 7D). If a 2D problem asks for cross product, embed the vectors in 3D by setting the $z$-component to 0.
>
> 6. **Angle in Degrees vs Radians:** KTU answers must specify units. If computing $\arccos$, state the result in **degrees** unless otherwise specified.
>
> 7. **Skipping Verification Steps:** Examiners reward verification. Always show $\vec{a} \cdot (\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) = 0$ as a perpendicularity check — it earns **partial marks** even if the main computation has a minor error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 📌 Rapid Revision Checklist

**Foundational Concepts:**
- ✅ A vector has both **magnitude** AND **direction**; a scalar has only magnitude
- ✅ Vectors are represented as ordered tuples in $\mathbb{R}^n$ or as arrows in geometric space
- ✅ Two vectors are **equal** if they have the same magnitude and direction (position-independent)
- ✅ The **zero vector** $\vec{0}$ has all components zero and is directionless

**Magnitude and Unit Vector:**
- ✅ Magnitude formula: $\|\vec{a}\| = \sqrt{\sum a_i^2}$ (L2 norm)
- ✅ Unit vector: $\hat{a} = \vec{a} / \|\vec{a}\|$ — has magnitude exactly 1
- ✅ Standard basis: $\hat{i} = (1,0,0)$, $\hat{j} = (0,1,0)$, $\hat{k} = (0,0,1)$

**Dot Product Essentials:**
- ✅ Algebraic form: $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = \sum a_i b_i$ (scalar result)
- ✅ Geometric form: $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = \|\vec{a}\|\|\vec{b}\|\cos\theta$
- ✅ **Orthogonality:** $\vec{a} \perp \vec{b} \iff \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 0$
- ✅ Self-dot: $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a} = \|\vec{a}\|^2$

**Cross Product Essentials:**
- ✅ Only defined in **3D** (and 7D), produces a **vector**
- ✅ Determinant expansion with the cyclic mnemonic
- ✅ $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$ is perpendicular to both $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$
- ✅ Anti-commutative: $\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = -(\vec{b} \times \vec{a})$
- ✅ **Parallel condition:** $\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \vec{0}$

**Big Data Connections to Remember:**
- ✅ **Cosine similarity** = $\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{\|\vec{a}\|\|\vec{b}\|}$ → NLP, recommendation systems
- ✅ **Euclidean distance** = $\|\vec{a} - \vec{b}\|$ → KNN, K-Means
- ✅ **Vector projection** → PCA, dimensionality reduction
- ✅ **Feature vectors** in $\mathbb{R}^n$ are the universal data representation in ML

**Formulas to Memorize for KTU:**
- ✅ $\|\vec{a}\|$, $\hat{a}$, $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}$, $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$, $\cos\theta$, projection, distance

**Exam Strategy Reminders:**
- ✅ Always state the **formula** before substituting values (earns 1 mark)
- ✅ Verify perpendicularity using $\vec{a} \cdot (\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) = 0$
- ✅ Use **degrees** for angles unless radians are explicitly required
- ✅ Draw diagrams for geometric problems (parallelogram law, triangle law)
- ✅ Simplify radicals (e.g., $\sqrt{294} = 7\sqrt{6}$)

# 🔍 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Big Data Analytics (BDA)** and distributed computing frameworks such as **Apache Spark**, **Apache Hadoop MapReduce**, and **Apache Pig/Hive**, **Filtering** is formally defined as a **narrow transformation** (or *data refinement operation*) that evaluates each incoming record against a user-defined **predicate function** $f: D \rightarrow \{True, False\}$ and retains only those records for which the predicate evaluates to **True**, discarding the rest.

Mathematically, for an input dataset $D$ containing $n$ records, the filter operation produces an output dataset $D'$ such that:

$$D' = \{x \in D \mid f(x) = \text{True}\}$$

Where:
- $D$ = Input Dataset (RDD, DataFrame, or Data collection)
- $f(x)$ = User-defined Boolean predicate
- $D' \subseteq D$ (Output is always a subset of the input)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Filtering is a **transformation** in Spark's lazy evaluation model. It does **not** immediately compute results; instead, it builds a **Lineage Graph (DAG)** that is executed only when an **Action** (like `count()`, `collect()`, `saveAsTextFile()`) is invoked.

> [!NOTE]
> In **Spark RDD API**, the filter transformation signature is:
> `def filter(f: T => Boolean): RDD[T]`
> In **Spark DataFrame API**, both `filter()` and `where()` (SQL-style) are supported.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡

Imagine you have a **large basket of mixed fruits** 🍎🍌🍇🥭 — apples, bananas, grapes, and mangoes. You want to keep **only the red, ripe apples** for a recipe. What do you do?

1. You **inspect each fruit one-by-one** (this is the *iteration over partitions*).
2. You **apply a rule** — "Is it red? Is it an apple?" (this is the *predicate function*).
3. You **separate** the matching fruits into a new basket and **discard** the rest (this is the *output RDD/DataFrame*).

This is **exactly** what a Filter operation does in Big Data — it sifts through terabytes of data and keeps only what matches your criteria. The "basket" here is distributed across hundreds of cluster nodes, but the principle remains identical.

| Real-World Concept | Big Data Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Mixed fruit basket | Input RDD / DataFrame |
| Inspection rule ("red apple?") | Boolean predicate $f(x)$ |
| Selection of matching fruits | Filtered output RDD |
| Discarded fruits | Records eliminated by filter |
| New basket | Persisted result (after Action) |

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Expected Definitions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Must-Know KTU Board Keywords:**
> - **Predicate Function**: A function returning a Boolean value (True/False) used to test each element.
> - **Narrow Transformation**: A transformation where each parent partition contributes to at most **one** child partition (no shuffle required). Filter is a **narrow** transformation. ✅
> - **Lazy Evaluation**: Spark delays execution until an action triggers the DAG.
> - **Lineage**: The sequence of transformations that produced an RDD, used for fault tolerance.
> - **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)**: The execution plan Spark constructs from transformations.
> - **Partition**: A logical chunk of the distributed dataset.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

In the context of Spark filtering, the following **default metrics** are essential for KTU exams:

| Metric | Default Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Default Parallelism | **2** (local), all cores (cluster) | Number of partitions in filtered output |
| Executor Memory | **1 GB** (default) | Memory for cached filtered RDDs |
| Shuffle Partitions | **200** (default `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`) | Only for wide transformations |
| Lazy Evaluation | **Enabled by default** | Transformations are deferred |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ⚡ **Filter is a NARROW transformation** — it does **NOT** trigger a **shuffle** across the network. This is a **frequently asked 3-mark question** in KTU exams!

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow of a Filter Operation

The filter operation in Spark follows a precise, well-defined workflow. Below is the structured logic broken into discrete steps:

### **Step 1: RDD/DataFrame Creation**
- An input RDD is created from an external source (HDFS, S3, local file, or `parallelize()`).
- Spark automatically partitions the data across the cluster nodes.

### **Step 2: Predicate Definition**
- The user defines a Boolean function $f(x)$ that returns either `True` or `False`.
- Example: $f(x) = (x.age \geq 18)$ or $f(x) = (x.salary > 50000)$.

### **Step 3: Lazy Transformation Registration**
- The `filter()` transformation is called.
- Spark **does not execute** the filter immediately.
- A new RDD is logically created and added to the **DAG**.

### **Step 4: DAG Construction (Lineage)**
- Spark records: `inputRDD → filter → newRDD`.
- This lineage enables **fault tolerance** — lost partitions can be recomputed.

### **Step 5: Action Trigger**
- An action like `collect()`, `count()`, or `saveAsTextFile()` is invoked.
- Spark now **materializes** the DAG and executes the filter on each partition.

### **Step 6: Parallel Execution**
- Each executor applies $f(x)$ to its local partition records **independently** (in parallel).
- No network shuffle is required.

### **Step 7: Result Return**
- Matching records are returned to the driver (for `collect()`) or written to storage.

---

## 2.2 Types of Filtering in Spark

Spark supports filtering at **three distinct API levels**, each of which is examinable in KTU:

### **A. RDD-Level Filter (Functional API)**
Uses a lambda or anonymous function as the predicate.

```scala
val filteredRDD = inputRDD.filter(x => x > 10)
```

### **B. DataFrame Filter (Column Expressions)**
Uses `filter()` or `where()` with column expressions.

```scala
val df2 = df.filter($"age" > 18)
val df3 = df.where($"salary" >= 50000)
```

### **C. SQL-Style Filter**
Registers a temp view and uses `WHERE` clause via Spark SQL.

```scala
df.createOrReplaceTempView("people")
val result = spark.sql("SELECT * FROM people WHERE age > 18")
```

---

## 2.3 Filter with Multiple Conditions (Compound Predicates)

Complex filtering uses **logical operators**:

| Operator | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `&&` / `AND` | Logical AND | `age > 18 && salary > 50000` |
| `\|\|` / `OR` | Logical OR | `dept == "IT" \|\| dept == "HR"` |
| `!` / `NOT` | Logical NOT | `!(status == "Inactive")` |

Boolean Algebra representation of compound predicates:

$$f_{compound}(x) = f_1(x) \land f_2(x) \lor \lnot f_3(x)$$

This simplifies via De Morgan's Laws:

$$\lnot(A \land B) \equiv \lnot A \lor \lnot B$$

$$\lnot(A \lor B) \equiv \lnot A \land \lnot B$$

---

## 2.6 Filter vs. Other Transformations

| Feature | `filter()` | `map()` | `distinct()` | `union()` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output size | $\leq$ Input | Same as input | $\leq$ Input | Sum of inputs |
| Shuffle required? | **No** | **No** | **Yes** (Wide) | **No** |
| Type preserved? | Yes | No (can change) | Yes | Yes |
| Returns | `RDD[T]` | `RDD[U]` | `RDD[T]` | `RDD[T]` |
| Use case | Selection | Transformation | Deduplication | Merging |

---

## 2.7 Internal Execution: How Spark Executes Filter Internally

When a filter is triggered by an action, Spark's execution engine follows this micro-flow:

1. **Stage Division**: The DAG scheduler divides the lineage into stages at shuffle boundaries. Since filter is narrow, it stays within the same stage.
2. **Task Scheduling**: One task is created per partition of the input RDD.
3. **Predicate Application**: Each task iterates over its partition's records and applies $f(x)$.
4. **Record Retention**: Matching records are kept; non-matching records are discarded.
5. **Output Materialization**: The resulting filtered records form the output partition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 🎯 **Board Exam Tip**: Always mention that filter is a **lazy**, **narrow**, **stateless**, and **type-preserving** transformation in your answers to score full marks.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Filter Execution on a Sample Dataset

Let us consider a concrete example to deeply understand how filtering works in Spark.

### **Sample Input Dataset (Hypothetical Sensor Readings)**

| Record ID | Sensor | Temperature (°C) | Humidity (%) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | S-A | 25 | 60 | OK |
| R2 | S-B | 85 | 30 | ALERT |
| R3 | S-A | 90 | 25 | ALERT |
| R4 | S-C | 22 | 70 | OK |
| R5 | S-B | 95 | 20 | ALERT |
| R6 | S-A | 28 | 65 | OK |

Total records: $n = 6$, distributed across **2 partitions**:

- **Partition P1**: R1, R2, R3
- **Partition P2**: R4, R5, R6

---

### **Predicate Function Definition**

We want to filter all records where:
$$f(x) = (x.temperature > 80) \land (x.status == "ALERT")$$

This is a **compound predicate** combining:
- $f_1(x) = (x.temperature > 80)$
- $f_2(x) = (x.status == "ALERT")$
- $f(x) = f_1(x) \land f_2(x)$

---

### **Step-by-Step Evaluation on Partition P1**

For each record in P1, we evaluate $f(x)$:

| Record | Temperature | Status | $f_1$ (Temp > 80?) | $f_2$ (Status==ALERT?) | $f = f_1 \land f_2$ | Kept? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | 25 | OK | False | False | False | ❌ Discarded |
| R2 | 85 | ALERT | True | True | **True** | ✅ Retained |
| R3 | 90 | ALERT | True | True | **True** | ✅ Retained |

**P1 Output**: $\{R2, R3\}$

---

### **Step-by-Step Evaluation on Partition P2**

| Record | Temperature | Status | $f_1$ | $f_2$ | $f$ | Kept? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R4 | 22 | OK | False | False | False | ❌ Discarded |
| R5 | 95 | ALERT | True | True | **True** | ✅ Retained |
| R6 | 28 | OK | False | False | False | ❌ Discarded |

**P2 Output**: $\{R5\}$

---

### **Final Filtered Result**

$$D' = D_{P1} \cup D_{P2} = \{R2, R3, R5\}$$

**Cardinality verification**:
$$|D| = 6, \quad |D'| = 3$$
$$0 \leq |D'| \leq |D| \implies 0 \leq 3 \leq 6 \quad ✅ \text{ Constraint satisfied}$$

---

## 3.2 Formal Derivation: Cardinality Bounds of Filter Output

**Theorem**: For any filter operation on a dataset $D$ with predicate $f$, the output cardinality $|D'|$ satisfies:
$$0 \leq |D'| \leq |D|$$

**Proof (Case Analysis)**:

**Case 1: Empty Output**
If $f(x) = \text{False}$ for all $x \in D$, then:

$$D' = \{x \in D \mid f(x) = \text{True}\} = \emptyset$$

Therefore, $|D'| = |\emptyset| = 0$.

**Case 2: Full Output**
If $f(x) = \text{True}$ for all $x \in D$, then:

$$D' = \{x \in D \mid f(x) = \text{True}\} = D$$

Therefore, $|D'| = |D|$.

**Case 3: Partial Output**
If $f(x) = \text{True}$ for some but not all $x \in D$, then:
$$D' \subsetneq D, \quad D' \neq \emptyset$$

Therefore, $0 < |D'| < |D|$.

**Conclusion**: In all cases, the constraint $0 \leq |D'| \leq |D|$ holds. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Time and Space Complexity

| Operation Stage | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Predicate Definition | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | One-time cost |
| Lazy DAG Build | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | Just a lineage pointer |
| Task Creation | $O(p)$ | $O(p)$ | $p$ = number of partitions |
| Per-Partition Filter | $O(n/p)$ | $O(k/p)$ | $k$ = output size |
| Result Collection | $O(k)$ | $O(k)$ | Materialized output |
| **Total** | $O(n)$ | $O(k)$ | Linear scan |

Where:
- $n$ = Total input records
- $p$ = Number of partitions
- $k$ = Number of retained records ($k \leq n$)

**Key insight**: Filter has **linear time complexity** $O(n)$ and **sub-linear to linear space complexity** depending on retention ratio.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step PySpark Code Walkthrough (Lab-Ready)

```python
# Step 1: Create a SparkSession
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("FilterDemo").getOrCreate()

# Step 2: Create input data
data = [("E101", "Anand", 28, 55000),
        ("E102", "Beena", 35, 72000),
        ("E103", "Chitra", 22, 38000),
        ("E104", "Deepak", 45, 95000),
        ("E105", "Esha", 30, 60000)]

# Step 3: Create DataFrame
columns = ["EmpID", "Name", "Age", "Salary"]
df = spark.createDataFrame(data, columns)

# Step 4: Show input
df.show()

# Step 5: Apply filter - employees with salary > 50000
high_salary_df = df.filter(df.Salary > 50000)

# Step 6: Apply compound filter - age > 25 AND salary > 50000
compound_df = df.filter((df.Age > 25) & (df.Salary > 50000))

# Step 7: Trigger action
high_salary_df.show()
compound_df.show()
```

**Expected Output for Step 7 (compound filter)**:
```
+-----+------+---+------+
|EmpID|  Name|Age|Salary|
+-----+------+---+------+
| E101| Anand| 28| 55000|
| E102| Beena| 35| 72000|
| E104|Deepak| 45| 95000|
| E105|  Esha| 30| 60000|
+-----+------+---+------+
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 📌 **KTU Lab Exam Note**: Always use `.show()`, `.count()`, or `.collect()` AFTER the filter transformation — otherwise the lazy DAG will never execute and you'll see no output!

# 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the filter transformation in Apache Spark. Why is it classified as a narrow transformation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

The **filter transformation** in Apache Spark is a lazy operation that evaluates each element of an RDD against a user-defined Boolean predicate function $f(x)$ and produces a new RDD containing only those elements for which $f(x)$ returns **True**.

**Signature**: `filter(f: T => Boolean): RDD[T]`

**Why Narrow**: Filter is classified as a **narrow transformation** because each parent partition contributes to at most **one** child partition. The filter operation is applied **locally** within each partition without requiring any data shuffle across the network. This makes filter computationally efficient and preserves the partition count. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of lazy evaluation in Spark with respect to the filter transformation. What triggers its execution?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

Spark uses **lazy evaluation**, which means transformations (like `filter`, `map`, `flatMap`) are **not executed immediately** when called. Instead, Spark builds a **logical DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)** representing the sequence of transformations — this is called the **lineage**.

The `filter()` transformation simply **adds a node** to the DAG and returns a new RDD pointer. **No computation occurs** at this stage.

Execution is triggered only when an **action** is invoked. Common actions include:
- `collect()` — returns all filtered elements to the driver
- `count()` — returns the number of filtered elements
- `saveAsTextFile()` — writes filtered output to storage
- `first()` — returns the first filtered element

**Benefit**: Lazy evaluation enables **optimization** (pipelining of narrow transformations) and **fault tolerance** (lineage-based recovery). **(3 marks)**

---
