```
+--------+------+---------+-------+----------+
```

---

### **Step (b): Aggregation — Total Sales per Region**

```python
# Step 4: Group by 'region' and sum 'amount'
region_sales = df.groupBy("region") \
                 .agg({"amount": "sum"}) \
                 .withColumnRenamed("sum(amount)", "total_sales")
```

**Logic:** The `groupBy` transformation partitions the data by `region` keys. Internally, this triggers a **shuffle** — rows with the same key are co-located on the same executor. The `agg({"amount":"sum"})` applies a reducer function per group.

Mathematically, for each region $r \in R$:

$$\text{total\_sales}(r) = \sum_{i : \text{region}(i) = r} \text{amount}(i)$$

```python
# Step 5: Order descending and pick the top region
top_region = region_sales.orderBy(region_sales["total_sales"].desc()).first()
print(f"Top Region: {top_region['region']} with sales {top_region['total_sales']}")
```

**Logic:** `orderBy` is a **global sort** — it requires a single partition or range partitioning across the cluster, hence expensive. `.first()` is an **Action** that returns one Row object.

**Sample Output:**
```
Top Region: North with sales 2450000
```

---

### **Step (c): Filtering + Writing to Parquet**

```python
# Step 6: Filter high-value orders
high_value = df.filter(df["amount"] > 50000)
```

**Logic:** `filter()` applies the predicate. Catalyst pushes this filter **down to the data source** (predicate pushdown) when reading from Parquet/ORC, skipping irrelevant row groups.

$$D_{high} = \{ r \in D \mid r.\text{amount} > 50000 \}$$

```python
# Step 7: Write the result in Parquet format
high_value.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("high_value_orders.parquet")
```

**Logic:** Parquet is a **columnar storage format** — ideal for analytical queries because Spark can read only the required columns. `mode("overwrite")` replaces existing data.

---

### **Step 8: Verification (Best Practice)**

```python
# Verify by reading the parquet file back
verify_df = spark.read.parquet("high_value_orders.parquet")
print(f"High-value orders count: {verify_df.count()}")
```

**Logic:** `count()` is an **Action** that triggers a full scan to compute the number of rows.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common DataFrame Operations Reference

| Operation Type | PySpark Method | SQL Equivalent | Is it an Action? | Triggers Shuffle? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Select columns | `df.select("col1", "col2")` | `SELECT col1, col2` | No (Transformation) | No |
| Filter rows | `df.filter(df.col > 5)` | `WHERE col > 5` | No | No (with pushdown) |
| Group + Aggregate | `df.groupBy("col").sum("val")` | `GROUP BY col` | No (until action) | **Yes** |
| Join | `df1.join(df2, "key")` | `JOIN ... ON` | No | **Yes** (huge shuffle) |
| Order By | `df.orderBy("col")` | `ORDER BY col` | No | **Yes** |
| Count | `df.count()` | `SELECT COUNT(*)` | **Yes** | Yes (job) |
| Show | `df.show(n)` | N/A (display) | **Yes** | Yes (job) |
| Collect | `df.collect()` | N/A | **Yes** | Yes (full data to driver!) |
| Write | `df.write.parquet(path)` | N/A | **Yes** | Yes |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never use `df.collect()` on a large dataset in production — it pulls **all** data to the driver node, causing **OutOfMemoryError**. Use `take(n)` or `limit(n)` instead.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Derivation — Pandas vs PySpark Query Equivalence

For the same operation:

**Pandas (Eager, Single-node):**
```python
import pandas as pd
pdf = pd.read_csv("sales.csv")
result = pdf[pdf["amount"] > 50000].groupby("region")["amount"].sum()
print(result)
```

**PySpark (Lazy, Distributed):**
```python
df = spark.read.csv("sales.csv", header=True, inferSchema=True)
result = df.filter(df["amount"] > 50000) \
           .groupBy("region") \
           .sum("amount") \
           .show()
```

**Logical Equivalence:**

$$Result_{PySpark} = Show\left( Sum_{groupBy\ region}\left( Filter_{amount > 50000}\left( D \right) \right) \right)$$

$$Result_{Pandas} = \sum_{r \in R} \left( \text{amount}(r) \mid \text{amount}(r) > 50000 \right)$$

Both yield identical logical results, but PySpark defers the computation until `show()` is called.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DataFrame Execution Pipeline (Catalyst + Tungsten)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User Code: df.select filter groupBy"] --> B["Logical Plan DAG"]
    B --> C["Catalyst Optimizer"]
    C --> D["Optimized Logical Plan"]
    D --> E["Physical Plan Generation"]
    E --> F["Tungsten Execution Engine"]
    F --> G["Partition 1: Executor Node 1"]
    F --> H["Partition 2: Executor Node 2"]
    F --> I["Partition 3: Executor Node 3"]
    G --> J["Final Result Aggregation"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K["Driver: Action Triggered Result"]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The driver's user code is parsed into a **Logical Plan (DAG)**.
- **Catalyst** applies rule-based + cost-based optimizations.
- The optimized plan is converted into a **Physical Plan** with specific join strategies and operators.
- **Tungsten** compiles this to efficient bytecode and ships tasks to executor nodes.
- Each executor processes its **partition** in parallel.
- Results are aggregated back to the driver upon an **Action**.

## 4.2 DataFrame vs RDD — Conceptual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RDD["RDD Layer (Low-Level)"]
        R1["RDD of Tuples: rdd = sc.parallelize[(1,South,Phone,45000), ...]"]
        R2["Manual Schema: User maintains structure"]
        R3["No Built-in Optimizer"]
        R4["Lambda Functions for Transformations"]
    end
    subgraph DF["DataFrame Layer (High-Level)"]
        D1["Structured: rows with named columns"]
        D2["Automatic Schema Inference"]
        D3["Catalyst Optimizer + Tungsten"]
        D4["SQL + Method API"]
    end
    R1 --> D1
    R2 --> D2
    R3 --> D3
    R4 --> D4
```

**Key Insight:** DataFrames are essentially **RDDs of `Row` objects with a schema attached**. They add three superpowers: **schema awareness, query optimization, and a SQL interface**.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DataFrame Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input["Input Stage"]
        I1["HDFS / S3 / Local CSV File"]
        I2["InferSchema Pass - Lazy unless inferSchema=True"]
    end
    subgraph Transform["Transformation Stage - Lazy DAG"]
        T1["Block A: Read - Creates Logical Plan"]
        T2["Block B: Filter - Predicate Pushdown"]
        T3["Block C: Select - Column Pruning"]
        T4["Block D: GroupBy - Hash Partitioning"]
        T5["Block E: Aggregate - Reducer per Partition"]
    end
    subgraph Action["Action Stage - Triggers Execution"]
        AC1["collect / count / show / write"]
        AC2["Job Submission to Cluster Manager"]
        AC3["Task Scheduling on Executors"]
    end
    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T4 --> T5
    T5 --> AC1
    AC1 --> AC2
    AC2 --> AC3
```

**Explanation:**
- **Input Stage:** Data is referenced but not loaded.
- **Transformation Stage:** Operations are **stacked** in a DAG. Catalyst **rewrites** them (e.g., pushes `filter` before `select` to reduce data early).
- **Action Stage:** The first Action materializes the entire pipeline into **stages and tasks** distributed across the cluster.

## 4.4 Join Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Two DataFrames to Join"] --> B{"Join Keys Specified?"}
    B -- No --> C["Cross Join - O of N x M - EXPENSIVE"]
    B -- Yes --> D{"Broadcast Hint or Small Table of less than 10MB?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Broadcast Hash Join - No Shuffle"]
    D -- No --> F{"Sort Required?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Sort Merge Join - Shuffle + Sort"]
    F -- No --> H["Shuffle Hash Join - Shuffle + Hash Build"]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Broadcast Hash Join:** Best when one table is small (≤10 MB default threshold). Driver broadcasts it to all executors → **no shuffle**.
- **Shuffle Hash Join:** Both tables are shuffled by join key, then hash tables are built locally.
- **Sort Merge Join:** Default for large-to-large joins. Both sides are shuffled and sorted, then merged.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** Spark's `spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold` defaults to **10 MB**. You can force a broadcast with `from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast`.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a DataFrame in Apache Spark. List any two advantages of using DataFrames over RDDs.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **DataFrame** is a distributed collection of data organized into named columns, providing a higher-level abstraction over RDDs. *(1 mark)*
>
> **Advantages over RDDs:**
> 1. **Optimized Execution** — Uses the Catalyst optimizer to generate efficient query plans, whereas RDDs have no built-in optimization. *(1 mark)*
> 2. **Schema Awareness** — DataFrames have a defined schema enabling SQL-like operations and better error detection at compile time. *(1 mark)*
>
> *(Other acceptable advantages: structured API, ease of use, support for multiple languages, integration with Spark SQL, automatic memory management via Tungsten.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is lazy evaluation in Spark DataFrames? Why is it beneficial?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Lazy Evaluation** means that transformations on a DataFrame are not executed immediately. Instead, Spark builds a logical plan (DAG) and only triggers computation when an **Action** (e.g., `count`, `show`, `write`) is called. *(2 marks)*
>
> **Benefits:**
> - **Optimization:** Catalyst can analyze the entire DAG and apply optimizations like predicate pushdown and column pruning before execution.
> - **Efficiency:** Avoids unnecessary intermediate data materialization, reducing I/O and computation overhead. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**Consider the dataset `employees.csv` with the following schema:**
`emp_id` (int), `name` (string), `department` (string), `salary` (int), `joining_date` (date), `city` (string).

#### **(a)** Write a PySpark program to:
- (i) Load the CSV with schema inference.
- (ii) Display the schema and the first 10 rows.
- (iii) Count the total number of employees in each department, showing only departments with more than 5 employees.
- (iv) Save the resulting DataFrame in Parquet format partitioned by `city`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```python
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, count

# (i) Create SparkSession and load CSV
spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("EmployeeAnalysis").getOrCreate()
df = spark.read.csv("employees.csv", header=True, inferSchema=True)
```
*(1 mark for SparkSession creation and correct read parameters)*

```python
# (ii) Display schema and first 10 rows
df.printSchema()
df.show(10)
```
*(1 mark)*

```python
# (iii) Group by department, count, filter
dept_count = df.groupBy("department") \
               .agg(count("emp_id").alias("emp_count")) \
               .filter(col("emp_count") > 5)
dept_count.show()
```
*(3 marks: 1 for groupBy, 1 for agg with alias, 1 for filter with col expression)*

```python
# (iv) Write partitioned by city
dept_count.write.mode("overwrite") \
                .partitionBy("city") \
                .parquet("dept_output.parquet")
```
*(2 marks: 1 for mode, 1 for partitionBy)*

#### **(b)** Explain with a neat diagram the **Catalyst Optimizer's role** in Spark DataFrame execution. Compare the **performance** of PySpark DataFrames with Pandas DataFrames for a 500 GB dataset. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Catalyst Optimizer Role (4 marks):**

The Catalyst Optimizer is Spark SQL's query optimization framework. It works in four phases:

1. **Analysis** — Resolves column names and types using the catalog (logical plan → analyzed logical plan).
2. **Logical Optimization** — Applies rule-based optimizations:
   - **Predicate Pushdown:** Moves `filter` operations closer to the data source.
   - **Column Pruning:** Removes unused columns early.
   - **Constant Folding:** Evaluates constant expressions at compile time.
3. **Physical Planning** — Generates multiple candidate physical plans (e.g., different join strategies) and selects the cheapest using cost models.
4. **Code Generation (Tungsten)** — Generates optimized Java bytecode, eliminating virtual function calls and leveraging CPU registers.

*Diagram: Catalyst's 4-phase pipeline (Logical Plan → Analyzed → Optimized → Physical Plan → RDDs)*

**Performance Comparison (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pandas DataFrame | PySpark DataFrame |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Scale** | 500 GB → **Out of Memory** on a single node | 500 GB → **Distributed** across cluster, handled easily |
| **Execution** | Eager — every operation executes immediately | Lazy — DAG optimized, then executed in parallel |
| **Speed on 500 GB** | **Crashes** or extreme slowness (swapping) | Linear speedup with more workers |
| **Fault Tolerance** | None | Lineage-based recovery via DAG |

**Conclusion:** For 500 GB, **PySpark DataFrames are the only viable option**; Pandas would fail on a single machine due to memory constraints.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

#### **(a)** With suitable PySpark code, demonstrate the following operations on a DataFrame `orders` (columns: `order_id, customer_id, product, quantity, price, order_date`): **(7 marks)**
- (i) Add a new column `total_cost = quantity * price`.
- (ii) Find the top 3 customers by total spending.
- (iii) Perform an **inner join** with a DataFrame `customers` (columns: `customer_id, name, city`) on `customer_id` and display the result.
- (iv) Write a Spark SQL query to find the average order value per city.

**Model Solution:**

```python
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum, avg, desc

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("OrderAnalysis").getOrCreate()
orders = spark.read.csv("orders.csv", header=True, inferSchema=True)
customers = spark.read.csv("customers.csv", header=True, inferSchema=True)

# (i) Add total_cost column
orders_with_cost = orders.withColumn("total_cost", col("quantity") * col("price"))
```
*(2 marks)*

```python
# (ii) Top 3 customers by total spending
top_customers = orders_with_cost.groupBy("customer_id") \
                                .agg(sum("total_cost").alias("total_spent")) \
                                .orderBy(desc("total_spent")) \
                                .limit(3)
top_customers.show()
```
*(2 marks)*

```python
# (iii) Inner join with customers
joined = orders_with_cost.join(customers, "customer_id", "inner")
joined.select("customer_id", "name", "city", "total_cost").show()
```
*(2 marks)*

```python
# (iv) Spark SQL — avg order value per city
orders_with_cost.createOrReplaceTempView("orders_view")
customers.createOrReplaceTempView("customers_view")

result = spark.sql("""
    SELECT c.city, AVG(o.total_cost) AS avg_order_value
    FROM orders_view o
    JOIN customers_view c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
    GROUP BY c.city
    ORDER BY avg_order_value DESC
""")
result.show()
```
*(1 mark)*

#### **(b)** Differentiate between **Transformations** and **Actions** in Spark DataFrames with two examples each. Why is `collect()` considered dangerous in distributed mode? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | **Transformation** | **Action** |
|---|---|---|
| **Return Type** | Returns a new DataFrame | Returns a value to driver / writes to external storage |
| **Execution** | Lazy — does not trigger a job | Eager — triggers job execution |
| **Examples** | `select()`, `filter()`, `groupBy()`, `withColumn()`, `join()` | `count()`, `show()`, `collect()`, `write()`, `first()`, `take(n)` |
| **Cost** | Cheap (just builds DAG) | Expensive (materializes result) |

**Two Examples Each:**

**Transformations (2 marks):**
1. `df.filter(df["age"] > 25)` — returns a new DataFrame with filtered rows.
2. `df.select("name", "salary")` — returns a new DataFrame with only the selected columns.

**Actions (2 marks):**
1. `df.count()` — returns the number of rows as an integer to the driver.
2. `df.write.parquet("output/")` — writes the DataFrame to storage.

**Why `collect()` is Dangerous (3 marks):**

The `collect()` action retrieves **all rows from all partitions across all executor nodes** and brings them into the **driver node's memory**. In a cluster environment:

- If the DataFrame is large (e.g., billions of rows), this will cause an **OutOfMemoryError** on the driver.
- It **defeats the purpose of distributed computing** — instead of parallel processing, all data converges to a single machine.
- **Best practice:** Use `take(n)`, `limit(n)`, or `show(n)` to retrieve only a sample. For aggregation, use `groupBy().agg()` so Spark computes summaries in parallel.

**Safer Alternatives:**
```python
df.take(10)       # Returns first 10 rows as a list
df.limit(10).show()  # Returns 10 rows
df.first()        # Returns the first row
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Avoid These in Your Exam:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `show()` with `collect()` (–2 marks):** `show()` prints the table; `collect()` returns a list of `Row` objects. Using `collect()` followed by printing is functionally similar but **much more memory-intensive** and examiners deduct marks for misuse in Big Data contexts.
>
> 2. **Forgetting `inferSchema=True` (–1 mark):** Without it, **all columns are read as strings** by default. Subsequent numeric operations like `sum`, `avg` will fail or produce wrong results. Examiners look for this in your CSV-read line.
>
> 3. **Not mentioning Catalyst Optimizer (–2 marks):** Any question about "why DataFrames are fast" *must* reference **Catalyst Optimizer + Tungsten Engine**. Omitting these is the #1 reason for losing marks on optimization questions.
>
> 4. **Using RDD-style lambda functions in DataFrame API (–1 mark):** Writing `df.rdd.map(lambda x: x[0])` instead of `df.select("col").rdd.flatMap(...)` or pure DataFrame ops. Examiners expect you to use the **DataFrame API**, not fall back to RDD logic.
>
> 5. **Not specifying partition strategy in `write` (–1 mark):** If the question says "save for efficient querying," you **must** add `.partitionBy("relevant_column")`. Otherwise, you lose the optimization mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These:**

- ✅ **DataFrame** = **Distributed table with named columns and a schema**, built on top of RDDs.
- ✅ DataFrames are **immutable** — every transformation produces a **new** DataFrame.
- ✅ **Lazy Evaluation:** Transformations are deferred; **Actions** trigger execution.
- ✅ **Entry point:** `SparkSession` (not `SparkContext` directly, in modern Spark 2.x+).
- ✅ **Read API:** `spark.read.csv(path, header=True, inferSchema=True)` / `.parquet()` / `.json()`.
- ✅ **Write API:** `df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet(path)` — modes: `append`, `overwrite`, `error`, `ignore`.
- ✅ **Core Transformations:** `select`, `filter`/`where`, `groupBy`, `agg`, `withColumn`, `join`, `orderBy`/`sort`, `drop`, `distinct`, `limit`.
- ✅ **Core Actions:** `show`, `count`, `collect` *(avoid)*, `take(n)`, `first`, `write`.
- ✅ **Performance Pillars:** **Catalyst Optimizer** (logical optimization) + **Tungsten Engine** (physical execution) + **Lazy Evaluation** + **Partitioning**.
- ✅ **Shuffle Triggers:** `groupBy`, `join`, `orderBy`, `repartition`, `distinct`. These are the **most expensive** operations.
- ✅ **Broadcast Join:** Use `from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast` for small tables (≤10 MB) to **avoid shuffle**.
- ✅ **Pandas vs PySpark:** Pandas = single-node, eager, mutable. PySpark = distributed, lazy, immutable.
- ✅ **Lineage:** Spark recovers lost partitions by **recomputing the DAG** from the original data — this is fault tolerance.
- ✅ **Column Expressions:** Always use `col("name")` from `pyspark.sql.functions` to avoid ambiguity, not bare string `"name"` in complex operations.
- ✅ **Spark SQL:** Register DataFrame as a temp view with `createOrReplaceTempView("name")`, then query with `spark.sql("SELECT ...")`.
- ✅ **Partitioning Principle:** $P_{optimal} \approx \frac{\text{Data Size (MB)}}{128 \text{ MB}}$ — avoid too few or too many partitions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Data Frames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creating Data Frames

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DataFrame Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **DataFrame** is a two-dimensional, size-mutable, tabular data structure in the **Apache Spark** ecosystem (and Pandas) with labeled axes (rows and columns). It is conceptually equivalent to a **relational database table**, an **Excel spreadsheet**, or a **dictionary of Series objects**. In the context of **PySpark (Python API for Spark)**, a DataFrame is a **distributed collection of rows organized into named columns**, providing a domain-specific language for structured data manipulation.

In PySpark, every DataFrame has:
- A **Schema** — the column names and their associated data types.
- A **logical plan** — a query plan that Spark Catalyst Optimizer refines into a physical execution plan.
- **Lazy evaluation semantics** — transformations are not executed until an action (like `show()` or `count()`) is triggered.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a DataFrame as a Spreadsheet in the Cloud, Powered by Thousands of Computers.**

Imagine you run a multinational e-commerce company like **Amazon**. Every second, millions of customers place orders from across the globe. You cannot store this data in one single Excel file on your laptop — it would crash. You cannot analyze it on a single machine — it would take days.

So, what do you do? You **split the giant table into smaller chunks** and distribute them across a **cluster** of computers. Each chunk is stored on a different machine, but Spark presents it to you as **one unified table** that you can query just like Excel.

That unified table is your **DataFrame**. To you, the developer, it looks like:

| OrderID | Customer | Product    | Amount |
|---------|----------|------------|--------|
| 1001    | Anu      | Laptop     | 75000  |
| 1002    | Rahul    | Headphones | 2500   |
| 1003    | Sneha    | Smartphone  | 32000  |

But **behind the scenes**, this single table is partitioned across dozens of worker nodes, processed in parallel using **Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)**, and optimized by the **Catalyst Optimizer**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Key Distributed Computing Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Parallelism Factor** — Number of partitions; default = `200` (or `sc.defaultParallelism`).
> - **Driver Memory** — Memory allocated to the master node (e.g., `2g`, `4g`).
> - **Executor Memory** — Memory per worker node.
> - **Shuffle Partitions** — Default is **200** (controlled by `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`).
> - **Lazy Evaluation Constant** — Transformations accumulate until an **action** triggers the **Job**.

---

## 1.4 Why DataFrames Are Central to Big Data Analytics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> DataFrames are the **de-facto standard abstraction** for structured data processing in Spark. They sit **above RDDs** in the Spark API hierarchy, offering:
> 1. **Schema awareness** — Knows column types.
> 2. **Catalyst optimization** — Automatic query optimization.
> 3. **Tungsten execution** — Off-heap memory, code generation.
> 4. **Language interoperability** — Same API across Python, Scala, R, Java.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of DataFrame Creation

A DataFrame can be created through **multiple pathways**, each suited to different data sources. The SparkSession object (`spark`) is the **entry point** for all DataFrame operations in PySpark.

### 2.1.1 Hierarchy of Spark APIs (Conceptual Stack)

> [!NOTE]
> **Spark API Layers (from low-level to high-level):**
>
> 1. **RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset)** — Low-level, unstructured, no schema.
> 2. **DataFrame** — Structured, schema-aware, optimized via Catalyst.
> 3. **Dataset (Scala/Java only)** — Type-safe, object-oriented.
> 4. **Spark SQL** — Query using ANSI SQL syntax on DataFrames.

---

### 2.2 The Five Primary Methods to Create DataFrames

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Experiment 2 — Core Methods:**

#### **Method 1: From a Python `list` of tuples / `list` of lists**
Used for small inline datasets during experimentation.

#### **Method 2: From a Python `list` of `Row` objects**
Provides named fields; more readable than tuples.

#### **Method 3: From a Python `dict` (via RDD)**
Maps column names to their values; common in prototyping.

#### **Method 4: From a Pandas DataFrame**
Bridges the PyData ecosystem with Spark for distributed processing.

#### **Method 5: From an external data source**
- **CSV** — `spark.read.csv("path", header=True, inferSchema=True)`
- **JSON** — `spark.read.json("path")`
- **Parquet** — `spark.read.parquet("path")`
- **Text** — `spark.read.text("path")`
- **JDBC/ODBC** — `spark.read.jdbc(...)`

---

### 2.3 The SparkSession — The Gateway

The **SparkSession** is the unified entry point introduced in Spark 2.0. It encapsulates:
- `SparkContext` (for RDDs)
- `SQLContext` (for Spark SQL)
- `HiveContext` (for Hive integration)
- `StreamingContext` (for streaming)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Initialization:**
> ```python
> from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
> 
> spark = SparkSession.builder \
>         .appName("DataFrameCreationExperiment") \
>         .master("local[*]") \
>         .getOrCreate()
> ```
> - `appName` — Logical name visible in the Spark UI.
> - `master("local[*]")` — Runs locally using all available CPU cores.

---

### 2.4 The `inferSchema` vs `schema` Decision

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Exam Concept — Schema Inference Trade-off:**
>
> | Aspect                  | `inferSchema=True`                         | Explicit `StructType` Schema              |
> |-------------------------|--------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
> | **Speed**               | Slower (extra pass over data)              | Faster (single pass)                      |
> | **Type Safety**         | Lower (may mis-infer)                      | Higher (exact control)                    |
> | **Production Use**      | Discouraged for large/streaming data       | **Recommended best practice**             |
> | **Manual Overhead**     | None                                       | Requires writing StructType/StructField   |

A schema is defined as:

```python
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, IntegerType, DoubleType

schema = StructType([
    StructField("Name", StringType(), True),
    StructField("Age", IntegerType(), True),
    StructField("Salary", DoubleType(), True)
])
```

Where:
- `StringType()`, `IntegerType()`, `DoubleType()` → Data types.
- `True` (third argument) → `nullable=True` (whether the column can contain `null`).

---

### 2.5 The Architecture: How DataFrame Creation Works Internally

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Workflow (KTU Board Exam Favorite):**
>
> 1. **User invokes** `spark.read.csv(...)` (this is a **transformation**, hence lazy).
> 2. Spark **parses** the request → builds a **Logical Plan** (Unresolved → Analyzed → Optimized).
> 3. **Catalyst Optimizer** applies rule-based and cost-based optimizations.
> 4. A **Physical Plan** is generated, comprising executable **RDD operations**.
> 5. When an **action** (`show()`, `count()`, `collect()`) is called, the DAG is **submitted as a Job**.
> 6. The **DAG Scheduler** splits the Job into **Stages**; the **Task Scheduler** launches **Tasks** on Executors.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept**              | **Syntax / Formula**                                                                 | **Notes**                                       |
|--------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| SparkSession Init        | `SparkSession.builder.appName("X").master("local[*]").getOrCreate()`                 | Master URL controls deployment mode             |
| Create from `list`       | `spark.createDataFrame(data, columns)`                                                | `data` is `list[tuple]`, `columns` is `list[str]` |
| Create from `Row`        | `spark.createDataFrame(row_data, schema)`                                            | `Row` allows field-name access                  |
| Create from `RDD`        | `spark.createDataFrame(rdd, schema)`                                                 | RDD must contain tuples or Rows                 |
| Create from `Pandas`     | `spark.createDataFrame(pandas_df)`                                                   | Useful for sampling or test data                |
| Read CSV                 | `spark.read.csv(path, header=True, inferSchema=True)`                                | `header` and `inferSchema` are critical         |
| Read JSON                | `spark.read.json(path)`                                                              | Auto-infers schema                              |
| Read Parquet             | `spark.read.parquet(path)`                                                           | Columnar, compressed — Spark's preferred format |
| Read Text                | `spark.read.text(path)`                                                              | One column: `value`                            |
| Show DataFrame           | `df.show(n=20, truncate=True, vertical=False)`                                       | `vertical=True` for row-wise display            |
| Print Schema             | `df.printSchema()`                                                                   | Recursive structure tree                        |
| Count Rows               | `df.count()`                                                                         | Action — triggers Job                           |
| Columns List             | `df.columns`                                                                         | Returns Python `list[str]`                      |
| Data Types               | `df.dtypes`                                                                          | Returns `list[tuple[str, str]]`                 |
| Number of Partitions     | `df.rdd.getNumPartitions()`                                                          | Returns `int`                                   |
| Repartition              | `df.repartition(n)`                                                                  | Round-robin shuffle                             |
| Coalesce                 | `df.coalesce(n)`                                                                     | Merges partitions without full shuffle          |
| Sample                   | `df.sample(withReplacement, fraction, seed)`                                         | Used for exploratory analysis                   |
| Stop Session             | `spark.stop()`                                                                       | Releases cluster resources                      |

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where DataFrame Creation is Used in Industry:**
> - **ETL Pipelines** — Reading CSVs from S3, transforming, writing to Parquet on a Data Lake.
> - **Log Analytics** — Parsing server logs in JSON for monitoring dashboards.
> - **Machine Learning** — Feature engineering using `pyspark.ml` requires DataFrame inputs.
> - **Streaming Analytics** — `readStream` creates streaming DataFrames for Kafka, Kinesis, etc.
> - **Data Warehousing** — Querying Hive tables using Spark SQL on DataFrames.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Method-by-Method Walkthrough with Code

Below is a **complete, executable PySpark lab script** demonstrating every primary method of DataFrame creation covered in Module Experiment 2.

---

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step: Method 1 — From a `list` of Tuples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use Case:** Inline test data; very common in KTU lab exams.

**Step 1:** Import the SparkSession builder.
```python
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
```

**Step 2:** Initialize the SparkSession.
```python
spark = SparkSession.builder \
        .appName("DF_From_List") \
        .master("local[*]") \
        .getOrCreate()
```

**Step 3:** Define the raw data as a list of tuples.
```python
employee_data = [
    ("Anu",     28, 55000.50),
    ("Rahul",   35, 72000.00),
    ("Sneha",   24, 48000.75),
    ("Vivek",   41, 95000.25),
    ("Priya",   29, 61000.00)
]
```

**Step 4:** Define column names.
```python
columns = ["Name", "Age", "Salary"]
```

**Step 5:** Create the DataFrame.
```python
df1 = spark.createDataFrame(employee_data, columns)
```

**Step 6:** Display the contents and structure.
```python
df1.show()
df1.printSchema()
```

**Expected Output (df1.show()):**
```
+-----+---+-------+
| Name|Age| Salary|
+-----+---+-------+
|  Anu| 28|55000.5|
|Rahul| 35|72000.0|
|Sneha| 24|48000.75|
|Vivek| 41|95000.25|
|Priya| 29|61000.0|
+-----+---+-------+
```

**Expected Output (df1.printSchema()):**
```
root
 |-- Name: string (nullable = true)
 |-- Age: long (nullable = true)
 |-- Salary: double (nullable = true)
```

**Explanation of the derivation:**
- The `list` of tuples is parallelized by Spark's internal RDD logic.
- Column names are inferred from the `columns` list.
- Spark **automatically infers** the data types: `string`, `long`, `double`.
- `nullable = true` by default — all columns can hold `null` values.

---

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step: Method 2 — From a `list` of `Row` Objects

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage:** Named field access via attributes (e.g., `row.Name`) instead of positional indexing.

**Step 1:** Import the `Row` class.
```python
from pyspark.sql import Row
```

**Step 2:** Construct Row objects.
```python
row_data = [
    Row(Name="Anu",     Age=28, Salary=55000.50),
    Row(Name="Rahul",   Age=35, Salary=72000.00),
    Row(Name="Sneha",   Age=24, Salary=48000.75)
]
```

**Step 3:** Create the DataFrame.
```python
df2 = spark.createDataFrame(row_data)
```

**Step 4:** Verify.
```python
df2.show()
df2.printSchema()
```

**Why this works:** The `Row` class acts as an ordered field-keyed tuple. Spark introspects the `__fields__` attribute to extract schema directly from the keys.

---

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step: Method 3 — From a `dict` via RDD

**Step 1:** Define the data.
```python
student_dict = {
    "Name":   ["Arjun", "Meera", "Karthik"],
    "Age":    [22, 24, 23],
    "Course": ["CSE", "ECE", "ME"]
}
```

**Step 2:** Convert to DataFrame (one of two ways).
```python
# Method A: Direct via createDataFrame
df3 = spark.createDataFrame(
    list(zip(student_dict["Name"], student_dict["Age"], student_dict["Course"])),
    ["Name", "Age", "Course"]
)
```

**Step 3:** Display.
```python
df3.show()
df3.printSchema()
```

**Output:**
```
+-------+---+------+
|   Name|Age|Course|
+-------+---+------+
|  Arjun| 22|   CSE|
|  Meera| 24|   ECE|
|Karthik| 23|    ME|
+-------+---+------+
```

---

### 3.1.4 Step-by-Step: Method 4 — From a Pandas DataFrame

> [!TIP]
> **Use Case:** When a Data Scientist has already done preprocessing in Pandas and wants to scale to a cluster.

**Step 1:** Import pandas and create a Pandas DataFrame.
```python
import pandas as pd

pandas_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Product":  ["Laptop", "Phone", "Tablet"],
    "Price":    [75000, 32000, 18000],
    "Quantity": [10, 50, 30]
})
```

**Step 2:** Convert to Spark DataFrame.
```python
df4 = spark.createDataFrame(pandas_df)
```

**Step 3:** Display.
```python
df4.show()
df4.printSchema()
```

**Output:**
```
+-------+-----+--------+
|Product|Price|Quantity|
+-------+-----+--------+
| Laptop|75000|      10|
|  Phone|32000|      50|
| Tablet|18000|      30|
+-------+-----+--------+
```

**Internal Logic:** Spark serializes the Pandas DataFrame via PyArrow (if available) or Pickle, then distributes the rows to executors.

---

### 3.1.5 Step-by-Step: Method 5 — From External Data Sources

#### **Sub-Method 5A: Reading a CSV File**

**Step 1:** Suppose the file `sales.csv` contains:
```
OrderID,Product,Quantity,Price
1001,Laptop,2,75000
1002,Phone,1,32000
1003,Tablet,3,18000
```

**Step 2:** Read using PySpark.
```python
df_csv = spark.read.csv(
    path="sales.csv",
    header=True,
    inferSchema=True,
    sep=","
)
```

**Step 3:** Display.
```python
df_csv.show()
df_csv.printSchema()
```

**Output Schema:**
```
root
 |-- OrderID: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- Product: string (nullable = true)
 |-- Quantity: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- Price: integer (nullable = true)
```

#### **Sub-Method 5B: Reading a JSON File**

Suppose `employees.json` contains:
```json
{"name": "Anu",   "age": 28, "dept": "IT"}
{"name": "Rahul", "age": 35, "dept": "HR"}
```

```python
df_json = spark.read.json("employees.json")
df_json.show()
df_json.printSchema()
```

#### **Sub-Method 5C: Reading a Parquet File**

```python
df_parquet = spark.read.parquet("transactions.parquet")
df_parquet.show(5)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Parquet is Spark's preferred storage format** because it is **columnar, compressed, and supports predicate pushdown**, dramatically reducing I/O.

---

### 3.1.6 Step-by-Step: Method 6 — Defining an Explicit Schema (Best Practice)

> [!WARNING]
> **Always use explicit schemas in production!** Inference is costly and unreliable for large datasets.

```python
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, IntegerType, DoubleType

explicit_schema = StructType([
    StructField("Customer", StringType(), True),
    StructField("OrderID",  IntegerType(), True),
    StructField("Amount",   DoubleType(), True)
])

raw_data = [
    ("Anu",    1001, 5500.75),
    ("Rahul",  1002, 3200.50),
    ("Sneha",  1003, 9800.00)
]

df_explicit = spark.createDataFrame(raw_data, explicit_schema)
df_explicit.show()
df_explicit.printSchema()
```

**Output Schema:**
```
root
 |-- Customer: string (nullable = true)
 |-- OrderID: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- Amount: double (nullable = true)
```

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Creation Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Comparison Table (Frequently Asked):**

| **Method**              | **Data Source**              | **Schema Source**    | **Typical Size** | **Production Use** |
|-------------------------|------------------------------|----------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| `list` of tuples        | Inline hard-coded            | Auto-inferred        | Tiny (rows < 100) | Lab / Unit Test     |
| `list` of `Row`         | Inline with named fields     | Extracted from Row   | Tiny             | Lab / Unit Test     |
| `dict` → zipped tuples  | Inline (column-oriented)     | Auto-inferred        | Tiny             | Prototyping         |
| Pandas DataFrame        | Local Pandas object          | From Pandas dtypes   | Small–Medium     | ML Preprocessing    |
| CSV / JSON / Parquet    | External file system / S3    | Inferred or manual   | Small–Petabytes  | **Production ETL**  |
| Explicit `StructType`   | Any + Custom schema          | User-defined         | Any              | **Production Best** |

---

### 3.3 Mathematical / Logical Formulation — Partitions & Parallelism

When a DataFrame is created from a list of, say, $N = 100{,}000$ records, Spark partitions the data into $P$ partitions where:

$$
P = \max\left(\text{sc.defaultParallelism}, \left\lceil \frac{N \cdot \text{rowSize}}{\text{blockSize}} \right\rceil \right)
$$

Typically, the default `sc.defaultParallelism` on `local[*]` equals the number of CPU cores $C$:

$$
P_{\text{default}} = C
$$

For a 4-core local machine with $N = 1000$ rows, the DataFrame is split into $P = 4$ partitions, each containing approximately:

$$
\text{Rows per Partition} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{P} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1000}{4} \right\rceil = 250
$$

This **parallel partitioning** is what enables Spark's distributed processing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete flow** from raw data input to a distributed DataFrame, highlighting Catalyst optimization and lazy evaluation.

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define a DataFrame in PySpark. Mention two advantages it has over RDDs.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **DataFrame** is a distributed collection of data organized into named columns. It is conceptually equivalent to a relational table with a schema, but distributed across a Spark cluster.

**Two Advantages over RDDs:**
1. **Schema Awareness** — DataFrames know the data types of each column, enabling better optimizations.
2. **Catalyst Optimizer** — Spark automatically optimizes DataFrame queries via the Catalyst engine, unlike RDDs which execute operations as written.
3. *(Bonus)* **Tungsten Execution** — Off-heap memory management and code generation make DataFrames significantly faster.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between `inferSchema=True` and an explicit `StructType` schema.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect**            | **`inferSchema=True`**                        | **Explicit `StructType`**               |
|-----------------------|-----------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| **Definition Source** | Spark scans the data to deduce types          | Developer manually writes the schema    |
| **Execution Speed**   | Slower (extra data pass)                      | Faster (single pass)                    |
| **Reliability**       | May mis-infer types (e.g., ID as integer)     | Guaranteed correct types                |
| **Use Case**          | Quick prototyping                             | Production / Streaming / Large datasets |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ESE Module Internal Choice Format — Answer ONE full question.**

---

### **Question A: DataFrame Creation Using Multiple Methods (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> Write a PySpark program to create a DataFrame from a `list` of tuples containing employee records with columns: `EmpID`, `Name`, `Department`, `Salary`. Display the DataFrame and its schema.

**Model Solution:**

```python
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

# Step 1: Initialize SparkSession
spark = SparkSession.builder \
        .appName("EmployeeDataFrame") \
        .master("local[*]") \
        .getOrCreate()

# Step 2: Define data
employee_data = [
    (101, "Anu",    "IT",      55000),
    (102, "Rahul",  "HR",      62000),
    (103, "Sneha",  "Finance", 70000),
    (104, "Vivek",  "IT",      58000),
    (105, "Priya",  "Sales",   48000)
]

# Step 3: Define columns
columns = ["EmpID", "Name", "Department", "Salary"]

# Step 4: Create DataFrame
df = spark.createDataFrame(employee_data, columns)

# Step 5: Display
df.show()
df.printSchema()
```

**Output:**
```
+-----+-----+----------+------+
|EmpID| Name|Department|Salary|
+-----+-----+----------+------+
|  101|  Anu|        IT| 55000|
|  102|Rahul|        HR| 62000|
|  103|Sneha|   Finance| 70000|
|  104|Vivek|        IT| 58000|
|  105|Priya|     Sales| 48000|
+-----+-----+----------+------+

root
 |-- EmpID: long (nullable = true)
 |-- Name: string (nullable = true)
 |-- Department: string (nullable = true)
 |-- Salary: long (nullable = true)
```

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> Explain how you would read a CSV file `sales.csv` containing columns `OrderID`, `Product`, `Quantity`, `Price` into a Spark DataFrame. Demonstrate both `inferSchema=True` and explicit schema approaches.

**Model Solution:**

**Approach 1: With `inferSchema=True`**
```python
df_csv = spark.read.csv(
    path="sales.csv",
    header=True,
    inferSchema=True,
    sep=","
)
df_csv.show()
df_csv.printSchema()
```

**Approach 2: With Explicit Schema (Best Practice)**
```python
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, IntegerType, DoubleType

sales_schema = StructType([
    StructField("OrderID",  IntegerType(), True),
    StructField("Product",  StringType(),  True),
    StructField("Quantity", IntegerType(), True),
    StructField("Price",    DoubleType(),  True)
])

df_csv_explicit = spark.read.csv(
    path="sales.csv",
    header=True,
    schema=sales_schema,
    sep=","
)
df_csv_explicit.show()
df_csv_explicit.printSchema()
```

**Conclusion:** The explicit schema approach is preferred for production because it avoids the extra data-scanning pass required by `inferSchema`.

---

### **Question B: Internal Architecture and DataFrame Creation Workflow (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> With a neat diagram, explain the internal architecture of Spark DataFrame creation including the roles of SparkSession, Logical Plan, Catalyst Optimizer, and DAG Scheduler.

**Model Solution:**

The Spark DataFrame creation follows a **multi-stage pipeline**:

1. **SparkSession** acts as the unified entry point. When `spark.read.csv(...)` is called, Spark creates an **Unresolved Logical Plan** describing the data source and intended operations.

2. **Logical Plan Analysis**: Catalyst resolves column references against the catalog to produce an **Analyzed Logical Plan**.

3. **Catalyst Optimizer** applies a series of rule-based optimizations (e.g., predicate pushdown, constant folding) and cost-based optimizations to produce an **Optimized Logical Plan**.

4. **Physical Planning**: Spark converts the optimized logical plan into one or more **Physical Plans**, selecting the most efficient strategy.

5. **DAG Scheduler** translates the physical plan into a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** of stages and tasks. Each stage contains tasks that can be executed in parallel.

6. **Task Scheduler** assigns tasks to executors on the cluster. The result is a fully realized, distributed DataFrame.

```
[User Code] → [SparkSession] → [Unresolved Plan] → [Analyzed Plan]
            → [Optimized Plan] → [Physical Plan] → [DAG] → [Executed]
```

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> Write a PySpark program that:
> (i) Creates a DataFrame from a `list` of `Row` objects.
> (ii) Converts a Pandas DataFrame into a Spark DataFrame.
> (iii) Reads a Parquet file.
> Compare the three methods.

**Model Solution:**

```python
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession, Row
import pandas as pd

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("ThreeMethods").master("local[*]").getOrCreate()

# (i) From Row objects
row_data = [
    Row(name="Anu",   age=28, city="Kochi"),
    Row(name="Rahul", age=35, city="Trivandrum"),
    Row(name="Sneha", age=24, city="Calicut")
]
df_row = spark.createDataFrame(row_data)
print("--- From Row ---")
df_row.show()

# (ii) From Pandas
pandas_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "name": ["Arjun", "Meera", "Karthik"],
    "age":  [22, 24, 23],
    "city": ["Kochi", "Trivandrum", "Kannur"]
})
df_pandas = spark.createDataFrame(pandas_df)
print("--- From Pandas ---")
df_pandas.show()

# (iii) From Parquet
df_parquet = spark.read.parquet("data/transactions.parquet")
print("--- From Parquet ---")
df_parquet.show(5)
```

**Comparison:**

| **Method**        | **Data Source**    | **Schema Source**      | **Best For**                  |
|-------------------|--------------------|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| `Row` objects     | Inline             | Auto from Row fields   | Lab testing, named fields     |
| Pandas DataFrame  | Local Python       | Pandas dtypes          | Transitioning Pandas → Spark  |
| Parquet file      | External storage   | Stored in file metadata| Production, performance       |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to call `.getOrCreate()`** — The SparkSession builder must be terminated with `getOrCreate()`; otherwise, it returns a `Builder` object, not a session.
> 2. **Confusing transformations and actions** — `createDataFrame` and `read.csv` are **lazy**; data is not loaded until `show()`, `count()`, or `collect()` is invoked.
> 3. **Using `inferSchema=True` in production** — This causes an additional full data scan, harming performance. Always prefer explicit `StructType` for large/streaming data.
> 4. **Path errors** — When reading external files, use the **absolute path** or ensure the working directory is correctly set; relative paths can silently fail.
> 5. **Forgetting `header=True` in CSV** — Without this, the first row is treated as data, not column names, leading to schema mismatches.
> 6. **Not calling `spark.stop()`** — In lab exams, failing to stop the session leaves zombie JVM processes.
> 7. **Mixing up `repartition(n)` and `coalesce(n)`** — `repartition` causes a full shuffle; `coalesce` does not. Use `coalesce` when reducing partitions for performance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module Experiment 2:**

- ⭐ **DataFrame** = 2D, distributed, schema-aware tabular data structure in Spark.
- ⭐ **SparkSession** is the **single entry point** for all DataFrame operations in PySpark 2.0+.
- ⭐ **Six primary methods** of creation: `list` of tuples, `list` of `Row`, `dict`, Pandas DataFrame, external files (CSV/JSON/Parquet/Text), and from RDDs.
- ⭐ **Lazy evaluation** is fundamental: transformations accumulate until an **action** triggers the job.
- ⭐ **Catalyst Optimizer** is the secret sauce — it rewrites queries for optimal execution.
- ⭐ **Tungsten** provides off-heap memory and code generation for performance.
- ⭐ **Always prefer `StructType` schemas** in production over `inferSchema=True`.
- ⭐ **`header=True` and `inferSchema=True`** are the two most-used options in `spark.read.csv()`.
- ⭐ **Parquet** is Spark's **preferred columnar storage format** — compact, fast, supports predicate pushdown.
- ⭐ **Partitions** control parallelism; `repartition(n)` triggers a shuffle, `coalesce(n)` does not.
- ⭐ **`df.show()` displays the data; `df.printSchema()` displays the schema; both are actions.**
- ⭐ **`nullable = true` (default)** in `StructField` allows null values; set `False` to enforce non-null.
- ⭐ **External file paths** can be local (`file:///...`), HDFS (`hdfs://...`), S3 (`s3a://...`), or Azure (`abfss://...`).
- ⭐ **`spark.stop()`** must be called to gracefully release cluster resources.
- ⭐ **Reading a Parquet file automatically reads its schema** from the file footer — no inference needed.
- ⭐ **DataFrame ≠ Dataset** — Datasets are type-safe and exist only in Scala/Java, not in Python.
- ⭐ **`pyspark.sql.functions`** module provides 200+ built-in functions (col, lit, when, avg, sum, etc.) for DataFrame manipulation.
- ⭐ **KTU exam focus:** expect questions on schema definition, methods of creation, advantages of DataFrames over RDDs, and reading external data sources.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matrix like Operations in Frames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Matrix like Operations in Frames

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Pandas DataFrames** and the broader **Big Data Analytics ecosystem**, **Matrix-like Operations in Frames** refer to the family of vectorized, element-wise and aggregate computations that can be applied on two-dimensional labeled tabular data structures (DataFrames) so that they behave analogously to mathematical matrices handled by linear algebra.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> A *matrix-like operation* on a Pandas `DataFrame` is any operation — element-wise arithmetic, broadcasting, transpose, dot product, or axis-wise aggregation — that treats the frame as a 2D numerical structure where each cell $A_{ij}$ belongs to row $i$ and column $j$, and the operation produces a new structure $C$ (either a DataFrame, Series, or scalar) by applying the corresponding mathematical rule uniformly across aligned cells.

These operations form the **theoretical backbone** of Experiment 2 because they connect classical linear algebra with modern, scalable data manipulation using the **NumPy–Pandas dual stack** (NumPy for raw numerical arrays, Pandas for labeled frames).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — "The Spreadsheet That Knows Linear Algebra"**
>
> Imagine a large accounting spreadsheet where rows are employees and columns are monthly salaries. If HR wants to apply a **10% bonus** to *every cell*, or compute the **total salary per employee** (summing across columns), or compute the **total bonus paid per month** (summing down rows), the *frame* is performing matrix-like operations. The DataFrame is just an intelligent spreadsheet that:
> - Auto-aligns columns by name (not just position).
> - Knows how to "stretch" a 1-row bonus table across every employee (broadcasting).
> - Can multiply two financial tables cell-by-cell or as a true matrix product.
> - Returns a labelled result, not just numbers.

In short, **matrix-like operations = "do linear algebra on labelled tables"** — preserving readability while gaining vectorized speed.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Big Data Analytics

- **Vectorization** is the *single most important* performance technique in Pandas. Naïve Python `for`-loops over millions of cells are replaced by these matrix-like operations, achieving **100×–1000× speed-ups**.
- These operations are the **gateway to Machine Learning preprocessing** — every ML pipeline (Scikit-learn, TensorFlow, PyTorch) converts a DataFrame into a NumPy matrix using exactly these operations.
- In a distributed setting (Spark `DataFrame`, Dask `DataFrame`), the same conceptual operations are translated into **lazy, distributed transformations** — so mastering them in Pandas is *directly transferable* to Big Data systems.

## 1.4 Core Terminology Checklist

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **DataFrame** | 2D labeled, size-mutable, tabular structure with potentially heterogeneous columns. |
| **Series** | 1D labeled array — a single row or column of a DataFrame. |
| **Axis = 0** | Operate **down columns** (row-wise collapse; result has one value per column). |
| **Axis = 1** | Operate **across columns** (column-wise collapse; result has one value per row). |
| **Broadcasting** | Stretching a smaller structure (scalar / Series / 1×n / m×1) to match a larger one's shape during arithmetic. |
| **Element-wise (⊙)** | Operation applied cell-by-cell: $C_{ij} = A_{ij} \odot B_{ij}$. |
| **Dot product (·)** | True matrix multiplication: $C_{ij} = \sum_k A_{ik} B_{kj}$. |
| **ufunc** | Universal function from NumPy applied element-wise on a frame. |
| **NaN-safe ops** | Functions that gracefully ignore `NaN` (e.g., `nansum`, `nanmean`). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Taxonomy of Matrix-like Operations

Matrix-like operations on frames can be rigorously classified into **five families**. Understanding this taxonomy is essential for answering KTU's analytical questions.

### Family 1 — Element-wise Arithmetic (Hadamard-style)
Operate cell-by-cell between two frames of **identical shape** (after label alignment).

$$
C_{ij} \;=\; A_{ij} \;\odot\; B_{ij}
$$

Where $\odot \in \{+, \;-, \;\times, \;/,\; \%,\; **\}$.

- Pandas operators: `df1 + df2`, `df1 - df2`, `df1 * df2`, `df1 / df2`, `df1 ** df2`.
- Misaligned labels produce `NaN` by default (alignment-before-operation semantics).
- A scalar broadcasts trivially: $C_{ij} = A_{ij} \odot s$.

### Family 2 — Broadcasting Operations
Allow operations between structures of **different but compatible shapes** without explicit replication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Broadcasting Compatibility Rule (NumPy/Pandas Standard):**
> Two shapes $(m, n)$ and $(p, q)$ are compatible iff:
> 1. They are equal, **OR**
> 2. One of them is **1** in a dimension, in which case it is **stretched** to match the other.
>
> Pandas extends this with **label-based alignment** on top of NumPy's shape-only rule.

Common compatible pairs:
- DataFrame $(m, n)$ **with** Series $(n,)$ → broadcast across rows.
- DataFrame $(m, n)$ **with** Series $(m,)$ treated as $(m, 1)$ → broadcast across columns.
- DataFrame $(m, n)$ **with** scalar → trivial broadcast.

### Family 3 — True Matrix Multiplication (Dot Product)
Distinct from element-wise multiplication; follows the **inner-product rule of linear algebra**.

$$
C_{ij} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{K} A_{ik} \cdot B_{kj}
$$

- Implemented in Pandas via `df.dot(other)` or the infix operator `@` (Python 3.5+).
- Requires that `A.shape[1] == B.shape[0]`.
- Returns a DataFrame whose **columns come from `B`'s columns** and **rows from `A`'s rows** (intersection in case of label-based dot).

### Family 4 — Transposition
Mirror reflection of the frame across its main diagonal.

$$
(A^{T})_{ij} \;=\; A_{ji}
$$

- Implemented as `df.T` or `df.transpose()`.
- Note: `df.T` is *itself* a DataFrame; operations on the transpose are equally valid.

### Family 5 — Axis-wise Aggregations
Reduction operations that collapse one axis.

$$
\text{sum along axis=0:}\quad s_{j} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{m} A_{ij}
$$

$$
\text{sum along axis=1:}\quad t_{i} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{ij}
$$

- Same rule applies to `mean`, `std`, `var`, `min`, `max`, `median`, `prod`, `cumsum`, `cumprod`.
- For an **m × n** frame: `axis=0` returns shape `(n,)`; `axis=1` returns shape `(m,)`.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Vectorization

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `df1 + df2` is faster than a Python `for`-loop:**
>
> 1. **C-level execution** — Pandas delegates to NumPy, which is implemented in C with SIMD instructions.
> 2. **No Python interpreter overhead per element** — one Python call dispatches millions of operations.
> 3. **Memory contiguity** — NumPy arrays store elements in contiguous memory blocks, enabling CPU cache efficiency.
> 4. **Parallelism-ready** — The same operation translates seamlessly to Apache Spark's distributed `DataFrame` API.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — Save This for Exam Day**

| # | Operation | Mathematical Form | Pandas Syntax | Shape Rule |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Element-wise add | $C_{ij} = A_{ij} + B_{ij}$ | `df1 + df2` | $(m,n)$ with $(m,n)$ |
| 2 | Scalar broadcast | $C_{ij} = A_{ij} + s$ | `df + 5` | $(m,n)$ with scalar |
| 3 | Row-broadcast (Series) | $C_{ij} = A_{ij} + s_j$ | `df + row_series` | $(m,n)$ with $(n,)$ |
| 4 | Column-broadcast (Series) | $C_{ij} = A_{ij} + s_i$ | `df + col_series` | $(m,n)$ with $(m,1)$ |
| 5 | Hadamard product | $C_{ij} = A_{ij} \cdot B_{ij}$ | `df1 * df2` | $(m,n)$ with $(m,n)$ |
| 6 | Matrix product | $C_{ij} = \sum_k A_{ik} B_{kj}$ | `df1 @ df2` / `df1.dot(df2)` | $(m,k)$ with $(k,n)$ |
| 7 | Transpose | $(A^{T})_{ij} = A_{ji}$ | `df.T` | $(m,n) \to (n,m)$ |
| 8 | Column-wise sum | $s_j = \sum_i A_{ij}$ | `df.sum(axis=0)` | $(m,n) \to (n,)$ |
| 9 | Row-wise sum | $t_i = \sum_j A_{ij}$ | `df.sum(axis=1)` | $(m,n) \to (m,)$ |
| 10 | Column-wise mean | $\mu_j = \frac{1}{m}\sum_i A_{ij}$ | `df.mean(axis=0)` | $(m,n) \to (n,)$ |
| 11 | Column-wise std | $\sigma_j = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m-1}\sum_i (A_{ij} - \mu_j)^2}$ | `df.std(axis=0)` | $(m,n) \to (n,)$ |
| 12 | Grand mean (scalar) | $\mu = \frac{1}{mn}\sum_{i,j} A_{ij}$ | `df.values.mean()` | $(m,n) \to (1,1)$ |
| 13 | NumPy ufunc | $C_{ij} = f(A_{ij})$ | `np.sqrt(df)`, `np.log(df)` | Shape preserved |
| 14 | NaN-safe mean | $\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert S_j \vert}\sum_{i \in S_j} A_{ij}$ | `np.nanmean(df, axis=0)` | $(m,n) \to (n,)$ |
| 15 | Cumulative sum | $C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{i} A_{kj}$ | `df.cumsum(axis=0)` | Shape preserved |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** In the KTU formula sheet above, the symbol $\vert S_j \vert$ denotes the **cardinality** (count) of the set $S_j$ of non-NaN entries in column $j$. Always write it explicitly in derivations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Matrix-like Frame Operation Used |
|---|---|
| **Financial Analytics** | Daily P&L matrix: row-wise `sum` gives per-portfolio totals; `cumsum` gives equity curve. |
| **IoT / Sensor Networks** | Each sensor = row, each timestamp = column; column-wise `mean` smooths noise; `df.diff()` gives rate of change. |
| **Recommender Systems** | User–Item rating matrix; `df @ item_factors.T` produces predicted ratings (matrix factorization). |
| **Image Processing** | RGB image as 3D array; `np.dot(weight_matrix, pixel_matrix)` performs color-space transforms. |
| **Genomics** | Gene-expression matrix; axis-wise `std`/`mean` gives z-score normalization per gene. |
| **Distributed ML (Spark)** | Identical APIs (`select`, `agg`, `withColumn`) execute the *same* matrix-like logic on petabyte-scale frames. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Mandate:** Every transition below is written out. No "it is easy to see" shortcuts. This is exactly how a KTU Board examiner expects a model answer to look.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Element-wise vs Matrix Multiplication

### Given DataFrames

Let the following two DataFrames be the input:

$$
A \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad B \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

In Pandas (for completeness — this **is** a CS subject):

```python
import pandas as pd
A = pd.DataFrame([[1, 2], [3, 4]], columns=['x', 'y'])
B = pd.DataFrame([[5, 6], [7, 8]], columns=['x', 'y'])
```

### Part (a) — Element-wise (Hadamard) Product: `A * B`

By definition:

$$
(A \odot B)_{ij} \;=\; A_{ij} \cdot B_{ij}
$$

**Cell $(0,0)$:**
$$
(A \odot B)_{00} \;=\; A_{00} \cdot B_{00} \;=\; 1 \cdot 5 \;=\; 5
$$

**Cell $(0,1)$:**
$$
(A \odot B)_{01} \;=\; A_{01} \cdot B_{01} \;=\; 2 \cdot 6 \;=\; 12
$$

**Cell $(1,0)$:**
$$
(A \odot B)_{10} \;=\; A_{10} \cdot B_{10} \;=\; 3 \cdot 7 \;=\; 21
$$

**Cell $(1,1)$:**
$$
(A \odot B)_{11} \;=\; A_{11} \cdot B_{11} \;=\; 4 \cdot 8 \;=\; 32
$$

Therefore:

$$
A \odot B \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 12 \\ 21 & 32 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Part (b) — Matrix (Dot) Product: `A @ B`

By definition, for an $m \times k$ times $k \times n$ product, the result is $m \times n$ with entries:

$$
C_{ij} \;=\; \sum_{r=1}^{k} A_{ir} \cdot B_{rj}
$$

Here $m = 2$, $k = 2$, $n = 2$. We compute each cell.

**Cell $(0,0)$:**

$$
C_{00} \;=\; \sum_{r=1}^{2} A_{0,r} \cdot B_{r,0} \;=\; A_{0,1} B_{1,0} + A_{0,2} B_{2,0}
$$

Substituting:

$$
C_{00} \;=\; (1)(5) + (2)(7) \;=\; 5 + 14 \;=\; 19
$$

**Cell $(0,1)$:**

$$
C_{01} \;=\; \sum_{r=1}^{2} A_{0,r} \cdot B_{r,1} \;=\; A_{0,1} B_{1,1} + A_{0,2} B_{2,1}
$$

Substituting:

$$
C_{01} \;=\; (1)(6) + (2)(8) \;=\; 6 + 16 \;=\; 22
$$

**Cell $(1,0)$:**

$$
C_{10} \;=\; \sum_{r=1}^{2} A_{1,r} \cdot B_{r,0} \;=\; A_{1,1} B_{1,0} + A_{1,2} B_{2,0}
$$

Substituting:

$$
C_{10} \;=\; (3)(5) + (4)(7) \;=\; 15 + 28 \;=\; 43
$$

**Cell $(1,1)$:**

$$
C_{11} \;=\; \sum_{r=1}^{2} A_{1,r} \cdot B_{r,1} \;=\; A_{1,1} B_{1,1} + A_{1,2} B_{2,1}
$$

Substituting:

$$
C_{11} \;=\; (3)(6) + (4)(8) \;=\; 18 + 32 \;=\; 50
$$

Therefore:

$$
A \cdot B \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 19 & 22 \\ 43 & 50 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Contrast:**
> $$
> A \odot B \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 12 \\ 21 & 32 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{vs.} \quad A \cdot B \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 19 & 22 \\ 43 & 50 \end{bmatrix}
> $$
> Identical input frames produce **completely different results** — a common KTU viva question.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Broadcasting with a Row-Series

### Setup
$$
D \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 20 & 30 \\ 40 & 50 & 60 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad s \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \;\;(\text{1-row Series})
$$

### Operation
Compute $E = D + s$ (Pandas auto-broadcasts `s` across all rows).

### Derivation

For an arbitrary row $i$ and column $j$, the broadcast operation is:

$$
E_{ij} \;=\; D_{ij} + s_{j}
$$

because $s$ has length $n = 3$ matching the column count. Pandas stretches $s$ into:

$$
S_{\text{stretched}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Cell $(0,0)$:**
$$
E_{00} \;=\; D_{00} + s_{0} \;=\; 10 + 1 \;=\; 11
$$

**Cell $(0,1)$:**
$$
E_{01} \;=\; D_{01} + s_{1} \;=\; 20 + 2 \;=\; 22
$$

**Cell $(0,2)$:**
$$
E_{02} \;=\; D_{02} + s_{2} \;=\; 30 + 3 \;=\; 33
$$

**Cell $(1,0)$:**
$$
E_{10} \;=\; D_{10} + s_{0} \;=\; 40 + 1 \;=\; 41
$$

**Cell $(1,1)$:**
$$
E_{11} \;=\; D_{11} + s_{1} \;=\; 50 + 2 \;=\; 52
$$

**Cell $(1,2)$:**
$$
E_{12} \;=\; D_{12} + s_{2} \;=\; 60 + 3 \;=\; 63
$$

Therefore:

$$
E \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 11 & 22 & 33 \\ 41 & 52 & 63 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Implementation
```python
D = pd.DataFrame([[10,20,30],[40,50,60]], columns=['a','b','c'])
s = pd.Series([1, 2, 3], index=['a','b','c'])
E = D + s   # broadcast across rows
```

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Axis-wise Aggregation: Mean and Standard Deviation

### Setup
$$
F \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 4 & 6 \\ 6 & 8 \end{bmatrix} \quad (m=3, \; n=2)
$$

### (a) Column-wise Mean: `F.mean(axis=0)`

$$
\mu_{j} \;=\; \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} F_{ij}
$$

**Column 0:**
$$
\mu_{0} \;=\; \frac{1}{3}(2 + 4 + 6) \;=\; \frac{12}{3} \;=\; 4.0
$$

**Column 1:**
$$
\mu_{1} \;=\; \frac{1}{3}(4 + 6 + 8) \;=\; \frac{18}{3} \;=\; 6.0
$$

Result (Series of length 2): $\mu = [4.0,\; 6.0]$.

### (b) Row-wise Mean: `F.mean(axis=1)`

$$
\mu_{i} \;=\; \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} F_{ij}
$$

**Row 0:**
$$
\mu_{0} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(2 + 4) \;=\; 3.0
$$

**Row 1:**
$$
\mu_{1} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(4 + 6) \;=\; 5.0
$$

**Row 2:**
$$
\mu_{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}(6 + 8) \;=\; 7.0
$$

Result (Series of length 3): $\mu = [3.0,\; 5.0,\; 7.0]$.

### (c) Column-wise Standard Deviation: `F.std(axis=0)`

Using the sample standard deviation formula (Bessel's correction, $N-1$):

$$
\sigma_{j} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{m-1} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( F_{ij} - \mu_{j} \right)^{2}}
$$

**Column 0** (mean $\mu_0 = 4$):
$$
\sigma_{0} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\left[(2-4)^2 + (4-4)^2 + (6-4)^2\right]}
\;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}(4 + 0 + 4)} \;=\; \sqrt{4} \;=\; 2.0
$$

**Column 1** (mean $\mu_1 = 6$):
$$
\sigma_{1} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\left[(4-6)^2 + (6-6)^2 + (8-6)^2\right]}
\;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}(4 + 0 + 4)} \;=\; \sqrt{4} \;=\; 2.0
$$

Result: $\sigma = [2.0,\; 2.0]$.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Transpose and the Resulting Shape

### Setup
$$
G \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \quad (2 \times 3)
$$

### Operation: `G.T`

By definition, for any $(i, j)$ entry of the transpose:

$$
(G^{T})_{ij} \;=\; G_{ji}
$$

Therefore the rows of $G$ become the columns of $G^T$:

$$
G^{T} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 4 \\ 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \quad (3 \times 2)
$$

**Verification of cell $(0,1)$:**
$$
(G^{T})_{01} \;=\; G_{10} \;=\; 4 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Verification of cell $(2,0)$:**
$$
(G^{T})_{20} \;=\; G_{02} \;=\; 3 \;\;\checkmark
$$

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Cumulative Sum Along Axis = 0

### Setup
$$
H \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Operation: `H.cumsum(axis=0)`

The cumulative sum along axis 0 means that for each column $j$:

$$
C_{ij} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{i} H_{kj}
$$

**Column 0:**

$$
C_{00} = H_{00} = 1
$$

$$
C_{10} = H_{00} + H_{10} = 1 + 3 = 4
$$

$$
C_{20} = H_{00} + H_{10} + H_{20} = 1 + 3 + 5 = 9
$$

**Column 1:**

$$
C_{01} = H_{01} = 2
$$

$$
C_{11} = H_{01} + H_{11} = 2 + 4 = 6
$$

$$
C_{21} = H_{01} + H_{11} + H_{21} = 2 + 4 + 6 = 12
$$

Result:

$$
C \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 4 & 6 \\ 9 & 12 \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Table — Operator Behaviour

| Operator / Function | Treats NaN as | Returns on Empty Frame | Reduces Shape? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` | Propagates NaN | Series of NaN | No |
| `@` (matrix mul) | Propagates NaN | Empty frame | Yes (if applicable) |
| `.sum()` | Skips NaN by default | 0 | Yes |
| `.mean()` | Skips NaN by default | NaN | Yes |
| `.cumsum()` | Skips initial NaN | All NaN | No |
| `.std()` | Skips NaN by default | NaN | Yes |
| `np.nanmean()` | Skips NaN (explicit) | RuntimeWarning → NaN | Yes |
| `np.sqrt(df)` | Propagates NaN | Element-wise NaN | No |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map of Matrix-like Operations on Frames

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DataFrame D of shape m by n] --> B{Operation Type}
    B --> C1[Element-wise Arithmetic]
    B --> C2[Broadcasting]
    B --> C3[Matrix Multiplication]
    B --> C4[Transpose]
    B --> C5[Axis-wise Aggregation]
    B --> C6[NumPy ufunc Application]

    C1 --> D1[Same shape operand]
    C1 --> D2[Scalar operand]
    D1 --> R1[Output shape m by n]
    D2 --> R1

    C2 --> E1[Series length n broadcasts across rows]
    C2 --> E2[Series length m broadcasts across columns]
    C2 --> E3[Scalar broadcasts everywhere]
    E1 --> R2[Output shape m by n]
    E2 --> R2
    E3 --> R2

    C3 --> F1[Shape rule: A m by k times B k by n]
    F1 --> R3[Output shape m by n]

    C4 --> G1[Reflect across main diagonal]
    G1 --> R4[Output shape n by m]

    C5 --> H1[axis 0: collapse rows]
    C5 --> H2[axis 1: collapse columns]
    C5 --> H3[axis None: scalar]
    H1 --> R5[Output shape n]
    H2 --> R6[Output shape m]
    H3 --> R7[Output scalar]

    C6 --> I1[ufunc f applied element-wise]
    I1 --> R8[Output shape m by n]

    R1 --> Z[Resulting Frame or Series]
    R2 --> Z
    R3 --> Z
    R4 --> Z
    R5 --> Z
    R6 --> Z
    R7 --> Z
    R8 --> Z

    style Z fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000

```

> - **Part B:** Module Internal Choice — answer ONE of two 14-mark questions = 14 marks
