## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define matrix-like operations in the context of Pandas DataFrames. List any four such operations with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Matrix-like operations in Pandas DataFrames are vectorized computations that apply linear-algebraic rules (element-wise arithmetic, broadcasting, dot product, transpose, or axis-wise aggregation) on a two-dimensional labeled tabular structure so that it behaves analogously to a mathematical matrix.

The four key operations are:

1. **Element-wise arithmetic** — `df1 + df2`, `df1 * df2`: applies the operator cell-by-cell after index/column alignment.
2. **Broadcasting** — `df + series`: stretches a smaller operand to match the DataFrame's shape.
3. **Matrix multiplication** — `df1 @ df2` or `df1.dot(df2)`: computes the inner product of rows of `df1` with columns of `df2`.
4. **Transpose** — `df.T`: swaps rows and columns, producing an $n \times m$ frame from an $m \times n$ input.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks for the list.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between element-wise multiplication and matrix multiplication on DataFrames. Show the difference using a $2 \times 2$ numerical example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Element-wise (`*`) | Matrix multiplication (`@` / `.dot`) |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $C_{ij} = A_{ij} \cdot B_{ij}$ | $C_{ij} = \sum_k A_{ik} B_{kj}$ |
| Shape rule | Identical shapes | Inner dimensions must match |
| Symbol | Hadamard product | Inner product / dot product |

**Example:** Let
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Element-wise** $A * B$:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1\cdot 5 & 2\cdot 6 \\ 3\cdot 7 & 4\cdot 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 12 \\ 21 & 32 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Matrix product** $A \cdot B$:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1\cdot 5 + 2\cdot 7 & 1\cdot 6 + 2\cdot 8 \\ 3\cdot 5 + 4\cdot 7 & 3\cdot 6 + 4\cdot 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 19 & 22 \\ 43 & 50 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for the tabular distinction + 2 marks for the worked example with correct arithmetic.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

#### (a) Explain the concept of **broadcasting** in Pandas DataFrames. State the broadcasting compatibility rules and illustrate with one example each for: (i) scalar broadcasting, (ii) Series (row) broadcasting. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Broadcasting is the mechanism by which Pandas (inheriting from NumPy) applies an operation between two structures of *different but compatible* shapes by virtually stretching the smaller one — without physically copying data — so that element-wise arithmetic can proceed.

**Broadcasting Rules (2 marks):** Two shapes are compatible iff, when aligned from the right:
1. They are **equal** in every dimension, **OR**
2. **One of them is 1** in that dimension, in which case it is broadcast (stretched) to match the other.
3. Pandas adds a *third* rule: **labels must align** on the non-broadcasted axis; otherwise `NaN` is produced.

**Example (i) — Scalar Broadcasting (1.5 marks):**
$$
D = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 20 \\ 30 & 40 \end{bmatrix}, \quad s = 100
$$
$$
D + s = \begin{bmatrix} 110 & 120 \\ 130 & 140 \end{bmatrix}
$$
The scalar `s` is stretched to a 2×2 grid of all-100s before the addition.

**Example (ii) — Series (row) Broadcasting (1.5 marks):**
$$
D = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 20 & 30 \\ 40 & 50 & 60 \end{bmatrix}, \quad s = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$
$$
D + s = \begin{bmatrix} 11 & 22 & 33 \\ 41 & 52 & 63 \end{bmatrix}
$$
The 1-D Series of length 3 is stretched to a 2×3 grid `[1,2,3; 1,2,3]` for the addition.

---

#### (b) Demonstrate **matrix multiplication** and **transpose** operations on DataFrames using a worked $2 \times 3$ by $3 \times 2$ example. State the resulting shapes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Setup (1 mark):**
$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \quad (2 \times 3), \qquad Q = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 8 \\ 9 & 10 \\ 11 & 12 \end{bmatrix} \quad (3 \times 2)
$$

**Matrix multiplication** $R = P \cdot Q$ using $R_{ij} = \sum_k P_{ik} Q_{kj}$ **(4 marks):**

**Cell $(0,0)$:**
$$
R_{00} = (1)(7) + (2)(9) + (3)(11) = 7 + 18 + 33 = 58
$$

**Cell $(0,1)$:**
$$
R_{01} = (1)(8) + (2)(10) + (3)(12) = 8 + 20 + 36 = 64
$$

**Cell $(1,0)$:**
$$
R_{10} = (4)(7) + (5)(9) + (6)(11) = 28 + 45 + 66 = 139
$$

**Cell $(1,1)$:**
$$
R_{11} = (4)(8) + (5)(10) + (6)(12) = 32 + 50 + 72 = 154
$$

Therefore:
$$
R = P \cdot Q = \begin{bmatrix} 58 & 64 \\ 139 & 154 \end{bmatrix} \quad (2 \times 2)
$$

**Pandas syntax:** `P @ Q` or `P.dot(Q)`.

**Transpose** $P^{T}$ **(2 marks):**

Using $(P^{T})_{ij} = P_{ji}$:
$$
P^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 4 \\ 2 & 5 \\ 3 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \quad (3 \times 2)
$$

**Pandas syntax:** `P.T` or `P.transpose()`.

**Final shape summary:**

| Operation | Input shape | Output shape |
|---|---|---|
| $P \cdot Q$ | $(2,3) \times (3,2)$ | $(2,2)$ |
| $P^{T}$ | $(2,3)$ | $(3,2)$ |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

#### (a) Explain **axis-wise aggregation** in DataFrames. Compute the **column-wise mean**, **row-wise mean**, and **column-wise standard deviation** for the following frame:
$$
M = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 4 & 6 \\ 6 & 8 \end{bmatrix}
$$
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 marks):** Axis-wise aggregation collapses one axis of the DataFrame using a reduction function. With `axis=0` (default), the function moves *down the rows* and returns one value per **column** (output shape = number of columns). With `axis=1`, the function moves *across the columns* and returns one value per **row** (output shape = number of rows).

**Column-wise mean** $\mu_j = \frac{1}{m}\sum_i M_{ij}$ **(2 marks):**

For column 0 ($m=3$):
$$
\mu_0 = \frac{1}{3}(2 + 4 + 6) = \frac{12}{3} = 4.0
$$

For column 1:
$$
\mu_1 = \frac{1}{3}(4 + 6 + 8) = \frac{18}{3} = 6.0
$$

Result: $\mu = [4.0,\; 6.0]$.

**Row-wise mean** $\mu_i = \frac{1}{n}\sum_j M_{ij}$ **(1.5 marks):**

$$
\mu_0 = \frac{1}{2}(2 + 4) = 3.0, \quad \mu_1 = \frac{1}{2}(4 + 6) = 5.0, \quad \mu_2 = \frac{1}{2}(6 + 8) = 7.0
$$

Result: $\mu = [3.0,\; 5.0,\; 7.0]$.

**Column-wise standard deviation** $\sigma_j = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m-1}\sum_i (M_{ij} - \mu_j)^2}$ **(1.5 marks):**

For column 0 ($\mu_0 = 4$):
$$
\sigma_0 = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}[(2-4)^2 + (4-4)^2 + (6-4)^2]} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}(4+0+4)} = \sqrt{4} = 2.0
$$

For column 1 ($\mu_1 = 6$):
$$
\sigma_1 = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}[(4-6)^2 + (6-6)^2 + (8-6)^2]} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}(4+0+4)} = \sqrt{4} = 2.0
$$

Result: $\sigma = [2.0,\; 2.0]$.

---

#### (b) What are **NumPy ufuncs**? Apply `np.sqrt`, `np.log`, and `np.exp` on the following DataFrame and write the resulting values:
$$
N = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 4 \\ 9 & 16 \end{bmatrix}
$$
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 marks):** NumPy *universal functions* (ufuncs) are vectorized C-implemented functions that operate element-wise on `ndarray`s and, by extension, on Pandas DataFrames. When a ufunc is called on a frame, the function is applied to *every numeric cell independently*, the shape of the frame is preserved, and the resulting frame inherits the original row/column labels.

The relevant ufuncs here are:
- $\text{sqrt}(x) = \sqrt{x}$
- $\log(x) = \ln(x)$ (natural logarithm)
- $\exp(x) = e^{x}$

**Application on N (5 marks):**

**`np.sqrt(N)`** — element-wise square root:

For cell $(0,0)$: $\sqrt{1} = 1.0$
For cell $(0,1)$: $\sqrt{4} = 2.0$
For cell $(1,0)$: $\sqrt{9} = 3.0$
For cell $(1,1)$: $\sqrt{16} = 4.0$

$$
\text{sqrt}(N) = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 2.0 \\ 3.0 & 4.0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**`np.log(N)`** — element-wise natural log:

For cell $(0,0)$: $\ln(1) = 0.0$
For cell $(0,1)$: $\ln(4) \approx 1.3863$
For cell $(1,0)$: $\ln(9) \approx 2.1972$
For cell $(1,1)$: $\ln(16) \approx 2.7726$

$$
\log(N) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0000 & 1.3863 \\ 2.1972 & 2.7726 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**`np.exp(N)`** — element-wise exponential:

For cell $(0,0)$: $e^{1} \approx 2.7183$
For cell $(0,1)$: $e^{4} \approx 54.5982$
For cell $(1,0)$: $e^{9} \approx 8103.0839$
For cell $(1,1)$: $e^{16} \approx 8886110.5205$

$$
\exp(N) = \begin{bmatrix} 2.7183 & 54.5982 \\ 8103.0839 & 8886110.5205 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Pandas syntax:**
```python
N = pd.DataFrame([[1,4],[9,16]], columns=['a','b'])
np.sqrt(N)
np.log(N)
np.exp(N)
```

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `*` with `@`** — The most common and most penalized mistake. `df1 * df2` is **element-wise (Hadamard)**; `df1 @ df2` or `df1.dot(df2)` is **true matrix product**. A wrong operator usually means **0 marks** for the entire sub-part.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Bessel's correction ($N-1$) in `std()`** — Pandas uses sample standard deviation with denominator $N-1$, not $N$. If the question says "standard deviation" without specifying population/sample, default to Pandas behaviour (sample, $N-1$).
>
> 3. **Mixing up `axis=0` and `axis=1`** — `axis=0` collapses **rows** (returns one value per column); `axis=1` collapses **columns** (returns one value per row). The standard mnemonic: *"axis=0 walks down rows."*
>
> 4. **Ignoring NaN handling in aggregations** — By default `.sum()`, `.mean()` skip NaN, but **element-wise arithmetic propagates NaN**. Failing to mention this in a "Discuss NaN handling" question loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Shape-rule violations in broadcasting** — Adding two frames of shape $(2,3)$ and $(2,4)$ raises a `ValueError`. Students often fabricate answers instead of stating the rule.
>
> 6. **Not showing the summation expansion in matrix multiplication** — Board examiners award 1 mark purely for writing $C_{ij} = \sum_k A_{ik} B_{kj}$ *before* substituting numbers. Skipping this loses a mark even if the arithmetic is correct.
>
> 7. **Treating `df.T` as a "view" or "copy" question** — `df.T` returns a *new* DataFrame (transposed view); subsequent modifications to `df.T` do **not** modify the original `df`. Mention this if the question is about mutability.
>
> 8. **Forgetting the `axis` parameter in `cumsum`** — `cumsum(axis=0)` is column-wise cumulative; `cumsum(axis=1)` is row-wise. Mixing them up reverses the answer.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read Before Entering Exam Hall)**

- [x] A **DataFrame** is a 2D labeled structure; matrix-like operations treat it as a numerical matrix.
- [x] **Five families** of matrix-like operations: arithmetic, broadcasting, dot product, transpose, aggregation.
- [x] **Element-wise** uses `*`; **matrix product** uses `@` or `.dot()` — *never confuse them.*
- [x] **Broadcasting rule:** shapes are compatible if equal or one is 1 in that dimension; Pandas *also* requires label alignment.
- [x] **A scalar** always broadcasts trivially; a **1D Series** of length $n$ broadcasts across the row axis of an $m \times n$ frame.
- [x] **Transpose** swaps rows and columns: $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n} \Rightarrow A^{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$.
- [x] **Matrix multiplication shape rule:** $(m, k) \cdot (k, n) \to (m, n)$. Inner dimensions must match.
- [x] **`axis=0`** → operate down rows → output has one value per *column*; **`axis=1`** → output per *row*.
- [x] **Sample standard deviation** uses $N-1$ (Bessel's correction); this is the Pandas default.
- [x] **NaN propagation:** arithmetic propagates NaN; aggregation (`sum`, `mean`, `std`) skips NaN by default.
- [x] **NumPy ufuncs** (`np.sqrt`, `np.log`, `np.exp`, `np.sin`, etc.) apply element-wise and preserve shape.
- [x] **Vectorization** is the *primary* reason for speed: 100×–1000× faster than Python `for`-loops.
- [x] The same conceptual operations exist in **Apache Spark** and **Dask** for distributed Big Data.
- [x] In KTU answers, *always* write the closed-form formula (e.g., $C_{ij} = \sum_k A_{ik} B_{kj}$) **before** substituting numbers.
- [x] Remember: $\vert S_j \vert$ (cardinality notation) when writing NaN-safe aggregation formulas.
- [x] **Mnemonic for `axis`:** *"axis=0 walks down rows" — like indexing the first axis of a list of rows."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applying Functions to Data Frames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Applying Functions to Data Frames # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** In the **Pandas** library of **Python**, *Applying Functions to Data Frames* refers to the systematic application of built-in, user-defined, or **NumPy** vectorized functions along the axes of a `DataFrame` object (rows or columns), across individual elements, or to entire groups of data. The primary tools used for this purpose are the methods: `apply()`, `applymap()`, `map()`, `agg()`, `transform()`, and `pipe()`. The function application paradigm in Pandas is built on the philosophy of **vectorization** — the idea that operations should be applied to entire arrays of data rather than iterating element-by-element in pure Python. This dramatically improves performance for large-scale **Big Data** analytics workloads. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Factory Assembly Line":** > Imagine a factory where you have a large table (DataFrame) of raw products (data) arranged in rows and columns. You want to perform specific operations on these products: > - **apply()** = Sending the **entire row** or **entire column** to a specialized machine for processing (axis-based). > - **applymap()** = Sending **every single item** on the table through a small polishing machine one by one (element-wise on a whole DataFrame). > - **map()** = Using a **lookup dictionary** to substitute values in a single column (Series). > - **agg()** = Running **multiple quality checks** on a batch and returning multiple summary statistics at once. > - **pipe()** = Adding a **conveyor belt extension** to chain multiple processing stages together cleanly. ## 1.3 The Core Family of Function Application Methods | Method | Scope (Target Object) | Direction | Returns | |---|---|---|---| | `Series.apply()` | Series | Element-wise | Scalar or Series | | `DataFrame.apply()` | DataFrame | Row-wise (`axis=1`) or Column-wise (`axis=0`) | Scalar or Series | | `DataFrame.applymap()` | DataFrame | Element-wise | DataFrame | | `Series.map()` | Series | Element-wise (with mapping) | Series | | `DataFrame.agg()` | DataFrame / Series | Row or Column (aggregations) | Scalar or DataFrame | | `DataFrame.transform()` | DataFrame / Series | Row or Column (transformations) | Same shape as input | | `DataFrame.pipe()` | DataFrame | Method chaining | DataFrame | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:** `applymap()` is **deprecated** in recent Pandas versions in favor of `DataFrame.map()`. However, for KTU 2024 syllabus alignment, both are valid and likely to be examined. Always check the version compatibility in your lab environment. --- ## 1.4 Lambda Functions — The Workhorse of Inline Logic A **lambda function** in Python is a small, anonymous, single-expression function used heavily when applying custom logic inline to data. ```python # Standard syntax lambda arguments: expression ``` **Example:** ```python square = lambda x: x ** 2 print(square(5)) # Output: 25 ``` > Lambdas are essential because `apply()` and friends expect a *function reference*, not a *value*. We pass a lambda where we would otherwise need a named `def` function. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Method-by-Method Analysis ### 2.1.1 `DataFrame.apply(func, axis=0)` — Column/Row-Wise Application The `apply()` method invokes a function on each **column** (when `axis=0`, the default) or each **row** (when `axis=1`) of a DataFrame. **Operational Logic Steps:** - The function `func` is called once for each column (or row) of the DataFrame. - The column/row is passed as a **Pandas Series** to the function. - If the function returns a scalar value, the result is a Series of those scalars (one per column/row). - If the function returns a Series, the result is a DataFrame. **Why this matters in Big Data:** In real Big Data pipelines (e.g., Spark, Dask), operations are frequently expressed as *"for each column, do X"*. `apply()` mirrors this declarative paradigm and is the most commonly tested method in KTU exams. ### 2.1.2 `Series.apply(func)` — Element-Wise Application on a Series This is the most flexible method. It applies `func` to each value in the Series individually. **Critical Note:** For purely numerical operations, **prefer vectorized operations** (e.g., `df['col'] * 2`) over `apply()` because they are **10x–100x faster** in pure Pandas. ### 2.1.3 `DataFrame.applymap(func)` — Element-Wise on Whole DataFrame Applies `func` to **every single value** in the DataFrame. Useful for formatting (e.g., converting all floats to 2 decimal strings) or type conversion. ### 2.1.4 `Series.map(arg)` — Value Mapping / Substitution The `map()` method on a Series is used to **substitute** values using: - A **dictionary** (key → new value). - A **function** (applied element-wise). - A **Series** (value alignment by label). Missing keys produce `NaN` (a critical exam pitfall!). ### 2.1.5 `DataFrame.agg(func)` — Multiple Aggregations Simultaneously `agg()` (alias: `aggregate()`) runs **one or more** aggregation functions across an axis. ```python df.agg(['sum', 'mean', 'max']) # Multiple functions → DataFrame df.agg({'A': 'sum', 'B': 'mean'}) # Per-column dict → DataFrame ``` ### 2.1.6 `DataFrame.transform(func)` — Same-Shape Transformations Unlike `agg()` which **reduces** the data, `transform()` returns an object with the **same shape** as the input. This is the standard for feature engineering in ML pipelines (e.g., z-score normalization per group). ### 2.1.7 `DataFrame.pipe(func, *args, **kwargs)` — Method Chaining `pipe()` applies a function that takes the **entire DataFrame** as its first argument. It enables clean, readable **method chains** instead of nested function calls. ```python result = (df.pipe(clean_data) .pipe(add_features) .pipe(export_to_csv)) ``` ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Syntax | Key Constraint | Typical Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | Column-wise apply | `df.apply(func, axis=0)` | `func` receives a Series | Per-column statistics | | Row-wise apply | `df.apply(func, axis=1)` | `func` receives a Series | Conditional row logic | | Element-wise DF | `df.applymap(func)` | All cells processed | Type/format conversion | | Series element-wise | `df['col'].apply(func)` | Returns same length | Custom transformations | | Value mapping | `df['col'].map(dict)` | Missing keys → `NaN` | Category replacement | | Multi-aggregation | `df.agg(['sum','mean'])` | Returns reduced shape | Summary statistics | | Same-shape transform | `df.transform(func)` | Must return same shape | Group-wise scaling | | Function chain | `df.pipe(func)` | 1st arg is the DF | Clean pipeline design | | Vectorized op (preferred) | `df['col'] * 2` | NumPy under the hood | Numerical computations | | Lambda | `lambda x: x*2` | Single expression only | Inline custom logic | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Big Data Analytics lab manual (Module 2) explicitly tests `apply()`, `applymap()`, `map()`, and `agg()` with both `lambda` and user-defined functions. Be prepared to write these from memory in the lab exam. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields - **Finance:** Apply risk-scoring functions row-wise across millions of transactions. - **IoT Analytics:** Use `applymap()` to convert raw sensor voltage readings to engineering units. - **Bioinformatics:** Use `map()` to translate gene IDs into readable gene names using a dictionary. - **ETL Pipelines:** Use `pipe()` to chain extraction → transformation → loading steps. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Setup — Building the Working DataFrame We will use a single consistent dataset throughout the derivations to make execution predictable. ```python import pandas as pd import numpy as np # Sample DataFrame for all derivations below data = { 'Name': ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie', 'Diana', 'Ethan'], 'Age': [25, 30, 35, 28, 40], 'Salary': [50000, 60000, 70000, 55000, 80000], 'Dept': ['IT', 'HR', 'IT', 'Finance', 'HR'] } df = pd.DataFrame(data) print(df) ``` **Output:** ``` Name Age Salary Dept 0 Alice 25 50000 IT 1 Bob 30 60000 HR 2 Charlie 35 70000 IT 3 Diana 28 55000 Finance 4 Ethan 40 80000 HR ``` ## 3.2 Derivation 1 — Column-Wise Application (`apply` with `axis=0`) **Problem:** Find the **range** (max − min) of each numerical column. ```python result = df.apply(lambda col: col.max() - col.min(), axis=0) print(result) ``` **Step-by-step logic:** - `axis=0` tells Pandas to iterate **column-by-column**. - For each column (a Series), the lambda computes `col.max() - col.min()`. - Age column: 40 − 25 = **15** - Salary column: 80000 − 50000 = **30000** - Name and Dept are non-numeric, so the lambda returns `NaN` for them (TypeError caught by Pandas). **Output:** ``` Name NaN Age 15 Salary 30000 Dept NaN dtype: object ``` ## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Row-Wise Application (`apply` with `axis=1`) **Problem:** Create a new column `Tax` that is **10% of Salary** if Age > 30, else **5%**. ```python df['Tax'] = df.apply( lambda row: row['Salary'] * 0.10 if row['Age'] > 30 else row['Salary'] * 0.05, axis=1 ) print(df) ``` **Step-by-step evaluation (row by row):** | Row | Name | Age | Salary | Age > 30? | Tax Calculation | Tax Value | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | Alice | 25 | 50000 | False | 50000 × 0.05 | 2500 | | 1 | Bob | 30 | 60000 | False | 60000 × 0.05 | 3000 | | 2 | Charlie | 35 | 70000 | True | 70000 × 0.10 | 7000 | | 3 | Diana | 28 | 55000 | False | 55000 × 0.05 | 2750 | | 4 | Ethan | 40 | 80000 | True | 80000 × 0.10 | 8000 | **Output:** ``` Name Age Salary Dept Tax 0 Alice 25 50000 IT 2500 1 Bob 30 60000 HR 3000 2 Charlie 35 70000 IT 7000 3 Diana 28 55000 Finance 2750 4 Ethan 40 80000 HR 8000 ``` ## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Element-Wise Application with `applymap()` **Problem:** Format all numeric entries as currency-style strings with a "$" prefix. ```python df_numeric = df[['Age', 'Salary', 'Tax']] formatted = df_numeric.applymap(lambda x: f"${x:,.2f}") print(formatted) ``` **Logic:** Every cell in the DataFrame is passed individually to the lambda. The f-string `f"${x:,.2f}"` formats the number with 2 decimal places and a thousands separator. **Output:** ``` Age Salary Tax 0 $25.00 $50,000.00 $2,500.00 1 $30.00 $60,000.00 $3,000.00 2 $35.00 $70,000.00 $7,000.00 3 $28.00 $55,000.00 $2,750.00 4 $40.00 $80,000.00 $8,000.00 ``` ## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Value Mapping with `Series.map()` **Problem:** Replace department codes with their full names using a dictionary. ```python dept_map = {'IT': 'Information Tech', 'HR': 'Human Resources', 'Finance': 'Finance & Accounts'} df['Dept_Full'] = df['Dept'].map(dept_map) print(df[['Name', 'Dept', 'Dept_Full']]) ``` **Logic:** The `map()` method looks up each value in `df['Dept']` against the keys of `dept_map` and substitutes the corresponding value. If a key is missing, the result is `NaN`. **Output:** ``` Name Dept Dept_Full 0 Alice IT Information Tech 1 Bob HR Human Resources 2 Charlie IT Information Tech 3 Diana Finance Finance & Accounts 4 Ethan HR Human Resources ``` ## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Multiple Aggregations with `agg()` **Problem:** Compute sum, mean, max, and min of all numerical columns in a single call. ```python summary = df.agg({ 'Age': ['sum', 'mean', 'max', 'min'], 'Salary':['sum', 'mean', 'max', 'min'], 'Tax': ['sum', 'mean', 'max', 'min'] }) print(summary) ``` **Logic:** `agg()` accepts a dictionary mapping column names to a **list of aggregation functions**. It applies each function to the specified column and returns a DataFrame where rows are the function names and columns are the original column names. **Output:** ``` Age Salary Tax sum 158.0 315000.00 23250.00 mean 31.6 63000.00 4650.00 max 40.0 80000.00 8000.00 min 25.0 50000.00 2500.00 ``` ## 3.7 Derivation 6 — Transformation with `transform()` **Problem:** Standardize the `Salary` column (z-score) and add it as a new column. The **z-score formula** is: $$ z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} $$ where $\mu$ is the mean and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation. **Numerical evaluation:** $$\mu_{\text{Salary}} = \frac{50000 + 60000 + 70000 + 55000 + 80000}{5} = \frac{315000}{5} = 63000$$ $$\sigma_{\text{Salary}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2}$$ Computing each squared deviation: $$(50000 - 63000)^2 = (-13000)^2 = 169{,}000{,}000$$ $$(60000 - 63000)^2 = (-3000)^2 = 9{,}000{,}000$$ $$(70000 - 63000)^2 = (7000)^2 = 49{,}000{,}000$$ $$(55000 - 63000)^2 = (-8000)^2 = 64{,}000{,}000$$ $$(80000 - 63000)^2 = (17000)^2 = 289{,}000{,}000$$ Summing the squared deviations: $$\sum (x_i - \mu)^2 = 169 + 9 + 49 + 64 + 289 \text{ (in millions)} = 580 \text{ million}$$ $$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{580{,}000{,}000}{5}} = \sqrt{116{,}000{,}000} \approx 10770.33$$ **Pandas implementation using `transform()`:** ```python mean_salary = df['Salary'].mean() std_salary = df['Salary'].std() df['Salary_Z'] = df['Salary'].transform(lambda x: (x - x.mean()) / x.std()) print(df[['Name', 'Salary', 'Salary_Z']]) ``` **Why use `transform()` instead of manual calculation?** `transform()` returns a Series with the **same index as the original DataFrame**, which means it can be **directly assigned as a new column** without alignment issues. This is critical for grouped operations. ## 3.8 Derivation 7 — Function Chaining with `pipe()` **Problem:** Build a reusable data-cleaning pipeline. ```python def drop_missing(df): return df.dropna() def add_bonus_column(df, bonus_pct): df = df.copy() df['Bonus'] = df['Salary'] * bonus_pct return df def filter_high_earners(df, threshold): return df[df['Salary'] > threshold] # Chain using pipe result = (df .pipe(drop_missing) .pipe(add_bonus_column, bonus_pct=0.10) .pipe(filter_high_earners, threshold=55000)) print(result) ``` **Output:** ``` Name Age Salary Dept Tax Dept_Full Bonus 1 Bob 30 60000 HR 3000 Human Resources 6000.0 2 Charlie 35 70000 IT 7000 Information Tech 7000.0 4 Ethan 40 80000 HR 8000 Human Resources 8000.0 ``` **Logic:** Each function takes a DataFrame and returns a DataFrame. `pipe()` passes the result of one stage to the next, producing a clean, linear, left-to-right readable pipeline. ## 3.9 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Method Should I Use? | If You Need To... | Use This Method | Reason | |---|---|---| | Compute a function over each column | `df.apply(func, axis=0)` | Default direction | | Compute a function over each row | `df.apply(func, axis=1)` | Row-by-row logic | | Modify every single cell in DF | `df.applymap(func)` | True element-wise | | Map values via dictionary in a Series | `df['col'].map(dict)` | Fastest mapping | | Run multiple aggregations at once | `df.agg([...])` | Returns DataFrame | | Get group-wise normalized values | `df.transform(func)` | Same shape as input | | Build a clean function chain | `df.pipe(func)` | Readable pipelines | | Do simple arithmetic on a Series | `df['col'] * n` | Vectorized, fastest | ## 3.10 Performance Comparison Formula (Conceptual) > [!IMPORTANT] > The performance hierarchy from **fastest to slowest** is: > 1. **Vectorized NumPy/Pandas ops** (e.g., `df['A'] + df['B']`) > 2. **`agg()` / `transform()`** with built-in string names > 3. **`apply()`** with NumPy ufuncs > 4. **`apply()` / `applymap()`** with Python lambdas > 5. **Python `for` loops via `iterrows()`** (avoid in Big Data!) Rule of thumb from KTU lab observations: **prefer vectorized operations whenever possible**, and reserve `apply()` for cases where vectorization is genuinely impossible. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Map — The Function Application Family ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([DataFrame or Series]) --> Q1{Apply to whole DF
or single column?} Q1 -->|Whole DataFrame| Q2{Need element-wise
or axis-wise?} Q1 -->|Single Series| S1{Element-wise?} S1 -->|Yes, with mapping| S1A[Series.map - dict, function, or Series] S1 -->|Yes, with logic| S1B[Series.apply - lambda or def] S1 -->|Aggregation| S1C[Series.agg - sum, mean, etc.] S1 -->|Same shape result| S1D[Series.transform] Q2 -->|Element-wise every cell| Q2A[DataFrame.applymap - lambda on every cell] Q2 -->|Column-wise axis 0| Q2B[DataFrame.apply axis equals 0 - per column] Q2 -->|Row-wise axis 1| Q2C[DataFrame.apply axis equals 1 - per row] Q2 -->|Multiple aggregations| Q2D[DataFrame.agg - dict or list of funcs] Q2 -->|Same shape result| Q2E[DataFrame.transform - lambda or func] Q2 -->|Chain with custom func| Q2F[DataFrame.pipe - method chaining] classDef default fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef method fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class Q1,Q2 decision class S1A,S1B,S1C,S1D,Q2A,Q2B,Q2C,Q2D,Q2E,Q2F method ``` ## 4.2 Data Flow Diagram — Row-Wise `apply()` Execution ```mermaid flowchart LR A[DataFrame df
5 rows x 4 cols] --> B[apply lambda, axis equals 1] B --> C1[Row 0: Series
Alice,25,50000,IT] B --> C2[Row 1: Series
Bob,30,60000,HR] B --> C3[Row 2: Series
Charlie,35,70000,IT] B --> C4[Row 3: Series
Diana,28,55000,Finance] B --> C5[Row 4: Series
Ethan,40,80000,HR] C1 --> D1[Lambda returns 2500] C2 --> D2[Lambda returns 3000] C3 --> D3[Lambda returns 7000] C4 --> D4[Lambda returns 2750] C5 --> D5[Lambda returns 8000] D1 --> E[New Column Tax
2500, 3000, 7000, 2750, 8000] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E D5 --> E classDef input fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000 classDef process fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 classDef output fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 class A input class B,C1,C2,C3,C4,C5 process class D1,D2,D3,D4,D5,E output ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Big Data Function Application Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InputLayer [INPUT LAYER] I1[Raw CSV File] I2[Database Table] I3[JSON / API Stream] end subgraph LoadLayer [LOAD LAYER] L1[pd.read_csv] L2[pd.read_sql] L3[pd.read_json] end subgraph ApplyLayer [FUNCTION APPLICATION LAYER] A1[applymap - element-wise formatting] A2[apply axis 0 - column aggregations] A3[apply axis 1 - row-wise logic] A4[map - dictionary substitution] A5[agg - multi-function summary] A6[transform - same-shape normalization] A7[pipe - chained pipeline] end subgraph OutputLayer [OUTPUT LAYER] O1[Cleaned DataFrame] O2[Summary Report] O3[ML-Ready Features] O4[Visualization Input] end I1 --> L1 I2 --> L2 I3 --> L3 L1 --> A1 L1 --> A2 L2 --> A3 L2 --> A4 L3 --> A5 L1 --> A6 L2 --> A7 A1 --> O1 A2 --> O2 A3 --> O3 A4 --> O1 A5 --> O2 A6 --> O3 A7 --> O4 classDef inputNode fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 classDef loadNode fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 classDef applyNode fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 classDef outputNode fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 class I1,I2,I3 inputNode class L1,L2,L3 loadNode class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7 applyNode class O1,O2,O3,O4 outputNode ``` ## 4.4 Transformation Sequence — `transform()` for Z-Score Normalization ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input: Salary Column] --> B[Compute Mean mu] A --> C[Compute Std Dev sigma] B --> D[Subtract mu from each x] C --> D D --> E[Divide each result by sigma] E --> F[Output: Z-Score Column
Same length as input] classDef step fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff8f00,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,E,F step ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]** > > **Q1.** What is the difference between `apply()` and `applymap()` in Pandas? When would you use each? **Model Answer:** > `apply()` is used to apply a function along an **axis** of a DataFrame (row-wise with `axis=1` or column-wise with `axis=0`) or element-wise on a **Series**. The function receives either a row/column as a Series or a single value. > > `applymap()` is used **only on a DataFrame** to apply a function to **every individual element** (cell), regardless of row or column. It does not have an axis parameter. > > **Use Cases:** > - Use `apply()` with `axis=1` when row-level logic is needed (e.g., conditional computation across columns). > - Use `apply()` with `axis=0` for column-wise aggregations. > - Use `applymap()` for element-wise transformations across the entire DataFrame, such as formatting all values to strings or type conversion. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]** > > **Q2.** Explain the purpose of the `map()` function in a Pandas Series with an example. **Model Answer:** > The `map()` function in a Pandas Series is used to **substitute or transform each value** in the Series according to a mapping correspondence. It accepts three types of arguments: > 1. A **dictionary** for value substitution. > 2. A **function** applied element-wise. > 3. Another **Series** for label-based alignment. > > **Example:** > ```python > import pandas as pd > s = pd.Series(['cat', 'dog', 'bird']) > mapping = {'cat': 'mammal', 'dog': 'mammal', 'bird': 'avian'} > print(s.map(mapping)) > ``` > **Output:** > ``` > 0 mammal > 1 mammal > 2 avian > dtype: object > ``` > If a value has no corresponding key in the dictionary, the result is `NaN`. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** > > **(a)** With a suitable example, explain how the `apply()` function can be used with both `axis=0` and `axis=1` parameters on a Pandas DataFrame. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Demonstrate the use of `applymap()` and `agg()` methods on a DataFrame. Show how multiple aggregation functions can be applied simultaneously. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution for Question A:** #### **Part (a) — `apply()` with `axis=0` and `axis=1`** **Step 1: Create a DataFrame** ```python import pandas as pd data = { 'Product': ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D'], 'Price': [100, 200, 150, 300], 'Qty': [5, 3, 7, 2] } df = pd.DataFrame(data) print(df) ``` **Output:** ``` Product Price Qty 0 A 100 5 1 B 200 3 2 C 150 7 3 D 300 2 ``` **Step 2: `axis=0` — Column-wise application** When `axis=0` (default), the lambda receives each **column as a Series**. ```python col_means = df.apply(lambda col: col.mean() if col.dtype != 'O' else None, axis=0) print(col_means) ``` **Output:** ``` Product None Price 187.5 Qty 4.25 dtype: object ``` **Logic:** The function iterates column-by-column. For numeric columns, it computes the mean; for the object (string) column, it returns `None`. **Step 3: `axis=1` — Row-wise application** ```python df['Total'] = df.apply(lambda row: row['Price'] * row['Qty'], axis=1) print(df) ``` **Output:** ``` Product Price Qty Total 0 A 100 5 500 1 B 200 3 600 2 C 150 7 1050 3 D 300 2 600 ``` **Logic:** The function iterates row-by-row. Each row is a Series, and we compute `Price * Qty` to get the row total. **Key Conceptual Difference:** | `axis=0` | `axis=1` | |---|---| | Function processes each **column** | Function processes each **row** | | Default in Pandas | Must be specified explicitly | | Used for column statistics | Used for row-level business logic | #### **Part (b) — `applymap()` and `agg()`** **Step 1: Use `applymap()` for element-wise formatting** ```python df_numeric = df[['Price', 'Qty', 'Total']] formatted = df_numeric.applymap(lambda x: f"₹{x:,.2f}") print(formatted) ``` **Output:** ``` Price Qy Total 0 ₹100.00 ₹5.00 ₹500.00 1 ₹200.00 ₹3.00 ₹600.00 2 ₹150.00 ₹7.00 ₹1,050.00 3 ₹300.00 ₹2.00 ₹600.00 ``` **Logic:** Every cell in `df_numeric` is passed to the lambda individually, which formats it as Indian Rupee currency. **Step 2: Use `agg()` for multiple aggregations** ```python summary = df.agg({ 'Price': ['sum', 'mean', 'min', 'max'], 'Qty': ['sum', 'mean', 'min', 'max'], 'Total': ['sum', 'mean', 'min', 'max'] }) print(summary) ``` **Output:** ``` Price Qy Total sum 750.00 17.00 2750.00 mean 187.50 4.25 687.50 min 100.00 2.00 500.00 max 300.00 7.00 1050.00 ``` **Logic:** `agg()` accepts a dictionary mapping column names to a list of aggregation function **names** (as strings). It returns a DataFrame where rows are the aggregation functions and columns are the original DataFrame columns. **Alternative: Using lambda inside `agg()`** ```python result = df[['Price', 'Qty']].agg(lambda x: x.max() - x.min()) print(result) ``` **Output:** ``` Price 200 Qty 5 dtype: int64 ``` This computes the **range** of each numeric column in a single call. --- > ### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]** > > **(a)** What is a lambda function in Python? Explain its use in `apply()` with a suitable example. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Demonstrate the use of `Series.map()`, `transform()`, and `pipe()` methods on a Pandas DataFrame. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### **Part (a) — Lambda Function and `apply()`** **Definition of Lambda Function:** A **lambda function** in Python is a small, anonymous, single-expression function defined using the `lambda` keyword. It can take any number of arguments but can only have **one expression** that is implicitly returned. **Syntax:** ```python lambda arguments: expression ``` **Example of a Normal Function vs Lambda:** ```python # Normal function def square(x): return x ** 2 # Equivalent lambda square_lambda = lambda x: x ** 2 print(square(5)) # Output: 25 print(square_lambda(5)) # Output: 25 ``` **Use of Lambda with `apply()`:** The `apply()` method expects a **function reference** as its first argument. Lambdas are perfect for this because we can define the function inline without naming it. **Example:** ```python import pandas as pd df = pd.DataFrame({ 'Name': ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie'], 'Marks': [85, 92, 78] }) # Using lambda with apply to assign grades df['Grade'] = df['Marks'].apply(lambda m: 'A' if m >= 90 else ('B' if m >= 80 else 'C')) print(df) ``` **Output:** ``` Name Marks Grade 0 Alice 85 B 1 Bob 92 A 2 Charlie 78 C ``` **Logic:** The lambda receives each value in the `Marks` column one at a time and returns a grade based on the conditional logic. **Why Lambdas Matter in Big Data:** In Big Data analytics, we often need to apply custom transformation logic to millions of rows. Writing a separate `def` function for every such transformation is verbose. Lambdas provide a **concise, inline** alternative that keeps the code readable and compact. #### **Part (b) — `Series.map()`, `transform()`, and `pipe()`** **Step 1: `Series.map()` for Value Substitution** ```python df = pd.DataFrame({ 'Name': ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie'], 'Dept': ['IT', 'HR', 'IT'] }) # Map department codes to full names dept_full = {'IT': 'Information Technology', 'HR': 'Human Resources'} df['Dept_Full'] = df['Dept'].map(dept_full) print(df) ``` **Output:** ``` Name Dept Dept_Full 0 Alice IT Information Technology 1 Bob HR Human Resources 2 Charlie IT Information Technology ``` **Step 2: `transform()` for Same-Shape Transformation** ```python # Standardize the Age column df['Age'] = [25, 30, 35] df['Age_Z'] = df['Age'].transform(lambda x: (x - x.mean()) / x.std()) print(df) ``` **Output:** ``` Name Dept Dept_Full Age Age_Z 0 Alice IT Information Technology 25 -1.224745 1 Bob HR Human Resources 30 0.000000 2 Charlie IT Information Technology 35 1.224745 ``` **Logic:** `transform()` returns a Series of the **same length** as the input, enabling direct assignment. The z-score formula: $$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$ is applied element-wise. **Step 3: `pipe()` for Method Chaining** ```python def uppercase_names(dataf): dataf = dataf.copy() dataf['Name'] = dataf['Name'].str.upper() return dataf def add_greeting(dataf): dataf = dataf.copy() dataf['Greeting'] = 'Hello, ' + dataf['Name'] return dataf # Chain using pipe result = (df .pipe(uppercase_names) .pipe(add_greeting)) print(result[['Name', 'Greeting']]) ``` **Output:** ``` Name Greeting 0 ALICE Hello, ALICE 1 BOB Hello, BOB 2 CHARLIE Hello, CHARLIE ``` **Logic:** `pipe()` passes the DataFrame through a sequence of user-defined functions, producing a clean, left-to-right readable pipeline. The 1st argument of each piped function is the DataFrame itself; additional arguments can be passed positionally or as keyword arguments. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting `axis=1` for row logic:** Students often write `df.apply(lambda x: x['A'] + x['B'])` without specifying `axis=1`. This silently fails because the lambda receives a column, not a row. **Always specify `axis=1` for row-wise operations.** > 2. **Confusing `applymap()` with `apply()`:** `applymap()` does not accept an `axis` parameter. Attempting `df.applymap(func, axis=1)` raises a `TypeError`. > 3. **`map()` produces `NaN` for missing keys:** When mapping with a dictionary, any value not in the dictionary becomes `NaN`. Students often miss this. Use `.fillna()` afterward or use `.replace()` as an alternative. > 4. **Vectorization is faster than `apply()`:** Writing `df['col'].apply(lambda x: x*2)` instead of `df['col'] * 2` is **penalized** in performance-conscious Big Data contexts. Always prefer vectorization. > 5. **`transform()` must return same shape:** If your lambda aggregates (e.g., `sum`), `transform()` will raise a `ValueError`. Use `agg()` for reductions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Applying Functions to Data Frames** - **Core Methods Mastered:** `apply()`, `applymap()`, `map()`, `agg()`, `transform()`, `pipe()`. - **Axis Convention:** `axis=0` → column-wise (default). `axis=1` → row-wise. **Memorize this — it appears in nearly every KTU question on this topic.** - **Lambda Syntax:** `lambda arguments: expression` — single expression only, no statements. - **`apply()` on DataFrame:** Function receives a Series (row or column). Can return scalar → Series, or Series → DataFrame. - **`applymap()` on DataFrame:** Function receives a single value (cell). Always returns a DataFrame. Deprecated in newer Pandas in favor of `DataFrame.map()`. - **`Series.map()`:** Three modes — dictionary, function, or Series. Missing keys → `NaN`. - **`agg()`:** Accepts string function names OR callables. Use a dictionary for per-column function lists. Returns **reduced** shape. - **`transform()`:** Must return the **same shape** as the input. Used for group-wise normalization and feature engineering. - **`pipe()`:** First argument is the DataFrame. Enables clean method chaining instead of nested calls. - **Performance Hierarchy (Fastest → Slowest):** Vectorized ops → `agg()`/`transform()` with strings → `apply()` with NumPy ufuncs → `apply()`/`applymap()` with Python lambdas → `iterrows()` loops. - **KTU Exam Tip:** Always specify `axis` explicitly in `apply()` calls. Examiners reward clarity and penalize ambiguity. - **Common Pitfall:** `df.applymap()` does not exist on a Series. Use `Series.apply()` instead. - **Big Data Context:** These methods form the backbone of feature engineering in Pandas and are direct precursors to Spark's `udf` (User Defined Functions) and Pandas UDFs in PySpark.

topics.title: Reading and Writing Files. 12 4 Overview of Spark - Hadoop Overview of Spark - Hadoop vs. Spark - Cluster Design - Cluster Management - performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Reading and Writing Files. 12 4 Overview of Spark - Hadoop Overview of Spark - Hadoop vs. Spark - Cluster Design - Cluster Management - performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Reading and Writing Files in Big Data Context

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
File I/O (Input/Output) operations in the context of Big Data Analytics refer to the systematic processes of ingesting data from heterogeneous external storage sources (local filesystems, HDFS, S3, HBase, CSV, JSON, Parquet, Avro) into distributed processing frameworks such as Apache Spark and Apache Hadoop, processing that data in-memory or on-disk in parallel across cluster nodes, and persisting the resulting transformed data back to storage for downstream consumption.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** File I/O in Big Data is not a single machine reading a single file — it is the **parallel, distributed, fault-tolerant** read/write of massive datasets (terabytes to petabytes) split across multiple nodes in a compute cluster.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**

Imagine a **massive library** containing 10 million books. If one librarian (a single machine) tries to catalog every book, it would take years. Instead, we hire **1,000 librarians** (cluster nodes), divide the library into 1,000 sections, and assign each librarian to one section. Each librarian reads their assigned section in parallel, writes notes (writes output), and shares findings with a head librarian (the driver/master node). This is **distributed file I/O**.

- **Reading Files** = Each librarian picks up their assigned books.
- **Writing Files** = Each librarian writes their catalog notes to a shared, indexed shelf system.
- **Format (CSV, JSON, Parquet)** = The standard template each librarian uses to write notes.
- **HDFS / S3** = The shared indexed shelf system.

---

### 1.2 Overview of Apache Spark

**Formal Definition:**
Apache Spark is an **open-source, distributed, in-memory cluster computing framework** designed for fast, large-scale data processing. It provides high-level APIs in Java, Scala, Python (PySpark), and R, and supports an optimized engine that supports general execution graphs (DAG-based). It extends the **MapReduce model** with in-memory data sharing across iterations, making it well-suited for machine learning, iterative algorithms, and interactive analytics.

**Key Architectural Metrics:**

| Metric | Value / Property |
|---|---|
| **Speed** | Up to **100x faster** than Hadoop MapReduce (in-memory) |
| **In-Memory Computation** | **Yes** (RDDs cached in RAM) |
| **Data Size Limit** | **Petabytes** |
| **Latency** | **Low** (sub-second for interactive queries) |
| **Language APIs** | **Scala, Python, R, Java** |
| **Original Developer** | **Matei Zaharia (UC Berkeley AMPLab, 2009)** |
| **Language** | **Scala** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Spark is a **compute engine**, NOT a storage system. It requires an external storage system (HDFS, S3, Cassandra, local FS) to read and write data.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of **Spark as a high-speed chef's kitchen**. Ingredients (data) come from the refrigerator (HDFS/S3). The chef (Spark driver) coordinates sous-chefs (executors) to prepare dishes (transformations) rapidly on the countertop (RAM) — much faster than repeatedly going back to the fridge (disk) like Hadoop MapReduce does.

---

### 1.3 Overview of Apache Hadoop

**Formal Definition:**
Apache Hadoop is an **open-source framework** that allows for the distributed storage and processing of large datasets across clusters of commodity computers. It is designed to scale up from a single server to thousands of machines, each offering local computation and storage. The core of Hadoop consists of a **storage part (Hadoop Distributed File System — HDFS)** and a **processing part (MapReduce programming model)**.

**The Hadoop Ecosystem — 3 Core Components:**

1. **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** — Storage layer
2. **MapReduce** — Batch processing engine (YARN-managed)
3. **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** — Cluster resource management

**Plus extended ecosystem:** HBase, Hive, Pig, Sqoop, Flume, ZooKeeper, Oozie, Mahout.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** Hadoop = **Storage (HDFS) + Processing (MapReduce) + Resource Management (YARN)**. Spark = **Processing ONLY** (relies on external storage like HDFS).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Hadoop is like a **traditional warehouse with conveyor belts**. Goods (data) are stored on pallets in the warehouse (HDFS). Workers (Map tasks) read goods, process them, and pass them to other workers (Reduce tasks). It's reliable but slow because workers constantly walk back to the warehouse shelves (disk reads/writes between every step).

---

### 1.4 Spark vs. Hadoop — KTU Board Comparison

**Conceptual Analogy:**
- **Hadoop MapReduce** = A chef who must walk to the refrigerator (disk) between every single chop and stir. Slow but methodical.
- **Spark** = A chef with a large countertop (RAM) where all ingredients are pre-laid out. 100x faster for iterative recipes (ML, graph, streaming).

---

### 1.5 Cluster Design (Architecture)

**Formal Definition:**
A Spark/Hadoop **cluster design** refers to the **topology, node roles, and physical/virtual resource layout** used to deploy distributed compute infrastructure. It defines how many master/worker nodes exist, how they communicate, how data partitions are placed, and how fault tolerance is handled.

**Standard Cluster Design:**

| Node Role | Function | Typical Count |
|---|---|---|
| **Master / NameNode / Driver** | Coordinates cluster, manages metadata | **1–3** (HA setup) |
| **Worker / DataNode / Executor** | Stores & processes data | **10s to 1000s** |
| **Edge / Gateway Node** | Client access, job submission | **1–2** |

---

### 1.6 Cluster Management (YARN, Mesos, Standalone)

**Formal Definition:**
**Cluster Management** is the orchestration layer that allocates cluster resources (CPU, memory, disk, network) to running applications, schedules tasks, monitors health, and handles node failures. The three primary cluster managers in the Spark/Hadoop ecosystem are:

1. **Standalone** — Spark's simple built-in manager.
2. **Apache YARN** — Hadoop's resource manager (most common in production).
3. **Apache Mesos** — General-purpose cluster manager (used by Twitter, Airbnb).
4. **Kubernetes** — Modern container orchestrator (cloud-native deployments).

---

### 1.7 Performance Considerations

**Formal Definition:**
Performance in distributed Big Data systems is measured by **throughput (records/sec)**, **latency (response time)**, **resource utilization (CPU/RAM/disk)**, and **fault tolerance overhead**. Key performance factors include data serialization format, partitioning strategy, caching, shuffle behavior, and executor configuration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Insight:** Spark's performance gain over MapReduce comes from **in-memory caching of RDDs/DataFrames** between iterative transformations, eliminating repeated disk I/O.

---

### 1.8 File Formats — Reading & Writing in Spark

**Formal Definition:**
A file format is the **binary or text encoding schema** used to serialize structured/semi-structured data on disk. The choice of format dramatically impacts read/write speed, storage size, and query performance.

| Format | Type | Compression | Schema | Splittable | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CSV** | Text | No | External | Yes | Human-readable, simple data |
| **JSON** | Text | No | Self-describing | Yes | APIs, semi-structured data |
| **Parquet** | Columnar | Yes (Snappy) | Embedded | Yes | **Analytical queries (default)** |
| **ORC** | Columnar | Yes (Zlib) | Embedded | Yes | Hive-heavy ecosystems |
| **Avro** | Row | Yes (Snappy) | Embedded (JSON) | Yes | **Schema evolution, streaming** |
| **SequenceFile** | Binary | Yes | External | Yes | Hadoop MapReduce intermediate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Parquet** is the **de facto default** in Spark for analytical workloads because of columnar pruning, predicate pushdown, and excellent compression ratios.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deep Dive: File Reading Mechanisms in Spark

When a Spark application reads a file, the following **step-by-step logical flow** occurs:

**Step 1: Driver Submission**
The user submits a Spark job via `spark.read.format(...).load(path)`. The **Driver** parses the path and queries the **FileSystem** (e.g., HDFS) to enumerate all data blocks.

**Step 2: Block Discovery**
HDFS splits large files into **blocks** (default **128 MB in HDFS v2+**, 64 MB in v1). The NameNode returns the list of block locations to the Driver.

**Step 3: Task Scheduling (Data Locality)**
The Driver schedules **one task per partition/block** on the **executor co-located with the data block** (NODELOCAL / RACKLOCAL preference). This is called **data locality optimization**.

**Step 4: Partitioning**
Data is distributed as **partitions** of an RDD/DataFrame. The number of partitions = number of tasks = level of parallelism.

**Step 5: Deserialization**
Each executor deserializes its block from the source format (Parquet, CSV, JSON) into in-memory **rows/tuples** for processing.

**Step 6: Lazy Evaluation**
Transformations (`map`, `filter`, `join`) are **lazy** — they build a **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)** of stages. Only an **action** (`count`, `write`, `collect`) triggers execution.

---

### 2.2 Deep Dive: File Writing Mechanisms in Spark

**Step 1: Compute Final Partitions**
Spark computes the final partitions of the DataFrame/RDD after all transformations.

**Step 2: Shuffle & Repartition**
If `partitionBy` or `repartition(n)` is specified, Spark performs a **shuffle** to redistribute data across executors.

**Step 3: Task Execution per Partition**
Each task writes its partition to disk using the specified format's writer.

**Step 4: Output Committer**
The **OutputCommitter** protocol ensures **atomic writes** — either all output files are committed (job success) or none (job failure). The `DirectOutputCommitter` avoids the slow `FileOutputCommitter` rename phase.

**Step 5: Metadata Update**
The NameNode (HDFS) updates its metadata with the new block locations.

---

### 2.3 Deep Dive: Apache Spark Architecture

**The 5-Pillar Spark Architecture:**

1. **Spark Driver** — Runs the `main()` function, creates `SparkContext`/`SparkSession`, builds DAG, schedules tasks.
2. **Cluster Manager** — Allocates resources (YARN, Mesos, K8s, Standalone).
3. **Executors** — Worker JVM processes that run tasks and store data in memory/disk.
4. **SparkContext** — Entry point for Spark functionality; coordinates the cluster.
5. **DAG Scheduler** — Breaks RDD lineage into **stages** of **tasks**.

**Data Abstractions (Stack from Low to High):**

$$
\text{RDD} \rightarrow \text{DataFrame} \rightarrow \text{Dataset}
$$

- **RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset)** — Low-level, type-safe, no schema. **Immutable**, **distributed**, **lazy**.
- **DataFrame** — RDD + **named columns** + **schema**. Catalyst optimizer applies. **Most used**.
- **Dataset** — Type-safe DataFrame (compile-time checks). **Scala/Java only**.

---

### 2.4 Deep Dive: Hadoop Architecture (HDFS + MapReduce + YARN)

**HDFS Architecture:**

- **NameNode (Master)** — Stores **metadata** (file → block mapping). Single point of failure (mitigated by HA: Secondary/Standby NameNode).
- **DataNode (Worker)** — Stores **actual data blocks**. Default replication = **3 copies**.

**MapReduce Programming Model:**

$$
\text{Map: } (k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{List}[(k_2, v_2)]
$$

$$
\text{Reduce: } (k_2, \text{List}[v_2]) \rightarrow \text{List}[(k_3, v_3)]
$$

**YARN Architecture:**

- **ResourceManager (RM)** — Global scheduler, allocates containers.
- **NodeManager (NM)** — Per-node agent, manages containers.
- **ApplicationMaster (AM)** — Per-application negotiator with RM.
- **Container** — Unit of resource allocation (CPU + RAM).

---

### 2.5 Deep Dive: Cluster Design Patterns

**Three Standard Cluster Topologies:**

| Design | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Node (Pseudo-Distributed)** | All daemons on 1 machine | Development, learning |
| **Small Cluster (3–10 nodes)** | 1 master + N workers | Small org, testing |
| **Production Cluster (50–1000+ nodes)** | HA master + rack-aware workers | Enterprise, cloud |

**Rack Awareness:**
HDFS uses a **rack-aware replica placement policy**:
- Replica 1: On the writing node's local rack (different node).
- Replica 2: Different rack from Replica 1.
- Replica 3: Same rack as Replica 1, different node.

This balances **fault tolerance** with **network bandwidth**.

---

### 2.6 Deep Dive: Cluster Management — Resource Allocation

**YARN Resource Model:**

$$
\text{Container} = (\text{vcores}, \text{memory\_MB})
$$

**Default Configurations:**

| Property | Default | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `yarn.nodemanager.resource.cpu-vcores` | **8** | CPU cores per NM |
| `yarn.nodemanager.resource.memory-mb` | **8 GB** | RAM per NM |
| `yarn.scheduler.maximum-allocation-vcores` | **32** | Max per container |
| `yarn.scheduler.maximum-allocation-mb` | **32 GB** | Max RAM per container |

---

### 2.7 Deep Dive: Performance Optimization

**Key Performance Formulas & Metrics:**

**Ideal Number of Partitions (Rule of Thumb):**

$$
\text{partitions} = 2 \text{ to } 3 \times \text{total\_cores\_in\_cluster}
$$

**Shuffle Read/Write Cost:**

$$
T_{\text{shuffle}} = T_{\text{serialize}} + T_{\text{transfer}} + T_{\text{deserialize}}
$$

**Data Locality Levels (Best → Worst):**

$$
\text{PROCESS\_LOCAL} > \text{NODE\_LOCAL} > \text{RACK\_LOCAL} > \text{ANY}
$$

**Storage Cost Comparison (relative to text):**

| Format | Storage Size | Read Speed (Analytical) | Write Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Text/CSV | **1.0x** (baseline) | Slow | Fast |
| Parquet | **0.2–0.3x** (Snappy compressed) | **Very Fast** | Medium |
| ORC | **0.2x** | Very Fast | Medium |
| Avro | **0.4–0.5x** | Medium | Fast |

---

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $B$ | HDFS Block Size | **MB** (default 128) | Configurable via `dfs.blocksize` |
| $R$ | Replication Factor | dimensionless (default **3**) | Configurable per file |
| $P$ | Number of Partitions | dimensionless | Affects parallelism |
| $N$ | Number of Executors | dimensionless | Cluster sizing parameter |
| $C$ | Cores per Executor | dimensionless | Default 1–5 |
| $M$ | Memory per Executor | **GB** | Default 1–8 GB |
| $T_{\text{latency}}$ | Task completion time | seconds | Optimization target |
| $\text{shuffle\_partitions}$ | Default Spark config | dimensionless | Default **200** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **cluster capacity planning** — determining the right number of nodes, cores, and memory to handle a given data volume within an SLA. Incorrect sizing leads to **OOM errors**, **slow jobs**, or **resource wastage**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Ideal Number of Spark Partitions for a Dataset

**Given:**
- Total dataset size: $D$ (in GB)
- HDFS block size: $B$ (in MB, default 128)
- Cluster total cores: $C_{\text{total}}$

**Derivation:**

The **minimum partitions** (one per HDFS block) is:

$$
P_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{D \times 1024}{B} \right\rceil
$$

The **optimal partitions** (to fully utilize cluster parallelism) is:

$$
P_{\text{opt}} = 2 \times C_{\text{total}} \quad \text{to} \quad 3 \times C_{\text{total}}
$$

Combining both constraints:

$$
P_{\text{final}} = \max\left(P_{\min}, P_{\text{opt}}\right)
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given $D = 1 \text{ TB} = 1024 \text{ GB}$, $B = 128 \text{ MB}$, and a cluster with $C_{\text{total}} = 64$ cores:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\min} &= \left\lceil \frac{1024 \times 1024 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil \frac{1048576}{128} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil 8192 \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= 8192 \text{ partitions}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
P_{\text{opt}} = 3 \times 64 = 192 \text{ partitions}
$$

$$
P_{\text{final}} = \max(8192, 192) = 8192 \text{ partitions}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** When the data is large (1 TB), the **HDFS block count** dominates. We cannot have fewer partitions than blocks, or we lose data parallelism. Therefore, the minimum constraint wins.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: HDFS Storage Overhead with Replication

**Given:**
- Raw data size: $D$ (in GB)
- Replication factor: $R$ (default 3)
- Block size: $B$

**Total logical storage used:**

$$
S_{\text{used}} = D \times R
$$

**Overhead ratio:**

$$
\text{Overhead} = \frac{S_{\text{used}}}{D} - 1 = R - 1
$$

**Numerical Example:**

With $D = 500 \text{ GB}$ and $R = 3$:

$$
S_{\text{used}} = 500 \times 3 = 1500 \text{ GB} = 1.5 \text{ TB}
$$

$$
\text{Overhead} = 3 - 1 = 2 \quad (\text{200\% overhead})
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Spark In-Memory Speedup vs. MapReduce

**Given:**
- $T_{\text{disk}}$ = Time per disk I/O read = **10 ms** (typical HDD)
- $T_{\text{mem}}$ = Time per RAM access = **0.0001 ms** (100 ns)
- $k$ = number of iterations (e.g., ML training)

**MapReduce total time** (writes to disk after every map AND reduce):

$$
T_{\text{MR}} = k \times (T_{\text{map}} + T_{\text{disk}} + T_{\text{reduce}} + T_{\text{disk}})
$$

**Spark total time** (caches intermediate in RAM):

$$
T_{\text{Spark}} = T_{\text{first\_read}} + (k - 1) \times (T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{mem}})
$$

**Speedup Factor:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{\text{MR}}}{T_{\text{Spark}}}
$$

For typical values, this yields **10x to 100x** speedup for iterative algorithms like k-means, gradient descent, and PageRank.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: File Format Selection Decision Tree

| Question | If YES → Choose | If NO → Continue |
|---|---|---|
| Need human-readable output? | **CSV / JSON** | Continue |
| Is it analytical/OLAP queries? | **Parquet** (default) | Continue |
| Is it streaming/Kafka with schema changes? | **Avro** | Continue |
| Is it Hive-heavy with high compression need? | **ORC** | Continue |
| Is it Hadoop MapReduce intermediate output? | **SequenceFile** | Re-evaluate |

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Spark vs. Hadoop Decision Tree

| Question | If YES → Choose | If NO → Continue |
|---|---|---|
| Is the workload iterative (ML, graph)? | **Spark** | Continue |
| Is low latency critical (sub-second)? | **Spark** | Continue |
| Is it simple batch ETL (linear, one-pass)? | **Hadoop MapReduce** (cheaper) | Continue |
| Is cost the primary concern (commodity HW, large scale)? | **Hadoop** | Continue |
| Do you need rich APIs (SQL, ML, Streaming)? | **Spark** | Use both |

---

### 3.6 Derivation: Cluster Sizing — Number of Executors

**Given:**
- Total cluster cores: $C_{\text{total}}$
- Cores per executor: $C_{\text{exec}}$ (typically 5)
- Memory per executor: $M_{\text{exec}}$ (typically 8 GB)
- Total cluster RAM: $M_{\text{total}}$

**Number of executors:**

$$
N_{\text{exec}} = \left\lfloor \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{C_{\text{exec}}} \right\rfloor
$$

**Validation against memory:**

$$
M_{\text{required}} = N_{\text{exec}} \times M_{\text{exec}} \leq M_{\text{total}}
$$

**Worked Example:**

Cluster has 100 cores, 500 GB RAM, $C_{\text{exec}} = 5$, $M_{\text{exec}} = 8 \text{ GB}$:

$$
N_{\text{exec}} = \left\lfloor \frac{100}{5} \right\rfloor = 20 \text{ executors}
$$

$$
M_{\text{required}} = 20 \times 8 = 160 \text{ GB} \leq 500 \text{ GB} \quad \checkmark
$$

With 1 core and ~1 GB for the ApplicationMaster, the cluster has ample headroom.

---

### 3.7 Code Walkthrough: Reading & Writing Files in PySpark

**Reading a CSV file:**

```python
df = spark.read.format("csv") \
    .option("header", "true") \
    .option("inferSchema", "true") \
    .load("hdfs://namenode:9000/data/sales.csv")
```

**Reading a Parquet file (the optimized default):**

```python
df = spark.read.parquet("hdfs://namenode:9000/data/sales.parquet")
```

**Writing a DataFrame as Parquet with partitioning:**

```python
df.write.mode("overwrite") \
    .partitionBy("year", "month") \
    .parquet("hdfs://namenode:9000/output/sales_parquet")
```

**Writing as a single CSV file (coalesce first):**

```python
df.coalesce(1).write.mode("overwrite") \
    .csv("hdfs://namenode:9000/output/sales_single.csv", header=True)
```

**Reading a JSON file:**

```python
df = spark.read.json("hdfs://namenode:9000/data/events.json")
```

**Reading & Writing Avro (requires `spark-avro` package):**

```python
df = spark.read.format("avro").load("hdfs:///data/orders.avro")
df.write.format("avro").save("hdfs:///output/orders_parquet.avro")
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Parquet is preferred:** Spark uses **Catalyst Optimizer** + **Tungsten Engine** with Parquet to perform **column pruning** (read only needed columns), **predicate pushdown** (skip irrelevant row groups), and **vectorized I/O** (process in batches of 1024 rows).

---

### 3.8 Hadoop MapReduce — WordCount Pseudocode

```java
// Mapper
public class WordCountMapper extends Mapper<LongWritable, Text, Text, IntWritable> {
    public void map(LongWritable key, Text value, Context context) {
        for (String word : value.toString().split("\\s+")) {
            context.write(new Text(word), new IntWritable(1));
        }
    }
}

// Reducer
public class WordCountReducer extends Reducer<Text, IntWritable, Text, IntWritable> {
    public void reduce(Text key, Iterable<IntWritable> values, Context context) {
        int sum = 0;
        for (IntWritable v : values) sum += v.get();
        context.write(key, new IntWritable(sum));
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **In Spark, this entire program is a single line:**
> ```python
> sc.textFile("hdfs:///input.txt").flatMap(lambda x: x.split()).countByValue()
> ```
> This dramatic reduction in code is a key reason Spark has **replaced MapReduce** for new workloads.

---

### 3.9 YARN Application Submission Flow

**Step-by-step lifecycle of a Spark-on-YARN job:**

1. **Client** calls `spark-submit --master yarn`.
2. **ApplicationMaster (AM)** is launched in a YARN container on a NodeManager.
3. **AM** requests containers from the **ResourceManager**.
4. **RM** allocates containers on NodeManagers based on scheduling policy (Capacity/FIFO/Fair).
5. **Executors** launch in allocated containers and register with AM.
6. **Driver** sends **tasks** to executors.
7. Executors execute tasks, return results to Driver.
8. On completion, **AM** unregisters, containers are released, resources returned to RM.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Apache Spark. List any four key features of Spark.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Apache Spark is an **open-source, distributed, in-memory cluster computing framework** designed for fast, large-scale data processing. It provides high-level APIs in Scala, Python, R, and Java, and uses a **DAG-based execution engine** for optimized task scheduling.

**Key Features (1.5 marks — any 4):**
1. **In-memory computation** — caches intermediate data in RAM for 100x speedup.
2. **Polyglot** — supports Scala, Python, R, Java.
3. **Lazy evaluation** — builds DAG, executes only on action.
4. **Real-time stream processing** via Spark Streaming / Structured Streaming.
5. **Rich ecosystem** — Spark SQL, MLlib, GraphX.
6. **Fault tolerance** — RDD lineage recomputation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is HDFS? Explain the role of NameNode and DataNode.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**HDFS Definition (1 mark):**
**HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** is a distributed, scalable, fault-tolerant file system designed to store very large files across multiple commodity machines. It follows a **Write Once, Read Many (WORM)** model with **block-level replication** (default replication factor = **3**).

**NameNode (1 mark):**
The **NameNode** is the **master daemon** that stores **metadata** — the file-to-block mapping, namespace tree, and block locations. It does NOT store actual data.

**DataNode (1 mark):**
**DataNodes** are **worker daemons** running on each slave node. They store the **actual data blocks** and serve read/write requests from clients. They send **heartbeat signals** to the NameNode every 3 seconds.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **Spark architecture** with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of **Driver, Cluster Manager, and Executors**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Apache Spark and Hadoop MapReduce** in detail (10 comparison points). Justify when to use each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.A(a): Spark Architecture (7 marks)**

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │           Spark Driver (JVM)                │
   │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌────────────────────┐  │
   │  │ SparkContext │  │   DAG Scheduler    │  │
   │  │ SparkSession │  │   Task Scheduler   │  │
   │  └──────────────┘  └────────────────────┘  │
   └────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                    │ (resource request)
                    ▼
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │      Cluster Manager (YARN/Mesos/K8s)      │
   └───────┬──────────┬──────────┬───────────────┘
           │          │          │
           ▼          ▼          ▼
       ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐
       │Exec 1│  │Exec 2│  │Exec 3│   (Worker Nodes)
       │Cache │  │Cache │  │Cache │
       └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘
```

**Driver (2 marks):**
The **Spark Driver** is the **JVM process** that runs the user's `main()` function. It:
- Creates the `SparkContext` / `SparkSession` (entry point).
- Converts user code into a **DAG** of stages and tasks.
- Schedules tasks on executors via the **Task Scheduler**.
- Coordinates with the **Cluster Manager** for resource allocation.

**Cluster Manager (1.5 marks):**
The **Cluster Manager** is a pluggable component responsible for **resource allocation** across the cluster. Spark supports:
- **Standalone** — Spark's built-in simple manager.
- **Apache YARN** — Most common in production; uses containers.
- **Mesos** — General-purpose, used by Twitter/Airbnb.
- **Kubernetes** — Cloud-native container orchestration.

**Executors (1.5 marks):**
**Executors** are **JVM worker processes** launched on cluster worker nodes. They:
- Execute the **tasks** assigned by the Driver.
- Store intermediate data in **memory (cache)** or **disk**.
- Return results to the Driver.
- Remain alive for the entire application lifetime (in `cluster` mode) or for the task duration (in `client` mode).

---

**Model Answer for Q.A(b): Spark vs. Hadoop MapReduce (7 marks)**

| S.No | Parameter | Apache Spark | Hadoop MapReduce |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Processing Model** | In-memory, DAG-based | Disk-based, two-stage (Map + Reduce) |
| 2 | **Speed** | **100x faster** (iterative) | Slower due to repeated disk I/O |
| 3 | **Ease of Use** | High-level APIs (SQL, ML, Graph) | Low-level Java API only |
| 4 | **Real-time Processing** | Yes (Spark Streaming) | No (batch only) |
| 5 | **Cost** | Higher RAM cost | Lower — uses commodity disks |
| 6 | **Fault Tolerance** | RDD Lineage recomputation | Task re-execution |
| 7 | **Iterative Algorithms** | Excellent (MLlib, GraphX) | Poor — writes to disk each iteration |
| 8 | **Storage** | Does NOT have own FS (uses HDFS/S3) | Has HDFS built-in |
| 9 | **Code Lines (WordCount)** | 1 line in PySpark | ~50+ lines in Java |
| 10 | **Latency** | Sub-second (interactive) | Minutes to hours |

**Justification (2 marks):**
- **Use Spark** for iterative ML, real-time streaming, interactive SQL, graph processing, and low-latency needs.
- **Use Hadoop MapReduce** for cost-sensitive, linear, one-pass batch ETL where RAM cost is a constraint and the workload does not need iterative computation.
- **Use Both Together** (most common production pattern): **HDFS for storage + Spark for compute** — leverages Hadoop's cheap reliable storage and Spark's fast processing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **Hadoop architecture (HDFS + MapReduce + YARN)** with diagrams. Discuss the **HDFS block replication strategy**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the different **cluster design topologies** and **cluster management strategies** (YARN, Mesos, Standalone, Kubernetes) for Big Data workloads. Explain **performance optimization techniques** in Spark. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.B(a): Hadoop Architecture (7 marks)**

**Three-Layer Architecture (3 marks):**

1. **HDFS (Storage Layer):**
   - **NameNode** — master, stores metadata.
   - **DataNodes** — workers, store blocks (default **128 MB**).
   - **Secondary NameNode** — checkpoint helper (NOT a hot standby).

2. **MapReduce (Processing Layer):**
   - **Map phase** — input $(k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list}[(k_2, v_2)]$.
   - **Shuffle & Sort** — groups by key, transfers across network.
   - **Reduce phase** — aggregates: $(k_2, \text{list}[v_2]) \rightarrow \text{list}[(k_3, v_3)]$.

3. **YARN (Resource Management Layer):**
   - **ResourceManager (RM)** — global arbiter of resources.
   - **NodeManager (NM)** — per-node agent.
   - **ApplicationMaster (AM)** — per-application master that negotiates with RM.
   - **Container** — bundle of CPU + RAM allocated to a task.

**HDFS Block Replication Strategy (4 marks):**

Default replication factor **$R = 3$**. The **Rack-Aware Replica Placement Policy** is:

- **Replica 1**: On the **local node** of the writer.
- **Replica 2**: On a **different node** within the **same rack** as Replica 1.
- **Replica 3**: On a **different rack** entirely (chosen randomly within that rack).

**Rationale:**
- Keeps one replica on a **different node, same rack** → low-latency local reads.
- Keeps one replica on a **different rack** → survives whole-rack failure (power, switch).
- If replication factor is increased (e.g., to 5), additional replicas are placed **randomly across the cluster**.

**Mathematical expression:**

$$
S_{\text{stored}} = D \times R
$$

For $D = 100 \text{ GB}$ and $R = 3$:

$$
S_{\text{stored}} = 100 \times 3 = 300 \text{ GB}
$$

Storage overhead = **200%** beyond raw data.

---

**Model Answer for Q.B(b): Cluster Design & Management + Performance (7 marks)**

**Cluster Design Topologies (3 marks):**

| Design | Nodes | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Node (Pseudo-Distributed)** | 1 | Dev, learning |
| **Small Cluster** | 3–10 | Testing, small org |
| **Production (Rack-Aware)** | 50–1000+ | Enterprise |
| **Cloud (Ephemeral)** | Auto-scaling | Bursty workloads |

**Key Design Principles:**
- **Rack awareness** — minimize cross-rack traffic.
- **Heterogeneous sizing** — heavier master nodes, lighter workers OR vice versa.
- **Headroom** — keep 20–30% resources free for failover and peak load.

**Cluster Managers Comparison (2 marks):**

| Manager | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|
| **Standalone** | Simple, built-in | Single-tenancy, no HA |
| **YARN** | Battle-tested, integrates with Hadoop | Complex config |
| **Mesos** | Fine-grained resource offers | Declining adoption |
| **Kubernetes** | Cloud-native, auto-scaling | Steeper learning curve |

**Spark Performance Optimization Techniques (2 marks):**

1. **Use Parquet** with Snappy compression.
2. **Partitioning & Bucketing** — `partitionBy("date")`.
3. **Cache/Persist** — `df.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)` for reused DataFrames.
4. **Avoid shuffles** — use `broadcast` joins for small tables.
5. **Tune `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`** (default 200, often too high).
6. **Use Kryo serializer** — `spark.serializer=org.apache.spark.serializer.KryoSerializer`.
7. **Adaptive Query Execution (AQE)** — `spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true`.
8. **Data locality tuning** — `spark.locality.wait`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Spark as a storage system** — Spark is a **compute engine only**; it does NOT have its own filesystem. Many students lose 1–2 marks by stating "Spark stores data" — it only reads from / writes to external storage like HDFS, S3, or local FS.
> 2. **Confusing Secondary NameNode with Standby NameNode** — The Secondary NameNode is a **checkpointing helper**, NOT a hot failover. In **HDFS HA (High Availability)**, there is a true **Standby NameNode** that takes over.
> 3. **Spark "100x faster" claim** — This is true only for **in-memory, iterative workloads**. For simple one-pass batch ETL, Spark is only marginally faster than MapReduce (often 2–3x).
> 4. **Confusing RDD, DataFrame, Dataset** — These are 3 distinct abstractions. RDD has no schema, DataFrame has schema, Dataset is type-safe. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Forgetting replication overhead** — A 1 TB dataset with $R = 3$ consumes **3 TB of HDFS storage**, not 1 TB. Students often miscalculate.
> 6. **Misnaming cluster manager components** — YARN's **ApplicationMaster** is per-application, NOT the global master. The **ResourceManager** is the global master.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- 📌 **Spark = Compute engine only**; needs external storage (HDFS, S3, local).
- 📌 **Hadoop = HDFS (storage) + MapReduce (compute) + YARN (resource mgmt)**.
- 📌 **Spark's 100x speedup** applies to **iterative in-memory workloads** (ML, graph, streaming).
- 📌 **HDFS default block size = 128 MB**; default **replication factor = 3**.
- 📌 **NameNode = metadata**; **DataNode = actual data blocks**.
- 📌 **HDFS rack-aware placement**: 1 local + 1 same-rack + 1 different-rack.
- 📌 **Data Abstractions**: `RDD → DataFrame → Dataset` (low to high level).
- 📌 **Lazy evaluation**: Transformations are lazy, actions trigger DAG execution.
- 📌 **Preferred file format in Spark = Parquet** (columnar, compressed, splittable).
- 📌 **Avro** is best for **schema evolution** and **streaming**.
- 📌 **CSV/JSON** are human-readable but **slow** and **inefficient** for big data.
- 📌 **YARN components**: ResourceManager, NodeManager, ApplicationMaster, Container.
- 📌 **Ideal partitions** = 2–3× total cluster cores, but **at least** equal to the number of HDFS blocks.
- 📌 **Storage overhead** = $(R - 1) \times 100\%$ (e.g., 200% for $R = 3$).
- 📌 **Cluster managers**: Standalone, YARN, Mesos, Kubernetes.
- 📌 **Performance tuning**: Cache, Parquet, Kryo, AQE, broadcast joins, partition tuning.
- 📌 **Fault tolerance in Spark** = RDD **lineage** (recompute from DAG).
- 📌 **Spark is polyglot**: Scala (primary), Python (PySpark), R (SparkR), Java.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Programming interface (API): Spark Context
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Application Programming interface (API): Spark Context ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **SparkContext (sc)** is the **entry point** of any Apache Spark application. It is an object that represents the connection between the Spark driver program and the Spark cluster.
