> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Recall this verbatim):**
> *Social Media Reputation Monitoring is the continuous, automated, sentiment-driven surveillance of brand mentions across social channels using Big Data pipelines that combine distributed storage (HDFS), stream processing (Spark/Kafka), and Natural Language Processing (NLP) lexicons to derive a quantifiable Reputation Score for stakeholder decision-making.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English)

Imagine you own a **small tea stall** in a busy Kerala market. Every day, customers walk past, some stop, taste your chai, and then either:
- Smile and tell a friend (positive word-of-mouth),
- Say nothing (neutral), or
- Walk away grumbling about it being too sweet (negative).

You, the owner, want to know — **without standing at the door all day** — what people are saying. So you hire a small boy to sit at the corner with a **notebook and a giant ear-trumpet** to write down every single comment, every facial expression, every sigh from every customer. At the end of the day, he hands you a summary:

> *"Boss, 60 smiles, 30 silent faces, 10 frowns today. Also, a man in a blue shirt cursed our sugar."*

You then decide: *tomorrow, I will reduce the sugar by 5%* — that is **reputation-driven business action**.

**Now scale this to a multinational brand like Apple or Tata**:
- The "tea stall" = a global brand.
- The "corner boy" = a **cluster of Hadoop/Spark nodes**.
- The "ear-trumpet" = **Kafka + Streaming APIs**.
- The "notebook" = **HDFS / HBase**.
- The "summary" = a **Sentiment Dashboard**.
- The "decision" = a **Crisis Response / Marketing Pivot**.

That is **Social Media Reputation Monitoring** — a digital ear-trumpet that never sleeps, never tires, and listens to **billions of voices** in parallel.

---

## 1.3 The Standard Reputation Metrics (KTU Important)

The following standardized quantitative metrics form the core vocabulary of SMRM. Every student must be able to **define and compute** these.

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Brand Mention Volume** | $V_m$ | mentions/day | Total number of times the brand is referenced |
| **Reach / Impressions** | $R$ | unique users | Number of unique users who *saw* the mention |
| **Engagement Rate** | $E_r$ | % | $(L + C + S) / R$ where $L$=likes, $C$=comments, $S$=shares |
| **Sentiment Polarity** | $p$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Negative to positive emotional tone of a post |
| **Subjectivity Score** | $s$ | $[0, 1]$ | 0 = objective fact, 1 = pure opinion |
| **Net Reputation Score (NRS)** | $NRS$ | $[-100, +100]$ | Composite health index of brand perception |
| **Share of Voice (SOV)** | $SOV$ | % | Brand mentions / Total category mentions × 100 |
| **Crisis Spike Index (CSI)** | $CSI$ | ratio | Sudden abnormal surge in negative mentions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Threshold Values used in Industry:**
> - **Polarity $p \geq 0.05$** → Positive sentiment
> - **Polarity $p \leq -0.05$** → Negative sentiment
> - **$-0.05 < p < 0.05$** → Neutral sentiment
> - **CSI $\geq 3.0$** → Triggers crisis alert
> - **NRS $\geq 30$** → Healthy brand
> - **NRS $\leq -10$** → Reputation damage

---

## 1.4 The Big Data Context — Why SMRM is Inherently "Big"

Social Media is the **largest unstructured data generator in human history**. The famous **5 V's of Big Data** map directly onto SMRM:

| V | Application to SMRM |
|---|---|
| **Volume** | **>500 million tweets/day**, **4.9 billion Facebook users** posting; petabyte-scale daily ingestion |
| **Velocity** | Tweets peak at **6,000 tweets/second** during events; needs sub-second stream processing |
| **Variety** | Text, emojis 😀😡, images, memes, short videos, hashtags, multi-lingual, code-mixed (Hinglish, Manglish) |
| **Veracity** | Bots, spam, sarcasm ("yeah right, *great* product 🤡"), fake reviews, paid trolls |
| **Value** | $1 of social insight = **$2.30 in marketing ROI** (industry benchmark) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SMRM Pipeline — Five Logical Layers

The SMRM system is engineered as a **layered Lambda/Kappa architecture**. Each layer has a strict, well-defined responsibility.

### Layer 1 — **Ingestion Layer** (Data Acquisition)
- **Tools used:** Twitter/X Streaming API v2, Facebook Graph API, Instagram Basic Display API, YouTube Data API, Reddit API, Web crawlers (Scrapy, BeautifulSoup).
- **Big Data tools:** **Apache Kafka**, **Apache Flume**, **Amazon Kinesis**.
- **Function:** Pulls raw posts in real time (streaming) or in batches (historical).

### Layer 2 — **Storage Layer** (Persistence)
- **Structured / semi-structured** → HDFS, HBase, Cassandra.
- **Document stores** → MongoDB, CouchDB (good for JSON-shaped tweets).
- **Search / index stores** → Elasticsearch, Solr (for fast keyword queries).
- **Data Lake** → S3, HDFS for raw archival.

### Layer 3 — **Processing Layer** (Distributed Compute)
- **Batch layer:** Hadoop **MapReduce**, Apache **Spark** Core.
- **Speed/Stream layer:** Apache **Spark Streaming**, **Flink**, **Storm**, **Samza**.
- **Function:** Cleansing, deduplication, tokenization, stop-word removal, language detection.

### Layer 4 — **Analytics Layer** (Intelligence)
- **NLP & Text Mining:** Tokenization, stemming, lemmatization, POS tagging, NER.
- **Sentiment Analysis engines:**
  - **Lexicon-based:** VADER, TextBlob, AFINN, Bing Liu lexicon.
  - **Machine-Learning based:** Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression.
  - **Deep-Learning based:** BERT, RoBERTa, LSTM, Transformer models.
- **Topic Modeling:** LDA (Latent Dirichlet Allocation), NMF.
- **Trend / Spike Detection:** Time-series anomaly detection (ARIMA, Prophet).

### Layer 5 — **Presentation Layer** (Visualization & Action)
- **Dashboards:** **Kibana**, **Tableau**, **Power BI**, **Grafana**.
- **Alerts:** Email, SMS, Slack webhooks, PagerDuty for crisis events.
- **Reports:** PDF/CSV exports for executive consumption.

---

## 2.2 The 5 High-Yield Formulas of SMRM (KTU Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These five equations are the most-tested formulae in the KTU ESE for this topic. Memorize symbol, formula, and unit.**

### Formula 1 — Sentiment Polarity (Lexicon Mean)

$$
p_i = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{n_i} w_k}{n_i}
$$

where:
- $p_i$ = polarity of post $i$ (range $[-1, +1]$)
- $w_k$ = weight of the $k$-th sentiment-bearing word (from VADER/AFINN lexicon)
- $n_i$ = number of sentiment words in post $i$

### Formula 2 — Engagement Rate

$$
E_r = \frac{L + C + S + R_s}{R} \times 100
$$

where $L$ = likes, $C$ = comments, $S$ = shares, $R_s$ = saves, $R$ = reach (impressions).

### Formula 3 — Net Reputation Score (NRS)

$$
NRS = \left( P_p \cdot \bar{p} \right) + \left( P_n \cdot \bar{p}_n \right) + \alpha \cdot E_r + \beta \cdot SOV
$$

where $P_p$ = % of positive mentions, $P_n$ = % of negative mentions, $\bar{p}$ = mean polarity, $\bar{p}_n$ = mean negative polarity, $\alpha, \beta$ = tunable weights ($\alpha=0.4, \beta=0.3$ are common defaults).

### Formula 4 — Share of Voice (SOV)

$$
SOV = \frac{M_{\text{brand}}}{M_{\text{brand}} + M_{\text{competitor}_1} + \ldots + M_{\text{competitor}_k}} \times 100
$$

where $M_x$ = mention volume of brand $x$.

### Formula 5 — Crisis Spike Index (CSI)

$$
CSI_t = \frac{N_t^{\text{neg}}}{\mu_{N^{\text{neg}}} + \epsilon}
$$

where $N_t^{\text{neg}}$ = negative mentions in window $t$, $\mu_{N^{\text{neg}}}$ = historical mean, $\epsilon$ = small constant (e.g., $10^{-6}$) to avoid division by zero. **CSI $\geq 3.0$ triggers a crisis alert.**

---

## 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter (Real-World Utility)

| Formula | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| Polarity $p_i$ | Customer-service bots, product review sorting, chatbot tone detection |
| Engagement Rate $E_r$ | Influencer selection for paid campaigns, A/B test of ad copy |
| NRS | Quarterly board-report metric, M&A due diligence on brand equity |
| SOV | Competitive intelligence in FMCG, telecom, automotive sectors |
| CSI | Real-time PR crisis management (e.g., airlines handling viral complaints) |

---

## 2.4 Sentiment Classification Approaches — Conceptual Comparison

| Approach | Example | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lexicon** | VADER, AFINN, TextBlob | Fast, interpretable, no training | Fails on sarcasm, domain-specific jargon |
| **Classical ML** | Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression | Trainable, handles custom vocab | Needs labeled data, hand-crafted features |
| **Deep Learning** | BERT, LSTM, RoBERTa | State-of-the-art accuracy, context-aware | Compute-heavy, black-box, GPU needed |
| **Hybrid** | Lexicon + ML ensemble | Balanced, robust | More engineering overhead |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "Which approach is used in streaming?" — answer **Lexicon-based (VADER)** because it is O(n) per post, GPU-free, and can run inside Spark's `map` function. Deep learning is for **offline batch analysis** of archived data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Full SMRM Calculation

A Kerala-based startup **"Cochin Chai Co."** ran an Instagram campaign on 5 July. The following data was harvested in 24 hours by the Spark Streaming job:

| Post ID | Text (truncated) | Likes ($L$) | Comments ($C$) | Shares ($S$) | Reach ($R$) | $w_k$ lexicon words found | $n_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | "Best masala chai ever! ☕❤️" | 250 | 40 | 60 | 5,000 | +1, +2, +3 | 3 |
| P2 | "Tastes like dirty water 😡" | 12 | 2 | 0 | 800 | -2, -3 | 2 |
| P3 | "It's okay, nothing special" | 8 | 1 | 0 | 500 | 0 (neutral) | 1 |
| P4 | "Loved the packaging, will buy again" | 180 | 25 | 40 | 3,500 | +2, +2, +1 | 3 |
| P5 | "Worst customer service 👎👎" | 5 | 12 | 3 | 1,200 | -3, -2, -1 | 3 |

Competitor "Kerala Tea Co." had **2,500 mentions** on the same day. Industry crisis threshold: **CSI = 3.0**.

> We are required to compute: **(i)** Polarity of each post, **(ii)** Engagement Rate, **(iii)** NRS, **(iv)** SOV, **(v)** CSI.

---

### Step 1 — Compute Polarity $p_i$ for Each Post

Recall:
$$
p_i = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{n_i} w_k}{n_i}
$$

**Post P1** (Positive, "Best masala chai ever"):
$$
p_1 = \frac{(+1) + (+2) + (+3)}{3} = \frac{+6}{3} = +2.0
$$

> **Wait — polarity must be in $[-1, +1]$!** The lexicon weights are integer AFINN-style; we **normalize by the maximum possible score per word = 5** (AFINN scale). Apply correction:
> $$
> p_1 = \frac{+2.0}{5.0} = +0.40
> $$

**Post P2** (Negative):
$$
p_2 = \frac{(-2) + (-3)}{2} = \frac{-5}{2} = -2.5 \;\Rightarrow\; p_2 = \frac{-2.5}{5.0} = -0.50
$$

**Post P3** (Neutral):
$$
p_3 = \frac{0}{1} = 0.0 \;\Rightarrow\; p_3 = 0.0
$$

**Post P4** (Positive):
$$
p_4 = \frac{(+2) + (+2) + (+1)}{3} = \frac{+5}{3} = +1.667 \;\Rightarrow\; p_4 = \frac{+1.667}{5.0} = +0.333
$$

**Post P5** (Negative):
$$
p_5 = \frac{(-3) + (-2) + (-1)}{3} = \frac{-6}{3} = -2.0 \;\Rightarrow\; p_5 = \frac{-2.0}{5.0} = -0.40
$$

**Polarity Table:**

| Post | Polarity $p_i$ | Class |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | **+0.40** | Positive |
| P2 | **-0.50** | Negative |
| P3 | **0.00** | Neutral |
| P4 | **+0.333** | Positive |
| P5 | **-0.40** | Negative |

---

### Step 2 — Compute Engagement Rate $E_r$ for Each Post

Recall:
$$
E_r = \frac{L + C + S + R_s}{R} \times 100
$$

(Assume $R_s = 0$ for all posts, as saves not given.)

**Post P1:**
$$
E_{r,1} = \frac{250 + 40 + 60 + 0}{5{,}000} \times 100 = \frac{350}{5{,}000} \times 100 = 7.00\%
$$

**Post P2:**
$$
E_{r,2} = \frac{12 + 2 + 0 + 0}{800} \times 100 = \frac{14}{800} \times 100 = 1.75\%
$$

**Post P3:**
$$
E_{r,3} = \frac{8 + 1 + 0 + 0}{500} \times 100 = \frac{9}{500} \times 100 = 1.80\%
$$

**Post P4:**
$$
E_{r,4} = \frac{180 + 25 + 40 + 0}{3{,}500} \times 100 = \frac{245}{3{,}500} \times 100 = 7.00\%
$$

**Post P5:**
$$
E_{r,5} = \frac{5 + 12 + 3 + 0}{1{,}200} \times 100 = \frac{20}{1{,}200} \times 100 = 1.67\%
$$

**Mean Engagement Rate** (for NRS):
$$
\bar{E_r} = \frac{7.00 + 1.75 + 1.80 + 7.00 + 1.67}{5} = \frac{19.22}{5} = 3.844\%
$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute Net Reputation Score (NRS)

Recall:
$$
NRS = (P_p \cdot \bar{p}_p) + (P_n \cdot \bar{p}_n) + \alpha \cdot E_r + \beta \cdot SOV
$$

**Step 3a — Class counts:**
- Positive: P1, P4 → $n_p = 2$
- Negative: P2, P5 → $n_n = 2$
- Neutral: P3 → $n_{neu} = 1$
- Total $N = 5$

**Step 3b — Percentages:**
$$
P_p = \frac{2}{5} \times 100 = 40\%
$$
$$
P_n = \frac{2}{5} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

**Step 3c — Mean polarities of each class:**
$$
\bar{p}_p = \frac{(+0.40) + (+0.333)}{2} = \frac{+0.733}{2} = +0.367
$$
$$
\bar{p}_n = \frac{(-0.50) + (-0.40)}{2} = \frac{-0.90}{2} = -0.45
$$

**Step 3d — SOV computation (deferred to Step 4).** Assume we use placeholder $SOV$ first.

Let $\alpha = 0.4$, $\beta = 0.3$, and accept $SOV$ after Step 4.

---

### Step 4 — Compute Share of Voice (SOV)

Cochin Chai Co. mentions $M_{\text{brand}} = 5$ (total posts harvested). Competitor $M_{\text{comp}} = 2{,}500$ (given).

$$
SOV = \frac{M_{\text{brand}}}{M_{\text{brand}} + M_{\text{comp}}} \times 100
$$

$$
SOV = \frac{5}{5 + 2{,}500} \times 100 = \frac{5}{2{,}505} \times 100 = 0.1996\% \approx 0.20\%
$$

> The brand has a **very low SOV** — the competitor dominates. This is a red-flag insight.

---

### Step 5 — Final NRS

Plug into:
$$
NRS = (P_p \cdot \bar{p}_p) + (P_n \cdot \bar{p}_n) + \alpha \cdot E_r + \beta \cdot SOV
$$

For the components, we use **normalized (0–100) values**. The polarity terms are scaled by **100** so they all align in $[-100, +100]$:

$$
NRS = (40 \times 0.367) + (40 \times (-0.45)) + (0.4 \times 3.844) + (0.3 \times 0.20)
$$

Compute each term:

$$
NRS = (14.68) + (-18.0) + (1.5376) + (0.06)
$$

$$
NRS = -1.72
$$

**Interpretation:** $NRS = -1.72$ is **negative and below the healthy threshold of +30** → brand reputation is currently **weak**, primarily because the negative mentions carry heavier emotional weight ($-0.45$) than the positive ones ($+0.367$).

---

### Step 6 — Crisis Spike Index (CSI)

We need historical baseline $\mu_{N^{\text{neg}}}$. Assume the previous 7-day average negative mentions per day for "Cochin Chai Co." = **1.5**.

Current negative mentions in 24-hr window: $N_t^{\text{neg}} = 2$.

$$
CSI = \frac{2}{1.5 + 10^{-6}} \approx 1.333
$$

Since $CSI = 1.33 < 3.0$ → **No crisis alert triggered**, but the system should **flag a yellow warning** for monitoring.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic

| Metric | Computed Value | Threshold | Verdict | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\bar{p}_p$ | +0.367 | $\geq 0.05$ | ✅ Positive | Continue strategy |
| $\bar{p}_n$ | -0.45 | $\leq -0.05$ | ⚠️ Strong negative | Investigate P2, P5 |
| $\bar{E_r}$ | 3.84% | $\geq 2\%$ | ✅ Healthy | Boost positive posts |
| $SOV$ | 0.20% | $\geq 5\%$ | ❌ Very low | Run paid ad campaign |
| $NRS$ | -1.72 | $\geq +30$ | ❌ Reputation damage | PR response needed |
| $CSI$ | 1.33 | $\geq 3.0$ | ✅ No crisis | Yellow-flag monitoring |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End SMRM Architecture (Lambda Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SM1[Twitter X API]
    SM2[Facebook Graph API]
    SM3[Instagram API]
    SM4[YouTube Data API]
    SM5[Reddit API]
    SM6[Web Crawler Scrapy]
    KAFKA[(Apache Kafka Broker)]
    STREAM[Spark Streaming Engine]
    BATCH[Hadoop MapReduce Batch]
    HDFS[(HDFS Raw Lake)]
    HBASE[(HBase Indexed Store)]
    MONGO[(MongoDB Document Store)]
    ES[(Elasticsearch)]
    NLP1[VADER Lexicon Polarity]
    NLP2[BERT Deep Sentiment]
    NLP3[LDA Topic Modeling]
    ANOM[Anomaly Detector CSI]
    DASH1[Kibana Dashboard]
    DASH2[Tableau Executive View]
    ALERT[PagerDuty Slack Email Crisis Alert]
    ARCH[(S3 Cold Archive)]
    node1[User Client App]:::client
    node2[Marketing Manager]:::client
    SM1 --> KAFKA
    SM2 --> KAFKA
    SM3 --> KAFKA
    SM4 --> KAFKA
    SM5 --> KAFKA
    SM6 --> KAFKA
    KAFKA --> STREAM
    SM1 -.historical.-> BATCH
    SM2 -.historical.-> BATCH
    BATCH --> HDFS
    STREAM --> HDFS
    HDFS --> HBASE
    HDFS --> MONGO
    HDFS --> ES
    ES --> NLP1
    ES --> NLP2
    HBASE --> NLP3
    ES --> ANOM
    NLP1 --> DASH1
    NLP2 --> DASH1
    NLP3 --> DASH1
    ANOM --> ALERT

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Define Social Media Reputation Monitoring. List any FOUR key metrics used to quantify online brand reputation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Social Media Reputation Monitoring (SMRM) is the continuous, automated, large-scale process of collecting, processing, and analyzing user-generated content from social media platforms to evaluate, quantify, and manage the public perception, sentiment, and trustworthiness of a brand, individual, or organization in real time.

**Four Key Metrics:**
1. **Sentiment Polarity ($p$):** Score in range $[-1, +1]$ indicating positive, negative, or neutral emotional tone of posts.
2. **Net Reputation Score (NRS):** Composite index in $[-100, +100]$ combining sentiment %, engagement, and share of voice.
3. **Engagement Rate ($E_r$):** Percentage measure of user interaction, calculated as $(L + C + S)/R \times 100$.
4. **Share of Voice (SOV):** Brand's mention volume as a percentage of total category mentions, indicating competitive presence.

> Examiner expects: crisp definition (1 mark) + 4 metrics (0.5 each = 2 marks).

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Differentiate between Lexicon-based and Machine Learning-based sentiment analysis approaches used in SMRM. State one advantage and one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Lexicon-Based | Machine Learning-Based |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Pre-built dictionary of words with sentiment scores; sums scores of words in text | Supervised model (NB, SVM, BERT) trained on labeled corpora |
| **Training Data** | Not required | Requires large labeled dataset |
| **Speed** | Very fast (O(n)) | Slower; needs feature extraction / embeddings |
| **Advantage** | Simple, interpretable, GPU-free, real-time ready | Handles context, sarcasm, domain jargon better |
| **Limitation** | Fails on sarcasm and new slang | Black-box, compute-intensive, needs retraining |

> Examiner expects: 1 mark for each approach description + 0.5 + 0.5 for advantage/limitation.

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

> **MODULE INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE full question.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the complete Lambda Architecture for a Social Media Reputation Monitoring system. List the responsibilities of EACH of the five layers: Ingestion, Storage, Processing, Analytics, and Presentation. Name ONE specific Big Data tool used in EACH layer.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Lambda Architecture** is a fault-tolerant, scalable data-processing pattern that splits the workload into three parallel paths — **Batch Layer**, **Speed (Stream) Layer**, and **Serving Layer** — to handle both historical and real-time social data.

**The Five Functional Layers and Their Roles:**

1. **Ingestion Layer**
   - **Role:** Pulls raw posts continuously from social APIs.
   - **Tools:** **Apache Kafka** (real-time streams) and **Apache Flume** (log-based ingestion).

2. **Storage Layer**
   - **Role:** Persists raw and processed data durably and queryably.
   - **Tools:** **HDFS** (raw archive), **HBase** (low-latency NoSQL), **Elasticsearch** (search index).

3. **Processing Layer**
   - **Role:** Cleans, transforms, tokenizes, and aggregates posts.
   - **Tools:** **Apache Spark Streaming** (micro-batch), **Apache Flink** (true streaming).

4. **Analytics Layer**
   - **Role:** Performs sentiment analysis, topic modeling, and anomaly detection.
   - **Tools:** **VADER** (lexicon polarity), **BERT / spaCy** (NLP), **Spark MLlib** (clustering).

5. **Presentation Layer**
   - **Role:** Renders dashboards, alerts, and executive reports.
   - **Tools:** **Kibana** (real-time dashboards), **Tableau** (BI reports), **Slack webhooks** (alerts).

**Why Lambda?**
- **Batch layer** provides comprehensive, accurate views from historical archives.
- **Speed layer** offers sub-second freshness for live crisis detection.
- **Serving layer** merges both views for unified queries.

(Diagram reference: See Section 4.1 for end-to-end architecture.)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A brand received the following aggregated data on a single day:**

- Positive mentions = 450, mean positive polarity $= +0.42$
- Negative mentions = 90, mean negative polarity $= -0.58$
- Engagement Rate $= 4.5\%$
- Share of Voice $= 12.3\%$
- Total category mentions = 5,000

**Compute the Net Reputation Score (NRS)** using $\alpha = 0.4$, $\beta = 0.3$. Interpret the result.

**Model Answer:**

Given:
- $P_p = 450 / (450+90) \times 100 = ?$ — need total. Assume total = 600.
- $P_p = (450/600) \times 100 = 75\%$
- $P_n = (90/600) \times 100 = 15\%$ → 15% negative
- $\bar{p}_p = +0.42$, $\bar{p}_n = -0.58$
- $E_r = 4.5\%$, $SOV = 12.3\%$
- $\alpha = 0.4$, $\beta = 0.3$

**Step 1:** Compute positive contribution:
$$
C_p = P_p \times \bar{p}_p = 75 \times 0.42 = 31.5
$$

**Step 2:** Compute negative contribution:
$$
C_n = P_n \times \bar{p}_n = 15 \times (-0.58) = -8.7
$$

**Step 3:** Engagement contribution:
$$
C_E = \alpha \cdot E_r = 0.4 \times 4.5 = 1.8
$$

**Step 4:** SOV contribution:
$$
C_S = \beta \cdot SOV = 0.3 \times 12.3 = 3.69
$$

**Step 5:** Sum:
$$
NRS = 31.5 + (-8.7) + 1.8 + 3.69 = 28.29
$$

**Interpretation:** $NRS = 28.29$ is **positive but below the healthy threshold of +30**. The brand has **moderately healthy** reputation; the negative mentions are emotionally intense ($-0.58$), pulling down the score. **Recommendation:** Address the high-intensity negatives (likely a service complaint) via customer service outreach to push NRS above 30.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the 5 V's of Big Data in the context of Social Media Reputation Monitoring. Give one concrete example for each V.**

**Model Answer:**

| V | Meaning | Concrete Example in SMRM |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Scale of data generated | Twitter/X generates **>500 million tweets per day**; Instagram adds **~100 million photos/day** |
| **Velocity** | Speed of data arrival and processing | During a viral event, tweet rate spikes to **6,000 tweets/second**, requiring sub-second processing |
| **Variety** | Heterogeneous data formats | Posts contain text, emojis, GIFs, images, videos, hashtags, multi-lingual code-mixed content (e.g., Hinglish) |
| **Veracity** | Trustworthiness / noise | **30% of social traffic is bot-generated**; sarcasm ("great, another *wonderful* update") is misclassified |
| **Value** | Business worth extracted | Each $1 invested in social listening yields **$2.30 in marketing ROI**; early crisis detection saves millions |

**Architectural Impact:** These 5 V's force SMRM systems to use **distributed, horizontally-scalable, fault-tolerant** infrastructure (Hadoop, Spark, Kafka) — a single server cannot handle the load.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A social media analytics system observed that on Monday, negative mentions for a brand were 240, while the 30-day historical mean of negative mentions per day is 80 (standard deviation 15). Calculate the Crisis Spike Index (CSI) using the formula $CSI = N_t^{\text{neg}} / (\mu_{N^{\text{neg}}} + \epsilon)$ where $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$. Comment on whether a crisis alert should be triggered.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $N_t^{\text{neg}} = 240$ (current Monday)
- $\mu_{N^{\text{neg}}} = 80$ (historical mean)
- $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$

**Step 1 — Apply Formula:**
$$
CSI = \frac{N_t^{\text{neg}}}{\mu_{N^{\text{neg}}} + \epsilon} = \frac{240}{80 + 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
CSI \approx \frac{240}{80} = 3.00
$$

**Step 2 — Compare to Threshold:**
- Industry crisis threshold: **CSI $\geq 3.0$ → ALERT**
- Our $CSI = 3.00$ → **On the threshold; ALERT TRIGGERED**

**Step 3 — Statistical Validation (Z-score):**
$$
Z = \frac{N_t^{\text{neg}} - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{240 - 80}{15} = \frac{160}{15} \approx 10.67
$$

A **Z-score of 10.67** means the spike is **~10.67 standard deviations above mean** — an extreme statistical outlier (probability $< 10^{-25}$).

**Conclusion & Recommendations:**
1. **CRISIS ALERT should be fired immediately** to the PR team via Slack/PagerDuty.
2. The PR team must perform **root-cause analysis** within the next 2 hours.
3. A **holding statement** should be drafted and pre-approved for release.
4. **Real-time monitoring window** should be tightened to every 5 minutes until CSI drops below 1.5.
5. **Post-mortem** within 48 hours to identify the triggering post/event and prevent recurrence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Polarity range:** Many students write $p \in [0, 1]$ instead of $p \in [-1, +1]$. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize SOV to percentage:** Always multiply by 100. **Lose 0.5 mark.**
> 3. **NRS computation sign errors:** Negative polarity × percentage = negative contribution; do not subtract twice. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 4. **CSI without $\epsilon$:** Always mention the small constant to avoid division by zero. **Lose 0.5 mark.**
> 5. **Mentioning ONE tool per layer only:** KTU requires specific named tools (Kafka, HDFS, Spark, VADER, Kibana) — not generic terms like "database" or "algorithm." **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 6. **Sarcasm and bot detection missed in Veracity:** Both must be explicitly stated. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 7. **Lambda vs Kappa confusion:** Lambda has BOTH batch and speed layers; Kappa is stream-only. Mixing them up loses **2 marks** in 14-mark questions.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **SMRM = real-time, automated, large-scale brand-perception surveillance** using Big Data pipelines.
- ✅ **Five layers:** Ingestion → Storage → Processing → Analytics → Presentation.
- ✅ **Five Big Data tools to memorize:** Kafka (ingestion), HDFS/HBase (storage), Spark Streaming (processing), VADER/BERT (analytics), Kibana/Tableau (presentation).
- ✅ **Polarity $p \in [-1, +1]$**; $p \geq +0.05$ = positive, $p \leq -0.05$ = negative.
- ✅ **Engagement Rate formula:** $E_r = (L + C + S + R_s)/R \times 100$.
- ✅ **NRS = composite** of positive %, negative %, $\bar{p}$, $E_r$, $SOV$ with weights $\alpha, \beta$.
- ✅ **SOV = brand mentions / total category mentions × 100** — competitive intelligence metric.
- ✅ **CSI $\geq 3.0$ → crisis alert**; $\epsilon$ constant avoids division by zero.
- ✅ **5 V's:** Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value — all 5 must be cited with one example each.
- ✅ **Lambda Architecture** = Batch + Speed + Serving layers; **Kappa Architecture** = Stream-only.
- ✅ **VADER** is preferred for real-time streaming; **BERT** is preferred for offline batch accuracy.
- ✅ **Big Data context:** 500M+ tweets/day, 6,000 tweets/sec peak, 30% bot traffic.
- ✅ **Standard thresholds:** NRS $\geq 30$ = healthy; CSI $\geq 3$ = crisis; SOV $\geq 5\%$ = competitive.
- ✅ **Mention 3 R's of SMRM:** Real-time, Reliable, Reputation (or Reach, Resonance, Reaction framework).
- ✅ **KTU-mandated real-world tie-in:** Always mention at least ONE Indian/local brand case study (e.g., Tata, Zomato, Kerala Tourism) to score full marks in application-based questions.

topics.title: Equity Research
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Equity Research

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Equity Research** is the disciplined process of collecting, processing, modeling, and interpreting financial data related to publicly listed stocks (equities) in order to produce actionable investment recommendations: *Buy*, *Hold*, or *Sell*. In the context of **Big Data Analytics (PBADT504)**, equity research evolves from traditional spreadsheet-driven fundamental analysis into a **high-throughput, distributed, multi-source analytical pipeline** that integrates structured market data with unstructured alternative data (news, social media, filings, satellite imagery).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Equity Research = *Fundamental + Technical + Alternative Data Analytics*, executed over a Big Data stack (Hadoop / Spark / NoSQL), producing *valuation outputs* and *trading signals* with quantified confidence.

Formally, the analyst's output is a research report containing:

- **Target Price** $P_t$ — a forecasted fair value of a stock.
- **Recommendation** $R \in \{\text{Buy}, \text{Hold}, \text{Sell}\}$.
- **Risk Metrics** — Beta ($\beta$), Standard Deviation ($\sigma$), Value at Risk (VaR), Sharpe Ratio ($S_r$).
- **Valuation Models** — DCF, Comparable Company Analysis (CCA), Dividend Discount Model (DDM).
- **Investment Horizon** $H$ — short-term ($H < 12$ months) vs. long-term ($H \geq 12$ months).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor for Stocks"

Imagine a cardiologist diagnosing a patient. The doctor does not rely on a single signal — she checks blood pressure, ECG scans, cholesterol reports, family history, and lifestyle data, then synthesizes everything into a recommendation: *Healthy, Monitor, or Operate*. 

**Equity Research works identically:**

| Medical Analogy | Equity Research Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Patient (Person) | Company / Stock |
| Vital signs (BP, Heart rate) | Price, Volume, Volatility |
| Medical history | Past earnings, filings |
| Lifestyle factors | News, social media, management quality |
| Lab reports (MRI, blood test) | Financial ratios, DCF models |
| Diagnosis | Recommendation (Buy/Hold/Sell) |
| Treatment plan | Target price + stop-loss |

> [!NOTE]
> In Big Data Analytics, the "lab tests" are now distributed across millions of data points — terabytes of tick data, millions of tweets, thousands of news articles — processed in parallel on clusters.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Equity Research in Big Data Context

1. **Fundamental Analysis (FA)** — *What is the company actually worth?* Uses earnings, revenue, debt, cash flow. Numerically heavy, mostly structured.
2. **Technical Analysis (TA)** — *What is the market price saying?* Uses historical price/volume patterns. Time-series heavy, computationally intensive.
3. **Alternative Data & Sentiment (ADA)** — *What is the crowd / context saying?* Uses NLP on news, Twitter, Reddit, satellite imagery. Unstructured, requires ML/DL.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** Experiment 2 typically tests your ability to **integrate all three pillars using a Big Data pipeline**, not just compute one ratio. Plan your viva answers to cover the *end-to-end pipeline* — ingestion → storage → processing → modeling → visualization.

## 1.4 Why Equity Research in a Big Data Course?

Traditional equity research involved reading 10-K filings and plotting a chart. Today:

- A single NYSE stock generates **~5 GB of tick data per day**.
- Twitter produces **~500 million tweets/day**, of which a significant fraction mention stock tickers.
- News APIs stream **~50,000 articles/minute** globally.

Human analysts **cannot** process this volume. Hence, Big Data tools (Hadoop, Spark, Kafka, HBase) are now standard in any quantitative research desk.

**The standard industry metric** for "informativeness" of a research signal is the **Information Coefficient (IC)**:

$$IC = \text{Corr}(\text{Predicted Return}, \text{Actual Return})$$

A senior research analyst at a buy-side firm is expected to maintain $IC \geq 0.05$ monthly to be considered valuable (a benchmark widely cited in quantitative finance literature).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Big Data Equity Research Pipeline (BDERP)

A KTU-grade equity research experiment follows a **five-stage pipeline**:

1. **Ingestion Layer** — Web scraping (BeautifulSoup, Scrapy), APIs (Yahoo Finance, Alpha Vantage, Quandl), streaming (Apache Kafka, Flume).
2. **Storage Layer** — HDFS for raw tick data, HBase / Cassandra for low-latency queries, Hive / Impala for warehouse queries.
3. **Processing Layer** — Apache Spark (PySpark / Spark Scala) for batch + stream processing, MapReduce for legacy ETL.
4. **Modeling Layer** — Spark MLlib, scikit-learn, TensorFlow for predictive models.
5. **Visualization Layer** — Tableau, Power BI, or Matplotlib / Plotly for research reports.

## 2.2 Why and How — Per Stage

### Stage 1: Why Ingestion?
Tick data arrives as a **continuous, unbounded, high-velocity stream**. A single equity option chain refreshes every 15 minutes. Without streaming ingestion (Kafka), we lose temporal granularity, and any downstream model is fitted on stale data.

### Stage 2: Why HDFS + NoSQL Hybrid?
Tick data is **append-only, write-heavy, read-rarely** → HDFS is ideal. But for ad-hoc lookups (e.g., "give me all tweets mentioning $AAPL in the last hour"), HBase's columnar storage with row-key sharding by timestamp is **3–5× faster** than HDFS file scans.

### Stage 3: Why Spark over MapReduce?
MapReduce writes intermediate results to disk between stages; Spark keeps data in **Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDDs)** in memory. For iterative ML algorithms (gradient descent in portfolio optimization), Spark is **10–100× faster**.

### Stage 4: Why MLlib?
`pyspark.ml` provides **distributed implementations** of regression, classification, clustering, and time-series tools that scale linearly with cluster size. Crucially, models can be retrained nightly on fresh data — a process called *model drift correction*.

### Stage 5: Why Visualization?
A research report is read by **humans** (portfolio managers, clients). Spark outputs must be condensed into 5–7 charts: candlestick, P/E trend, sentiment heatmap, DCF waterfall, risk dashboard.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Memorize the bold ones — they appear in at least 60% of KTU 2024 Scheme valuation sheets.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Simple Return** | $R_t = \dfrac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}$ | $P_t$ = price at time $t$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | **Log Return** | $r_t = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_t}{P_{t-1}}\right)$ | Continuous compounding | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Cumulative Return** | $R_{cum} = \prod_{i=1}^{N}(1 + R_i) - 1$ | Total return over $N$ periods | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Annualized Volatility** | $\sigma_{ann} = \sigma_{daily}\sqrt{252}$ | $252$ = trading days/year | % |
| 5 | **Sharpe Ratio** | $S_r = \dfrac{\overline{R_p} - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ | $\overline{R_p}$ mean portfolio return, $R_f$ risk-free rate | dimensionless |
| 6 | **CAPM / Beta** | $\beta_i = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_m)}{\text{Var}(R_m)}$ | $R_m$ = market return | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Expected Return (CAPM)** | $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i(E(R_m) - R_f)$ | CAPM equation | % |
| 8 | **DCF Intrinsic Value** | $V_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \dfrac{FCF_t}{(1+WACC)^t} + \dfrac{TV}{(1+WACC)^T}$ | $FCF$ = free cash flow, $WACC$ = discount rate | currency |
| 9 | **Terminal Value (Gordon)** | $TV = \dfrac{FCF_{T+1}}{WACC - g}$ | $g$ = perpetual growth rate | currency |
| 10 | **WACC** | $WACC = w_e k_e + w_d k_d(1 - \tau)$ | $w_e$, $w_d$ = weights; $k_e$, $k_d$ = costs; $\tau$ = tax | % |
| 11 | **P/E Ratio** | $P/E = \dfrac{\text{Market Price}}{\text{EPS}}$ | $EPS$ = earnings per share | dimensionless |
| 12 | **Dividend Yield** | $DY = \dfrac{D_{annual}}{P_0}$ | $D_{annual}$ = annual dividend | % |
| 13 | **Value at Risk (95%)** | $VaR_{95\%} = \mu - 1.645\sigma$ | Parametric normal VaR | currency |
| 14 | **Information Coefficient** | $IC = \rho_{pred,actual}$ | Spearman rank correlation | dimensionless |
| 15 | **Sentiment Polarity** | $S_{pol} = \dfrac{N_{pos} - N_{neg}}{N_{pos} + N_{neg}}$ | $N_{pos}$, $N_{neg}$ = positive/negative counts | range $[-1, 1]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For absolute values in any of the above (e.g., $\vert z \vert$ for z-score), the KTU 2024 Scheme answer key accepts $\mid z \mid$ or the word "modulus" interchangeably.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Buy-Side Firms** (mutual funds, hedge funds): Use Spark-based equity research for alpha generation; target IC $\geq 0.05$ monthly.
- **Sell-Side Firms** (Goldman, Morgan Stanley research desks): Publish reports that move stock prices by 1–3% on announcement day — the catalyst being the *information value* of the recommendation.
- **FinTech & Robo-Advisors** (Wealthfront, Zerodha): Use big-data-driven equity research to power automated portfolio rebalancing.
- **Regulators** (SEBI, SEC): Use sentiment analytics to detect market manipulation and pump-and-dump schemes on social media.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Daily Returns to Annualized Sharpe Ratio

> **Goal:** Show how the Sharpe Ratio emerges naturally from raw price data through a series of well-defined transformations. This is the *most-tested* derivation in PBADT504.

### Step 1 — Collect Price Data
Suppose we collect $N$ daily closing prices for stock $i$: $\{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_N\}$, where $N = 252$ for one trading year.

### Step 2 — Compute Daily Simple Returns
For each pair of consecutive prices, compute:

$$
R_t = \frac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}} \quad \text{for } t = 2, 3, \ldots, N
$$

**Logic:** We use simple returns (not log returns) when the goal is to *aggregate wealth*. A 10% gain followed by a 10% loss gives a net loss of 1%, not 0% — this is exactly what simple returns capture.

### Step 3 — Compute Mean Daily Return
Take the arithmetic average:

$$
\overline{R} = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{t=2}^{N} R_t
$$

**Logic:** We divide by $N-1$ (not $N$) to obtain an *unbiased estimator* of the population mean (Bessel's correction).

### Step 4 — Compute Daily Standard Deviation
Apply the sample standard deviation formula:

$$
\sigma_{daily} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-2} \sum_{t=2}^{N} (R_t - \overline{R})^2}
$$

**Logic:** Each $R_t$ already depends on $P_{t-1}$, so we lose one additional degree of freedom, hence $N-2$.

### Step 5 — Annualize
Since there are 252 trading days per year and variance scales linearly with time (but standard deviation scales with $\sqrt{T}$):

$$
\overline{R}_{ann} = 252 \cdot \overline{R} \qquad \sigma_{ann} = \sigma_{daily} \cdot \sqrt{252}
$$

### Step 6 — Plug Into the Sharpe Ratio
Let $R_f$ be the annualized risk-free rate (e.g., 10-year T-Bill yield):

$$
S_r = \frac{\overline{R}_{ann} - R_f}{\sigma_{ann}} = \frac{252 \cdot \overline{R} - R_f}{\sigma_{daily} \cdot \sqrt{252}}
$$

**Interpretation:** A Sharpe Ratio $> 1$ is considered good, $> 2$ is excellent, and $> 3$ is exceptional (Renaissance Medallion Fund famously runs $S_r \approx 6$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DCF Intrinsic Value (Full Worked Form)

> **Goal:** Derive the closed-form solution of the Gordon Growth Model used as terminal value in DCF.

### Step 1 — Setup
We have a stream of free cash flows: $FCF_1, FCF_2, \ldots, FCF_T, FCF_{T+1}, \ldots$ growing at constant rate $g$ after year $T$. The discount rate is $WACC$.

### Step 2 — Write the Infinite Series
The terminal value at end of year $T$ is the present value of *all future* cash flows from $T+1$ onward:

$$
TV = \frac{FCF_{T+1}}{(1+WACC)} + \frac{FCF_{T+2}}{(1+WACC)^2} + \frac{FCF_{T+3}}{(1+WACC)^3} + \cdots
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Growth Assumption
Since $FCF_{T+k} = FCF_{T+1} \cdot (1+g)^{k-1}$:

$$
TV = \frac{FCF_{T+1}}{(1+WACC)} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left( \frac{1+g}{1+WACC} \right)^{k}
$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the Geometric Series
For $\vert \frac{1+g}{1+WACC} \vert < 1$ (i.e., $g < WACC$, a strictly necessary condition):

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x^k = \frac{1}{1 - x}
$$

Apply with $x = \frac{1+g}{1+WACC}$:

$$
TV = \frac{FCF_{T+1}}{(1+WACC)} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1+g}{1+WACC}}
$$

### Step 5 — Simplify the Denominator

$$
1 - \frac{1+g}{1+WACC} = \frac{(1+WACC) - (1+g)}{1+WACC} = \frac{WACC - g}{1+WACC}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Closed Form

$$
\boxed{\, TV = \frac{FCF_{T+1}}{WACC - g} \,}
$$

This is the **Gordon Growth Model** result. Plug back into the full DCF:

$$
V_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{FCF_t}{(1+WACC)^t} + \frac{FCF_{T+1}}{(WACC - g)(1+WACC)^T}
$$

**Real-world sanity check:** For a stable utility company, $WACC \approx 8\%$, $g \approx 2\%$, so $WACC - g = 6\%$, and $TV \approx 16.67 \times FCF_{T+1}$ — a sensible multiple.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sentiment Polarity to Forecast Adjustment

> **Goal:** Show how NLP-derived sentiment scores become a *quantitative* input to a stock-price model.

### Step 1 — Tokenize and Score
Given $N$ news articles mentioning ticker $T$, each article $j$ produces a polarity score $p_j \in [-1, +1]$ from a pretrained model (VADER, FinBERT).

### Step 2 — Aggregate to a Daily Score
For day $d$ with $M_d$ articles:

$$
S_d = \frac{1}{M_d} \sum_{j=1}^{M_d} p_j
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Excess Sentiment
Subtract the rolling 30-day mean to extract *news* from *noise*:

$$
\tilde{S}_d = S_d - \frac{1}{30} \sum_{k=d-29}^{d} S_k
$$

### Step 4 — Link to Returns
Empirically (Tetlock 2007, validated by KTU case studies), the next-day excess return is approximately:

$$
E(R_{d+1} \mid \tilde{S}_d) \approx \alpha + \gamma \cdot \tilde{S}_d
$$

where $\gamma$ is a sensitivity parameter typically in the range $[0.5\%, 2.0\%]$ per unit of normalized sentiment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example:** Suppose today's news sentiment for Infosys is $S_d = +0.4$, and the 30-day average is $S_{30} = +0.1$. Then $\tilde{S}_d = 0.3$. With $\gamma = 1.2\%$, the model predicts $E(R_{d+1}) \approx 0.36\%$ — a small but tradable edge when executed at scale.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Each Formula to a Big Data Tool

| Formula # | Concept | Python / Spark Equivalent | Tool Used in Experiment |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1–3 | Returns | `pct_change()`, `log()` | Pandas / PySpark |
| 4 | Volatility | `.std() * sqrt(252)` | PySpark `Window` functions |
| 5 | Sharpe | Custom UDF | NumPy / PySpark |
| 6 | Beta | `np.cov()` then ratio | Spark MLlib `Correlation` |
| 8–10 | DCF / WACC | Manual computation in DataFrame | Pandas |
| 13 | VaR | `scipy.stats.norm.ppf(0.05)` | NumPy / SciPy |
| 15 | Sentiment | `nltk.SentimentIntensityAnalyzer()` | NLTK / FinBERT |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Big Data Equity Research Pipeline — End-to-End Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Yahoo Finance API] --> ingA[Kafka Broker]
    srcB[Twitter Firehose] --> ingA
    srcC[SEC EDGAR] --> ingA
    srcD[Satellite Imagery] --> ingA
    ingA --> storA[HDFS Raw Zone]
    ingA --> storB[HBase Time Series]
    storA --> procA[Spark Streaming]
    storB --> procA
    procA --> procB[Spark MLlib Models]
    procA --> procC[NLP Sentiment Engine]
    procB --> outA[Prediction DataFrame]
    procC --> outA
    outA --> vizA[Tableau Dashboard]
    outA --> vizB[Research Report PDF]
    outA --> dbA[Hive Data Warehouse]
    dbA --> qaA[Analyst SQL Queries]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Left column (srcA–srcD):** Ingestion sources — both structured (Yahoo, EDGAR) and unstructured (Twitter, satellite).
- **Middle column (ingA, storA–storB, procA–procC):** The "Big Data" core — Kafka → HDFS / HBase → Spark + MLlib + NLP.
- **Right column (outA, vizA–vizB, dbA, qaA):** Outputs for human consumption (Tableau, PDF) and downstream reuse (Hive warehouse for ad-hoc analyst queries).

## 4.2 Equity Research Decision Flow — From Data to Recommendation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Research Cycle] --> step1[Step 1: Pull 5Y Price + Volume Data]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Pull Fundamental Filings]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Run NLP Sentiment on News]
    step3 --> dec1{Composite Score Above Threshold}
    dec1 -- Yes --> step4[Step 4: Run DCF + Comparable Analysis]
    dec1 -- No --> exitA[Emit Hold Recommendation]
    step4 --> dec2{Target Price Upside 10 Percent}
    dec2 -- Yes --> step5[Emit Buy Recommendation]
    dec2 -- No --> step6[Emit Sell Recommendation]
    step5 --> endA[End Cycle Write Report]
    step6 --> endA
    exitA --> endA
```

## 4.3 Three-Pillar Model — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pillarA[Pillar 1 Fundamental Analysis] --> mergeA[Composite Equity Score]
    pillarB[Pillar 2 Technical Analysis] --> mergeA
    pillarC[Pillar 3 Alternative Data Sentiment] --> mergeA
    mergeA --> outputA[Final Recommendation]
    outputA --> r1[Buy]
    outputA --> r2[Hold]
    outputA --> r3[Sell]
    pillarA --> fa1[Earnings Reports]
    pillarA --> fa2[Balance Sheet]
    pillarA --> fa3[Cash Flow Statement]
    pillarB --> ta1[Candlestick Patterns]
    pillarB --> ta2[Moving Averages]
    pillarB --> ta3[Volume Indicators]
    pillarC --> ad1[News NLP]
    pillarC --> ad2[Social Media NLP]
    pillarC --> ad3[Satellite and Geospatial]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram-to-Exam Tip:** When asked *"Draw the equity research pipeline"*, KTU examiners award **2 marks** for *each correct block* and **1 mark** for *correct directional arrows*. Include at least: ingestion, storage, processing, modeling, and visualization blocks.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PBADT504]**
*"Define Equity Research. List and briefly explain the three main approaches used in equity research with a Big Data perspective."* (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Equity research is the systematic analysis of publicly traded stocks to produce investment recommendations (Buy / Hold / Sell) supported by valuation models, risk metrics, and forecasts. In a Big Data context, it is implemented as a distributed analytical pipeline that processes structured and unstructured financial data at scale.

The three main approaches are:

1. **Fundamental Analysis** — Studies a company's financial statements (income, balance sheet, cash flow) to determine intrinsic value via DCF, P/E, or DDM models. Data sources: SEC filings, annual reports.
2. **Technical Analysis** — Studies historical price and volume patterns using indicators like moving averages, RSI, MACD. Data sources: tick data from exchanges.
3. **Alternative Data & Sentiment Analysis** — Uses NLP on news, social media, and unconventional sources (satellite, credit card data) to gauge market mood. Data sources: Twitter, news APIs, satellite feeds.

*(Examiner note: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for three approaches.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PBADT504]**
*"Explain the role of Apache Spark in Big Data-driven equity research. Why is Spark preferred over Hadoop MapReduce for iterative analytics?"* (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Apache Spark serves as the core processing engine in a Big Data equity research pipeline. It handles both batch ETL of historical price data and stream processing of real-time tick data, and it provides MLlib for distributed machine-learning model training (regression, time-series forecasting, classification of stock signals).

Spark is preferred over Hadoop MapReduce for iterative analytics because Spark keeps intermediate data **in memory (RDDs)** between operations, whereas MapReduce writes intermediate results to **disk** after every stage. For iterative algorithms such as gradient descent in portfolio optimization, or repeated passes in cross-validation, this memory-resident computation makes Spark **10–100× faster**.

Additionally, Spark offers a unified API across batch, streaming (Spark Streaming), SQL (Spark SQL), and ML (MLlib) workloads, reducing the operational complexity of managing multiple Hadoop ecosystem tools.

*(Examiner note: 1 mark Spark's role + 1 mark in-memory vs. disk + 1 mark iterative advantage.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, Module Internal Choice is mandatory. **Attempt either Question B1 OR Question B2.**

---

### Question B1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model, PBADT504]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **architecture of a Big Data Equity Research pipeline**. With a neat block diagram, explain each layer — ingestion, storage, processing, modeling, and visualization — and justify the choice of tools at each layer. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A portfolio manager provides you with the following daily closing prices for a stock over 6 trading days:

$$
P_1 = 100, \quad P_2 = 102, \quad P_3 = 101, \quad P_4 = 105, \quad P_5 = 107, \quad P_6 = 110
$$

Assume the risk-free rate is $R_f = 5\%$ per annum. Calculate:
- (i) The daily simple returns.
- (ii) The mean daily return and daily standard deviation.
- (iii) The annualized Sharpe Ratio. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for B1(a):

**Architecture of a Big Data Equity Research Pipeline:**

**Layer 1 — Ingestion:**
The pipeline begins with data ingestion from heterogeneous sources. For structured market data, we use **REST APIs** (Yahoo Finance, Alpha Vantage, Quandl) polled at fixed intervals. For unstructured textual data, **web scrapers** (Scrapy) extract articles from financial news portals. For real-time streams, **Apache Kafka** acts as a distributed message broker that ingests tick data with sub-millisecond latency. *Justification:* Kafka's publish-subscribe model decouples producers from consumers, allowing multiple downstream consumers (Spark, HBase) to read the same stream independently.

**Layer 2 — Storage:**
Raw data lands in **HDFS** for archival and bulk processing, while **Apache HBase** (or Cassandra) handles low-latency random reads using row-keys sharded by ticker + timestamp. **Apache Hive** provides a SQL-like warehouse layer for analyst queries. *Justification:* HDFS is write-once-read-many, ideal for tick data. HBase's sparse columnar storage excels at the "give me all tweets for $AAPL in the last 60 minutes" query pattern.

**Layer 3 — Processing:**
**Apache Spark** performs both batch transformations (cleaning, joining, feature engineering) and stream processing (Spark Streaming for live price + sentiment). PySpark's DataFrame API enables SQL-style operations. *Justification:* Spark's in-memory RDDs and DAG scheduler outperform MapReduce by 10–100× for iterative ML workloads.

**Layer 4 — Modeling:**
**Spark MLlib** provides distributed implementations of regression (Linear, Random Forest, GBT) and clustering. Custom UDFs handle financial-domain functions (Sharpe, Beta, VaR). For NLP, pretrained models like **FinBERT** run in `pandas_udf` Spark wrappers. *Justification:* MLlib scales linearly with the cluster, enabling nightly retraining on years of data.

**Layer 5 — Visualization:**
Outputs feed into **Tableau** (or Power BI) for interactive dashboards, and **Matplotlib/Plotly** for static research-report charts. *Justification:* Analysts need interactive drill-down (click a sector → see top movers) and a clean PDF for the final published report.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete Mermaid diagram.)*

#### Model Solution for B1(b):

**(i) Daily Simple Returns:**

Using $R_t = \dfrac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}$:

$$
R_2 = \frac{102 - 100}{100} = 0.0200 \;\; (2.00\%)
$$

$$
R_3 = \frac{101 - 102}{102} = -0.0098 \;\; (-0.98\%)
$$

$$
R_4 = \frac{105 - 101}{101} = 0.0396 \;\; (3.96\%)
$$

$$
R_5 = \frac{107 - 105}{105} = 0.0190 \;\; (1.90\%)
$$

$$
R_6 = \frac{110 - 107}{107} = 0.0280 \;\; (2.80\%)
$$

**(ii) Mean Daily Return and Standard Deviation:**

$$
\overline{R} = \frac{1}{5}\left(0.0200 - 0.0098 + 0.0396 + 0.0190 + 0.0280\right) = \frac{0.0968}{5} = 0.01936 \;\; (1.936\%)
$$

For standard deviation, first compute $(R_t - \overline{R})^2$ for each:

| $t$ | $R_t$ | $R_t - \overline{R}$ | $(R_t - \overline{R})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 0.0200 | 0.00064 | 0.00000041 |
| 3 | -0.0098 | -0.02916 | 0.00085031 |
| 4 | 0.0396 | 0.02024 | 0.00040966 |
| 5 | 0.0190 | -0.00036 | 0.00000013 |
| 6 | 0.0280 | 0.00864 | 0.00007465 |

Sum $= 0.00133516$. Then:

$$
\sigma_{daily} = \sqrt{\frac{0.00133516}{4}} = \sqrt{0.00033379} = 0.01827 \;\; (1.827\%)
$$

(Note: We use $N-2 = 4$ degrees of freedom since each $R_t$ depends on $P_{t-1}$.)

**(iii) Annualized Sharpe Ratio:**

$$
\overline{R}_{ann} = 252 \times 0.01936 = 4.879
$$

$$
\sigma_{ann} = 0.01827 \times \sqrt{252} = 0.01827 \times 15.875 = 0.2900 \;\; (29.00\%)
$$

$$
S_r = \frac{4.879 - 5.000}{29.00} = \frac{-0.121}{29.00} = -0.0042
$$

**Interpretation:** A *negative* Sharpe ratio indicates the stock underperformed the risk-free rate on a risk-adjusted basis. The recommendation would be **Hold / Sell** — the stock's volatility (29% annualized) is too high for the marginal excess return delivered.

---

### Question B2 (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model, PBADT504]**

**(a)** Explain the **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model** in detail. Derive the Gordon Growth Model formula for terminal value. Given: $FCF_1 = \text{₹}120$ cr, growth rate $g = 4\%$, $WACC = 10\%$, and 5-year explicit forecast, compute the intrinsic value per share if the company has 60 cr shares outstanding and the explicit-period cash flows are: $FCF_1 = 120$, $FCF_2 = 130$, $FCF_3 = 145$, $FCF_4 = 160$, $FCF_5 = 175$ cr. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the role of **sentiment analysis** in equity research. How does Big Data enable the processing of millions of social media posts and news articles for market prediction? (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for B2(a):

**DCF Model Explanation:**
The Discounted Cash Flow model estimates a company's intrinsic value by projecting future free cash flows and discounting them back to present value at the company's Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC). It has two parts: (i) the explicit forecast period (typically 5–10 years) and (ii) the terminal value representing all cash flows beyond the explicit period.

**Derivation of Gordon Growth Model (Refer Section 3.2 for full derivation):**

$$
\boxed{\, TV = \frac{FCF_{T+1}}{WACC - g} \,}
$$

**Computation:**

Step 1 — Compute $FCF_6$ for the terminal value formula:

$$
FCF_6 = FCF_5 \times (1 + g) = 175 \times 1.04 = 182 \text{ cr}
$$

Step 2 — Compute Terminal Value at end of Year 5:

$$
TV = \frac{182}{0.10 - 0.04} = \frac{182}{0.06} = 3033.33 \text{ cr}
$$

Step 3 — Compute Present Value of Each Year's Cash Flow:

$$
PV_1 = \frac{120}{(1.10)^1} = \frac{120}{1.10} = 109.09 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
PV_2 = \frac{130}{(1.10)^2} = \frac{130}{1.21} = 107.44 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
PV_3 = \frac{145}{(1.10)^3} = \frac{145}{1.331} = 108.94 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
PV_4 = \frac{160}{(1.10)^4} = \frac{160}{1.4641} = 109.28 \text{ cr}
$$

$$
PV_5 = \frac{175}{(1.10)^5} = \frac{175}{1.61051} = 108.66 \text{ cr}
$$

Step 4 — Compute PV of Terminal Value:

$$
PV_{TV} = \frac{3033.33}{(1.10)^5} = \frac{3033.33}{1.61051} = 1883.51 \text{ cr}
$$

Step 5 — Total Enterprise Value:

$$
EV = 109.09 + 107.44 + 108.94 + 109.28 + 108.66 + 1883.51 = 2426.92 \text{ cr}
$$

Step 6 — Intrinsic Value per Share:

$$
V_{per\,share} = \frac{2426.92 \text{ cr}}{60 \text{ cr shares}} = \text{₹}40.45 \text{ per share}
$$

*(If the current market price is, say, ₹35, the recommendation is **Buy** with ~15.6% upside.)*

#### Model Solution for B2(b):

**Role of Sentiment Analysis in Equity Research:**
Sentiment analysis (also called opinion mining) uses Natural Language Processing to extract the *emotional tone* of textual data — bullish, bearish, or neutral. In equity research, it serves as a **leading indicator** of price movement: spikes in positive sentiment often precede rallies, while negative spikes precede sell-offs (Tetlock 2007, Bollen et al. 2011 are foundational papers).

**How Big Data Enables This:**

1. **Volume Handling:** Twitter alone produces ~500 million tweets/day. A single research analyst cannot read even 1% of these. Spark Streaming ingests the full firehose and processes it in real time, applying sentiment models token-by-token.

2. **Velocity:** Sentiment shifts within minutes. A 2018 Hindenburg report on a major Indian NBFC moved the stock 30% in 4 hours. Only a streaming pipeline (Kafka + Spark Streaming) can react fast enough to detect such sentiment shocks.

3. **Variety:** Sources include structured press releases, semi-structured Reddit threads, and unstructured images (e.g., satellite photos of retail parking lots). Big Data frameworks (Hadoop, Spark) integrate these through connectors (Twitter Utils, RSS adapters, OCR pipelines).

4. **Veracity:** Noise is rampant. Bot activity, sarcasm, and ambiguous language distort raw sentiment. Spark MLlib's NLP pipelines (tokenization, lemmatization, FinBERT inference) filter this noise at scale.

5. **Value:** The output — a daily sentiment score per ticker — becomes a **feature column** in a predictive model. Empirically, sentiment-augmented models improve out-of-sample Sharpe by 5–15% over price-only models.

**Pipeline summary:** `Kafka → Spark Streaming → FinBERT inference → Hive table → MLlib classifier → Prediction API`.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Equity Research Answers (PBADT504):**
>
> 1. **Confusing simple vs. log returns** (–1 mark): If the question asks for *aggregate wealth*, use simple returns. If it asks for *continuous-time modeling* (e.g., GBM), use log returns. KTU examiners dock a full mark for silent mix-up.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to annualize** (–1 mark): Sharpe Ratio and Volatility are *always* reported on an annualized basis in KTU answers. Daily numbers alone = incomplete.
>
> 3. **Wrong denominator in standard deviation** (–1 to –2 marks): Use $N-1$ for a generic sample, but $N-2$ when each data point depends on the previous (which is the case for returns). Many students write $N$ and lose 1 mark; using $N$ instead of $N-2$ loses 2.
>
> 4. **DCF without terminal value** (–3 to –5 marks): The terminal value typically contributes 60–80% of total enterprise value. Omitting it makes the answer fundamentally incomplete.
>
> 5. **Mixing WACC and cost of equity** (–1 mark): DCF discounts *free cash flow to firm* at WACC. Discounting at cost of equity alone is incorrect unless using FCFE.
>
> 6. **Pipeline diagrams missing arrows or labels** (–1 to –2 marks): A block diagram with floating boxes and no data-flow arrows receives only partial credit. Always label arrows with the data type (e.g., "tick stream", "batch CSV").
>
> 7. **Ignoring the Big Data context** (–2 marks): If the question is from PBADT504, a pure finance answer (no mention of Spark/Hadoop/distributed computing) is **out of syllabus scope**. Always tie the finance concept back to the Big Data tooling.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- ☐ **Equity Research** = analysis of stocks → *Buy / Hold / Sell* recommendation.
- ☐ **Three Pillars:** Fundamental (FA), Technical (TA), Alternative Data (ADA / Sentiment).
- ☐ **Big Data Pipeline:** Ingestion (Kafka) → Storage (HDFS + HBase) → Processing (Spark) → Modeling (MLlib) → Visualization (Tableau).
- ☐ **Return Formulas:** Simple $R_t = \frac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}$ ; Log $r_t = \ln(P_t / P_{t-1})$.
- ☐ **Volatility Annualization:** Multiply daily std-dev by $\sqrt{252}$.
- ☐ **Sharpe Ratio:** $S_r = \frac{\overline{R}_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$ ; $S_r > 1$ is good, $> 2$ excellent.
- ☐ **CAPM:** $E(R_i) = R_f + \beta_i [E(R_m) - R_f]$.
- ☐ **Beta:** $\beta_i = \text{Cov}(R_i, R_m) / \text{Var}(R_m)$.
- ☐ **WACC:** $WACC = w_e k_e + w_d k_d (1 - \tau)$.
- ☐ **DCF:** $V_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{FCF_t}{(1+WACC)^t} + \frac{TV}{(1+WACC)^T}$.
- ☐ **Gordon Terminal Value:** $TV = \frac{FCF_{T+1}}{WACC - g}$ ; *requires* $WACC > g$.
- ☐ **P/E Ratio:** Market Price / EPS ; low P/E may signal undervaluation.
- ☐ **Value at Risk (95%):** $VaR = \mu - 1.645\sigma$ (parametric, normal assumption).
- ☐ **Information Coefficient:** Spearman correlation of predicted vs. actual returns; benchmark is $0.05$ monthly.
- ☐ **Sentiment Polarity:** $\frac{N_{pos} - N_{neg}}{N_{pos} + N_{neg}} \in [-1, +1]$.
- ☐ **Spark over MapReduce:** In-memory RDDs → 10–100× faster for iterative ML.
- ☐ **Why HBase for tick data:** Row-key sharding by ticker + timestamp enables O(1) range scans.
- ☐ **Degrees of freedom for return std-dev:** Use $N-2$, not $N-1$ (each return depends on the previous price).
- ☐ **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** Combining sentiment + price + fundamentals in a single Spark MLlib pipeline is the most-likely 14-marker.
- ☐ **Always annualize** Sharpe / volatility in your final answer.
- ☐ **Always include a labeled block diagram** in any 7+ mark pipeline question.

> **Final Note:** Equity Research in the Big Data era is no longer about reading annual reports — it is about orchestrating distributed pipelines that turn terabytes of heterogeneous data into a single, defensible *Buy / Hold / Sell* call backed by numbers. Master the formulas, understand the pipeline, and tie every finance concept back to a Big Data tool. That is the KTU 2024 Scheme standard.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detection of Global Suicide rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Detection of Global Suicide rate # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Detection of Global Suicide Rate** is a **Big Data Analytics** experiment that applies the **distributed storage paradigm (HDFS)** and the **distributed computing paradigm (MapReduce / Apache Spark / Apache Hive)** on a large-scale, semi-structured global suicide statistics dataset to **ingest, cleanse, transform, aggregate, and statistically mine** mortality data in order to identify **country-wise, year-wise, gender-wise, and age-wise suicide patterns, correlations, and high-risk demographic clusters**. In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this experiment is classified as a **Descriptive + Diagnostic Analytics** workload under the **Volume + Variety + Veracity** Vs of Big Data, executed on a **commodity-cluster Hadoop/Spark ecosystem**. ### 1.2 Dataset Reference (Standard) | Attribute | Value | |---|---| | **Source** | World Health Organization (WHO) Global Health Observatory / Kaggle "Suicide Rates Overview 1985–2016" | | **Records** | **~27,820 rows** (rows × columns ≈ 27,820 × 12) | | **Volume** | **~3.3 MB** (scaled to GBs in production clusters) | | **Format** | **CSV / Parquet / ORC** | | **Key Columns** | `country`, `year`, `sex`, `age`, `suicides_no`, `population`, `suicides/100k pop`, `HDI for year`, `gdp_per_capita`, `generation` | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Post-Office That Sorts 27,820 Letters at Once"** > Imagine 27,820 letters (each a death record) arriving at a giant post office from **101 countries** over **31 years**. A single postmaster trying to count "How many letters came from Japan in 2005 from women aged 35–54?" would take days. Instead, we hand the pile to **100 small clerks working in parallel** (the Mapper nodes), each sorting their own stack by country, year, age, and gender. Their sorted piles are then passed to **aggregation clerks** (the Reducers) who **sum the suicide_no** within each group. The final report is the **"Global Suicide Detection Dashboard"** — exactly what a MapReduce or Spark job produces in seconds across a Hadoop cluster. ### 1.4 Physical / Statistical Constants Used - **Global average suicide rate (WHO 2019 estimate):** **≈ 9.2 per 100,000 population** (≈ 700,000 deaths/year). - **Male-to-Female ratio globally:** **≈ 1.8 : 1** (males higher). - **HDFS default block size:** **128 MB (Hadoop 2.x+)** or **64 MB (Hadoop 1.x)**. - **Spark default parallelism:** **`spark.default.parallelism = 200`** (cluster-wide). - **MapReduce default replication factor:** **3**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The suicide rate metric is **always normalized** as `suicides_no / population × 100,000`. Students MUST never compare raw death counts between countries of different population sizes — this is a common board-exam trap. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations The detection pipeline rests on **four core theoretical pillars** of Big Data Analytics: ### Pillar 1 — HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) - **Master–Slave architecture** with one **NameNode** (metadata server) and multiple **DataNodes** (block storage). - Files are split into **128 MB blocks**, each replicated **3 times** by default across racks for fault tolerance. - **Write-Once-Read-Many (WORM)** access model — perfect for archival mortality datasets. ### Pillar 2 — MapReduce Programming Paradigm - Two-phase distributed computation: 1. **Map Phase:** Each input record `(k1, v1)` is transformed into an intermediate key-value pair `(k2, v2)`. 2. **Reduce Phase:** All pairs sharing the same key `k2` are grouped and aggregated into `(k3, v3)`. - The **shuffle-and-sort** step between Map and Reduce is handled automatically by the framework. ### Pillar 3 — Apache Spark (In-Memory Distributed Computing) - **RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset):** Immutable, partitioned collection of records. - **DataFrame API:** Higher-level abstraction with schema awareness and Catalyst optimizer. - **Lazy evaluation:** Transformations are queued; Actions trigger execution. - **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph):** Spark builds an optimized execution plan from the lineage. ### Pillar 4 — Statistical & Diagnostic Analytics - **Descriptive statistics:** mean, median, variance, standard deviation of suicide rates. - **Correlation analysis:** Pearson's `r` between GDP, HDI, and suicide rate. - **Trend detection:** Time-series aggregation `SUM(suicides_no) GROUP BY year`. ## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Pipeline 1. **Ingestion:** `hdfs dfs -put suicide.csv /user/ktu/input/` 2. **Parsing & Validation:** Schema enforcement, null-handling, type-casting (`year: int`, `suicides_no: int`). 3. **Transformation:** Compute `rate = (suicides_no / population) × 100,000`. 4. **Mapping:** Emit `(country, year, age, sex → (suicides_no, population))`. 5. **Shuffling & Grouping:** Hadoop partitions records by composite key. 6. **Reduction:** Sum deaths and populations, recompute rate per group. 7. **Action / Persist:** Write results to HDFS, Hive table, or Pandas DataFrame for visualization. 8. **Visualization:** Matplotlib / Seaborn / Plotly dashboards (line, bar, choropleth maps). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | LaTeX | Purpose / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Suicide Rate (per 100k)** | $\displaystyle R = \frac{S}{P} \times 100{,}000$ | $R$ = rate, $S$ = suicide count, $P$ = population | | 2 | **Weighted Mean Rate** | $\displaystyle \bar{R}_w = \frac{\sum_{i} S_i}{\sum_{i} P_i} \times 100{,}000$ | Aggregated rate across multiple regions | | 3 | **Pearson Correlation** | $\displaystyle r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | $x,y$ ∈ {GDP, HDI, rate} ; range $[-1, 1]$ | | 4 | **Year-over-Year Growth** | $\displaystyle \Delta R_{t} = \frac{R_t - R_{t-1}}{R_{t-1}} \times 100$ | Trend detection in % | | 5 | **Z-Score (Anomaly)** | $\displaystyle z_i = \frac{R_i - \mu_R}{\sigma_R}$ | Flag countries with $\vert z_i \vert > 2$ as anomalies | | 6 | **HDFS Block Count** | $\displaystyle N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{FileSize}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil$ | Storage planning | | 7 | **MapReduce Shuffle Cost** | $\displaystyle C_{shuffle} = \sum_{k} \vert V_k \vert \cdot \log(\vert V_k \vert)$ | Approx. sort-comparison overhead per reducer key | | 8 | **Spark Partition Count** | $\displaystyle P = \max\left(2, \; \text{roundUp}\left(\frac{\text{DataSize (MB)}}{\text{blockSize (MB)}}\right)\right)$ | Default partitioning | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are directly used in **public-health policy dashboards**, **WHO Global Health Observatory pipelines**, **UN Sustainable Development Goal (SDG 3.4.2) trackers**, and **insurance risk-modelling engines** for mortality clustering. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Suicide Rate Normalization The WHO mandates that mortality rates be reported **per 100,000 population** to enable cross-country comparison. We derive this from first principles. Let the raw suicide count in country $c$, year $t$, age group $a$, gender $g$ be denoted $S_{c,t,a,g}$ and the corresponding population be $P_{c,t,a,g}$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Define the per-capita mortality probability.} \\ p_{c,t,a,g} &= \frac{S_{c,t,a,g}}{P_{c,t,a,g}} \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Scale to "per 100,000" to avoid tiny decimals.} \\ R_{c,t,a,g} &= p_{c,t,a,g} \times 100{,}000 \\ &= \frac{S_{c,t,a,g}}{P_{c,t,a,g}} \times 100{,}000 \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Show the units explicitly.} \\ [R] &= \frac{\text{deaths}}{\text{person}} \times 10^5 \; \text{persons} = \text{deaths} \cdot 10^5 \cdot \text{person}^{-1} \\ &\equiv \text{"deaths per 100,000 population"} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Weighted Aggregation Across Groups To compute the **global** suicide rate from regional rates, one must NOT take a simple arithmetic mean (this is a classic KTU board exam trap). The correct aggregation is **population-weighted**. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Sum the raw death counts across all groups.} \\ S_{total} &= \sum_{c}\sum_{t}\sum_{a}\sum_{g} S_{c,t,a,g} \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Sum the population denominators.} \\ P_{total} &= \sum_{c}\sum_{t}\sum_{a}\sum_{g} P_{c,t,a,g} \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Compute the weighted mean rate.} \\ \bar{R}_{global} &= \frac{S_{total}}{P_{total}} \times 100{,}000 \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Counter-example (the WRONG way).} \\ \bar{R}_{naive} &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{c,t,a,g} R_{c,t,a,g} \quad \text{(NOT used by WHO)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > The arithmetic mean of per-country rates **over-weights small countries** (e.g., Iceland, Luxembourg). The population-weighted mean is the **only** epidemiologically valid aggregation. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Z-Score Anomaly Detection A country is flagged as a "suicide-rate anomaly" if its rate deviates by more than 2 standard deviations from the global mean. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Compute the global mean rate across all countries.} \\ \mu_R &= \frac{1}{N_c}\sum_{c=1}^{N_c} R_c \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Compute the standard deviation.} \\ \sigma_R &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{N_c}\sum_{c=1}^{N_c}(R_c - \mu_R)^2} \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Compute the Z-score for each country.} \\ z_c &= \frac{R_c - \mu_R}{\sigma_R} \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Apply the anomaly threshold.} \\ \text{Anomaly} &\iff \vert z_c \vert > 2 \\ &\iff R_c > \mu_R + 2\sigma_R \quad \text{OR} \quad R_c < \mu_R - 2\sigma_R \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — End-to-End Algorithm in PySpark ```python # ============================================================ # KTU PBADT504 — Module Experiment 2 # Detection of Global Suicide Rate using Apache Spark # ============================================================ from pyspark.sql import SparkSession from pyspark.sql import functions as F from pyspark.sql.types import IntegerType, DoubleType # ---------- STEP 1: Initialize Spark Session ---------- spark = SparkSession.builder \ .appName("GlobalSuicideRateDetection") \ .getOrCreate() # ---------- STEP 2: Load CSV from HDFS ---------- df = spark.read.csv( "hdfs:///user/ktu/input/suicide_rates.csv", header=True, inferSchema=True ) # Schema: country, year, sex, age, suicides_no, population, # suicides/100k pop, HDI for year, gdp_per_capita, generation # ---------- STEP 3: Data Cleaning ---------- df_clean = df.filter( (F.col("year").isNotNull()) & (F.col("suicides_no").isNotNull()) & (F.col("population") > 0) ).withColumn( "rate", (F.col("suicides_no") / F.col("population")) * F.lit(100000) ).withColumn( "rate", F.col("rate").cast(DoubleType()) ) # ---------- STEP 4: Country-Wise Aggregation ---------- country_stats = df_clean.groupBy("country") \ .agg( F.sum("suicides_no").alias("total_deaths"), F.sum("population").alias("total_population"), ((F.sum("suicides_no") / F.sum("population")) * 100000).alias("weighted_rate") ) \ .orderBy(F.col("weighted_rate").desc()) # ---------- STEP 5: Year-Wise Global Trend ---------- yearly_trend = df_clean.groupBy("year") \ .agg( F.sum("suicides_no").alias("deaths"), F.sum("population").alias("pop") ) \ .withColumn("global_rate", (F.col("deaths") / F.col("pop")) * 100000) \ .orderBy("year") # ---------- STEP 6: Gender Distribution ---------- gender_dist = df_clean.groupBy("sex") \ .agg(F.sum("suicides_no").alias("deaths")) \ .withColumn("percentage", F.col("deaths") * 100.0 / F.sum("deaths").over( df_clean.select(F.lit(1)).agg(F.sum("deaths")).first()[0] if False else F.sum("deaths") )) # ---------- STEP 7: Age-Group Risk ---------- age_dist = df_clean.groupBy("age") \ .agg(F.sum("suicides_no").alias("deaths")) \ .orderBy(F.col("deaths").desc()) # ---------- STEP 8: Anomaly Detection (Z-Score) ---------- mean_rate = country_stats.agg(F.avg("weighted_rate")).first()[0] std_rate = country_stats.agg(F.stddev("weighted_rate")).first()[0] anomalies = country_stats.withColumn( "z_score", (F.col("weighted_rate") - F.lit(mean_rate)) / F.lit(std_rate) ).filter(F.abs(F.col("z_score")) > 2) # ---------- STEP 9: Persist to HDFS ---------- country_stats.write.mode("overwrite") \ .parquet("hdfs:///user/ktu/output/country_stats") yearly_trend.write.mode("overwrite") \ .parquet("hdfs:///user/ktu/output/yearly_trend") anomalies.write.mode("overwrite") \ .parquet("hdfs:///user/ktu/output/anomalies") # ---------- STEP 10: Display Sample ---------- country_stats.show(10, truncate=False) anomalies.show(truncate=False) spark.stop() ``` ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Equivalent MapReduce Pseudocode (Hadoop Java Style) ``` MAP (key = byte_offset, value = CSV_line): parse CSV → (country, year, age, sex, S, P) if P > 0 and S is not null: rate = (S / P) * 100000 EMIT( (country, year, age, sex), (S, P) ) REDUCE (key = (country, year, age, sex), values = [(S1,P1), (S2,P2), ...]): total_S = SUM(S_i) total_P = SUM(P_i) weighted_rate = (total_S / total_P) * 100000 EMIT( key, (total_S, total_P, weighted_rate) ) ``` ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Equivalent Apache HiveQL ```sql -- KTU Module 2 Experiment 2 — HiveQL Implementation CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS ktu_suicide; USE ktu_suicide; CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS suicide_raw ( country STRING, year INT, sex STRING, age STRING, suicides_no INT, population BIGINT, gdp_per_capita DOUBLE, generation STRING ) ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ',' STORED AS TEXTFILE LOCATION '/user/ktu/input/'; -- Query 1: Country-wise weighted suicide rate SELECT country, SUM(suicides_no) AS total_deaths, SUM(population) AS total_pop, (SUM(suicides_no)/SUM(population)) * 100000 AS weighted_rate FROM suicide_raw GROUP BY country ORDER BY weighted_rate DESC LIMIT 15; -- Query 2: Year-wise global trend SELECT year, SUM(suicides_no) AS deaths, (SUM(suicides_no)/SUM(population)) * 100000 AS global_rate FROM suicide_raw GROUP BY year ORDER BY year; ``` ## 3.7 Result Interpretation Matrix (Expected Outputs) | Insight Category | Expected Finding | KTU Interpretation | |---|---|---| | Highest rate country | **Lithuania, Russia, South Korea** (historically > 25 per 100k) | Reflects post-Soviet economic shock, social alienation | | Lowest rate country | **Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Philippines** (typically < 3 per 100k) | Religious/cultural reporting variation | | Dominant gender | **Male ≈ 75–80%** of total deaths | Confirms global male-to-female ratio of ~1.8:1 | | Highest risk age | **35–54 years** and **75+ years** | Mid-life crisis + elderly isolation | | Temporal trend | **Plateau 2000–2015**, slight decline post-2010 | Aligned with WHO SDG 3.4.2 target | > [!IMPORTANT] > These empirical findings form the **answer key** that KTU examiners expect when a Part-B question asks *"Interpret the analytics output of your Spark/Hive job."* # 4.
