KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question] **"Define the term 'Detection of Global Suicide Rate' in the context of Big Data Analytics. List any FOUR Big Data Vs that this experiment addresses."** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** Detection of Global Suicide Rate is a Big Data Analytics experiment that applies distributed storage (HDFS) and distributed processing (MapReduce / Spark / Hive) on a large-scale global mortality dataset to extract country-wise, year-wise, gender-wise, and age-wise suicide patterns, correlations, and high-risk demographic clusters. The FOUR Big Data Vs addressed are: 1. **Volume** — Tens of thousands of records spanning 101 countries × 31 years. 2. **Variety** — CSV / Parquet / ORC structured and semi-structured inputs. 3. **Veracity** — Missing HDI values, null populations, data quality issues in WHO records. 4. **Velocity** (optional) — Streaming ingestion via Flume/Kafka in production systems. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question] **"Differentiate between the population-weighted mean suicide rate and the simple arithmetic mean of per-country rates. Which one is used by WHO and why?"** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Arithmetic Mean | Population-Weighted Mean | |---|---|---| | **Formula** | $\displaystyle \frac{1}{N}\sum R_c$ | $\displaystyle \frac{\sum S_c}{\sum P_c} \times 100{,}000$ | | **Bias** | Over-weights small countries | Proportional to actual population | | **Used by WHO?** | **No** | **Yes (standard)** | | **Epidemiological Validity** | Low | High | The WHO uses the **population-weighted mean** because it correctly represents the **actual mortality risk per capita** at a global scale, avoiding the bias introduced by treating Iceland (population 370,000) and China (population 1.4 billion) with equal statistical weight. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) ### Question A (Choice 1) — *Spark + MapReduce Implementation* **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain the architecture of the Big Data pipeline you used to detect the global suicide rate. Draw a labelled block diagram and describe the role of **HDFS, MapReduce, and Spark** in the pipeline. *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** The pipeline is organized in **five logical layers**: 1. **Ingestion Layer:** The WHO/Kaggle CSV file is uploaded to HDFS using `hdfs dfs -put` or ingested via **Apache Sqoop / Flume** in production environments. 2. **Storage Layer — HDFS:** The CSV is split into **128 MB blocks** and stored across DataNodes with a **replication factor of 3**. The NameNode maintains the metadata (file → block → DataNode mapping). HDFS provides fault tolerance and supports the **Write-Once-Read-Many** access model ideal for archival mortality data. 3. **Processing Layer — MapReduce / Spark:** - **MapReduce** executes the two-phase `map()` and `reduce()` in parallel across the cluster. Mappers parse each record, validate, and emit `(composite_key, (suicides_no, population))`. Reducers sum the values and emit the weighted rate. - **Spark** improves on MapReduce by keeping intermediate data **in memory** (RDD lineage + DAG). The DataFrame API with the **Catalyst optimizer** produces efficient query plans, and lazy evaluation defers execution until an action is called. 4. **Analytical Layer:** Aggregations (`groupBy country / year / age / sex`), **Z-score anomaly detection** ($z_c = (R_c - \mu_R)/\sigma_R$), and **Pearson correlation** between GDP, HDI, and suicide rate are computed. 5. **Output & Visualization Layer:** Parquet files are written back to HDFS, then loaded into Pandas/Matplotlib/Seaborn for bar charts, line plots, and choropleth world maps. **Labelled Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.1) --- **(b)** Write the complete **PySpark program** to load the suicide dataset, clean it, compute the **country-wise weighted suicide rate**, and identify the **top-3 anomalous countries** using the **Z-score method**. Show sample expected output. *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** ```python from pyspark.sql import SparkSession from pyspark.sql import functions as F spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("SuicideAnomaly").getOrCreate() # 1. Load df = spark.read.csv("hdfs:///user/ktu/input/suicide_rates.csv", header=True, inferSchema=True) # 2. Clean clean = df.filter( F.col("suicides_no").isNotNull() & (F.col("population") > 0) ).withColumn("rate", (F.col("suicides_no")/F.col("population")) * 100000) # 3. Country-wise weighted rate country = clean.groupBy("country").agg( F.sum("suicides_no").alias("deaths"), F.sum("population").alias("pop"), ((F.sum("suicides_no")/F.sum("population")) * 100000).alias("rate") ) # 4. Z-score anomaly stats = country.agg(F.avg("rate").alias("mu"), F.stddev("rate").alias("sig")).first() anomaly = country.withColumn( "z", (F.col("rate") - F.lit(stats["mu"])) / F.lit(stats["sig"]) ).filter(F.abs(F.col("z")) > 2).orderBy(F.col("z").desc()) anomaly.show(3, truncate=False) ``` **Expected Sample Output:** ``` +-----------------+-------+-----------+-------------------+---------+ |country |deaths |pop |rate |z | +-----------------+-------+-----------+-------------------+---------+ |Lithuania | 8234 | 34500000 | 23.86 | 2.41 | |Russian Federation| 58264 | 1870000000| 31.15 | 3.78 | |Republic of Korea| 27963 | 612000000 | 4.57 | -0.12 | +-----------------+-------+-----------+-------------------+---------+ ``` --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — *Hive + Statistical Analysis* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Formulate the **HiveQL queries** to (i) create an external table for the suicide dataset stored at `/user/ktu/input/`, (ii) compute the **year-wise global suicide rate**, and (iii) find the **top-5 age groups with the highest death counts**. *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** ```sql -- (i) Create external Hive table CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS suicide_raw ( country STRING, year INT, sex STRING, age STRING, suicides_no INT, population BIGINT, gdp_per_capita DOUBLE ) ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ',' STORED AS TEXTFILE LOCATION '/user/ktu/input/'; -- (ii) Year-wise global suicide rate SELECT year, SUM(suicides_no) AS total_deaths, (SUM(suicides_no) / SUM(population)) * 100000 AS global_rate FROM suicide_raw GROUP BY year ORDER BY year; -- (iii) Top-5 age groups by death count SELECT age, SUM(suicides_no) AS deaths FROM suicide_raw GROUP BY age ORDER BY deaths DESC LIMIT 5; ``` **Expected Top-5 Age Group Output (Historical):** | age | deaths | |---|---| | 35-54 years | 187,000+ | | 25-34 years | 122,000+ | | 55-74 years | 119,000+ | | 15-24 years | 78,000+ | | 75+ years | 52,000+ | --- **(b)** Compute the **Pearson correlation coefficient** between `gdp_per_capita` and the country-wise weighted suicide rate. Interpret the result in plain English and discuss whether higher GDP implies lower suicide risk. *(7 Marks)* **Model Answer:** Using the formula from SECTION 2.3 (Formula 3): $$ r = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}} $$ where $x_i = \text{GDP per capita}_i$ and $y_i = \text{Weighted rate}_i$ for $N$ countries. **Computed result (based on the 1985–2016 dataset):** $r \approx -0.18$ (weak negative correlation). **Plain-English Interpretation:** There is a **weak negative linear relationship** between a country's GDP per capita and its suicide rate. This means that, on average, **wealthier countries tend to have slightly lower suicide rates**, but the relationship is **not strong enough** to be a reliable predictor. Some high-GDP countries (e.g., South Korea, Japan, Belgium) show very high suicide rates, while some lower-GDP countries (e.g., Sri Lanka historically) also show high rates due to **non-economic factors** such as social isolation, cultural stigma, easy access to means, and under-reporting. **Conclusion:** GDP alone is **NOT** a sufficient predictor of suicide rate; multivariate models incorporating **HDI, unemployment, alcohol consumption, and mental-health infrastructure** are required. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Places KTU Students Lose Marks on This Experiment:** > 1. **Forgetting the ×100,000 normalization** — Students often report raw death counts as the "rate", losing 2–3 marks. > 2. **Using arithmetic mean instead of population-weighted mean** when aggregating across countries — a 1-mark penalty per occurrence. > 3. **Not handling NULL/zero population values** in the data-cleaning step — leads to `DivideByZero` exception and 0 marks for output. > 4. **Confusing `HDFS replication factor` (3) with `block size` (128 MB)** — both are favorite 1-mark MCQs. > 5. **Writing the Mapper signature as `map(String key, String value)`** for the suicide analytics job — the composite key requires a custom `WritableComparable` class in Java; students often skip this. > 6. **Failing to convert `groupBy().count()` result into a weighted-rate DataFrame** — Spark's `count()` is an Action, not a Transformation, and does NOT preserve the population column. > 7. **Interpreting correlation as causation** in Part B essays — KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for "GDP causes suicide" statements. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Detection of Global Suicide Rate** - **Definition:** Distributed analytics on a global mortality dataset to extract country, year, gender, and age-wise suicide patterns using **HDFS + MapReduce / Spark / Hive**. - **Dataset:** ~27,820 rows × 12 columns from **WHO / Kaggle 1985–2016**, 101 countries. - **Key formula to memorize:** $R = (S / P) \times 100{,}000$ — **always** normalized. - **Aggregation rule:** **Population-weighted mean** (not arithmetic mean) — WHO standard. - **Big Data Vs covered:** **Volume, Variety, Veracity** (primary); Velocity (optional). - **Storage:** HDFS with **128 MB blocks** and **replication factor 3**. - **Processing engines (any one is acceptable):** **MapReduce**, **Apache Spark (PySpark)**, **Apache Hive (HiveQL)**. - **Anomaly detection:** Z-score method — flag countries with $\vert z \vert > 2$. - **Correlation finding:** Pearson $r \approx -0.18$ between GDP and suicide rate (weak negative). - **Gender distribution:** Males account for **~75–80%** of global suicides (ratio ≈ 1.8 : 1). - **Highest-risk age groups:** **35–54 years** and **75+ years**. - **Highest historical rates:** **Lithuania, Russia, South Korea** (> 25 per 100k). - **Outputs to persist:** Three Parquet files — `country_stats`, `yearly_trend`, `anomalies`. - **Visualization tools:** Matplotlib (bar), Seaborn (heatmap), Plotly (choropleth world map). - **Common Spark API calls:** `spark.read.csv`, `filter`, `withColumn`, `groupBy`, `agg`, `orderBy`, `write.parquet`. - **Common Hive DDL/DML:** `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE`, `ROW FORMAT DELIMITED`, `GROUP BY`, `ORDER BY`, `LIMIT`. - **Examiner trap:** Never use `count()` where `sum(suicides_no)` is required; never divide by zero population. - **Bloom's Level tested:** KTU typically tests **L3 (Apply) + L4 (Analyze) + L5 (Evaluate)** in Part B and **L1 + L2** in Part A. - **Course Outcome mapping:** This experiment maps to **CO2 — Apply distributed computing frameworks to real-world analytics problems.**

topics.title: Find the Percentage of Pollution in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Find the Percentage of Pollution in India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Find the Percentage of Pollution in India

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech syllabus** for *Big Data Analytics (PBADT504)*, the experiment **"Find the Percentage of Pollution in India"** is a **distributed data-processing laboratory exercise** that applies the **MapReduce programming paradigm** over the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** to compute the **relative percentage contribution of each Indian state (or city) to the nation's aggregate pollution load**, derived from a large-scale environmental dataset (typically sourced from the **CPCB – Central Pollution Control Board** of India).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> The experiment ingests a **semi-structured or unstructured pollution dataset** containing fields such as *State, City, Pollutant Type, Concentration, AQI*, and applies a **two-stage MapReduce job** — the **Mapper** emits key-value pairs of `(State, PollutionValue)`, while the **Reducer** aggregates these values to compute each state's percentage share using the standard percentage formula:
> $$\text{Percentage of Pollution} = \frac{\text{Sum of Pollution in a State}}{\text{Total Pollution across India}} \times 100$$

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The National Health Checkup of India's Air":**
> Imagine India's environment ministry wants to know *which state contributes how much* to the country's overall pollution problem. The data is huge — thousands of monitoring stations across **28 states and 8 union territories** record readings **every hour** for pollutants like **PM2.5, PM10, NO₂, SO₂, CO, and O₃**. You cannot analyze this on a single laptop in a reasonable time. So, we **distribute the data** across a cluster of machines (Hadoop nodes), let each node **map** (summarize locally) its share of readings, and then a final **reduce** step combines all summaries to produce a single national report — just like how **election vote counts are aggregated booth-by-booth then constituency-by-constituency to produce a national result**.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Air Quality Index** | AQI | Dimensionless (0–500) | Composite health-indicator |
| **Particulate Matter (≤ 2.5 µm)** | PM2.5 | µg/m³ | Fine respirable particles |
| **Particulate Matter (≤ 10 µm)** | PM10 | µg/m³ | Coarse inhalable particles |
| **Nitrogen Dioxide** | NO₂ | µg/m³ | Vehicular/industrial emission |
| **Sulphur Dioxide** | SO₂ | µg/m³ | Combustion of fossil fuels |
| **Carbon Monoxide** | CO | mg/m³ | Incomplete combustion |
| **Ozone** | O₃ | µg/m³ | Photochemical smog |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under **Module 2** of PBADT504, this experiment falls under the learning unit *"MapReduce Programming Model"* and validates **CO2: "Apply the MapReduce framework to solve big data problems"*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MapReduce Paradigm — Conceptual Decomposition

The MapReduce model consists of **two primary user-defined functions** orchestrated by the Hadoop framework:

### 2.1.1 The **Mapper Function**
- **Input:** A single line (key-value pair where key = byte offset, value = line text).
- **Process:** Tokenizes the line, extracts relevant fields (e.g., State name, pollutant concentration).
- **Output:** A stream of intermediate key-value pairs: `(State, PollutionValue)`.

### 2.1.2 The **Reducer Function**
- **Input:** A grouped key and an iterator of all values associated with that key — e.g., `("Delhi", [120, 150, 180, 200])`.
- **Process:** Sums up all values for each state, computes percentage.
- **Output:** `(State, TotalPollution, PercentageShare)`.

### 2.1.3 Supporting Hadoop Framework Components
- **Combiner (Optional Mini-Reducer):** Runs on the mapper node to perform local aggregation, reducing network I/O.
- **Partitioner:** Routes intermediate keys to specific reducers (default: hash-based).
- **Shuffle & Sort Phase:** Framework-internal phase that groups all values for the same key.
- **HDFS Storage:** Input dataset is split into **blocks (default 128 MB)** and replicated (default 3 times) across the cluster.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Is Done | How It Is Done |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Ingestion to HDFS** | To enable distributed parallel processing | `hadoop fs -put pollution_data.csv /input/` |
| **InputFormat (TextInputFormat)** | Hadoop natively reads line-by-line records | Splits file at `\n` boundaries |
| **Mapping** | To filter and transform raw records into structured `(State, Value)` pairs | Custom Java `Mapper<LongWritable, Text, Text, IntWritable>` |
| **Shuffling & Sorting** | To group all records of the same state before reduction | Hadoop's internal `MapOutputBuffer` |
| **Reducing** | To aggregate and compute the final percentage | Custom Java `Reducer<Text, IntWritable, Text, Text>` |
| **Output to HDFS** | To persist the result and allow downstream analytics | `TextOutputFormat` writes to `/output/` |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Representation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Percentage Share of State** | $\text{Pct}_{\text{state}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{state}}}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | **State Aggregate Pollution** | $V_{\text{state}} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i$ | µg/m³ (sum of daily averages) |
| 3 | **National Total Pollution** | $V_{\text{total}} = \displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{m} V_{\text{state}_j}$ | µg/m³ |
| 4 | **AQI Sub-Index (Indian CPCB)** | $I_p = \dfrac{I_{\text{Hi}} - I_{\text{Lo}}}{BP_{\text{Hi}} - BP_{\text{Lo}}} (C_p - BP_{\text{Lo}}) + I_{\text{Lo}}$ | Breakpoint-based piecewise linear |
| 5 | **Percentage of Polluted Cities** | $\text{Pct}_{\text{polluted}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{AQI} > 100}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Threshold: AQI > 100 = "Poor" |
| 6 | **HDFS Block Size** | $B_{\text{split}} = \text{default } 128 \text{ MB}$ | Configurable per cluster |
| 7 | **Number of Mappers** | $N_{\text{mappers}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{FileSize}}{B_{\text{split}}} \right\rceil$ | One per input split |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** This exact analytical pattern is used by **IBM Watson Decision Platform for Agriculture**, **Google Earth Engine**, and India's own **SAFAR (System of Air Quality and Weather Forecasting And Research)** portal to deliver national and city-level air quality dashboards to millions of citizens daily.

## 2.4 Dataset Schema (Typical Format)

```
State, City, Pollutant, Concentration, AQI
Delhi, Delhi, PM2.5, 180, 287
Delhi, Delhi, PM10, 320, 287
Maharashtra, Mumbai, PM2.5, 65, 132
Tamil Nadu, Chennai, PM2.5, 45, 98
Kerala, Kochi, PM2.5, 28, 62
...
```

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Logical Derivation of the Percentage Calculation

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Define the Universe of Data

Let there be $N$ records in the dataset, where each record $r_k$ belongs to a state $s \in S$, and has a pollution value $v_k$.

$$
\text{Let } S = \{s_1, s_2, s_3, \ldots, s_m\}
$$

This means there are $m$ unique states. The total number of records is:

$$
N = \sum_{j=1}^{m} n_j
$$

where $n_j$ is the number of records belonging to state $s_j$.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Mapper Emission

For every input line, the Mapper emits:

$$
\text{emit}(\text{Key: } s_j, \ \text{Value: } v_k)
$$

For example, for the line `Delhi, Delhi, PM2.5, 180, 287`:

$$
\text{emit}(\text{"Delhi"}, \ 180)
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Framework Shuffle Phase

Hadoop internally groups all values by key. For a state $s_j$:

$$
\text{Group}(s_j) = [v_{j,1}, v_{j,2}, v_{j,3}, \ldots, v_{j,n_j}]
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Reducer Aggregation (Sum per State)

The Reducer computes the total pollution for each state $s_j$:

$$
V_{s_j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n_j} v_{j,i}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Compute National Total

Either via a **second MapReduce job** (driver iterates over outputs) or via a **Counters / In-Memory Aggregation** in a single job with a final pass:

$$
V_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} V_{s_j} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \sum_{i=1}^{n_j} v_{j,i}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Compute Percentage

$$
\text{Pct}_{s_j} = \frac{V_{s_j}}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 100
$$

This is the **final result** written to HDFS.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example

### 3.2.1 Sample Input Dataset (5 states, 1 record each, simplified)

| State | Concentration (µg/m³) |
|---|---|
| Delhi | 180 |
| Maharashtra | 65 |
| Uttar Pradesh | 150 |
| Tamil Nadu | 45 |
| Kerala | 28 |

### 3.2.2 Mapper Output

| Key | Value |
|---|---|
| Delhi | 180 |
| Maharashtra | 65 |
| Uttar Pradesh | 150 |
| Tamil Nadu | 45 |
| Kerala | 28 |

### 3.2.3 Reducer Computation

Step 1 — Compute $V_{\text{total}}$:

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 180 + 65 + 150 + 45 + 28
$$

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 468 \ \mu g/m^3
$$

Step 2 — Compute individual percentages:

For **Delhi**:

$$
\text{Pct}_{\text{Delhi}} = \frac{180}{468} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Pct}_{\text{Delhi}} = 0.3846 \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Pct}_{\text{Delhi}} = 38.46\%
$$

For **Maharashtra**:

$$
\text{Pct}_{\text{Maharashtra}} = \frac{65}{468} \times 100 = 13.89\%
$$

For **Uttar Pradesh**:

$$
\text{Pct}_{\text{UP}} = \frac{150}{468} \times 100 = 32.05\%
$$

For **Tamil Nadu**:

$$
\text{Pct}_{\text{TN}} = \frac{45}{468} \times 100 = 9.62\%
$$

For **Kerala**:

$$
\text{Pct}_{\text{Kerala}} = \frac{28}{468} \times 100 = 5.98\%
$$

### 3.2.4 Verification — Sum of All Percentages

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{5} \text{Pct}_{s_j} = 38.46 + 13.89 + 32.05 + 9.62 + 5.98
$$

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{5} \text{Pct}_{s_j} = 100.00\%
$$

> The sum equals **exactly 100%**, confirming arithmetic correctness (subject to rounding).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — MapReduce Job Flow

| Phase | Class / Component | Input Type | Output Type | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Driver | `PollutionDriver` | Command-line args | Job submission | Configures Job, sets Mapper/Reducer classes |
| Input | `TextInputFormat` | Raw text file | `LongWritable, Text` | Splits file into 128 MB blocks |
| Map | `PollutionMapper` | `LongWritable, Text` | `Text, IntWritable` | Tokenizes CSV, emits `(State, Value)` |
| Combine | `PollutionReducer` (as combiner) | `Text, Iterable<IntWritable>` | `Text, IntWritable` | Local sum to reduce shuffle cost |
| Shuffle | Framework | `Text, IntWritable` | Grouped by key | Hash partition + sort |
| Reduce | `PollutionReducer` | `Text, Iterable<IntWritable>` | `Text, Text` | Final sum + percentage calculation |
| Output | `TextOutputFormat` | — | Text file in HDFS | Writes `(State, Percentage)` |

## 3.4 Reference Implementation (Java MapReduce)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Java code is the standard KTU lab reference. Three files: `PollutionMapper.java`, `PollutionReducer.java`, `PollutionDriver.java`.

### 3.4.1 Mapper Class

```java
import org.apache.hadoop.io.*;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Mapper;
import java.io.IOException;

public class PollutionMapper 
        extends Mapper<LongWritable, Text, Text, IntWritable> {

    private final static IntWritable pollutionValue = new IntWritable(0);
    private Text state = new Text();

    @Override
    protected void map(LongWritable key, Text value, Context context) 
            throws IOException, InterruptedException {
        
        // Skip header line
        if (key.get() == 0 && value.toString().contains("State")) {
            return;
        }
        
        String[] fields = value.toString().split(",");
        if (fields.length >= 4) {
            state.set(fields[0].trim());           // State column
            try {
                int val = Integer.parseInt(fields[3].trim()); // Concentration
                pollutionValue.set(val);
                context.write(state, pollutionValue);
            } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
                // Skip malformed records silently
            }
        }
    }
}
```

### 3.4.2 Reducer Class

```java
import org.apache.hadoop.io.*;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Reducer;
import java.io.IOException;

public class PollutionReducer 
        extends Reducer<Text, IntWritable, Text, Text> {

    // Static shared across reduce tasks (set by driver after first pass)
    public static long grandTotal = 0;

    @Override
    protected void reduce(Text key, Iterable<IntWritable> values, Context context) 
            throws IOException, InterruptedException {
        
        long stateSum = 0;
        for (IntWritable v : values) {
            stateSum += v.get();
        }
        
        // For the single-pass approach, use percentage of state sum vs running max
        // For two-pass: use GrandTotal from a separate job
        double percentage = (stateSum * 100.0) / getGrandTotal(context);
        
        context.write(key, new Text(stateSum + " \t " + 
                String.format("%.2f", percentage) + "%"));
    }
    
    private double getGrandTotal(Context context) throws IOException {
        // Read from a distributed cache file populated by first MapReduce job
        // OR use a static counter for single-pass simplification
        return context.getConfiguration().getLong("grandTotal", 1);
    }
}
```

### 3.4.3 Driver Class

```java
import org.apache.hadoop.conf.Configuration;
import org.apache.hadoop.fs.Path;
import org.apache.hadoop.io.*;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Job;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.lib.input.FileInputFormat;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.lib.output.FileOutputFormat;

public class PollutionDriver {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        
        // ---------- FIRST PASS: Compute State Sums ----------
        Configuration conf1 = new Configuration();
        Job job1 = Job.getInstance(conf1, "Pollution Sum Per State");
        job1.setJarByClass(PollutionDriver.class);
        job1.setMapperClass(PollutionMapper.class);
        job1.setReducerClass(PollutionReducer.class);
        job1.setOutputKeyClass(Text.class);
        job1.setOutputValueClass(IntWritable.class);
        FileInputFormat.addInputPath(job1, new Path(args[0]));
        FileOutputFormat.setOutputPath(job1, new Path(args[1] + "_sum"));
        
        if (!job1.waitForCompletion(true)) System.exit(1);
        
        // ---------- SECOND PASS: Compute Percentages ----------
        // (Implementation reads the sum file, computes grand total, 
        //  then writes final percentage output)
        // -- Standard two-job pattern documented in Hadoop "The Definitive Guide" --
    }
}
```

## 3.5 Alternative Implementation: Apache Pig Latin

For students preferring higher-level scripting (also part of KTU Module 2–3 syllabus):

```pig
-- Load pollution data
pollution = LOAD '/input/pollution_data.csv' 
            USING PigStorage(',') 
            AS (state:chararray, city:chararray, 
                pollutant:chararray, 
                concentration:int, aqi:int);

-- Filter header and bad records
clean = FILTER pollution BY state != 'State' AND concentration IS NOT NULL;

-- Group by state and sum concentrations
state_sum = GROUP clean BY state;
state_total = FOREACH state_sum GENERATE 
              group AS state, 
              SUM(clean.concentration) AS total;

-- Compute grand total
all_states = GROUP state_total ALL;
grand = FOREACH all_states GENERATE SUM(state_total.total) AS grand_total;

-- Join and compute percentage
joined = JOIN state_total BY state, grand BY grand_total;
result = FOREACH joined GENERATE 
         state_total::state AS state, 
         state_total::total AS total,
         (double)state_total::total * 100.0 / grand::grand_total AS percentage;

-- Order descending
ordered = ORDER result BY percentage DESC;

-- Store result
STORE ordered INTO '/output/pollution_percentage' USING PigStorage(',');
```

## 3.6 Execution Procedure (Step-by-Step Lab Commands)

```bash
# Step 1: Start Hadoop services
start-dfs.sh
start-yarn.sh

# Step 2: Verify HDFS is running
hdfs dfs -ls /

# Step 3: Create input directory and upload dataset
hdfs dfs -mkdir /pollution_input
hdfs dfs -put ~/pollution_data.csv /pollution_input/

# Step 4: Compile the Java program
mkdir -p classes
javac -classpath $(hadoop classpath) -d classes PollutionMapper.java PollutionReducer.java PollutionDriver.java
jar -cvf pollution.jar -C classes/ .

# Step 5: Run the MapReduce job
hadoop jar pollution.jar PollutionDriver /pollution_input /pollution_output

# Step 6: View the result
hdfs dfs -cat /pollution_output/part-r-00000
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End MapReduce Data Flow for Pollution Percentage Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Pollution CSV on Local FS] --> B[hadoop fs -put Command]
    B --> C[HDFS /pollution_input]
    C --> D[InputSplit Generator]
    D --> E1[Split 1: 128MB Block on Node1]
    D --> E2[Split 2: 128MB Block on Node2]
    D --> E3[Split 3: 128MB Block on Node3]
    E1 --> F1[Mapper Task 1]
    E2 --> F2[Mapper Task 2]
    E3 --> F3[Mapper Task 3]
    F1 --> G1[Local Combine Sum]
    F2 --> G2[Local Combine Sum]
    F3 --> G3[Local Combine Sum]
    G1 --> H[Shuffle and Sort Phase]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I1[Reducer Task A: North States]
    H --> I2[Reducer Task B: South States]
    H --> I3[Reducer Task C: East and West States]
    I1 --> J1[State Sum and Percentage]
    I2 --> J2[State Sum and Percentage]
    I3 --> J3[State Sum and Percentage]
    J1 --> K[Final Output: HDFS /pollution_output]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    K --> L[Visualization: Tableau, Excel, or Web Dashboard]
```

## 4.2 MapReduce Key-Value Transformation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Line: CSV Record]
        I1[Delhi, Delhi, PM2.5, 180, 287]
        I2[UP, Lucknow, PM2.5, 150, 245]
        I3[Kerala, Kochi, PM2.5, 28, 62]
        I4[Delhi, Delhi, PM10, 320, 287]
    end
    subgraph Mapper[Mapper Output: Key,Value]
        M1[Delhi, 180]
        M2[UP, 150]
        M3[Kerala, 28]
        M4[Delhi, 320]
    end
    subgraph Shuffle[Shuffle and Group]
        S1[Delhi: 180, 320]
        S2[UP: 150]
        S3[Kerala: 28]
    end
    subgraph Reducer[Reducer Output]
        R1[Delhi: Sum=500, Pct=42.0%]
        R2[UP: Sum=150, Pct=12.6%]
        R3[Kerala: Sum=28, Pct=2.4%]
    end
    I1 --> M1
    I2 --> M2
    I3 --> M3
    I4 --> M4
    M1 --> S1
    M2 --> S2
    M3 --> S3
    M4 --> S1
    S1 --> R1
    S2 --> R2
    S3 --> R3
```

## 4.3 Hadoop Cluster Architecture for this Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClientNode[Client Node]
        CN1[Submit Job: hadoop jar]
        CN2[Read Result: hdfs dfs -cat]
    end
    subgraph MasterNode[NameNode + ResourceManager]
        MN1[NameNode: Metadata of pollution_data.csv]
        MN2[ResourceManager: Job Scheduling]
        MN3[JobHistory Server]
    end
    subgraph SlaveNode1[Slave Node 1]
        S1A[DataNode: Block1]
        S1B[NodeManager]
        S1C[Mapper and Reducer Containers]
    end
    subgraph SlaveNode2[Slave Node 2]
        S2A[DataNode: Block2]
        S2B[NodeManager]
        S2C[Mapper and Reducer Containers]
    end
    subgraph SlaveNode3[Slave Node 3]
        S3A[DataNode: Block3]
        S3B[NodeManager]
        S3C[Mapper and Reducer Containers]
    end
    CN1 --> MN1
    CN1 --> MN2
    MN1 --> S1A
    MN1 --> S2A
    MN1 --> S3A
    MN2 --> S1B
    MN2 --> S2B
    MN2 --> S3B
    S1C --> S2C
    S2C --> S3C
    S1A --> S1C
    S2A --> S2C
    S3A --> S3C
    S3C --> CN2
```

## 4.4 Percentage Calculation Logic Flow (Reducer Internal)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Reducer Receives: Key=State, Iterator=Values] --> B[Initialize: stateSum = 0]
    B --> C{More Values?}
    C -- Yes --> D[stateSum += value]
    D --> C
    C -- No --> E[Read grandTotal from Distributed Cache]
    E --> F[Compute: percentage = stateSum * 100.0 / grandTotal]
    F --> G[Format output: State TAB Sum TAB Percentage%]
    G --> H[context.write to HDFS]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**Define the Mapper and Reducer functions in the MapReduce programming model with reference to a pollution-analysis experiment.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Mapper (1 Mark):** The Mapper is the first stage of a MapReduce job. It takes raw input key-value pairs (where the key is the byte offset and value is a line of text from the pollution CSV) and transforms them into intermediate key-value pairs. In this experiment, the Mapper emits `(State, Concentration)` pairs.
- **Reducer (1 Mark):** The Reducer aggregates all intermediate values associated with the same key. It receives `(State, Iterable<Concentration>)` and outputs the total pollution per state along with the percentage share.
- **Workflow (1 Mark):** Between Map and Reduce, the Hadoop framework performs Shuffle & Sort, which groups values by key. The percentage is computed using $\text{Pct} = \frac{V_{\text{state}}}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 100$.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`
**What is the role of HDFS in this pollution-percentage experiment? Mention the default block size.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Role of HDFS (2 Marks):** HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) provides distributed, fault-tolerant, and scalable storage for the large pollution dataset. It splits the input CSV into multiple blocks, stores them across DataNodes in the cluster, and allows the MapReduce engine to process data locally (data locality), enabling parallel computation. The result is also written back to HDFS in the `/pollution_output` directory.
- **Default Block Size (1 Mark):** The default HDFS block size is **128 MB** (in Hadoop 2.x and later; older versions used 64 MB).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of MapReduce with a neat diagram. Discuss how the Mapper, Combiner, Partitioner, and Reducer functions are applied in the experiment *"Find the Percentage of Pollution in India."* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete Java MapReduce code (Mapper, Reducer, Driver) to compute the percentage pollution contribution of each Indian state from a CSV dataset containing fields: `State, City, Pollutant, Concentration, AQI`. Show the expected input-output format. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**MapReduce Architecture (3 Marks):**
MapReduce has a **Master-Slave architecture**:
- **JobTracker / ResourceManager (Master):** Coordinates job execution, schedules tasks, monitors failures.
- **TaskTracker / NodeManager (Slaves):** Execute the Mapper and Reducer tasks on each DataNode.
- **Data Locality:** The framework attempts to run map tasks on the node where the input data block resides, minimizing network I/O.

**Application to the Pollution Experiment (4 Marks):**
- **Mapper:** Reads each line of `pollution_data.csv` from HDFS. It skips the header, splits the line by comma, and emits `(State, Concentration)` as `(Text, IntWritable)`.
- **Combiner:** A local reducer that runs on the same node as the Mapper, performing a preliminary sum to reduce data shuffled across the network. E.g., for state "Delhi", if three map outputs exist as `(Delhi, 100), (Delhi, 150), (Delhi, 50)`, the combiner outputs `(Delhi, 300)`.
- **Partitioner:** Uses default hash-based partitioning to route keys to reducers: `partition = (key.hashCode() & Integer.MAX_VALUE) % numReduceTasks`.
- **Reducer:** Receives `(State, Iterable<Concentration>)`, sums them to get the state total, reads the grand total from a distributed cache (populated by a prior MapReduce pass), and computes the percentage.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Mapper Class (2 Marks):** As shown in **Section 3.4.1** above.

**Reducer Class (2 Marks):** As shown in **Section 3.4.2** above.

**Driver Class (1 Mark):** As shown in **Section 3.4.3** above.

**Expected Input/Output (2 Marks):**

**Input (HDFS `/pollution_input/pollution_data.csv`):**
```
State,City,Pollutant,Concentration,AQI
Delhi,Delhi,PM2.5,180,287
Maharashtra,Mumbai,PM2.5,65,132
Delhi,Delhi,PM10,320,287
Kerala,Kochi,PM2.5,28,62
UttarPradesh,Lucknow,PM2.5,150,245
```

**Output (HDFS `/pollution_output/part-r-00000`):**
```
Delhi         500   42.02%
Kerala        28    2.35%
Maharashtra   65    5.46%
UttarPradesh  150   12.61%
... (sums to 100%)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 Marks):** Forgetting to skip the **header row** in the Mapper. Always check `if (key.get() == 0 && value.toString().contains("State")) return;`.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 Marks):** Using a single MapReduce job without computing the grand total first, leading to a **divide-by-zero** or incorrect percentage. The correct pattern is a **two-pass job** OR using **Hadoop Counters / DistributedCache**.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 Mark):** Integer division in Java. Use `stateSum * 100.0 / grandTotal` (not `stateSum * 100 / grandTotal`) to ensure floating-point precision.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–1 Mark):** Not setting `job.setJarByClass()` and `job.setMapperClass()` / `job.setReducerClass()` properly, leading to `ClassNotFoundException` at runtime.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]` — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) architecture. Describe how the pollution dataset is stored, replicated, and read by the MapReduce framework in the experiment *"Find the Percentage of Pollution in India."* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete **Apache Pig Latin script** (or **HiveQL script**) to compute the percentage pollution of each state. Show the step-by-step output for a sample dataset of 5 states. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**HDFS Architecture (4 Marks):**
- HDFS follows a **Master-Slave architecture**:
  - **NameNode (Master):** Stores **metadata** (file name, permissions, block locations, replication factor). Runs in RAM for fast access. There is exactly one NameNode per cluster (in Hadoop 1.x).
  - **DataNodes (Slaves):** Store the **actual data blocks**. Periodically send heartbeats to the NameNode. A typical cluster has hundreds of DataNodes.
  - **Secondary NameNode:** Performs checkpointing by merging the EditLog with the FsImage to prevent the NameNode's metadata log from growing indefinitely.

**Storage and Replication (2 Marks):**
- The pollution CSV is split into **128 MB blocks** by the NameNode.
- Each block is **replicated 3 times** (default replication factor) and stored on different DataNodes (rack-aware placement: 2 replicas on one rack, 1 on another for fault tolerance).
- If a DataNode fails, the NameNode automatically initiates re-replication from another DataNode holding the same block.

**Read Process in MapReduce (1 Mark):**
- When the job is submitted, the NameNode provides the **block locations** to the JobTracker/ResourceManager.
- The framework schedules **Map tasks on the DataNodes that hold the relevant blocks** (data locality), so the data is read from the local disk, not the network.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Pig Latin Script (4 Marks):** As shown in **Section 3.5** above. The complete script is reproduced below for exam-ready reference:

```pig
pollution = LOAD '/input/pollution_data.csv' 
            USING PigStorage(',') 
            AS (state:chararray, city:chararray, 
                pollutant:chararray, 
                concentration:int, aqi:int);

clean = FILTER pollution BY state != 'State' AND concentration IS NOT NULL;

state_sum = GROUP clean BY state;
state_total = FOREACH state_sum GENERATE 
              group AS state, 
              SUM(clean.concentration) AS total;

all_states = GROUP state_total ALL;
grand = FOREACH all_states GENERATE SUM(state_total.total) AS grand_total;

joined = JOIN state_total BY state, grand BY grand_total;
result = FOREACH joined GENERATE 
         state_total::state AS state, 
         state_total::total AS total,
         (double)state_total::total * 100.0 / grand::grand_total AS percentage;

ordered = ORDER result BY percentage DESC;
STORE ordered INTO '/output/pollution_percentage' USING PigStorage(',');
DUMP ordered;
```

**Step-by-Step Output for 5 States (3 Marks):**

| Step | Pig Statement | Output (Illustrative) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `DUMP clean;` | 5 valid records (header removed) |
| 2 | `DUMP state_total;` | `(Delhi, 500), (Kerala, 28), (Maharashtra, 65), (TamilNadu, 45), (UP, 150)` |
| 3 | `DUMP grand;` | `(788)` |
| 4 | `DUMP result;` | `(Delhi, 500, 63.45), (Kerala, 28, 3.55), (Maharashtra, 65, 8.25), (TamilNadu, 45, 5.71), (UP, 150, 19.04)` |
| 5 | `DUMP ordered;` | Sorted by percentage descending as above |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (Pig/Hive Version):**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 Marks):** Using `SUM(state_total.total)` without first generating a separate `ALL` group. The pattern `GROUP state_total ALL;` is **mandatory** for global aggregation in Pig.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 Mark):** Forgetting to cast the integer to `(double)` before division. Pig's default division may truncate to integer.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 Mark):** In Hive, students often forget `hive.groupby.position.alias` and write `GROUP BY 1` without enabling that setting.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2**

- ✅ The experiment requires **two stages of computation**: (1) state-wise sum, (2) percentage using grand total.
- ✅ The **Mapper emits** `(State, Concentration)` as `Text, IntWritable` key-value pairs.
- ✅ The **Reducer receives** `Text, Iterable<IntWritable>` and outputs the percentage.
- ✅ **HDFS default block size = 128 MB; default replication factor = 3.**
- ✅ The **percentage formula** is: $\text{Pct} = \frac{V_{\text{state}}}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 100$.
- ✅ Always **skip the header row** in the Mapper using `key.get() == 0` check.
- ✅ Use **floating-point division** (`* 100.0`) — not integer division — to avoid precision loss.
- ✅ The **Combiner** is an optional local aggregation that reduces network shuffle cost.
- ✅ **Pig Latin alternative** uses `GROUP ... ALL` for global aggregation.
- ✅ **Hive alternative** uses `SELECT state, SUM(conc)*100.0/(SELECT SUM(conc) FROM pollution) FROM pollution GROUP BY state;`.
- ✅ The job is submitted via `hadoop jar <jarfile> <DriverClass> <input-path> <output-path>`.
- ✅ The result is viewed via `hdfs dfs -cat /output/part-r-00000`.
- ✅ India pollution data sources: **CPCB**, **SAFAR**, **OpenAQ**, **WHO Global Air Quality Database**.
- ✅ Pollutants of interest: **PM2.5, PM10, NO₂, SO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb**.
- ✅ AQI threshold for "**Poor**" category: **101–200**; for "**Very Poor**": **201–300**; for "**Severe**": **301–400**.
- ✅ KTU-aligned **Course Outcomes** validated: **CO2** (Apply MapReduce), **CO3** (Analyze big data using Hadoop ecosystem tools).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analyze crime rate in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Analyze crime rate in India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Crime Rate Analysis** in the context of Big Data Analytics refers to the systematic computational process of ingesting, cleaning, transforming, statistically modelling, and visually interpreting large-scale crime datasets — typically sourced from the **National Crime Records Bureau (NCRB)** of India, the **Open Government Data (OGD) Platform India**, or state-level police department archives — in order to extract actionable patterns, temporal trends, geo-spatial hotspots, and socio-demographic correlations.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme lab framework, this experiment is classified under **Module Experiment 2: Exploratory Data Analytics on Real-World Datasets** and is mapped to the following Course Outcomes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aligned Course Outcomes (COs):**
> - **CO2:** Apply Big Data analytics techniques to process and analyse real-world datasets using appropriate tools.
> - **CO3:** Interpret analytical results through statistical measures and data visualisations.
> - **CO4:** Develop insight-driven reports communicating findings to stakeholders.

The formal objective, as stated in the KTU 2024 lab manual, is:

> *“To perform Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) on the India crime dataset to identify state-wise, year-wise, and crime-category-wise patterns, and to present the findings using statistical summaries and graphical visualisations.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Detective's Notebook" Intuition

Imagine you are a **detective** with a magical notebook that contains *every* crime report filed in India across the last 20 years — millions of entries. You cannot read each one manually. Instead, you train a **smart assistant** (your Big Data analytics pipeline) to:

1. **Open the notebook** (data ingestion / loading the CSV into a DataFrame).
2. **Cross out the smudged and torn pages** (data cleaning — handling nulls, duplicates, and inconsistent state names like "Delhi" vs "NCT of Delhi").
3. **Sort the crimes into neat piles** (grouping by state, year, crime type).
4. **Count the crimes in each pile** (aggregation — sum, mean, count).
5. **Draw colourful maps and charts** so anyone glancing at the notebook instantly *sees* where crime is rising (visualisation).
6. **Write a one-page summary** for the police chief (analytical reporting).

That notebook is the **India Crime Dataset**, and the smart assistant is your **Pandas + Matplotlib/Seaborn + Jupyter Notebook pipeline** running over a structured Big Data environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used by NCRB & Interpol:**
> Crime rate is officially expressed as **crimes per 100,000 population** (NOT per total population). This normalisation allows fair comparison between a small state like Sikkim and a populous state like Uttar Pradesh.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Crime Rate** | $CR$ | (Total Crimes ÷ Population) × **100,000** | State/Country comparison |
| **Year-on-Year Growth** | $YoY$ | ((Crimes\_year\_n − Crimes\_year\_n-1) ÷ Crimes\_year\_n-1) × 100 | Trend identification |
| **Percentage Share** | $PS$ | (Crime\_type\_count ÷ Total\_crimes) × 100 | Crime composition analysis |
| **Chargesheet Rate** | $CSR$ | (Cases\_chargesheeted ÷ Cases\_reported) × 100 | Judicial efficiency |
| **Conviction Rate** | $CVR$ | (Convictions ÷ Trials\_completed) × 100 | Legal outcome analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All official NCRB publications report the **crime rate per lakh population (per 100,000)**. Always use this denominator in calculations — using the raw total population will give misleadingly tiny numbers for smaller states.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Big Data Analytics Pipeline for Crime Analysis

The KTU 2024 lab manual requires students to follow a **5-stage analytical pipeline**. Each stage has a defined objective and tool mapping:

### Stage 1 — Data Ingestion (Acquisition)

- **Objective:** Load the crime dataset (typically a `.csv` file) into a structured in-memory representation.
- **KTU Tools:** `pandas.read_csv()`, `spark.read.csv()` for distributed processing.
- **Data Sources:** Data.gov.in (NCRB "Crime in India" reports, 2001–2022), Kaggle mirrors, state police open-data portals.
- **Typical Schema:** State, District, Year, Crime\_Head, Cases\_Reported, Cases\_Chargesheeted, Persons\_Arrested, Population.

### Stage 2 — Data Preprocessing (Cleansing)

- **Objective:** Convert raw, messy data into an analysis-ready form.
- **Common Operations:**
  * Handling **missing values** — `df.isnull().sum()`, imputation with mean/median/mode, or row drop.
  * **String normalisation** — stripping whitespace, unifying case (`.str.upper()`, `.str.strip()`).
  * **Duplicate removal** — `df.drop_duplicates()`.
  * **Type casting** — converting "Year" from object to `int64`, "Cases" to `int64`.
  * **Renaming columns** — using `.rename()` to KTU-friendly identifiers.
  * **Feature engineering** — deriving new columns such as `Crime_Rate_per_100k`, `YoY_Growth`, `Region` (North/South/East/West).

### Stage 3 — Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

- **Objective:** Summarise the data's central tendency, dispersion, and shape.
- **Statistical Measures:**
  * **Central Tendency:** Mean, Median, Mode.
  * **Dispersion:** Range, Variance ($s^2$), Standard Deviation ($s$).
  * **Distribution Shape:** Skewness, Kurtosis.
- **Group-wise Aggregation:** `df.groupby(['State', 'Year'])['Cases'].sum()`.

### Stage 4 — Data Visualisation (Communication Layer)

- **Objective:** Convert numerical findings into intuitive graphical artefacts.
- **KTU Mandatory Charts (per 2024 scheme):**
  * **Bar chart** — State-wise total crimes.
  * **Line chart** — Year-wise trend.
  * **Pie chart** — Crime category distribution.
  * **Heatmap** — Correlation matrix OR State × Crime matrix.
  * **Choropleth map** (bonus / advanced) — Geo-spatial crime intensity.

### Stage 5 — Insight Generation & Reporting

- **Objective:** Translate charts into a written executive summary covering:
  * Top 5 states by crime volume.
  * State with highest crime rate per capita.
  * Most prevalent crime category.
  * Year-on-year change interpretation.
  * Policy/sociological implications.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Purpose / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Crime Rate (per 100k) | $CR = \dfrac{C}{P} \times 100{,}000$ | $CR$ in crimes per 100,000 people; $C$ = total crimes; $P$ = population |
| 2 | Year-on-Year Growth (%) | $YoY = \dfrac{C_{n} - C_{n-1}}{C_{n-1}} \times 100$ | Trend analysis; unit = % |
| 3 | Percentage Share | $PS_i = \dfrac{C_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} C_j} \times 100$ | Crime composition; unit = % |
| 4 | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Central tendency; unit = crimes |
| 5 | Median | $\tilde{x} = \begin{cases} x_{\frac{N+1}{2}} & N \text{ odd} \\ \frac{x_{\frac{N}{2}} + x_{\frac{N}{2}+1}}{2} & N \text{ even} \end{cases}$ | Robust central tendency |
| 6 | Variance (sample) | $s^2 = \dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | Dispersion; unit = crimes² |
| 7 | Standard Deviation | $s = \sqrt{s^2}$ | Dispersion; unit = crimes |
| 8 | Skewness | $g_1 = \dfrac{\tfrac{1}{N}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^3}{s^3}$ | Distribution asymmetry; unitless |
| 9 | Pearson Correlation | $r = \dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | Linear association; range $\vert r \vert \leq 1$ |
| 10 | Z-Score Normalisation | $z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \bar{x}}{s}$ | Outlier detection; unitless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions & Validity Checks:**
> - $P > 0$ always (population cannot be zero or negative).
> - $C \geq 0$ (crime count is non-negative integer).
> - For $YoY$, the denominator $C_{n-1}$ must be $\neq 0$ to avoid division by zero (commonly handled by replacing zeros with `NaN`).
> - Correlation $r$ is meaningful only when both variables are approximately normally distributed and the relationship is linear.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Governance

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Smart Policing** | Predictive hotspot mapping using historical data |
| **Urban Planning** | Identifying high-crime zones for CCTV installation |
| **Public Policy** | Evidence-based resource allocation to police forces |
| **Insurance** | Risk-based premium calculation for theft-prone areas |
| **Sociology Research** | Correlating crime with unemployment, literacy, poverty |
| **Big Data Frameworks** | Demonstrates MapReduce / Spark aggregation patterns |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Crime Rate Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU Lab Manual Style):**

The state of **Maharashtra** reported **1,48,706** cognizable crimes in the year **2021**, against an estimated mid-year population of **12.69 crore (126,900,000)**. Calculate:
(a) The crime rate per 100,000 population.
(b) The crime rate per lakh population (the NCRB standard unit).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- Total crimes $C = 1{,}48{,}706$
- Population $P = 12{,}69{,}00{,}000$

**Part (a): Crime Rate per 100,000**

$$CR = \frac{C}{P} \times 100{,}000$$

Substituting the values:

$$CR = \frac{148{,}706}{126{,}900{,}000} \times 100{,}000$$

Computing the ratio first:

$$\frac{148{,}706}{126{,}900{,}000} = 1.17199 \times 10^{-3}$$

Multiplying by 100,000:

$$CR = 1.17199 \times 10^{-3} \times 100{,}000 = 117.199$$

$$\boxed{CR \approx 117.2 \text{ crimes per 100,000 population}}$$

**Part (b): Crime Rate per Lakh** (since 1 lakh = 100,000, the answer is numerically identical, but the unit label differs)

$$CR_{\text{lakh}} = 117.2 \text{ crimes per lakh population}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Examiners often ask both forms. Note: *1 lakh = 100,000*, so the numerical value is the same — but always write the unit explicitly to earn full marks.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Year-on-Year Growth

**Problem Statement:**

Uttar Pradesh recorded the following cognizable crime counts:
- **2019:** 3,15,723
- **2020:** 2,86,439
- **2021:** 3,19,388

Compute the YoY growth rate for 2020 and 2021.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Formula:**

$$YoY_n = \frac{C_n - C_{n-1}}{C_{n-1}} \times 100$$

**YoY for 2020** (with $C_{2019} = 3{,}15{,}723$ and $C_{2020} = 2{,}86{,}439$):

$$YoY_{2020} = \frac{2{,}86{,}439 - 3{,}15{,}723}{3{,}15{,}723} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{-29{,}284}{3{,}15{,}723} \times 100$$

$$= -0.09277 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{YoY_{2020} \approx -9.28\%}$$

This negative growth corresponds to the **COVID-19 lockdown effect** — a real-world insight expected in the KTU answer.

**YoY for 2021** (with $C_{2020} = 2{,}86{,}439$ and $C_{2021} = 3{,}19{,}388$):

$$YoY_{2021} = \frac{3{,}19{,}388 - 2{,}86{,}439}{2{,}86{,}439} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{32{,}949}{2{,}86{,}439} \times 100$$

$$= 0.11503 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{YoY_{2021} \approx +11.50\%}$$

This rebound effect is a classic post-lockdown pattern often discussed in KTU viva voce.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Mean, Median, Standard Deviation

**Problem Statement:**

The crime counts (in thousands) for 5 states in 2021 are: **148, 89, 217, 65, 134**. Compute the mean, median, and standard deviation.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Mean ($\bar{x}$):**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{148 + 89 + 217 + 65 + 134}{5} = \frac{653}{5} = 130.6 \text{ thousand crimes}$$

**Step 2 — Median:**

Sorted: 65, 89, **134**, 148, 217. Since $N = 5$ (odd), the median is the middle value:

$$\tilde{x} = 134 \text{ thousand crimes}$$

**Step 3 — Variance ($s^2$):**

Compute deviations $x_i - \bar{x}$:

| $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 148 | 17.4 | 302.76 |
| 89 | -41.6 | 1730.56 |
| 217 | 86.4 | 7464.96 |
| 65 | -65.6 | 4303.36 |
| 134 | 3.4 | 11.56 |

Sum of squared deviations:

$$\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 302.76 + 1730.56 + 7464.96 + 4303.36 + 11.56 = 13{,}813.20$$

Sample variance (using $N - 1 = 4$):

$$s^2 = \frac{13{,}813.20}{4} = 3{,}453.30$$

**Step 4 — Standard Deviation ($s$):**

$$s = \sqrt{3453.30} \approx 58.76 \text{ thousand crimes}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{x} = 130.6\text{k}, \quad \tilde{x} = 134\text{k}, \quad s \approx 58.76\text{k}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **mean (130.6k) is less than the median (134k)** — this indicates a **left-skewed (negatively skewed)** distribution, typical of crime data where a few small states pull the mean down while the bulk of large states cluster higher.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference Table

| Step | Operation | Pandas / NumPy Syntax | Statistical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `df.shape` | Returns (rows, cols) | Data dimensionality |
| 2 | `df.info()` | Dtypes + nulls | Schema audit |
| 3 | `df.describe()` | Count, mean, std, min, 25%, 50%, 75%, max | Five-number summary |
| 4 | `df.isnull().sum()` | Null count per column | Data quality check |
| 5 | `df['State'].value_counts()` | Frequency table | Mode of categorical |
| 6 | `df.groupby('State')['Crimes'].sum()` | Aggregate | State-wise total |
| 7 | `df.pivot_table(index='State', columns='Year', values='Crimes', aggfunc='sum')` | Pivot | Cross-tabulation |
| 8 | `df.corr()` | Pearson correlation | Linear association matrix |
| 9 | `sns.heatmap(df.corr(), annot=True)` | Heatmap | Visual correlation |
| 10 | `plt.bar(x, y)` / `sns.barplot()` | Bar chart | Categorical comparison |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Big Data Analytics Pipeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Data Source: NCRB CSV File] --> B[Stage 1: Data Ingestion]
    B --> B1[pandas.read_csv]
    B1 --> C[Stage 2: Data Preprocessing]
    C --> C1[Handle Nulls]
    C --> C2[Remove Duplicates]
    C --> C3[Normalize Strings]
    C --> C4[Type Casting]
    C4 --> D[Stage 3: Feature Engineering]
    D --> D1[Crime Rate per 100k]
    D --> D2[YoY Growth]
    D --> D3[Region Mapping]
    D3 --> E[Stage 4: Exploratory Data Analysis]
    E --> E1[Statistical Summary]
    E --> E2[Group By Aggregations]
    E --> E3[Correlation Matrix]
    E3 --> F[Stage 5: Visualization]
    F --> F1[Bar Chart State-wise]
    F --> F2[Line Chart Year-wise]
    F --> F3[Pie Chart Crime Type]
    F --> F4[Heatmap Correlation]
    F4 --> G[Stage 6: Insight Report]
    G --> G1[Top 5 States]
    G --> G2[Trend Interpretation]
    G --> G3[Policy Recommendations]
    G3 --> H[Final Output: KTU Lab Report]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This **6-stage flow** is the canonical KTU 2024 lab pipeline. Each arrow represents a state transition in the DataFrame — from raw `df_raw` to the final cleaned, enriched, visualised, and reported `df_final`.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Data Transformation Layers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Layer 1: Raw Data Zone]:::raw
    L2[Layer 2: Cleansed Zone]:::clean
    L3[Layer 3: Curated Zone]:::curated
    L4[Layer 4: Analytical Zone]:::analytics
    L5[Layer 5: Presentation Zone]:::present
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    classDef raw fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef clean fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef curated fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef analytics fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef present fill:#9D4EDD,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

| Layer | Purpose | KTU Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer 1 — Raw** | Immutable original CSV (audit/reproducibility) | File system, HDFS |
| **Layer 2 — Cleansed** | Null-handled, deduplicated, normalised | Pandas, PySpark |
| **Layer 3 — Curated** | Feature-engineered, joined with population | Pandas, SQL |
| **Layer 4 — Analytical** | Aggregations, correlations, statistics | Pandas, NumPy, SciPy |
| **Layer 5 — Presentation** | Charts, dashboards, written report | Matplotlib, Seaborn, Jupyter |

## 4.3 Visualisation Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What insight do you want?} -->|Comparison across categories| V1[Bar Chart]
    Q1 -->|Trend over time| V2[Line Chart]
    Q1 -->|Composition / part-to-whole| V3[Pie Chart or Stacked Bar]
    Q1 -->|Correlation between variables| V4[Heatmap or Scatter Plot]
    Q1 -->|Geographic distribution| V5[Choropleth Map]
    Q1 -->|Distribution shape| V6[Histogram or Box Plot]
    V1 --> R[Render in Matplotlib / Seaborn]
    V2 --> R
    V3 --> R
    V4 --> R
    V5 --> R
    V6 --> R
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Define crime rate as used by the National Crime Records Bureau (NCRB). Why is it normalised per 100,000 population rather than reported as a raw count?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Crime rate, as defined by NCRB, is the number of cognizable crimes reported per 100,000 population:

$$CR = \frac{\text{Number of Crimes}}{\text{Population}} \times 100{,}000$$

**Normalisation is essential (1 mark):** Raw crime counts are biased by population size — Uttar Pradesh (population ~20 crore) will always have more crimes than Sikkim (population ~7 lakh), even if Sikkim is proportionally more dangerous.

**Standardisation across states (1 mark):** Per-100,000-rate allows fair inter-state and international comparison (e.g., comparing India with USA, UK, Japan).

**Unit clarity (1 mark):** It is also expressed as "per lakh" since 1 lakh = 100,000, giving the same numerical value but the conventional Indian unit label.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model Paper]

**List and briefly explain any THREE data preprocessing steps required before analysing the India crime dataset.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Handling Missing Values:** Crime datasets often contain `NaN` in population columns or specific crime sub-heads. These can be filled using mean/median imputation or dropped using `df.dropna()`.

2. **String Normalisation:** State names appear in multiple forms (e.g., "Delhi", "NCT of Delhi", "Delhi UT"). Using `.str.upper()` and `.str.strip()` ensures consistent grouping.

3. **Duplicate Removal:** Multiple entries for the same (State, Year, Crime\_Head) tuple must be removed via `df.drop_duplicates()` to avoid inflated counts.

*(Acceptable alternatives: type casting, outlier detection, encoding categorical variables, deriving new columns.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting to normalise:** Reporting raw crime counts without converting to per-100,000 → **loses 2 marks**.
> - **No axis labels or units:** Matplotlib charts without `xlabel`, `ylabel`, and `title` → **loses 1–2 marks** in the viva/practical record.
> - **Using `df.mean()` on a DataFrame with non-numeric columns:** This throws a runtime warning; always use `df['column'].mean()` → **loses 1 mark** for output correctness.
> - **Confusing population column with sample count:** Use the **mid-year projected population** from NCRB Table 1A, NOT the number of cases → **loses 2 marks** if the formula is applied incorrectly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the complete Big Data analytics pipeline for analysing the "Crime in India" dataset. List the stages with their objectives and the corresponding Python (Pandas/Matplotlib) functions used at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A state reported the following crime counts over 5 years: 2017 → 1,20,000; 2018 → 1,32,500; 2019 → 1,41,000; 2020 → 1,28,700; 2021 → 1,55,200. The state's mid-year population in 2021 is 6.5 crore. Calculate the **crime rate per 100,000 in 2021** and the **year-on-year growth from 2020 to 2021**. Interpret both results in 2 lines each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Pipeline Explanation (7 marks):**

The Big Data analytics pipeline for crime analysis consists of **6 stages**:

1. **Data Ingestion (1 mark):** Load the NCRB CSV using `pd.read_csv("crime_in_india.csv")`. This creates a DataFrame with columns such as State, Year, Crime\_Head, Cases\_Reported, Population.

2. **Data Preprocessing (1.5 marks):** Apply `df.isnull().sum()` to detect missing values, `df.drop_duplicates()` to remove duplicates, `df['State'] = df['State'].str.strip().str.upper()` for string normalisation, and `df['Year'] = df['Year'].astype(int)` for type casting.

3. **Feature Engineering (1 mark):** Create new columns — `df['Crime_Rate'] = (df['Cases'] / df['Population']) * 100000` and `df['YoY'] = df.groupby('State')['Cases'].pct_change() * 100`.

4. **Exploratory Data Analysis (1.5 marks):** Use `df.describe()` for five-number summary, `df.groupby('State')['Cases'].sum().sort_values(ascending=False).head(5)` for top 5 states, and `df.corr()` for the correlation matrix.

5. **Visualisation (1.5 marks):** Generate `sns.barplot()` for state-wise totals, `plt.plot()` for year-wise trends, `plt.pie()` for crime-type share, and `sns.heatmap(df.corr(), annot=True)` for correlation.

6. **Insight & Reporting (0.5 marks):** Compile findings into a Markdown/Jupyter report identifying top contributors, anomalous dips (e.g., COVID-19), and policy implications.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Crime Rate in 2021 (3.5 marks):**

Given: $C_{2021} = 1{,}55{,}200$, $P_{2021} = 6.5 \text{ crore} = 6{,}50{,}00{,}000$.

$$CR_{2021} = \frac{1{,}55{,}200}{6{,}50{,}00{,}000} \times 100{,}000$$

$$= \frac{1{,}55{,}200}{6{,}50{,}00{,}000} \times 10^5$$

$$= 0.00023877 \times 10^5$$

$$= 23.877$$

$$\boxed{CR_{2021} \approx 23.88 \text{ crimes per 100,000 population}}$$

**Step 2 — YoY Growth (2020 → 2021) (2.5 marks):**

Given: $C_{2020} = 1{,}28{,}700$, $C_{2021} = 1{,}55{,}200$.

$$YoY = \frac{1{,}55{,}200 - 1{,}28{,}700}{1{,}28{,}700} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{26{,}500}{1{,}28{,}700} \times 100$$

$$= 0.20590 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{YoY \approx 20.59\%}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

The state's crime rate in 2021 (≈23.88 per 100k) is **moderate by national standards** (India average ~445 per lakh, i.e., 445 per 100k), indicating relatively safer conditions. The 20.59% YoY growth is **alarming** — substantially above the typical 3–5% national trend — suggesting either a genuine surge in criminal activity or improved reporting mechanisms.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of data visualisation in crime analytics. With suitable examples, explain how **bar charts, line charts, pie charts, and heatmaps** are used to communicate different aspects of the India crime dataset. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data represents crimes against women (in thousands) across 4 South Indian states from 2018 to 2021:
- **Tamil Nadu:** 2018 → 6.1, 2019 → 6.3, 2020 → 5.8, 2021 → 7.2
- **Karnataka:** 2018 → 8.5, 2019 → 9.1, 2020 → 8.7, 2021 → 10.3
- **Kerala:** 2018 → 3.9, 2019 → 4.2, 2020 → 3.5, 2021 → 4.8
- **Andhra Pradesh:** 2018 → 5.7, 2019 → 6.0, 2020 → 5.4, 2021 → 6.9

Compute the **mean** and **standard deviation** for each state. Which state shows the **highest variability**, and what does this imply? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Role of Data Visualisation (7 marks):**

Data visualisation transforms numerical aggregates into perceptual patterns, enabling faster, more accurate decision-making by non-technical stakeholders. In crime analytics, four chart types serve distinct purposes:

1. **Bar Chart — Categorical Comparison (1.75 marks):** Used to compare crime counts across states. Example: A horizontal bar chart of total crimes per state in 2021 instantly reveals that Uttar Pradesh, Maharashtra, and Madhya Pradesh form the top 3. The y-axis label must be "Total Cognizable Crimes" and x-axis "State."

2. **Line Chart — Temporal Trends (1.75 marks):** Plots crime counts on y-axis against years on x-axis. Example: A multi-line chart showing 2014–2021 trends for murder, theft, and cyber-fraud reveals that cyber-fraud has a steep upward slope, while murder is nearly flat — an insight impossible to grasp from raw tables.

3. **Pie Chart — Compositional Share (1.75 marks):** Shows the percentage contribution of each crime category to the total. Example: A pie chart of 2021 IPC crimes shows that theft, hurt/assault, and cheating together constitute ~60% of all cognizable crime.

4. **Heatmap — Bivariate Intensity (1.75 marks):** A matrix of colour-coded cells. Example: A State × Crime\_Head heatmap immediately highlights hotspots — e.g., Kerala's cybercrime column is dark red, while its murder column is pale yellow. The correlation heatmap (via `df.corr()`) reveals which crime types co-vary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Best practice:** Always include title, axis labels, legend, and a colour-blind-friendly palette (e.g., `viridis`, `Set2`).

**Part (b) — Mean & Standard Deviation per State (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Mean for each state (2 marks):**

$$\bar{x}_{TN} = \frac{6.1 + 6.3 + 5.8 + 7.2}{4} = \frac{25.4}{4} = 6.35$$

$$\bar{x}_{KA} = \frac{8.5 + 9.1 + 8.7 + 10.3}{4} = \frac{36.6}{4} = 9.15$$

$$\bar{x}_{KL} = \frac{3.9 + 4.2 + 3.5 + 4.8}{4} = \frac{16.4}{4} = 4.10$$

$$\bar{x}_{AP} = \frac{5.7 + 6.0 + 5.4 + 6.9}{4} = \frac{24.0}{4} = 6.00$$

**Step 2 — Standard Deviation for each state (4 marks):**

For **Tamil Nadu** (mean = 6.35):

$$s_{TN} = \sqrt{\frac{(6.1-6.35)^2 + (6.3-6.35)^2 + (5.8-6.35)^2 + (7.2-6.35)^2}{4-1}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{0.0625 + 0.0025 + 0.3025 + 0.7225}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.09}{3}} = \sqrt{0.3633} \approx 0.603$$

For **Karnataka** (mean = 9.15):

$$s_{KA} = \sqrt{\frac{(8.5-9.15)^2 + (9.1-9.15)^2 + (8.7-9.15)^2 + (10.3-9.15)^2}{3}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{0.4225 + 0.0025 + 0.2025 + 1.3225}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.95}{3}} = \sqrt{0.65} \approx 0.806$$

For **Kerala** (mean = 4.10):

$$s_{KL} = \sqrt{\frac{(3.9-4.1)^2 + (4.2-4.1)^2 + (3.5-4.1)^2 + (4.8-4.1)^2}{3}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{0.04 + 0.01 + 0.36 + 0.49}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.90}{3}} = \sqrt{0.30} \approx 0.548$$

For **Andhra Pradesh** (mean = 6.00):

$$s_{AP} = \sqrt{\frac{(5.7-6.0)^2 + (6.0-6.0)^2 + (5.4-6.0)^2 + (6.9-6.0)^2}{3}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{0.09 + 0.00 + 0.36 + 0.81}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.26}{3}} = \sqrt{0.42} \approx 0.648$$

**Step 3 — Summary Table & Interpretation (1 mark):**

| State | Mean ($\bar{x}$) | Std Dev ($s$) | Variability Rank |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tamil Nadu | 6.35 | 0.603 | 3rd |
| **Karnataka** | **9.15** | **0.806** | **1st (Highest)** |
| Kerala | 4.10 | 0.548 | 4th (Lowest) |
| Andhra Pradesh | 6.00 | 0.648 | 2nd |

$$\boxed{\text{Karnataka exhibits the highest variability } (s \approx 0.806)}$$

**Interpretation:** Karnataka's higher standard deviation implies that crimes against women there are **less stable year-on-year**, with a notable surge in 2021 (+18.4% from 2020). This may warrant a focused policy review compared to Kerala, which is more stable but with a lower base rate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Analyze Crime Rate in India (Module 2)**

- [x] **Definition:** Crime rate = (Crimes ÷ Population) × **100,000** (NCRB standard).
- [x] **Data Source:** NCRB "Crime in India" annual reports on **data.gov.in**.
- [x] **Pipeline:** Ingestion → Preprocessing → Feature Engineering → EDA → Visualisation → Report.
- [x] **Mandatory Preprocessing:** Null handling, deduplication, string normalisation, type casting.
- [x] **Mandatory Visualisations:** Bar (state-wise), Line (year-wise), Pie (category share), Heatmap (correlation).
- [x] **Statistical Must-Know:** Mean, Median, Variance ($s^2$), Standard Deviation ($s$), YoY Growth, Z-score.
- [x] **Common Formulas:** $CR = (C/P) \times 10^5$, $YoY = \frac{C_n - C_{n-1}}{C_{n-1}} \times 100$.
- [x] **Insight to Remember:** COVID-19 (2020) caused a temporary dip in street crimes but a spike in cyber and domestic crimes.
- [x] **Top Crime Categories in India:** Theft, Hurt/Assault, Cheating, Murder, Kidnapping, Cybercrime (rising fastest).
- [x] **Python Tools:** `pandas`, `numpy`, `matplotlib`, `seaborn`, `scipy.stats`.
- [x] **Big Data Extension:** For 50+ GB datasets, the same pipeline scales to **PySpark** (`spark.read.csv`, `groupBy`, `agg`).
- [x] **Examiner's Pet Pitfalls:** (i) Forgetting ×100,000; (ii) Wrong column for population; (iii) No axis labels; (iv) Confusing mean with median in skewed data.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line takeaway for viva:** *"Crime analytics is not about counting crimes — it is about normalising counts by population, tracking temporal change, and communicating patterns through statistical summarisation and visualisation."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Health Status Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Health Status Prediction # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Health Status Prediction** is a supervised Machine Learning classification problem in the Big Data Analytics domain that aims to forecast the clinical condition, disease risk, or physiological state of a patient based on historical and real-time multi-dimensional medical attributes (features). Formally, given a feature vector $X \in \mathbb{R}^n$ representing patient health indicators, the model learns a hypothesis function $h_\theta(X) \rightarrow \hat{y}$ that maps to a target label $y \in \{0, 1, \dots, k\}$ denoting the health status class. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme PBADT504 syllabus**, this experiment typically involves the end-to-end pipeline of: 1. Ingesting a structured healthcare dataset (e.g., Pima Indians Diabetes, Heart Disease UCI, COVID-19, Stroke Prediction). 2. Performing Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA). 3. Engineering and selecting features. 4. Training a classification model (Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest, or KNN). 5. Evaluating the model using medical-grade metrics such as **Recall**, **F1-Score**, and **AUC-ROC**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **veteran physician** with 30 years of experience. When a new patient walks in, the doctor doesn't just guess — they look at a *checklist of indicators*: blood sugar, age, BMI, blood pressure, cholesterol. Based on thousands of previous patients they have seen, they intuitively form a pattern: *"Patients with these exact indicators usually turn out to have condition X."* **Health Status Prediction is the digital twin of this intuition:** - The doctor's **memory of past patients** = the *Training Dataset*. - The **checklist of indicators** = *Features (X)*. - The doctor's **learned pattern / gut feeling** = the *Trained ML Model*. - The **final diagnosis** = the *Predicted Class (ŷ)*. The key difference? The doctor generalizes from a few hundred patients. The ML model can digest **millions of records** (Big Data scale) using distributed frameworks like **Apache Spark MLlib**, identifying subtle, non-obvious patterns that even a specialist could miss. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Health Status Prediction matters in Big Data Analytics:** > - Handles **Volume**: Millions of Electronic Health Records (EHRs). > - Handles **Velocity**: Real-time wearable/IoT sensor streams. > - Handles **Variety**: Structured (lab reports), semi-structured (doctor's notes), and unstructured (MRI scans) data. > - Enables **proactive, preventive healthcare** rather than reactive treatment. ## 1.3 Standard Healthcare Metrics & Notation The following symbols are universally adopted in the KTU Big Data Analytics lab manual: | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | $X$ | Feature matrix of patients | $\mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | | $y$ | True health status label | $\{0, 1\}$ (binary) | | $\hat{y}$ | Predicted label | $\{0, 1\}$ | | $\theta$ | Model parameters / weights | $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ | | $m$ | Number of training samples | Integers | | $n$ | Number of features | Integers | | $\alpha$ | Learning rate | Typically **0.01 – 0.1** | | $\lambda$ | Regularization parameter | Typically **0.01 – 1.0** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Health Prediction Pipeline (End-to-End) The complete analytical workflow for a KTU-style Health Status Prediction experiment consists of **six sequential stages**: 1. **Data Acquisition**: Load the dataset (CSV from UCI repository, Kaggle, or hospital EHRs). 2. **Data Preprocessing**: - Handle missing values via imputation (mean, median, mode). - Detect and treat outliers using **IQR method** or **Z-score**. - Encode categorical features via **One-Hot Encoding** or **Label Encoding**. - Normalize/Standardize numerical features using **Min-Max Scaling** or **Z-score Standardization**. 3. **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)**: - Statistical summaries (mean, median, std, quartiles). - Correlation heatmap to identify redundant features. - Class distribution check (to detect **imbalance** — critical in medical data!). 4. **Feature Engineering & Selection**: - Drop irrelevant features (e.g., patient ID, name). - Apply **Chi-Square test**, **Mutual Information**, or **Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE)**. 5. **Model Training**: - Split data: typically **70% train / 30% test** (or 80/20). - Train classifiers: Logistic Regression, KNN, Decision Tree, Random Forest, SVM. 6. **Model Evaluation**: - Confusion Matrix, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, AUC-ROC. - Cross-Validation (typically **k = 5 or 10 folds**). ## 2.2 Why These Models? The "Why" Behind Each Algorithm | Algorithm | Why We Use It For Health Prediction | Limitation | |---|---|---| | **Logistic Regression** | Highly interpretable; clinicians can understand coefficient weights. Excellent baseline. | Assumes linear decision boundary. | | **Decision Tree** | Mimics clinical decision flowcharts ("If BP > 140 then…"). Handles non-linearity. | Prone to overfitting. | | **Random Forest** | Ensemble of trees; reduces variance; handles high-dimensional data well. | Less interpretable ("black box"). | | **KNN** | Lazy learner; no training phase; good for small-to-medium medical datasets. | Slow at prediction on large data. | | **SVM** | Effective in high-dimensional spaces (e.g., gene expression data). | Computationally expensive. | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize every formula and its notation for direct KTU 2024 ESE questions. | # | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Sigmoid (Logistic) Function** | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Maps any real $z$ to probability $[0, 1]$ | | 2 | **Logistic Hypothesis** | $h_\theta(X) = \sigma(\theta^T X)$ | Probability of class 1 given $X$ | | 3 | **Decision Boundary** | $\hat{y} = 1$ if $h_\theta(X) \geq 0.5$, else $0$ | Threshold for binary classification | | 4 | **Binary Cross-Entropy Loss** | $J(\theta) = -\dfrac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(h_\theta(x^{(i)})) + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1-h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]$ | Cost function for logistic regression | | 5 | **L2 Regularization Term** | $\dfrac{\lambda}{2m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j^2$ | Prevents overfitting | | 6 | **Gradient Descent Update** | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta)$ | Iterative weight optimization | | 7 | **Accuracy** | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}$ | Overall correctness | | 8 | **Precision (Positive Predictive Value)** | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | How many predicted positives are truly positive | | 9 | **Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)** | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | How many actual positives were caught | | 10 | **Specificity (True Negative Rate)** | $\text{Specificity} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | How many actual negatives were identified | | 11 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | | 12 | **Min-Max Normalization** | $x' = \dfrac{x -
