<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)

The **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)** is the in-semester assessment mechanism mandated by **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University** under the **NEP 2020 aligned 2024 Scheme** for evaluating laboratory and project-based courses such as **PBADT504 - Big Data Analytics**. For this course, the **CIE carries a total weightage of 60 marks**, which is evaluated entirely by the faculty handling the lab, while the remaining **40 marks** are awarded by an external examiner during the **End Semester Examination (ESE)**.

The official KTU 2024 Scheme document states that CIE is **"not limited to above topics"** — meaning the assessment pattern described in the syllabus is a *minimum framework*; institutions and faculty may extend, refine, or add components (viva, surprise tests, mini-projects, peer reviews) to comprehensively gauge the **Course Outcomes (COs)** and **Programme Outcomes (POs)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The Cricket IPL Auction**
> Think of CIE 60 marks like an **IPL franchise's season-long player evaluation system**:
> - The franchise doesn't judge a player ONLY in the final match (ESE = 40 marks).
> - Throughout the tournament (the semester), coaches track: net practice (lab attendance), match-day performance (experiments), strategic thinking (viva), and consistency (record submission).
> - The aggregate of all these observations forms the CIE score.

> **In essence:** CIE is a **continuous, holistic, multi-touchpoint** evaluation — NOT a single high-stakes test. It rewards **consistency, documentation, and conceptual clarity** over last-minute cramming.

## 1.3 Key KTU Terminology

| Term | Full Form | KTU Definition |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| **CIE** | Continuous Internal Evaluation | 60 marks internal assessment by course faculty |
| **ESE** | End Semester Examination | 40 marks external exam at semester end |
| **CO** | Course Outcome | Specific skills/knowledge the course aims to impart |
| **PO** | Programme Outcome | Graduate attributes per NBA/Washington Accord |
| **PSO** | Programme Specific Outcome | Specialization-specific outcomes |
| **Bloom's Level** | Revised Bloom's Taxonomy | Cognitive depth (Remember → Create) |
| **Rubric** | Scoring Guideline | Detailed marking criteria for each component |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU 2024 CIE Weights for Lab Courses (PBADT504 type):**
> - **Total CIE**: **60 marks** (must secure ≥ 50% i.e., **30/60** to be eligible for ESE)
> - **Pass Requirement**: CIE + ESE combined ≥ **50%** (typically 50/100)
> - **Minimum ESE Marks**: **20/40** (mandatory, otherwise course is failed even if CIE is high)
> - **Lab Sessions/Week**: As per the scheme (typically 2 hours × 2 sessions/week)
> - **Number of Experiments**: **Minimum 8–10 experiments** across all modules
> - **Record Submission Frequency**: Continuous, reviewed periodically

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the 60-Mark CIE

The 60 marks are **not a single score** — they are a *composite index* derived from multiple sub-components. While KTU allows institutional flexibility, the **standard recommended distribution** for a Big Data Analytics lab course follows this pattern:

### Component 1: Internal Lab Test (End of Module)

| Sub-Component | Marks | Frequency | Evaluator |
|---------------|-------|-----------|-----------|
| **Module-wise Internal Tests** | **20 marks** (≈ 2 tests × 10 marks) | End of each module | Course Faculty |
| **Final Internal Lab Exam** | Often clubbed with internal tests | Last week | Course Faculty |

### Component 2: Continuous Daily Assessment (Day-to-Day Lab Work)

| Sub-Component | Marks | Description |
|---------------|-------|-------------|
| **Experiment Execution** | **10 marks** | Quality of coding, output verification, hands-on skill |
| **Viva Voce (Per Experiment)** | **10 marks** | Oral questioning on concept, algorithm, troubleshooting |
| **Observation/Record Notebook** | **10 marks** | Neatness, completeness, correctness of written work |
| **Attendance & Discipline** | **05 marks** | Mandatory ≥ 75% attendance for ESE eligibility |
| **Mini-Project / Open-Ended Experiment** | **05 marks** | Beyond-syllabus innovation, optional bonus |
| **TOTAL CIE** | **60 marks** | (Subject to institutional variation) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The phrase **"Not limited to above topics"** in the KTU syllabus means the faculty MAY add or modify sub-components. However, the **total must remain 60**, and the **CO–PO mapping** must remain traceable.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value notation (NOT the vertical pipe `|` which breaks markdown tables).

| Formula / Rule | Expression | Purpose / Application |
|----------------|------------|----------------------|
| **CIE Total** | $\text{CIE}_{\text{Total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$ | Weighted sum of $n$ sub-components with weights $w_i$ and scores $s_i$ |
| **CIE Eligibility** | $\text{CIE}_{\text{Secured}} \geq 0.5 \times 60 = 30$ | Minimum CIE required to appear for ESE |
| **Combined Pass Rule** | $\text{CIE} + \text{ESE} \geq 50 \text{ and } \text{ESE} \geq 20$ | Mandatory dual condition for course clearance |
| **Attendance Penalty** | $\text{Att}_{\%} = \dfrac{\text{Present Days}}{\text{Total Days}} \times 100$ | If Att% < 75%, detention from ESE |
| **CO Attainment (Direct)** | $\text{CO}_{\text{Attainment}} = \dfrac{\text{Students scoring} \geq \text{Target \%}}{\text{Total Students}} \times 100$ | Used for NBA accreditation reporting |
| **PO/PSO Mapping** | $\text{PO}_j = \sum_{k} \alpha_{jk} \cdot \text{CO}_k$ | Linear combination of COs mapped to PO $j$ with correlation $\alpha_{jk}$ |
| **Normalized CIE (Class Average)** | $\overline{\text{CIE}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{CIE}_i$ | Average of all $N$ students' CIE marks |
| **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\text{CIE}_i - \overline{\text{CIE}})^2}$ | Measures assessment spread / fairness index |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why is CIE structured this way?**
> - **Industry Parallel**: Tech companies use **continuous performance reviews** (quarterly + project-based), not just one annual test. CIE mimics this.
> - **Skill Verification**: Big Data Analytics requires **Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL, Python/R** — these are best assessed through **hands-on execution**, not theory.
> - **NEP 2020 Compliance**: The National Education Policy 2020 mandates **continuous, holistic, multidisciplinary assessment**, replacing the one-shot exam model.
> - **NBA Accreditation**: CIE data feeds the **Course Outcome (CO) Attainment** calculations required for **National Board of Accreditation**.

## 2.4 Operational Logic — The "Why" Behind Each Step

1. **Why 60 marks for CIE, not 50 or 70?**
   → Lab skills take time to develop; 60% internal weight gives faculty enough signal to judge *process* not just *product*.

2. **Why split CIE into sub-components?**
   → A student strong in coding but weak in viva (or vice versa) gets **granular feedback** instead of a single opaque score.

3. **Why "Not limited to above topics"?**
   → Big Data is a **fast-evolving domain**. Faculty must be free to add new tools (Kafka, Airflow, Snowflake) without rewriting the syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CIE Weight Distribution Logic

The KTU 2024 framework demands that **CIE must be a valid, reliable, and fair assessment tool**. Let us derive *why* the standard 60-mark split follows the pattern: **(20 + 10 + 10 + 10 + 5 + 5)**.

### Step 1: Define the Assessment Dimensions

For a Big Data Analytics lab, we need to measure **4 distinct cognitive/psychomotor dimensions** (per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy + Dave's Psychomotor):

$$
D = \{D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4\}
$$

where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= \text{Cognitive Knowledge (Concepts, Theory)} \\
D_2 &= \text{Application Skill (Coding, Execution)} \\
D_3 &= \text{Documentation (Record, Reports)} \\
D_4 &= \text{Communication & Discipline (Viva, Attendance)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Assign Normalized Weights

KTU recommends the following weight ratios (these are *standard best-practice* ratios, derivable from Bloom's emphasis on application for lab courses):

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(D_1) &= 0.33 \quad \text{(Tests assess knowledge + analysis)} \\
w(D_2) &= 0.17 \quad \text{(Day-to-day execution)} \\
w(D_3) &= 0.17 \quad \text{(Record quality)} \\
w(D_4) &= 0.17 \quad \text{(Viva + Attendance combined)} \\
w(\text{Bonus}) &= 0.08 \quad \text{(Mini-project / innovation)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Convert Ratios to Marks (Out of 60)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Marks for } D_1 &= 0.33 \times 60 = 20 \text{ marks} \\
\text{Marks for } D_2 &= 0.17 \times 60 \approx 10 \text{ marks} \\
\text{Marks for } D_3 &= 0.17 \times 60 \approx 10 \text{ marks} \\
\text{Marks for } D_4 &= 0.17 \times 60 \approx 10 \text{ marks} \\
\text{Marks for Bonus} &= 0.08 \times 60 \approx 05 \text{ marks} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= 20 + 10 + 10 + 10 + 5 = 55 \\
\text{Adjust to } 60 &\Rightarrow \text{Reallocate } 5 \text{ marks among core components} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Most institutions reallocate the 5 marks to enhance **internal test weight** (from 20 → 25) or split between **viva and execution**. The final distribution is **institution-specific but the total is fixed at 60**.

### Step 4: Bloom's Level Mapping (Per Question in Internal Test)

For each 10-mark internal test, the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** (Anderson & Krathwohl, 2001) is mapped as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{L1 (Remember)} &: 1 \text{ question} \times 2 \text{ marks} = 2 \text{ marks} \\
\text{L2 (Understand)} &: 1 \text{ question} \times 2 \text{ marks} = 2 \text{ marks} \\
\text{L3 (Apply)} &: 1 \text{ question} \times 3 \text{ marks} = 3 \text{ marks} \\
\text{L4 (Analyze)} &: 1 \text{ question} \times 3 \text{ marks} = 3 \text{ marks} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total = 10 marks** per internal test, covering all four cognitive levels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this distribution?**
> The 40% weight on **L1–L2 (Remember + Understand)** ensures conceptual foundation, while the 60% on **L3–L4 (Apply + Analyze)** pushes students to **higher-order thinking** — exactly what a Big Data Analytics course demands.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Score Maximization Strategy

| CIE Sub-Component | Weight (Typical) | Where Students Lose Marks | How to Maximize |
|------------------|------------------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| **Internal Tests** | 20 marks | Skipping lab → weak theory; mug-up answers | Revise previous experiment's concepts before each test |
| **Lab Execution** | 10 marks | Incomplete code, runtime errors not resolved | Practice each experiment **before** the lab session |
| **Viva Voce** | 10 marks | Cannot explain "why" a step was done | Maintain a **concept diary** alongside code |
| **Record Submission** | 10 marks | Missing date, index, neatness, hand-written diagrams | Submit **on time**; use proper index page |
| **Attendance** | 5 marks | Bunking → below 75% → detention | Maintain ≥ 90% attendance buffer |
| **Mini-Project** | 5 marks | Skipped due to laziness | Always attempt — easy 4–5 marks |

> [!TIP]
> **The "60/40 Strategy"**: Aim for **55/60 in CIE** (very achievable) and **30/40 in ESE** = total 85/100 = a **Distinction-grade performance**. This is *much easier* than chasing 50/40 in ESE alone.

## 3.3 CO–PO Mapping for CIE Components

The KTU 2024 scheme requires every CIE question to be mapped to **at least one CO and one PO**. The standard mapping for PBADT504 is:

| CIE Component | CO Mapped | PO Mapped (e.g.) |
|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| Internal Test (Theory) | CO1, CO2, CO3 | PO1 (Engineering Knowledge) |
| Lab Execution | CO1, CO2, CO3, CO4 | PO5 (Modern Tool Usage) |
| Viva Voce | CO1–CO5 | PO10 (Communication) |
| Record Writing | CO1–CO4 | PO9 (Individual & Team Work) |
| Mini-Project | CO5, CO6 | PO2 (Problem Analysis), PO12 (Life-long Learning) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
> Define the term **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)** as per the KTU 2024 Scheme. State the total marks allocated to CIE for the lab course PBADT504.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)** is the in-semester assessment procedure prescribed by **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU)** under the **2024 Scheme** aligned with **NEP 2020**, in which the **course faculty continuously evaluates a student** through multiple assessment touchpoints — including internal tests, viva voce, lab execution, record submission, and attendance — rather than relying solely on a single end-semester exam. For the laboratory course **PBADT504 - Big Data Analytics**, the CIE carries a total of **60 marks**, while the remaining **40 marks** are awarded in the **End Semester Examination (ESE)** by an external examiner. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Sample Paper]**
> List any **four components** that constitute the 60-mark CIE for a lab course in the KTU 2024 Scheme. What is the minimum CIE score required to be eligible for the ESE?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The four main components of the 60-mark CIE for PBADT504 are:
> 1. **Internal Tests / Module Tests** (typically 20 marks)
> 2. **Daily Lab Execution / Performance** (typically 10 marks)
> 3. **Viva Voce** (typically 10 marks)
> 4. **Record / Observation Notebook + Attendance** (typically 10 + 5 marks)
>
> A student must score a **minimum of 30 out of 60 marks (i.e., 50%)** in CIE to be eligible to appear for the ESE. Additionally, the student must have **at least 75% attendance** in the lab sessions. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> KTU 2024 scheme Part B (14 marks) provides internal choice. The student answers **either Question A OR Question B**.

### **Question A (14 marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]*

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **structure of the 60-mark CIE** for the lab course PBADT504 as per the KTU 2024 Scheme. Mention the weightage, evaluator, and CO mapping for each component. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Why does the KTU 2024 syllabus state that the CIE components are **"not limited to the above topics"**? Justify with reference to the evolving nature of Big Data Analytics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Structure of 60-mark CIE (7 marks):**

The KTU 2024 Scheme CIE for PBADT504 (Big Data Analytics lab) is divided into **two major clusters**:

**Cluster 1 — Internal Assessment (35 marks):**
- **Module Test 1** (10 marks): Evaluates CO1, CO2 — Bloom's Levels L1 to L3.
- **Module Test 2** (10 marks): Evaluates CO3, CO4 — Bloom's Levels L1 to L3.
- **Final Internal Lab Exam** (15 marks): Comprehensive; tests CO1 to CO5, Bloom's L1 to L4.

**Cluster 2 — Continuous Lab Work (20 marks):**
- **Daily Lab Execution** (10 marks): Evaluated by course faculty; assesses **hands-on skill, code correctness, output verification**. Maps to **PO5 (Modern Tool Usage)**.
- **Viva Voce** (10 marks): Oral questioning on each experiment; maps to **PO10 (Communication)**.

**Cluster 3 — Discretionary (5 marks):**
- **Mini-Project / Innovation Task** (5 marks): Open-ended; maps to **PO12 (Life-long Learning)**.

> **A neat block diagram (as shown in Section 4.1)** must accompany this answer. The diagram should clearly show the **60-mark total**, the three clusters, and the sub-components with their marks.

**(b) Why "not limited to above topics" — Justification (7 marks):**

The phrase **"not limited to above topics"** is a deliberate design choice in the KTU 2024 syllabus. It allows institutions and faculty to:

1. **Incorporate emerging tools**: Big Data is a rapidly evolving domain. New tools like **Apache Kafka, Apache Airflow, Snowflake, Databricks, dbt** emerge frequently. The syllabus cannot enumerate them all.

2. **Encourage industry alignment**: Faculty can invite **industry mentors**, evaluate students on **real-world datasets from Kaggle / government open data portals**, or use **live cloud platforms (AWS EMR, GCP Dataproc)**.

3. **Support NEP 2020 flexibility**: The National Education Policy 2020 mandates **multidisciplinary, flexible, and skill-based learning**. A rigid CIE would defeat this purpose.

4. **Enable holistic CO attainment**: Faculty may add components like **peer review, group discussions, seminar presentations, or hackathons** to better measure **PO9 (Individual & Team Work)** and **PO12 (Life-long Learning)**.

5. **Adapt to local industry needs**: A college in Kerala's IT corridor (Technopark, Infopark) may emphasize **Spark + Scala**, while a college near a manufacturing hub may emphasize **IoT + Hadoop**. The CIE should reflect this.

**Hence, the phrase is not a loophole — it is a deliberate empowerment mechanism for the institution to remain current and relevant.**

---

### **Question B (14 marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Sample Paper]*

> **(a)** Define the **Course Outcome (CO)** attainment process. Derive the formula for **direct CO attainment** using CIE marks. Show with a numerical example how a class of 50 students would have CO3 evaluated as "Attained" or "Not Attained". **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **role of attendance** in the CIE. What is the minimum attendance required? What happens if a student has attendance between 65% and 75%? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CO Attainment Process and Formula (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Course Outcome (CO) attainment is the **measurement of the extent to which students have achieved the defined learning outcomes** of a course. As per NBA / KTU norms, it is computed through:
- **Direct Methods** (CIE marks, ESE marks, viva responses) — weight 80%.
- **Indirect Methods** (course exit survey, alumni feedback) — weight 20%.

**Formula Derivation (3 marks):**

$$
\text{CO}_i^{\text{Attainment}} = \frac{N_{\text{students scoring} \geq \text{Target}}}{N_{\text{Total students}}} \times 100
$$

**Numerical Example (2 marks):**

Let **N = 50 students**. For CO3, the faculty sets a **target threshold = 60% of max CIE marks** allocated to CO3. Suppose CIE total = 60, and CO3 is tested in 15 marks (via internal test + viva). The threshold is:

$$
\text{Target} = 60\% \times 15 = 9 \text{ marks}
$$

Suppose **40 students score ≥ 9/15 in CO3-mapped questions**.

$$
\text{CO}_3^{\text{Attainment}} = \frac{40}{50} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

**KTU Attainment Levels:**
- **≥ 70%** → **Attained (Level 3)**
- **60–69%** → **Partially Attained (Level 2)**
- **50–59%** → **Minimally Attained (Level 1)**
- **< 50%** → **Not Attained (Level 0)**

In our example, **80% > 70% → CO3 is "Attained"** with high confidence. ✅

---

**(b) Role of Attendance in CIE (7 marks):**

**Minimum Attendance (2 marks):**
- The **minimum attendance required** to be eligible for the ESE is **75%** of the total lab sessions conducted.
- If attendance falls below 75%, the student is **detained** and cannot appear for the ESE, regardless of CIE marks.

**Calculation (2 marks):** If the semester has **40 lab sessions**, the student must attend at least:

$$
\text{Required Classes} = 0.75 \times 40 = 30 \text{ classes}
$$

**Scenario: Attendance between 65% and 75% (3 marks):**
- The student is **NOT eligible** for the ESE.
- The student may be allowed to **attend the next semester's lab** (carry over) OR may be given a **condonation opportunity** if the institution permits (with valid reason: medical, family emergency).
- Some institutions may allow a **medical condonation** of up to 5% (i.e., attendance can be 70% with a medical certificate).
- During this period, the **CIE marks already earned are carried forward** and added to the next attempt.

> **Practical tip**: Students should maintain a **safety buffer of 85–90% attendance** to absorb unforeseen absences.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where Students Lose Marks in CIE-related Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CIE and ESE weightage**: Some students write "CIE = 40 marks" — this is WRONG. **CIE = 60, ESE = 40** for lab courses in KTU 2024.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the dual pass condition**: Even with full CIE (60/60), if ESE < 20/40, the student FAILS. Always mention BOTH conditions.
>
> 3. **Not drawing the block diagram**: In Question A(a) above, the 7-mark question specifically asks for a **"neat block diagram"**. A textual answer without a diagram loses 2–3 marks easily.
>
> 4. **Mis-stating the CO attainment threshold**: Students often write "60% is attained" — actually, **70% is the standard "Attained" threshold** (Level 3). 60% is only "Partially Attained" (Level 2).
>
> 5. **Confusing "not limited to above topics"**: Students misinterpret this as "CIE is optional" or "any marks can be given". The phrase means **the CIE components are flexible, NOT the total marks (60) or the pass criteria**.
>
> 6. **Skipping the CO/PO mapping**: KTU examiners explicitly look for **CO–PO mapping** in your answers. Always state which CO a question maps to.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]

### 🎯 Core Facts
- [x] **CIE Total = 60 marks** | **ESE Total = 40 marks** | **Grand Total = 100 marks**
- [x] **Minimum CIE for ESE eligibility = 30/60 (50%)**
- [x] **Minimum ESE marks to pass = 20/40**
- [x] **Minimum attendance = 75%** (buffer to 85%+ recommended)
- [x] **Combined pass = CIE + ESE ≥ 50 AND ESE ≥ 20**

### 📊 Standard CIE Component Split
- [x] Internal Tests: **20–25 marks**
- [x] Daily Lab Execution: **~10 marks**
- [x] Viva Voce: **~10 marks**
- [x] Record + Attendance: **~10–15 marks**
- [x] Mini-Project / Innovation: **~5 marks** (bonus / discretionary)

### 🧠 Bloom's Levels for CIE Tests
- [x] L1 Remember → MCQ / one-liner
- [x] L2 Understand → short answer
- [x] L3 Apply → code snippet / hands-on
- [x] L4 Analyze → debug / compare

### 🏆 Topper Strategy (60/60 in CIE)
- [x] **Pre-prepare** each experiment before the lab session
- [x] **Maintain a concept diary** for viva
- [x] **Submit records on time** with proper index + neat diagrams
- [x] **Attend every lab** (90%+ attendance = safe zone)
- [x] **Attempt the mini-project** — easy 4–5 marks
- [x] **Revise previous experiments** before each internal test

### ⚖️ KTU Compliance Checklist
- [x] Every CIE question **maps to a CO**
- [x] Every CO **maps to a PO/PSO**
- [x] CO attainment reported **every semester** for NBA
- [x] "Not limited to above topics" = **flexibility, not optionality**

### 📝 Exam-Ready Definitions
- **CIE** = Continuous Internal Evaluation (60 marks, internal faculty)
- **ESE** = End Semester Examination (40 marks, external examiner)
- **CO Attainment** = % of students crossing the target score threshold
- **Attainment Levels** = Level 0 (<50%), Level 1 (50–59%), Level 2 (60–69%), Level 3 (≥70%)

> **Final Tip**: Treat CIE as a **marathon, not a sprint**. Students who consistently perform in every lab session (not just before the test) easily cross **55+/60 marks** and significantly reduce ESE pressure. This is the **single biggest advantage** of the KTU 2024 scheme — *use it!*

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

In the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** under **NEP 2020 alignment**, the assessment of a **Lab / Project Integrated Course** (such as the Module Experiment series in PBADT504 – Big Data Analytics) is conducted through a **two-tier evaluation framework**: **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)** and **End Semester Examination (ESE)**.

The complete marks distribution for a Module Experiment is fixed at **100 marks**, partitioned as:

$$\text{Total Marks} = \text{CIE} + \text{ESE} = 60 + 40 = 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) — 60 Marks Total**
> CIE is the **ongoing, in-semester assessment** conducted by the **faculty member handling the course** (not an external examiner). It measures the student's **continuous learning progress, attendance discipline, project/lab work quality, and in-semester test performance**.

The 60 marks of CIE are split into four sub-components:

| **CIE Component** | **Marks** | **Nature of Assessment** |
|---|---|---|
| Attendance | **5** | Regularity in lab/project sessions |
| Project / Lab Record Evaluation | **30** | Quality of code, viva, report, innovation |
| Internal Examination – 1 | **12.5** | First in-semester written/practical test |
| Internal Examination – 2 | **12.5** | Second in-semester written/practical test |
| **Total CIE** | **60** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **End Semester Examination (ESE) — 40 Marks**
> The ESE is the **final, university-conducted examination** held at the end of the semester. It is typically evaluated by an **external examiner appointed by KTU** (or a combination of internal + external for lab courses). It tests the student's **holistic understanding, practical execution ability, and presentation skills** for the entire module.

The ESE 40 marks are split into two parts:

- **Part A** — Short-answer / conceptual questions (carries the foundational recall marks).
- **Part B** — Long-answer / design / coding / case-study questions (carries the analytical and application marks).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of CIE and ESE like a **driving license test** in India:

> **Analogy — The Driving Test Model**
> - **CIE = Your daily driving lessons and instructor's logbook.** Your driving school (the college/faculty) tracks every day: *Did you attend class?* (5 marks), *Did you practice well?* (30 marks project/lab), *How did you perform in mock tests?* (12.5 + 12.5 = 25 marks internal exams). The instructor gives you a **continuous score out of 60** based on your day-to-day progress.
> - **ESE = The final RTO driving test.** An **external officer** (university-appointed examiner) takes you out for a final 40-mark road test. You can't bribe the logbook, and the final examiner doesn't see your daily practice — they just see **how you perform on test day**.

**Why two tiers?**
- A student who only memorizes the night before (high ESE, low CIE) is **not a true engineer**.
- A student who only attends regularly but cannot perform under exam pressure (high CIE, low ESE) is **not ready for industry**.
- **Both must be passed** to declare the student competent in the module.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** CIE rewards **consistency and process**, while ESE rewards **culmination and competence**. KTU 2024 Scheme insists on **both** because the **NEP 2020** philosophy emphasizes *continuous and comprehensive evaluation* over one-shot high-stakes testing.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Highlighted

- **Total Course Marks:** **100**
- **CIE Weightage:** **60%**
- **ESE Weightage:** **40%**
- **Minimum CIE to appear for ESE:** **≥ 50% of maximum CIE** (i.e., ≥ 30/60) — *as per KTU 2024 regulations*
- **Minimum ESE pass mark:** **≥ 40% of ESE** (i.e., ≥ 16/40) — *as per KTU 2024 regulations*
- **Minimum Total pass mark:** **≥ 50% of aggregate** (i.e., ≥ 50/100) — *with CIE ≥ 30 AND ESE ≥ 16*

---

## 1.4 Module Experiment 2 Context (PBADT504 – Big Data Analytics)

In **PBADT504 (Big Data Analytics)**, **Module Experiment 2** typically refers to the second lab/module experiment in the series — e.g., a **Hadoop MapReduce experiment, a Spark RDD/DataFrame experiment, or a NoSQL data modelling experiment** (depending on the syllabus mapping). The marks distribution described in this topic applies to **how the student will be graded for that specific experiment**, and is uniform across all module experiments in the lab course.

> [!NOTE]
> The phrase *"In Part A"* in the topic statement refers to the **Part A section of the ESE question paper**, which carries shorter, recall-type questions — usually worth **2 to 3 marks each** — and is **compulsory** (no internal choice in Part A).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Evaluation Framework

### 2.1.1 The 60-Mark CIE Architecture

The **Continuous Internal Evaluation** is not a single test — it is a **multi-component, semester-long observation system**. Each sub-component captures a **different cognitive and behavioural dimension** of the student:

#### **Component 1: Attendance — 5 Marks**
- **What it measures:** *Discipline, regularity, and seriousness*.
- **How it is awarded:** A pro-rata calculation based on the student's **percentage of attendance** in lab/project sessions.
- **Logic:** Engineering labs require hands-on presence; you cannot learn Hadoop or Spark by proxy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Attendance Slab (KTU 2024 — refer to your institution's lab manual for exact slab):**
> - ≥ 95% attendance → **5/5**
> - 90% – 94% → **4/5**
> - 85% – 89% → **3/5**
> - 80% – 84% → **2/5**
> - 75% – 79% → **1/5**
> - < 75% → **0/5** (and the student may be debarred from ESE)

#### **Component 2: Project / Lab Record Evaluation — 30 Marks**
- **What it measures:** *Quality of practical work, code correctness, viva-voce, report writing, innovation*.
- **Why it is the largest sub-component:** The 30 marks reflect the **hands-on, project-based nature** of NEP 2020 lab courses. Big Data Analytics is a **skill-based subject** — you must *write code, run it on real clusters (or simulated ones), debug it, and present results*.
- **Sub-criteria (typical):**
  - Code/Program correctness: 10 marks
  - Output verification and screenshots: 5 marks
  - Lab record / Report: 5 marks
  - Viva-voce: 5 marks
  - Innovation / Extension / Beyond-syllabus: 5 marks

#### **Component 3 & 4: Internal Exam 1 + Internal Exam 2 — 12.5 + 12.5 = 25 Marks**
- **What it measures:** *Twice-a-semester formal testing of concepts and practical ability*.
- **Why two exams?** To give students **two chances to demonstrate mastery** and to **spread the cognitive load** (no single high-stakes test). This is a hallmark of **NEP 2020's continuous assessment philosophy**.
- **Format:** Usually a **2-hour combined written + practical test**, covering the experiments done up to that point.

### 2.1.2 The 40-Mark ESE Architecture

The **End Semester Examination** is the **final, summative assessment** held during the KTU-designated exam window.

- **Conducted by:** University (KTU), evaluated by **internal examiner + external examiner** (typical lab pattern) or by an **external examiner alone**.
- **Duration:** Typically **2 to 3 hours** (for lab courses).
- **Part A:** Short-answer questions — *compulsory, no choice*. Tests *definitions, commands, syntax recall*.
- **Part B:** Long-answer questions — *with internal choice*. Tests *design, implementation, debugging, case-study analysis*.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind This Evaluation Pattern

| **Educational Goal** | **Which Component Addresses It** | **Marks Allocated** |
|---|---|---|
| Ensure students **attend** lab regularly | Attendance | 5 |
| Ensure students **do the work**, not just copy | Project / Lab Record | 30 |
| Test **recall of concepts** mid-semester | Internal Exam 1, Internal Exam 2 | 12.5 + 12.5 = 25 |
| Test **final integrated competence** | ESE | 40 |
| **Total** | — | **100** |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The formulas below are the **official KTU 2024 Scheme calculation rules**. Master them — they appear in viva voce, academic audits, and (occasionally) in Part A of the ESE itself.

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Rule** | **Unit / Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Course Marks | $M_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{CIE}} + M_{\text{ESE}}$ | **100 marks** |
| 2 | CIE Total | $M_{\text{CIE}} = M_{\text{att}} + M_{\text{proj}} + M_{\text{int1}} + M_{\text{int2}}$ | **60 marks** |
| 3 | Attendance Sub-marks | $M_{\text{att}} = f(\% \text{ attendance})$ | **0 to 5** |
| 4 | Project Sub-marks | $M_{\text{proj}} = \sum_{i} w_i \cdot s_i$ where $w_i$ are sub-weights, $s_i$ are sub-scores | **0 to 30** |
| 5 | Internal Exam Average | $M_{\text{int}} = M_{\text{int1}} + M_{\text{int2}}$ | **0 to 25** (12.5 + 12.5) |
| 6 | ESE Total | $M_{\text{ESE}} = M_{\text{PartA}} + M_{\text{PartB}}$ | **40 marks** |
| 7 | Minimum CIE to be eligible for ESE | $M_{\text{CIE}} \geq 0.5 \times 60 = 30$ | **30 marks** |
| 8 | Minimum ESE to pass | $M_{\text{ESE}} \geq 0.4 \times 40 = 16$ | **16 marks** |
| 9 | Minimum Total to pass | $M_{\text{total}} \geq 50$ AND $M_{\text{CIE}} \geq 30$ AND $M_{\text{ESE}} \geq 16$ | **50 marks** (all 3 conditions) |
| 10 | Attendance % | $\% \text{Att} = \dfrac{N_{\text{present}}}{N_{\text{conducted}}} \times 100$ | **%** (0 to 100) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of This Evaluation Pattern

This **60 (CIE) + 40 (ESE) pattern** is not arbitrary — it mirrors **industry performance review systems**:

- The **5-mark attendance** ↔ *Punctuality KPI* in corporate HR reviews.
- The **30-mark project** ↔ *Project delivery quality* in IT companies.
- The **12.5 + 12.5 internal exams** ↔ *Quarterly performance reviews* in tech firms.
- The **40-mark ESE** ↔ *Annual appraisal / promotion test* in industry.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Parallel:** A Big Data Engineer at Infosys/TCS/Google is **not** evaluated by one final test — they are evaluated continuously (sprint reviews, code commits, peer reviews, manager 1-on-1s) **and** by a final annual review. KTU's 60+40 pattern is the **academic blueprint** for the industry's continuous-and-summative model.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Total Marks

The complete derivation of the **total marks for Module Experiment 2** is shown below in a step-by-step manner. Each step corresponds to a KTU 2024 Scheme regulation.

### **Step 1: Define the Two-Tier Marks Identity**

By the KTU 2024 Scheme regulation for Lab/Project-integrated courses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{total}} &= M_{\text{CIE}} + M_{\text{ESE}} && \text{(Definition of total marks as sum of CIE and ESE)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 2: Substitute the Official CIE Sub-Components**

The 60-mark CIE is split into four official sub-components as per the KTU 2024 Scheme:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{CIE}} &= M_{\text{att}} + M_{\text{proj}} + M_{\text{int1}} + M_{\text{int2}} && \text{(Sum of four CIE sub-components)} \\[4pt]
&= 5 + 30 + 12.5 + 12.5 && \text{(Substitute official marks for each sub-component)} \\[4pt]
&= 60 && \text{(Verify: 5 + 30 + 12.5 + 12.5 = 60.0)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 3: Substitute the Official ESE Value**

The ESE is fixed at 40 marks for the module-level examination:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{ESE}} &= 40 && \text{(Official KTU 2024 Scheme value)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 4: Combine to Get the Total**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{total}} &= M_{\text{CIE}} + M_{\text{ESE}} && \text{(From Step 1)} \\[4pt]
&= 60 + 40 && \text{(Substitute values from Steps 2 and 3)} \\[4pt]
&= 100 && \text{(Final total marks for the module experiment)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Step 4 simply adds the two pre-defined weights (60 and 40) to give 100, which is the **standard KTU 2024 ceiling for any course/module**.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Calculating a Student's Final Score

Suppose a student in **Module Experiment 2 (PBADT504 – Big Data Analytics)** has the following performance data:

- **Attendance:** 88% → 3 marks (per the slab in Section 2.1.1)
- **Project / Lab Record:** Faculty awarded 24/30
- **Internal Exam 1:** 9 / 12.5
- **Internal Exam 2:** 10 / 12.5
- **ESE:** 28 / 40

Let us compute the **CIE, ESE, and Total** step by step.

### **Step A: Calculate CIE Sub-totals**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{att}} &= 3 && \text{(Given: 88\% attendance → 3 marks)} \\[4pt]
M_{\text{proj}} &= 24 && \text{(Given: project score)} \\[4pt]
M_{\text{int1}} &= 9 && \text{(Given: Internal Exam 1 score)} \\[4pt]
M_{\text{int2}} &= 10 && \text{(Given: Internal Exam 2 score)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step B: Sum the CIE Sub-totals**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{CIE}} &= M_{\text{att}} + M_{\text{proj}} + M_{\text{int1}} + M_{\text{int2}} && \text{(CIE definition)} \\[4pt]
&= 3 + 24 + 9 + 10 && \text{(Substitute the values from Step A)} \\[4pt]
&= 46 && \text{(Arithmetic: 3 + 24 = 27; 27 + 9 = 36; 36 + 10 = 46)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step C: Verify CIE Eligibility for ESE**

The student must have $M_{\text{CIE}} \geq 30$ to be eligible for ESE:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{CIE}} &= 46 \;\geq\; 30 && \text{(46 is greater than 30)} \\[4pt]
\text{Eligibility} &= \text{ELIGIBLE} && \text{(Student is allowed to write ESE)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step D: Record the ESE Score**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{ESE}} &= 28 && \text{(Given: ESE score out of 40)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step E: Verify ESE Pass Criterion**

The student must have $M_{\text{ESE}} \geq 16$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{ESE}} &= 28 \;\geq\; 16 && \text{(28 is greater than 16)} \\[4pt]
\text{ESE Pass} &= \text{PASS} && \text{(Student cleared the ESE minimum)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step F: Calculate the Final Aggregate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{total}} &= M_{\text{CIE}} + M_{\text{ESE}} && \text{(Total marks definition)} \\[4pt]
&= 46 + 28 && \text{(Substitute the computed values)} \\[4pt]
&= 74 && \text{(Arithmetic: 46 + 28 = 74)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step G: Verify the Final Pass Criterion**

The student needs $M_{\text{total}} \geq 50$ **AND** $M_{\text{CIE}} \geq 30$ **AND** $M_{\text{ESE}} \geq 16$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{total}} &= 74 \;\geq\; 50 && \checkmark \\[4pt]
M_{\text{CIE}} &= 46 \;\geq\; 30 && \checkmark \\[4pt]
M_{\text{ESE}} &= 28 \;\geq\; 16 && \checkmark \\[4pt]
\text{Final Result} &= \textbf{PASS (Grade eligible for 74/100)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Marks-to-Grade Conversion (KTU 2024 Typical)

Once the aggregate is computed, the grade letter is assigned using the official KTU 2024 grading scale:

| **Aggregate Marks Range** | **Letter Grade** | **Grade Point** | **Performance Descriptor** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 90 – 100 | **S** | 10 | Outstanding |
| 80 – 89 | **A+** | 9 | Excellent |
| 70 – 79 | **A** | 8.5 | Very Good |
| 60 – 69 | **B+** | 8 | Good |
| 50 – 59 | **B** | 7 | Above Average |
| 40 – 49 | **C** | 6 | Average |
| 30 – 39 | **D** | 5 | Pass (Marginal) |
| < 30 | **F** | 0 | Fail |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For our worked example, **74/100** falls in the **70–79** band → **Grade A (8.5 grade points)**.

---

## 3.4 Edge-Case Derivations

### **Edge Case 1: Student Misses Internal Exam 1 (Medical Leave)**

KTU 2024 Scheme allows a **make-up internal exam** with proper medical/certified reason. If a make-up is permitted, the score of the make-up replaces $M_{\text{int1}}$ in the formula. **No averaging** with Internal Exam 2 is allowed.

### **Edge Case 2: Student Scores 0 in Project**

A student who scores **0 in the 30-mark project** (e.g., did not submit, plagiarized, or code does not compile) will almost certainly fail because:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{CIE, max possible}} &= 5 + 0 + 12.5 + 12.5 = 30 && \text{(Even with full marks everywhere else)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The student's CIE is **at the threshold 30**, but their aggregate will likely fall below 50. **Lesson:** Never skip the project work.

### **Edge Case 3: Student Has CIE = 30 but ESE = 15**

The student has CIE = 30 (eligible) but ESE = 15 (< 16). The student **fails ESE** and must reappear, even though the aggregate might be 45.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style]`
**Q: Distinguish between Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) and End Semester Examination (ESE) under the KTU 2024 Scheme. Mention the marks allocated to each for a Module Experiment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **CIE (Continuous Internal Evaluation)** is the ongoing, in-semester assessment conducted by the **internal faculty** to evaluate the student's continuous learning, attendance, project/lab work, and in-semester test performance. For a Module Experiment, CIE carries **60 marks**, distributed as: Attendance (5) + Project (30) + Internal Exam 1 (12.5) + Internal Exam 2 (12.5).
>
> **ESE (End Semester Examination)** is the **final, university-conducted summative examination** held at the end of the semester, typically evaluated by an **external examiner**. For a Module Experiment, ESE carries **40 marks**, divided into Part A (compulsory short-answer) and Part B (choice-based long-answer).
>
> **Key Difference:** CIE is *continuous, formative, and internal*, while ESE is *terminal, summative, and university-controlled*.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`
**Q: List the four sub-components of the 60-mark CIE for a Module Experiment in PBADT504 and state the minimum CIE required to be eligible to write the ESE.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four sub-components of the 60-mark CIE are:
1. **Attendance** — **5 marks**
2. **Project / Lab Record Evaluation** — **30 marks**
3. **Internal Examination – 1** — **12.5 marks**
4. **Internal Examination – 2** — **12.5 marks**

**Minimum CIE eligibility:** A student must secure **at least 50% of the maximum CIE**, i.e., **30 out of 60 marks**, to be eligible to appear for the ESE.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)** scheme for a Module Experiment under the KTU 2024 Scheme. Describe all four sub-components, the marks allocated, and the rationale behind each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A student in Module Experiment 2 of PBADT504 has the following scores: Attendance = 4/5, Project = 22/30, Internal Exam 1 = 8/12.5, Internal Exam 2 = 10/12.5, ESE = 25/40. **Compute the CIE, the aggregate, and determine whether the student passes the module. Show all calculations step-by-step.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The CIE is a 60-mark continuous assessment framework designed to evaluate a student's progress throughout the semester in a holistic manner, aligning with the **NEP 2020 philosophy of continuous and comprehensive evaluation (CCE)**.

**Sub-component 1 — Attendance (1 mark):**
- **5 marks** are awarded based on the student's attendance percentage in lab/project sessions.
- **Rationale:** Engineering labs require hands-on presence; consistent attendance ensures skill acquisition. Students with <75% attendance may be debarred from ESE.

**Sub-component 2 — Project / Lab Record (2 marks):**
- **30 marks** are the **largest sub-component**, reflecting the skill-based nature of the course.
- **Rationale:** Big Data Analytics is a *practical* discipline — students must write code (e.g., MapReduce, Spark), execute it, and present outputs.
- The 30 marks are typically split into: code correctness, output verification, lab record/report, viva-voce, and innovation.

**Sub-component 3 & 4 — Internal Exams (2 marks):**
- Two internal exams of **12.5 marks each** (total 25 marks).
- **Rationale:** Two tests provide multiple opportunities for the student to demonstrate mastery and reduce the risk of a single high-stakes failure. They test *recall of concepts* and *practical ability* mid-semester.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The 60-mark CIE, combined with the 40-mark ESE, ensures a **balanced 60:40 ratio** that rewards both **continuous effort** and **culminating competence** — fully aligned with NEP 2020 outcomes.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Given Data (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{att}} &= 4, \quad M_{\text{proj}} = 22, \quad M_{\text{int1}} = 8, \quad M_{\text{int2}} = 10, \quad M_{\text{ESE}} = 25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute CIE (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{CIE}} &= M_{\text{att}} + M_{\text{proj}} + M_{\text{int1}} + M_{\text{int2}} \\[4pt]
&= 4 + 22 + 8 + 10 \\[4pt]
&= 44
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Verify CIE Eligibility (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{CIE}} &= 44 \;\geq\; 30 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ELIGIBLE for ESE} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verify ESE Pass (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{ESE}} &= 25 \;\geq\; 16 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ESE PASS} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Aggregate (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{total}} &= M_{\text{CIE}} + M_{\text{ESE}} = 44 + 25 = 69
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Final Decision (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{total}} &= 69 \;\geq\; 50 \;\checkmark, \quad M_{\text{CIE}} = 44 \;\geq\; 30 \;\checkmark, \quad M_{\text{ESE}} = 25 \;\geq\; 16 \;\checkmark \\[4pt]
\text{Result} &= \textbf{PASS with 69/100 → Grade B+ (8 grade points)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern for a Module Experiment, including the role of Part A and Part B, the marks split, and the minimum pass criteria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A faculty member awards the following scores to a student. **Verify all three pass conditions** (CIE ≥ 30, ESE ≥ 16, Total ≥ 50) and **identify which condition (if any) fails**: Attendance = 2/5, Project = 18/30, Internal Exam 1 = 7/12.5, Internal Exam 2 = 6/12.5, ESE = 18/40. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The ESE is the **40-mark, university-conducted final examination** for the module. It is **summative** (tests end-of-semester competence) and is typically evaluated by an **external examiner** (or a panel of internal + external for lab courses).

**Part A (2 marks):**
- **Compulsory** short-answer questions (no internal choice).
- Tests *definitions, commands, syntax recall, and one-liner conceptual clarity*.
- Typically carries **8 to 10 marks** within the 40-mark ESE (sub-divided as 3-mark or 2-mark sub-questions).

**Part B (2 marks):**
- **Choice-based** long-answer questions (e.g., *answer any 5 out of 7*).
- Tests *design, implementation, debugging, case-study analysis, and code writing*.
- Typically carries **30 marks** within the 40-mark ESE.

**Minimum Pass Criteria (2 marks):**
- **ESE ≥ 40% of 40 = 16 marks** (must be cleared independently).
- **CIE ≥ 50% of 60 = 30 marks** (must be cleared independently).
- **Aggregate ≥ 50% of 100 = 50 marks** (overall).
- **All three** must be satisfied — there is **no compensation** between CIE and ESE.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Given Data (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{att}} &= 2, \quad M_{\text{proj}} = 18, \quad M_{\text{int1}} = 7, \quad M_{\text{int2}} = 6, \quad M_{\text{ESE}} = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute CIE (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{CIE}} &= 2 + 18 + 7 + 6 = 33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Verify CIE ≥ 30 (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{CIE}} &= 33 \;\geq\; 30 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PASS} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verify ESE ≥ 16 (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{ESE}} &= 18 \;\geq\; 16 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PASS (but marginal)} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Aggregate and Verify Total ≥ 50 (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{total}} &= 33 + 18 = 51 \;\geq\; 50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PASS} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Final Decision (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{All three conditions satisfied.} \\[4pt]
\text{Result} &= \textbf{PASS with 51/100 → Grade B (7 grade points) — BARELY CLEARED}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the CIE Independence Rule:** A common student error is to assume that a *high ESE* can "compensate" for a *low CIE*. **It cannot.** KTU 2024 Scheme mandates that **CIE ≥ 30 must be cleared independently**, regardless of ESE performance. If CIE < 30, the student is **not eligible** to even write ESE, and the result is **FAIL (CIE insufficient)**.
>
> 2. **Misreading the 12.5 Marks:** Students often treat 12.5 as 12 or 13. Always use **decimal precision** (e.g., $M_{\text{int1}} = 8.5/12.5$ is valid). Rounding errors can cost 1–2 marks in the final aggregate.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the Project Sub-marks (30):** The project is the **largest** sub-component. Many students focus only on internal exams and neglect the project. A weak project (e.g., 12/30) **guarantees CIE failure** because the best possible CIE becomes $5 + 12 + 12.5 + 12.5 = 42$, which is borderline.
>
> 4. **Confusing "Total Pass" with "ESE Pass":** Even if $M_{\text{total}} \geq 50$, if $M_{\text{ESE}} < 16$, the student **fails the module** and must reappear for the ESE only. The CIE marks are **not** re-evaluated.
>
> 5. **Attendance is a "Silent Killer":** The 5 attendance marks seem small, but losing them reduces the CIE ceiling. A student with 0/5 attendance and full marks elsewhere gets $M_{\text{CIE}} = 55$, which is safe, but the **real risk** is the **debarment at < 75% attendance** (you cannot write ESE at all).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **Total Marks = 100**, split as **CIE (60) + ESE (40)**.
- ✅ **CIE = Attendance (5) + Project (30) + Internal Exam 1 (12.5) + Internal Exam 2 (12.5) = 60**.
- ✅ **ESE = 40 marks**, split into **Part A (compulsory short-answer)** and **Part B (choice-based long-answer)**.
- ✅ **Minimum CIE for ESE eligibility = 30/60 (50%)** — *below this, the student is debarred*.
- ✅ **Minimum ESE to pass = 16/40 (40%)** — *must be cleared independently*.
- ✅ **Minimum Aggregate to pass = 50/100** — *with all three conditions met simultaneously*.
- ✅ **No compensation** between CIE and ESE — a brilliant ESE cannot save a failed CIE, and vice versa.
- ✅ **CIE is internal** (faculty-evaluated), **ESE is university-conducted** (external or mixed panel).
- ✅ **Project (30 marks)** is the **largest** sub-component — *never neglect lab work*.
- ✅ **Attendance (5 marks)** is small but **debarment at <75%** is the real risk.
- ✅ **Two internal exams (12.5 each)** give students two chances — *use them as mock ESEs*.
- ✅ **Grade scale:** S (≥90), A+ (80–89), A (70–79), B+ (60–69), B (50–59), C (40–49), D (30–39), F (<30).
- ✅ **NEP 2020 philosophy:** Continuous **and** comprehensive evaluation — the 60:40 ratio embodies this balance.
- ✅ For **PBADT504 Module Experiment 2**, this marks distribution applies uniformly across all module experiments in the lab course.
- ✅ **Make-up exams** are allowed for genuine medical/certified absences, but **no averaging** with the missed internal exam is permitted.
- ✅ The phrase **"In Part A"** in the topic refers to the **compulsory short-answer section of the ESE question paper** — students must attempt **all** Part A questions without choice.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
