<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module Experiment 2 — HDFS & MapReduce Programming (Comprehensive Part B Answer Bank)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Module Experiment 2** in the KTU 2024 Scheme PBADT504 (Big Data Analytics) lab typically evaluates the student's hands-on competency with the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** and the **MapReduce programming model** — the foundational storage and processing pillars of the Apache Hadoop ecosystem. The experiment tests the ability to deploy HDFS operations (file manipulation, replication, block management) and author MapReduce programs (driver, mapper, reducer classes) using the **Hadoop Java API** or **Hadoop Streaming**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:**
> *HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)* is a distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant Java-based file system designed to store very large files across multiple machines in a Hadoop cluster. *MapReduce* is a parallel programming model for processing and generating large datasets using two primary user-defined functions — `map()` and `reduce()` — coordinated by a master `JobTracker`/`ResourceManager` and worker `TaskTracker`/`NodeManager` processes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **gigantic library** that needs to hold **millions of books**:

- **HDFS** is like a **warehouse storage manager** that doesn't keep all books in one room. Instead, it splits each book into chapters, places each chapter in different physical shelves (DataNodes), and maintains a **master index** (NameNode) that knows exactly where every chapter sits. If a shelf collapses, the index knows to fetch a duplicate from another shelf — that's the **replication factor of 3** by default.

- **MapReduce** is like having **hundreds of librarians** working in parallel. One group of librarians (**Mappers**) reads every book and writes down each unique word they find along with a count of "1" on sticky notes. Another group (**Reducers**) collects all the sticky notes for the same word, sums the counts, and writes the final tally. The result is a complete word-frequency index of the entire library — done in a fraction of the time a single librarian would take.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard HDFS Metrics (must be memorized for KTU exams):**
> - **Default Block Size:** **128 MB** (in Hadoop 2.x and later; was 64 MB in Hadoop 1.x)
> - **Default Replication Factor:** **3**
> - **NameNode Port:** **50070** (Web UI), **9000** (RPC)
> - **DataNode Port:** **50075** (Web UI), **50010** (Data Transfer)

### 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

The 2024 NEP-aligned BDA syllabus explicitly mandates that students demonstrate:
1. **CO2 (Apply):** Execute HDFS shell commands to manage distributed storage.
2. **CO3 (Apply):** Implement MapReduce programs for batch processing.
3. **CO4 (Analyze):** Interpret HDFS architecture diagrams and trace data flow during job execution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HDFS Architecture — Operational Breakdown

HDFS follows a **master-slave (or leader-follower) topology**. Understanding the role of each daemon is critical for the 14-mark KTU Part B questions.

### 2.1.1 NameNode (Master Daemon)

- Stores **metadata** (file-to-block mapping, block locations, permissions, replication factors).
- Maintains two critical in-memory + on-disk files: **FsImage** (checkpoint of metadata) and **EditLog** (transactional log of changes).
- **Single point of failure** in HDFS 1.x — addressed in HDFS 2.x via **High Availability (HA)** with a **Standby NameNode** using **ZooKeeper** for leader election.

### 2.1.2 DataNode (Slave Daemon)

- Stores the **actual data blocks** on local disks.
- Sends **heartbeat signals** every **3 seconds** to the NameNode.
- Sends **BlockReport** every **6 hours** (default) listing all blocks it hosts.
- On heartbeat failure for **10 minutes + 30 seconds** (the timeout threshold), the NameNode marks the DataNode as **dead** and triggers re-replication.

### 2.1.3 Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node)

- **Misnomer** — it is NOT a backup NameNode.
- Periodically merges `FsImage + EditLog` to prevent the EditLog from growing unbounded.
- Default checkpoint interval: **1 hour** OR when EditLog reaches **64 MB** (whichever comes first).

## 2.2 MapReduce Execution Pipeline

The complete data flow during a MapReduce job involves **4 distinct phases**:

| Phase | Executed By | Purpose | Output |
|-------|-------------|---------|--------|
| **Input Split** | `InputFormat` (e.g., `TextInputFormat`) | Logical division of input file | List of splits, each processed by one Mapper |
| **Map Phase** | `Mapper` (User-defined) | Process each `<K1, V1>` record | Intermediate `<K2, V2>` pairs |
| **Shuffle & Sort** | Hadoop Framework (automatic) | Group same keys together | `<K2, List<V2>>` per Reducer |
| **Reduce Phase** | `Reducer` (User-defined) | Aggregate values per key | Final `<K3, V3>` output |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is exam-critical. Memorize the formulas — KTU examiners routinely test these.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------|------|-------|
| 1 | HDFS Block Size | $B_{\text{default}} = 128 \text{ MB}$ | MB | Configurable via `dfs.blocksize` in `hdfs-site.xml` |
| 2 | Number of Blocks per File | $N_{\text{blocks}} = \lceil \text{FileSize} \,/\, B \rceil$ | blocks | Always rounded UP (ceiling) |
| 3 | Total Storage with Replication | $S_{\text{total}} = \text{FileSize} \times R$ | bytes | $R$ = replication factor (default **3**) |
| 4 | Number of Mappers | $N_{\text{mappers}} = N_{\text{blocks}}$ | mappers | One mapper per input split (typically per block) |
| 5 | Number of Reducers | $1 \le N_{\text{reducers}} \le \infty$ | reducers | Set via `job.setNumReduceTasks(n)`; default is 1 |
| 6 | Hadoop Heartbeat Interval | $T_{\text{heartbeat}} = 3 \text{ s}$ | seconds | Configurable: `dfs.heartbeat.interval` |
| 7 | DataNode Death Timeout | $T_{\text{timeout}} = 630 \text{ s}$ | seconds | $10 \text{ min} + 30 \text{ s}$; configurable: `dfs.namenode.heartbeat.recheck-interval` |
| 8 | MapReduce Data Locality Speedup | $S = T_{\text{remote}} \,/\, T_{\text{local}}$ | ratio | Ideally $S \to 1$ for local reads |
| 9 | Replication Pipeline Write | $T_{\text{write}} = T_{\text{disk}} \times R$ (parallel) | seconds | Pipeline is sequential per node but parallel across rack |
| 10 | Hadoop Split Size | $\text{Split} = \min(\text{BlockSize},\text{MaxSplitSize})$ | bytes | Default equals block size |

### 2.3.1 Worked Example of Formula 2 (Block Calculation)

> Given a file of size **450 MB** and default block size of **128 MB**:
> $$N_{\text{blocks}} = \lceil 450 \,/\, 128 \rceil = \lceil 3.5156 \rceil = 4 \text{ blocks}$$
> Block sizes will be: **128 MB, 128 MB, 128 MB, 66 MB** (last block holds the remainder).

### 2.3.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

HDFS and MapReduce power **petabyte-scale batch analytics** across industries:
- **Yahoo** processes **hundreds of petabytes** on HDFS for ad-targeting and search indexing.
- **Facebook** (Meta) uses a customized HDFS variant called **HDFS-RocksDB** for its data warehouse.
- **Netflix** runs MapReduce jobs for viewer recommendation batch training.

## 2.4 The Mapper and Reducer — Deep Dive

### 2.4.1 Mapper Class Structure (Java)

Every Mapper in Hadoop implements the `Mapper<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>` interface with a single overridden method:

```java
protected void map(KEYIN key, VALUEIN value, 
                  Context context) 
        throws IOException, InterruptedException
```

### 2.4.2 Reducer Class Structure (Java)

Every Reducer implements the `Reducer<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>` interface:

```java
protected void reduce(KEYIN key, Iterable<VALUEIN> values, 
                      Context context) 
        throws IOException, InterruptedException
```

### 2.4.3 Partitioner and Combiner (Frequently Asked)

- **Partitioner:** Routes each Mapper's intermediate output to the correct Reducer using a hash function: `getPartition(K2, V2, numReduceTasks)`.
- **Combiner:** A *local reducer* that runs on the Mapper node to pre-aggregate data **before** the shuffle phase, reducing network I/O. **Not always safe** to use (e.g., not safe for computing mean — but safe for sum, count, max).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: HDFS File Write Data Flow

When a client writes a file of size $F$ bytes to HDFS with replication factor $R$, the following sequence executes:

### Step 1 — Client-Side Request
The client contacts the NameNode via RPC to request the creation of a file. The NameNode performs a permission check and metadata validation, then returns a list of DataNode destinations for the first block.

### Step 2 — Pipeline Construction
The client opens a **replication pipeline** $P$ of length $R$:

$$
P = \{D_1 \to D_2 \to \cdots \to D_R\}
$$

where each $D_i$ is a DataNode selected by the NameNode's **rack-aware replica placement policy**.

### Step 3 — Block Streaming and Acknowledgment
The client pushes the block in **packets** of size **64 KB** (default) through the pipeline. Each $D_i$ forwards the packet to $D_{i+1}$ and sends an acknowledgment back. The total time to write a single block is:

$$
T_{\text{write}} = \frac{B}{P_{\text{net}}} \cdot (R - 1) \cdot L_{\text{net}} + T_{\text{disk}}
$$

where:
- $B$ = block size (bytes)
- $P_{\text{net}}$ = network packet size (**64 KB**)
- $R$ = replication factor
- $L_{\text{net}}$ = network latency between consecutive DataNodes
- $T_{\text{disk}}$ = disk write time per replica

### Step 4 — Block Report
After the write completes, the DataNodes send a **BlockReceived** message to the NameNode, which updates its metadata in the `EditLog`.

### Step 5 — Final Close
Once all blocks are written, the client calls `close()`. The NameNode finalizes the file by committing the metadata transaction.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Number of Mappers for a Given Input

The number of mappers is **not arbitrary** — it is determined by the `InputFormat` and the block size:

$$
N_{\text{mappers}} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Total Input Size}}{S_{\text{split}}} \right\rceil
$$

where:

$$
S_{\text{split}} = \min\left(B_{\text{HDFS}},\; S_{\text{max}}\right)
$$

with $S_{\text{max}}$ being the configurable maximum split size (default = `Long.MAX_VALUE`).

**Example derivation:** If a file is 1 GB (= 1024 MB) and the HDFS block size is 128 MB:

$$
N_{\text{mappers}} = \left\lceil \frac{1024}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 8 \right\rceil = 8 \text{ mappers}
$$

Each mapper processes exactly **128 MB** of input — one full HDFS block.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Walkthrough: MapReduce Word Count on a Sample Input

Consider the input file:

```
line 1: hello world hello
line 2: world hadoop
line 3: hello hadoop
```

### Phase 1 — Input Split
The file is split into 3 logical splits (one per line, given `TextInputFormat`'s line-based splitting).

### Phase 2 — Map Phase (per line)

For **line 1** (`<0, "hello world hello">`):
- Token 1: `<"hello", 1>`
- Token 2: `<"world", 1>`
- Token 3: `<"hello", 1>`

For **line 2** (`<12, "world hadoop">`):
- Token 1: `<"world", 1>`
- Token 2: `<"hadoop", 1>`

For **line 3** (`<26, "hello hadoop">`):
- Token 1: `<"hello", 1>`
- Token 2: `<"hadoop", 1>`

### Phase 3 — Shuffle & Sort (Framework-Handled)
The Hadoop framework groups all values for the same key:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{"hadoop"} &\to [1, 1] \\
\text{"hello"}  &\to [1, 1, 1] \\
\text{"world"}  &\to [1, 1]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Phase 4 — Reduce Phase (per key)
The reducer iterates through the list and sums:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{"hadoop"} &\to 2 \\
\text{"hello"}  &\to 3 \\
\text{"world"}  &\to 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Output (written to HDFS as `part-r-00000`):
```
hadoop    2
hello     3
world     2
```

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Combiner vs. Reducer vs. Partitioner

| Property | Combiner | Partitioner | Reducer |
|----------|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Runs on** | Mapper node | Mapper node | Reducer node |
| **When** | After map, before shuffle | During map output write | After shuffle & sort |
| **Purpose** | Pre-aggregate to reduce I/O | Route keys to correct reducer | Final aggregation |
| **User-defined?** | Optional | Optional (default: `HashPartitioner`) | Mandatory (in most jobs) |
| **Affects correctness?** | **YES** — must be associative & commutative | NO (purely routing) | YES |
| **Example safe use** | Sum, Count, Max | Hash(key) mod R | All aggregations |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common 3-mark question is: *"Can a Combiner be used in place of a Reducer?"* Answer: **No, a Combiner is an optimization that runs locally on the Mapper node; it does not replace the Reducer, which performs the final global aggregation.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDFS Architecture — Master/Slave Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["CLIENT APPLICATION"] -->|RPC Write Request| B["NAMENODE Master Daemon"]
    A -->|Read Write Pipeline| C["DATANODE 1"]
    A -->|Read Write Pipeline| D["DATANODE 2"]
    A -->|Read Write Pipeline| E["DATANODE 3"]
    B -->|Heartbeat 3s| C
    B -->|Heartbeat 3s| D
    B -->|Heartbeat 3s| E
    B -->|Metadata Checkpoint| F["SECONDARY NAMENODE Checkpoint Node"]
    B -->|Stores| G["FSIMAGE EditLog Metadata"]
    C -->|Stores Block Replica| H["LOCAL DISK BLOCK 1A 1B 1C"]
    D -->|Stores Block Replica| I["LOCAL DISK BLOCK 2A 2B 2C"]
    E -->|Stores Block Replica| J["LOCAL DISK BLOCK 3A 3B 3C"]
    F -.->|Periodic Merge| G
```

## 4.2 MapReduce Job Execution Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["HDFS Input File 1 GB"] -->|Split 128MB each| B["INPUT SPLIT 1"]
    A -->|Split| C["INPUT SPLIT 2"]
    A -->|Split| D["INPUT SPLIT 3"]
    B -->|map| E["MAPPER 1"]
    C -->|map| F["MAPPER 2"]
    D -->|map| G["MAPPER 3"]
    E -->|Intermediate K2 V2| H["SHUFFLE AND SORT Framework"]
    F -->|Intermediate K2 V2| H
    G -->|Intermediate K2 V2| H
    H -->|Partitioned by Key| I["REDUCER 1"]
    H -->|Partitioned by Key| J["REDUCER 2"]
    I -->|Final K3 V3| K["HDFS Output part r 00000"]
    J -->|Final K3 V3| L["HDFS Output part r 00001"]
```

## 4.3 HDFS Rack-Aware Replica Placement Policy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Client Writes Block B1"] -->|Replica 1| B["DATANODE on Local Rack R1 Node N1"]
    A -->|Replica 2| C["DATANODE on Local Rack R1 Different Node N2"]
    A -->|Replica 3| D["DATANODE on Different Rack R2 Node N3"]
    B -.->|Rack R1| E["RACK R1"]
    C -.->|Rack R1| E
    D -.->|Rack R2| F["RACK R2"]
    E <-->|Inter Rack Traffic| F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Default HDFS Replica Placement Rule:**
> - **Replica 1:** On the **local node** (where the client is running).
> - **Replica 2:** On a **different node, same rack**.
> - **Replica 3:** On a **different rack**.
> - **Replicas 4+:** Random placement (anywhere in the cluster).
>
> This policy minimizes inter-rack network traffic while maintaining fault tolerance.

## 4.4 MapReduce Data Locality Levels

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Data Locality Optimization"] --> B["NODE LOCAL Read same node"]
    A --> C["RACK LOCAL Read same rack different node"]
    A --> D["OFF RACK Read from different rack"]
    B -->|Fastest| E["MINIMAL NETWORK I/O"]
    C -->|Moderate| F["SWITCH LEVEL TRAFFIC"]
    D -->|Slowest| G["HIGH LATENCY"]
```

## 4.5 HDFS Read Operation Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant NameNode
    participant DataNode1
    participant DataNode2
    participant DataNode3
    Client->>NameNode: 1 open file read request
    NameNode-->>Client: 2 Return block locations sorted by proximity
    Client->>DataNode1: 3 Read block from nearest replica
    DataNode1-->>Client: 4 Stream data packet
    DataNode1->>DataNode2: 5 Replica exists at DN2 standby
    Note over Client,DataNode3: Client reads sequentially across blocks
    Client->>NameNode: 6 close file finalization
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Part A — Question 1** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]
**Q:** Differentiate between **HDFS** and **NFS** (Network File System) in terms of architecture, scalability, and fault tolerance.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | HDFS | NFS |
|-----------|------|-----|
| Architecture | Distributed master-slave (NameNode + DataNodes) | Centralized client-server |
| Scalability | **Horizontally scalable** to petabytes | Limited by single server capacity |
| Fault Tolerance | Built-in via **replication** (default factor 3) | Single point of failure |
| Block Size | **128 MB** | Typically 4–8 KB |
| Data Locality | Computation moved to data | Data fetched to computation |
| Best Suited For | Batch processing of large files | Small random-access files |

> **Memory Aid:** HDFS is for **Big Data**; NFS is for **Small Office/Home networks**.

---

### **Part A — Question 2** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Q:** What is the role of the **JobTracker** in Hadoop 1.x MapReduce? How is this role distributed in Hadoop 2.x with YARN?

**Model Answer:**

In **Hadoop 1.x**, the `JobTracker` is a **single master daemon** that performs **two** responsibilities:
1. **Resource Management** — tracking available slots across TaskTrackers.
2. **Job Scheduling/Monitoring** — assigning map/reduce tasks to slots and handling failures.

In **Hadoop 2.x (YARN)**, these responsibilities are **decoupled** into separate daemons:
- `ResourceManager` (RM) — global resource arbitration.
- `NodeManager` (NM) — per-node resource monitoring.
- `ApplicationMaster` (AM) — per-job task scheduling and fault tolerance.

This separation improves **scalability** (YARN can run non-MapReduce workloads like Spark, Tez) and eliminates the **single point of failure**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never confuse NameNode with DataNode** — examiners deduct up to 3 marks for swapped roles.
> 2. **Always specify the unit** in block calculations (e.g., "128 MB" not just "128").
> 3. **Draw diagrams in pencil first** then label in pen — KTU expects clean, labeled diagrams.
> 4. **In Java code, mention the package and import statements** — partial credit is awarded for completeness.
> 5. **For Word Count**, ensure you explain BOTH the Mapper and Reducer logic — writing only one loses 5 marks.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q (a)** — **7 Marks**: Explain the **HDFS architecture** with a neat diagram. Discuss the **rack-aware replica placement policy** and justify why HDFS uses a block size of **128 MB** instead of the traditional 4 KB.

**Model Answer:**

**HDFS Architecture** follows a **master-slave** model comprising:

1. **NameNode (Master):** Stores all filesystem metadata in RAM + disk (`FsImage` + `EditLog`). It does NOT store actual data. It is the **single source of truth** for file-to-block mapping.

2. **DataNodes (Slaves):** Store the actual data blocks. On startup, each DataNode registers with the NameNode and periodically sends **heartbeat (3 s)** and **block reports (6 h)**.

3. **Secondary NameNode:** Performs **checkpointing** by periodically merging FsImage + EditLog to prevent log overflow. It is **not** a hot standby.

4. **Client:** The user-facing library that interacts with both NameNode (for metadata) and DataNodes (for data transfer).

**Rack-Aware Replica Placement Policy:**

When a file is written with replication factor $R = 3$, the NameNode allocates replicas as follows:
- **Replica 1:** On the **local node** (where the writer resides).
- **Replica 2:** On a **different node, same rack** as Replica 1.
- **Replica 3:** On a **different rack** entirely.
- **Replicas 4+:** Assigned arbitrarily to any node.

**Justification:** This policy achieves two goals:
- **Fault tolerance** — survives a full rack failure.
- **Network efficiency** — at most **2 replicas share a rack**, reducing inter-rack bandwidth.

**Why 128 MB Block Size?**

The block size of **128 MB** is chosen because:
1. **Reduces NameNode memory footprint** — the NameNode stores ~150 bytes of metadata per block. With 128 MB blocks, 1 PB of data requires only $\sim$8 million blocks (manageable in RAM).
2. **Minimizes disk seek overhead** — large blocks mean sequential reads dominate over random seeks, maximizing disk throughput.
3. **Reduces metadata RPC traffic** — fewer block reports and heartbeats.
4. **Optimizes MapReduce data locality** — each Mapper processes a full block, enabling **node-local computation**.

For comparison, a 4 KB block (traditional FS) on 1 PB of data would require $\sim$256 billion blocks — impossible to fit in any single NameNode's RAM.

---

**Q (b)** — **7 Marks**: Write a complete **MapReduce Word Count program** in Java. Explain the role of the **Driver class**, **Mapper class**, and **Reducer class** with a sample input/output trace.

**Model Answer:**

**Complete Java Code:**

```java
import java.io.IOException;
import java.util.StringTokenizer;
import org.apache.hadoop.conf.Configuration;
import org.apache.hadoop.fs.Path;
import org.apache.hadoop.io.IntWritable;
import org.apache.hadoop.io.Text;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Job;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Mapper;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Reducer;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.lib.input.FileInputFormat;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.lib.output.FileOutputFormat;

public class WordCount {

    // ----- MAPPER CLASS -----
    public static class TokenizerMapper 
           extends Mapper<Object, Text, Text, IntWritable> {
        
        private final static IntWritable one = new IntWritable(1);
        private Text word = new Text();
        
        public void map(Object key, Text value, Context context)
                throws IOException, InterruptedException {
            StringTokenizer itr = new StringTokenizer(value.toString());
            while (itr.hasMoreTokens()) {
                word.set(itr.nextToken());
                context.write(word, one);
            }
        }
    }

    // ----- REDUCER CLASS -----
    public static class IntSumReducer 
           extends Reducer<Text, IntWritable, Text, IntWritable> {
        
        private IntWritable result = new IntWritable();
        
        public void reduce(Text key, Iterable<IntWritable> values, 
                           Context context)
                throws IOException, InterruptedException {
            int sum = 0;
            for (IntWritable val : values) {
                sum += val.get();
            }
            result.set(sum);
            context.write(key, result);
        }
    }

    // ----- DRIVER CLASS -----
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Configuration conf = new Configuration();
        Job job = Job.getInstance(conf, "word count");
        job.setJarByClass(WordCount.class);
        job.setMapperClass(TokenizerMapper.class);
        job.setCombinerClass(IntSumReducer.class);
        job.setReducerClass(IntSumReducer.class);
        job.setOutputKeyClass(Text.class);
        job.setOutputValueClass(IntWritable.class);
        FileInputFormat.addInputPath(job, new Path(args[0]));
        FileOutputFormat.setOutputPath(job, new Path(args[1]));
        System.exit(job.waitForCompletion(true) ? 0 : 1);
    }
}
```

**Role Explanation:**

| Class | Responsibility |
|-------|----------------|
| **Driver** | Configures the `Job`, sets Mapper/Reducer classes, declares I/O paths, and submits the job to the cluster. It is the `main()` entry point. |
| **Mapper** | Tokenizes each line of input, emits `<word, 1>` for every word. Runs once per input split. |
| **Reducer** | Receives grouped `<word, [1,1,1,...]>` pairs, sums the list, and writes `<word, total>`. |

**Sample Input:**
```
hello world hello
world hadoop
hello hadoop
```

**Sample Output (in HDFS `/output/part-r-00000`):**
```
hadoop   2
hello    3
world    2
```

**Compilation & Execution Commands (frequently asked in KTU lab viva):**
```bash
hadoop com.sun.tools.javac.Main WordCount.java
jar cf wc.jar WordCount*.class
hadoop jar wc.jar WordCount /input /output
hdfs dfs -cat /output/part-r-00000
```

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]

**Q (a)** — **7 Marks**: A company stores log files in HDFS. Each day, a **1.5 GB** log file is written with default block size and replication factor 3. Calculate:
(i) The number of HDFS blocks created.
(ii) The total physical storage consumed.
(iii) The number of Mapper tasks if a MapReduce job processes this file.
(iv) Justify why the default replication factor is **3**, not 1 or 5.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- File size $F = 1.5 \text{ GB} = 1.5 \times 1024 = 1536 \text{ MB}$
- Block size $B = 128 \text{ MB}$
- Replication factor $R = 3$

**(i) Number of Blocks:**
$$N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1536}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 12 \right\rceil = 12 \text{ blocks}$$

**(ii) Total Physical Storage:**
$$S_{\text{total}} = F \times R = 1536 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 4608 \text{ MB} = 4.5 \text{ GB}$$

**(iii) Number of Mappers:**
$$N_{\text{mappers}} = N_{\text{blocks}} = 12 \text{ mappers}$$
(One mapper per input split, which by default equals one HDFS block.)

**(iv) Justification for Default Replication Factor = 3:**

- **$R = 1$ (no replication):** Zero fault tolerance. If the single DataNode hosting the block fails, the data is **permanently lost**. Unacceptable for production.
- **$R = 5$ (over-replication):** Wastes **5× storage** and **5× write bandwidth**. Reduces cluster's effective capacity by 80%. Diminishing returns beyond $R = 3$.
- **$R = 3$ (optimal):** Provides **fault tolerance against up to 2 simultaneous DataNode failures** (or an entire rack failure due to rack-aware placement). Achieves the best **trade-off** between reliability, storage cost, and write performance.

This is why **Hadoop's default `dfs.replication` parameter is 3**.

---

**Q (b)** — **7 Marks**: Explain the **MapReduce execution pipeline** in detail with a neat flow diagram. Describe the **shuffle and sort** phase and explain why it is considered the **most expensive phase** of a MapReduce job.

**Model Answer:**

**MapReduce Execution Pipeline** consists of **4 phases**:

**Phase 1 — Input Split:**
The `InputFormat` (default: `TextInputFormat`) logically divides the input file into splits. Each split is typically one HDFS block in size (128 MB). For a 1 GB file, this yields 8 splits.

**Phase 2 — Map Phase:**
Each split is assigned to one `Mapper` task. The Mapper processes each `<key, value>` record (default: line offset, line text) and emits zero or more intermediate `<K2, V2>` pairs. Output is written to a **circular in-memory buffer** of size 100 MB (default: `mapreduce.task.io.sort.mb`).

**Phase 3 — Shuffle and Sort (THE EXPENSIVE PHASE):**
When the buffer reaches **80% threshold** (`mapreduce.map.sort.spill.percent`), a **background thread** spills the buffer to disk. Each spill goes through:
1. **Partitioning** — keys are partitioned to reducers using `Partitioner.getPartition()`.
2. **Sorting** — within each partition, keys are sorted using a quick-sort variant.
3. **Combining** — optional Combiner merges partial aggregates per partition.
4. **Merge** — multiple spilled files are merged into a single sorted file per partition.
5. **HTTP transfer** — the merged output is **pushed via HTTP** to the appropriate Reducer's local disk (the **"fetch"** stage).

**Why Shuffle & Sort is the Most Expensive:**
- It involves **disk I/O** (spill + merge) on both Mapper and Reducer sides.
- It involves **network I/O** (HTTP transfer across nodes/racks).
- It involves **serialization/deserialization** of every key-value pair.
- In a 1000-node cluster, a poorly tuned shuffle can consume **>70% of job execution time**.

**Phase 4 — Reduce Phase:**
Each `Reducer` merges the incoming sorted segments from all Mappers, then iterates through each unique key and its associated value list, calling the user's `reduce()` method. Final output is written back to HDFS as `part-r-XXXXX`.

**Optimization Tip (KTU Bonus Point):**
- Increase `mapreduce.task.io.sort.mb` to **200–400 MB** to reduce spill frequency.
- Enable **Combiner** to pre-aggregate before shuffle.
- Use **map-side joins** or **reduce-side joins with `DistributedCache`** to avoid shuffle entirely.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Last-Minute Revision Checklist for Module Experiment 2**

- [ ] **HDFS Block Size = 128 MB** (not 64 MB; that was Hadoop 1.x).
- [ ] **Default Replication Factor = 3** (write trade-off question is a KTU favorite).
- [ ] **NameNode stores metadata ONLY**, not data. DataNodes store blocks.
- [ ] **Heartbeat interval = 3 seconds**; **DataNode death timeout = 10 min 30 s**.
- [ ] **Secondary NameNode is NOT a backup** — it only does checkpointing.
- [ ] **NameNode = 50070 (Web UI), 9000 (RPC)**; DataNode = 50075.
- [ ] **Word Count Mapper emits `<Text, IntWritable(1)>`**; **Reducer sums the `Iterable<IntWritable>`**.
- [ ] **Driver class sets `Mapper`, `Reducer`, `Combiner`, and I/O paths** — it is the `main()` entry.
- [ ] **Number of Mappers = Number of Input Splits ≈ Number of HDFS Blocks.**
- [ ] **Combiner is an OPTIMIZATION, not a replacement** for Reducer.
- [ ] **Partitioner is the default `HashPartitioner`** — routes `key.hashCode() mod numReducers`.
- [ ] **Shuffle & Sort is the most expensive phase** — involves disk + network I/O.
- [ ] **Rack-aware placement:** R1 (local node) → R1 (different node) → R2 (different rack) → random.
- [ ] **YARN separates resource management (RM) from job scheduling (AM)** — unlike Hadoop 1.x JobTracker.
- [ ] **Compile with `hadoop com.sun.tools.javac.Main`**, package as JAR, then `hadoop jar wc.jar WordCount /in /out`.
- [ ] **Always mention units** in numerical answers (MB, GB, seconds).
- [ ] **In MapReduce, "moving computation is cheaper than moving data"** — this is the **data locality principle**.

---

**END OF NOTES — Module Experiment 2: HDFS & MapReduce Programming (Comprehensive Part B Answer Bank)**

*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework. All formulas, architectures, and code patterns are aligned with Apache Hadoop 2.x/3.x specifications and KTU board examiner expectations.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: each student can choose any one full question out of two questions Part A Part B Total ● 2 Questions from each module. ● Total of 8 Questions
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** each student can choose any one full question out of two questions Part A Part B Total ● 2 Questions from each module. ● Total of 8 Questions

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – End Semester Examination (ESE) Question Paper Pattern**
> 
> *"Each student can choose any one full question out of two questions. Part A carries X marks and Part B carries Y marks. A total of 2 questions are asked from each of the 4 modules, and the total number of questions in the question paper is 8."*

### Formal Academic Definition

In the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme B.Tech End Semester Examination (ESE)** structure, every question paper follows a **modular choice-based pattern** designed to test the student's grasp of all four modules of the syllabus while simultaneously offering internal flexibility. Under this scheme:

- The paper is divided into **two parts — Part A and Part B**.
- **Two (2) questions** are set from **each of the 4 modules** (Modules 1 to 4), making it a total of **8 questions** in the paper.
- For every module, the student is required to **answer only one full question** chosen from the two options available.
- Thus, the student finally answers **4 full questions** (one per module), with a clear **internal choice** within each module.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight for PBADT504 (Big Data Analytics):**
> The student has **NO choice across modules** — i.e., you must answer at least one question from each module. However, within each module, you have the **freedom to choose 1 out of 2** questions based on your preparation strength.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **restaurant menu** with 4 sections (Starters, Mains, Desserts, Beverages) corresponding to the 4 modules. From each section, the chef offers you **2 dishes** (the 2 questions), and you must pick **exactly 1 dish** from each section. You cannot skip a section (module), but you can decide *which* dish (question) is easier for you. That's exactly the KTU modular choice pattern.

In simpler terms:
- **8 questions on the paper** → like 8 dishes on the menu.
- **4 must be answered** → one from each of the 4 sections.
- **2 options per module** → your "internal choice."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term Glossary (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
> 
> | Term | KTU Definition |
> |------|---------------|
> | **Part A** | Shorter answer section (typically 3-mark conceptual/descriptive questions) |
> | **Part B** | Longer answer section (typically 14-mark analytical/derivation questions with sub-parts) |
> | **Module** | A self-contained unit of the syllabus (Modules 1–4 in PBADT504) |
> | **Internal Choice** | The student's right to pick between the two questions offered per module |
> | **Full Question** | The entire question including all its sub-parts must be answered |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decoding the Pattern — Operational Logic

Let us break down the **exact mechanics** of the question paper pattern step-by-step:

- **Step 1 — Question Pool Generation:** The KTU Board of Examiners (BoE), under the chairmanship of the Board of Studies (BoS) convener, prepares **2 questions per module**. For 4 modules, this yields **2 × 4 = 8 questions** in the question paper.

- **Step 2 — Modular Locking:** Each question is mapped to **exactly one module** to ensure syllabus coverage. The exam setter cannot set a question spanning two modules — every question must be unambiguously traceable to a single module.

- **Step 3 — Part A and Part B Distribution:** Each module's 2 questions are split into the two parts:
  - **Part A** → 1 question per module, usually a 3-mark direct/descriptive question.
  - **Part B** → 1 question per module, usually a 14-mark analytical/derivation question (often with sub-parts of 7+7 marks).
  - The **other question** in each part serves as the **alternative choice**.

- **Step 4 — Student Choice Application:** When the student receives the paper, for each module and each part, they examine the 2 available questions and **choose 1** to answer. Total questions answered = **4** (one Part A + one Part B from each of 4 modules).

- **Step 5 — Mark Aggregation:** The marks for the answered questions are summed to obtain the **ESE total** for the course.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Insight:** The pattern is essentially a **"4 × (1A + 1B) = 4 questions answered"** structure. The student never answers 8 questions — only 4.

### 2.2 Why This Pattern? — The Pedagogical Rationale

The KTU 2024 scheme adopts this pattern for three core academic reasons:

1. **Comprehensive Syllabus Coverage:** By forcing 1 question per module, the university guarantees that no module can be entirely skipped by the student, ensuring a holistic assessment.

2. **Student Accommodation (Reduced Exam Anxiety):** The internal choice (1 out of 2) gives students the flexibility to answer questions aligned with their stronger topics, reducing the cognitive load and exam stress.

3. **Bloom's Taxonomy Compliance:** Part A targets **Lower-order thinking (Remember/Understand — Bloom Level 1 & 2)**, while Part B targets **Higher-order thinking (Apply/Analyze/Evaluate/Create — Bloom Level 3 to 6)**. This two-tier structure aligns perfectly with **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** principles under **NEP 2020**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Exam Pattern Metrics

Below is a concise cheat sheet of the key numerical relationships governing the KTU 2024 ESE question paper pattern.

| Metric | Value / Formula | Unit / Notes |
|--------|----------------|--------------|
| **Total Modules in PBADT504** | 4 | Modules 1, 2, 3, 4 |
| **Questions Set per Module** | 2 | 1 in Part A, 1 in Part B |
| **Total Questions in Paper** | $Q_{total} = M \times 2 = 4 \times 2 = 8$ | Where $M$ = number of modules |
| **Questions Answered per Module** | 1 | Student's choice (1 of 2) |
| **Total Questions Answered** | $Q_{answered} = M \times 1 = 4 \times 1 = 4$ | Always exactly 4 |
| **Part A Marks per Question** | **3 marks** | Standard for KTU 2024 |
| **Part B Marks per Question** | **14 marks** | Typically split 7+7 |
| **ESE Total Marks (PBADT504)** | $T = (M \times 3) + (M \times 14)$ | $= 12 + 56 = 68$ marks approx. (excluding internal marks) |
| **Choice Freedom Ratio** | $50\%$ | 1 out of 2 questions selected |
| **Mandatory Module Coverage** | $100\%$ | All 4 modules must be attempted |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of This Pattern in Engineering Education

This pattern is not just an administrative convenience — it has direct engineering education parallels:

- **Software Engineering Analogy:** It mirrors the **"modular testing"** philosophy in software development, where each module (class/component) is independently unit-tested before integration.
- **Reliability Engineering Analogy:** The "must answer one per module" rule acts as a **fault-tolerant assessment**, ensuring no knowledge gap goes undetected.
- **Project Management Analogy:** It parallels the **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** approach, where a complex project (syllabus) is decomposed into smaller deliverables (modules), each tested independently.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Question Paper Structure

We now rigorously derive the **mathematical structure** of the KTU 2024 ESE question paper pattern. Every transition is shown explicitly.

**Given Parameters:**
- Let $M$ = number of modules in the syllabus.
- Let $Q_m$ = number of questions set per module.
- Let $C_m$ = number of questions the student must answer per module (the choice count).
- Let $A$ = marks per Part A question.
- Let $B$ = marks per Part B question.

**Step 1: Total questions in the paper.**

The KTU pattern specifies that 2 questions are set per module. Therefore:

$$
Q_{total} = M \times Q_m
$$

Substituting the KTU standard values for PBADT504:

$$
Q_{total} = 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ questions}
$$

This matches the explicit KTU statement: *"Total of 8 Questions."*

**Step 2: Total questions the student answers.**

The student must answer exactly 1 question per module (the chosen one):

$$
Q_{answered} = M \times C_m
$$

Substituting $C_m = 1$ and $M = 4$:

$$
Q_{answered} = 4 \times 1 = 4 \text{ questions}
$$

**Step 3: Splitting into Part A and Part B.**

For each module, the 2 questions are distributed as 1 in Part A and 1 in Part B:

$$
Q_m = Q_{A} + Q_{B} = 1 + 1 = 2 \text{ questions per module}
$$

**Step 4: Total Part A questions (both seen and answered).**

$$
Q_{A,total} = M \times Q_{A} = 4 \times 1 = 4 \text{ questions}
$$

**Step 5: Total Part B questions (both seen and answered).**

$$
Q_{B,total} = M \times Q_{B} = 4 \times 1 = 4 \text{ questions}
$$

**Step 6: Part A questions answered by the student.**

The student answers 1 Part A question per module:

$$
Q_{A,answered} = M \times 1 = 4 \text{ Part A questions}
$$

**Step 7: Part B questions answered by the student.**

The student answers 1 Part B question per module:

$$
Q_{B,answered} = M \times 1 = 4 \text{ Part B questions}
$$

**Step 8: Total ESE marks (from external exam only).**

$$
T_{ESE} = (Q_{A,answered} \times A) + (Q_{B,answered} \times B)
$$

Substituting the KTU standard marks:

$$
T_{ESE} = (4 \times 3) + (4 \times 14) = 12 + 56 = 68 \text{ marks}
$$

**Step 9: Total course marks (including internal assessment).**

For a typical KTU 2024 theory course, internal marks (continuous evaluation) are added:

$$
T_{course} = T_{ESE} + T_{internal}
$$

Assuming the standard KTU split (e.g., 60 ESE + 40 internal for a 100-mark course, or 50 ESE + 50 internal):

$$
T_{course} = 68 + T_{internal} \text{ (in proportional terms)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** The exact ESE-to-internal ratio depends on the specific course credit structure. For PBADT504, refer to the official KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus PDF for the precise credit and mark distribution.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Framework for Students

The following matrix provides a **strategic decision-making framework** for KTU students facing this exam pattern.

| Decision Point | Recommended Action | Rationale |
|----------------|--------------------|-----------|
| **Which question to choose (1 of 2)?** | Choose the question where you can recall **at least 70% of the answer** confidently. | Maximizes score; avoids wasting time on unfamiliar questions. |
| **Time allocation (Part A — 3 marks)** | Spend **~8–10 minutes** per Part A question. | Total Part A time: 4 × 9 = ~36 minutes. |
| **Time allocation (Part B — 14 marks)** | Spend **~25–30 minutes** per Part B question. | Total Part B time: 4 × 27 = ~108 minutes. |
| **Total exam duration** | Typically **3 hours (180 minutes)** for the ESE. | Leaves ~36 minutes for revision. |
| **If unsure between two questions** | Read both fully, then choose the one with **clearer sub-parts** you can address. | Sub-parts (a) and (b) often have independent marks. |
| **Module skip risk** | **Never skip a module.** You must answer one question from each. | Skipping = guaranteed zero marks from that module. |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Applying the Formula

> **Scenario:** A student is preparing for PBADT504 ESE. They have 4 modules to cover. For Module 2, they find two Part B questions:
> - **Q2(a):** Explain the HDFS architecture with a neat diagram. (7 marks) + Differentiate between HDFS and RDBMS. (7 marks)
> - **Q2(b):** Explain the MapReduce programming model with an example. (7 marks) + Discuss Hadoop ecosystem components. (7 marks)
> 
> The student is more confident about **MapReduce** than **HDFS architecture**.

**Application of the Pattern:**

The student applies the **choice rule**: pick 1 of 2. They select **Q2(b)** because:
- They have stronger recall of MapReduce's phases (Map → Shuffle & Sort → Reduce).
- They can list Hadoop ecosystem components (Hive, Pig, HBase, Sqoop, Flume) confidently.

**Marks secured (hypothetical):**
- Sub-part (a): 6/7 (minor diagram labeling error)
- Sub-part (b): 7/7 (full marks)
- **Total for Module 2 Part B: 13/14**

This same logic is applied across all 4 modules, yielding the final ESE score.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — KTU 2024 ESE Question Paper Architecture

Below is a **Mermaid block diagram** illustrating the structural flow of the KTU 2024 ESE question paper pattern for PBADT504.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startNode([KTU 2024 ESE Question Paper]) --> totalQ[Total Questions in Paper: 8]
    totalQ --> partA[PART A - 3 marks each]
    totalQ --> partB[PART B - 14 marks each]
    
    partA --> mod1A[Module 1: Q1A or Q1A alt]
    partA --> mod2A[Module 2: Q2A or Q2A alt]
    partA --> mod3A[Module 3: Q3A or Q3A alt]
    partA --> mod4A[Module 4: Q4A or Q4A alt]
    
    partB --> mod1B[Module 1: Q1B or Q1B alt]
    partB --> mod2B[Module 2: Q2B or Q2B alt]
    partB --> mod3B[Module 3: Q3B or Q3B alt]
    partB --> mod4B[Module 4: Q4B or Q4B alt]
    
    mod1A --> choice1{Student Choice 1 of 2}
    mod2A --> choice2{Student Choice 1 of 2}
    mod3A --> choice3{Student Choice 1 of 2}
    mod4A --> choice4{Student Choice 1 of 2}
    
    mod1B --> choice5{Student Choice 1 of 2}
    mod2B --> choice6{Student Choice 1 of 2}
    mod3B --> choice7{Student Choice 1 of 2}
    mod4B --> choice8{Student Choice 1 of 2}
    
    choice1 --> ans1[Answered: 3 marks]
    choice2 --> ans2[Answered: 3 marks]
    choice3 --> ans3[Answered: 3 marks]
    choice4 --> ans4[Answered: 3 marks]
    
    choice5 --> ans5[Answered: 14 marks]
    choice6 --> ans6[Answered: 14 marks]
    choice7 --> ans7[Answered: 14 marks]
    choice8 --> ans8[Answered: 14 marks]
    
    ans1 --> totalA[Part A Total: 12 marks]
    ans2 --> totalA
    ans3 --> totalA
    ans4 --> totalA
    
    ans5 --> totalB[Part B Total: 56 marks]
    ans6 --> totalB
    ans7 --> totalB
    ans8 --> totalB
    
    totalA --> eseTotal[ESE Total: 68 marks]
    totalB --> eseTotal
    eseTotal --> finalAdd[Plus Internal Assessment Marks]
    finalAdd --> courseTotal([Final Course Grade])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Student Decision-Making Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startNode([Exam Begins]) --> readQ[Read all 8 questions]
    readQ --> analyzeMod[Analyze module-wise confidence]
    analyzeMod --> mod1Check{Module 1: Confident in Q1A or Q1A alt?}
    mod1Check -->|Q1A| ans1A[Answer Q1A - 3 marks]
    mod1Check -->|Q1A alt| ans1AAlt[Answer Q1A alt - 3 marks]
    
    analyzeMod --> mod2Check{Module 2: Choose Part B option}
    mod2Check -->|Option X| ans2BX[Answer - 14 marks]
    mod2Check -->|Option Y| ans2BY[Answer - 14 marks]
    
    ans1A --> loop[Repeat for all 4 modules]
    ans1AAlt --> loop
    ans2BX --> loop
    ans2BY --> loop
    loop --> revision[Reserve 30 min for revision]
    revision --> submitNode([Submit Paper])
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Module Coverage Matrix

| Module | Part A (3 marks) | Part B (14 marks) | Student Must Answer |
|--------|:-----------------:|:------------------:|:-------------------:|
| **Module 1: Introduction to Big Data** | Q1A or Q1A' | Q1B or Q1B' | 1 from A + 1 from B |
| **Module 2: Hadoop & HDFS** | Q2A or Q2A' | Q2B or Q2B' | 1 from A + 1 from B |
| **Module 3: MapReduce & Spark** | Q3A or Q3A' | Q3B or Q3B' | 1 from A + 1 from B |
| **Module 4: NoSQL & Analytics** | Q4A or Q4A' | Q4B or Q4B' | 1 from A + 1 from B |
| **TOTAL** | 4 questions | 4 questions | **4 answered** |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> ⚠️ **Common Mistake 1:** Students sometimes attempt **both** questions from a single module to "score more." This wastes time and often results in **incomplete answers**, leading to marks deduction in *both* questions rather than full marks in one.
> 
> ⚠️ **Common Mistake 2:** Students skip a module thinking they can compensate elsewhere. Since **each module is mandatory**, skipping any module guarantees a **ceiling on your maximum possible score**.
> 
> ⚠️ **Common Mistake 3:** Students misread Part B sub-part marks (e.g., writing 14 marks worth for a 7-mark sub-part, or vice versa). Always read sub-part mark allocations carefully.
> 
> ⚠️ **Common Mistake 4:** Not drawing diagrams where asked. In PBADT504 (Big Data Analytics), **neat diagrams** (HDFS architecture, MapReduce flow, Spark architecture, NoSQL CAP triangle) are worth **2–3 marks** in Part B. Skipping diagrams = direct mark loss.

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]**

*Explain the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE question paper pattern for a 4-module theory course like PBADT504. How many questions are set, and how many must a student answer?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, the ESE question paper for PBADT504 (Big Data Analytics) consists of **8 questions** in total — **2 questions from each of the 4 modules**. The paper is divided into **Part A (3-mark questions)** and **Part B (14-mark questions)**, with one question of each type per module. The student is required to **answer exactly 4 questions** — one full question (either Part A or Part B options) from each module, choosing **1 out of the 2** available options per module. The total ESE marks computed are 68 (12 from Part A + 56 from Part B), excluding internal assessment.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

*What is "internal choice" in the context of a KTU ESE question paper? Why is it provided to students?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Internal choice** refers to the provision in KTU ESE papers where the student can **choose 1 question out of 2** offered from the same module (e.g., choosing between Q1A or Q1A' in Part A, and Q1B or Q1B' in Part B). It is provided to **reduce exam anxiety**, accommodate **differences in student preparation**, and allow students to answer questions aligned with their **stronger topics**, while still ensuring **complete module coverage** since one question per module is mandatory.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** The student must answer **ONE** full question from the two given below. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) architecture**. Discuss the roles of **NameNode**, **DataNode**, and **Secondary NameNode**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Differentiate between **HDFS** and **Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS)**. Under what scenarios would you prefer HDFS over RDBMS? (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HDFS Architecture — 7 marks:**

HDFS follows a **master-slave (master-worker) architecture** designed to store very large files across multiple machines in a Hadoop cluster. Its core components are:

1. **NameNode (Master):**
   - Acts as the **master daemon** that manages the metadata of all files stored in HDFS.
   - Maintains the **file system namespace** — the directory tree, file-to-block mapping, and block locations.
   - Does **not store actual data**; it stores metadata in memory (RAM) and on disk (fsimage and edit logs).
   - Single point of failure in classic Hadoop 1.x (addressed by HA in Hadoop 2.x+).

2. **DataNode (Slave/Worker):**
   - Stores the **actual data blocks** (default block size = **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x+).
   - Sends **heartbeat signals** to the NameNode every 3 seconds to confirm liveness.
   - On instruction from NameNode, performs block creation, deletion, and replication (default replication factor = **3**).

3. **Secondary NameNode:**
   - **Misnomer** — it is NOT a backup NameNode.
   - Periodically **merges the edit logs with the fsimage** to prevent the edit log from growing indefinitely (a process called **checkpointing**).
   - Helps reduce NameNode startup time.

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**
```
        [Client Application]
              |
              v
     +-------------------+
     |     NameNode      |  <-- Metadata, fsimage, edit logs
     |   (Master)        |
     +-------------------+
       |    |    |
       v    v    v
   +----+ +----+ +----+
   | DN | | DN | | DN |  <-- DataNodes store actual data blocks
   +----+ +----+ +----+
   |Data| |Data| |Data|
   |Block| |Block| |Block|
   +-----+ +-----+ +-----+
```

**Mark allocation for (a):**
- Diagram: 2 marks
- NameNode role: 2 marks
- DataNode role: 2 marks
- Secondary NameNode role: 1 mark

---

**(b) HDFS vs RDBMS — 7 marks:**

| Feature | HDFS | RDBMS |
|---------|------|-------|
| **Data Type** | Structured, semi-structured, unstructured | Primarily structured (tables) |
| **Schema** | Schema-on-read (flexible) | Schema-on-write (rigid) |
| **Scaling** | Horizontal (scale-out) | Vertical (scale-up) |
| **Data Volume** | Petabytes / Exabytes | Gigabytes / Terabytes |
| **Processing** | Batch (MapReduce, Spark) | ACID transactions, OLTP |
| **Consistency Model** | Eventual consistency | Strong (ACID) consistency |
| **Cost** | Commodity hardware (low cost) | High-end servers (expensive) |
| **Best For** | Big Data analytics, log files, IoT | Banking, ERP, transactional systems |

**When to prefer HDFS over RDBMS:**
- When data volume exceeds **terabytes** and grows to **petabytes**.
- When data is **semi-structured or unstructured** (logs, images, videos, sensor data).
- When **batch analytics** (not real-time transactions) is the primary use case.
- When **cost-effective horizontal scaling** on commodity hardware is required.

**Mark allocation for (b):**
- Comparison table: 4 marks
- Scenarios: 3 marks

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **MapReduce programming model** with a suitable example. Describe the roles of **Mapper**, **Combiner**, **Partitioner**, and **Reducer** in detail. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **Hadoop ecosystem components** — namely **HBase, Hive, Pig, Sqoop, and Flume**. State the primary function of each. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MapReduce Programming Model — 7 marks:**

MapReduce is a **distributed data processing model** introduced by Google (Dean & Ghemawat, 2004) and implemented in Apache Hadoop. It processes massive datasets in **two main phases**:

1. **Map Phase:**
   - The **Mapper** function takes input as **key-value pairs** $(K_1, V_1)$ and produces intermediate key-value pairs $(K_2, V_2)$.
   - Example: For a word count problem, input line "hello world hello" → Mapper emits $(hello, 1)$, $(world, 1)$, $(hello, 1)$.

2. **Shuffle & Sort Phase (Framework-managed):**
   - Groups all values for the same key together.
   - The **Partitioner** decides which **Reducer** receives which key (default: hash partitioning).
   - The **Combiner** (optional, mini-reducer) performs local aggregation on the mapper's output to reduce network traffic.

3. **Reduce Phase:**
   - The **Reducer** function takes $(K_2, \text{list}(V_2))$ and produces final output $(K_3, V_3)$.
   - Example: $(hello, [1, 1]) → (hello, 2)$.

**Example: Word Count Pseudocode**
```
Mapper:
  input: (line_offset, line_text)
  for each word w in line_text:
    emit(w, 1)

Reducer:
  input: (word, counts_list)
  sum = 0
  for each count in counts_list:
    sum += count
  emit(word, sum)
```

**Mark allocation for (a):**
- Map phase explanation: 2 marks
- Shuffle & Sort with Partitioner/Combiner: 2 marks
- Reduce phase: 1 mark
- Example: 2 marks

---

**(b) Hadoop Ecosystem Components — 7 marks:**

| Component | Type | Primary Function |
|-----------|------|------------------|
| **HBase** | NoSQL Database (Columnar) | Provides **real-time, random read/write access** to big data on top of HDFS. Inspired by Google's BigTable. |
| **Hive** | Data Warehouse (SQL-on-Hadoop) | Provides **SQL-like querying** (HQL/HiveQL) for batch analytics on HDFS data. Translates queries into MapReduce/Spark jobs. |
| **Pig** | Data Flow Language (Pig Latin) | High-level **scripting language** for data transformation and analysis. Suitable for ETL pipelines. |
| **Sqoop** | Data Transfer Tool | Imports data **from RDBMS to HDFS** (import) and **from HDFS to RDBMS** (export) using JDBC. |
| **Flume** | Log Collection Tool | Collects, aggregates, and transfers **streaming log data** (e.g., web server logs) into HDFS/HBase. |

**Mark allocation for (b):**
- HBase: 1.5 marks
- Hive: 1.5 marks
- Pig: 1.5 marks
- Sqoop: 1.25 marks
- Flume: 1.25 marks

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ESE Pattern for PBADT504**

- ✅ **Total questions in paper = 8** (2 per module × 4 modules).
- ✅ **Questions to answer = 4** (1 per module, choosing 1 of 2 options).
- ✅ **Part A = 3 marks** (shorter, conceptual/descriptive).
- ✅ **Part B = 14 marks** (longer, often with 7+7 sub-parts).
- ✅ **Mandatory module coverage:** You **cannot skip** any module.
- ✅ **Internal choice = 50%** (1 of 2 within each module's part).
- ✅ **ESE marks = 68** (12 from Part A + 56 from Part B, excluding internal).
- ✅ **Time management:** ~9 min/Part A, ~27 min/Part B, ~36 min revision (3-hour exam).
- ✅ **Diagrams are critical** — always draw neat, labeled diagrams for architecture questions.
- ✅ **Higher Bloom's levels** (Apply/Analyze/Evaluate) dominate Part B — practice derivations, comparisons, and real-world applications.
- ✅ **Lower Bloom's levels** (Remember/Understand) dominate Part A — focus on definitions, list-type answers, and short explanations.
- ✅ **Choose wisely:** Pick the question where you can recall **≥70%** of the answer content.
- ✅ **Never leave sub-parts blank** — partial marks are awarded for correct steps/formulas even if the final answer is incomplete.
- ✅ **NEP 2020 alignment:** The pattern supports **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** by testing Course Outcomes (COs) systematically across all modules.

---
