> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** each carrying 2 marks (8x2 =16 marks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The KTU 2-Mark Question Pattern (8 × 2 = 16 Marks)

In the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** examination pattern for the course **PBADT504 – Big Data Analytics**, every Module-level internal assessment and end-semester evaluation includes a specific sub-section where students must answer **eight (8) short-answer questions**, with each question carrying exactly **2 marks**, summing to a total of **16 marks** per module evaluation block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A *2-mark short-answer question* is a single-step, definition-oriented, or one-line conceptual recall item that tests a student's ability to state a fact, define a term, list components, identify a tool, or describe a single operational step. It maps directly to **Cognitive Level L1 (Remember)** and **L2 (Understand)** of **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (RBT)**.

**Key Constraints (as per KTU 2024 Scheme):**
- Each question = **2 marks** (rigid, non-negotiable).
- Total of 8 such questions = **8 × 2 = 16 marks**.
- Expected answer length: **2 to 4 lines** of handwritten or typed response.
- Time allocation: approximately **1.5 minutes per question** in an ESE.
- One-word, one-line, or short-definition answers are accepted.
- No sub-parts, no derivations, no lengthy explanations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – The "Rapid-Fire Quiz" Model:**
> Imagine you are taking a quiz on a TV game show such as *Kaun Banega Crorepati* (KBC) Fastest-Finger-First round. The host fires 8 crisp, single-fact questions at you, and you have only a few seconds to recall the precise answer. There is **no room for storytelling** — just the crisp, correct fact.

In KTU exams, this 2-mark pattern functions identically:

- **The Question (Q):** A *needle-sharp* probe targeting a single nugget of Module 2 knowledge.
- **The Student (A):** Must retrieve the **exact term, definition, or one-step procedure** from memory.
- **The Marks (2):** Awarded only if the answer is *substantively correct* — partial credit is rarely granted in 2-mark slots.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Big Data Analytics):**
> The Module 2 experiment block typically tests knowledge of **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** architecture, **MapReduce** programming model, **Hadoop Daemons** (`NameNode`, `DataNode`, `JobTracker`, `TaskTracker`), fault tolerance via **block replication**, and **YARN** resource negotiation. Each 2-mark question drills into one of these micro-concepts.

## 1.3 The Eight Standard Categories of 2-Mark Questions in KTU 2024

To internalize the pattern, recognize that nearly every 2-mark question in this module falls into one of these **eight recurring categories**:

| # | Category | Typical Cognitive Level | Sample Verb in Question |
|---|----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Definition Recall** | L1 – Remember | "Define…", "What is…?" |
| 2 | **Component Listing** | L1 – Remember | "List the components of…" |
| 3 | **Diagram Identification** | L2 – Understand | "Draw & label…" |
| 4 | **Acronym Expansion** | L1 – Remember | "Expand HDFS / YARN / RPC" |
| 5 | **Default Value Recall** | L1 – Remember | "What is the default block size in HDFS?" |
| 6 | **Process Step Identification** | L2 – Understand | "What is the role of…?" |
| 7 | **Differentiation in one line** | L2 – Understand | "Distinguish between… in one line" |
| 8 | **Command / API Syntax** | L3 – Apply | "Write the command to…" |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Students often write paragraphs for 2-mark questions. The KTU evaluator follows a strict marking scheme — only the **first 2–3 correct lines** earn credit. Anything beyond is wasted time and effort.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mark-Allocation Logic — A Deeper Look

The 2-mark question is **not arbitrary**. It is carefully engineered to test **breadth of syllabus coverage** rather than depth. Eight such questions, when grouped, virtually guarantee that the examiner can sample *all* major sub-topics of Module 2 within a single 16-mark block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-to-Minute Ratio in KTU ESE:**
> The university mandates approximately **1 minute per mark**. Therefore, 2 marks = **2 to 3 minutes** of writing time. A student who spends 5 minutes on a 2-mark question has *already lost* the chance to attempt 2–3 other questions.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Answering a 2-Mark Question

The optimal answering protocol, validated against KTU board examiner reports, follows a **3-tier micro-structure**:

1. **Tier 1 – The Anchor Phrase (≈ ½ mark):** Begin with the **head-term** or **headline definition** the examiner is scanning for. E.g., *"HDFS is a distributed, scalable, fault-tolerant file system…"*
2. **Tier 2 – The Expansion (≈ 1 mark):** Add the **single most distinguishing feature** or one **core operational detail**. E.g., *"…designed to run on commodity hardware and store very large files (typically in GBs to TBs) by splitting them into blocks of 128 MB (default)."*
3. **Tier 3 – The Tag/Example (≈ ½ mark):** Optionally include a **one-line example, formula, or tag** for the extra edge. E.g., *"Example: 1 GB file → 8 blocks → replicated 3× → 24 blocks across DataNodes."*

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (2-Mark Module 2 Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the **high-yield factual payload** that the 8 × 2 = 16 marks slot typically probes in Module 2 of Big Data Analytics. Mastery of every row is mandatory for full marks.

| # | Concept | Core Fact (One-Line Answer) | Default Value / Unit | Maps to Question Type |
|---|---------|------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **HDFS** | Distributed file system for storing very large files across commodity machines | Block size = **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+) | Definition |
| 2 | **Block Size** | Smallest unit of data read/written in HDFS; default 128 MB | **128 MB** | Default Value |
| 3 | **Replication Factor** | Number of copies of each block maintained across the cluster | Default = **3** | Default Value |
| 4 | **NameNode** | Master daemon storing **metadata** (file → block map) | RAM-resident | Component Listing |
| 5 | **DataNode** | Slave daemon storing **actual data blocks** on local disk | Per cluster, multiple | Component Listing |
| 6 | **MapReduce** | Distributed programming model with **Map** + **Reduce** phases | Phases = 2 | Definition |
| 7 | **Mapper** | Processes input key-value pairs and emits intermediate k-v pairs | Input format: `<K1, V1>` → `<K2, V2>` | Process Step |
| 8 | **Reducer** | Aggregates intermediate values sharing the same key | Input: `<K2, list(V2)>` → `<K3, V3>` | Process Step |
| 9 | **JobTracker** | Master that schedules and monitors MapReduce jobs (Hadoop 1.x) | Single per cluster | Component |
| 10 | **TaskTracker** | Slave that executes map/reduce tasks on individual nodes (Hadoop 1.x) | Per DataNode | Component |
| 11 | **YARN** | Yet Another Resource Negotiator; resource management layer in Hadoop 2.x+ | Replaces JobTracker/TaskTracker | Acronym + Definition |
| 12 | **ResourceManager** | YARN master daemon arbitrates cluster resources | One per cluster | Component |
| 13 | **NodeManager** | YARN per-node agent managing containers and resources | Per DataNode | Component |
| 14 | **HDFS Read Operation** | Client → NameNode (metadata) → DataNode (data) → Client | 2-phase protocol | Process |
| 15 | **HDFS Write Operation** | Client → NameNode (allocation) → DataNode pipeline → Client ack | 3-phase pipeline | Process |
| 16 | **Heartbeat Signal** | DataNode sends periodic signal (every **3 sec**) to NameNode | Period = **3 seconds** | Default Value |
| 17 | **Fault Tolerance** | Achieved via **block replication** (default 3 copies) | RF = **3** | Process |
| 18 | **Secondary NameNode** | Periodically merges **fsimage** + **edit logs** into a new fsimage (NOT a backup!) | Merge interval = **1 hour** (default) | Component |

## 2.4 The Scoring Equation for the 2-Mark Slot

While KTU does not publish a closed-form equation, an effective **expected score** can be modeled:

$$S_{\text{2-mark block}} = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \left( C_i \cdot w_i \right)$$

where:

- $C_i \in \{0, 1, 2\}$ is the **credit** awarded for question $i$,
- $w_i = 1$ is the **uniform weight** of each 2-mark question,
- $S_{\text{max}} = 8 \times 2 = \mathbf{16 \text{ marks}}$.

For an average student attempting 6 out of 8 correctly at full credit and 2 partially:

$$S_{\text{expected}} = (6 \times 2) + (2 \times 1) = \mathbf{14 \text{ marks}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world Hadoop cluster administration, knowing these defaults (128 MB block, 3× replication, 3-sec heartbeat) is non-negotiable. Misconfiguration causes silent data loss or cluster outages.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How the "8 × 2 = 16" Mark Distribution is Computed

This section demonstrates the **analytical reasoning** that KTU follows when constructing a 2-mark question paper. While not an algebraic derivation, the *coverage matrix* logic is rigorous.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 2-mark section is engineered using a **Coverage-Matrix Algorithm** that ensures every Learning Outcome (LO) of the module is sampled.

### 3.1.1 The Module Topic List (Module 2 – Big Data Analytics)

Let the Module 2 syllabus be partitioned into $N$ discrete knowledge units (KU):

$$KU = \{KU_1, KU_2, KU_3, KU_4, KU_5, KU_6, KU_7, KU_8\}$$

For Module 2 of PBADT504, the eight knowledge units are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
KU_1 &= \text{HDFS Architecture} \\
KU_2 &= \text{Block Storage \& Replication} \\
KU_3 &= \text{NameNode Internals (fsimage, edit logs)} \\
KU_4 &= \text{DataNode Operations (heartbeats, block reports)} \\
KU_5 &= \text{MapReduce Programming Model} \\
KU_6 &= \text{MapReduce Execution Flow (JobTracker/TaskTracker)} \\
KU_7 &= \text{YARN Architecture (ResourceManager/NodeManager)} \\
KU_8 &= \text{Hadoop Ecosystem \& I/O APIs}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 The One-to-One Mapping Theorem

KTU's question-paper construction follows the **Bijective Coverage Principle**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } Q = \{Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, Q_4, Q_5, Q_6, Q_7, Q_8\} \text{ be the 2-mark question set.} \\
&\text{Constraint: } |Q| = 8, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{8} \text{marks}(Q_i) = 16. \\
&\text{Principle: A well-designed paper ensures } f: Q \rightarrow KU \text{ is a bijection,}
\end{aligned}
$$

which means **every knowledge unit is tested by exactly one 2-mark question**, and the total marks are:

$$M_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \text{marks}(Q_i) = 8 \times 2 = 16 \text{ marks}$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical: Time Budget per Question

The KTU 2024 ESE for PBADT504 allots **3 hours (180 minutes)** for a **100-mark** paper. The 2-mark Part-A section of one module is allotted proportional time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{2-mark section}} &= \left(\frac{16}{100}\right) \times 180 \text{ min} \\
&= 0.16 \times 180 \\
&= 28.8 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{per question}} &= \frac{T_{\text{2-mark section}}}{8} = \frac{28.8}{8} = 3.6 \text{ minutes per question}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A student has approximately **3.6 minutes** to write a 2-mark answer — exactly enough for **2 to 4 lines** of crisp, well-structured content. Exceeding this time on any single question is the **#1 cause of incomplete papers** in KTU exams.

### 3.1.4 Analytical Matrix: Question-Type Distribution Across the 8 Slots

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Slot} & \textbf{KU Tested} & \textbf{Question Type} & \textbf{Expected Word Count} \\
\hline
Q_1 & KU_1\ (\text{HDFS Arch.}) & \text{Definition} & 25 \pm 5 \text{ words} \\
Q_2 & KU_2\ (\text{Block \& Repl.}) & \text{Default Value} & 15 \pm 5 \text{ words} \\
Q_3 & KU_3\ (\text{NameNode}) & \text{Component Listing} & 30 \pm 5 \text{ words} \\
Q_4 & KU_4\ (\text{DataNode}) & \text{Process Step} & 25 \pm 5 \text{ words} \\
Q_5 & KU_5\ (\text{MapReduce Model}) & \text{Definition} & 25 \pm 5 \text{ words} \\
Q_6 & KU_6\ (\text{Execution Flow}) & \text{Diagram/Listing} & 30 \pm 5 \text{ words} \\
Q_7 & KU_7\ (\text{YARN}) & \text{Acronym + Role} & 25 \pm 5 \text{ words} \\
Q_8 & KU_8\ (\text{Ecosystem APIs}) & \text{Command/Syntax} & 20 \pm 5 \text{ words} \\
\hline
\textbf{Total} & \textbf{8 KUs covered} & \textbf{—} & \textbf{16 marks} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight:** Notice that **all 8 knowledge units are tested in a single 16-mark block**. A student who memorizes only 5 out of 8 KUs *will lose a minimum of 6 marks* even if they answer the other 5 perfectly. Hence, **100% syllabus coverage** of factual knowledge is the minimum bar.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: HDFS Read-Write Flow (Module 2 Core)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the **HDFS Read and Write operations**, which are among the most frequently asked 2-mark diagram topics in KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Application] -->|1. Request file metadata| B[NameNode Master Daemon]
    B -->|2. Return block locations| A
    A -->|3. Read block data| C1[DataNode 1]
    A -->|3. Read block data| C2[DataNode 2]
    A -->|3. Read block data| C3[DataNode 3]
    C1 -->|4. Stream data| A
    C2 -.->|replica| A
    C3 -.->|replica| A

    D[Client Application Write] -->|1. Create request| E[NameNode]
    E -->|2. Allocate DataNode pipeline| F1[DataNode A]
    F1 -->|3. Forward packet| F2[DataNode B]
    F2 -->|4. Forward packet| F3[DataNode C]
    F3 -->|5. Send ack backward| D

    G[Heartbeat every 3 sec] -.-> B
    H[Block Report] -.-> B
    I[fsimage + Edit Logs] -.-> B
    J[Secondary NameNode] -.->|merge hourly| I
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: MapReduce Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input1[Input Split 1] --> Map1[Mapper 1]
    Input2[Input Split 2] --> Map2[Mapper 2]
    Input3[Input Split 3] --> Map3[Mapper 3]
    Map1 --> Shuffle[Shuffle and Sort Phase]
    Map2 --> Shuffle
    Map3 --> Shuffle
    Shuffle --> Reduce1[Reducer 1]
    Shuffle --> Reduce2[Reducer 2]
    Reduce1 --> Output1[Final Output HDFS Block 1]
    Reduce2 --> Output2[Final Output HDFS Block 2]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: YARN Architecture (Hadoop 2.x+)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client[Client Application] --> RM[ResourceManager Master]
    RM --> AM1[ApplicationMaster for Job 1]
    RM --> AM2[ApplicationMaster for Job 2]
    AM1 --> NM1[NodeManager Node 1]
    AM1 --> NM2[NodeManager Node 2]
    AM2 --> NM3[NodeManager Node 3]
    AM2 --> NM4[NodeManager Node 4]
    NM1 --> C1[Container CPU Memory]
    NM2 --> C2[Container CPU Memory]
    NM3 --> C3[Container CPU Memory]
    NM4 --> C4[Container CPU Memory]
    RM --> Scheduler[Scheduler Capacity Fair]
    RM --> ASM[ApplicationManager]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: The 2-Mark Answering Protocol (Pedagogical Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Read 2-mark question] --> P1[Identify KU being tested]
    P1 --> P2{Recall anchor phrase}
    P2 -->|Yes| P3[Write 1-line definition Tier 1]
    P2 -->|No| P4[Re-read question: pick keyword]
    P4 --> P2
    P3 --> P5[Add distinguishing feature Tier 2]
    P5 --> P6[Optional: example or formula Tier 3]
    P6 --> P7[Move to next question]
    P7 --> End[Complete 8 questions in 28 min]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Model Question Paper – 2-Mark Section (8 × 2 = 16 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following question bank is constructed following the **exact KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** for PBADT504 Module 2. Every question carries exactly **2 marks** as mandated by the *"each carrying 2 marks (8×2 = 16 marks)"* instruction.

---

### Part A: 2-Mark Short-Answer Questions (Answer ALL – 8 × 2 = 16 Marks)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Mock]`**
Define **HDFS**. State its default block size.

> **Model Answer (2 marks):**
> HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) is a distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant file system designed to store very large files (GBs to PBs) across clusters of commodity hardware. **Default block size = 128 MB** (in Hadoop 2.x and later; 64 MB in Hadoop 1.x).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Mock]`**
What is the **default replication factor** in HDFS? Where is this value configured?

> **Model Answer (2 marks):**
> The default **replication factor = 3**, meaning every block is stored on three different DataNodes. It is configured in the file `hdfs-site.xml` using the property `dfs.replication = 3`.

---

**Q3. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retrospective]`**
List the **two main daemons** of HDFS and state the role of each.

> **Model Answer (2 marks):**
> 1. **NameNode** (Master) – Stores metadata: filename, directory structure, block-to-DataNode mapping, and permissions. Runs on RAM.
> 2. **DataNode** (Slave) – Stores the actual data blocks on local disk and serves read/write requests from clients.

---

**Q4. `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023 Retrospective]`**
Expand the acronym **YARN**. What does it replace in Hadoop 1.x?

> **Model Answer (2 marks):**
> **YARN = Yet Another Resource Negotiator.** Introduced in Hadoop 2.x, it replaces the **JobTracker and TaskTracker** of Hadoop 1.x by separating **resource management** (ResourceManager) from **application scheduling/monitoring** (ApplicationMaster + NodeManager).

---

**Q5. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Mock]`**
Differentiate between **Mapper** and **Reducer** in one line each.

> **Model Answer (2 marks):**
> - **Mapper** – Processes input key-value pairs `<K1, V1>` independently and emits intermediate key-value pairs `<K2, V2>`.
> - **Reducer** – Receives all intermediate values grouped by key `<K2, list(V2)>` and aggregates them into final output `<K3, V3>`.

---

**Q6. `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Mock]`**
What is a **heartbeat** in HDFS? What is its default interval?

> **Model Answer (2 marks):**
> A **heartbeat** is a periodic signal sent by every **DataNode** to the **NameNode** to confirm it is alive and functioning. **Default interval = 3 seconds.** If the NameNode misses 10 consecutive heartbeats (i.e., 30 sec), the DataNode is declared dead and its blocks are re-replicated.

---

**Q7. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retrospective]`**
State the role of the **Secondary NameNode**. Is it a backup of the NameNode?

> **Model Answer (2 marks):**
> The **Secondary NameNode** periodically (default every 1 hour) merges the **fsimage** (file system snapshot) with the **edit logs** (transaction log) to prevent the edit log from growing unbounded. **No, it is NOT a backup NameNode**; in a failure, the cluster cannot automatically fail-over to it.

---

**Q8. `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023 Retrospective]`**
Write the HDFS shell command to **list files** in the root `/` directory of HDFS.

> **Model Answer (2 marks):**
> ```bash
> hdfs dfs -ls /
> ```
> (or equivalently `hadoop fs -ls /`). The output displays file/directory permissions, owner, size, modification time, and absolute path.

---

## 5.2 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in 2-Mark Questions (Module 2):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Secondary NameNode with a "Backup NameNode"** — Costs 1 full mark. (Correct: it is a *checkpointer* helper, not a hot standby.)
> 2. **Writing the wrong Hadoop version for default block size** — 64 MB is for Hadoop 1.x; KTU 2024 syllabus follows Hadoop 3.x where it is **128 MB**.
> 3. **Spelling MapReduce as "Map-Reduce" or "MapReducer"** — Examiners deduct ½ mark for spelling errors in technical terms.
> 4. **Confusing JobTracker (Hadoop 1.x) with ApplicationMaster (Hadoop 2.x+/YARN)** — Be explicit about which architecture you reference.
> 5. **Writing paragraphs instead of crisp definitions** — The examiner scans for the *anchor phrase*. If the anchor is buried in the third sentence, partial credit may still apply, but it is risky.
> 6. **Omitting the default value** in definition questions — E.g., defining HDFS *without* mentioning 128 MB loses ½ mark.

---

## 5.3 Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> The 14-mark sub-section is a *separate* block from the 2-mark × 8 = 16 marks section. The 2-mark pattern is exclusive to **Part A**. The following is provided as a *reference* for the broader module pattern.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU 2024 ESE Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the **HDFS architecture** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of NameNode, DataNode, and Secondary NameNode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **MapReduce programming model** with an example word-count program. List the phases of execution. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU 2024 ESE Pattern]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **YARN architecture**. Differentiate Hadoop 1.x from Hadoop 2.x in terms of resource management. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **block replication and fault tolerance** in HDFS. How does HDFS handle DataNode failure? **(7 marks)**

> [!TIP]
> **Sample Model Solution Sketch for B(a):**
> *YARN has two main daemons: **ResourceManager (RM)** at cluster level and **NodeManager (NM)** at node level. RM has two sub-components: **Scheduler** (allocates resources via Capacity/Fair scheduler) and **ApplicationManager (AsM)** (accepts job submissions and launches the first **ApplicationMaster (AM)** for each job). The AM negotiates containers with RM and works with NMs to execute tasks. Hadoop 1.x had a single JobTracker doing both scheduling and monitoring (a scalability bottleneck), while Hadoop 2.x splits these roles across RM, AM, and NM.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – KTU 2024 Module 2: 8 × 2 = 16 Marks Pattern**

- ✅ **HDFS** = Hadoop Distributed File System; **default block = 128 MB**; **replication = 3**.
- ✅ **NameNode** = Master; stores **metadata** in RAM (fsimage + edit logs).
- ✅ **DataNode** = Slave; stores **actual blocks**; sends **heartbeat every 3 sec**.
- ✅ **Secondary NameNode** = **NOT a backup**; it merges fsimage + edit logs hourly.
- ✅ **MapReduce** = Two phases: **Map** → **Shuffle & Sort** → **Reduce**.
- ✅ **Mapper** input = `<K1, V1>`; **Reducer** input = `<K2, list(V2)>`; output = `<K3, V3>`.
- ✅ **YARN** = Yet Another Resource Negotiator; replaces JobTracker/TaskTracker of Hadoop 1.x.
- ✅ **YARN daemons**: ResourceManager (master), NodeManager (slave), ApplicationMaster (per-job).
- ✅ **HDFS Write** = 3-phase pipeline; **HDFS Read** = 2-phase metadata + data fetch.
- ✅ **Fault tolerance** is achieved via **block replication** (default 3 copies on different racks).
- ✅ **Time budget**: ~3.6 minutes per 2-mark question; write **2 to 4 lines**, not paragraphs.
- ✅ **Answer protocol**: Tier 1 (anchor) → Tier 2 (expansion) → Tier 3 (example/default value).
- ✅ **Total marks of the 2-mark block** = 8 × 2 = **16 marks**; **do not skip any** — all 8 are compulsory.
- ✅ **Always state the default value** in definition questions (e.g., 128 MB, 3 replicas, 3-sec heartbeat) — this is the easiest ½ mark to secure.
- ✅ **Avoid spelling errors** in technical terms: MapReduce, NameNode, DataNode, YARN, HDFS, fsimage.
- ✅ **Draw simple block diagrams** for 2-mark diagram questions (HDFS arch, MapReduce flow) — labeled boxes with arrows.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Course Outcome Bloom’s Knowledge Level (KL) CO1 Demonstrate the Hadoop framework
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Course Outcome Bloom’s Knowledge Level (KL) CO1 Demonstrate the Hadoop framework

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apache Hadoop** is an open-source, Java-based distributed computing framework that enables the storage (using **HDFS - Hadoop Distributed File System**) and processing (using the **MapReduce** programming paradigm) of extremely large datasets (ranging from **gigabytes to petabytes**) across clusters of commodity hardware in a reliable, fault-tolerant, and scalable manner.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBADT504 - Big Data Analytics)**, demonstrating the Hadoop framework involves practically installing Hadoop in **pseudo-distributed mode**, interacting with the **HDFS shell**, and executing a **MapReduce program (typically WordCount)** — this maps to **Course Outcome CO1** at **Bloom's Knowledge Level (KL) = K3 (Apply)**.

## 1.2 Core Components of the Hadoop Ecosystem

| Component | Full Form | Primary Function |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| **HDFS** | Hadoop Distributed File System | Distributed, fault-tolerant storage |
| **MapReduce** | Map + Reduce Programming Model | Parallel data processing engine |
| **YARN** | Yet Another Resource Negotiator | Cluster resource management & job scheduling |
| **Hadoop Common** | Core Utilities | Java libraries & utilities supporting other modules |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Library Analogy for Hadoop:**
> 
> Imagine a **massive library** containing billions of books (your **Big Data**). 
> - A **single librarian** (traditional system) cannot manage, search, or count words in all these books efficiently.
> - So you build a **distributed system of librarians**:
>   - The **Head Librarian (NameNode)** maintains a **catalog/index** of where every book is stored.
>   - The **Section Librarians (DataNodes)** actually store the physical books on shelves (**Data Blocks**).
>   - When you ask *"How many times does the word 'hadoop' appear in the entire library?"*, the Head Librarian **delegates (Map)** smaller counting tasks to each Section Librarian in parallel.
>   - Each librarian returns their partial count, and the Head Librarian **aggregates (Reduce)** the final answer.
> - This is exactly how **Hadoop** works with **HDFS (storage)** and **MapReduce (processing)**.

## 1.4 Why Hadoop? — The 5 V's Justification

Hadoop was specifically engineered to handle data exhibiting the **5 V's of Big Data**:
- **Volume** — Petabyte-scale storage using commodity machines
- **Velocity** — High-throughput batch processing
- **Variety** — Structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data
- **Veracity** — Fault tolerance through data replication (default **3x replication**)
- **Value** — Extract insights from raw, voluminous data

## 1.5 KTU Syllabus Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module Experiment 2 (KTU 2024 Scheme)** maps to:
> - **CO1:** Demonstrate the Hadoop framework
> - **KL Level:** K3 (Apply) — Student must *execute* commands, not just recall.
> - **Bloom's Verb:** *Demonstrate, Install, Configure, Execute, Submit*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HDFS — Hadoop Distributed File System (Storage Layer)

HDFS follows a **master-slave architecture** designed for **write-once, read-many (WORM)** workloads.

### 2.1.1 HDFS Architecture Components

- **NameNode (Master)**: Stores **metadata** (file names, permissions, block locations, replication factor). Runs on high-RAM commodity hardware. A single point of failure (mitigated by **Secondary NameNode** or **HA NameNode** in Hadoop 2.x+).
- **DataNode (Slave)**: Stores the actual **data blocks**. Reports heartbeats (default every **3 seconds**) to the NameNode. Multiple DataNodes exist per cluster.
- **Block**: The smallest unit of data read/write. Default block size is **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x+ (was 64 MB in Hadoop 1.x).

### 2.1.2 HDFS Read/Write Flow (Operational Logic)

**Write Operation (Step-by-Step):**
1. Client requests the NameNode to create a file `/input/data.txt`.
2. NameNode checks permissions and responds with a list of DataNode IPs to write to.
3. Client splits the file into **blocks** (default 128 MB).
4. Blocks are written in a **pipeline** — Block → DN1 → DN2 → DN3 (replication pipeline).
5. Each DataNode acknowledges receipt back through the pipeline.
6. Once all replicas are written, the client receives success confirmation.

**Read Operation (Step-by-Step):**
1. Client requests the NameNode to read file `/input/data.txt`.
2. NameNode returns the **block locations** (list of DataNodes holding each block).
3. Client reads blocks in **parallel** from the **nearest DataNode** (rack-awareness).
4. Data is reassembled in the correct order on the client side.

## 2.2 MapReduce — Programming Model (Processing Layer)

MapReduce is a **functional programming paradigm** that processes data in two primary phases:

### 2.2.1 The Three Core Phases

| Phase | Function Signature | Input | Output |
|-------|--------------------|-------|--------|
| **Map** | `map(K1, V1) → list(K2, V2)` | Raw input record | Intermediate key-value pairs |
| **Shuffle & Sort** | Framework-managed | All map outputs | Grouped by key (K2 → list(V2)) |
| **Reduce** | `reduce(K2, list(V2)) → list(K3, V3)` | Grouped key-value pairs | Final aggregated output |

### 2.2.2 Execution Phases in Detail

- **Splitting**: Input file is divided into **InputSplits** (typically matching HDFS block size).
- **Mapping**: Each split is processed by a **Mapper task** running on the node where the data resides (**data locality**).
- **Combiner (Optional)**: A local reducer that performs **pre-aggregation** on the mapper node to reduce network traffic.
- **Partitioner**: Determines which **Reducer** will receive a particular key. Default uses `hash(key) mod R`.
- **Shuffling & Sorting**: Framework groups all values for the same key and sorts them.
- **Reducing**: Aggregates the grouped values to produce the final output.
- **Writing**: Output is written back to HDFS.

## 2.3 YARN — Yet Another Resource Negotiator

Introduced in **Hadoop 2.x**, YARN decouples resource management from the processing model.

| YARN Daemon | Role |
|-------------|------|
| **ResourceManager (RM)** | Global resource scheduler for the entire cluster |
| **NodeManager (NM)** | Per-node agent managing containers and monitoring resources |
| **ApplicationMaster (AM)** | Per-application negotiator that requests containers from RM |
| **Container** | Allocation of CPU cores + RAM for a specific task |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Memorize for KTU Examinations**

| Concept | Formula / Constant | Description |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| HDFS Block Size (Hadoop 2.x+) | **128 MB** | Default block size per file |
| HDFS Block Size (Hadoop 1.x) | **64 MB** | Legacy default |
| Default Replication Factor | **3** | Number of copies maintained |
| Number of Blocks | $\vert n \vert$ | $B = \lceil \vert \text{FileSize} \vert \,/\, \vert \text{BlockSize} \vert \rceil$ |
| Actual Storage Used | $\vert u \vert$ | $U = B \times R \times \text{BlockSize}$ |
| Partitioner Formula | $\vert i \vert$ | $P(\text{key}) = \text{hash}(\text{key}) \bmod \vert R \vert$ |
| Heartbeat Interval | **3 seconds** | DataNode → NameNode |
| Block Report Interval | **6 hours** | DataNode → NameNode (default) |
| DataNode Failure Timeout | **10 minutes** | Time before NameNode marks DN as dead |

> Where:
> - $B$ = Number of blocks in a file
> - $\vert n \vert$ = File size in bytes
> - $\vert \text{BlockSize} \vert$ = 134217728 bytes (128 MB)
> - $R$ = Replication factor
> - $\vert R \vert$ = Number of reducers
> - $\vert i \vert$ = Reducer index (0 to $R-1$)

### 2.4.1 Worked Formula Example (KTU Frequently Asked)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Calculation:** A file of size **512 MB** is stored in HDFS with the **default block size** and **replication factor = 3**.
> 
> Number of blocks: $B = \lceil 512 \,/\, 128 \rceil = 4$ blocks
> 
> Actual HDFS storage consumed: $U = 4 \times 3 \times 128 = 1536 \text{ MB} = 1.5 \text{ GB}$
> 
> **Storage Overhead:** $1536 - 512 = 1024 \text{ MB} = 1 \text{ GB of replication overhead}$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Yahoo, Facebook, Twitter**: Use Hadoop for log analysis, recommendation engines, and ETL pipelines.
- **Healthcare**: Processing petabytes of genomic data (DNA sequencing).
- **Finance**: Fraud detection through parallel transaction analysis.
- **IoT**: Processing sensor data streams from millions of devices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This section is the EXAMINATION GOLD MINE for KTU.** Demonstrating Hadoop involves: **(A) Installation, (B) HDFS Commands, (C) MapReduce WordCount Program, (D) Execution.**

## 3.1 Part A — Hadoop Installation in Pseudo-Distributed Mode

### Step 1: Verify Java Installation

```bash
$ java -version
openjdk version "11.0.x"  # Java 8 or 11 required
```

### Step 2: Download and Extract Hadoop

```bash
$ wget https://archive.apache.org/dist/hadoop/common/hadoop-3.3.6/hadoop-3.3.6.tar.gz
$ tar -xzf hadoop-3.3.6.tar.gz
$ sudo mv hadoop-3.3.6 /usr/local/hadoop
```

### Step 3: Configure Environment Variables (`~/.bashrc`)

```bash
export HADOOP_HOME=/usr/local/hadoop
export HADOOP_MAPRED_HOME=$HADOOP_HOME
export HADOOP_COMMON_HOME=$HADOOP_HOME
export HADOOP_HDFS_HOME=$HADOOP_HOME
export YARN_HOME=$HADOOP_HOME
export HADOOP_COMMON_LIB_NATIVE_DIR=$HADOOP_HOME/lib/native
export PATH=$PATH:$HADOOP_HOME/sbin:$HADOOP_HOME/bin
export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-11-openjdk-amd64
```

```bash
$ source ~/.bashrc
```

### Step 4: Configure Core Hadoop XML Files

**File 1: `$HADOOP_HOME/etc/hadoop/core-site.xml`**

```xml
<configuration>
    <property>
        <name>fs.defaultFS</name>
        <value>hdfs://localhost:9000</value>
    </property>
</configuration>
```

**File 2: `$HADOOP_HOME/etc/hadoop/hdfs-site.xml`**

```xml
<configuration>
    <property>
        <name>dfs.replication</name>
        <value>1</value>   <!-- Use 1 for pseudo-distributed mode -->
    </property>
    <property>
        <name>dfs.name.dir</name>
        <value>/usr/local/hadoop_tmp/hdfs/namenode</value>
    </property>
    <property>
        <name>dfs.data.dir</name>
        <value>/usr/local/hadoop_tmp/hdfs/datanode</value>
    </property>
</configuration>
```

**File 3: `$HADOOP_HOME/etc/hadoop/mapred-site.xml`**

```xml
<configuration>
    <property>
        <name>mapreduce.framework.name</name>
        <value>yarn</value>
    </property>
</configuration>
```

**File 4: `$HADOOP_HOME/etc/hadoop/yarn-site.xml`**

```xml
<configuration>
    <property>
        <name>yarn.nodemanager.aux-services</name>
        <value>mapreduce_shuffle</value>
    </property>
</configuration>
```

### Step 5: Format the NameNode (ONE-TIME Operation)

```bash
$ hdfs namenode -format
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Never run `hdfs namenode -format` twice on the same cluster** — it erases all HDFS metadata.

### Step 6: Start HDFS and YARN Daemons

```bash
$ start-dfs.sh
$ start-yarn.sh
```

### Step 7: Verify Running Java Processes

```bash
$ jps
1234 NameNode
1456 DataNode
1678 SecondaryNameNode
1890 ResourceManager
2010 NodeManager
```

If all 5 processes appear, Hadoop is successfully running.

## 3.2 Part B — Essential HDFS Commands (KTU High-Frequency)

| Operation | Command Syntax | Example |
|-----------|---------------|---------|
| Create directory | `hdfs dfs -mkdir /path` | `hdfs dfs -mkdir /input` |
| List files | `hdfs dfs -ls /path` | `hdfs dfs -ls /input` |
| Upload file (copyFromLocal) | `hdfs dfs -put <local> <hdfs>` | `hdfs dfs -put file.txt /input/` |
| Download file (copyToLocal) | `hdfs dfs -get <hdfs> <local>` | `hdfs dfs -get /output/result.txt ./` |
| View file contents | `hdfs dfs -cat /path/file` | `hdfs dfs -cat /input/file.txt` |
| Delete file | `hdfs dfs -rm /path/file` | `hdfs dfs -rm /output/result.txt` |
| Delete directory | `hdfs dfs -rm -r /path` | `hdfs dfs -rm -r /output` |
| Check filesystem | `hdfs dfs -df -h` | — |
| Check file size | `hdfs dfs -du -h /path` | `hdfs dfs -du -h /input/file.txt` |
| View block info | `hdfs fsck /path -files -blocks` | — |

## 3.3 Part C — The WordCount MapReduce Program (THE Classic KTU Question)

### 3.3.1 Java Implementation — `WordCount.java`

```java
import java.io.IOException;
import java.util.StringTokenizer;
import org.apache.hadoop.conf.Configuration;
import org.apache.hadoop.fs.Path;
import org.apache.hadoop.io.IntWritable;
import org.apache.hadoop.io.Text;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Job;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Mapper;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Reducer;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.lib.input.FileInputFormat;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.lib.output.FileOutputFormat;

public class WordCount {

    // ============ MAPPER CLASS ============
    public static class TokenizerMapper 
            extends Mapper<Object, Text, Text, IntWritable> {
        
        private final static IntWritable one = new IntWritable(1);
        private Text word = new Text();

        public void map(Object key, Text value, Context context)
                throws IOException, InterruptedException {
            StringTokenizer itr = new StringTokenizer(value.toString());
            while (itr.hasMoreTokens()) {
                word.set(itr.nextToken());
                context.write(word, one);
            }
        }
    }

    // ============ REDUCER CLASS ============
    public static class IntSumReducer 
            extends Reducer<Text, IntWritable, Text, IntWritable> {
        
        private IntWritable result = new IntWritable();

        public void reduce(Text key, Iterable<IntWritable> values, Context context)
                throws IOException, InterruptedException {
            int sum = 0;
            for (IntWritable val : values) {
                sum += val.get();
            }
            result.set(sum);
            context.write(key, result);
        }
    }

    // ============ MAIN DRIVER ============
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Configuration conf = new Configuration();
        Job job = Job.getInstance(conf, "word count");
        job.setJarByClass(WordCount.class);
        job.setMapperClass(TokenizerMapper.class);
        job.setReducerClass(IntSumReducer.class);
        job.setOutputKeyClass(Text.class);
        job.setOutputValueClass(IntWritable.class);
        FileInputFormat.addInputPath(job, new Path(args[0]));
        FileOutputFormat.setOutputPath(job, new Path(args[1]));
        System.exit(job.waitForCompletion(true) ? 0 : 1);
    }
}
```

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Execution Flow

**Step 1: Create input directory on HDFS**

```bash
$ hdfs dfs -mkdir /input
```

**Step 2: Create a local sample file `input.txt`**

```bash
$ echo "Hadoop is great. Hadoop is scalable. Hadoop is fault tolerant." > input.txt
```

**Step 3: Upload to HDFS**

```bash
$ hdfs dfs -put input.txt /input/
```

**Step 4: Compile the Java program**

```bash
$ hadoop com.sun.tools.javac.Main WordCount.java
$ jar cf wc.jar WordCount*.class
```

**Step 5: Execute the MapReduce job**

```bash
$ hadoop jar wc.jar WordCount /input /output
```

**Step 6: View the output**

```bash
$ hdfs dfs -cat /output/part-r-00000
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Hadoop    3
is        3
great.    1
scalable. 1
fault     1
tolerant. 1
```

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — MapReduce Data Flow for WordCount

> [!NOTE]
> **Trace the execution for input line:** `"Hadoop is great Hadoop"`
> 
> **Map Phase Output (per line):**
> $$\text{map}(\text{line}) \rightarrow [(\text{Hadoop},1), (\text{is},1), (\text{great},1), (\text{Hadoop},1)]$$
> 
> **Shuffle & Sort Phase (grouping by key):**
> $$\text{grouped} \rightarrow [(\text{Hadoop},[1,1]), (\text{great},[1]), (\text{is},[1])]$$
> 
> **Reduce Phase (summing values):**
> $$\text{reduce}(\text{Hadoop}, [1,1]) \rightarrow (\text{Hadoop}, 2)$$
> $$\text{reduce}(\text{great}, [1]) \rightarrow (\text{great}, 1)$$
> $$\text{reduce}(\text{is}, [1]) \rightarrow (\text{is}, 1)$$
> 
> **Final Output (written to HDFS):**
> $$\text{Hadoop} \rightarrow 2, \quad \text{great} \rightarrow 1, \quad \text{is} \rightarrow 1$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDFS Architecture (Master-Slave Topology)

```mermaid
graph TB
    clientApp[Client Application]
    nn[NameNode Master]
    snn[Secondary NameNode]
    dn1[DataNode 1]
    dn2[DataNode 2]
    dn3[DataNode 3]
    dn4[DataNode 4]
    metaStore[Metadata fsimage and edits]
    blockA[Block A Replica 1]
    blockA2[Block A Replica 2]
    blockA3[Block A Replica 3]
    
    clientApp -->|Read Write Requests| nn
    nn -->|Heartbeat every 3s| dn1
    nn -->|Heartbeat every 3s| dn2
    nn -->|Heartbeat every 3s| dn3
    nn -->|Heartbeat every 3s| dn4
    nn -.->|Stores| metaStore
    nn -.->|Checkpoints| snn
    snn -.->|Merges fsimage| nn
    dn1 --> blockA
    dn2 --> blockA2
    dn3 --> blockA3
```

## 4.2 MapReduce Job Execution Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    inputFile[Input File on HDFS]
    split[Input Splits]
    mapT1[Mapper Task 1]
    mapT2[Mapper Task 2]
    mapT3[Mapper Task 3]
    part[Partitioner]
    comb1[Combiner Optional]
    sort[Shuffle and Sort]
    redT1[Reducer Task 1]
    redT2[Reducer Task 2]
    output1[Output Part 1]
    output2[Output Part 2]
    hdfsOut[Output on HDFS]
    
    inputFile --> split
    split --> mapT1
    split --> mapT2
    split --> mapT3
    mapT1 --> comb1
    comb1 --> part
    mapT2 --> part
    mapT3 --> part
    part --> sort
    sort --> redT1
    sort --> redT2
    redT1 --> output1
    redT2 --> output2
    output1 --> hdfsOut
    output2 --> hdfsOut
```

## 4.3 YARN Architecture (Resource Management)

```mermaid
graph TB
    clientA[Client Submits Job]
    rm[ResourceManager]
    am[ApplicationMaster]
    nm1[NodeManager Node 1]
    nm2[NodeManager Node 2]
    c1[Container CPU and RAM]
    c2[Container CPU and RAM]
    c3[Container CPU and RAM]
    scheduler[Scheduler]
    appsManager[ApplicationsManager]
    
    clientA --> rm
    rm --> scheduler
    rm --> appsManager
    appsManager --> am
    am -->|Requests Containers| rm
    rm -->|Allocates| nm1
    rm -->|Allocates| nm2
    nm1 --> c1
    nm1 --> c2
    nm2 --> c3
    am -->|Runs in| c1
    am -->|Monitors| c2
    am -->|Monitors| c3
```

## 4.4 WordCount Data Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input: Hadoop is great Hadoop] --> B[Mapper Output]
    B --> C[Hadoop comma 1]
    B --> D[is comma 1]
    B --> E[great comma 1]
    B --> F[Hadoop comma 1]
    C --> G[Shuffle and Sort Group by Key]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Hadoop comma 1 comma 1]
    G --> I[great comma 1]
    G --> J[is comma 1]
    H --> K[Reducer Sum equals Hadoop 2]
    I --> L[Reducer Sum equals great 1]
    J --> M[Reducer Sum equals is 1]
    K --> N[Final Output part-r-00000]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the Hadoop framework. List any two advantages of HDFS over traditional file systems.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Apache Hadoop is an open-source distributed computing framework that uses the **HDFS** for storage and the **MapReduce** programming model for parallel processing of large datasets across clusters of commodity hardware.
> 
> **Two Advantages of HDFS (1 mark each — write any two):**
> 1. **Fault Tolerance:** Data is automatically replicated across multiple DataNodes (default replication factor = 3), ensuring no data loss on hardware failure.
> 2. **Scalability:** HDFS can scale horizontally by simply adding more DataNodes to the cluster (scalable to thousands of nodes and petabytes of data).
> 3. **Cost-Effective:** Runs on commodity hardware instead of expensive enterprise storage.
> 4. **Data Locality:** Computation is moved to the data, not the other way around, reducing network congestion.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between HDFS and MapReduce in Hadoop.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular Format Preferred by Examiners):**

| Parameter | HDFS | MapReduce |
|-----------|------|-----------|
| **Type** | Storage layer | Processing layer |
| **Function** | Stores data in distributed blocks | Processes data in parallel |
| **Architecture** | Master-Slave (NameNode + DataNodes) | Mapper + Reducer tasks |
| **Default Block Size** | 128 MB | Splits matching block size |
| **Key Operation** | `put`, `get`, `mkdir`, `cat` | `map()`, `reduce()` |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Data replication (factor 3) | Task re-execution on failure |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of HDFS. Describe the role of NameNode and DataNode. A file of size 1024 MB is stored in HDFS with block size 128 MB and replication factor 3. Calculate the total storage consumed and the number of blocks. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — HDFS Architecture & Components (7 Marks)

**HDFS Architecture Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TB
    client[Client]
    nn[NameNode Master]
    dn1[DataNode 1]
    dn2[DataNode 2]
    dn3[DataNode 3]
    fs[Metadata Storage]
    
    client -->|Read Write| nn
    nn -->|Heartbeat Block Reports| dn1
    nn -->|Heartbeat Block Reports| dn2
    nn -->|Heartbeat Block Reports| dn3
    nn -.->|fsimage edits| fs
```

**NameNode (Master) — Role (2 marks):**
1. Stores **metadata** of all files (file name, path, block locations, permissions, replication factor).
2. Manages the **filesystem namespace** and regulates client file access.
3. Receives **heartbeat signals** (every 3 seconds) and **block reports** (every 6 hours) from all DataNodes.
4. Decides placement of replicas based on **rack-awareness policy**.
5. Runs on high-memory, high-end hardware (typically 64–128 GB RAM).

**DataNode (Slave) — Role (2 marks):**
1. Stores the actual **data blocks** on local disk.
2. Performs **read/write operations** as instructed by the NameNode.
3. Sends periodic **heartbeats** to confirm liveness.
4. Sends **block reports** listing all blocks stored on that node.
5. If a DataNode fails to send a heartbeat for **10 minutes**, the NameNode marks it as **dead** and initiates re-replication of its blocks.

#### Part (b) — Storage Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- File size: $1024$ MB
- Block size: $128$ MB
- Replication factor: $R = 3$

**Step 1: Calculate Number of Blocks (3 marks)**

Using the formula:
$$B = \lceil \vert \text{FileSize} \vert \,/\, \vert \text{BlockSize} \vert \rceil$$

Substituting the values:
$$B = \lceil 1024 \,/\, 128 \rceil = \lceil 8 \rceil = 8 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total Storage Consumed (3 marks)**

Using the formula:
$$U = B \times R \times \text{BlockSize}$$

Substituting the values:
$$U = 8 \times 3 \times 128 = 3072 \text{ MB}$$

Converting to GB:
$$U = 3072 \,/\, 1024 = 3 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 3: Final Result (1 mark)**

> **Final Answer:** Number of blocks = **8**, Total HDFS storage consumed = **3072 MB (3 GB)**, Replication overhead = **2048 MB (2 GB)**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the MapReduce programming model. Write the Mapper and Reducer pseudo-code (or Java code) for the WordCount program and trace its execution for the input: "Big Data Big Hadoop". (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — MapReduce Architecture & Phases (7 Marks)

**MapReduce Flow Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Split] --> B[Mapper]
    B --> C[Intermediate KV Pairs]
    C --> D[Combiner Optional]
    D --> E[Partitioner]
    E --> F[Shuffle and Sort]
    F --> G[Reducer]
    G --> H[Final Output]
```

**Phases of MapReduce (2 marks each):**
1. **Input Splitting:** Input is divided into fixed-size splits (typically matching HDFS block size = 128 MB).
2. **Mapping:** Each split is processed by a Mapper, emitting key-value pairs in the form $(K_2, V_2)$.
3. **Shuffling & Sorting:** Framework groups all values for the same key and sorts them.
4. **Reducing:** Reducer aggregates the grouped values to produce the final output $(K_3, V_3)$.
5. **Writing Output:** Final result is written back to HDFS as `part-r-00000`, `part-r-00001`, etc.

#### Part (b) — WordCount Code & Execution Trace (7 Marks)

**Mapper Pseudo-Code (2 marks):**

```java
map(key, value):
    for each word w in value.split(" "):
        emit(w, 1)
```

**Reducer Pseudo-Code (2 marks):**

```java
reduce(key, values):
    sum = 0
    for each v in values:
        sum = sum + v
    emit(key, sum)
```

**Execution Trace for Input `"Big Data Big Hadoop"` (3 marks):**

**Map Phase Output (per token):**
$$\text{map}(\text{Big}) \rightarrow (\text{Big}, 1)$$
$$\text{map}(\text{Data}) \rightarrow (\text{Data}, 1)$$
$$\text{map}(\text{Big}) \rightarrow (\text{Big}, 1)$$
$$\text{map}(\text{Hadoop}) \rightarrow (\text{Hadoop}, 1)$$

**Shuffle & Sort (Grouped by Key):**
$$(\text{Big}, [1, 1]), \quad (\text{Data}, [1]), \quad (\text{Hadoop}, [1])$$

**Reduce Phase Output:**
$$\text{reduce}(\text{Big}, [1,1]) \rightarrow (\text{Big}, 2)$$
$$\text{reduce}(\text{Data}, [1]) \rightarrow (\text{Data}, 1)$$
$$\text{reduce}(\text{Hadoop}, [1]) \rightarrow (\text{Hadoop}, 1)$$

**Final Output Stored in `/output/part-r-00000`:**
$$\text{Big} \rightarrow 2, \quad \text{Data} \rightarrow 1, \quad \text{Hadoop} \rightarrow 1$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `hdfs namenode -format`**: Submitting the job without formatting causes `InconsistentFSStateException` — **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Using `/output` as both input and output path**: HDFS does not allow an existing non-empty directory as output. Always delete the output dir first using `hdfs dfs -rm -r /output` — **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing `put` with `copyFromLocal`**: Both work, but writing `hdfs dfs -copyFromLocal` without configuring correctly loses marks in viva — **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Missing `setJarByClass()` in the Driver code**: Causes `ClassNotFoundException` at runtime — **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Wrong block size in calculations**: Students often use 64 MB (Hadoop 1.x default) instead of 128 MB (Hadoop 2.x/3.x default) — **−2 marks**.
> 6. **Not explaining data locality**: Examiners expect you to mention "MapReduce moves computation to data, not data to computation" — **−1 mark**.
> 7. **Forgetting the Partitioner formula** $P(\text{key}) = \text{hash}(\text{key}) \bmod \vert R \vert$ — direct −2 marks in 14-mark questions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minent Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION — Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall**

### **Key Concepts to Memorize**
- [ ] Hadoop = **HDFS (Storage) + MapReduce (Processing) + YARN (Resource Management)**
- [ ] HDFS default block size = **128 MB** (NOT 64 MB)
- [ ] Default replication factor = **3**
- [ ] NameNode stores **metadata only**; DataNode stores **actual data**
- [ ] Heartbeat interval = **3 seconds**; Block report = **6 hours**; DN timeout = **10 minutes**
- [ ] MapReduce has **2 main functions**: `map()` and `reduce()` (+ optional `combiner()`)
- [ ] WordCount is the **Hello World of Hadoop** — must know the trace by heart
- [ ] HDFS uses **write-once, read-many (WORM)** model
- [ ] MapReduce principle: **"Move computation to data, not data to computation"**
- [ ] YARN was introduced in **Hadoop 2.0** to overcome scalability limits of Hadoop 1.x

### **Essential HDFS Commands (Memorize All 10)**
- [ ] `hdfs dfs -mkdir /path`
- [ ] `hdfs dfs -ls /path`
- [ ] `hdfs dfs -put <local> <hdfs>`
- [ ] `hdfs dfs -get <hdfs> <local>`
- [ ] `hdfs dfs -cat /path/file`
- [ ] `hdfs dfs -rm /path/file`
- [ ] `hdfs dfs -rm -r /path/dir`
- [ ] `hdfs dfs -df -h`
- [ ] `hdfs dfs -du -h /path`
- [ ] `hdfs fsck /path -files -blocks`

### **Critical Formulas (Final 1-Minute Glance)**
- [ ] Number of blocks: $B = \lceil \vert \text{FileSize} \vert \,/\, \vert \text{BlockSize} \vert \rceil$
- [ ] Total storage used: $U = B \times R \times \text{BlockSize}$
- [ ] Partitioner: $P(\text{key}) = \text{hash}(\text{key}) \bmod \vert R \vert$

### **Common Exam Traps**
- [ ] **Trap 1:** "Hadoop is a database" → **WRONG** (it's a distributed computing framework)
- [ ] **Trap 2:** "NameNode stores the actual data" → **WRONG** (it stores metadata)
- [ ] **Trap 3:** "MapReduce processes data in serial" → **WRONG** (it's parallel)
- [ ] **Trap 4:** "YARN was in Hadoop 1.x" → **WRONG** (introduced in Hadoop 2.x)
- [ ] **Trap 5:** "Block size is 64 MB in Hadoop 3.x" → **WRONG** (it's 128 MB)

### **Viva-Voce Quick-Fire Questions**
- [ ] Q: What is rack awareness? → **A: HDFS policy to place one replica on a local rack and another on a different rack to balance reliability and bandwidth.**
- [ ] Q: What is a Combiner? → **A: A mini-reducer that runs on the mapper node for local aggregation to reduce network traffic.**
- [ ] Q: What is a Partitioner? → **A: It decides which Reducer receives a given key using `hash(key) mod R`.**
- [ ] Q: What is speculative execution? → **A: Running a slow task a second time on another node to finish faster — uses the first one to complete.**
- [ ] Q: Name 4 Hadoop ecosystem tools. → **A: HDFS, MapReduce, YARN, Hive, Pig, HBase, Sqoop, Flume, Spark, ZooKeeper.**

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — PBADT504 Big Data Analytics (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Apache Hadoop — The Foundation Framework

**Apache Hadoop** is an open-source framework that allows for the **distributed storage and distributed processing** of very large datasets across clusters of commodity computers using simple programming models. It is engineered to scale up from a single server to thousands of machines, each offering local computation and storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Hadoop is a framework that permits the distributed processing of large data sets across clusters of computers using a simple programming model (MapReduce). It is designed to scale up from a single server to thousands of machines, with high fault tolerance.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of Hadoop like a **massive library with thousands of librarians**:
- **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** is the library's storage system — it splits a giant encyclopedia into smaller volumes and stores them in different library branches, with multiple copies of each volume for safety.
- **MapReduce** is the librarian workflow — one set of librarians (Mappers) reads through each volume and extracts relevant sentences, then another set (Reducers) combines those sentences into a final summary report.
- Instead of one librarian struggling through 10,000 books alone, hundreds work in parallel, finish in minutes, and if one gets sick, the system still works.

---

## 1.2 Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)

**HDFS** is a **Java-based, fault-tolerant, distributed, scalable, and write-once-read-many** file system designed to store very large files (terabytes to petabytes) across multiple machines in a Hadoop cluster.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Design Assumptions of HDFS:**
> 1. **Hardware Failure** is the norm, not the exception (every component fails eventually).
> 2. **Streaming Data Access** — designed for batch processing, not interactive use.
> 3. **Large Data Sets** — files are typically **100 MB to several GB** in size.
> 4. **Simple Coherency Model** — write-once, read-many access (WORM model).
> 5. **Commodity Hardware** — runs on low-cost, easily available machines.

### Key HDFS Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)
- **Default Block Size: 128 MB** (in Hadoop 2.x and 3.x; was **64 MB** in Hadoop 1.x).
- **Default Replication Factor: 3** (one original + two replicas).
- **Heartbeat Interval: 3 seconds** (DataNode sends heartbeat to NameNode).
- **Block Report Interval: 6 hours** (default in Hadoop 2.x).

---

## 1.3 MapReduce — The Programming Model

**MapReduce** is a **parallel and distributed programming paradigm** for processing massive datasets in two primary phases: a **Map** phase (filtering and sorting) and a **Reduce** phase (aggregation and summarization). It is the processing engine that operates over data stored in HDFS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Core Concept:** MapReduce is a software framework that allows developers to write programs that process massive amounts of unstructured data in parallel across a distributed cluster of processors or stand-alone computers.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine counting the number of occurrences of every word in a 10,000-page encyclopedia:
- **Map Phase (Parallelization):** Assign one chapter to each of 100 students. Each student counts words in their chapter and creates a list of `(word, count)` pairs. This happens **in parallel**.
- **Shuffle & Sort Phase:** All pairs with the same word (e.g., all "Hadoop" entries) are **grouped together** and routed to the same Reducer. This is automatic.
- **Reduce Phase (Aggregation):** Each of 5 students receives a subset of unique words and **sums up** all the counts to get a final `(word, total_count)` output. This happens **in parallel**.
- **Final Output:** The 5 students' outputs are combined into a single dictionary of word frequencies.

---

## 1.4 Apache Pig — High-Level Abstraction over MapReduce

**Apache Pig** is a **platform for analyzing large data sets** that sits on top of Hadoop. It consists of:
1. A high-level language called **Pig Latin** for expressing data analysis programs.
2. An infrastructure for evaluating these programs (compiler that translates Pig Latin → MapReduce jobs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Pig?** Writing raw MapReduce code in Java is **verbose, complex, and slow** to develop. Pig Latin provides a **SQL-like scripting language** that automatically converts to optimized MapReduce jobs, reducing development time by an order of magnitude.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of Pig as **"the friendly translator"**:
- You write a simple English-like script: *"Load sales data, filter out refunds, group by region, sum the revenue, store the result."*
- Pig's **Compiler** translates it into hundreds of lines of Java MapReduce code automatically.
- You, the analyst, focus on the **business logic**, not the technical plumbing.

---

## 1.5 KTU 2024 Module Experiment 2 — Scope

| Component | Role in Experiment | KTU CO Mapping |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| **HDFS Commands** | Create directories, upload files, view blocks, set replication | CO2, K3 |
| **MapReduce WordCount** | Implement, compile, execute on HDFS data | CO2, K3 |
| **Pig Latin Script** | Load HDFS data, transform, group, aggregate | CO2, K3 |
| **Simulation** | Run in Cloudera VM / Hortonworks sandbox | CO2, K3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU CO2 Statement:** *Simulate various Big data technologies like Pig, Hive, HBase, and Sqoop to process and analyze large datasets.*
> **Cognitive Level K3:** APPLY — Students must execute commands, run scripts, and interpret outputs.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 HDFS Architecture — The Master/Slave Paradigm

HDFS follows a **Master/Slave (NameNode/DataNode) architecture** to coordinate storage and retrieval across the cluster.

### 2.1.1 NameNode (Master Daemon)
- **Role:** Stores metadata (directory tree, file-to-block mapping, block locations).
- **Files maintained:**
  - `fsimage` → Snapshot of filesystem metadata at a checkpoint.
  - `edit logs` → Sequence of changes made to the filesystem since the last fsimage.
- **Runs on:** High-memory commodity hardware (RAM-intensive).
- **In-memory data structures:** Maps each file → list of its blocks → list of DataNodes hosting them.

### 2.1.2 DataNode (Slave Daemon)
- **Role:** Stores the **actual data blocks** on local disk.
- **Lifecycle signals:** Sends **heartbeat** (every 3 seconds) + **Block Report** (every 6 hours) to NameNode.
- **Runs on:** Standard commodity hardware.
- **Block storage path:** `${dfs.datanode.data.dir}/current/`

### 2.1.3 Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node)
- **Role:** Periodically **merges fsimage + edit logs** to prevent edit logs from growing unbounded.
- **Important:** It is **NOT a backup** NameNode; it cannot serve requests if NameNode fails.
- **Merge trigger:** When `dfs.namenode.checkpoint.period` (default 1 hour) elapses OR `dfs.namenode.checkpoint.txns` (default 1 million transactions) reached.

### 2.1.4 High Availability (HA) in Hadoop 2.x+
- **Active NameNode** and **Standby NameNode** — the Standby takes over automatically using **ZooKeeper** if the Active fails.

---

## 2.2 HDFS Block, Replication & Capacity Formulas

> [!NOTE]
> **Block:** The smallest unit of data that HDFS reads/writes. A file is split into fixed-size blocks distributed across DataNodes.

### 2.2.1 Block Calculation
If a file of size **S MB** is stored in HDFS with block size **B MB** and replication factor **R**:

- **Number of blocks (n)** = $\lceil S \div B \rceil$ (ceiling division)
- **Total physical storage** = $n \times B \times R$ MB
- **Logical storage** = $S$ MB
- **Storage overhead** = $(n \times B \times R) - S$ MB (wasted space in last block)

### 2.2.2 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit |
|---|----------------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $n = \lceil S \div B \rceil$ | Number of HDFS blocks for a file of size S | blocks |
| 2 | $T_{phys} = n \times B \times R$ | Total physical storage consumed | MB |
| 3 | $T_{log} = S$ | Logical (user-visible) file size | MB |
| 4 | $O = T_{phys} - T_{log}$ | Storage overhead due to replication | MB |
| 5 | $H_{disk} = \frac{T_{phys}}{D}$ | Required disk capacity per DataNode (D = # of DNs) | MB |
| 6 | $M_{NameNode} \approx \frac{F \times n \times 40 \text{ bytes}}{10^6}$ | NameNode RAM estimation (F = # files) | GB |
| 7 | $HB_{timeout} = 10 \times 3 \text{ s} = 30 \text{ s}$ | DataNode declared dead if no heartbeat | seconds |
| 8 | $R_{effective} \leq N_{DataNodes}$ | Replication factor limited by available DataNodes | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Block Size Constants:**
> - Hadoop 1.x: **64 MB**
> - Hadoop 2.x onwards: **128 MB** (default), configurable up to **512 MB** or more
> - Default Replication Factor: **3**
> - Minimum Replication: **1** (HDFS cannot store with RF=0)

---

## 2.3 HDFS File Read Operation — Step-by-Step

1. **Client** invokes `FileSystem.open(path)` → HDFS client library contacts the **NameNode**.
2. **NameNode** checks file existence, permissions, and returns a list of block locations (DataNodes ordered by proximity).
3. Client opens an **InputStream** and connects to the **nearest DataNode** holding the first block.
4. DataNode streams the block data; checksum is verified packet-by-packet.
5. When one block is fully read, client transparently connects to the next block's DataNode.
6. **FSDataInputStream** keeps the connection open; if the current DataNode fails, the client transparently connects to the next replica without notifying the user.
7. Upon close, the connection to the NameNode is terminated.

## 2.4 HDFS File Write Operation — Step-by-Step

1. **Client** invokes `FileSystem.create(path)` → contacts **NameNode**.
2. NameNode performs **permission checks** and creates a new file entry in metadata (initially empty).
3. NameNode allocates blocks and returns a list of DataNode replicas (forming a **replication pipeline**).
4. Client writes data to the **first DataNode**, which forwards to the **second**, which forwards to the **third** (pipeline write).
5. Each DataNode stages the block on local disk; ack flows back up the pipeline.
6. After all blocks are written, client calls `close()`; NameNode finalizes the file metadata.
7. **Replication factor can be set** at any time using `hdfs dfs -setrep`. Files in HDFS are **write-once** (no random writes supported).

---

## 2.5 MapReduce — The Programming Model in Detail

### 2.5.1 The Two Core Functions

$$
\text{Map: } (K_1, V_1) \rightarrow \text{list of } (K_2, V_2)
$$

$$
\text{Reduce: } (K_2, \text{list of } (V_2)) \rightarrow \text{list of } (V_3)
$$

| Phase | Input | Output | Operation |
|-------|-------|--------|-----------|
| **Map** | $(K_1, V_1)$ | $\text{list}(K_2, V_2)$ | Transforms, filters, parses input |
| **Shuffle & Sort** | $\text{list}(K_2, V_2)$ | $(K_2, \text{list}(V_2))$ grouped by key | Hadoop framework responsibility |
| **Reduce** | $(K_2, \text{list}(V_2))$ | $\text{list}(K_3, V_3)$ | Aggregates, summarizes, transforms |

### 2.5.2 MapReduce Architecture Components

| Component | Role | Runs On |
|-----------|------|---------|
| **JobClient** | Submits job, monitors progress | Client machine |
| **JobTracker** (Hadoop 1.x) / **ResourceManager** (YARN) | Schedules and tracks jobs | Master node |
| **TaskTracker** (Hadoop 1.x) / **NodeManager** (YARN) | Executes map/reduce tasks | Slave node |
| **Map Task** | Runs the `map()` function | Data-local DataNode |
| **Reduce Task** | Runs the `reduce()` function | Assigned NodeManager |

### 2.5.3 Data Locality Optimization
Hadoop prefers to schedule **Map tasks on the DataNode that physically holds the input block**. This minimizes network traffic. Locality levels:
- **Node-local** (same node) — preferred
- **Rack-local** (same rack) — acceptable
- **Off-rack** (different rack) — fallback

---

## 2.6 Apache Pig — Architecture & Execution Modes

### 2.6.1 Pig Latin Execution Modes

| Mode | Execution Environment | Use Case |
|------|----------------------|----------|
| **Local Mode** | Single JVM, local filesystem | Development, testing small datasets |
| **MapReduce Mode** | Hadoop cluster, HDFS | Production big-data jobs (converts Pig Latin → MapReduce) |
| **Tez Mode** | Apache Tez engine on HDFS | Faster DAG-based execution |
| **Spark Mode** | Apache Spark on HDFS | In-memory execution, even faster |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Experiment Focus:** Students typically run Pig in **MapReduce mode** on the **Cloudera QuickStart VM** to see actual job submission in the YARN ResourceManager UI.

### 2.6.2 Pig Latin Operator Categories

| Category | Operators | Purpose |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| **Loading/Storing** | `LOAD`, `STORE`, `DUMP` | Read/write data |
| **Filtering** | `FILTER`, `DISTINCT`, `SAMPLE` | Remove unwanted rows |
| **Grouping/Joins** | `GROUP`, `COGROUP`, `JOIN`, `CROSS` | Combine relations |
| **Transformation** | `FOREACH ... GENERATE`, `MAPREDUCE` | Apply functions to rows |
| **Sorting/Limiting** | `ORDER`, `LIMIT` | Order and restrict output |
| **Combining/Splitting** | `UNION`, `SPLIT` | Merge or split relations |
| **Diagnostic** | `DESCRIBE`, `EXPLAIN`, `ILLUSTRATE` | Debug/inspect |

### 2.6.3 Pig Data Types (Key)

| Type | Example |
|------|---------|
| `int`, `long`, `float`, `double` | Scalar numerics |
| `chararray` | String (UTF-8) |
| `bytearray` | Binary blob |
| `boolean` | True / False |
| `datetime` | ISO 8601 timestamps |
| `tuple` | Ordered set of fields: `('Alice', 30)` |
| `bag` | Unordered collection of tuples: `{('Alice',30), ('Bob',25)}` |
| `map` | Key-value pairs: `['name'#'Alice','age'#30]` |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Technology | Industry Application |
|------------|----------------------|
| **HDFS** | Data lake storage for retail transaction logs, IoT sensor streams, genomic data |
| **MapReduce** | ETL pipelines, log analytics, clickstream analysis, indexing for search engines |
| **Pig Latin** | Ad-hoc data exploration, clickstream analysis, social media sentiment pre-processing |
| **HDFS+MapReduce+Pig** | Foundation of Yahoo's search indexing, Facebook's friend recommendations, Netflix's content recommendation engine |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always mention **fault tolerance** and **data locality** as the two key reasons HDFS is preferred over traditional distributed file systems like NFS for big data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of HDFS Blocks for a File

### Problem
A file of size **$S = 530$ MB** is stored in HDFS with a default block size of **$B = 128$ MB** and replication factor **$R = 3$**. Compute:
1. Number of blocks
2. Total physical storage consumed
3. Storage overhead
4. Storage per DataNode (assuming 4 DataNodes)
5. NameNode RAM required (estimate for 100 such files)

---

### Step 1: Number of Blocks

We use the ceiling division formula:

$$
n = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil
$$

Substitute $S = 530$ and $B = 128$:

$$
n = \left\lceil \frac{530}{128} \right\rceil
$$

$$
\frac{530}{128} = 4.1406
$$

$$
n = \left\lceil 4.1406 \right\rceil = 5 \text{ blocks}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Five blocks are required. Four blocks are full (128 MB each = 512 MB), and the fifth block holds the remaining 18 MB.

---

### Step 2: Total Physical Storage

$$
T_{phys} = n \times B \times R
$$

$$
T_{phys} = 5 \times 128 \times 3
$$

$$
T_{phys} = 1920 \text{ MB}
$$

---

### Step 3: Storage Overhead

$$
T_{log} = S = 530 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
O = T_{phys} - T_{log}
$$

$$
O = 1920 - 530 = 1390 \text{ MB}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The 1390 MB overhead is the "wasted" space. Of this, **4 × 128 MB × 2 = 1024 MB** is from the four full blocks (2 extra replicas each), and **18 MB × 2 = 36 MB** is from the partially-filled last block. The remaining **330 MB** = 4 full blocks × 128 MB × R − 4 full blocks × 128 MB comes from the under-filled last block (110 MB unused × 3 replicas).

---

### Step 4: Storage per DataNode

Assume 4 DataNodes; HDFS distributes blocks pseudo-randomly (each block + its replicas on distinct DNs):

$$
H_{disk} = \frac{T_{phys}}{D} = \frac{1920}{4} = 480 \text{ MB per DataNode}
$$

---

### Step 5: NameNode RAM Estimate

For 100 such files ($F = 100$):

$$
M_{NameNode} \approx \frac{F \times n \times 40 \text{ bytes}}{10^6}
$$

$$
M_{NameNode} \approx \frac{100 \times 5 \times 40}{10^6}
$$

$$
M_{NameNode} \approx \frac{20000}{10^6} = 0.02 \text{ MB} = 20 \text{ KB}
$$

> **Note:** This is a rough estimate. Real NameNode memory scales with files × blocks × replicas and the average object size, often using the heuristic of **1 GB of NameNode heap per 1 million blocks**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: MapReduce Data Flow for WordCount

### Input
A text file (line by line) stored in HDFS.

### Algorithm Trace

**Input Lines (Split 1 — handled by Mapper 1):**
```
"Deer Bear River"
"Car Car River"
"Deer Car Bear"
```

**Step 1: `map()` Phase**

For each word in each line, emit `(word, 1)$:

$$
\text{Mapper 1 output: } \{(\text{Deer},1), (\text{Bear},1), (\text{River},1), (\text{Car},1), (\text{Car},1), (\text{River},1), (\text{Deer},1), (\text{Car},1), (\text{Bear},1)\}
$$

**Step 2: Combiner (Local Aggregation, Optional)**

The Combiner runs on the Mapper node to reduce data shuffled:

$$
\text{After Combiner: } \{(\text{Deer},2), (\text{Bear},2), (\text{River},2), (\text{Car},3)\}
$$

**Step 3: Shuffle & Sort (Framework-Managed)**

Keys are sorted and grouped:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bear} &\rightarrow [1, 1] \\
\text{Car} &\rightarrow [1, 1, 1] \\
\text{Deer} &\rightarrow [1, 1] \\
\text{River} &\rightarrow [1, 1]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: `reduce()` Phase**

For each unique key, sum the values:

$$
\text{Reducer output: } \{(\text{Bear},2), (\text{Car},3), (\text{Deer},2), (\text{River},2)\}
$$

---

### Final Output (HDFS):
```
Bear    2
Car     3
Deer    2
River   2
```

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: MapReduce Job Latency Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> The total time for a MapReduce job is bounded by the **slowest task** (the "straggler problem"). A simple model is:

$$
T_{job} = T_{map} + T_{shuffle} + T_{reduce}
$$

### Step-by-Step

$$
T_{map} = \max_{i=1}^{M}(t_{map,i})
$$

$$
T_{shuffle} = \frac{\text{Total shuffled data}}{\text{Network bandwidth}}
$$

$$
T_{reduce} = \max_{j=1}^{R}(t_{reduce,j})
$$

Where:
- $M$ = number of map tasks
- $R$ = number of reduce tasks
- $t_{map,i}$ = execution time of the $i^{th}$ map task
- $t_{reduce,j}$ = execution time of the $j^{th}$ reduce task

---

### Numerical Example
A job has 10 map tasks (each processes 1 GB) and 3 reduce tasks. Each map task takes ~60 seconds, network shuffle is 2 GB at 100 MB/s, each reduce task takes ~90 seconds.

$$
T_{map} = \max(60, 60, ..., 60) = 60 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{shuffle} = \frac{2 \times 1024 \text{ MB}}{100 \text{ MB/s}} = 20.48 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{reduce} = \max(90, 90, 90) = 90 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{job} = 60 + 20.48 + 90 = 170.48 \text{ seconds}
$$

> **KTU Insight:** If one map task is a "straggler" taking 600 seconds (e.g., on a slow DataNode), the entire job is bottlenecked. Hadoop mitigates this with **speculative execution** — launching a duplicate of the slow task and using whichever finishes first.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pig Latin vs MapReduce vs Hive

| Criteria | Pig Latin | Raw MapReduce (Java) | Hive (SQL) |
|----------|-----------|----------------------|------------|
| **Lines of code** for WordCount | ~5 lines | ~60+ lines (Java) | ~5 lines (SQL) |
| **Learning curve** | Low (scripting) | High (Java, Hadoop API) | Low (SQL-like) |
| **Execution engine** | Translates to MapReduce / Tez / Spark | Direct MapReduce | Translates to MapReduce / Tez / Spark |
| **Optimization** | Automatic (rule-based optimizer) | Manual | Cost-based optimizer (CBO) |
| **Best for** | ETL, data pipelines, ad-hoc analysis | Complex custom logic | Batch SQL analytics, BI tools |
| **Schema** | Schema-on-read (flexible) | Schema-on-read | Schema-on-read (with metastore) |
| **Latency** | Higher (batch) | Higher (batch) | Higher (batch) |
| **User persona** | Data engineers / scientists | Hadoop developers | Analysts / DBAs |

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Storage Calculation with HDFS Trash

HDFS has a **trash mechanism** similar to a recycle bin. The default retention is **6 hours** (configurable via `fs.trash.interval`).

### Effective Usable Storage

$$
S_{usable} = S_{total} - S_{used} - S_{trash}
$$

Where:
- $S_{total}$ = Total cluster disk capacity
- $S_{used}$ = Currently occupied by live data
- $S_{trash}$ = Files in `.Trash` directory awaiting deletion

> **Example:** Cluster of 4 DataNodes × 1 TB = 4 TB total. If 2.5 TB is used and 300 GB is in trash:
> 
> $$
> S_{usable} = 4000 - 2500 - 300 = 1200 \text{ GB}
> $$

## 3.7 Derivation 5: Replication Pipeline Bandwidth

When writing a block, HDFS uses a **replication pipeline** — the first DN writes to local disk while forwarding the packet to the second DN, which forwards to the third.

### Effective Write Throughput

$$
W_{eff} = \frac{B_{link}}{R}
$$

Where $B_{link}$ is the per-hop link bandwidth and $R$ is the replication factor.

### Example
- Network: 1 Gbps (≈ 125 MB/s)
- $R = 3$

$$
W_{eff} = \frac{125}{3} \approx 41.67 \text{ MB/s per file}
$$

> **Trade-off:** Higher replication factor → Higher fault tolerance, but **lower effective write throughput**.

## 4.1 HDFS Architecture (Master/Slave Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ClientA[Client Application 1]
    ClientB[Client Application 2]
    NameNode[NameNode Master]
    SNameNode[Secondary NameNode Checkpoint]
    DataNode1[DataNode 1]
    DataNode2[DataNode 2]
    DataNode3[DataNode 3]
    DataNode4[DataNode 4]
    FSImage[(fsimage + EditLogs)]
    Block1[(Block A 128MB)]
    Block2[(Block B 128MB)]
    Block3[(Block C 128MB)]

    ClientA -->|1. RPC open/ read write| NameNode
    ClientB -->|1. RPC metadata request| NameNode
    NameNode -->|2. block locations list| ClientA
    NameNode -.->|merge checkpoint| SNameNode
    NameNode -->|metadata read write| FSImage
    ClientA -->|3. direct data transfer| DataNode1
    ClientA -->|3. direct data transfer| DataNode2
    DataNode1 -->|3. replica write| DataNode2
    DataNode2 -->|3. replica write| DataNode3
    DataNode1 -.->|block A stored| Block1
    DataNode2 -.->|block B stored| Block2
    DataNode3 -.->|block C stored| Block3
    DataNode4 -.->|replica of A| Block1
    DataNode1 -->|heartbeat 3s block report 6h| NameNode
    DataNode2 -->|heartbeat 3s block report 6h| NameNode
    DataNode3 -->|heartbeat 3s block report 6h| NameNode
    DataNode4 -->|heartbeat 3s block report 6h| NameNode
```

## 4.2 HDFS File Write Pipeline (Block Replication)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Client[Client] -->|1 Write packet| DN1[DataNode 1]
    DN1 -->|2 Forward packet| DN2[DataNode 2]
    DN2 -->|3 Forward packet| DN3[DataNode 3]
    DN3 -->|4 Ack success| DN2
    DN2 -->|5 Ack success| DN1
    DN1 -->|6 Ack to client| Client
    DN1 -.->|stored| Disk1[(Local Disk 1)]
    DN2 -.->|stored| Disk2[(Local Disk 2)]
    DN3 -.->|stored| Disk3[(Local Disk 3)]
```

## 4.3 HDFS File Read Operation Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant NN as NameNode
    participant DN1 as DataNode 1
    participant DN2 as DataNode 2
    participant DN3 as DataNode 3

    C->>NN: 1. open(path) - request metadata
    NN-->>C: 2. Return ordered list of block locations
    C->>DN1: 3. read(block_1) - nearest DN
    DN1-->>C: 4. Stream block_1 data
    Note over C,DN1: Checksum verified per packet
    C->>DN2: 5. read(block_2) - next block
    DN2-->>C: 6. Stream block_2 data
    alt DN2 fails during read
        C->>DN3: 5a. Transparent retry on next replica
        DN3-->>C: 5b. Resume from failed offset
    end
    C->>NN: 7. close() - release connection
```
