## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part A questions test **K1 (Remember) and K2 (Understand)** cognitive levels. Answers should be precise, 3–5 sentences, and reference core terminology.

### Question 1: HDFS Architecture  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`

**Q: Explain the role of NameNode and DataNode in HDFS. Why is HDFS designed as a Master/Slave architecture?**

**Model Answer:**

The **NameNode** is the master daemon that manages the metadata of the HDFS filesystem. It stores the directory tree of all files, maintains the mapping of files to their constituent blocks, and tracks the location of each block replica across the cluster. The NameNode also enforces access control and decides on block placement.

The **DataNode** is the slave daemon that runs on every worker machine and stores the **actual data blocks** on its local disk. Each DataNode periodically sends a **heartbeat** (every 3 seconds) and a **block report** (every 6 hours) to the NameNode to confirm it is alive and to inform it of the blocks it hosts.

HDFS follows a **Master/Slave architecture** because:
1. **Centralized metadata management** at the NameNode simplifies the design and ensures consistency.
2. **Data is stored in a distributed manner** across DataNodes, allowing horizontal scaling to petabytes.
3. **Separation of concerns** — metadata is small (fits in RAM) while data is large (stored on disk), enabling fast name lookups and parallel data access.
4. The design optimizes for the use case of **batch processing of large files** rather than low-latency random access.

> **KTU Examiner's Tip (3 marks):** Award 1 mark each for NameNode role, DataNode role, and 1 mark for clearly stating why Master/Slave is used. Drawing a small diagram adds value.

---

### Question 2: MapReduce Fundamentals  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**Q: Differentiate between the Map phase and the Reduce phase in a MapReduce program. What is the role of the Combiner?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Map phase** is the first phase of MapReduce, executed in parallel on each input split. The `map()` function transforms input key-value pairs $(K_1, V_1)$ into intermediate key-value pairs $(K_2, V_2)$. It performs **local transformation, filtering, and parsing** on each record. Multiple map tasks run simultaneously across different DataNodes to leverage data locality.

The **Reduce phase** is the second phase, executed after the Shuffle & Sort. The `reduce()` function receives all intermediate values for each unique key $(K_2, \text{list}(V_2))$ and aggregates them to produce final output $(K_3, V_3)$. It performs **summary operations** like sum, count, max, or complex joins.

The **Combiner** is an **optional, local mini-reducer** that runs on the output of the Map phase **on the same node**, before the data is shuffled across the network. It performs a local aggregation (e.g., partial sum) to **reduce the volume of data transferred** between Map and Reduce, thereby decreasing network congestion and improving job performance. The Combiner's function must be **associative and commutative** (e.g., sum, max) to be safe.

> **KTU Examiner's Tip (3 marks):** 1 mark for Map role, 1 mark for Reduce role, 1 mark for Combiner role and its constraint (associative/commutative).

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`

#### (a) HDFS Operations and Block Management  (7 Marks)

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the HDFS architecture. A 640 MB file is stored in HDFS with a default block size of 128 MB and a replication factor of 3. Calculate the number of blocks, total physical storage, and storage overhead. If the cluster has 4 DataNodes, how much disk space is required per DataNode on average?**

**Model Solution:**

**HDFS Architecture (Diagram + Explanation — 3 marks):**

```
   CLIENT                NAMENODE              DATANODES
  ┌──────┐  metadata    ┌──────────┐  heartbeat ┌──────────┐
  │ App  │ ──────────>  │ Master   │ <───────── │ Slave 1  │ Block A
  │      │ <──────────  │          │ <───────── │ Slave 2  │ Block B
  │      │  block list  │          │ <───────── │ Slave 3  │ Block C
  └──────┘              │          │ <───────── │ Slave 4  │ Block A'
                        │ fsimage  │
                        │ editlogs │
                        └──────────┘
                        Secondary NN
                        (Checkpoint)
```

- **NameNode** stores metadata in RAM (fsimage + edit logs).
- **DataNodes** store actual data blocks; send heartbeat (3s) and block report (6h).
- **Secondary NameNode** periodically merges fsimage and edit logs to prevent unbounded log growth.

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

Given: $S = 640$ MB, $B = 128$ MB, $R = 3$, $D = 4$ DataNodes.

**Step 1: Number of Blocks**

$$
n = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{640}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 5.0 \right\rceil = 5 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 2: Total Physical Storage**

$$
T_{phys} = n \times B \times R = 5 \times 128 \times 3 = 1920 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 3: Storage Overhead**

$$
O = T_{phys} - S = 1920 - 640 = 1280 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 4: Disk per DataNode (Average)**

$$
H_{disk} = \frac{T_{phys}}{D} = \frac{1920}{4} = 480 \text{ MB}
$$

> **Final Answer:** 5 blocks, 1920 MB physical storage, 1280 MB overhead, 480 MB per DataNode.

---

#### (b) MapReduce WordCount Implementation  (7 Marks)

**Q: Explain the complete execution flow of the MapReduce WordCount algorithm. Write the pseudo-code (or Java skeleton) for the Mapper, Reducer, and Driver classes.**

**Model Solution:**

**Execution Flow (3 marks):**

1. **Input Splitting:** The input file is split into fixed-size chunks (default 128 MB). Each split is assigned to one Mapper.
2. **Map Phase:** Each Mapper reads its split line-by-line, tokenizes each line into words, and emits `(word, 1)` for every occurrence.
3. **Combine Phase (Optional):** A local Combiner aggregates counts per word on the Mapper node to reduce data transfer.
4. **Shuffle & Sort Phase:** The framework groups all values for the same key and routes them to the same Reducer (using a hash partitioner).
5. **Reduce Phase:** Each Reducer sums the counts for each unique word, producing `(word, total_count)`.
6. **Output Writing:** The Reducer writes its output to HDFS in a separate file (`part-r-00000`, `part-r-00001`, ...).

**Pseudo-Code (4 marks):**

**Mapper Class:**
```java
public class WordCountMapper extends Mapper<LongWritable, Text, Text, IntWritable> {
    private final static IntWritable one = new IntWritable(1);
    private Text word = new Text();

    public void map(LongWritable key, Text value, Context context)
            throws IOException, InterruptedException {
        StringTokenizer itr = new StringTokenizer(value.toString());
        while (itr.hasMoreTokens()) {
            word.set(itr.nextToken());
            context.write(word, one);
        }
    }
}
```

**Reducer Class:**
```java
public class WordCountReducer extends Reducer<Text, IntWritable, Text, IntWritable> {
    public void reduce(Text key, Iterable<IntWritable> values, Context context)
            throws IOException, InterruptedException {
        int sum = 0;
        for (IntWritable val : values) {
            sum += val.get();
        }
        context.write(key, new IntWritable(sum));
    }
}
```

**Driver Class (Main):**
```java
public class WordCount {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        Configuration conf = new Configuration();
        Job job = Job.getInstance(conf, "word count");
        job.setJarByClass(WordCount.class);
        job.setMapperClass(WordCountMapper.class);
        job.setReducerClass(WordCountReducer.class);
        job.setOutputKeyClass(Text.class);
        job.setOutputValueClass(IntWritable.class);
        FileInputFormat.addInputPath(job, new Path(args[0]));
        FileOutputFormat.setOutputPath(job, new Path(args[1]));
        System.exit(job.waitForCompletion(true) ? 0 : 1);
    }
}
```

**Execution Command:**
```bash
hadoop jar WordCount.jar WordCount /user/input /user/output
```

> **Final Output Format (HDFS):**
```
Hadoop    3
MapReduce 2
Pig       1
HDFS      2
```

```
              │  Pig Parser & Type   │
              │  Checker (Logical    │
              │  Plan)               │
              └──────────┬───────────┘
                         ▼
              ┌──────────────────────┐
              │  Logical Optimizer   │
              │  (rule-based)        │
              └──────────┬───────────┘
                         ▼
              ┌──────────────────────┐
              │  Physical Plan       │
              │  (MapReduce / Tez /  │
              │  Spark jobs)         │
              └──────────┬───────────┘
                         ▼
              ┌──────────────────────┐
              │  Execution Engine    │
              │  (YARN on HDFS)      │
              └──────────────────────┘
```

**Components explained:**
1. **Pig Latin Script** — high-level data flow language written by the user.
2. **Parser** — produces an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
3. **Logical Optimizer** — applies rule-based optimizations (e.g., predicate pushdown, column pruning).
4. **Physical Plan** — translates the optimized logical plan into actual MapReduce / Tez / Spark jobs.
5. **Execution Engine** — submits jobs to YARN, monitors progress, and writes results to HDFS.

**Five Pig Latin Operators with Examples (3 marks):**

```pig
-- Sample Pig Latin Script for Sales Analysis
sales = LOAD '/user/data/sales.csv' USING PigStorage(',')
        AS (order_id:int, product:chararray, region:chararray,
            quantity:int, price:double);

-- 1. FILTER: remove rows with zero or negative quantity
valid = FILTER sales BY quantity > 0;

-- 2. FOREACH ... GENERATE: compute revenue
revenue = FOREACH valid GENERATE product, region,
                          (double)quantity * price AS total;

-- 3. GROUP: aggregate by region
by_region = GROUP revenue BY region;

-- 4. FOREACH ... GENERATE: sum revenue per region
summary = FOREACH by_region GENERATE group AS region,
                            SUM(revenue.total) AS total_revenue;

-- 5. ORDER ... BY: sort descending
sorted = ORDER summary BY total_revenue DESC;

-- STORE: persist result
STORE sorted INTO '/user/data/output/region_revenue' USING PigStorage(',');
```

**Operator Summary Table:**

| Operator | Example | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `LOAD` | `LOAD 'file' USING PigStorage(',')` | Read data with optional schema |
| `FILTER` | `FILTER X BY price > 100` | Keep rows matching condition |
| `FOREACH ... GENERATE` | `FOREACH X GENERATE a, b*2 AS c` | Apply transformations |
| `GROUP` | `GROUP X BY category` | Group rows by key |
| `ORDER ... BY` | `ORDER X BY col DESC` | Sort by column |
| `STORE / DUMP` | `STORE Y INTO '/out'` | Write or display output |

---

#### (b) Pig Latin — Data Transformation and Aggregation  (7 Marks)

**Q: A dataset `employee.txt` is stored in HDFS at `/user/data/employee.txt` with the schema: `(emp_id:int, name:chararray, department:chararray, salary:double)`. Write a Pig Latin script to: (i) Load the data, (ii) Filter employees with salary > 50000, (iii) Group by department, (iv) Compute the average salary per department, and (v) Store the result in HDFS. Show the expected output format.**

**Model Solution:**

**Complete Pig Latin Script (5 marks):**

```pig
-- (i) Load the data from HDFS
employee = LOAD '/user/data/employee.txt' USING PigStorage(',')
           AS (emp_id:int, name:chararray,
               department:chararray, salary:double);

-- (ii) Filter employees with salary > 50000
high_earners = FILTER employee BY salary > 50000;

-- (iii) Group by department
grouped = GROUP high_earners BY department;

-- (iv) Compute the average salary per department
avg_salary = FOREACH grouped GENERATE
                  group AS department,
                  AVG(high_earners.salary) AS avg_sal,
                  COUNT(high_earners) AS emp_count;

-- (v) Store result in HDFS
STORE avg_salary INTO '/user/data/output/avg_salary_by_dept'
        USING PigStorage(',');
```

**Execution in Grunt Shell:**

```bash
pig
grunt> exec /home/cloudera/avg_salary.pig
```

**Expected Output (HDFS `part-r-00000`):**
```
IT,72500.0,12
HR,65000.0,8
Finance,81000.0,10
Sales,58000.0,15
Engineering,95000.0,20
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation (2 marks):**

1. **`LOAD`** uses `PigStorage(',')` because the input is comma-separated. The schema assigns types to each field.
2. **`FILTER`** applies the `salary > 50000` predicate. This is **pushed up** by the Pig optimizer so the filtering happens early.
3. **`GROUP BY department`** produces a bag of tuples for each unique department.
4. **`FOREACH ... GENERATE`** computes two aggregate functions: `AVG()` for average salary and `COUNT()` for employee count.
5. **`STORE`** writes the output relation to HDFS as comma-separated text files (`part-r-00000`, etc.).

> **KTU Insight:** The Pig optimizer will automatically reorder steps so that FILTER runs before GROUP, reducing the data shuffled. Students can verify with `EXPLAIN avg_salary;` in Grunt.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **No diagram = 1–2 mark loss** in any 7-mark question that asks "with a neat diagram". Always draw HDFS architecture, MapReduce flow, or Pig architecture.
> 2. **Confusing NameNode with DataNode roles.** NameNode = metadata, DataNode = data blocks. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting replication factor in storage calculation.** Many students compute blocks correctly but skip the $\times R$ multiplier, losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Not specifying Combiner constraints.** Stating "Combiner reduces network traffic" without mentioning **associative/commutative** loses the 1 mark for the constraint.
> 5. **Writing Pig Latin without schema or storage clause.** Always include `USING PigStorage(',')` and a clear `STORE` step.
> 6. **MapReduce pseudo-code without Driver class.** The Driver is what makes the JAR runnable; omitting `Job.getInstance()` and `waitForCompletion()` loses marks.
> 7. **Wrong unit conversions.** $1 \text{ GB} = 1024 \text{ MB}$ (not 1000). KTU examiners are strict.
> 8. **In Pig, using SQL syntax.** `SELECT`, `FROM`, `WHERE` are NOT Pig Latin. Always use `FOREACH`, `FILTER`, `GROUP`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module Experiment 2**

### HDFS — Key Points
- ✅ HDFS = **Hadoop Distributed File System**, Java-based, fault-tolerant, write-once-read-many.
- ✅ **Default block size = 128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+), **default replication = 3**.
- ✅ **Architecture:** NameNode (master, metadata) + DataNodes (slaves, data blocks) + Secondary NameNode (checkpoint).
- ✅ **Heartbeat interval = 3 seconds**; **Block report interval = 6 hours**.
- ✅ **HDFS rack awareness:** 2 replicas on one rack, 1 on a different rack → minimizes cross-rack traffic.
- ✅ **Write pipeline:** Client → DN1 → DN2 → DN3 with acks flowing back.
- ✅ **Read:** Client requests metadata from NameNode → directly reads from nearest DataNode.
- ✅ **Key commands:** `-ls`, `-put`, `-get`, `-cat`, `-rm`, `-mkdir`, `-setrep`, `-du`, `-fsck`.
- ✅ **Formulas:** $n = \lceil S/B \rceil$, $T_{phys} = n \times B \times R$, overhead $= T_{phys} - S$.

### MapReduce — Key Points
- ✅ Two functions: **`map(K1,V1) → list(K2,V2)`** and **`reduce(K2, list(V2)) → list(K3,V3)`**.
- ✅ **Phases:** Input Split → Map → Combine (optional) → Shuffle & Sort → Reduce → Output.
- ✅ **Data locality:** Map task scheduled on node holding the input block.
- ✅ **Combiner:** local mini-reducer; must be **associative & commutative** (e.g., SUM, MAX).
- ✅ **Partitioner:** default = hash mod R; controls which reducer receives which keys.
- ✅ **YARN components:** Client → ResourceManager → ApplicationMaster → NodeManagers → Containers.
- ✅ **Speculative execution:** Hadoop runs a duplicate of a straggler task and uses the faster result.
- ✅ **WordCount is the canonical example** — knows it inside-out for KTU exams.

### Apache Pig — Key Points
- ✅ **Pig Latin** = high-level data flow language; auto-translates to MapReduce/Tez/Spark.
- ✅ **Execution modes:** Local, MapReduce, Tez, Spark.
- ✅ **Data types:** Scalar (`int`, `long`, `chararray`, `float`, `double`, `boolean`, `datetime`) + Complex (`tuple`, `bag`, `map`).
- ✅ **Core operators:** `LOAD`, `STORE`, `DUMP`, `FILTER`, `FOREACH ... GENERATE`, `GROUP`, `JOIN`, `ORDER BY`, `LIMIT`, `DISTINCT`, `UNION`, `SPLIT`, `DESCRIBE`, `EXPLAIN`.
- ✅ **Diagnostic operators:** `DESCRIBE` (schema), `EXPLAIN` (execution plan), `ILLUSTRATE` (sample execution).
- ✅ **Pig vs Hive:** Pig = procedural data flow; Hive = declarative SQL.
- ✅ **Pig vs MapReduce:** Pig requires ~80% less code; built-in optimization.

### KTU Exam Strategy — Last-Minute Tips
- 🎯 Always draw a **labeled block diagram** in 7-mark questions.
- 🎯 For numerical problems, show **all substitution steps** in formulas.
- 🎯 Mention **fault tolerance** and **data locality** as the two pillars of HDFS.
- 🎯 In MapReduce pseudo-code, always show the **Driver class** with `Job.getInstance()`.
- 🎯 In Pig scripts, always include a **schema** in `LOAD` and a `STORE` clause.
- 🎯 Internalize **formulas** 1–8 from the KTU Formula Sheet (Section 2.2.2).
- 🎯 Practice WordCount: hand-trace the Map → Shuffle → Reduce phases.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hive
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME
- **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
- **Module:** Module Experiment 2
- **Topic:** Hive

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apache Hive** is a data warehouse software infrastructure built on top of **Apache Hadoop** that provides **data summarization, querying, and analysis** of large datasets stored in **HDFS** (Hadoop Distributed File System) or compatible file systems. It enables users to write SQL-like queries called **HiveQL (HQL)** which are transparently converted into **MapReduce**, **Tez**, or **Spark** jobs for distributed execution.

Hive was originally developed at **Facebook** in **2007** to handle massive volumes of structured and semi-structured data and later became an **Apache top-level project** in **2010**. It follows the **Schema-on-Read** paradigm where data is brought into the system in its raw form and only parsed when a query is issued.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Excel Spreadsheet for Petabytes**
>
> Imagine you are working in a massive company that generates **terabytes of log data, sales records, and user activity** every single day. If you were to use traditional **SQL-based RDBMS** like MySQL or Oracle, the data would simply not fit. However, your business analysts are comfortable with **SQL syntax** and do not want to learn complex Java MapReduce code.
>
> **Apache Hive acts as a translator and bridge**:
> - Your analysts write familiar SQL-like statements (HiveQL).
> - Hive converts them into underlying distributed **MapReduce/Tez/Spark jobs**.
> - The huge dataset on **HDFS** gets processed in parallel across the cluster.
>
> Think of **Hive as a "SQL interpreter" sitting on top of Hadoop's storage layer**, allowing non-programmers to perform Big Data analytics using declarative query language.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of Hive

| Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|
| **Language** | HiveQL (HQL) — SQL-like declarative language |
| **Storage** | HDFS, HBase, Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake |
| **Execution Engine** | MapReduce, Tez, Spark (YARN) |
| **Schema** | Schema-on-Read (lazy evaluation) |
| **Data Types** | Primitive + Complex (arrays, maps, structs) |
| **Extensibility** | UDFs, UDAFs, UDTFs (User-Defined Functions) |
| **Latency** | High latency (batch-oriented), not for OLTP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hive is NOT suitable for real-time OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) operations**. It is specifically designed for **OLAP (Online Analytical Processing)** workloads such as reporting, data mining, and business intelligence over historical data.

### 1.4 Hive vs Traditional RDBMS — Quick Comparison

| Parameter | Traditional RDBMS | Apache Hive |
|---|---|---|
| Data Scale | Gigabytes | Petabytes |
| Storage | Local/Network FS | HDFS (Distributed) |
| Query Language | ANSI SQL | HiveQL (subset of SQL) |
| Latency | Low (milliseconds) | High (minutes to hours) |
| Transactions | Full ACID | Limited ACID (since v0.14) |
| Schema | Schema-on-Write | Schema-on-Read |
| Indexing | Full B-Tree, Hash | Limited (bitmap since v0.8) |
| Best For | OLTP | OLAP / Data Warehousing |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hive System Architecture — Component Breakdown

The Hive architecture consists of the following major components that interact with Hadoop's underlying storage and processing infrastructure:

### 2.1.1 Core Architectural Components

**(a) Hive Client (Thrift Client / JDBC / ODBC)**
- External applications connect to Hive through standard interfaces.
- **Thrift Server**: Cross-language service allowing clients written in Python, C++, Java, Ruby to submit queries.
- **JDBC/ODBC Drivers**: Enable BI tools like Tableau, Power BI to integrate with Hive.

**(b) Hive Services**
- **CLI (Command Line Interface)**: Default `hive` shell for executing HQL.
- **HiveServer2 (HS2)**: A daemon supporting concurrent client connections via JDBC/ODBC.
- **Web Interface (HWI)**: Browser-based interface (deprecated in newer versions).
- **Beeline**: Lightweight CLI that connects to HiveServer2 using JDBC.

**(c) Processing Framework**
- **Compiler**: Parses HQL → converts to Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) → generates execution plan.
- **Optimizer**: Performs query optimization (predicate pushdown, partition pruning, join reordering).
- **Execution Engine**: Executes the optimized plan using MapReduce, Tez, or Spark.

**(d) Metastore**
- Centralized repository for **metadata** about tables, columns, partitions, data types, etc.
- Typically stored in **MySQL, PostgreSQL, or Derby** (default in-memory for unit tests).
- Critical for schema management — separates data storage from schema storage.

**(e) Storage Layer**
- **HDFS**: Default distributed file system.
- **HBase**: For NoSQL-style random read/write access.
- **S3 / Azure Blob Storage**: For cloud-based data lakes.

### 2.2 Hive Data Model — Hierarchical Structure

Hive organizes data in the following hierarchy (similar to a traditional database):

$$
\text{Database} \rightarrow \text{Table} \rightarrow \text{Partition} \rightarrow \text{Bucket} \rightarrow \text{Row}
$$

| Level | Description | Storage Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **Database** | Namespace to group related tables | Directory in HDFS |
| **Table** | Logical entity with schema | Directory under database path |
| **Partition** | Horizontal slice based on column value | Sub-directory in table path |
| **Bucket** | Hash-based further division within a partition | Files in partition directory |
| **Row** | Individual record | Line in the HDFS file |

### 2.3 Hive Data Types — Complete Classification

**Primitive Data Types:**

| Type | Size | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `TINYINT` | 1 byte | Signed integer | `127` |
| `SMALLINT` | 2 bytes | Signed integer | `32000` |
| `INT` | 4 bytes | Signed integer | `2000000000` |
| `BIGINT` | 8 bytes | Signed integer | `9223372036854775807` |
| `FLOAT` | 4 bytes | Single precision | `3.14` |
| `DOUBLE` | 8 bytes | Double precision | `3.14159265359` |
| `DECIMAL` | Variable | Exact precision | `DECIMAL(10,2)` |
| `BOOLEAN` | 1 bit | True/False | `TRUE`, `FALSE` |
| `STRING` | Unlimited | String of characters | `'Hello Hive'` |
| `VARCHAR` | 1–65535 | Variable-length string | `VARCHAR(50)` |
| `CHAR` | 1–255 | Fixed-length string | `CHAR(10)` |
| `BINARY` | Variable | Byte array | Binary blob |
| `TIMESTAMP` | Variable | Date + Time | `2024-11-15 10:30:00` |
| `DATE` | Variable | Date only | `2024-11-15` |

**Complex Data Types:**

| Type | Syntax | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `ARRAY` | `ARRAY<data_type>` | Ordered list of values (1-indexed) |
| `MAP` | `MAP<primitive_type, data_type>` | Key-value pairs |
| `STRUCT` | `STRUCT<field_name : data_type, ...>` | Named fields (like a record) |
| `UNIONTYPE` | `UNIONTYPE<data_type1, data_type2, ...>` | Heterogeneous value of one of listed types |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point**: Complex data types are what make Hive **schema-flexible** when dealing with semi-structured data like **JSON, Avro, or Parquet** formats.

### 2.4 Hive Table Types — Managed vs External

| Feature | Managed Table (Internal) | External Table |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage** | Managed by Hive | Managed by user |
| **Default Location** | `/user/hive/warehouse/<db>.db/<table>` | User-specified LOCATION |
| **DROP Behavior** | Data + metadata deleted | **Only metadata** deleted |
| **Use Case** | Temporary tables | Production data shared across tools |
| **CREATE Keyword** | `CREATE TABLE` | `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE` |

$$
\boxed{
\text{Use Managed Tables for: ETL outputs, temporary analytics}
}
\qquad
\boxed{
\text{Use External Tables for: shared HDFS data, multi-tool access}
}
$$

### 2.5 Partitioning vs Bucketing

**Partitioning** = Directory-based horizontal segmentation of table data based on column values.

**Bucketing** = File-level hash-based segmentation within a partition for efficient sampling and joins.

| Aspect | Partitioning | Bucketing |
|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Directory level | File level |
| **Method** | Value-based | Hash-based (modulo) |
| **Improves** | Partition pruning | Join performance, sampling |
| **Number** | Variable | Fixed (`CLUSTERED BY ... INTO n BUCKETS`) |
| **Risk** | Too many partitions = small files problem | Even data distribution |

### 2.6 HiveQL DDL & DML Operations (Cheat Sheet)

| Category | Operation | HiveQL Syntax (Pattern) |
|---|---|---|
| **DDL** | Create Database | `CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS dbname;` |
| **DDL** | Use Database | `USE dbname;` |
| **DDL** | Drop Database | `DROP DATABASE dbname CASCADE;` |
| **DDL** | Create Table | `CREATE TABLE t(col TYPE) ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ',';` |
| **DDL** | External Table | `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE t(col TYPE) LOCATION '/path';` |
| **DDL** | Drop Table | `DROP TABLE t;` |
| **DDL** | Truncate | `TRUNCATE TABLE t;` |
| **DDL** | Alter Table | `ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMNS (c INT);` |
| **DML** | Load Data | `LOAD DATA INPATH '/path' INTO TABLE t;` |
| **DML** | Insert from Query | `INSERT OVERWRITE TABLE t2 SELECT * FROM t WHERE cond;` |
| **DML** | Insert Values | `INSERT INTO t VALUES (1,'abc',50000);` |
| **DQL** | Select | `SELECT col, COUNT(*) FROM t GROUP BY col;` |
| **DQL** | Join | `SELECT * FROM a JOIN b ON a.id=b.id;` |

### 2.7 SerDe — Serializer/Deserializer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SerDe (Serializer/Deserializer)** is the interface that allows Hive to read and write data in various formats such as **CSV, JSON, Avro, Parquet, ORC, and Regex**.

| Format | SerDe Class | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **CSV/TSV** | `LazySimpleSerDe` | Default delimited text |
| **JSON** | `org.apache.hive.hcatalog.data.JsonSerDe` | Semi-structured data |
| **Avro** | `AvroSerDe` | Schema evolution |
| **Parquet** | `ParquetHiveSerDe` | Columnar storage |
| **ORC** | `OrcSerde` | Optimized Row Columnar (faster) |
| **Regex** | `RegexSerDe` | Log parsing |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hive is used across industries for:
- **Log Analytics** at Facebook, Netflix (user behavior, recommendation systems)
- **Clickstream Analysis** in e-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart)
- **ETL Pipelines** in banking and finance
- **Business Intelligence** dashboards over massive datasets
- **IoT Data Warehousing** for sensor data aggregation
- **Ad-hoc querying** by data scientists on raw data lakes

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation, Analytical Matrix & Experiment Walkthrough

## 3.1 Hive Query Execution — Complete Lifecycle (Analytical Steps)

When a user submits a HiveQL query, it undergoes a **multi-stage pipeline** before being executed on the cluster. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step analytical breakdown:

**Step 1 — Query Submission**
The user types a query in the **Hive CLI** (or submits via JDBC/ODBC/Thrift to HiveServer2).

**Step 2 — Lexical Analysis & Parsing**
The `Driver` (Compilation component) invokes the **AntLR-based parser** which performs:
- **Lexical analysis**: Tokenization (keywords, identifiers, operators)
- **Syntax analysis**: Builds the **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**

**Step 3 — Semantic Analysis**
- Resolves table and column names against the **Metastore**
- Performs type checking and validates expressions
- Generates the **Query Block Tree (QBT)**

**Step 4 — Logical Plan Generation**
- The QBT is transformed into a **logical operator tree** containing:
  - TableScan, Filter, Join, GroupBy, ReduceSink, SelectOperator, etc.
- Equivalent to relational algebra in RDBMS.

**Step 5 — Optimization**
The **Optimizer** applies various rewrite rules:
- **Predicate Pushdown**: Move filters closer to the scan
- **Partition Pruning**: Eliminate irrelevant partitions
- **Join Reordering**: Place smaller tables on the build side
- **Map Join (Broadcast Join)**: Convert joins to map-side joins
- **Column Pruning**: Read only required columns (with ORC/Parquet)

**Step 6 — Physical Plan Generation**
- Converts logical operators to **MapReduce / Tez / Spark tasks**
- Each operator is mapped to a stage in the DAG

**Step 7 — Execution Engine Submission**
- The physical plan is submitted to **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)**
- YARN allocates **ApplicationMaster** and **Container** resources
- Job executes on the **HDFS** data

**Step 8 — Result Return**
- Final results are fetched back to the user via the **Driver** → **CLI/HiveServer2**

The complete pipeline can be expressed as:

$$
\boxed{
\text{HQL} \xrightarrow{\text{Parse}} \text{AST} \xrightarrow{\text{Semantic}} \text{QBT} \xrightarrow{\text{Logical}} \text{Operator Tree} \xrightarrow{\text{Optimize}} \text{Optimized Plan} \xrightarrow{\text{Physical}} \text{MapReduce/Tez/Spark DAG}
}
$$

## 3.2 Partitions and Buckets — Analytical Derivation

### 3.2.1 Partitioning — Directory Structure

When a table is partitioned on column `country`, HDFS structure becomes:

$$
\text{Path} = \text{/user/hive/warehouse/sales.db/orders/country=IN/}
$$
$$
\text{Path} = \text{/user/hive/warehouse/sales.db/orders/country=US/}
$$

When a query contains a filter `WHERE country='IN'`, Hive performs **partition pruning** and reads only the relevant directory, drastically reducing I/O.

**Query Optimization Impact:**
$$
\text{Files Scanned}_{\text{partitioned}} = \frac{\text{Total Files}}{\text{Number of Partitions}}
$$

### 3.2.2 Bucketing — Hash Distribution

For a table bucketed on `user_id` into **4 buckets**, each record is placed in bucket:

$$
\text{Bucket Number} = \text{hash}(\text{user\_id}) \mod 4
$$

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B(r) &= \text{hash}(r.\text{user\_id}) \mod N_b
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $B(r)$ = bucket assignment function for record $r$
- $\text{hash}(\cdot)$ = Hive's internal hash function (MD5-based)
- $N_b$ = number of buckets
- $r.\text{user\_id}$ = bucketing column value for record $r$

### 3.3 HiveQL Aggregate Function — Analytical Flow

For a query `SELECT dept, COUNT(*) FROM emp GROUP BY dept;`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1 (Map):} &\quad \text{Emit } (\text{dept}, 1) \text{ for each record} \\
\text{Shuffle:} &\quad \text{Group by } \text{dept} \text{ key} \\
\text{Stage 2 (Reduce):} &\quad \text{Sum values for each } \text{dept} \text{ key} \\
\text{Output:} &\quad (\text{dept}, \text{count})
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Hive Join Operations — Analytical Comparison

| Join Type | When Used | Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **Inner Join** | Matching records in both tables | Shuffle join / Map join |
| **Left Outer Join** | All left + matching right | Shuffle join |
| **Right Outer Join** | All right + matching left | Shuffle join |
| **Full Outer Join** | All records from both | Shuffle join |
| **Cross Join** | Cartesian product | Single MapReduce job (no reducer) |
| **Map Join (Broadcast)** | Small table fits in memory | Replicated in every mapper |
| **Bucket Map Join** | Both tables bucketed on join key | Sorted merge at map side |
| **Sort Merge Bucket (SMB) Join** | Both tables sorted + bucketed | Most efficient join |

### 3.5 Hive Join Execution Algorithm — Derivation

For a standard **Shuffle Join** between tables $A$ (large) and $B$ (small):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Map Phase:} &\quad \text{For each row } r_A \in A, \text{ emit } (\text{key}=r_A.\text{id}, \text{value}=(A, r_A)) \\
&\quad \text{For each row } r_B \in B, \text{ emit } (\text{key}=r_B.\text{id}, \text{value}=(B, r_B)) \\
\\
\text{Shuffle \& Sort:} &\quad \text{Group by key} \\
\\
\text{Reduce Phase:} &\quad \text{For each key } k, \text{ compute } A[k] \bowtie B[k] \\
&\quad = \{ (a, b) \mid a \in A[k], b \in B[k] \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.6 Hive Performance Optimization — Analytical Matrix

| Optimization | Mechanism | Performance Gain |
|---|---|---|
| **Partitioning** | Skip irrelevant data directories | 10x–100x for selective queries |
| **Bucketing** | Hash-based file grouping | 2x–5x for joins on bucketed keys |
| **ORC/Parquet Format** | Columnar storage + compression | 5x–20x storage, 2x–10x query |
| **Tez Execution** | DAG-based execution (vs linear MR) | 3x–10x faster than MapReduce |
| **Vectorization** | Process 1024 rows at a time | 2x–5x scan speedup |
| **Cost-Based Optimization (CBO)** | Statistics-driven plan selection | 1.5x–3x typical |
| **Map Join (Small Table Broadcast)** | Avoid shuffle for small tables | 5x–20x for small-large joins |
| **Predicate Pushdown** | Filter at storage layer | 2x–10x for filtered queries |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1:** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Define **Apache Hive**. List any **four features** of Hive.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Apache Hive is a data warehouse infrastructure built on top of Hadoop that provides SQL-like query capability (HiveQL) for analyzing large datasets stored in HDFS.

**Four Key Features:**
1. **SQL-like query language (HiveQL)** — easy for SQL-proficient users.
2. **Schema-on-Read** — flexible schema definition at query time.
3. **Scalability** — handles petabytes of data by leveraging Hadoop's distributed storage.
4. **Extensibility** — supports UDFs, UDAFs, and UDTFs in Java/Python.

(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for features — 4 features @ 0.5 mark each)

---

> **Question 2:** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> Differentiate between **Managed Table** and **External Table** in Hive with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Managed Table | External Table |
|---|---|---|
| **Ownership** | Hive owns both data and metadata | User owns data; Hive only stores metadata |
| **Storage Location** | `/user/hive/warehouse/<db>.db/<table>` | User-specified LOCATION clause |
| **DROP behavior** | Deletes data + metadata | Deletes only metadata |

**Example:**
```sql
-- Managed Table
CREATE TABLE sales_managed (id INT, amount DOUBLE);

-- External Table
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE sales_external (id INT, amount DOUBLE)
LOCATION '/data/shared/sales/';
```
(2 marks for table, 1 mark for example)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern 2024]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **Hive architecture** in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the **Metastore** and **Hive Driver** in query processing. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write HiveQL statements to perform the following:
> 1. Create a database `employee_db`.
> 2. Create an **external table** `emp_salary(emp_id INT, name STRING, dept STRING, salary DECIMAL(10,2))` stored as **ORC** file with location `/data/emp/`.
> 3. Load data from HDFS path `/input/emp_data.csv` into the table.
> 4. Display the **average salary per department** sorted in **descending order**.
> 5. Create a **partitioned table** `emp_sales` on column `region`.
> 6. Drop the table `emp_salary` and verify the HDFS data is retained.
> **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Hive Architecture (7 Marks)**

Hive follows a layered architecture with the following components:

**1. Hive Clients (1 Mark)**
- CLI (`hive` shell)
- Beeline (JDBC-based CLI)
- Thrift Client (cross-language)
- JDBC/ODBC drivers (for BI tools like Tableau)

**2. Hive Services (1.5 Marks)**
- **Driver**: Session handle that receives queries. Manages the entire lifecycle from compilation to execution.
- **Compiler**: Parses HQL → builds AST → performs semantic analysis → generates execution plan.
- **Optimizer**: Applies rules like predicate pushdown, partition pruning, join reordering.
- **Execution Engine**: Submits the optimized plan to MapReduce/Tez/Spark via YARN.

**3. Metastore (2 Marks)**
- Central repository for metadata: database names, table schemas, column types, partition information, SerDe, storage locations, statistics.
- Stores metadata in relational DB (MySQL/PostgreSQL recommended for production; Derby is default for testing).
- Provides **lazy/eager mode** and **thread-safe** metadata access.

**4. Storage & Processing Layer (1.5 Marks)**
- HDFS stores the actual data
- MapReduce/Tez/Spark performs the processing
- YARN manages cluster resources

**5. Diagram (1 Mark)** — Refer to Section 4.1 architecture diagram.

#### **Part (b) — HiveQL Statements (7 Marks)**

```sql
-- (1) Create database (0.5 mark)
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS employee_db;
USE employee_db;

-- (2) Create external table with ORC format (2 marks)
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS emp_salary (
    emp_id   INT,
    name     STRING,
    dept     STRING,
    salary   DECIMAL(10,2)
)
STORED AS ORC
LOCATION '/data/emp/';

-- (3) Load data from HDFS (1 mark)
LOAD DATA INPATH '/input/emp_data.csv' INTO TABLE emp_salary;

-- (4) Average salary per department DESC (1.5 marks)
SELECT dept, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
FROM emp_salary
GROUP BY dept
ORDER BY avg_sal DESC;

-- (5) Partitioned table on region (1.5 marks)
CREATE TABLE emp_sales (
    emp_id     INT,
    sale_amt   DOUBLE
)
PARTITIONED BY (region STRING)
STORED AS ORC;

-- (6) Drop external table and verify data retained (0.5 mark)
DROP TABLE emp_salary;
-- HDFS path /data/emp/ data remains intact (since it's an external table)
```

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — **[Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Explain **Partitioning** and **Bucketing** in Hive with a suitable example. How do they improve query performance? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write a complete Hive experiment to:
> 1. Create a **partitioned-bucketed table** `student_marks(roll_no INT, name STRING, marks INT)` partitioned by `subject` and bucketed into **3 buckets** by `roll_no`, stored as **TEXTFILE**.
> 2. **Insert** sample data into the table.
> 3. Display the **maximum marks in each subject** using a Hive query.
> 4. Demonstrate the use of the **GROUP BY** and **HAVING** clauses with a query showing subjects having average marks greater than 75.
> 5. Use a **JOIN** between two tables `student_info` and `student_marks` to display roll_no, name, subject, and marks.
> **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Partitioning and Bucketing (7 Marks)**

**Partitioning (3.5 Marks):**
- Partitioning divides a table into **directories** based on column values.
- Each unique partition value creates a separate HDFS subdirectory.
- Enables **partition pruning**: queries that filter on partition columns scan only relevant directories.

**Example:**
```sql
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id   INT,
    amount     DOUBLE,
    cust_id    INT
)
PARTITIONED BY (order_date STRING)
STORED AS ORC;
```
HDFS Layout:
```
/user/hive/warehouse/orders/order_date=2024-01-01/
/user/hive/warehouse/orders/order_date=2024-01-02/
```

**Bucketing (3.5 Marks):**
- Bucketing divides table data into a **fixed number of files** within a partition using a **hash function** on a column.
- Useful for efficient **sampling**, **map-side joins**, and **parallel processing**.

**Mathematical Foundation:**
$$
B(r) = \text{hash}(r.\text{roll\_no}) \mod 3
$$

**Example:**
```sql
CREATE TABLE student_marks (
    roll_no INT,
    name    STRING,
    marks   INT
)
PARTITIONED BY (subject STRING)
CLUSTERED BY (roll_no) INTO 3 BUCKETS;
```

**Performance Improvement:**
- Partitioning reduces **I/O** by skipping irrelevant data.
- Bucketing reduces **shuffle** in joins and enables **bucket sampling** `TABLESAMPLE(BUCKET 1 OUT OF 2)`.

---

#### **Part (b) — Complete Hive Experiment (7 Marks)**

```sql
-- (1) Create partitioned-bucketed TEXTFILE table (2 marks)
CREATE TABLE student_marks (
    roll_no INT,
    name    STRING,
    marks   INT
)
PARTITIONED BY (subject STRING)
CLUSTERED BY (roll_no) INTO 3 BUCKETS
STORED AS TEXTFILE;

-- (2) Insert sample data (1.5 marks)
INSERT INTO student_marks PARTITION(subject='Math') VALUES
    (1, 'Arun',  85),
    (2, 'Beena', 78),
    (3, 'Chitra',92);

INSERT INTO student_marks PARTITION(subject='Science') VALUES
    (1, 'Arun',  72),
    (2, 'Beena', 88),
    (3, 'Chitra',95);

-- (3) Maximum marks per subject (1 mark)
SELECT subject, MAX(marks) AS max_marks
FROM student_marks
GROUP BY subject;

-- (4) GROUP BY + HAVING (1.5 marks)
SELECT subject, AVG(marks) AS avg_marks
FROM student_marks
GROUP BY subject
HAVING AVG(marks) > 75;

-- (5) JOIN between two tables (1 mark)
CREATE TABLE student_info (
    roll_no INT,
    name    STRING,
    dept    STRING
) STORED AS TEXTFILE;

INSERT INTO student_info VALUES
    (1, 'Arun',  'CSE'),
    (2, 'Beena', 'ECE'),
    (3, 'Chitra','Mech');

SELECT s.roll_no, s.name, m.subject, m.marks
FROM student_info s
JOIN student_marks m
ON s.roll_no = m.roll_no;
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Mark-Loss Areas)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use `LOCATION` in External Table** — Examiner deducts 0.5 mark. Always specify LOCATION for EXTERNAL TABLE.
> 2. **Using backticks or quotes in wrong places** — Hive is **case-insensitive for keywords** but **case-sensitive for table/column names** only when configured.
> 3. **Not enabling dynamic partitioning** — For multiple-partition inserts, set:
>    `SET hive.exec.dynamic.partition=true;`
>    `SET hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode=nonstrict;`
> 4. **Confusing `LOAD DATA INPATH` vs `LOAD DATA LOCAL INPATH`** — The first copies from HDFS (cuts the source), the second copies from local FS (keeps source).
> 5. **Using `TRUNCATE` on External Tables** — Not allowed; produces error.
> 6. **Forgetting `STORED AS ORC/TEXTFILE` clause** — Examiner expects explicit format specification.
> 7. **Not specifying `ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','`** for CSV data — produces single-column output if omitted.
> 8. **JOIN syntax** — Old-style implicit joins (comma-separated tables) are **deprecated**; always use explicit `JOIN ... ON` syntax.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLET POINTS — Hive (Module Experiment 2)**
>
> - **Definition:** Hive = data warehouse software on Hadoop for SQL-like querying of HDFS data.
> - **Language:** HiveQL (HQL) — a declarative SQL-like language.
> - **Architecture:** User → CLI/Beeline/JDBC → Driver → Compiler → Optimizer → Execution Engine → YARN → HDFS.
> - **Metastore:** Stores schema metadata in a relational DB (MySQL/PostgreSQL/Derby).
> - **Schema-on-Read:** Schema is applied at query time, not at write time.
> - **Two Table Types:** **Managed** (Hive owns data) vs **External** (User owns data).
> - **DROP Behavior:** Managed → data + metadata deleted; External → only metadata deleted.
> - **Partitioning:** Directory-level horizontal segmentation; reduces I/O via partition pruning.
> - **Bucketing:** File-level hash-based segmentation; improves joins and sampling.
> - **Data Types:** Primitive (INT, STRING, DECIMAL, BOOLEAN, TIMESTAMP) + Complex (ARRAY, MAP, STRUCT, UNIONTYPE).
> - **SerDe:** Serializer/Deserializer for various formats (CSV, JSON, Avro, ORC, Parquet).
> - **File Formats:** TEXTFILE, SEQUENCEFILE, ORC (columnar, optimized), PARQUET (columnar, cross-platform).
> - **Execution Engines:** MapReduce (slowest), Tez (DAG-based, fast), Spark (in-memory, fastest).
> - **DDL Commands:** CREATE, DROP, ALTER, TRUNCATE, SHOW, DESCRIBE.
> - **DML Commands:** LOAD DATA, INSERT INTO/OVERWRITE.
> - **OLAP Tool:** Hive is designed for OLAP, NOT OLTP — high latency, no real-time.
> - **Default Warehouse Path:** `/user/hive/warehouse/<database>.db/<table>/`.
> - **Dynamic Partition Settings Required:** `hive.exec.dynamic.partition=true` and `hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode=nonstrict`.
> - **JOIN Types:** Inner, Left/Right/Full Outer, Cross, Map (Broadcast), Bucket Map, Sort Merge Bucket (SMB).
> - **Optimization Techniques:** Partitioning, Bucketing, ORC/Parquet, Vectorization, Tez, CBO, Map Join, Predicate Pushdown.
> - **ACID Support:** Limited ACID transactions since Hive 0.14 — supports INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE with ORC.
> - **Common Commands to Start Hive:**
>   - Start Hadoop: `start-dfs.sh` + `start-yarn.sh`
>   - Launch Hive: `hive` (CLI) or `beeline -u jdbc:hive2://localhost:10000` (HS2)
> - **Commands to Verify Results:** `SHOW TABLES;`, `DESCRIBE FORMATTED table_name;`, `SELECT * FROM table;`
> - **HDFS Commands:** `hdfs dfs -ls /user/hive/warehouse/`, `hdfs dfs -put localfile /hdfs/path/`
> - **Exam Tip:** Always include the **LOCATION** clause for external tables and **STORED AS** clause for format specification.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Hbase K3 CO3 Resolve problems associated with big data with the features of R programming K3 CO4 Demonstrate spark programming with different programming languages K3 CO5 Develop and Implement innovative ideas on big data technologies K3 Note: K1- Remember

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Apache HBase** is an open-source, distributed, versioned, non-relational (NoSQL) database modeled after Google's **Bigtable** (Chang et al., 2006). It is built on top of the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** and provides real-time, random read/write access to massive datasets (billions of rows × millions of columns) with horizontal scalability and strong consistency for reads and writes on a single row.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy, HBase is classified under the **K3 – Apply** cognitive level, which means students must not only *remember* definitions (K1) but also *apply* HBase commands, schema design, and architectural reasoning to solve big data problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PBADT504 – Module 2):**
> HBase is a critical component of the Hadoop ecosystem. It solves the "random real-time read/write" problem that HDFS (a write-once, read-many batch system) cannot handle efficiently. Every KTU question on HBase tests either its **data model**, **architecture (HMaster, RegionServer, ZooKeeper)**, or **shell operations (put, get, scan, delete)**.

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — "The Infinite Spreadsheet"

Imagine the world's largest, ever-expanding **Google Spreadsheet**:

- The spreadsheet has **unlimited rows** (each identified by a unique **Row Key**, like a primary key).
- Columns are **NOT predefined**. You can add a new column at any time, for any row, without affecting other rows. This is called a **sparse** structure.
- Every time you edit a cell, HBase **does not overwrite** the old value — it stamps a new **timestamp/version** on it, so you can go back in time.
- The spreadsheet is automatically **sharded** (split into chunks called **Regions**) and distributed across thousands of cheap commodity servers.
- A **traffic controller (ZooKeeper)** and a **librarian (HMaster)** keep track of which chunk is on which server, so you don't have to.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** HBase = "Google Bigtable for the rest of us." It gives you Bigtable's powers (sparse, multi-dimensional, sorted, versioned maps) on commodity hardware using Hadoop's storage layer.

## 1.3 Key Terminology with Standard Metrics

| Term | Meaning | Standard Metric (Bold) |
|---|---|---|
| **Row Key** | Unique identifier of a row (lexicographically sorted as bytes) | **String ≤ 64 KB** (recommended < 100 bytes for hotspot avoidance) |
| **Column Family** | Group of related columns stored together physically | **Max 3–4 per table** (HBase design rule) |
| **Column Qualifier** | Sub-column inside a family (dynamic, schema-less) | **Unlimited** (frequently millions per family) |
| **Cell** | Intersection of row + column + timestamp | Holds a **byte[]** value |
| **Version / Timestamp** | Each write creates a new version | **Default 3 versions retained** |
| **Region** | Horizontal shard of a table (sorted row range) | Default size **256 MB** (configurable up to **1–2 GB**) |
| **RegionServer** | Worker node serving reads/writes for assigned regions | One RS typically hosts **20–200 regions** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to remember for KTU exams:**
> - Default Region size: **256 MB** (10 GB in newer versions, but KTU syllabus cites **256 MB**)
> - Default HFile block size: **64 KB**
> - Default versions retained: **3**
> - HBase is **CP** (Consistency + Partition tolerance) in CAP theorem, **NOT** the Hadoop batch layer (which is AP).
> - HBase uses **HDFS** for storage but **does NOT use MapReduce** for queries (uses its own coprocessors/scans).
> - HBase requires **ZooKeeper** for coordination (HMaster ↔ RegionServer ↔ Client).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The HBase Data Model — Logical View

The logical model of HBase is a **sparse, multi-dimensional sorted map**. Formally, it is a mapping:

$$
\text{HBase} : (\text{RowKey}, \text{ColumnFamily}, \text{ColumnQualifier}, \text{Timestamp}) \rightarrow \text{Value}
$$

This **4D sparse map** is the heart of every KTU question on the data model.

### 2.1.1 Decomposition of the 4D Map

- **Dimension 1 — Row Key (RowKey):**
  - Byte-string that uniquely identifies a row.
  - Rows are stored **lexicographically** sorted by row key. This is the single most important design decision.
  - Access patterns: `get(rowKey)`, `scan(startRow, stopRow)`, `scan(rowPrefix)`.

- **Dimension 2 — Column Family (CF):**
  - Physical grouping of columns. All columns in a family are stored together in HFiles.
  - Defined at **table creation time** and rarely changed. Cardinality should be small (≤ 4).
  - Example: `personal`, `professional`, `contact`.

- **Dimension 3 — Column Qualifier (CQ):**
  - Sub-column inside a family. **Schema-less** — created on-the-fly during a `put`.
  - Example: in family `personal`, qualifiers could be `name`, `dob`, `gender`.

- **Dimension 4 — Timestamp / Version:**
  - 64-bit integer (long), default = `System.currentTimeMillis()` at write time.
  - HBase keeps the **N most recent versions** (configurable via `hbase.hstore.compaction.max.version`).

### 2.1.2 Visual Logical Model

| RowKey | personal:name | personal:dob | professional:salary | contact:email | contact:phone |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| user_001 | "Anand" @t1 | "1998-04-12" @t1 | "₹60,000" @t5 | "a@x.com" @t3 | "+91-9876543210" @t2 |
| user_002 | "Beena" @t1 | (empty) | "₹45,000" @t4 | (empty) | "+91-9123456789" @t2 |

> Notice: `user_002` has NO `personal:dob` and NO `contact:email` — this is **sparsity**. No storage cost for absent cells.

## 2.2 HBase Architecture — The Three-Tier Master/Worker Pattern

HBase follows a classic **HMaster / RegionServer** master-slave architecture, with **ZooKeeper** as the coordination backbone.

### 2.2.1 Component Roles

| Component | Role | Failure Recovery |
|---|---|---|
| **HMaster** | Manages region assignment, DDL operations (create/delete tables), coordinates failover. **Lightweight.** | Backup masters run hot-standby; new master elected via ZooKeeper |
| **RegionServer (RS)** | Serves reads/writes for one or more Regions. Holds MemStore + BlockCache + HFiles. **Heavy worker.** | HMaster re-assigns its regions to other live RS |
| **ZooKeeper (ZK)** | Maintains cluster state: which RS is alive, which RS hosts which region, root/meta table location. **NOT optional.** | Quorum of 3/5 nodes; ensemble must have odd count |
| **HDFS** | Underlying storage for HFiles, WAL, root/meta tables | NameNode HA with ZKFC |
| **Client API** | Talks to ZK first → finds -ROOT- → .META. → actual RS. Caches region locations. | Client-side retry + region location cache |

### 2.2.2 The Read/Write Path (Stepwise Logic)

#### WRITE PATH (Put)
1. Client contacts **ZooKeeper** to find the -ROOT- region, then locates the `.META.` table, then the target RegionServer.
2. Client sends `Put(rowKey, ...)` to the responsible RegionServer.
3. RS first writes to **WAL (Write-Ahead Log)** on HDFS — durability.
4. RS then writes the data into **MemStore** (in-memory sorted buffer, per column family).
5. When MemStore exceeds threshold (default **128 MB**), it is **flushed** to disk as an immutable **HFile** in HDFS.
6. RS returns success to client. (Eventually consistent across replicas after WAL replication.)

#### READ PATH (Get)
1. Client merges new cell into the target **HFile** (last step is optional compaction).
2. Client uses BlockCache + MemStore + HFile scan to find the cell.
3. Bloom filters prune HFiles that cannot contain the row.
4. BlockCache (LRU cache of 64 KB HFile blocks) serves hot data from RAM.
5. Cell is returned to client.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why HBase is fast for random reads/writes (the KTU "Why" question):**
> - **WAL** guarantees durability without blocking on disk.
> - **MemStore** absorbs writes in memory (no random disk I/O).
> - **HFiles** are immutable + sorted, enabling binary search and Bloom filters.
> - **BlockCache** keeps hot data in RAM.
> - **Region-level parallelism** scales horizontally — just add a RegionServer.

## 2.3 HBase vs RDBMS vs HDFS — The KTU Comparison Table

| Feature | HBase | RDBMS (MySQL) | HDFS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Model | Wide-column, sparse, versioned | Relational, fixed schema | Flat files (block-based) |
| Schema | Flexible (column qualifiers dynamic) | Rigid | Schema-less |
| Scalability | Horizontal (add RegionServers) | Vertical (bigger box) | Horizontal (add DataNodes) |
| Access Pattern | Random read/write (low latency) | Random read/write (low latency) | Sequential read/write (high throughput) |
| Consistency | Strong (per-row) | Strong (ACID) | Write-once, eventual |
| Query Language | Java API, Shell, SQL-via-Phoenix | SQL | MapReduce, Spark |
| Best For | Time-series, IoT, messaging, fraud | Transactional OLTP | Batch analytics, archival |
| CAP Trade-off | **CP** | **CA** (typically) | **AP** (via HDFS replication) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HBase Shell Commands (MUST memorize for 3-mark & 14-mark questions):**

| Command | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `create` | `create 'table', 'cf1', 'cf2'` | Create a table with column families |
| `list` | `list` | List all tables |
| `put` | `put 'table', 'rowkey', 'cf:col', 'value'` | Insert/update a cell |
| `get` | `get 'table', 'rowkey'` | Read a full row |
| `get` (specific) | `get 'table', 'rowkey', 'cf:col'` | Read one cell |
| `scan` | `scan 'table'` | Read all rows |
| `scan` (range) | `scan 'table', {STARTROW=>'a', STOPROW=>'z'}` | Range scan |
| `delete` | `delete 'table', 'rowkey', 'cf:col'` | Delete a cell (tombstone) |
| `deleteall` | `deleteall 'table', 'rowkey'` | Delete entire row |
| `count` | `count 'table'` | Count rows (slow — full scan) |
| `disable` + `drop` | `disable 'table'; drop 'table'` | Remove a table |
| `describe` | `describe 'table'` | Show table metadata |
| `truncate` | `truncate 'table'` | Recreate table (faster than delete+recreate) |

| Architectural Fact | Value |
|---|---|
| HBase is based on | **Google Bigtable** paper (2006) |
| Default Region size | **256 MB** |
| Default block size | **64 KB** |
| Default versions | **3** |
| Default MemStore flush size | **128 MB** |
| Coordination service | **Apache ZooKeeper** |
| File format | **HFile** (built on Hadoop TFile) |
| Write-Ahead Log file | **HLog** (one per RS) |
| Index for region lookup | **-ROOT-** → **.META.** (in newer HBase 2.x, replaced by ZK-based `MetaTableLocator`) |
| Compaction types | **Minor** (merges small HFiles) + **Major** (merges ALL HFiles of a region) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> The following worked examples are written in exhaustive, step-by-step KTU valuation style. Each logical transition is explicit. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Designing an HBase Schema for a Student Database (K3 Application)

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-mark question):**
Design an HBase schema to store student records with the following fields: `rollno, name, branch, semester, marks_sub1, marks_sub2, marks_sub3`. Justify your design choices. Write the shell commands to (a) create the table, (b) insert two records, (c) retrieve rollno `S001`'s `name`.

### Step 1 — Identify the Row Key
The **Row Key** must enable fast, evenly-distributed lookups. `rollno` is a natural choice (e.g., `S001`, `S002`).

> **Logic:** Row keys are sorted lexicographically. Choosing `S001`, `S002` ... ensures scans in roll-number order. To avoid hot-spotting with sequential keys, real systems use hashing/ salting — but for KTU, sequential keys are acceptable.

### Step 2 — Group Columns into Column Families
Apply the HBase design rule: **column families should group columns that are accessed together.**

- Family `profile` → `name`, `branch`, `semester` (low cardinality, stable, accessed together)
- Family `academics` → `marks_sub1`, `marks_sub2`, `marks_sub3` (numeric, accessed together for grade reports)

### Step 3 — Justify Design Choices
- **Two families** keep related data physically co-located in HFiles → faster scans for grade reports.
- Families are **small in number** (≤ 4) → fewer flushes, less compaction overhead.
- Column qualifiers inside `academics` are **dynamic** — if the curriculum adds `marks_sub4`, no schema change needed.
- `marks_sub1` etc. could alternatively be qualifiers in **separate row versions** for time-series tracking per semester.

### Step 4 — HBase Shell Commands

**Step 4a — Create the table:**
```bash
hbase(main):001:0> create 'student', 'profile', 'academics'
```

**Step 4b — Insert two records:**
```bash
hbase(main):002:0> put 'student', 'S001', 'profile:name', 'Anand Kumar'
hbase(main):003:0> put 'student', 'S001', 'profile:branch', 'CSE'
hbase(main):004:0> put 'student', 'S001', 'profile:semester', '5'
hbase(main):005:0> put 'student', 'S001', 'academics:marks_sub1', '85'
hbase(main):006:0> put 'student', 'S001', 'academics:marks_sub2', '78'
hbase(main):007:0> put 'student', 'S001', 'academics:marks_sub3', '92'

hbase(main):008:0> put 'student', 'S002', 'profile:name', 'Beena Joseph'
hbase(main):009:0> put 'student', 'S002', 'profile:branch', 'ECE'
hbase(main):010:0> put 'student', 'S002', 'profile:semester', '5'
hbase(main):011:0> put 'student', 'S002', 'academics:marks_sub1', '74'
hbase(main):012:0> put 'student', 'S002', 'academics:marks_sub2', '88'
hbase(main):013:0> put 'student', 'S002', 'academics:marks_sub3', '81'
```

**Step 4c — Retrieve `S001`'s name only:**
```bash
hbase(main):014:0> get 'student', 'S001', 'profile:name'
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Expected Output:**
> ```
> COLUMN                  CELL
>  profile:name           timestamp=1700000000000, value=Anand Kumar
> 1 row(s) in 0.0200 seconds
> ```

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — The Read Path (Step-by-Step Trace)

**Question:** When a client issues `get 'student', 'S001'`, trace the exact sequence of events.

### Step 1 — Client-Side Bootstrap
The HBase client library (Java) reads its configuration (`hbase-site.xml`) and locates the **ZooKeeper ensemble** address (e.g., `zk1:2181,zk2:2181,zk3:2181`).

### Step 2 — ZK Lookup for Meta Table
The client connects to ZooKeeper and reads the znode `/hbase/meta-region-server`. This znode holds the hostname of the RegionServer hosting the **`hbase:meta`** table (formerly `.META.`).

### Step 3 — Query the Meta Table
The client queries the `hbase:meta` table for the row key `S001`. The meta table is itself an HBase table, so this is recursive — but only one level deep. The meta table returns:
- **Region name:** `student,,1700000000000.abc123def456.`
- **RegionServer hosting it:** `rs1.example.com:16020`

### Step 4 — Cache the Location
The client **caches** this region→server mapping in its local `MetaCache` for a configurable TTL. Subsequent gets skip ZK and meta queries.

### Step 5 — Direct RS Communication
The client sends the `Get` request to `rs1.example.com:16020`.

### Step 6 — RegionServer Internal Lookup
The RS checks its in-memory `RegionCache`. It locates the `student` region and looks for the row `S001`.

### Step 7 — Multi-Source Read
The RS performs a parallel scan across:
- **BlockCache** (RAM, 64 KB blocks, LRU)
- **MemStore** (in-memory sorted skip list)
- **HFiles on disk** (filtered by Bloom filter first, then binary search)

> **Why Bloom filter matters:** For each HFile, a Bloom filter is consulted. If it returns "definitely not in this file," the file is skipped entirely. This is what makes HBase's random reads fast even with millions of HFiles.

### Step 8 — Result Assembly
The RS merges results from all sources, applies version filtering (returns the cell with timestamp ≤ scan timestamp, max N versions), and returns the row to the client.

### Step 9 — Return to Client
The result is sent over the Protobuf/HBase RPC channel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total network hops on first read: 3** (ZK → Meta RS → Data RS).
> **On subsequent reads: 1** (cached, only Data RS).
> This is why HBase latency improves as the client warms its cache.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — MemStore Flush & Compaction Math (For Numerical Questions)

**Question:** A RegionServer serves a table with column family `cf1`. The MemStore size is **128 MB**. The HFile block size is **64 KB**. A single row occupies approximately **2 KB**. How many rows fit in one MemStore? After flush, if the resulting HFile contains exactly 1,000 blocks, what is its on-disk size?

### Step 1 — Rows per MemStore

$$
\text{Rows per MemStore} = \frac{\text{MemStore size}}{\text{Size per row}} = \frac{128 \, \text{MB}}{2 \, \text{KB}}
$$

Convert units: $128 \, \text{MB} = 128 \times 1024 \, \text{KB} = 131072 \, \text{KB}$.

$$
\text{Rows per MemStore} = \frac{131072 \, \text{KB}}{2 \, \text{KB}} = 65536 \, \text{rows}
$$

### Step 2 — On-Disk HFile Size

$$
\text{HFile size} = \text{Number of blocks} \times \text{Block size} = 1000 \times 64 \, \text{KB}
$$

$$
\text{HFile size} = 64000 \, \text{KB} = \frac{64000}{1024} \, \text{MB} \approx 62.5 \, \text{MB}
$$

> **Note:** The actual on-disk size will be larger because of HFile metadata (block index, Bloom filter, file info, trailer). For KTU exams, the calculation above is the expected model answer.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Timestamp & Versioning

**Question:** A cell `(row='r1', cf='c', cq='v')` is written with values `"A"` at t=1, `"B"` at t=2, `"C"` at t=3, then `"D"` at t=4. If `VERSIONS=3`, what does a `get` at the latest timestamp return? What if `VERSIONS=2`?

### Step 1 — Identify Stored Versions (VERSIONS=3)
HBase keeps the 3 most recent versions: timestamps t=2, t=3, t=4 with values `"B"`, `"C"`, `"D"`. The oldest (t=1, `"A"`) is purged.

### Step 2 — Result of `get`
HBase returns the **latest version first** by default → `value="D"`.

### Step 3 — If VERSIONS=2
Now only t=3, t=4 are kept → `value="D"` is still the answer for the default get, but the cell history now contains 2 versions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** Versioning is what makes HBase suitable for time-series and audit-log use cases. It is NOT free — it increases storage and compaction cost. Always specify `VERSIONS` carefully.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 HBase Cluster Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client[Client Application] -->|1. Locate Meta| ZK[ZooKeeper Ensemble ZK1 ZK2 ZK3]
    ZK -->|2. Return Meta RS Address| Client
    Client -->|3. Query Row Region| MetaRS[RegionServer hosting hbase:meta]
    MetaRS -->|4. Return Region Info| Client
    Client -->|5. Direct Get Put Scan| DataRS1[RegionServer RS1]
    Client -->|5. Direct Get Put Scan| DataRS2[RegionServer RS2]
    Client -->|5. Direct Get Put Scan| DataRS3[RegionServer RS3]
    Master[HMaster] -->|Assigns Regions| DataRS1
    Master -->|Assigns Regions| DataRS2
    Master -->|Assigns Regions| DataRS3
    Master -->|Cluster Coordination| ZK
    DataRS1 -->|Reads Writes| HDFS1[HDFS DataNode]
    DataRS2 -->|Reads Writes| HDFS2[HDFS DataNode]
    DataRS3 -->|Reads Writes| HDFS3[HDFS DataNode]
    HDFS1 -->|Heartbeat| NameNode[HDFS NameNode]
    HDFS2 -->|Heartbeat| NameNode
    HDFS3 -->|Heartbeat| NameNode
```

## 4.2 HBase Data Model — Logical View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[HBase Table: student] --> R1[Row: S001]
    T --> R2[Row: S002]
    T --> R3[Row: S003]
    R1 --> CF1[Column Family: profile]
    R1 --> CF2[Column Family: academics]
    CF1 --> CQ1[Qualifier: name]
    CF1 --> CQ2[Qualifier: branch]
    CF1 --> CQ3[Qualifier: semester]
    CF2 --> CQ4[Qualifier: marks_sub1]
    CF2 --> CQ5[Qualifier: marks_sub2]
    CF2 --> CQ6[Qualifier: marks_sub3]
    CQ1 --> TS1[Timestamp t1: Anand Kumar]
    CQ1 --> TS2[Timestamp t2: Anand K]
    CQ4 --> TS3[Timestamp t5: 85]
```

## 4.3 RegionServer Internal Architecture (Storage Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ClientReq[Client Request Put Get Scan] --> WAL[Write Ahead Log HLog on HDFS]
    WAL --> MemStore[MemStore In Memory Sorted Buffer per CF]
    MemStore -->|Flush at 128 MB| HFile[HFile on HDFS Immutable Sorted]
    ClientReq --> BlockCache[BlockCache LRU 64 KB blocks]
    BlockCache -->|Hot Data| ClientResp[Response to Client]
    HFile -->|Bloom Filter Check| Lookup[Region Lookup]
    MemStore --> Lookup
    BlockCache --> Lookup
    Lookup --> ClientResp
    HFile -->|Minor Compaction| HFile
    HFile -->|Major Compaction| HFile2[Merged HFile]
```

## 4.4 HBase Write Path Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client Put] --> B[Locate Region via ZK and Meta]
    B --> C[Send to RegionServer]
    C --> D[Write to WAL HLog]
    D --> E[Write to MemStore]
    E --> F[MemStore full 128 MB]
    F --> G[Flush to new HFile on HDFS]
    G --> H[Return Ack to Client]
    H -.->|Background| I[Minor Compaction merges HFiles]
    I -.->|Background| J[Major Compaction single HFile]
```

## 4.5 HBase vs HDFS vs RDBMS — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start{Need Random Real Time Read Write?}
    Start -->|Yes| HBase[Use HBase on top of HDFS]
    Start -->|No| Batch{Need Batch Analytics?}
    Batch -->|Yes| HDFS[Use HDFS with MapReduce or Spark]
    Batch -->|No| Trans{Need ACID Transactions?}
    Trans -->|Yes| RDBMS[Use RDBMS MySQL PostgreSQL]
    Trans -->|No| HBase
```
