# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]
**Define HBase. List any four features of HBase that distinguish it from a traditional RDBMS.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Apache HBase is an open-source, distributed, non-relational (NoSQL), versioned database built on top of HDFS, modeled after Google's Bigtable. It provides real-time random read/write access to large sparse datasets.

**Four distinguishing features (2 marks):**
1. **Schema flexibility:** Column qualifiers are dynamic; no ALTER TABLE needed to add new fields.
2. **Horizontal scalability:** Scales by adding RegionServers (commodity hardware), not by upgrading a single node.
3. **Sparse, multi-dimensional storage:** Cells that are absent consume no storage — ideal for sparse big data.
4. **Versioning:** Every cell is timestamped; HBase keeps the N most recent versions by default (N=3), enabling time-travel queries.
5. *(Bonus point: strong consistency per row + built on HDFS for fault tolerance.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**What is the role of ZooKeeper in an HBase cluster? What happens if ZooKeeper fails?**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of ZooKeeper (2 marks):**
- Maintains cluster state in znodes (e.g., `/hbase/rs/*` for live RegionServers, `/hbase/meta-region-server` for meta table location).
- Acts as the **coordination service** between HMaster, RegionServers, and Clients.
- Performs **leader election** for the HMaster (active + backup masters).
- Stores root-region location, schema, and assignment metadata.

**Failure impact (1 mark):**
- If ZK fails (and the entire ensemble goes down, not just one node), the HBase cluster **becomes unavailable** for writes and may become inconsistent.
- Reads may continue from cached client state, but new region lookups will fail.
- **HBase cannot function without ZooKeeper** — it is a hard dependency.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> In 14-mark HBase questions, students commonly lose marks at three points:
> 1. **Confusing HDFS with HBase** — HDFS is the storage layer (flat files, sequential); HBase is the database layer (random access, indexed). Do not mix up their roles.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify column families in `create`** — without CFs, the command fails. Always write `create 'table', 'cf1', 'cf2'`.
> 3. **Writing `delete` instead of `deleteall` for row removal** — `delete` removes a single cell, not the row. To remove a full row, use `deleteall 'table', 'rowkey'`.
> 4. **Forgetting the `disable` step before `drop`** — `drop` fails on an enabled table. Always write `disable 'table'; drop 'table'`.
> 5. **Mis-stating Region size as 64 MB or 128 MB** — the standard textbook value is **256 MB** (HBase 0.94+). Some newer versions default to 10 GB, but for KTU, **256 MB is the safe answer**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**(a)** Explain the HBase data model with a suitable diagram. Discuss the concepts of row key, column family, column qualifier, cell, and timestamp. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe the architecture of HBase. Explain the role of HMaster, RegionServer, and ZooKeeper with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer (a) — HBase Data Model (7 marks)

The HBase data model is a **sparse, distributed, persistent, multi-dimensional sorted map**. Formally, the map is indexed by a four-part key:

$$
(\text{RowKey},\ \text{ColumnFamily},\ \text{ColumnQualifier},\ \text{Timestamp}) \rightarrow \text{Value}
$$

**Components (5 marks):**
1. **Row Key** — Unique byte-string identifier of a row. Rows are stored in **lexicographic order** by row key. Design of the row key is the single most important schema decision because it determines data locality and scan performance.
2. **Column Family** — A physical grouping of columns. Defined at table creation. All members of a family are stored together in HFiles. Number of families should be small (≤ 4) for performance.
3. **Column Qualifier** — Sub-column inside a family. Schema-less — can be created on the fly. A family can have millions of distinct qualifiers.
4. **Cell** — The intersection of (row, family, qualifier, timestamp). Holds an uninterpreted byte-array value.
5. **Timestamp** — A 64-bit integer (epoch ms by default). Identifies a version. HBase keeps the N most recent versions (default N=3).

**Sparsity example (1 mark):** A row with 1000 possible columns where only 10 are populated still occupies storage for only 10 cells. This is the defining feature for big data use cases (e.g., IoT sensor data).

**Diagram (1 mark):** See Section 4.2 — the logical view Mermaid diagram.

#### Model Answer (b) — HBase Architecture (7 marks)

HBase uses a **centralized coordination + distributed workers** model with three core components (plus HDFS):

1. **HMaster (1.5 marks):**
   - Lightweight coordinator process. Manages DDL operations (`create`, `delete`, `enable`, `disable` tables).
   - Assigns regions to RegionServers during startup and rebalance.
   - Handles RegionServer failover — if an RS dies, the HMaster reassigns its regions to live RSes.
   - Does **NOT** participate in the read/write data path.

2. **RegionServer (2.5 marks):**
   - The **worker node** that serves client reads and writes.
   - Each RS hosts multiple **Regions** (contiguous row ranges of a table).
   - Internally contains: **WAL** (durability), **MemStore** (in-memory write buffer), **BlockCache** (read cache), and **HFiles** (immutable on-disk sorted files).
   - Write path: WAL → MemStore → flush to HFile.
   - Read path: BlockCache + MemStore + HFile scan (with Bloom filter optimization).

3. **ZooKeeper (1.5 marks):**
   - Distributed coordination service (quorum of odd number, typically 3 or 5).
   - Maintains znodes for: live RegionServer list, meta table location, current HMaster address.
   - Performs leader election for the HMaster (active + standby).
   - **Mandatory dependency** — HBase cannot run without it.

4. **HDFS (1 mark):**
   - Underlying storage for all HFiles, WAL, and the `hbase:meta` table.
   - Provides fault tolerance via HDFS replication (default 3x).

5. **Diagram (0.5 marks):** See Section 4.1 Mermaid block.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Alternative Choice]
**(a)** Compare and contrast HBase with RDBMS and HDFS. Highlight the use cases where HBase is preferred. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Write the HBase shell commands to perform the following operations on a table named `library` with column families `book` and `borrower`:
   (i) Create the table.
   (ii) Insert a row with row key `B001`, family `book` with qualifiers `title`="HBase Guide" and `author`="George", and family `borrower` with qualifier `name`="Anand".
   (iii) Retrieve the row `B001`.
   (iv) Update the `title` to "HBase Definitive Guide" and scan the table.
   (v) Delete the entire row `B001`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer (a) — HBase vs RDBMS vs HDFS (7 marks)

| Dimension (1 mark for the row of comparison + 1 mark for use cases) | HBase | RDBMS | HDFS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Model** | Wide-column, sparse, versioned | Relational, fixed schema | Flat files, no schema |
| **Scalability** | Horizontal (add RS) | Vertical (bigger box) | Horizontal (add DN) |
| **Access Pattern** | Real-time random R/W (low-latency) | Random R/W (low-latency) | Sequential R/W (high-throughput) |
| **Consistency** | Strong per row | Strong (ACID) | Eventual (write-once) |
| **Query** | Get/Scan/Filter (no SQL native) | SQL | MapReduce / Spark |
| **Data Size** | Petabytes | GBs–TBs (typical) | Petabytes |
| **CAP Class** | CP | CA | AP |

**Use cases where HBase is preferred (2 marks):**
- **Time-series data:** IoT sensor streams, financial tick data — versioned cells are perfect.
- **Messaging / chat backends:** Facebook Messenger used HBase for years.
- **Write-heavy workloads:** Log aggregation, clickstream analytics.
- **Sparse, wide tables:** User profiles with hundreds of optional fields (most rows have only a few populated).
- **Real-time OLAP on Hadoop:** Random R/W complements MapReduce's batch analytics.

**Where HBase is NOT preferred (1 mark):**
- Small relational data with complex joins and transactions → use RDBMS.
- Pure batch analytics → use HDFS + Spark.

#### Model Answer (b) — HBase Shell Commands (7 marks)

**(i) Create the table (1 mark):**
```bash
hbase(main):001:0> create 'library', 'book', 'borrower'
```

**(ii) Insert the row B001 (2 marks):**
```bash
hbase(main):002:0> put 'library', 'B001', 'book:title', 'HBase Guide'
hbase(main):003:0> put 'library', 'B001', 'book:author', 'George'
hbase(main):004:0> put 'library', 'B001', 'borrower:name', 'Anand'
```

**(iii) Retrieve row B001 (1 mark):**
```bash
hbase(main):005:0> get 'library', 'B001'
```

**(iv) Update title and scan (2 marks):**
```bash
hbase(main):006:0> put 'library', 'B001', 'book:title', 'HBase Definitive Guide'
hbase(main):007:0> scan 'library'
```

**(v) Delete the entire row B001 (1 mark):**
```bash
hbase(main):008:0> deleteall 'library', 'B001'
```

> **Optional follow-up (mention in answer for full marks):** Major compaction reclaims the storage. To remove the table entirely: `disable 'library'; drop 'library'`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets for KTU Vivas & ESE:**

- ✅ **HBase = Google Bigtable + HDFS** — open-source NoSQL wide-column store with real-time random R/W.
- ✅ **Data model is a 4D sparse sorted map:** `(RowKey, CF, CQ, Timestamp) → Value`.
- ✅ **Row keys are sorted lexicographically** — design them carefully (avoid hot-spotting; use hashing/salting for sequential keys).
- ✅ **Column families = physical grouping** — keep the count small (≤ 4); defined at table creation.
- ✅ **Column qualifiers = dynamic, schema-less** — added on the fly during a `put`.
- ✅ **Cells store byte[] values** — HBase is **type-agnostic** at the storage layer.
- ✅ **Default Region size = 256 MB**; default block size = **64 KB**; default versions = **3**; MemStore flush at **128 MB**.
- ✅ **HMaster:** DDL + region assignment + failover coordination. Lightweight. NOT on data path.
- ✅ **RegionServer:** the WORKER. Holds WAL + MemStore + BlockCache + HFiles. Serves all reads/writes.
- ✅ **ZooKeeper:** mandatory coordination. Stores live RS list, meta table location, HMaster election.
- ✅ **HDFS:** underlying storage. HFile + WAL are stored here. Replication factor typically 3.
- ✅ **Write path:** Client → RS → **WAL** → **MemStore** → (flush at threshold) → **HFile on HDFS**.
- ✅ **Read path:** Client → RS → **BlockCache + MemStore + HFiles** (filtered by Bloom filter).
- ✅ **HFile is immutable + sorted** — enables binary search and Bloom filter pruning.
- ✅ **HBase vs HDFS:** HBase is for real-time random R/W; HDFS is for sequential batch processing.
- ✅ **HBase vs RDBMS:** HBase is schema-flexible, horizontally scalable, sparse; RDBMS is rigid, vertically scalable, dense.
- ✅ **CAP:** HBase chooses **CP** (Consistency + Partition tolerance) per row.
- ✅ **HBase shell commands to memorize:** `create`, `put`, `get`, `scan`, `delete`, `deleteall`, `list`, `count`, `describe`, `disable`, `drop`, `truncate`, `enable`.
- ✅ **For `drop` table:** first `disable`, then `drop` — order matters.
- ✅ **Bloom filter** is what makes random reads fast in HBase — skips HFiles that cannot contain the row.
- ✅ **Compaction:** Minor (merges small HFiles), Major (merges ALL HFiles of a region into one). Major compaction reclaims deleted cells (tombstones).
- ✅ **HBase does NOT support SQL natively** — use Apache Phoenix or Hive on HBase for SQL access.
- ✅ **HBase integration:** HBase is often the serving layer for Hadoop (Spark/Hive writes to HBase; HBase serves real-time queries).

> **End of HBase Module Notes — KTU PBADT504 / 2024 Scheme / Module Experiment 2**

topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module Experiment 2: Understanding MapReduce Programming Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Cognitive Level K2 – Understand**: This experiment focuses on *comprehending* the MapReduce paradigm, its architectural components, and the *flow of data* across mappers and reducers. K2 questions test whether the student can explain, summarize, and interpret concepts — they do **not** require code execution or debugging.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**MapReduce** is a distributed programming model introduced by *Jeffrey Dean and Sanjay Ghemawat* at Google (2004) for processing and generating large datasets on commodity clusters. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, MapReduce is the parallel data processing layer that sits on top of the **HDFS** storage layer of the Hadoop ecosystem.

Formally, MapReduce expresses a computation as two user-defined functions:

$$\text{map}: (k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_2, v_2)$$

$$\text{reduce}: (k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \rightarrow \text{list}(v_3)$$

Where $k_1, v_1$ are the input key-value pairs, $k_2, v_2$ are the intermediate key-value pairs, and $v_3$ are the final output values.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Census Takers of a Country"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy (Library Census Problem):**
> 
> Imagine the Government of India asks you to count how many times each **distinct English word** appears across **10 Crore newspaper articles** stored in a warehouse. One person cannot finish this in years. So the government hires **1,000 workers**:
> 
> 1. **The Warehouse Manager (HDFS)** distributes stacks of newspapers to each worker — this is the *Input Split* phase.
> 2. **Each Worker (Mapper)** reads their pile, and for every word they see, they shout it aloud with a sticky note marked "1" — this is the *Map* phase.
> 3. **Sorting Clerks (Shuffle & Sort Framework)** collect all sticky notes and group them in separate bins by the word printed on them — this is the *Shuffle & Sort* phase.
> 4. **Tally Officers (Reducers)** take each bin, count all the "1"s, and write the final total in a register — this is the *Reduce* phase.
> 
> The final register is your **MapReduce output** stored back in HDFS. Notice that the framework (Hadoop) handled Steps 1, 3, and the *transport* of data — the programmer only had to write the logic for Steps 2 and 4.

## 1.3 Why MapReduce? — The Motivation

| Problem with Traditional RDBMS | How MapReduce Solves It |
|---|---|
| **Vertical scaling** limited (cannot add CPU/RAM beyond a point) | **Horizontal scaling** — just add more commodity nodes |
| **Single point of failure** | Data is **replicated 3 times** by default in HDFS |
| **Schema-on-write** is rigid | Schema is flexible, handles **semi-structured** data (logs, JSON, XML) |
| Fails on **Petabyte-scale** data | Designed for **exabytes** of data |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember for KTU Viva:**
> - Default HDFS Block Size = **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x onwards; was 64 MB in 1.x)
> - Default Replication Factor = **3**
> - NameNode is the **master**, DataNodes are **slaves** in HDFS
> - JobTracker (master) and TaskTracker (slave) in **Hadoop 1.x** MapReduce
> - In **Hadoop 2.x+**, MapReduce runs on **YARN** (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis: Anatomy of a MapReduce Job

A MapReduce job execution follows a strict pipeline of **5 sequential phases**. Understanding each phase is essential for K2-level exam questions.

## 2.1 The Five Phases of MapReduce Execution

### Phase 1 — Input Splitting
- The input file stored in HDFS is logically divided into **input splits**.
- Each split is typically equal to one **HDFS block** (128 MB by default).
- The number of input splits determines the **number of Mappers**.

$$N_{\text{mappers}} = N_{\text{splits}} = \left\lceil \frac{D_{\text{total}}}{B_{\text{block}}} \right\rceil$$

where $D_{\text{total}}$ is the total data size and $B_{\text{block}}$ is the HDFS block size.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters at K2 level:** Exam questions often ask *"How many mappers will run for a 512 MB file with default block size?"* — Answer: $512 / 128 = 4$ mappers.

### Phase 2 — Mapping
- The user-defined `map()` function is invoked once per **(key, value)** record of the input split.
- It emits **zero or more** intermediate key-value pairs.
- Output is written to a **local buffer** (not HDFS — this is a key performance optimization).

### Phase 3 — Combiner (Optional Optimization)
- A **mini-reducer** that runs **on the mapper node** itself, performing local aggregation *before* the data is sent over the network.
- Example: Before shuffling 7 instances of `(hello, 1)`, the combiner can pre-aggregate to `(hello, 7)`, drastically reducing network traffic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Combiner is **NOT guaranteed to execute** — Hadoop decides based on the associativity and commutativity of the reduce function. The output type of combiner must match the input type of reducer.

### Phase 4 — Shuffle and Sort
- The single most **network-intensive** phase.
- Intermediate (key, value) pairs from all mappers are **partitioned, transferred across the network, sorted by key, and merged**.
- Default partitioner = `HashPartitioner`: $\text{partition} = \text{hash}(k) \bmod R$, where $R$ = number of reducers.

### Phase 5 — Reducing
- The user-defined `reduce()` function is invoked once **per unique key**.
- It receives the key and an **iterable list of all values** associated with that key.
- The output is written to HDFS (replicated as per the replication factor).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Mappers | $M = \left\lceil D_{\text{total}} / B_{\text{block}} \right\rceil$ | Cannot be explicitly set; determined by splits |
| Number of Reducers | $R$ (configurable in driver code) | Set by `job.setNumReduceTasks(n)` |
| Hadoop Block Size | **128 MB** (default) | Tunable via `dfs.blocksize` |
| Replication Factor | **3** (default) | Set via `dfs.replication` |
| Partitioner Function | $P(k) = \text{hash}(k) \bmod R$ | Decides which reducer gets which key |
| Combiner Output Constraint | $\text{Output}_{\text{combiner}} = \text{Input}_{\text{reducer}}$ | Type must match |
| Data Locality | Process runs on node where data block resides | Avoids network I/O |
| Speculative Execution | $T_{\text{slow}} > 1.2 \times T_{\text{fast}}$ | Hadoop launches a backup task for stragglers |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** In tables, students often confuse **Mappers** with **Reducers**. Remember: Mappers = Number of Input Splits (automatic), Reducers = Number explicitly set by the programmer.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

MapReduce is foundational to many modern systems:
- **Search engines** (Google used MapReduce to build its web index)
- **Log analytics** at Facebook, Yahoo, Twitter
- **ETL pipelines** for data warehouses
- **Genome sequencing** in bioinformatics
- **Social graph analysis** in recommendation systems

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Worked Example: Word Count via MapReduce

This is the **"Hello World"** of Big Data — appearing in nearly every KTU 2024 exam paper.

## 3.1 Problem Statement

> Given a large text file stored in HDFS, count the frequency of every distinct word and produce output of the form `<word, frequency>`.

## 3.2 Input Data (Toy Example for Understanding)

Suppose the input split assigned to **Mapper 1** contains the text:

$$\text{Line 1: } \text{"dear bear bear"} \quad \text{Line 2: } \text{"bear car car dear"} \quad \text{Line 3: } \text{"car dear car dear bear"}$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Execution

### Step 1: Input Splitting (Framework-Handled)

The Hadoop framework converts each line of the text file into a record of the form `(line_offset, line_text)`. This is the `(k_1, v_1)` pair given to the mapper.

### Step 2: Mapping Phase (User-Defined)

The `map()` function tokenizes each line by whitespace and emits a `(word, 1)` pair for every word encountered.

**Logic of the mapper (pseudocode):**

```
function map(key k1, value v1):
    for each word w in v1.split(" "):
        emit(w, 1)
```

**Emitted intermediate pairs from Mapper 1:**

| Input Line | Tokens | Emitted (key, value) Pairs |
|---|---|---|
| Line 1 | dear, bear, bear | (dear, 1), (bear, 1), (bear, 1) |
| Line 2 | bear, car, car, dear | (bear, 1), (car, 1), (car, 1), (dear, 1) |
| Line 3 | car, dear, car, dear, bear | (car, 1), (dear, 1), (car, 1), (dear, 1), (bear, 1) |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the *order* of emission is **not guaranteed** by the framework — it depends on the JVM, the buffer flushing schedule, and partition assignment. K2 questions often test this.

### Step 3: Combiner Phase (Optional, Local Aggregation)

If a combiner is used, it performs a **local pre-aggregation** on the mapper's output before sending data over the network.

**Local aggregation result on Mapper 1's output:**

$$\{(\text{bear}, 3),\ (\text{car}, 4),\ (\text{dear}, 4)\}$$

This reduces **10 pairs → 3 pairs**, saving significant network bandwidth.

### Step 4: Shuffle and Sort Phase (Framework-Handled)

Assuming there is only one Reducer ($R = 1$), all intermediate pairs are sorted alphabetically and grouped by key.

**Sorted and grouped intermediate state:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{dear} &\rightarrow [1, 1, 1, 1] \\
\text{bear} &\rightarrow [1, 1, 1] \\
\text{car}  &\rightarrow [1, 1, 1, 1]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Reducing Phase (User-Defined)

The `reduce()` function sums up the values for each key.

**Logic of the reducer (pseudocode):**

```
function reduce(key k2, values list(v2)):
    sum = 0
    for each v in values:
        sum = sum + v
    emit(k2, sum)
```

**Mathematical aggregation:**

$$C(w) = \sum_{v \in \text{list}(v_2)} v$$

For each word $w$:

$$\begin{aligned}
C(\text{bear}) &= 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 \\
C(\text{car})  &= 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4 \\
C(\text{dear}) &= 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Final Output to HDFS

| Word | Count |
|---|---|
| bear | 3 |
| car  | 4 |
| dear | 4 |

This output is written back to HDFS, replicated **3 times** across the cluster for fault tolerance.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Phase-Wise Responsibility

| Phase | Handled By | Function Type | Output Storage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input Splitting | Hadoop Framework | Automatic | Logical splits |
| Mapping | **User-Defined** | `map(k1, v1)` | Local buffer (RAM) |
| Combiner | **User-Defined** (Optional) | `reduce(k2, list[v2])` | Local buffer (RAM) |
| Shuffle & Sort | Hadoop Framework | Automatic | Network + Disk |
| Reducing | **User-Defined** | `reduce(k2, list[v2])` | HDFS (replicated) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical K2 Distinction:** Students often incorrectly say *"Mapper writes to HDFS"*. In reality, the **mapper writes to a local disk buffer on the mapper's own node**, and only the **reducer writes to HDFS**. This avoids network bottlenecks during the map phase.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MapReduce Data Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input File on HDFS] --> B1[Split 1: 128 MB]
    A --> B2[Split 2: 128 MB]
    A --> B3[Split 3: 128 MB]

    B1 --> C1[Mapper 1]
    B2 --> C2[Mapper 2]
    B3 --> C3[Mapper 3]

    C1 --> D1[Local Buffer M1]
    C2 --> D2[Local Buffer M2]
    C3 --> D3[Local Buffer M3]

    D1 --> E1[Combiner M1]
    D2 --> E2[Combiner M2]
    D3 --> E3[Combiner M3]

    E1 --> F[Shuffle and Sort Framework]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F

    F --> G1[Reducer 1]
    F --> G2[Reducer 2]

    G1 --> H[Final Output HDFS]
    G2 --> H

    H --> I[Replicated Block 1]
    H --> J[Replicated Block 2]
    H --> K[Replicated Block 3]
```

## 4.2 MapReduce on YARN — Resource Management View

```mermaid
graph TB
    Client[Client Application] --> RM[ResourceManager YARN]

    RM --> NM1[NodeManager 1]
    RM --> NM2[NodeManager 2]
    RM --> NM3[NodeManager 3]

    NM1 --> AM[ApplicationMaster]
    AM --> C1[Container Mapper Task]
    AM --> C2[Container Reducer Task]

    C1 --> C2
```

## 4.3 Word Count — Conceptual Data Transformation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Raw Text dear bear bear] --> B[Map Function Tokenize]
    B --> C[Intermediate dear 1 bear 1 bear 1]
    C --> D[Shuffle and Sort Group by Key]
    D --> E[Grouped dear 1 1 bear 1 1 1]
    E --> F[Reduce Function Sum Values]
    F --> G[Final dear 2 bear 3]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In the diagrams above, observe the *linear data dependency*: **Mappers must complete fully before Reducers can begin**. This is a fundamental constraint of MapReduce and a frequent K2 examination question — *"Can a reducer start before all mappers finish?"* — **No**, it cannot.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Define MapReduce. List and briefly explain its two primary functions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
MapReduce is a distributed programming model proposed by Google in 2004 for processing massive datasets in parallel on commodity clusters. It works on the **divide-and-conquer** principle, splitting work across many nodes for horizontal scalability.

The two primary functions are:

1. **Map Function** (1 Mark): The user-defined function that processes each input `(key1, value1)` pair independently and emits zero or more intermediate `(key2, value2)` pairs. It runs in parallel on mapper nodes.

2. **Reduce Function** (1 Mark): The user-defined function that aggregates all intermediate values for a given key. It receives `(key2, list(values2))` and emits the final output `(key3, value3)` pairs. It runs in parallel on reducer nodes.

The Hadoop framework automatically handles data partitioning, shuffling, sorting, and fault tolerance between these two user-defined stages (1 Mark).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**Differentiate between Mapper and Combiner in MapReduce.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Mapper (1.5 Marks) | Combiner (1.5 Marks) |
|---|---|---|
| **When it runs** | During the Mapping phase | After Mapping, before Shuffle & Sort |
| **Where it runs** | On the mapper node | On the **same** mapper node (local) |
| **Purpose** | Tokenize / process input data | Local aggregation to reduce network traffic |
| **Mandatory?** | Yes — every job must have a mapper | No — it is an optional optimization |
| **Output written to** | Local buffer (RAM + spill to local disk) | Local buffer (then sent to reducers) |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For 14-mark MapReduce questions, examiners allocate **7 marks for the diagram + explanation** and **7 marks for the algorithm/walkthrough**. Students who skip the diagram or omit data-locality details typically lose 2–3 marks. **Always draw a labeled block diagram** showing Input → Split → Map → Shuffle/Sort → Reduce → Output.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Explain the complete MapReduce execution flow with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of HDFS, InputFormat, RecordReader, and OutputFormat. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the Word Count algorithm in MapReduce. Show the intermediate (key, value) pairs emitted by the mapper and the final output produced by the reducer. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**The MapReduce execution flow consists of 5 phases:**

**Phase 1 – Input Splitting (1 Mark):**
The `InputFormat` class (default: `TextInputFormat`) validates the input and divides the HDFS file into logical splits. For a file of size $D$ MB with default block size $128$ MB, the number of splits is:
$$N_{\text{splits}} = \left\lceil D / 128 \right\rceil$$
A `RecordReader` then converts each split into individual `(line_offset, line_text)` records and feeds them to the mapper.

**Phase 2 – Mapping (1 Mark):**
The user-defined `map()` function is invoked once per record. Output is written to a circular in-memory buffer (default 100 MB). When 80% of the buffer is full, the remaining 20% is reserved for spilling, and the buffer contents are spilled to the local disk of the mapper node.

**Phase 3 – Combiner (Optional) (0.5 Mark):**
Runs a local mini-reduce on the spilled data to pre-aggregate values per key, minimizing data shuffled over the network.

**Phase 4 – Shuffle and Sort (1.5 Marks):**
The `Partitioner` (default: `HashPartitioner`) assigns each key to a reducer using $\text{hash}(k) \bmod R$. Data from all mapper nodes is **transferred over the network** to the assigned reducer nodes, then **merged and sorted by key**. This is the most network-intensive stage.

**Phase 5 – Reducing and Output (1.5 Marks):**
The `reduce()` function processes the sorted groups. The `OutputFormat` (default: `TextOutputFormat`) writes the final `(key, value)` pairs to HDFS as a new file, which is then replicated across DataNodes (default replication = 3).

**Block Diagram (1.5 Marks):**
```
Input File (HDFS)
      ↓
[InputFormat → RecordReader]
      ↓
[Split 1] [Split 2] [Split 3]
   ↓        ↓        ↓
[Mapper 1][Mapper 2][Mapper 3]
   ↓        ↓        ↓
[Combiner][Combiner][Combiner]   (optional)
   ↓        ↓        ↓
[  Shuffle & Sort  ]   ← Partitioner routes by key
   ↓        ↓
[Reducer 1][Reducer 2]
   ↓        ↓
[OutputFormat → Writer]
   ↓
HDFS (replicated 3 times)
```

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Problem:** Count occurrences of each word in a text file.

**Input:** `Hello World Hello Hadoop World`

**Mapper Logic (2 Marks):**
The mapper tokenizes the input line by whitespace and emits `(word, 1)` for each token:
```
emit(word, 1)
```

**Intermediate Output (2 Marks):**
| Key (Word) | Value (Count) |
|---|---|
| Hello | 1 |
| World | 1 |
| Hello | 1 |
| Hadoop | 1 |
| World | 1 |

**Reducer Logic (1 Mark):**
The reducer sums all the values for each unique key:
```
sum = sum + value
emit(word, sum)
```

**Final Output (1 Mark):**
| Key (Word) | Final Count |
|---|---|
| Hadoop | 1 |
| Hello | 2 |
| World | 2 |

**Mathematical Expression (1 Mark):**
$$C(w) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I(w = w_i)$$
where $I$ is the indicator function that returns 1 if the word at position $i$ matches $w$, else 0.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** What are Combiners and Partitioners in MapReduce? Explain their roles, advantages, and limitations with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Hadoop 1.x MapReduce architecture (JobTracker and TaskTracker) and compare it briefly with the YARN-based architecture of Hadoop 2.x. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Combiners (3.5 Marks):**
A Combiner is a **user-defined, optional optimization function** that runs on the mapper node *after* the map output is generated and *before* the data is sent to reducers. It performs **local aggregation** to reduce the volume of intermediate data shuffled across the network.

**Example:** In Word Count, if a mapper produces:
`{(hello,1), (hello,1), (hello,1), (world,1), (world,1)}`
A combiner can locally reduce this to:
`{(hello,3), (world,2)}` — sending 2 pairs instead of 5.

**Advantages:** (i) Reduces network bandwidth by up to **80%** in skewed data; (ii) Decreases shuffle-sort time.
**Limitations:** (i) Not always executed — only when the reduce function is **commutative and associative** (e.g., SUM works, but AVERAGE does not); (ii) Cannot replace the reducer.

**Partitioners (3.5 Marks):**
A Partitioner determines **which reducer** will receive a given intermediate key. The default `HashPartitioner` computes:
$$P(k) = \text{hash}(k) \bmod R$$
where $R$ is the number of reducers. Custom partitioners can be written to optimize data distribution — for example, partitioning customer records by geographic region.

**Example:** With 2 reducers, key `"apple"` (hash = 7) goes to reducer $7 \bmod 2 = 1$.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Hadoop 1.x Architecture (3 Marks):**
Hadoop 1.x uses a **two-tier master-slave architecture**:
- **JobTracker (Master):** Runs on the NameNode. Receives the job from the client, breaks it into tasks, assigns tasks to TaskTrackers, monitors their progress, and handles failure recovery by re-scheduling failed tasks.
- **TaskTracker (Slave):** Runs on every DataNode. Executes the Mapper or Reducer task assigned to it, and sends periodic heartbeats (every 3 seconds) to the JobTracker.

**Limitations of Hadoop 1.x:** (i) **Single point of failure** — if JobTracker crashes, the entire cluster fails; (ii) JobTracker is overloaded with both resource management and job scheduling responsibilities; (iii) Only MapReduce jobs are supported.

**Hadoop 2.x with YARN Architecture (4 Marks):**
YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) **decouples resource management from job scheduling**, providing a three-tier architecture:
- **ResourceManager (RM):** Global master. Allocates cluster resources (CPU, RAM) to applications. Contains a **Scheduler** and **ApplicationsManager (AsM)**.
- **NodeManager (NM):** Per-node slave. Manages containers, monitors resource usage, and sends heartbeats to the RM.
- **ApplicationMaster (AM):** Per-application. Negotiates resources with the RM and works with NodeManagers to execute tasks.

**Advantage:** YARN allows **multiple processing engines** (MapReduce, Spark, Tez, Storm) to share the same cluster, enabling multi-tenancy and better resource utilization.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — K2 MapReduce:**
> 
> - **MapReduce** = Parallel programming model for distributed data processing (Google, 2004).
> - **Two user-defined functions:** `map(k1, v1) → list(k2, v2)` and `reduce(k2, list(v2)) → list(v3)`.
> - **Three framework-handled phases:** Input Splitting, Shuffle & Sort, Output Writing.
> - **Mappers = Input Splits** (automatic); **Reducers = user-defined count**.
> - **Default HDFS block size = 128 MB**; **Default replication = 3**.
> - **Mapper output → local disk buffer** (NOT HDFS); **Reducer output → HDFS**.
> - **Combiner = optional local reducer**; runs on mapper node; commutative + associative required.
> - **Partitioner = routes keys to reducers**; default is `hash(k) mod R`.
> - **Hadoop 1.x** = JobTracker + TaskTracker; **Hadoop 2.x** = YARN (RM + NM + AM).
> - **Reducers cannot start before all mappers finish** (data dependency).
> - **Speculative execution** launches backup tasks for slow nodes (stragglers).
> - Word Count is the **canonical MapReduce example** — know it inside out.
> - **Data Locality:** Process runs on the node where the data block resides — minimizes network I/O.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: K3- Apply
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** K3- Apply

# KTU 2024 Scheme – PBADT504 Big Data Analytics
## Module Experiment 2: Implementing a MapReduce Word Count Program using Hadoop HDFS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Context:** This experiment trains the **K3 – Apply** cognitive level (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy). Students are expected to *apply* the theoretical MapReduce paradigm onto a working Hadoop cluster, execute HDFS shell commands, package a Java/Pig/Hive solution, and observe the parallel processing output. This is the **most frequently asked lab-based question** in the Continuous Evaluation (CE) and End Semester Evaluation (ESE) viva for BDA.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **MapReduce** is a distributed programming model proposed by Google (Dean & Ghemawat, 2004) and adopted by **Apache Hadoop** as its core data processing engine. It processes petabyte-scale datasets by splitting the input, applying a **Map** function (transformation) in parallel across cluster nodes, then a **Reduce** function (aggregation) to consolidate the intermediate results.

**Word Count** is the canonical "Hello World" application of MapReduce. Given an input corpus of textual files, it computes the frequency of every distinct word using the tuple form:

$$ \text{WordCount}: \mathcal{D} \rightarrow \{ (w_i, c_i) \mid w_i \in \text{Vocab}(\mathcal{D}),\ c_i = \sum_{d \in \mathcal{D}} f(d, w_i) \} $$

where $f(d, w_i)$ is the number of occurrences of word $w_i$ in document $d$.

**HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** is the storage backbone of Hadoop, designed with a master/slave architecture that provides:
- **Fault tolerance** through block replication (default replication factor = **3**)
- **Streaming data access** pattern (write-once, read-many)
- **Scalability** up to thousands of commodity nodes

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **"The Library Census" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a librarian is asked: *"How many books mention the word 'Elephant' across 10 million volumes stored in 50 branch libraries?"*
>
> - **HDFS** = The branch library network. The librarian splits the 10 million books into **chunks (blocks)** and stores multiple copies of each chunk in different branches — so a fire in one branch doesn't destroy a book.
> - **Map Step** = Each branch librarian scans their assigned books and prepares a *local tally slip*: `(elephant, 14)`, `(python, 7)`, etc.
> - **Shuffle & Sort** = All branch slips with the same word are collected into a single envelope (e.g., all `(elephant, ...)` slips are bundled together).
> - **Reduce Step** = The head office opens the `elephant` envelope, sums up all the numbers, and writes the final answer: `elephant = 4,82,917`.
>
> This parallel *divide → map → shuffle → reduce* is exactly how Hadoop's MapReduce works on a cluster.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical Constants

| Term | Definition | KTU Standard Value |
|------|-----------|--------------------|
| **Block Size** | HDFS unit of storage | **128 MB** (default in Hadoop 3.x) |
| **Replication Factor** | Number of block copies | **3** (configurable) |
| **NameNode** | Master daemon — metadata server | 1 active + 1 standby |
| **DataNode** | Slave daemon — block storage | Scales horizontally |
| **Mapper** | Processes one input split at a time | 1 per split (default) |
| **Reducer** | Aggregates intermediate key-value pairs | Configured per job |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU explicitly tests students on the **default block size (128 MB)** and **default replication factor (3)**. Memorise these values — they appear in almost every KTU exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 HDFS Architecture – Operational Breakdown

HDFS follows a **Master–Slave topology** with the following moving parts:

- **NameNode (NN)** — The "brain" of HDFS. It stores **metadata** in RAM and on disk (FsImage + EditLogs). It does **NOT** store actual data.
- **DataNode (DN)** — The "worker". It stores actual **blocks** of files. On startup, it sends a heartbeat (default every **3 seconds**) to the NameNode.
- **Secondary NameNode (SNN)** — Misnomer! It is **not a standby NameNode**. It only checkpoints the FsImage periodically (default every **1 hour** or when 1 million edits accumulate).

> [!NOTE]
> In Hadoop 2.x+, the **Standby NameNode** replaced the Secondary NameNode role for true High Availability (HA) via the ZooKeeper-based leader election.

### 2.2 MapReduce Job Lifecycle — The 4 Phases

Every MapReduce job executes through these distinct phases:

1. **Input Split & RecordReader** — The input file is logically divided into *splits* (typically 1 per HDFS block). RecordReader converts raw bytes into `(K1, V1)` pairs (e.g., `LongWritable` offset → `Text` line).
2. **Map Phase** — User-defined `map()` function emits intermediate `(K2, V2)` pairs.
3. **Shuffle & Sort Phase** (framework-managed) — Partitioner (default: `HashPartitioner`) routes each `(K2, V2)` to a specific Reducer. All values for the same key land on the same Reducer, sorted by key.
4. **Reduce Phase** — User-defined `reduce()` function iterates over grouped values and emits final `(K3, V3)` pairs.

### 2.3 The Driver Class — Job Submission

The **Driver** class is the entry point that:
- Creates a `Configuration` and `Job` object.
- Sets the **Mapper**, **Reducer**, **InputFormat** (`TextInputFormat`), **OutputFormat** (`TextOutputFormat`).
- Sets the **Output Key/Value classes** (`Text` and `IntWritable`).
- Specifies the input path on HDFS and the output path (which must NOT pre-exist).
- Submits the job via `job.waitForCompletion(true)`.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------|---------------|
| **Number of Mappers** | $N_{\text{map}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{File Size (MB)}}{\text{Block Size (MB)}} \right\rceil$ | Determined by input splits, not job config |
| **Number of Reducers** | $N_{\text{reduce}} = \texttt{job.setNumReduceTasks}(n)$ | User-controlled; default = 1 |
| **Total HDFS Storage Used** | $S_{\text{total}} = S_{\text{file}} \times R$ | $R$ = replication factor |
| **Replication Penalty** | $\text{Overhead} = (R - 1) \times 100\%$ | For $R = 3$, overhead = **200%** |
| **Cluster Storage Capacity** | $C = N_{\text{DN}} \times D_{\text{DN}}$ bytes | $D_{\text{DN}}$ = per-node disk |
| **Map Task Wall Time** | $T_{\text{map}} = T_{\text{read}} + T_{\text{process}} + T_{\text{write}}$ | Measured per split |
| **Data Locality Speed Gain** | $\text{Speedup} \approx \dfrac{T_{\text{remote}}}{T_{\text{local}}}$ | Local reads avoid network hop |
| **Map → Reduce Intermediate Tuples** | $\vert \text{Intermediate} \vert = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{map}}} \vert \text{Out}_i \vert$ | Shuffled across the network |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol has been replaced with `\vert` in all formulas above. This prevents the markdown table parser from misinterpreting it as a column separator. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value bars in tables.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Word Count underpins inverted index construction.
- **Log Analytics (Cloudera, Datadog):** Counting error codes, request types, IP frequencies.
- **Genomics (NIH 1000 Genomes):** Counting k-mers in DNA sequences using MapReduce.
- **Social Media Sentiment:** Aggregating word frequencies across tweets for trend detection.
- **E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Counting product mentions in customer reviews.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: How Many Mappers for a Given Input?

**Problem Statement:** A log file of size **450 MB** is stored in HDFS with the default block size of **128 MB**. Determine the number of mapper tasks that will be launched by MapReduce.

**Step 1 — Identify the Block Size**
$$ B = 128 \text{ MB} \quad \text{(Hadoop 3.x default)} $$

**Step 2 — Identify the Total File Size**
$$ S = 450 \text{ MB} $$

**Step 3 — Apply the Number-of-Mappers Formula**

$$ N_{\text{map}} = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil $$

**Step 4 — Substitute Values**

$$ N_{\text{map}} = \left\lceil \frac{450}{128} \right\rceil $$

**Step 5 — Compute the Decimal**

$$ \frac{450}{128} = 3.515625 $$

**Step 6 — Apply the Ceiling Function**

$$ N_{\text{map}} = \lceil 3.515625 \rceil = 4 \text{ mappers} $$

**Step 7 — Verify the Block Distribution**

$$ 128 + 128 + 128 + 66 = 450 \text{ MB} \quad \checkmark $$

> Three full blocks of 128 MB and one partial block of **66 MB** ⇒ 4 mappers.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: HDFS Storage Footprint

**Problem Statement:** A 2 GB dataset is uploaded to HDFS. Compute the total physical disk consumption, given the default replication factor $R = 3$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Variables**

$$ S_{\text{file}} = 2 \text{ GB} = 2 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 2048 \text{ MB} $$

$$ R = 3 $$

**Step 2 — Apply the Storage Formula**

$$ S_{\text{total}} = S_{\text{file}} \times R $$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values**

$$ S_{\text{total}} = 2048 \times 3 $$

**Step 4 — Compute the Result**

$$ S_{\text{total}} = 6144 \text{ MB} = 6 \text{ GB} $$

**Step 5 — Compute the Replication Overhead**

$$ \text{Overhead} = (R - 1) \times 100\% = (3 - 1) \times 100\% = 200\% $$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** 2 GB of "logical" data consumes **6 GB** of physical disk. This 200% overhead is the price of fault tolerance in commodity clusters. KTU frequently tests this overhead calculation.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: The WordCount MapReduce Trace

Suppose the input HDFS file (`/input/poem.txt`) contains a single line:

> `big data is big data is data`

**Step 1 — InputSplit & RecordReader Output**

| Offset (K1) | Line (V1) |
|-------------|-----------|
| 0 | `big data is big data is data` |

**Step 2 — TokenizerMapper Output (`map()` function)**

The mapper emits `(word, 1)` for every whitespace-separated token:

| K2 (word) | V2 (count) |
|-----------|------------|
| big | 1 |
| data | 1 |
| is | 1 |
| big | 1 |
| data | 1 |
| is | 1 |
| data | 1 |

**Step 3 — Partitioner (Hash Function)**

Using the default `HashPartitioner`:

$$ \text{partition} = \big( \text{hash}(K2) \;\bmod\; R \big) $$

All keys with the same hash bucket are routed to the same Reducer.

**Step 4 — Shuffle & Sort Phase (Framework-Managed)**

The framework groups all values for each unique key:

| K2 (word) | List of V2 |
|-----------|------------|
| big | [1, 1] |
| data | [1, 1, 1] |
| is | [1, 1] |

**Step 5 — IntSumReducer Output (`reduce()` function)**

The reducer iterates through the list and sums:

| K3 (word) | V3 (total) |
|-----------|------------|
| big | 2 |
| data | 3 |
| is | 2 |

**Step 6 — Final HDFS Output (`/output/part-r-00000`)**

```
big    2
data   3
is     2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Order Caveat:** The HDFS output file is **not** sorted alphabetically. Hadoop only sorts *within* a reducer. The KTU examiner expects students to mention that output ordering is *not guaranteed* without a secondary sort.

---

### 3.4 Reference Pseudocode (Mapper & Reducer Logic)

**Mapper (TokenizerMapper.java):**

```java
public class TokenizerMapper 
       extends Mapper<LongWritable, Text, Text, IntWritable> {
    private final static IntWritable one = new IntWritable(1);
    private Text word = new Text();

    public void map(LongWritable key, Text value, Context context)
            throws IOException, InterruptedException {
        StringTokenizer itr = new StringTokenizer(value.toString());
        while (itr.hasMoreTokens()) {
            word.set(itr.nextToken());
            context.write(word, one);
        }
    }
}
```

**Reducer (IntSumReducer.java):**

```java
public class IntSumReducer 
       extends Reducer<Text, IntWritable, Text, IntWritable> {
    public void reduce(Text key, Iterable<IntWritable> values, Context context)
            throws IOException, InterruptedException {
        int sum = 0;
        for (IntWritable val : values) {
            sum += val.get();
        }
        context.write(key, new IntWritable(sum));
    }
}
```

**Driver Class Skeleton:**

```java
Configuration conf = new Configuration();
Job job = Job.getInstance(conf, "word count");
job.setJarByClass(WordCount.class);
job.setMapperClass(TokenizerMapper.class);
job.setReducerClass(IntSumReducer.class);
job.setOutputKeyClass(Text.class);
job.setOutputValueClass(IntWritable.class);
job.setInputFormatClass(TextInputFormat.class);
job.setOutputFormatClass(TextOutputFormat.class);
FileInputFormat.addInputPath(job, new Path(args[0]));
FileOutputFormat.setOutputPath(job, new Path(args[1]));
System.exit(job.waitForCompletion(true) ? 0 : 1);
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The Driver class is the **most commonly forgotten** part in lab viva. Examiners often ask: *"Which class in the WordCount code specifies the input/output HDFS paths?"* Answer: `FileInputFormat.addInputPath()` and `FileOutputFormat.setOutputPath()` inside the **Driver**, NOT the Mapper/Reducer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HDFS Master–Slave Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client["Client Application"] -->|"Read/Write Request"| NN
    subgraph MasterLayer["Master Layer - NameNode"]
        NN["NameNode"]
        FSImage["FsImage - Metadata Snapshot"]
        EditLog["EditLog - Transaction Log"]
        NN --- FSImage
        NN --- EditLog
    end
    subgraph SlaveLayer1["Slave Layer - DataNodes"]
        DN1["DataNode 1"]
        DN2["DataNode 2"]
        DN3["DataNode 3"]
    end
    subgraph Block1["Block A - Replicated 3x"]
        B1A["DataNode 1"]
        B1B["DataNode 2"]
        B1C["DataNode 3"]
    end
    NN -->|"Heartbeat every 3s Block Report"| DN1
    NN -->|"Heartbeat every 3s Block Report"| DN2
    NN -->|"Heartbeat every 3s Block Report"| DN3
    DN1 -.->|"Replicates"| B1A
    DN2 -.->|"Replicates"| B1B
    DN3 -.->|"Replicates"| B1C
    DN1 --- DN2
    DN2 --- DN3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The `MasterLayer` and `SlaveLayer1` are Mermaid `subgraph` definitions. Inside double-quoted labels, no markdown formatting is used (per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards). All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed (`MasterLayer`, `SlaveLayer1`) to avoid reserved-keyword collisions.

---

### 4.2 MapReduce Job Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputPhase["1. Input Phase"]
        HDFS["HDFS Input File"]
        Split["InputSplit - 128MB blocks"]
        RR["RecordReader"]
        HDFS --> Split --> RR
    end
    subgraph MapPhase["2. Map Phase"]
        M1["Mapper 1"]
        M2["Mapper 2"]
        M3["Mapper 3"]
        M4["Mapper 4"]
    end
    subgraph ShufflePhase["3. Shuffle and Sort Phase"]
        Part["Partitioner - Hash Function"]
        Sort["Framework Sort by Key"]
        Part --> Sort
    end
    subgraph ReducePhase["4. Reduce Phase"]
        R1["Reducer 1"]
        R2["Reducer 2"]
    end
    subgraph OutputPhase["5. Output Phase"]
        HDFSOut["HDFS Output Directory"]
        F1["part-r-00000"]
        F2["part-r-00001"]
    end
    RR --> M1
    RR --> M2
    RR --> M3
    RR --> M4
    M1 --> Part
    M2 --> Part
    M3 --> Part
    M4 --> Part
    Sort --> R1
    Sort --> R2
    R1 --> F1 --> HDFSOut
    R2 --> F2 --> HDFSOut
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture – WordCount Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph StageA["Stage A - HDFS Storage Layer"]
        A1["User Uploads input.txt to HDFS"]
        A2["NameNode Splits into 3 Blocks of 128MB each"]
        A3["Each Block Replicated 3 Times across DataNodes"]
    end
    subgraph StageB["Stage B - Map Phase Parallel Execution"]
        B1["Mapper on Node1 - Processes Block 1"]
        B2["Mapper on Node2 - Processes Block 2"]
        B3["Mapper on Node3 - Processes Block 3"]
        B4["Emit Key=word Value=1 for every token"]
    end
    subgraph StageC["Stage C - Shuffle Sort and Partition"]
        C1["HashPartitioner Routes by Key Hash"]
        C2["Framework Sorts Keys within each Partition"]
        C3["Network Transfer to Reducer Nodes"]
    end
    subgraph StageD["Stage D - Reduce Aggregation"]
        D1["Reducer Receives Grouped List of values"]
        D2["Iterates and Sums IntegerWritable values"]
        D3["Writes Final Count to HDFS Output"]
    end
    StageA --> StageB --> StageC --> StageD
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are **modelled on the KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech assessment pattern** with marks, choice, and Bloom's-level tags matching the official Board Examiner style. The simulated past-year tag `[KTU University Exam]` is for reference only.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1.b]`

> **Differentiate between HDFS and a traditional relational database file system in the context of Big Data Analytics. Mention the default block size and replication factor of HDFS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | HDFS | RDBMS File System |
|--------|------|-------------------|
| Data Model | Write-once, read-many (WORM) | Read-write, transactional (OLTP) |
| Block Size | **128 MB** (default) | 4 KB to 16 KB |
| Replication | **3** (default) | RAID-based, often 1:1 mirroring |
| Schema | Schema-on-read | Schema-on-write |
| Scale | Petabytes, thousands of nodes | Terabytes, single/clustered servers |
| Use Case | Batch analytics | Transactional workloads |

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1.c]`

> **What is a NameNode? Why is it considered a single point of failure, and how does Hadoop 2.x address this issue?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **NameNode (1 mark):** The master daemon of HDFS that stores **metadata** — filenames, directory tree, block-to-Datanode mappings, and replication details — in RAM and on disk (FsImage + EditLog).
- **Single Point of Failure (1 mark):** If the NameNode crashes, the entire cluster becomes inaccessible because all metadata is lost. The DataNodes cannot be located or coordinated.
- **HA Solution in Hadoop 2.x (1 mark):** The **Standby NameNode** (with ZooKeeper-based leader election using **ZooKeeper Failover Controller — ZKFC**) maintains a real-time copy of the NameNode's state via a shared edit log (**Quorum Journal Manager — QJM**). On NameNode failure, the Standby is promoted within seconds, eliminating the SPOF.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern, Module 2, 14-Mark Question]**
> **Note:** You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the complete architecture of HDFS with a neat diagram. Discuss the roles of NameNode, DataNode, and Secondary NameNode. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 1.5 TB log file is uploaded to HDFS. Compute:
- (i) The number of HDFS blocks created.
- (ii) The total physical storage consumed on the cluster.
- (iii) The number of mapper tasks that will be launched.
Assume default block size = 128 MB and replication factor = 3. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — HDFS Architecture (7 marks):**

The HDFS follows a **Master–Slave architecture** (refer to Section 4.1 diagram).

**1. NameNode (2 marks):**
- The master daemon running on a dedicated high-RAM machine.
- Stores metadata: file-to-block mapping, block locations, namespace tree, permissions.
- Holds two on-disk files: **FsImage** (checkpoint of metadata) and **EditLog** (incremental changes).
- Receives **heartbeats** from DataNodes every **3 seconds** to monitor liveness.

**2. DataNode (2 marks):**
- The slave daemon running on every commodity worker node.
- Stores the actual **HDFS blocks** (default 128 MB each) on local disk.
- On startup, registers with the NameNode and sends periodic **block reports** (default every 6 hours).
- Reads/writes blocks as instructed by the NameNode and the client.

**3. Secondary NameNode (2 marks):**
- **Misnomer:** It is **NOT** a backup NameNode.
- Periodically (default every **1 hour** or after **1 million** transactions) downloads the FsImage and EditLog from the NameNode, merges them, and uploads the new FsImage back.
- This prevents the EditLog from growing unboundedly.
- In Hadoop 2.x, this role is replaced by the **Standby NameNode** for true High Availability.

**4. Client Interaction (1 mark):**
- Client contacts NameNode for metadata (block locations).
- Client then talks **directly** to DataNodes for data transfer (no NameNode bottleneck in the data path).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $S = 1.5 \text{ TB}$, $B = 128 \text{ MB}$, $R = 3$.

**Step 1 — Unit Conversion (1 mark)**

$$ S = 1.5 \times 1024 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 1{,}572{,}864 \text{ MB} $$

**Step 2 — Number of Blocks (2 marks)**

$$ N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1{,}572{,}864}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 12288.0 \right\rceil = 12288 \text{ blocks} $$

**Step 3 — Total Physical Storage (2 marks)**

$$ S_{\text{total}} = S \times R = 1{,}572{,}864 \times 3 = 4{,}718{,}592 \text{ MB} = 4.5 \text{ TB} $$

**Step 4 — Number of Mappers (2 marks)**

$$ N_{\text{map}} = N_{\text{blocks}} = 12288 \text{ mappers} $$

> Each block spawns exactly one mapper by default in Hadoop's `TextInputFormat`.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the MapReduce programming model in detail. Explain the role of the Mapper, Combiner, Partitioner, and Reducer with a suitable example. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Trace the complete execution of a MapReduce WordCount job on the following input text, showing all intermediate key-value pairs at each stage. **[7 marks]**

**Input (`/input/data.txt`):**
```
hadoop is big data
big data is powerful
hadoop is scalable
```

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — MapReduce Programming Model (7 marks):**

MapReduce is a **functional programming model** that processes data in two primary user-defined phases plus two framework-managed phases.

**1. Map Phase (2 marks):**
- User implements `map(K1, V1) → List[(K2, V2)]`.
- Each Mapper processes **one input record** at a time.
- Example: For `line = "hadoop is big"`, mapper emits `(hadoop, 1), (is, 1), (big, 1)`.

**2. Combiner (Local Reducer) (1 mark):**
- Runs **on the Mapper node** after the map output to perform a local pre-aggregation.
- Reduces network traffic in the shuffle phase.
- Must be **associative and commutative** (e.g., `IntSumReducer` can also act as a Combiner).

**3. Partitioner (1 mark):**
- Decides which Reducer receives which key.
- Default: `HashPartitioner` → `hash(K2) mod R`.
- Ensures all values for the same key reach the same Reducer.

**4. Shuffle & Sort (Framework) (1 mark):**
- Framework automatically sorts intermediate keys and groups values for each key.
- Data is transferred across the network (HTTP/SSH) to the appropriate Reducer node.

**5. Reduce Phase (2 marks):**
- User implements `reduce(K2, List[V2]) → List[(K3, V3)]`.
- Each Reducer processes **one key group** at a time.
- Example: For `key = "hadoop"`, reducer receives `[1, 1]` and emits `(hadoop, 2)`.

---

**Part (b) — WordCount Trace (7 marks):**

**Stage 1 — InputSplit & RecordReader (1 mark)**

| K1 (Offset) | V1 (Line) |
|-------------|-----------|
| 0 | `hadoop is big data` |
| 21 | `big data is powerful` |
| 43 | `hadoop is scalable` |

**Stage 2 — Mapper Output (2 marks)**

| K2 | V2 |
|----|----|
| hadoop | 1 |
| is | 1 |
| big | 1 |
| data | 1 |
| big | 1 |
| data | 1 |
| is | 1 |
| powerful | 1 |
| hadoop | 1 |
| is | 1 |
| scalable | 1 |

**Stage 3 — Combiner Output (Local Pre-Aggregation) (2 marks)**

If a Combiner is enabled on the same node:

| K2 | V2 (List) |
|----|-----------|
| hadoop | 2 |
| is | 3 |
| big | 2 |
| data | 2 |
| powerful | 1 |
| scalable | 1 |

**Stage 4 — Partitioner & Shuffle (Hash routing to 2 reducers) (1 mark)**

| Reducer 0 | Reducer 1 |
|-----------|-----------|
| big: [2] | data: [2] |
| is: [3] | hadoop: [2] |
| powerful: [1] | scalable: [1] |

(Partition assignment is illustrative — actual hash depends on the keys.)

**Stage 5 — Reducer Output (1 mark)**

| K3 | V3 |
|----|----|
| big | 2 |
| data | 2 |
| hadoop | 2 |
| is | 3 |
| powerful | 1 |
| scalable | 1 |

**HDFS Output Files (`/output/part-r-00000`, `part-r-00001`):**

```
big    2
is     3
powerful 1
```
```
data    2
hadoop  2
scalable 1
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Partitioner with Combiner** (−2 marks): A *Combiner* runs on the **Mapper node for local aggregation*; a *Partitioner* routes keys to Reducers. Many students swap these definitions.
> 2. **Forgetting the Driver class** (−1 mark): A WordCount program has **THREE** classes — Mapper, Reducer, **and** Driver. The Driver contains the `main()` method, sets `Job` configuration, and submits the job.
> 3. **Output directory pre-existence** (−1 mark): HDFS will throw an `org.apache.hadoop.mapred.FileAlreadyExistsException` if the output directory already exists. Always delete or use a unique timestamped path.
> 4. **Mapper count confusion** (−2 marks): Students often write *"one mapper per file"*. The correct rule is **one mapper per HDFS block (= 128 MB)**.
> 5. **Replication factor miscalculation** (−1 mark): The default is **3** (not 2, not 5). The block size is **128 MB** (not 64 MB) in Hadoop 3.x.
> 6. **Forgetting to set `setOutputKeyClass` and `setOutputValueClass`** (−1 mark): Without these, Hadoop throws a `IllegalStateException` at job submission.
> 7. **Not packaging the JAR correctly** (−2 marks in lab): The Driver, Mapper, and Reducer must be compiled into a single JAR (`jar -cvf wordcount.jar *.class`) before running `hadoop jar wordcount.jar WordCount /input /output`.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

**Core Concepts**
- [ ] MapReduce = distributed parallel processing model (Map → Shuffle → Sort → Reduce).
- [ ] WordCount is the canonical "Hello World" of Hadoop.
- [ ] HDFS = Hadoop Distributed File System (write-once, read-many, fault-tolerant).

**Numerical Constants (MUST memorise)**
- [ ] **Default block size = 128 MB** (Hadoop 3.x).
- [ ] **Default replication factor = 3**.
- [ ] **Heartbeat interval = 3 seconds**.
- [ ] **Block report interval = 6 hours** (default).
- [ ] **Secondary NameNode checkpoint = 1 hour or 1 million edits**.

**Key Architecture**
- [ ] **NameNode** = metadata master, in-RAM + FsImage + EditLog.
- [ ] **DataNode** = block storage slave, sends heartbeats.
- [ ] **Secondary NameNode** = NOT a backup; checkpointing only.
- [ ] **Standby NameNode** (Hadoop 2.x+) = true HA via ZKFC + QJM.

**MapReduce Job Pipeline**
- [ ] **Mapper count = ⌈FileSize / BlockSize⌉** (one per split, not per file).
- [ ] **Reducer count** is **user-controlled** (default = 1).
- [ ] **Combiner** = local pre-aggregation, runs on Mapper node, reduces network traffic.
- [ ] **Partitioner** = routes keys to Reducers via `hash(K) mod R`.
- [ ] **Shuffle & Sort** = framework-managed; transfers across network.

**Three Classes in WordCount**
- [ ] `TokenizerMapper` — emits `(word, 1)`.
- [ ] `IntSumReducer` — sums the `1`s.
- [ ] `WordCount` (Driver) — configures `Job`, sets paths, calls `waitForCompletion()`.

**HDFS Storage Math**
- [ ] $S_{\text{total}} = S_{\text{file}} \times R$.
- [ ] Overhead = $(R - 1) \times 100\% = 200\%$ at $R=3$.
- [ ] Storage cost: 2 GB logical → 6 GB physical at $R = 3$.

**Common Exam Traps**
- [ ] Output directory must NOT pre-exist.
- [ ] Output ordering is NOT guaranteed without secondary sort.
- [ ] Combiner must be **associative and commutative**.
- [ ] Always set `setJarByClass()` before `setMapperClass()`.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Pro Tip:** If the question asks *"How many mappers?"* — the answer is **never** the number of files. It is **always** the ceiling of file size divided by block size. This single rule has earned 2 marks in every KTU BDA exam since 2019.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# K4-ANALYSE: HDFS COMMANDS & FILE OPERATIONS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** is the primary storage layer of the Apache Hadoop framework, designed to store very large files across multiple machines in a cluster with streaming data access patterns, fault tolerance, and high throughput. It follows a **master-slave (NameNode-DataNode) architecture** and is built to handle data in the range of **petabytes to exabytes**.

HDFS implements the concept of **Write Once, Read Many (WORM)** — a file is created once and read multiple times, which makes it ideal for batch processing and analytical workloads (not low-latency random reads).

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Library Warehouse" Analogy**
> Imagine a massive national library (the cluster) that cannot fit inside one building. The librarian-in-chief (the **NameNode**) maintains a catalog/index of every book and which branch building stores it. Each branch building (a **DataNode**) holds actual shelves of books. When you request a book, the librarian tells you exactly which branch has it; the book is then delivered from that branch to you. If a branch building burns down, the librarian has 3 photocopies of every book already distributed to other branches — so nothing is lost. This is exactly how HDFS works!

| Component | Real-world Counterpart | KTU Symbol/Constant |
|---|---|---|
| **NameNode** | Librarian-in-chief (Master) | Stores **metadata** only |
| **DataNode** | Branch library (Slave) | Stores **actual data blocks** |
| **Block** | Chapter in a book | Default size: **64 MB (older) / 128 MB (Hadoop 2.x+)** |
| **Replication Factor** | Photocopies in other branches | Default value: **3** |
| **Secondary NameNode** | Assistant librarian (checkpoint helper) | Merges **fsimage + edit logs** |

---

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in Bold)

- **Default Block Size:** **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x and above) — configurable via `dfs.blocksize` in `hdfs-site.xml`
- **Default Replication Factor:** **3** — configurable via `dfs.replication`
- **Heartbeat Interval:** **3 seconds** (DataNode → NameNode)
- **Block Report Interval:** **6 hours** (default)
- **fsimage checkpoint interval:** **1 hour** or when **1 million transactions** accumulate

> [!TIP]
> KTU Board Exam Tip: Always quote **128 MB** as the modern default block size unless the question specifies Hadoop 1.x (where **64 MB** is correct).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HDFS Architecture — Structured Logic Breakdown

HDFS follows a **Master/Slave topology** with the following working principles:

### Step 1: The NameNode (Master) — "The Brain"
- Stores **metadata in RAM** for fast access: filename, directory structure, block-to-Datanode mapping, permissions, replication factor.
- Persists two files to disk:
  - **fsimage** — snapshot of the entire filesystem namespace at a checkpoint.
  - **edit logs (edits)** — log of every metadata change since the last fsimage.
- **Does NOT store actual user data** — only pointers to it.
- **Single point of failure** in HDFS (mitigated by **HA — High Availability** in Hadoop 2+ using Standby NameNode + ZooKeeper).

### Step 2: The DataNode (Slave) — "The Muscle"
- Stores actual **data blocks** on local disk.
- Sends **heartbeat signals** every **3 seconds** to the NameNode.
- Sends **Block Reports** periodically (default every **6 hours**) listing all blocks it stores.
- On startup, registers itself with the NameNode.
- Can run multiple instances per machine in large clusters.

### Step 3: The Secondary NameNode — "The Assistant"
- **Misnamed!** It is NOT a backup NameNode.
- Downloads fsimage + edits from NameNode, merges them into a new fsimage, and uploads it back. This is the **checkpointing process**.
- Reduces NameNode startup time and edit log size.
- In Hadoop 2+ HA setups, replaced by **Standby NameNode**.

### Step 4: The Client — "The User"
- Talks to NameNode for **metadata** (where is the file? which blocks? which DataNodes?).
- Talks **directly** to DataNodes for **actual data transfer** (NameNode is bypassed for I/O — this is the "co-located data" optimization).

---

## 2.2 HDFS File Write Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. Client calls `create()` on `DistributedFileSystem`.
2. RPC to NameNode to create a new file entry in namespace (no blocks allocated yet).
3. NameNode performs various checks (file doesn't exist, permissions OK).
4. NameNode returns a list of suitable DataNodes to host the replicas of the first block.
5. Client pushes data to the first DataNode in the pipeline.
6. That DataNode forwards the data to the next DataNode in the pipeline (chain replication).
7. Each DataNode stores the block and sends a **write confirmation** (ACK) back up the chain.
8. Once the block fills up, the client requests new DataNodes for the next block.
9. When all blocks are written, client calls `close()`.

> [!NOTE]
> The replication pipeline design means the **client only sends ONE copy** of data, and HDFS automatically replicates it to other DataNodes in parallel — saving network bandwidth.

---

## 2.3 HDFS File Read Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. Client calls `open()` on `DistributedFileSystem`.
2. RPC to NameNode to get block locations (closest DataNode first).
3. NameNode returns a list of DataNodes for each block, sorted by network proximity.
4. Client connects **directly** to the nearest DataNode for each block.
5. Data streams from DataNode → Client.
6. If the DataNode fails mid-read, client transparently fails over to the next DataNode holding a replica.
7. Client calls `close()` when done.

---

## 2.4 Fault Tolerance & Self-Healing

- **Heartbeat failure detection:** If a DataNode misses **10 consecutive heartbeats (≈ 30 seconds)**, the NameNode marks it as **dead**.
- **Under-replication trigger:** If a replica is missing, the NameNode instructs another DataNode to replicate the block.
- **Rebalancer:** Distributes data evenly across DataNodes to prevent hotspots.
- **Rack Awareness:** Replicas are placed on **at least 2 different racks** (1 replica local rack, 1 replica different rack, 1 replica random) for disaster recovery.

---

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Number of Blocks per File** | $\vert B \vert = \lceil \dfrac{F}{S} \vert$ | $F$ = File size, $S$ = Block size | Integer |
| 2 | **Total Storage Used (with replication)** | $\vert T \vert = \vert B \vert \times S \times R$ | $R$ = Replication factor | Bytes |
| 3 | **Actual Logical File Size** | $\vert F \vert = \vert B \vert \times S \vert$ | Without counting replicas | Bytes |
| 4 | **Effective Disk Utilization** | $\vert U \vert = \dfrac{\vert F \vert}{\vert T \vert} = \dfrac{1}{R} \vert$ | Always = $1/R$ by definition | Ratio |
| 5 | **Time to Heartbeat Timeout** | $\vert t_{dead} \vert = h_{interval} \times N_{missed} \vert$ | Default: $3 \times 10 = 30$ s | Seconds |
| 6 | **Storage Overhead** | $\vert O \vert = T - F = F \times (R - 1) \vert$ | Extra space consumed by replicas | Bytes |
| 7 | **Write Throughput Bound** | $\vert W \vert = \dfrac{S \times R}{t_{pipeline}} \vert$ | Depends on slowest DataNode | MB/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** HDFS storage calculations are critical in **capacity planning** for enterprise data lakes (Cloudera, AWS EMR, Azure HDInsight), **cost estimation** for cloud storage, and **cluster sizing** decisions. A 1 PB logical dataset with replication factor 3 actually consumes 3 PB of physical storage.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Blocks Required for a Given File

**Problem:** A file of size **F = 640 MB** is to be stored in HDFS with default block size **S = 128 MB**. Calculate the number of blocks.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

We use the ceiling function because the last block, even if partially filled, still occupies a full block on disk.

$$
B = \left\lceil \frac{F}{S} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 1: Substitute the given values into the formula.**

$$
B = \left\lceil \frac{640 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 2: Perform the division.**

$$
B = \left\lceil 5.0 \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3: Apply the ceiling function.**

$$
B = 5 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 4: Verification — Each block is exactly 128 MB (no partial block).**

$$
5 \text{ blocks} \times 128 \text{ MB/block} = 640 \text{ MB} \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Storage Used With Replication (Extended Case)

**Problem:** A file of size **F = 700 MB** is stored in HDFS with block size **S = 128 MB** and replication factor **R = 3**. Calculate the **total physical storage** consumed on the cluster.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate the number of blocks (B).**

$$
B = \left\lceil \frac{F}{S} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
B = \left\lceil \frac{700}{128} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3: Perform division.**

$$
B = \lceil 5.46875 \rceil
$$

**Step 4: Apply ceiling — the last block is only 700 − (5 × 128) = 700 − 640 = 60 MB, but still occupies a full 128 MB block.**

$$
B = 6 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate total physical storage (T).**

$$
T = B \times S \times R
$$

**Step 6: Substitute values.**

$$
T = 6 \times 128 \text{ MB} \times 3
$$

**Step 7: Multiply.**

$$
T = 2304 \text{ MB} = 2.25 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 8: Calculate the storage overhead (O).**

$$
O = T - F = 2304 - 700 = 1604 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 9: Calculate the effective disk utilization (U).**

$$
U = \frac{F}{T} = \frac{700}{2304} \approx 0.3039 \approx 30.39\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The effective disk utilization is **always equal to $1/R$** in the limit of large files, regardless of file size. For $R = 3$, the theoretical maximum utilization is **33.33%** — the rest is overhead for fault tolerance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heartbeat Dead-Node Detection Time

**Problem:** With heartbeat interval **h = 3 seconds** and missed heartbeat threshold **N = 10**, calculate the time taken for the NameNode to declare a DataNode as dead.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the formula.**

$$
t_{dead} = h_{interval} \times N_{missed}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
t_{dead} = 3 \text{ s} \times 10
$$

**Step 3: Multiply.**

$$
t_{dead} = 30 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4: Engineering interpretation.**

After 30 seconds, the NameNode considers the DataNode dead, removes it from the active set, and starts under-replication recovery by instructing other DataNodes to create new replicas of the lost blocks.

---

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix: Common HDFS Command Categories

| # | Category | Example Commands | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **File System Operations** | `hdfs dfs -ls /`, `-mkdir /user`, `-put localfile /hdfs/path`, `-get /hdfs/file localpath` | Browse, create, copy |
| 2 | **File Inspection** | `hdfs dfs -cat`, `-head`, `-tail`, `-text`, `-stat`, `-du`, `-df` | Read & inspect file contents |
| 3 | **File Management** | `hdfs dfs -mv`, `-cp`, `-rm`, `-rm -r`, `-chmod`, `-chown` | Move, delete, modify permissions |
| 4 | **Administration** | `hdfs dfsadmin -report`, `-safemode`, `-refreshNodes` | Cluster health, admin tasks |
| 5 | **Quota Management** | `hdfs dfsadmin -setQuota`, `-setSpaceQuota` | Limit directory size/count |
| 6 | **Replication & Balancing** | `hdfs balancer`, `hdfs dfs -setrep` | Set replication factor of a file |
| 7 | **Archival** | `hdfs archive`, `-expunge` | Hadoop Archives (HAR files) |

---

## 3.5 Sample HDFS Command Workflow (Sequential Analysis)

```bash
# Step 1: List root directory contents
hdfs dfs -ls /

# Step 2: Create a user directory under /user
hdfs dfs -mkdir /user/krish

# Step 3: Create a subdirectory for a project
hdfs dfs -mkdir /user/krish/bda_lab

# Step 4: Upload a local file 'sales_2024.csv' to HDFS
hdfs dfs -put ./sales_2024.csv /user/krish/bda_lab/

# Step 5: List the contents of the destination directory
hdfs dfs -ls /user/krish/bda_lab/

# Step 6: View first 20 lines of the uploaded file
hdfs dfs -head /user/krish/bda_lab/sales_2024.csv

# Step 7: Check size of the file in HDFS
hdfs dfs -du -h /user/krish/bda_lab/sales_2024.csv

# Step 8: Check overall HDFS filesystem usage
hdfs dfs -df -h

# Step 9: Set replication factor to 2 for this file
hdfs dfs -setrep 2 /user/krish/bda_lab/sales_2024.csv

# Step 10: Download the file back to local
hdfs dfs -get /user/krish/bda_lab/sales_2024.csv ./copy_sales.csv

# Step 11: Remove the file from HDFS
hdfs dfs -rm /user/krish/bda_lab/sales_2024.csv

# Step 12: Remove the empty directory
hdfs dfs -rmdir /user/krish/bda_lab/

# Step 13: Get cluster health report
hdfs dfsadmin -report
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Command Syntax Pattern:** All HDFS shell commands begin with `hdfs dfs -<command>` (for user files) or `hadoop fs -<command>` (legacy alias). For cluster administration, use `hdfs dfsadmin -<command>`.
