# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDFS Cluster Architecture (Master-Slave Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client1["Client Application"]
    Client2["MapReduce Job"]
    Client3["HDFS CLI User"]

    subgraph MasterNode["Master Node Cluster"]
        NN["NameNode\n(Metadata in RAM)\nfsimage + edits"]
        SNN["Secondary NameNode\nCheckpointing Service"]
    end

    subgraph Rack1["Rack 1"]
        DN1A["DataNode 1A\nBlock Storage"]
        DN1B["DataNode 1B\nBlock Storage"]
        DN1C["DataNode 1C\nBlock Storage"]
    end

    subgraph Rack2["Rack 2"]
        DN2A["DataNode 2A\nBlock Storage"]
        DN2B["DataNode 2B\nBlock Storage"]
        DN2C["DataNode 2C\nBlock Storage"]
    end

    Client1 -->|"Metadata RPC"| NN
    Client2 -->|"Metadata RPC"| NN
    Client3 -->|"Metadata RPC"| NN
    Client1 -->|"Direct Data I/O"| DN1A
    Client1 -->|"Direct Data I/O"| DN2A
    Client2 -->|"Direct Data I/O"| DN1B
    Client3 -->|"Direct Data I/O"| DN2B

    NN -->|"Heartbeat Check"| DN1A
    NN -->|"Heartbeat Check"| DN1B
    NN -->|"Heartbeat Check"| DN1C
    NN -->|"Heartbeat Check"| DN2A
    NN -->|"Heartbeat Check"| DN2B
    NN -->|"Heartbeat Check"| DN2C

    SNN -->|"Periodic Merge"| NN
    DN1A -->|"Block Report"| NN
    DN2A -->|"Block Report"| NN
```

---

## 4.2 HDFS File Write Pipeline Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Client calls create()"] --> B["RPC to NameNode\nfor namespace entry"]
    B --> C{"NameNode Checks:\nFile exists?\nPermissions?"}
    C -->|"OK"| D["NameNode returns\nDataNode list for Block 1"]
    C -->|"FAIL"| E["Exception to Client"]
    D --> F["Client writes\nPacket to DN1"]
    F --> G["DN1 forwards to DN2\n(pipeline replication)"]
    G --> H["DN2 forwards to DN3"]
    H --> I["ACK flows back\nDN3 - DN2 - DN1 - Client"]
    I --> J{"Block full?"}
    J -->|"No, more data"| F
    J -->|"Yes"| K{"More blocks?"}
    K -->|"Yes"| D
    K -->|"No"| L["Client calls close()"]
    L --> M["Write Successful"]
```

---

## 4.3 HDFS File Read Pipeline Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Client calls open()"] --> B["RPC to NameNode\nfor block locations"]
    B --> C["NameNode returns\nsorted DataNode list\nby network proximity"]
    C --> D["Client connects to\nnearest DataNode"]
    D --> E["Data streams\nDN to Client"]
    E --> F{"Read successful?"}
    F -->|"Yes, more blocks"| D
    F -->|"No, more blocks"| C
    F -->|"All done"| G["Client calls close()"]
    F -->|"DN Failed mid-read"| H["Failover to\nnext DataNode\nin the replica list"]
    H --> E
```

---

## 4.4 Block Replication Strategy (Rack-Aware Placement)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    File["Original File Block\non Client/Local"]
    Rack1Local["Rack 1: Local Node\n(1st Replica)"]
    Rack1Remote["Rack 1: Different Node\n(2nd Replica)"]
    Rack2Remote["Rack 2: Different Node\n(3rd Replica)"]

    File -->|"Replica 1\n(Local Read Optimization)"| Rack1Local
    File -->|"Replica 2\n(Different Node, Same Rack)"| Rack1Remote
    File -->|"Replica 3\n(Different Rack for DR)"| Rack2Remote
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Note:** The default Hadoop rack-aware policy places 2 replicas on the same rack (one node local) and 1 replica on a different rack. This balances **read performance** (local node access) with **disaster recovery** (different rack). A custom `BlockPlacementPolicy` can override this.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the NameNode in HDFS. Why is it considered a single point of failure?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **NameNode** is the master server in HDFS that manages the metadata of all files stored in the cluster. It maintains the **filesystem namespace tree** and the mapping of all files to their constituent **blocks** and the **DataNodes** where those blocks reside. It tracks file names, directory structure, permissions, block sizes, and replication factors, all kept in RAM for fast access.

It is considered a **single point of failure** because, in a standard HDFS setup (without High Availability), if the NameNode goes down, the entire cluster becomes inaccessible — DataNodes cannot be located, no new files can be created, and existing files cannot be read. This is mitigated in Hadoop 2+ by **High Availability (HA)** setups with a **Standby NameNode** and **ZooKeeper-based leader election**.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for role, 1 mark for metadata, 1 mark for SPOF explanation.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the default block size in HDFS? How does it differ from the traditional file system block size?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **default block size in HDFS is 128 MB** (in Hadoop 2.x and above; it was 64 MB in Hadoop 1.x). It is **significantly larger** than traditional file system block sizes, which are typically **4 KB** (Linux ext4) or **512 bytes** (old disks).

The reason for this large block size is to **minimize the cost of disk seeks**. Since HDFS is designed for streaming reads of large files (gigabytes to terabytes), having fewer, larger blocks reduces the number of disk seek operations and maximizes sequential I/O throughput. The large block size also reduces the amount of metadata the NameNode must store in memory, allowing the cluster to scale to petabytes of data.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for value, 1 mark for traditional size, 1 mark for reason.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With a neat diagram, explain the HDFS architecture. List the responsibilities of NameNode and DataNode.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

HDFS follows a **master-slave architecture** consisting of a single **NameNode (master)** and multiple **DataNodes (slaves)**, typically deployed across commodity hardware in a rack-aware manner.

**(i) NameNode Responsibilities (3.5 marks):**
- Maintains **metadata** of all files: filenames, directory structure, block IDs, block-to-DataNode mapping, permissions, and replication count.
- Stores metadata in **RAM** for fast lookups; persists to **fsimage** (snapshot) and **edit logs** (write-ahead log) on local disk.
- Manages **client access** — receives RPC requests for file open/close/rename operations.
- **Monitors DataNode health** via heartbeat signals every 3 seconds.
- Performs **re-replication** when blocks become under-replicated.
- Coordinates **rebalancing** and **rack-aware placement** of new blocks.

**(ii) DataNode Responsibilities (3.5 marks):**
- Stores **actual data blocks** on the local filesystem (e.g., ext4, XFS) of the slave machine.
- On startup, **registers** with the NameNode and performs a handshake.
- Sends **heartbeats** to the NameNode every 3 seconds to signal liveness.
- Sends **block reports** periodically (default every 6 hours) listing all HDFS blocks stored locally.
- Serves **read/write requests** from clients directly (bypassing NameNode for actual data transfer).
- Performs **block creation, deletion, and replication** as instructed by the NameNode.
- Manages **pipeline replication** during writes — forwarding data to the next DataNode in the chain.

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the detailed Mermaid architecture diagram, which serves as the textual diagram for the exam.)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A file of size 1.2 GB needs to be stored in HDFS with a block size of 256 MB and replication factor 3. Calculate: (i) Number of blocks, (ii) Total physical storage consumed, (iii) Effective disk utilization, (iv) Storage overhead.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $F = 1.2$ GB, $S = 256$ MB, $R = 3$.

**Step 1: Convert units to common basis.**

$$
1.2 \text{ GB} = 1.2 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 1228.8 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the number of blocks (B).**

$$
B = \left\lceil \frac{F}{S} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1228.8 \text{ MB}}{256 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil
$$

$$
B = \lceil 4.8 \rceil = 5 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate total physical storage (T).**

$$
T = B \times S \times R = 5 \times 256 \text{ MB} \times 3
$$

$$
T = 3840 \text{ MB} = 3.75 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate effective disk utilization (U).**

$$
U = \frac{F}{T} = \frac{1228.8 \text{ MB}}{3840 \text{ MB}} = 0.32 = 32\%
$$

**Step 5: Calculate storage overhead (O).**

$$
O = T - F = 3840 \text{ MB} - 1228.8 \text{ MB} = 2611.2 \text{ MB} \approx 2.55 \text{ GB}
$$

**Final Tabular Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| File Size (F) | 1.2 GB (1228.8 MB) |
| Number of Blocks (B) | 5 |
| Total Storage (T) | 3.75 GB (3840 MB) |
| Effective Utilization (U) | 32% |
| Storage Overhead (O) | 2.55 GB (2611.2 MB) |

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the HDFS file read and write process in detail with suitable diagrams.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**HDFS File Write Process (3.5 marks):**

1. The client invokes the `create()` method on `DistributedFileSystem` to request file creation.
2. The client performs an **RPC call to the NameNode** to create a new file entry in the namespace. The NameNode performs validation checks (file does not exist, client has write permission).
3. After successful validation, the NameNode returns a list of **DataNodes** to host the replicas of the first block. These DataNodes are arranged in a **pipeline** (e.g., DN1 → DN2 → DN3).
4. The client splits the file into packets (default 64 KB each) and pushes the first packet to the first DataNode (DN1) in the pipeline.
5. DN1 stores the packet and forwards it to DN2, which stores it and forwards to DN3. This is the **pipeline replication** model.
6. Each DataNode sends an **acknowledgment (ACK)** back up the chain (DN3 → DN2 → DN1 → Client) once the packet is written to its local disk.
7. When a block is full, the client requests the next set of DataNodes from the NameNode for the next block. This process repeats until all blocks are written.
8. The client calls `close()` to finalize the write. The NameNode records the new file metadata in the edit log.

**HDFS File Read Process (3.5 marks):**

1. The client invokes the `open()` method on `DistributedFileSystem` to read a file.
2. The client performs an **RPC call to the NameNode** to retrieve the **block locations** (list of DataNodes holding each block's replicas).
3. The NameNode returns the DataNode list for each block, **sorted by network proximity** (closest first, often the same node for co-located clients).
4. The client connects **directly** to the nearest DataNode for each block and starts reading data as a stream. The NameNode is bypassed for actual data transfer.
5. If the nearest DataNode fails mid-read, the client **transparently fails over** to the next DataNode in the replica list without informing the user.
6. The client reads all blocks sequentially. When finished, it calls `close()` to release resources.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Write | Read |
|---|---|---|
| Topology | Pipeline replication (chain) | Direct connection to nearest replica |
| NameNode role | Allocates DataNodes | Provides DataNode list |
| Data flow | Client → DN1 → DN2 → DN3 | DN → Client (single hop) |
| Acknowledgment | ACK flows back up pipeline | No ACK needed |
| Failure handling | Removes failed DN from pipeline, restarts | Fails over to next replica |

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the fault tolerance mechanisms in HDFS. How does HDFS detect and recover from DataNode failures?**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

HDFS is designed to be **highly fault-tolerant** through a combination of replication, heartbeats, and self-healing mechanisms:

**(i) Block Replication (2 marks):**
By default, every block is **replicated 3 times** across different DataNodes (and across racks, if rack awareness is enabled). This redundancy ensures that the loss of any single DataNode does not result in data loss.

**(ii) Heartbeat-based Failure Detection (2 marks):**
- Each DataNode sends a **heartbeat signal** to the NameNode every **3 seconds**.
- If the NameNode **does not receive 10 consecutive heartbeats** (i.e., **30 seconds** of silence), it marks the DataNode as **dead**.
- Once marked dead, the DataNode is removed from the cluster's active set.

**(iii) Under-replication Recovery (1.5 marks):**
- When a DataNode is declared dead, all its blocks are now under-replicated.
- The NameNode detects this by comparing the actual replicas with the expected replication count.
- The NameNode instructs other DataNodes to **create new replicas** of the lost blocks to restore the replication factor to 3.
- This happens automatically without manual intervention.

**(iv) Self-Healing on Corrupt Blocks (1 mark):**
- DataNodes run a **BlockScanner** background thread that periodically verifies all stored blocks via checksum.
- If a corrupt block is detected, the DataNode reports it to the NameNode.
- The NameNode instructs another DataNode with a healthy replica to replicate a fresh copy, replacing the corrupt one.

**(v) Rack Awareness (0.5 mark):**
- Replicas are placed on **at least 2 different racks** to survive the failure of an entire rack (switch failure, power outage, natural disaster).

**Summary Table:**

| Failure | Detection Time | Recovery Action |
|---|---|---|
| Single DataNode down | ~30 seconds | Re-replicate lost blocks to other nodes |
| Corrupt block detected | Next BlockScanner pass | Replace with healthy replica |
| Rack failure | ~30 seconds | Re-replicate to other rack |
| NameNode failure (non-HA) | Immediate | Cluster inaccessible (HA setup needed) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the Secondary NameNode with a Backup NameNode.** It only does checkpointing — it does NOT serve client requests or act as a hot standby. In HA setups, the **Standby NameNode** (not the Secondary NameNode) serves as the actual backup.
> 2. **Always state 128 MB** as the default block size unless the question explicitly specifies Hadoop 1.x. Writing "64 MB" for a modern question will cost 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **In storage calculations, use the CEILING function** for the number of blocks. Writing $F/S$ without ceiling (and getting a non-integer answer) is a common mistake that costs 1 mark.
> 4. **For replication storage:** Total physical storage = $B \times S \times R$ — do not forget to multiply by the replication factor. Many students forget the factor and only get $B \times S$.
> 5. **For the write pipeline, mention "ACK flows back up the chain"** — the ACK direction is opposite to the data direction. Examiners specifically test this.
> 6. **For the read process, emphasize "NameNode is bypassed for data transfer"** — this is a high-yield point worth 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **HDFS = Hadoop Distributed File System** = primary storage layer of Hadoop, designed for **Write Once, Read Many (WORM)** workloads.
- **Default Block Size = 128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+); **Default Replication Factor = 3**.
- **NameNode** = Master = stores **metadata only** in RAM; persists to **fsimage + edit logs**; single point of failure.
- **DataNode** = Slave = stores **actual blocks** on local disk; sends **heartbeat every 3 seconds** and **block reports every 6 hours**.
- **Secondary NameNode** = Checkpoint helper, NOT a backup; merges fsimage + edit logs to reduce NameNode restart time.
- **Heartbeat Timeout = 10 × 3 s = 30 s** → DataNode declared dead after this.
- **Rack Awareness** = at least 2 replicas per rack for disaster recovery.
- **HDFS Write Pipeline** = Client → DN1 → DN2 → DN3 → ACK flows back (DN3 → DN2 → DN1 → Client).
- **HDFS Read Pipeline** = Client gets DataNode list from NameNode, then reads **directly** from nearest DataNode; fails over to next replica on failure.
- **Storage Formula:** $B = \lceil F/S \rceil$, $T = B \times S \times R$, $U = F/T = 1/R$ (asymptotic).
- **Common HDFS Commands:**
  - Browse: `hdfs dfs -ls /path`
  - Upload/Download: `-put localfile /hdfs/path`, `-get /hdfs/file localpath`
  - Inspect: `-cat`, `-head`, `-tail`, `-du`, `-df`, `-stat`
  - Manage: `-mkdir`, `-rm`, `-rm -r`, `-mv`, `-cp`, `-chmod`, `-chown`
  - Admin: `hdfs dfsadmin -report`, `-safemode get/set`
  - Replication: `hdfs dfs -setrep <count> /path`
- **BlockScanner** = background thread that verifies block integrity via checksum; corrupt blocks are auto-replaced from healthy replicas.
- **Effective utilization** of HDFS = $1/R$ = **33.33%** for default R = 3 — the rest is replication overhead.
- **High Availability (HA)** in Hadoop 2+ uses **Standby NameNode + ZooKeeper** to eliminate the NameNode SPOF.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: K5- Evaluate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** K5- Evaluate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module Experiment 2 — K5 Evaluate)**
> In the context of Big Data Analytics laboratory work, **K5 – Evaluate** refers to the cognitive process of *critiquing, judging, and validating* the outcomes of a Big Data experiment by comparing them against expected benchmarks, performance metrics, correctness criteria, and analytical standards. It encompasses assessing the **efficiency** (time, memory, I/O), **effectiveness** (accuracy of results), and **scalability** of distributed algorithms executed on a Big Data framework (Hadoop MapReduce / Apache Spark).

Under the **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, the Course Outcome for this experiment is typically mapped as:

$$
\text{CO} \longmapsto \text{Bloom Level K5 (Evaluate)}
$$

This means the student is expected to *justify*, *criticize*, *appraise*, and *validate* the experimental output of a Big Data program (commonly a **MapReduce Job** or a **Spark Transformation/Action**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen Inspector**
> Imagine a chef (your MapReduce/Spark program) prepares a thousand dishes in parallel across many kitchens (Hadoop cluster nodes). A **K5 Evaluator** is like a *food critic* who walks in after cooking and asks:
> 1. **Correctness** — *"Did the chef produce the exact recipe I ordered?"* → *Output validation*
> 2. **Speed** — *"How fast was the kitchen?"* → *Execution time / latency*
> 3. **Resource Use** — *"Did the chef waste gas, water, and pans?"* → *CPU, memory, disk I/O*
> 4. **Scalability** — *"What if I order 10,000 dishes tomorrow?"* → *Throughput vs. data size*
> 5. **Robustness** — *"What happens if one stove breaks?"* → *Fault tolerance evaluation*

Just as a critic doesn't *cook* the food (that was K3 *Apply*), the **K5 Evaluator** does not write the program — they **judge** whether the program meets the engineering and analytical standards of Big Data processing.

## 1.3 The Big Data Evaluation Triad

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every K5 evaluation in Module Experiment 2 must be reported using the **three pillars of Big Data assessment**:

| Pillar | Question Answered | Metric Type | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Correctness** | *"Is the output right?"* | Qualitative / Set comparison | Logical match (%) |
| **Performance** | *"How fast and lean is it?"* | Quantitative (time, memory) | **seconds**, **MB** |
| **Scalability** | *"Does it handle growth?"* | Quantitative (vs. data size) | **records/sec**, slope |

The constant **$1\,\text{MiB} = 2^{20}\,\text{bytes} = 1{,}048{,}576\,\text{bytes}$** and **$1\,\text{GB} = 10^9\,\text{bytes}$** (HDFS default) are the **standard units** used throughout all K5 metric reporting in this experiment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition of the Evaluation Pipeline

The K5 evaluation of a Big Data experiment proceeds through five logical stages. Each stage answers one specific evaluative question:

- **Stage 1 — Input Characterization**
  Evaluate the *properties* of the input dataset before processing.
  - File size in HDFS: $S_{\text{input}}$ (bytes)
  - Block size used: $B = 128\,\text{MB}$ (HDFS default in KTU lab)
  - Number of splits: $\displaystyle N_{\text{splits}} = \left\lceil \frac{S_{\text{input}}}{B} \right\rceil$

- **Stage 2 — Mapper Phase Evaluation**
  - Number of map tasks: equals $N_{\text{splits}}$ (one mapper per split by default)
  - Intermediate key-value pairs emitted: $K_{\text{intermediate}}$
  - Data shuffled between Map and Reduce: $S_{\text{shuffle}}$ (bytes over network)

- **Stage 3 — Shuffle & Sort Evaluation**
  - Cost dominated by **network I/O** and **disk I/O**
  - Combiners (local reducers) reduce $K_{\text{intermediate}}$ to $K_{\text{local}}$ before network transfer

- **Stage 4 — Reducer Phase Evaluation**
  - Number of reduce tasks: $R$ (configurable via `mapreduce.job.reduces`)
  - Output size: $S_{\text{output}}$ (bytes written to HDFS)

- **Stage 5 — Aggregate Metric Computation**
  - Total job time: $T_{\text{total}}$
  - Throughput: $\Theta = \dfrac{S_{\text{input}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$
  - Speedup when scaling from $n_1$ to $n_2$ nodes: $S_{p} = \dfrac{T(n_1)}{T(n_2)}$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Why evaluate at the K5 level?**
> Big Data systems are inherently *distributed* and *non-deterministic* in runtime. A program that produces the correct word count on a $1\,\text{MB}$ file may *fail*, *hang*, or *produce duplicates* on a $10\,\text{GB}$ file. K5 evaluation forces the student to *prove* the algorithm's behaviour beyond a single happy-path run — the core engineering demand of NEP 2020.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for any K5 evaluation question in Module Experiment 2. All units follow the SI/IEC standard (**seconds, bytes, MB, GB**).

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HDFS Block Count | $N_{\text{blocks}} = \lceil S_{\text{file}} / B \rceil$ | $S_{\text{file}}$ = file size, $B$ = block size | dimensionless | Default $B = 128\,\text{MB}$ |
| 2 | Number of Mappers | $N_{\text{map}} = N_{\text{blocks}}$ | — | dimensionless | One mapper per block |
| 3 | Data Locality Ratio | $D_{L} = N_{\text{local}} / N_{\text{map}}$ | $N_{\text{local}}$ = locally-run mappers | dimensionless (0–1) | Higher is better; **ideal = 1.0** |
| 4 | Throughput | $\Theta = S_{\text{input}} / T_{\text{total}}$ | $S_{\text{input}}$ = input bytes, $T_{\text{total}}$ = total time | **MB/s** or **GB/s** | K5 performance key metric |
| 5 | Speedup (Amdahl observable) | $S_{p} = T_{\text{serial}} / T_{\text{parallel}}$ | $T_{\text{serial}}$ = 1-node time, $T_{\text{parallel}}$ = $p$-node time | dimensionless | Theoretically $\le p$ |
| 6 | Efficiency | $E_{p} = S_{p} / p$ | $p$ = number of nodes | dimensionless (0–1) | K5 scalability key metric |
| 7 | Word Count Correctness | $\text{Rec}_{\text{out}} = \sum_{w \in V} 1$ | $V$ = vocabulary set | dimensionless | Equals number of distinct words |
| 8 | Precision | $P = \text{TP} / (\text{TP} + \text{FP})$ | TP = true positive, FP = false positive | dimensionless | Used when experiment includes classification |
| 9 | Recall | $R = \text{TP} / (\text{TP} + \text{FN})$ | FN = false negative | dimensionless | Complement-of-missed rate |
| 10 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2PR / (P + R)$ | $P$ = precision, $R$ = recall | dimensionless | Harmonic mean — **KTU favourite** |
| 11 | MapReduce Cost Model | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{map}} + T_{\text{shuffle}} + T_{\text{reduce}}$ | all time components | **seconds** | Linear decomposition |
| 12 | Relative Error of Count | $\varepsilon = \vert C_{\text{exp}} - C_{\text{truth}} \vert / C_{\text{truth}}$ | $C_{\text{exp}}$ = experimental count, $C_{\text{truth}}$ = known truth | dimensionless | K5 correctness gate |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note (Formula Discipline):** In the answer script, always *define every variable* before substituting. Marks are awarded for the *symbolic expression* and the *substitution* separately. A bare number with no formula loses 1 mark in the 14-mark question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

K5 evaluation is the bedrock of **production-grade Big Data engineering**. In industry:

- **Cloudera / Hortonworks** SREs use throughput ($\Theta$) and Data Locality ($D_L$) dashboards to size clusters.
- **Netflix** evaluates Spark Streaming jobs using end-to-end latency $T_{\text{total}}$ to honour SLAs.
- **Banking fraud pipelines** use $F_1$ score to validate that their MapReduce-implemented classifiers don't miss transactions.
- **IoT analytics** at **Siemens** rely on $S_p$ and $E_p$ to decide whether to add nodes to a Hadoop cluster running predictive maintenance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical K5 Evaluation Scenario (Module Experiment 2)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Scenario:** A KTU student runs a **Word Count MapReduce job** on a $640\,\text{MB}$ text file using a Hadoop cluster with the HDFS block size set to $128\,\text{MB}$. The cluster has 4 DataNodes. The job produced $12{,}430$ distinct words. A reference truth file (built using `wc -w` and `sort -u` on the same input) reports $12{,}427$ distinct words. The job ran in $58$ seconds. When the same file was processed on a single-node pseudo-distributed setup, it took $210$ seconds.

You are required to **evaluate (K5)** the experiment on the three pillars: *Correctness*, *Performance*, and *Scalability*.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 1 — Block & Mapper Count

**Step 1: Identify the input parameters.**

$$
S_{\text{file}} = 640\,\text{MB}, \qquad B = 128\,\text{MB}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the block count formula.**

$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{S_{\text{file}}}{B} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3: Substitute.**

$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{640\,\text{MB}}{128\,\text{MB}} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.0 \rceil = 5
$$

**Step 4: Equate mappers to blocks.**

$$
N_{\text{map}} = N_{\text{blocks}} = 5
$$

> **Interpretation (K5 Critique):** A single $640\,\text{MB}$ file spawns **5 mappers** in parallel. If the cluster has only 4 DataNodes, one mapper must *wait* — this is an **evaluation point**: data-node imbalance reduces achievable $S_p$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 2 — Correctness via Relative Error

**Step 1: Define the relative error formula.**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\vert C_{\text{exp}} - C_{\text{truth}} \vert}{C_{\text{truth}}}
$$

**Step 2: Plug in the observed values.**

$$
C_{\text{exp}} = 12{,}430, \qquad C_{\text{truth}} = 12{,}427
$$

**Step 3: Compute the absolute difference.**

$$
\vert C_{\text{exp}} - C_{\text{truth}} \vert = \vert 12{,}430 - 12{,}427 \vert = 3
$$

**Step 4: Final evaluation.**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{3}{12{,}427} = 2.414 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.024\,\%
$$

> **K5 Evaluation Verdict:** The relative error is well below the KTU-accepted **1 % correctness gate**. The job is **CORRECT**. The 3 extra "words" likely come from case-sensitivity (e.g., `"Hadoop"` vs `"hadoop"`) — a documented **evaluation insight** in the KTU marking scheme.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Throughput Evaluation

**Step 1: Define throughput.**

$$
\Theta = \frac{S_{\text{input}}}{T_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Step 2: Convert $S_{\text{file}}$ to a common unit (MB).**

$$
S_{\text{input}} = 640\,\text{MB}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute time.**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 58\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 4: Compute.**

$$
\Theta = \frac{640\,\text{MB}}{58\,\text{s}} = 11.034\,\text{MB/s}
$$

> **K5 Evaluation Verdict:** A throughput of $\approx 11\,\text{MB/s}$ on commodity lab hardware is **acceptable for a 4-node academic cluster** (industry standard for Cloudera is $\approx 100\,\text{MB/s}$ per node). The student should comment on the bottleneck — typically the *shuffle phase I/O*.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Speedup and Parallel Efficiency

**Step 1: Define Speedup.**

$$
S_p = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute.**

$$
T_{\text{serial}} = 210\,\text{s}, \qquad T_{\text{parallel}} = 58\,\text{s}, \qquad p = 4
$$

**Step 3: Compute Speedup.**

$$
S_p = \frac{210}{58} = 3.6207
$$

**Step 4: Compute Efficiency.**

$$
E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{3.6207}{4} = 0.9052
$$

> **K5 Evaluation Verdict:** Efficiency of **$90.5\,\%$** is **excellent**. By Amdahl's law, with $E_p > 0.85$, the parallel portion $P$ of the code satisfies $P > 0.95$, which is the hallmark of a well-written MapReduce job.

---

### 3.6 Derivation 5 — Amdahl's Bound Cross-Check (Optional, for high marks)

**Step 1: Recall Amdahl's law.**

$$
S_p \le \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{p}}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $P$ given $S_p = 3.6207$ and $p = 4$.**

$$
3.6207 = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{4}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.75\,P}
$$

**Step 3: Invert both sides.**

$$
\frac{1}{3.6207} = 1 - 0.75\,P \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 0.75\,P = 1 - 0.2762 = 0.7238
$$

**Step 4: Solve.**

$$
P = \frac{0.7238}{0.75} = 0.9651
$$

> **K5 Evaluation Insight:** Approximately **$96.5\,\%$** of the job is parallelizable. The remaining **$3.5\,\%$** (e.g., driver setup, HDFS write of `_SUCCESS` flag) is serial — this is the *root cause* of $S_p < 4$.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — K5 Evaluation Summary

| Metric | Symbol | Computed Value | KTU Threshold | Verdict (K5) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number of Mappers | $N_{\text{map}}$ | $5$ | Optimal $= \lceil S/B \rceil$ | ✅ Correct |
| Relative Error | $\varepsilon$ | $0.024\,\%$ | $< 1\,\%$ | ✅ Pass |
| Throughput | $\Theta$ | $11.03\,\text{MB/s}$ | $> 5\,\text{MB/s}$ (lab scale) | ✅ Pass |
| Speedup | $S_p$ | $3.62$ | $\le p = 4$ | ✅ Pass |
| Efficiency | $E_p$ | $0.905$ | $> 0.85$ | ✅ Excellent |
| Parallel Fraction | $P$ | $0.965$ | $> 0.90$ | ✅ Excellent |

> **Final K5 Statement (model answer sentence):**
> *"The MapReduce Word Count experiment is evaluated to be **correct** ($\varepsilon = 0.024\,\%$), **performant** ($\Theta = 11.03\,\text{MB/s}$), and **scalable** ($E_p = 90.5\,\%$), satisfying all KTU 2024 Big Data Analytics Module 2 evaluation criteria."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 K5 Evaluation Workflow (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **end-to-end K5 evaluation workflow** for Module Experiment 2. Each block represents a *judgement step* performed by the evaluator.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: K5 Evaluation of Big Data Experiment] --> B[Stage 1: Input Characterization]
    B --> B1[Measure S_input in MB]
    B --> B2[Identify Block Size B = 128 MB]
    B --> B3[Compute N_blocks = ceil S/B]

    B3 --> C[Stage 2: Mapper Phase Evaluation]
    C --> C1[N_map = N_blocks]
    C --> C2[Check Data Locality Ratio D_L]
    C --> C3[Count Intermediate KV pairs K_intermediate]

    C3 --> D[Stage 3: Shuffle and Sort Evaluation]
    D --> D1[Measure Shuffle Bytes S_shuffle]
    D --> D2[Evaluate Combiner Effectiveness]
    D --> D3[Compute Network I/O Cost]

    D3 --> E[Stage 4: Reducer Phase Evaluation]
    E --> E1[Confirm N_reduce configuration]
    E --> E2[Measure S_output in HDFS]
    E --> E3[Verify Output Schema and Format]

    E3 --> F[Stage 5: Aggregate Metric Computation]
    F --> F1[Correctness: Relative Error epsilon]
    F --> F2[Performance: Throughput Theta MB per s]
    F --> F3[Scalability: Speedup S_p and Efficiency E_p]

    F3 --> G{K5 Verdict}
    G -->|All Thresholds Met| H[Result: Experiment VALIDATED]
    G -->|Any Threshold Failed| I[Result: Experiment REJECTED with Reasons]

    H --> J[Write K5 Evaluation Report]
    I --> J
    J --> K[End: Submit to KTU Examiner]
```

## 4.2 The Three Pillars of K5 Evaluation (Hierarchical View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root[K5 Evaluate Big Data Experiment] --> P1[Pillar 1 Correctness]
    Root --> P2[Pillar 2 Performance]
    Root --> P3[Pillar 3 Scalability]

    P1 --> P1a[Output Schema Match]
    P1 --> P1b[Row Count Match]
    P1 --> P1c[Relative Error epsilon below 1 percent]

    P2 --> P2a[Total Runtime T_total]
    P2 --> P2b[Throughput Theta MB per s]
    P2 --> P2c[Memory Footprint M_heap]

    P3 --> P3a[Speedup S_p]
    P3 --> P3b[Efficiency E_p]
    P3 --> P3c[Parallel Fraction P from Amdahl]
```

## 4.3 MapReduce Job Timeline for K5 Time Decomposition

```mermaid
gantt
    title K5 Time Decomposition of a MapReduce Job
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S s

    section Map Phase
    Mapper 1 (Block 1)   :a1, 0, 12s
    Mapper 2 (Block 2)   :a2, 0, 14s
    Mapper 3 (Block 3)   :a3, 0, 11s
    Mapper 4 (Block 4)   :a4, 0, 13s
    Mapper 5 (Block 5)   :a5, 0, 15s

    section Shuffle Phase
    Partition and Sort   :b1, after a5, 10s
    Network Transfer     :b2, after b1, 8s

    section Reduce Phase
    Reducer 1            :c1, after b2, 6s
    Reducer 2            :c2, after b2, 7s
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Total job time is $T_{\text{total}} = \max(\text{Mapper times}) + T_{\text{shuffle}} + T_{\text{reduce}}$. The K5 evaluator must *identify* the longest mapper (Block 5, $15\,\text{s}$) as the *straggler* — a classic K5 critique point.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Define K5 Evaluation in the context of a Big Data experiment. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
K5 Evaluation, mapped to the *Evaluate* level of **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**, is the cognitive act of *judging* the outcome of a Big Data experiment against correctness, performance, and scalability criteria. In Module Experiment 2, the student *appraises* the MapReduce (or Spark) job by computing metrics such as relative error $\varepsilon$, throughput $\Theta$, speedup $S_p$, and parallel efficiency $E_p$, and *justifies* whether the experiment meets the KTU-defined engineering thresholds.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q2. List any THREE metrics used for K5 evaluation of a Big Data job. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
The three primary K5 evaluation metrics are:
1. **Correctness** — Relative error $\varepsilon = \vert C_{\text{exp}} - C_{\text{truth}} \vert / C_{\text{truth}}$ — must be below **$1\,\%$**.
2. **Performance** — Throughput $\Theta = S_{\text{input}} / T_{\text{total}}$ measured in **MB/s**.
3. **Scalability** — Parallel efficiency $E_p = S_p / p$, where $S_p = T_{\text{serial}} / T_{\text{parallel}}$ and $p$ is the node count; threshold $E_p > 0.85$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 | K5 Evaluate]**

### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks

**(a)** A $768\,\text{MB}$ CSV file is processed by a MapReduce Word Count job on a 6-node Hadoop cluster with the default HDFS block size of $128\,\text{MB}$. Compute the **number of mappers** and the **number of blocks** in HDFS. Justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The same experiment yields a relative error of $0.08\,\%$, a throughput of $9.2\,\text{MB/s}$, and a parallel efficiency of $0.88$. **Evaluate** the experiment on all three K5 pillars and state whether it passes the KTU acceptance gate. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Identify inputs.

$$
S_{\text{file}} = 768\,\text{MB}, \qquad B = 128\,\text{MB}, \qquad p = 6
$$

**Step 2:** Apply block count formula.

$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{768\,\text{MB}}{128\,\text{MB}} \right\rceil = \lceil 6.0 \rceil = 6
$$

**Step 3:** Equate mappers to blocks.

$$
N_{\text{map}} = N_{\text{blocks}} = 6
$$

**Justification (K5 Critique):** Since $N_{\text{map}} = p = 6$, each DataNode executes exactly one mapper. This is the **ideal data-parallel mapping** — no mapper has to wait, and maximum data locality is achieved ($D_L = 1.0$). The setup is therefore *optimal for K5 evaluation*.

> **Marks distribution:** Block count formula — 2 marks; substitution — 2 marks; mapper count — 1 mark; K5 justification — 2 marks. **Total = 7.**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**K5 Evaluation Table:**

| Pillar | Metric | Observed | Threshold | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Correctness | $\varepsilon = 0.08\,\%$ | $< 1\,\%$ | ✅ **PASS** |
| Performance | $\Theta = 9.2\,\text{MB/s}$ | $> 5\,\text{MB/s}$ (academic) | ✅ **PASS** |
| Scalability | $E_p = 0.88$ | $> 0.85$ | ✅ **PASS** |

**K5 Verdict (Model Sentence):**
All three pillars satisfy the KTU acceptance criteria. The MapReduce Word Count job is **APPROVED** under K5 evaluation. The slight deviation of $E_p$ from the ideal $1.0$ is attributable to the small but non-zero **serial fraction** (driver setup, HDFS replication factor $= 3$).

> **Marks distribution:** Each pillar — 2 marks; final integrated verdict — 1 mark. **Total = 7.**

---

### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the **K5 evaluation workflow** for a MapReduce experiment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A student runs the same Word Count job on a single-node cluster in $300\,\text{s}$ and on a 5-node cluster in $75\,\text{s}$. Compute the **speedup** $S_p$ and **efficiency** $E_p$. Using **Amdahl's Law**, estimate the **parallel fraction** $P$ of the program. **Evaluate** whether the program is well-parallelized. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Draw the **K5 Evaluation Workflow** (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the full Mermaid block diagram; reproduce it on paper with five labelled stages):

1. **Input Characterization** — measure $S_{\text{input}}$, $B$, compute $N_{\text{blocks}}$. *(1 mark)*
2. **Mapper Evaluation** — confirm $N_{\text{map}}$, data locality $D_L$. *(1 mark)*
3. **Shuffle/Sort Evaluation** — quantify network I/O, combiner use. *(1 mark)*
4. **Reducer Evaluation** — verify $N_{\text{reduce}}$ and $S_{\text{output}}$. *(1 mark)*
5. **Aggregate Metrics** — compute $\varepsilon$, $\Theta$, $S_p$, $E_p$. *(2 marks)*
6. **K5 Verdict & Report** — pass/fail decision. *(1 mark)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Identify inputs.

$$
T_{\text{serial}} = 300\,\text{s}, \qquad T_{\text{parallel}} = 75\,\text{s}, \qquad p = 5
$$

**Step 2:** Compute speedup.

$$
S_p = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}} = \frac{300}{75} = 4.0
$$

**Step 3:** Compute efficiency.

$$
E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{4.0}{5} = 0.80
$$

**Step 4:** Apply Amdahl's law to find $P$.

$$
S_p = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{p}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 4.0 = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8\,P}
$$

**Step 5:** Invert.

$$
\frac{1}{4.0} = 1 - 0.8\,P \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 0.8\,P = 0.75 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad P = 0.9375
$$

**Step 6:** K5 Evaluation.

The efficiency $E_p = 0.80$ is **below the KTU ideal of $0.85$** but **acceptable** for a lab-scale experiment. The parallel fraction $P = 93.75\,\%$ indicates that the **serial overhead** ($\approx 6.25\,\%$) — likely from the **HDFS NameNode metadata writes** and the **driver's `waitForCompletion()` loop** — is the bottleneck.

> **K5 Recommendation:** To raise $E_p$ above $0.85$, the student should *increase the input dataset size* (so the parallel portion dominates the serial setup) or *reduce the HDFS replication factor* from 3 to 1 in the lab `hdfs-site.xml`.

> **Marks distribution:** Speedup — 2 marks; efficiency — 1 mark; Amdahl derivation — 3 marks; K5 evaluation sentence — 1 mark. **Total = 7.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Writing the number of mappers as $1$ instead of $N_{\text{blocks}}$. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Pitfall 2:** Omitting the **units** ($\text{MB/s}$, $\text{seconds}$, dimensionless) in throughput/speedup. **Loss: 1 mark per metric.**
> - **Pitfall 3:** Computing $E_p$ without first computing $S_p$ — examiners treat this as a *chain error* and award **0 marks for both** if $S_p$ is wrong.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Forgetting to *state* the KTU threshold when giving a verdict. A bare "PASS" with no threshold reference loses 1 mark.
> - **Pitfall 5:** Confusing **Amdahl's $P$** (parallel fraction) with **Precision $P$** in classification. **Loss: 3 marks** in the F1-score sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — K5 Evaluate (Module Experiment 2)**

- 🎯 **Bloom Level:** K5 — *Evaluate* = judge, critique, justify, appraise.
- 📦 **Three Pillars:** **Correctness** ($\varepsilon$), **Performance** ($\Theta$), **Scalability** ($S_p$, $E_p$).
- 🧮 **Block Count:** $N_{\text{blocks}} = \lceil S_{\text{file}} / B \rceil$; HDFS default $B = 128\,\text{MB}$.
- 🧑‍🍳 **Mappers = Blocks:** $N_{\text{map}} = N_{\text{blocks}}$ (one-to-one).
- ⏱️ **Throughput:** $\Theta = S_{\text{input}} / T_{\text{total}}$ — measured in **MB/s**.
- 🚀 **Speedup:** $S_p = T_{\text{serial}} / T_{\text{parallel}}$; always $S_p \le p$.
- ⚙️ **Efficiency:** $E_p = S_p / p$; **KTU gate = $0.85$**.
- 📐 **Amdahl's Law:** $S_p = 1 / \big((1 - P) + P/p\big)$ — used to back-solve $P$.
- ✅ **Correctness Gate:** Relative error $\varepsilon < 1\,\%$.
- 🗣️ **Answer-Script Discipline:** Define every variable, state units, give a one-line *K5 verdict sentence* at the end.
- ⚠️ **Common Trap:** Don't confuse **Parallel Fraction $P$** (Amdahl) with **Precision $P$** (classification).
- 🧠 **Examiner's Favourite:** Always report $E_p$ and use it to justify whether the experiment "passes" — this is the single most heavily-weighted K5 mark in the 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: K6- Create CO-PO Mapping Table: Text Books Sl. No Title of the Book Name of the Author/s Name of the Publisher Edition and Year 1 Hadoop: The Definitive Guide Tom White O'reilly Media 4/e
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** K6- Create CO-PO Mapping Table: Text Books Sl. No Title of the Book Name of the Author/s Name of the Publisher Edition and Year 1 Hadoop: The Definitive Guide Tom White O'reilly Media 4/e

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 KTU Module Experiment 2 — K6: Constructing the CO–PO Mapping Table

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 OBE Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome (CO):** A statement that describes what a student is expected to **know, understand, or be able to demonstrate** after successful completion of a specific course. Each course typically declares **3 to 6 COs** aligned with Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Remember → Create).

> [!NOTE]
> **Program Outcome (PO):** A statement that describes what graduates are expected to **know and demonstrate** upon completion of the entire B.Tech program. Under NBA / KTU 2024 scheme, every engineering program declares **12 Program Outcomes (PO1 – PO12)** plus **Program Specific Outcomes (PSO1 – PSO2)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CO–PO Mapping:** A rigorously defined **correlation matrix** that quantifies, on a discrete ordinal scale, the strength of the pedagogical relationship between every Course Outcome of a course and every Program Outcome of the parent program. It is the **single most important instrument** in Outcome-Based Education (OBE) for accreditation audits (NBA, NAAC, ABET).

### 1.2 The Official KTU / NBA Ordinal Correlation Scale

The strength of association is denoted by a numeric weight assigned to each CO–PO pair.

| Mapping Level | Symbol | Strength of Correlation | Interpretation |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Level 1** | `1` | **Slight (Low)** | The CO marginally touches the PO. The PO is **addressed** but not the focus. |
| **Level 2** | `2` | **Moderate (Medium)** | The CO has a clear, teaching-supported association with the PO. |
| **Level 3** | `3` | **Substantial (High)** | The CO is a **principal vehicle** for attaining the PO. |
| **No Correlation** | `-` | **None** | The CO has **no discernible** contribution to that PO. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU mandates that every course must demonstrate that **all 12 POs and both PSOs are addressed at least at Level 1** across the entire course, otherwise the program fails the NBA adequacy test. **A "–" is permitted, but every PO column must have at least one non-zero entry across the whole CO table.**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Electrical Wiring Diagram"

Imagine your B.Tech degree is a **house** that must deliver 12 utilities (PO1–PO12) — electricity, water, internet, gas, etc. Each subject you study is a **room** with specific appliances (COs). The **CO–PO mapping table** is the **electrician's wiring diagram** that shows, room by room, which appliance is wired to which utility line and at what **amperage (mapping level 1/2/3)**.

Just as an inspector (NBA auditor) verifies that every utility is properly wired somewhere in the house, your program must show that every PO is wired into at least one course with a non-zero load. A course with only "–" across an entire PO column is like a room where a vital utility is missing — a red flag during accreditation.

### 1.4 Reference Textbook (KTU Module Experiment 2 — Text Books)

| Sl. No | Title of the Book | Name of the Author(s) | Name of the Publisher | Edition and Year |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| **1** | **Hadoop: The Definitive Guide** | **Tom White** | **O'Reilly Media** | **4/e (2015)** |

> [!NOTE]
> This textbook serves as the **primary reference** for all technical content of the PBADT504 course. The CO statements themselves and the CO–PO mapping procedure, however, are **institutional artefacts** derived from the KTU 2024 scheme curriculum document, **not** from the textbook. Tom White's book is cited because every CO in BDA traces back to a Hadoop ecosystem concept (HDFS, MapReduce, YARN, Spark, Pig, Hive, etc.) described in it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a CO–PO Mapping Table

A standard KTU CO–PO mapping table is a **rectangular matrix** of dimensions `(N_COs + 1) × (N_POs + 1)`, where the leading row hosts the PO identifiers and the leading column hosts the CO identifiers.

**Row Vector Definition** — for a given Course Outcome $CO_i$:

$$\vec{R}_i = \left[m_{i,1},\ m_{i,2},\ m_{i,3},\ \ldots,\ m_{i,12}\right] \quad \text{where } m_{i,j} \in \{-, 1, 2, 3\}$$

**Column Vector Definition** — for a given Program Outcome $PO_j$:

$$\vec{C}_j = \left[m_{1,j},\ m_{2,j},\ m_{3,j},\ \ldots,\ m_{N_{CO}, j}\right]^{T}$$

**Adequacy Constraint (NBA Mandatory):**

$$\forall j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 12\}, \quad \max_{i}\left(m_{i,j}\right) \geq 1$$

This formula means: for every Program Outcome, **at least one CO must contribute** with mapping level 1 or higher.

## 2.2 The Three-Step Methodology to Create a CO–PO Mapping Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU evaluation scripts check three sequential logical steps. Memorize the order.

### **Step 1 — Decompose the CO into Action Verb + Object + Condition**
Every CO follows the Bloom's template: `[Action Verb] + [Object] + [Condition/Criterion]`. The action verb determines the cognitive level, which directly informs the PO weights.

### **Step 2 — Match the CO's Verbs to the PO Definitions**
Compare the action verbs in the CO against the 12 PO definitions. POs like PO5 (Modern Tool Usage) and PO6 (The Engineer and Society) have very specific keywords (e.g., "tool", "society", "environment").

### **Step 3 — Assign the Mapping Level (1, 2, or 3) by Cognitive Load**
- **Level 3 (Substantial):** CO is taught, practiced, and assessed. Direct primary content.
- **Level 2 (Moderate):** CO contributes to the PO, but the PO is not the focus. Indirect delivery.
- **Level 1 (Slight):** CO has incidental exposure to the PO. Awareness only.

## 2.3 The 12 NBA Program Outcomes — Quick Reference

| PO | Full Title | Identifying Keywords (for matching) |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **PO1** | Engineering Knowledge | apply, knowledge, mathematics, science, engineering fundamentals |
| **PO2** | Problem Analysis | identify, formulate, analyze, review, complex problem |
| **PO3** | Design / Development of Solutions | design, solution, system, component, process |
| **PO4** | Conduct Investigations of Complex Problems | investigation, research, experiment, data interpretation |
| **PO5** | Modern Tool Usage | modern tools, ICT, modeling, simulation, software |
| **PO6** | The Engineer and Society | society, environment, safety, ethics, legal, cultural |
| **PO7** | Environment and Sustainability | sustainable, environment, ecological |
| **PO8** | Ethics | ethics, professional, responsibility, norms |
| **PO9** | Individual and Team Work | team, individual, multidisciplinary |
| **PO10** | Communication | communication, report, presentation, documentation |
| **PO11** | Project Management & Finance | project, management, finance, cost, resource |
| **PO12** | Life-Long Learning | life-long, self-learning, contemporary |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Purpose | Unit / Domain |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\forall j,\ \max_i(m_{i,j}) \geq 1$ | NBA adequacy constraint — no PO column can be all zero/dash | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 2 | $\text{CO}_{\text{Attainment}} = \frac{\text{Students scoring} \geq \text{Target}}{\text{Total Students}} \times 100$ | Used to compute **attainment** after mapping is done | Percentage (%) |
| 3 | $\text{PO}_j\ \text{Attainment} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N_{CO}} m_{i,j} \times \text{CO}_i\ \text{Attainment}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N_{CO}} m_{i,j}}$ | Weighted-mean PO attainment using mapping weights | Percentage (%) |
| 4 | $\text{Correlation Strength} \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ | Ordinal scale — must not use 0, must not use 4 | Discrete ordinal |
| 5 | $\text{Mapping Directness} = \frac{\#\text{ of Level 3 cells}}{\#\text{ of non-dash cells}} \times 100$ | Indicates how "tightly wired" a course is to POs | Percentage (%) |

> [!NOTE]
> The two attainment formulas (#2 and #3) are the **post-mapping analytical products** that KTU's NBA audit form demands. Marks for a CO–PO mapping answer are awarded for **constructing the table correctly**, not for computing attainment, but the relationship above is frequently asked in viva.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Construction of the CO–PO Mapping Table for PBADT504

## 3.1 Step 1 — Declare the Course Outcomes (COs) of PBADT504

Following the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus for **Big Data Analytics (PBADT504)**, the canonical CO set is:

| CO ID | Course Outcome Statement | Bloom's Level |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| **CO1** | Understand the fundamental concepts, characteristics, and challenges of Big Data and the Hadoop ecosystem. | Understand (L2) |
| **CO2** | Apply Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) and MapReduce programming model to process large-scale data. | Apply (L3) |
| **CO3** | Utilize Hadoop ecosystem tools (Pig, Hive, Sqoop, Flume) for data ingestion, transformation, and querying. | Apply (L3) |
| **CO4** | Analyze big data using NoSQL databases, Spark, and analytics frameworks. | Analyze (L4) |
| **CO5** | Design and evaluate end-to-end big data solutions for real-world problems with emphasis on performance, ethics, and security. | Design + Evaluate (L5+L6) |

> [!NOTE]
> These COs are **institutionally fixed** by the KTU syllabus. A student may not invent new COs. The mapping exercise is **purely a mapping exercise**, not a CO-design exercise.

## 3.2 Step 2 — Verb-by-Verb Mapping Analysis

We will now walk through the cognitive mapping for **CO2** as a worked example, since it is the most representative application-level outcome of the course.

### Worked Mapping for CO2: "Apply HDFS and MapReduce to process large-scale data."

| PO | Verb/Keyword Match | Reasoning | Mapped Level |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| **PO1** | *apply* + *mathematics, science, engineering fundamentals* | MapReduce is built on parallel algorithmic thinking; CS fundamentals required. | **3** |
| **PO2** | *identify, formulate, analyze* | Designing a MapReduce job requires decomposing a problem into map/reduce stages. | **3** |
| **PO3** | *design, solution* | Students design a working MapReduce program. | **2** |
| **PO4** | *investigation, experiment* | Students experimentally test on a Hadoop cluster. | **2** |
| **PO5** | *modern tools* | Hadoop is explicitly a modern tool. | **3** |
| **PO6** | *society* | Not the focus. | **-** |
| **PO7** | *sustainability* | Not the focus. | **-** |
| **PO8** | *ethics* | Not the focus. | **-** |
| **PO9** | *team work* | Lab work is typically individual/paired. | **1** |
| **PO10** | *communication* | Lab report submission required. | **1** |
| **PO11** | *project management* | Not the focus. | **-** |
| **PO12** | *life-long learning* | Open-source Hadoop ecosystem encourages self-learning. | **1** |

This row vector for CO2 is:

$$\vec{R}_{CO2} = [3,\ 3,\ 2,\ 2,\ 3,\ -,\ -,\ -,\ 1,\ 1,\ -,\ 1]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The sum of this row, $\sum m_{2,j} = 16$, is a useful sanity check. There is no fixed "correct" total; it depends on the verb intensity and the instructor's pedagogical design.

## 3.3 Step 3 — Apply the Same Logic to All Five COs

Repeating the verb-by-verb mapping logic from Step 2 for **all 5 COs** yields the complete row vectors below.

| CO | PO1 | PO2 | PO3 | PO4 | PO5 | PO6 | PO7 | PO8 | PO9 | PO10 | PO11 | PO12 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **CO1** | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | - | 1 |
| **CO2** | 3 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 3 | - | - | - | 1 | 1 | - | 1 |
| **CO3** | 2 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 1 | - | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| **CO4** | 3 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| **CO5** | 2 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 2 |

## 3.4 Step 4 — Verify the NBA Adequacy Constraint

We must confirm that **every PO column has at least one non-dash entry**. Let us compute the column-wise maximum:

$$\max_i m_{i,1} = 3, \quad \max_i m_{i,2} = 3, \quad \max_i m_{i,3} = 3, \quad \max_i m_{i,4} = 3$$

$$\max_i m_{i,5} = 3, \quad \max_i m_{i,6} = 3, \quad \max_i m_{i,7} = 2, \quad \max_i m_{i,8} = 3$$

$$\max_i m_{i,9} = 2, \quad \max_i m_{i,10} = 2, \quad \max_i m_{i,11} = 3, \quad \max_i m_{i,12} = 2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $\forall j,\ \max_i(m_{i,j}) \geq 1$, the table **passes the NBA adequacy test**. If any column had all dashes, we would need to revise the corresponding CO to explicitly address that PO.

## 3.5 Step 5 — Compute the CO-wise and PO-wise Aggregates

### 3.5.1 CO-wise Total Mapping Weight

The total pedagogical weight contributed by a CO to the entire program:

$$W_{CO_i} = \sum_{j=1}^{12} m_{i,j}$$

| CO | $W_{CO_i}$ | Interpretation |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **CO1** | 13 | Foundational; touches many POs lightly. |
| **CO2** | 16 | Strong core technical CO. |
| **CO3** | 20 | Tool-heavy CO; high PO5 contribution. |
| **CO4** | 23 | Highest weight — analytics is the broadest CO. |
| **CO5** | 27 | Capstone; touches almost all POs (ethics, project, society, tools). |

### 3.5.2 PO-wise Total Mapping Weight (Sum across all COs)

$$W_{PO_j} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} m_{i,j}$$

| PO | $W_{PO_j}$ | PO | $W_{PO_j}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **PO1** | 12 | **PO7** | 4 |
| **PO2** | 11 | **PO8** | 6 |
| **PO3** | 11 | **PO9** | 6 |
| **PO4** | 10 | **PO10** | 6 |
| **PO5** | 14 | **PO11** | 5 |
| **PO6** | 6 | **PO12** | 8 |

> [!NOTE]
> PO5 (Modern Tool Usage) has the highest weight, $W_{PO_5} = 14$, which is appropriate because **Big Data Analytics is fundamentally a tools-driven course** (Hadoop, Spark, Hive, Pig). PO7 (Environment & Sustainability) has the lowest weight, $W_{PO_7} = 4$, which is also realistic for a technical data-engineering course.

## 3.6 Step 6 — Compute the Program Articulation Matrix Normalization

The normalized articulation weight per CO is computed as:

$$A_{CO_i} = \frac{W_{CO_i}}{\sum_{k=1}^{5} W_{CO_k}} = \frac{W_{CO_i}}{99}$$

| CO | $W_{CO_i}$ | $A_{CO_i}$ (as %) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **CO1** | 13 | **13.13%** |
| **CO2** | 16 | **16.16%** |
| **CO3** | 20 | **20.20%** |
| **CO4** | 23 | **23.23%** |
| **CO5** | 27 | **27.27%** |
| **Total** | 99 | 100.00% |

These articulation values are what the **NBA audit form (Section 2.2.1)** requires to be filed under the "Articulation Matrix" for the course file.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CO–PO Mapping Construction Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[KTU 2024 Syllabus PBADT504] --> B[Step 1: Extract Course Outcomes CO1 to CO5]
    B --> C[Step 2: Identify Bloom Verbs in each CO]
    C --> D[Step 3: Match verbs against 12 NBA PO Definitions]
    D --> E[Step 4: Assign Ordinal Level 1 2 or 3]
    E --> F[Step 5: Build 5 by 12 Matrix]
    F --> G{Step 6: Check NBA Adequacy}
    G -->|All POs covered at Level 1 or more| H[Step 7: Compute Articulation Weights]
    G -->|Some PO has all dashes| I[Revise CO statement and remap]
    I --> D
    H --> J[Step 8: File in Course File for NBA Audit]
```

## 4.2 The Mapping Correlation Hierarchy (3-Tier Strength Pyramid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[PO Column Dash -]:::nolevel
    L1[Level 1 Slight Low]:::low
    L2[Level 2 Moderate Medium]:::mod
    L3[Level 3 Substantial High]:::high

    L3 -->|Principal vehicle for attaining the PO| L3desc[CO is the PRIMARY teaching content for that PO]
    L2 -->|Clear teaching-supported association| L2desc[PO is supported but not the main focus]
    L1 -->|Incidental awareness only| L1desc[PO is mentioned or touched upon briefly]
    L0 -->|No contribution| L0desc[No discernible link to the PO]

    classDef high fill:#1a7f37,stroke:#0d4d22,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mod fill:#9a6700,stroke:#5a3d00,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef low fill:#cf222e,stroke:#7a0d18,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef nolevel fill:#6e7781,stroke:#3d434a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 The Articulation Matrix Flow (How Mapping Becomes Attainment)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
    A1[CO1 Attainment %] --> S
    A2[CO2 Attainment %] --> S
    A3[CO3 Attainment %] --> S
    A4[CO4 Attainment %] --> S
    A5[CO5 Attainment %] --> S
    end

    S[Weighted Mean Calculator using m i j] --> P1[PO1 Attainment]
    S --> P2[PO2 Attainment]
    S --> P3[PO3 Attainment]
    S --> P4[PO4 Attainment]
    S --> P5[PO5 Attainment]
    S --> P6[PO6 Attainment]
    S --> P7[PO7 Attainment]
    S --> P8[PO8 Attainment]
    S --> P9[PO9 Attainment]
    S --> P10[PO10 Attainment]
    S --> P11[PO11 Attainment]
    S --> P12[PO12 Attainment]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **S** node represents the standard KTU PO-attainment formula shown earlier in Section 2.4, formula #3. The attainment percentage for each PO is the **weighted average** of the contributing CO attainments, weighted by the mapping levels $m_{i,j}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** Define CO–PO mapping. List the three correlation levels used by KTU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Definition (1 mark):** CO–PO mapping is a correlation matrix that quantifies the strength of association between the Course Outcomes of a specific course and the 12 Program Outcomes of the B.Tech program.
> - **Level 1 (1 mark):** Slight (Low) correlation — the CO marginally touches the PO.
> - **Level 2 (1 mark):** Moderate (Medium) — clear teaching-supported association.
> - **Level 3 (1 mark):** Substantial (High) — the CO is the principal vehicle for attaining the PO.
>
> *(If a 4th level is mentioned, deduct 0.5 marks; KTU uses only 3 levels.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** What is the NBA adequacy constraint that every CO–PO mapping table must satisfy?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - For every Program Outcome $PO_j$, at least one Course Outcome $CO_i$ must contribute with a mapping level $m_{i,j} \geq 1$. (1.5 marks)
> - Mathematically: $\forall j \in \{1, \ldots, 12\},\ \max_i(m_{i,j}) \geq 1$. (1 mark)
> - This ensures that the entire B.Tech program covers all 12 POs across all courses. (0.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU Model — Module Experiment 2]*

**(a)** For the course PBADT504 — Big Data Analytics, list the five Course Outcomes as defined by the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus. (7 marks)

**(b)** Construct the complete CO–PO mapping table for PBADT504 using the standard three-level KTU scale. Justify any Level 3 mapping for CO2 with reference to the verbs "apply" and "process". (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

| CO ID | Course Outcome Statement | Bloom's Level |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| CO1 | Understand the fundamental concepts, characteristics, and challenges of Big Data and the Hadoop ecosystem. | Understand (L2) |
| CO2 | Apply Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) and MapReduce programming model to process large-scale data. | Apply (L3) |
| CO3 | Utilize Hadoop ecosystem tools (Pig, Hive, Sqoop, Flume) for data ingestion, transformation, and querying. | Apply (L3) |
| CO4 | Analyze big data using NoSQL databases, Spark, and analytics frameworks. | Analyze (L4) |
| CO5 | Design and evaluate end-to-end big data solutions for real-world problems with emphasis on performance, ethics, and security. | Design + Evaluate (L5+L6) |

> *(1 mark per CO statement correctly stated with Bloom's level. 0.5 mark deduction if Bloom's level is missing for any CO.)*

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Build the matrix** (3 marks for the table):

| CO | PO1 | PO2 | PO3 | PO4 | PO5 | PO6 | PO7 | PO8 | PO9 | PO10 | PO11 | PO12 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **CO1** | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | - | 1 |
| **CO2** | 3 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 3 | - | - | - | 1 | 1 | - | 1 |
| **CO3** | 2 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 1 | - | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| **CO4** | 3 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| **CO5** | 2 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 2 |

**Step 2 — Justify Level 3 for CO2** (4 marks):
- The verb **"Apply"** is a Level 3 Bloom verb (Apply tier), and CO2's primary delivery is hands-on programming of MapReduce. (1 mark)
- For **PO1 (Engineering Knowledge)**: applying parallel algorithmic thinking + CS fundamentals → Level 3 because the CO is the **primary vehicle** for demonstrating this. (1 mark)
- For **PO2 (Problem Analysis)**: students must analyze data, identify map/reduce stages → Level 3 because the CO is a **principal instrument** for analytical decomposition. (1 mark)
- For **PO5 (Modern Tool Usage)**: Hadoop is a modern tool, and the CO is **entirely focused** on tool usage → Level 3. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — NBA adequacy check** (carry over from the previous step, 0 mark deduction if all 12 PO columns have a non-dash maximum; 0.5 mark deduction if any column is all dashes).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the 12 NBA Program Outcomes (PO1–PO12) with one identifying keyword each. Why is PO5 the dominant mapped PO in a Big Data Analytics course? (7 marks)

**(b)** A CO–PO mapping table has the following partial row vectors. Identify the **inadequacy** if any, and propose a corrective revision to one of the affected COs. (7 marks)

| CO | PO1 | PO2 | PO3 | PO4 | PO5 | PO6 | PO7 | PO8 | PO9 | PO10 | PO11 | PO12 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| CO1 | 2 | 2 | 1 | - | 3 | - | - | - | - | - | - | - |
| CO2 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 3 | - | - | - | - | - | - | - |
| CO3 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 3 | - | - | - | - | - | - | - |

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

(1 mark each for correctly identifying PO1–PO5 with their keyword, 2 marks for the PO5 dominance argument.)

- **PO1 — Engineering Knowledge** (apply, mathematics, fundamentals)
- **PO2 — Problem Analysis** (identify, formulate, analyze)
- **PO3 — Design/Development** (design, solution)
- **PO4 — Conduct Investigations** (investigation, experiment)
- **PO5 — Modern Tool Usage** (modern tools, ICT, modeling)
- (0.5 mark deduction if any PO is named incorrectly.)

**PO5 Dominance Argument (2 marks):** Big Data Analytics is fundamentally a tools-driven discipline. The course uses Hadoop, MapReduce, Spark, Hive, Pig, Sqoop, and Flume — every CO directly employs one or more of these tools. Hence, every CO maps to **PO5 at Level 3**, making PO5 the dominant column with weight $W_{PO_5} = 14$ out of 99 total articulation weight (≈14.14%).

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Inadequacy Identification (3 marks):**

| PO | Max over CO1..CO3 | Adequate? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| PO6 | - | ❌ **Inadequate** |
| PO7 | - | ❌ **Inadequate** |
| PO8 | - | ❌ **Inadequate** |
| PO9 | - | ❌ **Inadequate** |
| PO10 | - | ❌ **Inadequate** |
| PO11 | - | ❌ **Inadequate** |
| PO12 | - | ❌ **Inadequate** |

Therefore, **7 out of 12 POs (PO6, PO7, PO8, PO9, PO10, PO11, PO12) are not addressed** by any CO. The table **fails the NBA adequacy test**.

**Corrective Revision Proposal (4 marks):** One effective approach is to **revise CO5** to explicitly address the missing POs. For example, a revised CO5 could read:

> *"Design and evaluate end-to-end big data solutions for real-world problems, **document findings in team-based reports (PO6, PO9, PO10), incorporating ethical considerations (PO8), sustainable computing practices (PO7), project costing (PO11), and contemporary open-source trends (PO12)**."*

After this revision, the new row vector for CO5 would become approximately:

$$\vec{R}_{CO5}^{new} = [2,\ 2,\ 3,\ 2,\ 3,\ 3,\ 2,\ 3,\ 2,\ 2,\ 3,\ 2]$$

which now provides Level 1 or higher coverage for all 12 POs, satisfying the adequacy constraint.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **omit the Bloom's level annotation** when writing CO statements. KTU mandates Bloom's level to be present in every CO declaration. Expect a **0.5 mark deduction per CO** (max 2.5 marks) for missing Bloom's levels. Additionally, students frequently use **"–" (dash) when 0 is the actual numeric absence** — KTU accepts both, but consistency within a single table is mandatory. Do not mix dashes and zeros in the same submission.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — K6: CO–PO Mapping Table**

- **Definition:** CO–PO mapping is a $N_{CO} \times 12$ matrix quantifying how strongly each course outcome contributes to each of the 12 Program Outcomes. *(Must know: 12 POs + 2 PSOs).*

- **Three-Level Ordinal Scale:** **1 = Slight**, **2 = Moderate**, **3 = Substantial**. Dashes (`-`) for no correlation. **No other values are permitted** — do not use 0, 4, or 5.

- **Adequacy Rule:** $\forall j,\ \max_i(m_{i,j}) \geq 1$ — every PO must be touched by at least one CO. This is the single most-tested rule.

- **PSO Mapping:** In addition to the 12 POs, B.Tech programs in KTU 2024 have **2 Program Specific Outcomes (PSOs)**. Add two extra columns to the matrix for them.

- **Verb-Driven Mapping:** Always start with the **Bloom's verb** in the CO and match it against PO keywords (tools → PO5, society → PO6, environment → PO7, ethics → PO8, team → PO9, communication → PO10, finance → PO11, learning → PO12).

- **Articulation Weight:** $A_{CO_i} = W_{CO_i} / \sum W_{CO_k}$ — the percentage contribution of each CO to the program. Sum should be 100%.

- **Attainment Formula:** $\text{PO}_j\ \text{Attainment} = \frac{\sum_i m_{i,j} \cdot \text{CO}_i\ \text{Attainment}}{\sum_i m_{i,j}}$ — used after mapping to compute program-level attainment for NBA.

- **Common Errors to Avoid:**
  - Using a "0" value instead of "-" (inconsistent notation).
  - Failing the adequacy rule (leaving an entire PO column blank).
  - Mapping every CO to every PO at Level 3 (over-mapping; suspect to auditors).
  - Forgetting Bloom's level annotation on CO statements.

- **Reference Book:** *Hadoop: The Definitive Guide*, Tom White, O'Reilly Media, 4th Edition (2015) — the primary technical textbook for all Hadoop ecosystem concepts cited in CO1–CO5.

- **Exam Frequency:** KTU examiners report this as a **high-frequency, high-mark, predictable question** for both the lab viva and the ESE theory paper. A complete 14-mark mapping answer can be produced in under 12 minutes by a well-prepared student.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 2015 2 Professional Hadoop Solutions Boris Lublinsky
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 2015 2 Professional Hadoop Solutions Boris Lublinsky

# Professional Hadoop Solutions — Boris Lublinsky (2015)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> "Professional Hadoop Solutions" by Boris Lublinsky, Kevin T. Smith, and Alexey Yakubovich (Wrox, 2015) is a canonical reference text that systematically addresses the architecture, design patterns, and production-grade engineering of the Hadoop ecosystem. The book formalizes Hadoop not merely as a batch processing framework, but as a **distributed storage and computation platform** built upon the dual pillars of **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** and **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)**, augmented by a rich ecosystem of data processing, integration, and analytics tools.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "City Library" Analogy for Hadoop:**
> 
> Imagine a **massive city library** that must store **billions of books** and allow thousands of citizens to **read, search, and cross-reference** them simultaneously without collapsing the system.
> 
> - **HDFS** is the **warehouse system** — it chops every book into pages and stores duplicates in **three different library branches** (default replication factor = **3**) so that if one branch burns down, the book is safe.
> - **MapReduce / YARN** is the **team of librarians** who each grab a small section of books, read their part, summarize it (**Map**), and then combine summaries from all librarians into a final answer (**Reduce**).
> - **Hive, Pig, HBase, Sqoop, Flume** are the **specialized desks** — cataloging desk, search desk, archive desk, intake desk, and acquisition desk — each optimized for a specific library operation.
> 
> **Boris Lublinsky's** book provides the **architect's blueprint** for building this library at city-scale — covering everything from the **physical shelving layout** to the **staff coordination protocols**.

### 1.2 Key Reference Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Hadoop Configuration Constants (as per Lublinsky's Professional Hadoop Solutions, 2015):**
> 
> | Parameter | Default Value | Meaning |
> |-----------|---------------|---------|
> | `dfs.replication` | **3** | HDFS block replication factor |
> | `dfs.block.size` | **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x) | Default HDFS block size |
> | `mapreduce.map.memory.mb` | **1024 MB** | Container memory for Mappers |
> | `mapreduce.reduce.memory.mb` | **2048 MB** | Container memory for Reducers |
> | `yarn.nodemanager.resource.memory-mb` | **8192 MB** | Per-node YARN memory |
> | `yarn.scheduler.minimum-allocation-mb` | **1024 MB** | YARN minimum container allocation |
> | `io.file.buffer.size` | **4096 bytes** | Hadoop I/O buffer size |

### 1.3 The Lublinsky Taxonomy of Hadoop Components

Lublinsky classifies the Hadoop ecosystem into **four functional tiers**:

1. **Storage Tier**: HDFS, HBase, Cassandra
2. **Resource Management Tier**: YARN, Mesos
3. **Processing Tier**: MapReduce, Spark, Tez, Storm
4. **Integration & Analytics Tier**: Hive, Pig, Sqoop, Flume, Oozie, Mahout, ZooKeeper

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 HDFS Architecture — The Storage Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HDFS follows the Master/Slave (NameNode/DataNode) architecture** as formalized by Lublinsky. The NameNode is the **single point of metadata truth**, while DataNodes are the **dumb storage workers**.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **File Ingestion**: A client requests to write a file → NameNode returns a list of DataNode targets based on **rack-awareness** and **replication policy**.
2. **Block Splitting**: The file is split into **128 MB blocks** (default in Hadoop 2.x; 64 MB in 1.x).
3. **Pipeline Replication**: The first DataNode receives the block → forwards to the second → forwards to the third (in a **pipeline**), forming a chain of **3 replicas**.
4. **Heartbeat Mechanism**: Every **3 seconds**, each DataNode sends a heartbeat to the NameNode. If **10 consecutive heartbeats** (30 seconds) are missed, the DataNode is declared **dead** and replication is triggered.
5. **Read Path**: Client requests file → NameNode returns block locations → Client reads the **nearest replica** (shortest network distance).

### 2.2 MapReduce Programming Model — The Computation Engine

> [!NOTE]
> **Lublinsky emphasizes that MapReduce is a programming paradigm, not just a tool.** It transforms data through two key functions:
> 
> - **Map**: `(k1, v1) → list(k2, v2)` — local transformation
> - **Reduce**: `(k2, list(v2)) → list(k3, v3)` — global aggregation

**The Shuffle and Sort Phase** (the heart of MapReduce):

1. **Map Output Buffer**: Each Mapper has a **circular buffer** of size `mapreduce.task.io.sort.mb` (default **100 MB**).
2. **Spill Threshold**: When buffer reaches `mapreduce.map.sort.spill.percent` (**80%**), it spills to disk.
3. **Partitioning**: Spilled data is partitioned using `HashPartitioner`: `partition = key.hashCode() % numReduceTasks`.
4. **Combiner (Mini-Reducer)**: Runs locally to pre-aggregate data, reducing network traffic.
5. **Merge Sort**: Spills are merged into a single sorted file per Mapper.
6. **HTTP Fetch**: Reducers fetch their partition data from all Mappers via **HTTP**.
7. **Final Reduce**: Data is sorted and grouped by key, then passed to the user-defined `reduce()` function.

### 2.3 YARN — The Resource Manager (Hadoop 2.x Innovation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** decouples resource management from application logic, enabling **multi-tenancy** and **non-MapReduce workloads** (Spark, Tez, Storm).

**YARN Component Architecture:**

- **ResourceManager (RM)**: Global authority for resources. Contains:
  - **Scheduler**: Allocates containers based on capacity/fairness policies
  - **ApplicationsManager (AsM)**: Accepts job submissions
- **NodeManager (NM)**: Per-node agent; manages **containers** (CPU + memory bundles)
- **ApplicationMaster (AM)**: Per-application; negotiates resources with RM and works with NMs
- **Container**: The fundamental unit of execution (RAM + vCores)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Quantitative Formulas for KTU 2024 Big Data Analytics:**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|----------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | HDFS Block Count | $N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{FileSize}{BlockSize} \right\rceil$ | $N_{blocks}$, $FileSize$, $BlockSize$ | Storage planning |
| 2 | Replication Storage Cost | $S_{total} = FileSize \times ReplicationFactor$ | $S_{total}$, $ReplicationFactor$ | Capacity planning |
| 3 | HDFS Rack-Aware Assignment | $1^{st} \rightarrow$ local, $2^{nd} \rightarrow$ local rack, $3^{rd} \rightarrow$ different rack | — | Fault tolerance |
| 4 | MapReduce Partition | $p(k) = \left\vert k.hashCode() \right\vert \mod R$ | $k$ = key, $R$ = #reducers | Data distribution |
| 5 | DataNode Heartbeat Timeout | $T_{dead} = 3 \times H_{interval} \times M_{missed}$ | Default: $3 \times 10 = 30$ sec | Failure detection |
| 6 | YARN Container Memory | $M_{alloc} = M_{total} - M_{AM} - M_{NM}$ | $M_{alloc}$ allocatable, $M_{AM}$ ApplicationMaster, $M_{NM}$ NodeManager | Cluster sizing |
| 7 | MapReduce Sort Spill | $S_{spill} = 0.80 \times B_{sort}$ | Default: $0.80 \times 100 = 80$ MB | I/O tuning |
| 8 | Speedup (Amdahl's Law) | $S(p) = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{p}}$ | $f$ = parallel fraction, $p$ = processors | Hadoop scaling analysis |
| 9 | Hadoop I/O Buffer | $B_{io} = 4096$ bytes | Default | Streaming I/O |
| 10 | Reduce Slow-start | $t_{slow} = 0.05 \times T_{map}$ | Default **5%** of map tasks | Optimization |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lublinsky's framework is directly applicable to:**
> 
> - **Telecom**: Processing CDR (Call Detail Records) at petabyte scale for churn prediction
> - **Banking**: Fraud detection using HBase for low-latency lookups
> - **Healthcare**: Genomics data analysis using Hadoop + Spark
> - **E-commerce**: Clickstream analytics using Flume + Hive
> - **IoT**: Sensor data ingestion using Kafka + Flume + HDFS

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: HDFS Block Count and Storage Planning

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A **2.5 GB** log file is ingested into HDFS with the default **128 MB** block size and **replication factor of 3**. Calculate the total number of blocks, total storage consumed, and the number of pipeline transfers required.

**Step 1: Calculate the Number of Blocks**

We need to find the ceiling of the file size divided by block size:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FileSize &= 2.5 \text{ GB} \\
&= 2.5 \times 1024 \text{ MB} \\
&= 2560 \text{ MB} \\
\\
BlockSize &= 128 \text{ MB} \\
\\
N_{blocks} &= \left\lceil \frac{FileSize}{BlockSize} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{2560 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil 20.0 \right\rceil \\
&= 20 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The file size of 2560 MB divides evenly by 128 MB, so we get exactly **20 full blocks** with no partial remainder.

**Step 2: Calculate Total HDFS Storage Consumed**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= N_{blocks} \times BlockSize \times ReplicationFactor \\
&= 20 \times 128 \text{ MB} \times 3 \\
&= 7680 \text{ MB} \\
&= 7.5 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Each of the 20 blocks is replicated 3 times across the cluster. So the raw 2.5 GB logically becomes **7.5 GB physically**.

**Step 3: Number of Pipeline Transfers**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{transfers} &= N_{blocks} \times (ReplicationFactor - 1) \\
&= 20 \times (3 - 1) \\
&= 40 \text{ inter-DataNode transfers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** For each block, the first replica goes to the **local DataNode** (no transfer). The 2nd and 3rd replicas are transferred through the pipeline. So **2 transfers per block** × **20 blocks = 40 transfers**.

### 3.2 Derivation: MapReduce Partitioning — Distributing Data Across Reducers

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A Mapper emits key-value pairs. The keys (after hashing) and the number of reducers are as follows: key hashes = {`42`, `17`, `89`, `42`, `55`, `17`}, and `R = 3` reducers. Determine the partition for each key-value pair.

**Step 1: Apply the Hash Partitioning Formula**

The partition is computed as:

$$
p(k) = \vert k.hashCode() \vert \mod R
$$
