topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 2011 4 Spark in Action Jean-Georges Perrin O'Reilly Media 1/e

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Apache Spark

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apache Spark** is a unified analytics engine for **large-scale distributed data processing** that provides high-level APIs in **Java, Scala, Python (PySpark), and R**, along with an optimized engine that supports general execution graphs (Directed Acyclic Graphs – DAGs) for in-memory computation and fault tolerance.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Big Data Analytics syllabus (Module Experiment 2 — *Spark in Action*), Spark is positioned as the **successor and enhancement to Hadoop MapReduce**, solving its primary limitations: iterative disk I/O, lack of in-memory processing, and limited expressiveness for complex algorithms (e.g., Machine Learning, Graph processing, Streaming).

| **Spark Property** | **Value / Behavior** |
|---|---|
| Initial Release | **May 2014** (open-sourced by AMPLab, UC Berkeley) |
| Current Generation (2024) | **Spark 3.5.x / 4.x** lineage |
| Core Abstraction | **RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset)** |
| Speed Claim | Up to **100× faster** than Hadoop MapReduce (in-memory) |
| Languages Supported | **Scala, Java, Python, R, SQL** |
| Cluster Manager Support | Standalone, YARN, Mesos, Kubernetes |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy for Spark:**
> 
> Imagine a massive restaurant that must serve **10,000 dishes per hour**. 
> 
> - **Hadoop MapReduce** is like a chef who must write each ingredient to a *fridge (disk)* after every tiny prep step, then read it back, then write again. Tons of fridge-opening (disk I/O) → **slow**.
> - **Apache Spark** is like a chef who keeps all the *intermediate ingredients* on the **prep counter (RAM/memory)**. The whole multi-course meal (DAG of tasks) is assembled on the counter, then plated and served (output) in one go. The counter is **resilient** — if the chef spills something, the *recipe card* (lineage) tells them exactly how to recreate it.

This analogy captures the **three defining traits** of Spark:
1. **In-Memory Computation** — keeping intermediate data in RAM.
2. **DAG Execution Engine** — planning the whole job as a recipe before cooking.
3. **Fault Tolerance via Lineage** — rebuilding lost data using the *recipe card* (lineage graph), not a *fridge photocopy* (replication).

---

## 1.3 The "Spark in Action" Philosophical Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> The reference text (*Spark in Action* by Jean-Georges Perrin) frames Spark not as a single tool but as a **multi-library ecosystem** where every analytics workload (Batch, Streaming, ML, Graph, SQL) is expressed through the same **distributed collection abstraction (RDD / DataFrame / Dataset)**.

| Spark Library | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Spark Core** | RDD APIs, task scheduling, memory management |
| **Spark SQL** | Structured data + DataFrame/Dataset APIs |
| **Spark Streaming (DStreams & Structured Streaming)** | Real-time stream processing |
| **MLlib** | Scalable Machine Learning |
| **GraphX** | Graph-parallel computation |
| **SparkR** | R language bindings |

---

## 1.4 Why Spark Was Built — The Motivation

The **three V's of Big Data** that Spark must tame:

- **Volume** → Petabytes of data
- **Velocity** → Real-time streams
- **Variety** → Structured, semi-structured, unstructured

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** MapReduce was a **two-stage** (Map → Shuffle → Reduce) rigid model. Spark's **DAG engine** allows an **N-stage** directed acyclic pipeline, with stages fused when possible — drastically reducing disk I/O.

$$\text{MapReduce Stages} = 2 \text{ (Map, Reduce)} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Spark Stages} = 1 \text{ to } N$$

---

## 1.5 Core Abstraction — RDD

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset)** is the *fundamental* data structure of Spark — an **immutable**, **distributed collection of objects** that can be processed **in parallel** across a cluster.

The five properties of an RDD (defined by Zaharia et al.):

1. **Partitions** — atomic chunks of the dataset.
2. **Dependencies** — lineage to parent RDDs (narrow vs. wide).
3. **Compute Function** — `f: Iterator[T] => Iterator[U]`.
4. **Partitioner (optional)** — e.g., `HashPartitioner`.
5. **Preferred Locations (optional)** — HDFS block / data locality hints.

---

## 1.6 Module Experiment 2 Context (KTU 2024)

> [!NOTE]
> **Experiment 2 — "Spark in Action"** in the KTU B.Tech BDA lab typically requires students to:
> 1. Install / configure Spark (local mode or on a Hadoop cluster).
> 2. Launch `spark-shell` (Scala) / `pyspark` (Python) / `spark-submit` (jobs).
> 3. Load a dataset (text, CSV, JSON, Parquet) into an RDD / DataFrame.
> 4. Apply a series of **transformations** and **actions**.
> 5. Observe **lazy evaluation** through DAG visualization (`toDebugString`).
> 6. Persist / cache data and measure performance.
> 7. Write the result back to HDFS / local FS.

This is the *practical* counterpart of theoretical Module 1 (Hadoop/MapReduce).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Spark Architecture — The Cluster Topology

A Spark application runs as **two independent processes**:

| Process | Role | Runs On |
|---|---|---|
| **Driver Program** | Builds `SparkContext`, builds DAG, schedules tasks | **Client / Master node** |
| **Executor(s)** | Runs tasks, holds data in memory/disk | **Worker nodes** |

### 2.1.1 Internal Architecture Stack

The Spark runtime is layered (bottom to top):

1. **Cluster Manager** — Standalone, YARN, Mesos, K8s (allocates resources).
2. **Spark Driver** — contains `SparkContext` (entry point), `DAGScheduler`, `TaskScheduler`.
3. **Spark Executors** — JVM processes with cached RDDs and task slots.
4. **SparkSession (Unified Entry Point since 2.0)** — wraps `SparkContext`, `SQLContext`, `HiveContext`, `StreamingContext`.

---

## 2.2 The Execution Flow — From Code to Cluster

The end-to-end journey of a Spark job:

$$\text{User Code} \rightarrow \text{DAG of RDDs} \rightarrow \text{DAGScheduler (stages)} \rightarrow \text{TaskScheduler (tasks)} \rightarrow \text{Executors (run)} \rightarrow \text{Result back to Driver}$$

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown:

1. **RDD Object Graph Created** — transformations are *recorded*, not executed.
2. **Action Triggered** — e.g., `count()`, `collect()`, `saveAsTextFile()`.
3. **DAGScheduler builds the DAG** of stages, separated by **shuffle boundaries** (wide dependencies).
4. **TaskScheduler** sends tasks to executors via the **Cluster Manager**.
5. **Executors run tasks** in parallel; intermediate shuffle data is written to local disk.
6. **Results returned** to the driver or written to external storage.

---

## 2.3 RDD Dependencies — Narrow vs. Wide

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is one of the **most heavily tested concepts** in KTU exams.

| Dependency Type | Definition | Shuffle Required? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Narrow** | Each partition of parent → **at most one** partition of child | ❌ No | `map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, `union` |
| **Wide** | Each partition of parent → **multiple** partitions of child | ✅ Yes (network) | `groupByKey`, `reduceByKey`, `join`, `repartition` |

$$\text{Number of Stages} = \text{Number of Wide Dependency Boundaries} + 1$$

---

## 2.4 Lazy Evaluation — The Heart of Spark's Speed

> [!NOTE]
> Spark does **not** execute transformations immediately. It builds a **logical plan (DAG)** and only fires physical execution when an **action** is called.

- **Transformations** (lazy): `map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, `groupByKey`, `reduceByKey`, `join`, `distinct`, `union`, `sample`, `coalesce`, `repartition`.
- **Actions** (eager): `collect`, `count`, `first`, `take(n)`, `reduce`, `fold`, `aggregate`, `countByKey`, `foreach`, `saveAsTextFile`, `saveAsSequenceFile`.

**Why lazy?**
- **Optimization** — Catalyst-style pipelining (fused stages).
- **Efficiency** — only the path required by the final action is computed.
- **Fault Tolerance** — lineage is preserved.

---

## 2.5 Caching and Persistence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If an RDD is reused multiple times, persist it. Otherwise Spark recomputes from lineage — expensive for long chains.

**Storage Levels** (from fastest/most fragile to slowest/safest):

| Level | Memory | Disk | Serialized | Replicated |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `MEMORY_ONLY` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `MEMORY_AND_DISK` | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `MEMORY_ONLY_SER` | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `DISK_ONLY` | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `OFF_HEAP` | Off-heap Tungsten | | | |

**Rule of Thumb:** Use `MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER` when objects are **large and memory-tight**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| **Concept** | **Equation / Rule** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Speed-up vs MapReduce | $S_{\text{Spark}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{MapReduce}}}{T_{\text{Spark}}} \approx 10 \text{ to } 100$ | Order-of-magnitude claim |
| Partition Count | $P = \text{total cores} \times 2 \text{ to } 4$ | Recommended cluster sizing |
| Memory per Executor | $M_{\text{exec}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{total}} - M_{\text{OS}}}{N_{\text{exec}}}$ | Leaves RAM for OS overhead |
| Task Parallelism | $\text{Parallelism} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{exec}}} \text{cores}_i$ | Total concurrent tasks |
| Stages Count | $\text{Stages} = \text{wide deps} + 1$ | DAGScheduler metric |
| Lineage Cost | $C_{\text{recompute}} = \prod_{i=1}^{k} \frac{\vert D_i \vert}{\vert P_i \vert}$ | Re-execution cost if a partition is lost |
| Data Locality Levels | $L \in \{\text{PROCESS\_LOCAL}, \text{NODE\_LOCAL}, \text{NO\_PREF}, \text{RACK\_LOCAL}, \text{ANY}\}$ | Delay = $1, 2, 2, 3, \infty$ |
| Executor Memory Split | $M_{\text{heap}} = M_{\text{exec}} \times 0.6$ (storage) $+ M_{\text{exec}} \times 0.4$ (shuffle) | Default Spark memoryFraction |

---

## 2.7 Transformations vs. Actions — A Complete Inventory

### Transformations (Lazy)

| Transformation | Return Type | Dependency |
|---|---|---|
| `map(f)` | RDD[U] | Narrow |
| `flatMap(f)` | RDD[U] | Narrow |
| `filter(f)` | RDD[T] | Narrow |
| `mapPartitions(f)` | RDD[U] | Narrow |
| `union(other)` | RDD[T] | Narrow |
| `distinct()` | RDD[T] | Wide |
| `groupByKey()` | RDD[(K, Iterable[V])] | **Wide** |
| `reduceByKey(f)` | RDD[(K, V)] | Wide (with map-side combine) |
| `sortByKey(asc)` | RDD[(K, V)] | Wide |
| `join(other)` | RDD[(K, (V, W))] | Wide |
| `repartition(n)` | RDD[T] | **Wide (shuffle)** |
| `coalesce(n)` | RDD[T] | Narrow (no shuffle) |
| `sample(withReplacement, frac, seed)` | RDD[T] | Narrow |
| `subtract(other)` | RDD[T] | Wide |
| `cartesian(other)` | RDD[(T, U)] | Wide |

### Actions (Eager)

| Action | Returns | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `collect()` | Array[T] | Brings everything to driver (DANGEROUS for large data) |
| `count()` | Long | |
| `first()` | T | |
| `take(n)` | Array[T] | |
| `takeOrdered(n)` | Array[T] | |
| `top(n)` | Array[T] | |
| `reduce(f)` | T | |
| `fold(zero)(f)` | T | |
| `aggregate(zero)(seq, comb)` | U | |
| `countByKey()` | Map[K, Long] | |
| `foreach(f)` | Unit | Side-effect only |
| `saveAsTextFile(path)` | Unit | Writes to HDFS / FS |
| `saveAsSequenceFile(path)` | Unit | Hadoop SequenceFile |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Spark Use Case** |
|---|---|
| **Finance** | Real-time fraud detection on transaction streams |
| **Healthcare** | Genomics pipeline (GATK, variant calling) |
| **E-commerce** | Recommendation engines (MLlib Alternating Least Squares) |
| **Telecom** | CDR (Call Detail Record) log analytics |
| **IoT** | Sensor stream anomaly detection |
| **Search** | PageRank and graph algorithms (GraphX) |
| **Cybersecurity** | Log correlation, threat hunting (ELK + Spark) |

---

## 2.9 Spark vs. MapReduce — Why Spark Won

| Feature | Hadoop MapReduce | Apache Spark |
|---|---|---|
| Processing Model | Disk-based, 2-stage | In-memory, DAG-based |
| Speed | Baseline | **10–100× faster** |
| Iterative Algorithms (ML) | Very slow (each iteration writes to disk) | **Native, in-memory** |
| Ease of Use | Verbose Java | Concise, interactive shells |
| Streaming | Storm / S4 | Built-in (DStreams, Structured Streaming) |
| ML | Mahout (limited) | MLlib (full suite) |
| Cost of Failure | Re-execute from scratch (no lineage) | Re-execute from lineage DAG |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Word Count in Spark (The "Hello World" of Big Data)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical** worked example from *Spark in Action*. It demonstrates transformation chaining, lazy evaluation, and actions. We will derive the **DAG**, count the **stages**, and compute the **partitioning** mathematically.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

Given a text file `shakespeare.txt` containing the complete works of William Shakespeare (~5.5 MB, ~124,000 lines), compute the **frequency of every word**.

### 3.1.2 The PySpark Code (Step by Step, with Annotations)

```python
# Step 0: Create a SparkSession (entry point)
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName("WordCount") \
    .master("local[2]") \
    .getOrCreate()

sc = spark.sparkContext   # SparkContext — the legacy low-level entry point

# Step 1: Read the file → base RDD (lazy)
lines = sc.textFile("shakespeare.txt")
# lines: RDD[String], partitions = 2 (because local[2])

# Step 2: Split each line into words (Transformation 1 — Narrow)
words = lines.flatMap(lambda line: line.split(" "))
# words: RDD[String], no shuffle

# Step 3: Normalize (Transformation 2 — Narrow)
clean_words = words.filter(lambda w: len(w) > 0).map(lambda w: w.lower().strip(".,;:!?\"'()[]"))
# filter + map fuse into one stage (narrow)

# Step 4: Map each word to a (word, 1) tuple (Transformation 3 — Narrow)
pairs = clean_words.map(lambda w: (w, 1))
# pairs: RDD[(String, Int)]

# Step 5: Reduce by key (Transformation 4 — WIDE — triggers shuffle)
counts = pairs.reduceByKey(lambda a, b: a + b)
# counts: RDD[(String, Int)]
# This is the shuffle boundary → Stage 1 ends, Stage 2 begins

# Step 6: ACTION — triggers full execution
result = counts.collect()

# Step 7: Print top 10
for word, count in sorted(result, key=lambda x: -x[1])[:10]:
    print(f"{word:>15}  {count}")

spark.stop()
```

### 3.1.3 Lineage DAG Derivation

$$\text{lines} \xrightarrow{\text{flatMap (narrow)}} \text{words} \xrightarrow{\text{filter + map (narrow)}} \text{clean\_words} \xrightarrow{\text{map (narrow)}} \text{pairs} \xrightarrow{\text{reduceByKey (WIDE)}} \text{counts} \xrightarrow{\text{collect (ACTION)}}$$

### 3.1.4 Stage Boundary Analysis

| Stage | Transformations | Type | Shuffle? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stage 0 | `textFile` → `flatMap` → `filter` → `map` | Narrow chain | ❌ No |
| Stage 1 | `reduceByKey` | Wide | ✅ Yes |
| Stage 2 (final) | `collect` | Driver-side aggregation | ❌ No |

$$\text{Stages} = 1 \text{ wide boundary} + 1 = 2 \text{ stages}$$

### 3.1.5 Numerical Derivation — Partition Count

For `local[2]` with a 5.5 MB file, the **default block size in HDFS is 128 MB**, so on a local file system Spark applies `minPartitions = 2` (matching the local threads).

$$P = 2 \quad ; \quad \text{Bytes per partition} = \frac{5.5 \text{ MB}}{2} = 2.75 \text{ MB / partition}$$

### 3.1.6 Word Count Output (Top 10 Expected)

| Word | Approx. Count |
|---|---|
| the | ~27,800 |
| and | ~26,500 |
| i | ~21,400 |
| to | ~20,200 |
| of | ~18,300 |
| a | ~15,600 |
| you | ~14,800 |
| my | ~13,200 |
| in | ~12,900 |
| that | ~11,500 |

> [!NOTE]
> The exact counts depend on the version of the text. The numbers above are derived from a typical `gutenberg.org` copy of the *Complete Works of William Shakespeare*.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — `reduceByKey` vs. `groupByKey`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favorite 14-mark KTU question**: *"Compare reduceByKey and groupByKey. Which is more efficient and why?"*

### 3.2.1 The Mathematical Problem

We have $N$ key-value pairs distributed across $P$ partitions. For each unique key, we want to compute the sum.

### 3.2.2 The `groupByKey` Algorithm

Step 1 (MAP-SIDE): For each pair $(k, v)$, emit $(k, v)$. **Cost = $O(N)$ in memory.**

Step 2 (SHUFFLE): All values for key $k$ are co-located on one executor. **Network cost = $O(N)$ bytes transferred.**

Step 3 (REDUCE-SIDE): For each key, sum the iterable. **Cost = $O(N)$ in CPU.**

**Total network bytes**:
$$B_{\text{groupByKey}} = N \cdot s_v$$
where $s_v$ is the average serialized size of a value.

### 3.2.3 The `reduceByKey` Algorithm (with map-side combine)

Step 1 (MAP-SIDE COMBINE): For each key in the **local partition**, pre-aggregate. **Cost = $O(N)$ CPU, $O(\text{unique keys per partition})$ memory.**

Step 2 (SHUFFLE): Only the **partially-aggregated** tuples are sent over the network.

Step 3 (REDUCE-SIDE): Final reduction per key.

**Total network bytes**:
$$B_{\text{reduceByKey}} = K_P \cdot s_v$$
where $K_P$ is the number of **distinct keys per partition** (typically $K_P \ll N$).

### 3.2.4 The Comparative Ratio

$$\eta = \frac{B_{\text{groupByKey}}}{B_{\text{reduceByKey}}} = \frac{N}{K_P}$$

For the Shakespeare example with $N \approx 980{,}000$ tokens and $K_P \approx 8{,}000$ unique words per partition:

$$\eta = \frac{980{,}000}{8{,}000} \approx 122.5$$

**Conclusion:** `reduceByKey` is **~122× cheaper on the network** than `groupByKey` for this dataset.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule:** Always prefer `reduceByKey`, `aggregateByKey`, or `foldByKey` over `groupByKey` when the operation is **associative and commutative** (e.g., sum, count, max, min).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Cache Impact on Iterative Algorithms

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic interview and exam** problem. Demonstrates why **ML algorithms (e.g., K-Means, Logistic Regression)** benefit dramatically from `persist()`.

### 3.3.1 Setup

Suppose an RDD `trainingData` is used **10 times** in a loop (e.g., 10 iterations of gradient descent). Each iteration triggers a transformation chain that is **20 stages long**, taking $T_1 = 200$ ms per chain. Without caching, each iteration re-executes the full lineage.

### 3.3.2 Without Caching

$$T_{\text{no cache}} = 10 \times 200 = 2{,}000 \text{ ms} = 2 \text{ s}$$

### 3.3.3 With Caching (in-memory)

First iteration computes and caches: $T_{\text{first}} = 200$ ms. Subsequent 9 iterations read from RAM: $T_{\text{cached}} = 5$ ms (memory read).

$$T_{\text{cached}} = 200 + 9 \times 5 = 245 \text{ ms}$$

### 3.3.4 Speedup

$$S = \frac{T_{\text{no cache}}}{T_{\text{cached}}} = \frac{2{,}000}{245} \approx 8.16\times$$

### 3.3.5 Threshold for Caching

The **break-even** point is when the cost of re-computation exceeds the cost of memory storage:

$$C_{\text{recompute}} > C_{\text{storage}}$$

$$T_{\text{lineage}} \times (N_{\text{uses}} - 1) > C_{\text{mem}} \times \vert D \vert$$

For very large $N_{\text{uses}}$ (like 10, 100, 1000 in ML), **caching is always a win**.

---

## 3.4 Complete Analytical Matrix — Transformations & Their Properties

| Transformation | Return Type | Shuffle? | Associative / Commutative? | Use Case | Lazy? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `map(f)` | RDD[U] | ❌ | N/A | Element-wise transform | ✅ |
| `filter(p)` | RDD[T] | ❌ | N/A | Drop unwanted | ✅ |
| `flatMap(f)` | RDD[U] | ❌ | N/A | Flatten + map | ✅ |
| `mapPartitions(f)` | RDD[U] | ❌ | N/A | Iterator-based (e.g., DB writes) | ✅ |
| `union(rdd)` | RDD[T] | ❌ | N/A | Concatenate | ✅ |
| `distinct()` | RDD[T] | ✅ | N/A | Deduplicate | ✅ |
| `groupByKey()` | RDD[(K, Iter[V])] | ✅ | N/A | Group (avoid if possible!) | ✅ |
| `reduceByKey(f)` | RDD[(K, V)] | ✅ | Required | Aggregate per key | ✅ |
| `aggregateByKey(zero)(seq, comb)` | RDD[(K, U)] | ✅ | seq+comb required | Different input/output type | ✅ |
| `sortByKey()` | RDD[(K, V)] | ✅ | N/A | Range partitioning | ✅ |
| `join(rdd)` | RDD[(K, (V, W))] | ✅ | N/A | Inner join | ✅ |
| `cogroup(rdd)` | RDD[(K, (Iter[V], Iter[W]))] | ✅ | N/A | Group multiple RDDs by key | ✅ |
| `repartition(n)` | RDD[T] | ✅ | N/A | Increase or decrease partitions | ✅ |
| `coalesce(n)` | RDD[T] | ❌ | N/A | Decrease partitions (no shuffle) | ✅ |
| `sample(p, seed)` | RDD[T] | ❌ | N/A | Random sample | ✅ |

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation — `aggregate` Action

> [!NOTE]
> `aggregate` is a **generalized reduce** that allows the **return type to differ from the RDD element type** — essential for things like computing the mean, variance, or building a dictionary.

### 3.5.1 Definition

$$\text{aggregate}[U](z: U)(seqOp: (U, T) \Rightarrow U, combOp: (U, U) \Rightarrow U): U$$

- $z$ — **zero value** (initial accumulator, e.g., `0` for sum, `("min", ∞)` for min).
- `seqOp` — **sequence operator** (combines an element with the accumulator).
- `combOp` — **combine operator** (merges two accumulators).

### 3.5.2 Step-by-Step Execution (work it out)

Suppose `RDD = [1, 2, 3, 4]` split into 2 partitions: `P1 = [1, 2]`, `P2 = [3, 4]`.

**Goal:** Compute the **mean** using `aggregate`.

**Step 0:** Choose the accumulator type: `(sum: Int, count: Int)`.

**Step 1:** Define zero value:
$$z = (0, 0)$$

**Step 2:** Define `seqOp`:
$$\text{seqOp}((s, c), x) = (s + x, c + 1)$$

**Step 3:** Define `combOp`:
$$\text{combOp}((s_1, c_1), (s_2, c_2)) = (s_1 + s_2, c_1 + c_2)$$

**Step 4:** Per-partition reduction:

Partition P1 starts with $z = (0, 0)$:
- Take 1: $(0 + 1, 0 + 1) = (1, 1)$
- Take 2: $(1 + 2, 1 + 1) = (3, 2)$

Partition P2 starts with $z = (0, 0)$:
- Take 3: $(0 + 3, 0 + 1) = (3, 1)$
- Take 4: $(3 + 4, 1 + 1) = (7, 2)$

**Step 5:** Combine partitions:
$$\text{combOp}((3, 2), (7, 2)) = (3 + 7, 2 + 2) = (10, 4)$$

**Step 6:** Final result:
$$\text{Mean} = \frac{\text{sum}}{\text{count}} = \frac{10}{4} = 2.5$$

**Verification by hand:** $\dfrac{1 + 2 + 3 + 4}{4} = \dfrac{10}{4} = 2.5$ ✓

```python
# PySpark code for the same:
rdd = sc.parallelize([1, 2, 3, 4], 2)
result = rdd.aggregate(
    (0, 0),
    lambda acc, value: (acc[0] + value, acc[1] + 1),
    lambda acc1, acc2: (acc1[0] + acc2[0], acc1[1] + acc2[1])
)
mean = result[0] / result[1]
print(mean)  # 2.5
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Mistake:** Students often write `combOp` as `lambda a, b: a + b`, which fails because `(s, c)` tuples are **not summable** with `+` — you must explicitly unpack `acc1[0] + acc2[0]` and `acc1[1] + acc2[1]`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Spark Cluster Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client["User Client / spark-submit"] --> Driver["Driver Program (JVM)"]
    Driver -->|creates| SparkCtx["SparkContext / SparkSession"]
    SparkCtx -->|registers with| CM["Cluster Manager (YARN / Standalone / K8s)"]
    CM -->|allocates resources| E1["Executor 1 (Worker Node)"]
    CM -->|allocates resources| E2["Executor 2 (Worker Node)"]
    CM -->|allocates resources| E3["Executor 3 (Worker Node)"]
    E1 --> C1["Core 1 - Task Slot"]
    E1 --> C2["Core 2 - Task Slot"]
    E2 --> C3["Core 3 - Task Slot"]
    E2 --> C4["Core 4 - Task Slot"]
    E3 --> C5["Core 5 - Task Slot"]
    E3 --> C6["Core 6 - Task Slot"]
    Driver -->|sends DAG stages| TaskSched["TaskScheduler"]
    TaskSched -->|launches tasks| E1
    TaskSched -->|launches tasks| E2
    TaskSched -->|launches tasks| E3
    E1 -->|shuffle write block| SFS["Shuffle Service / Disk"]
    E2 -->|shuffle read block| SFS
    E3 -->|shuffle read block| SFS
    Driver -->|collects results| Result["Result / Output HDFS"]
```

**Description:**
- The **Driver** is the brain — it builds the DAG, negotiates with the **Cluster Manager**, and assigns work to **Executors**.
- **Executors** are JVM processes that hold **task slots** (one per CPU core) and run tasks in parallel.
- The **Shuffle Service** sits on each worker node and serves shuffle blocks to other executors (in YARN, it runs as an auxiliary service).

---

## 4.2 RDD Lineage DAG — The "Recipe Card" Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F["HDFS File: shakespeare.txt"] -->|textFile| R0["RDD0 lines (2 partitions)"]
    R0 -->|flatMap split| R1["RDD1 words"]
    R1 -->|filter + map| R2["RDD2 clean_words"]
    R2 -->|map to pair| R3["RDD3 (word, 1)"]
    R3 -->|reduceByKey SHUFFLE BOUNDARY| R4["RDD4 (word, count)"]
    R4 -->|collect ACTION| D["Driver Result"]
    style R3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Description:**
- **RDD0 → RDD3** is **Stage 0** (all narrow dependencies, fused into one task group).
- **RDD3 → RDD4** is the **shuffle boundary** (wide dependency), so a new **Stage 1** starts.
- The yellow node `(word, 1)` marks the pre-shuffle pair RDD.
- The green node is the post-reduce RDD.
- The red node is the final action result.

---

## 4.3 Spark Job Execution Pipeline — End-to-End Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User submits Spark Application"] --> B["Driver launches SparkContext"]
    B --> C["Build DAG of RDD transformations (LAZY)"]
    C --> D{"Action called?"}
    D -->|No| C
    D -->|Yes| E["DAGScheduler: split DAG into Stages"]
    E --> F["TaskScheduler: assign Tasks to Executors"]
    F --> G["Executors run Tasks on data partitions"]
    G --> H{"Shuffle required?"}
    H -->|Yes| I["Shuffle Write to local disk"]
    I --> J["Shuffle Read by next stage's Executors"]
    J --> K["Final Stage produces result"]
    H -->|No| K
    K --> L["Result returned to Driver / Written to HDFS"]
    L --> M["Job complete; resources released"]
```

---

## 4.4 Caching / Persistence Flow — When to `persist()`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["RDD Created"] --> Use{"Will this RDD be used more than once?"}
    Use -->|No| Plain["Leave unpersisted - clean lineage recompute"]
    Use -->|Yes| Size{"Does it fit in memory?"}
    Size -->|Yes| M["persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_ONLY)"]
    Size -->|No - but tolerable| MD["persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)"]
    Size -->|No - very large| MS["persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER)"]
    M --> Trigger["First action: compute + cache in RAM"]
    MD --> Trigger
    MS --> Trigger
    Trigger --> Reuse["Subsequent actions: read from cache"]
    Reuse --> Unpersist["unpersist() when done to free RAM"]
```

---

## 4.5 Spark vs. MapReduce — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph H["HADOOP MAPREDUCE"]
        H1["Input (HDFS)"] --> H2["MAP phase\nwrite to local disk"]
        H2 --> H3["SHUFFLE & SORT\nnetwork transfer"]
        H3 --> H4["REDUCE phase\nwrite to HDFS"]
        H4 --> H5["Final Output (HDFS)"]
    end
    subgraph S["APACHE SPARK"]
        S1["Input (HDFS / S3 / local)"] --> S2["In-memory partitions\n(RDD cache)"]
        S2 --> S3["DAG of transformations\n(narrow chain)"]
        S3 --> S4{"Shuffle required?"}
        S4 -->|Yes| S5["Shuffle across executors"]
        S4 -->|No| S6["Next stage directly"]
        S5 --> S6
        S6 --> S7["In-memory intermediate results"]
        S7 --> S8["Action output"]
    end
    style H2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style H4 fill:#FFB6C1
    style S2 fill:#90EE90
    style S7 fill:#90EE90
```

**Key Visual Takeaway:** Pink nodes (Hadoop) require **disk I/O**; green nodes (Spark) keep data **in RAM** — this is the *single biggest reason* for Spark's speed advantage.

---

## 4.6 Data Locality Hierarchy (Spark's Network Minimization Strategy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["PROCESS_LOCAL - same executor\n delay 0"]
    L2["NODE_LOCAL - same node, different executor\n delay 1"]
    L3["RACK_LOCAL - same rack, different node\n delay 2"]
    L4["ANY - anywhere in cluster\n delay 3+"]
    L1 -->|best| L2
    L2 -->|fallback| L3
    L3 -->|fallback| L4
```

> [!NOTE]
> Spark's `TaskScheduler` waits a configurable time (`spark.locality.wait`) for each level before falling back. This is why Spark on HDFS is so efficient: the scheduler reads the block locations and tries to place each task on a node that already has the data.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**

**Q1.** Define an RDD. List any **four** properties of an RDD.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset)** is the fundamental data structure of Apache Spark — an immutable, distributed collection of objects that can be processed in parallel across a cluster.
>
> **Four properties:**
> 1. **Partitions** — the dataset is divided into atomic chunks (partitions) stored on different nodes.
> 2. **Dependencies** — the RDD tracks its lineage, i.e., the series of transformations from which it was derived.
> 3. **Compute function** — each RDD knows how to compute its elements (e.g., `Iterator[T] => Iterator[U]`).
> 4. **Partitioner (optional)** — for keyed RDDs, a `Partitioner` object (e.g., `HashPartitioner`) defines how keys are distributed.

> *(For the 4th property, "preferred locations" is also acceptable.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **transformations** and **actions** in Spark. Give **two examples** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Transformations | Actions |
|---|---|---|
| **Execution** | Lazy (recorded in DAG, not executed immediately) | Eager (trigger full job execution) |
| **Return Type** | A new RDD | A non-RDD value (e.g., `Long`, `Array`, `Unit`) |
| **Examples** | `map()`, `filter()`, `flatMap()`, `reduceByKey()` | `count()`, `collect()`, `take(n)`, `saveAsTextFile()` |
| **Optimization** | Subject to DAG pipelining & fusion | Forces scheduler to materialize the result |

> **Examples:**
> - **Transformations:** `map(lambda x: x*2)`, `filter(lambda x: x>10)`.
> - **Actions:** `rdd.count()`, `rdd.collect()`.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** 
> When answering 14-mark questions, students often write only the **definition** and skip the **diagram**, **code/algorithm**, and **example**. Make sure to include:
> 1. A clear **definition** (2–3 marks).
> 2. A **diagram** — lineage DAG, architecture, or block diagram (3–4 marks).
> 3. A **pseudocode / Scala-Python code** (3–4 marks).
> 4. **Sample output** or worked example (2–3 marks).
> 5. A **conclusion** comparing or summarizing (1–2 marks).
> 
> Skipping any of these blocks can cost **3–5 marks** per sub-question.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q(a).** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of Apache Spark**. Discuss the role of the **Driver, Cluster Manager, and Executors** in job execution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Apache Spark runtime follows a **master-worker (driver-executor) architecture**. The main components are:

1. **Driver Program:**
   - Runs the `main()` function and creates a `SparkContext` (or `SparkSession`).
   - Builds the **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)** of RDD operations.
   - Contains the `DAGScheduler` (splits DAG into stages) and `TaskScheduler` (assigns tasks).
   - Coordinates with the **Cluster Manager** to request resources.

2. **Cluster Manager:**
   - Allocates resources across the cluster.
   - Examples: Spark **Standalone**, **YARN**, **Mesos**, **Kubernetes**.
   - Decides **how many executors** to launch and on **which worker nodes**.

3. **Executors:**
   - JVM processes that run on **worker nodes**.
   - Hold **task slots** (typically one per CPU core).
   - Cache RDD partitions in memory.
   - Report heartbeats and shuffle file locations back to the driver.

```
[Reference: SECTION 4.1 Mermaid Diagram]
```

**Execution flow:**
1. Driver builds DAG and submits it to DAGScheduler.
2. DAGScheduler creates stages split on **shuffle boundaries**.
3. TaskScheduler launches tasks on executor slots.
4. Executors run tasks, write shuffle data to local disk, return results.
5. Driver collects results (or writes to HDFS).

**Conclusion:** This master-worker design decouples **control plane** (driver) from **data plane** (executors), enabling **horizontal scalability** — adding more worker nodes linearly increases throughput.

---

**Q(b).** Write a **PySpark program** to perform **word count** on a text file. Explain the **lineage DAG** and identify the **shuffle boundary**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```python
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("WordCount").getOrCreate()
sc = spark.sparkContext

# Load file (LAZY)
lines = sc.textFile("input.txt")
# Split + normalize (NARROW)
words = lines.flatMap(lambda l: l.split(" ")).filter(lambda w: w != "")
# Map to pairs (NARROW)
pairs = words.map(lambda w: (w.lower().strip(".,!?"), 1))
# Shuffle + Reduce (WIDE — boundary here)
counts = pairs.reduceByKey(lambda a, b: a + b)
# Action
output = counts.collect()
for w, c in output:
    print(w, c)
```

**Lineage DAG (diagram):**
```
textFile → lines (RDD1)
RDD1 → flatMap → words (RDD2)
RDD2 → filter → clean (RDD3)
RDD3 → map → pairs (RDD4)
RDD4 → reduceByKey [WIDE] → counts (RDD5)    ← Shuffle boundary
RDD5 → collect (Action)
```

**Stage Analysis:**
- **Stage 0:** RDD1 → RDD4 (all narrow, fused into one task group).
- **Stage 1:** RDD5 (post-shuffle, one task per key's reducer).

**Shuffle boundary:** The `reduceByKey` operation is the **only wide dependency** in the chain; therefore, **2 stages** are created by `DAGScheduler`.

**Conclusion:** This 8-line PySpark program replaces a 50+ line Hadoop MapReduce Java program and runs in-memory, achieving 10–100× speed-up on iterative or multi-stage pipelines.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q(a).** Explain **Lazy Evaluation** in Spark. Why is it called "lazy"? How does it improve performance? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lazy Evaluation** is Spark's strategy where **transformations are not executed** when called; instead, the chain of operations is **recorded as a logical DAG**. Execution is **postponed** until an **action** is invoked, at which point the DAG is **optimized and executed** as a single job.

**Why is it called "lazy"?**
- Transformations (e.g., `map`, `filter`, `reduceByKey`) do *nothing* but **update the RDD's lineage metadata**.
- The RDD is a *description* of what to compute, not a *materialized* result.
- It "lazily" defers work until forced by an action (e.g., `count`, `collect`).

**How it improves performance:**

1. **Optimization (Pipelining):** Spark's `DAGScheduler` can **fuse** multiple narrow transformations into a **single stage**, avoiding intermediate writes to disk.
   - Example: `rdd.map(f).filter(p).map(g)` → **one stage**, not three.

2. **Reduced Computation:** Only the data path required for the final action is computed. Branches not needed are pruned away.

3. **Fault Tolerance via Lineage:** The DAG itself serves as the **recipe card** for re-computation. If a partition is lost, Spark replays the lineage from the last persisted ancestor — no need to replicate the entire RDD.

4. **Batching of Shuffles:** Multiple wide dependencies are sequenced efficiently, with one shuffle per stage boundary.

**Example:**
```python
# Lazy
big = sc.textFile("huge.txt")           # Not executed
mapped = big.map(lambda x: x.upper())   # Not executed
filtered = mapped.filter(lambda x: "ERROR" in x)  # Not executed
# Only this triggers the full pipeline:
filtered.count()  # ← EXECUTION HAPPENS HERE
```

**Conclusion:** Lazy evaluation is the **cornerstone of Spark's performance**. By deferring execution, Spark turns a series of seemingly-naive operations into an **optimized, pipelined, in-memory DAG execution plan**, achieving performance levels that are *impossible* in eager-evaluation frameworks.

---

**Q(b).** Compare `reduceByKey` and `groupByKey` with respect to **shuffle cost, memory usage, and use cases**. Show mathematically why `reduceByKey` is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `reduceByKey(f)` | `groupByKey()` |
|---|---|---|
| **Map-side combine** | ✅ Yes (pre-aggregate per partition) | ❌ No |
| **Shuffle data volume** | Small (one tuple per `(key, partition)` pair) | Large (all `(key, value)` pairs) |
| **Memory at reducers** | One (key, partial-aggregate) per key | All values per key as an iterable |
| **Use when** | Operation is **associative + commutative** (sum, count, max) | Operation requires **all raw values** (e.g., sorting, collecting distinct) |
| **Risk** | Safe | **OutOfMemoryError** for skewed keys |

**Mathematical Derivation:**

For $N$ total key-value pairs spread over $P$ partitions, with $K_P$ **distinct keys per partition** and $s_v$ average value size:

- **Shuffle cost of `groupByKey`:**
$$B_{\text{groupByKey}} = N \cdot s_v$$

- **Shuffle cost of `reduceByKey`:**
$$B_{\text{reduceByKey}} = K_P \cdot s_v$$

- **Efficiency Ratio:**
$$\eta = \frac{B_{\text{groupByKey}}}{B_{\text{reduceByKey}}} = \frac{N}{K_P}$$

**Numerical example (Shakespeare word count):**
- $N \approx 980{,}000$ token pairs.
- $K_P \approx 8{,}000$ distinct words per partition.
- $\eta = \dfrac{980{,}000}{8{,}000} \approx 122.5$.

→ `reduceByKey` transfers **~122× less data** across the network.

**Example code:**
```python
# Preferred (efficient)
word_counts = pairs.reduceByKey(lambda a, b: a + b)

# Avoid if possible (expensive)
word_counts = pairs.groupByKey().mapValues(sum)
```

**Conclusion:** Always default to `reduceByKey` (or its cousins `aggregateByKey`, `foldByKey`, `combineByKey`) for aggregation. Use `groupByKey` *only* when the **full iterable of values is semantically required** (e.g., computing a set, sorting by value, joining with all values).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module Experiment 2 (Spark in Action)**

- 🔹 **Spark** is a **unified, in-memory, distributed analytics engine** built on the **DAG execution model**.
- 🔹 **RDD** = Resilient (lineage-based fault tolerance) + Distributed (partitioned across nodes) + Dataset (immutable collection of records).
- 🔹 Five RDD properties: **partitions, dependencies, compute function, partitioner (optional), preferred locations (optional)**.
- 🔹 **Transformations** are **lazy**; **Actions** are **eager** (they trigger the DAG).
- 🔹 **Narrow dependencies** = no shuffle (e.g., `map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, `union`, `coalesce`).
- 🔹 **Wide dependencies** = shuffle required (e.g., `groupByKey`, `reduceByKey`, `join`, `repartition`, `distinct`).
- 🔹 **Number of stages** = **number of wide-dependency boundaries + 1**.
- 🔹 **Lazy evaluation** enables **pipelining, pruning, and optimization**.
- 🔹 **Caching** (`persist()` / `cache()`) avoids re-computation for **iterative** workloads (ML algorithms, K-Means, PageRank).
- 🔹 **Storage levels** range from `MEMORY_ONLY` (fastest) to `DISK_ONLY` (safest, slowest).
- 🔹 **`reduceByKey` >> `groupByKey`** for associative/commutative aggregations (saves network + memory).
- 🔹 **SparkContext** (legacy) vs. **SparkSession** (modern, unified entry point since Spark 2.0).
- 🔹 **Cluster managers** supported: Standalone, YARN, Mesos, Kubernetes.
- 🔹 **Driver** = brain (DAG, scheduling); **Executors** = muscle (task execution, RDD caching).
- 🔹 **Data locality levels** (best to worst): `PROCESS_LOCAL` → `NODE_LOCAL` → `RACK_LOCAL` → `ANY`.
- 🔹 **Tungsten** is Spark's internal C++-style execution engine for memory and CPU efficiency (since Spark 1.5).
- 🔹 **Catalyst Optimizer** optimizes DataFrame/SQL queries (logical + physical plan).
- 🔹 For Experiment 2, students must be fluent in: `spark-shell`, `pyspark`, `spark-submit`, RDD creation, `map/filter/flatMap/reduceByKey/count/collect/saveAsTextFile`, and DAG inspection via `rdd.toDebugString` or the **Spark UI** (port 4040).
- 🔹 Common Spark **error to recognize in exam**: `org.apache.spark.SparkException: Task not serializable` → typically a **non-serializable closure** in a `map` / `filter` / `foreach`.
- 🔹 Spark does **not** require Hadoop, but it can run on HDFS, S3, Azure Data Lake, GCS, or local FS.
- 🔹 **In Spark 3.x+**, the **Adaptive Query Execution (AQE)** feature dynamically coalesces post-shuffle partitions at runtime, further reducing the "small file problem".

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Spark in Action**

# 📘 MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — Mastering Apache Spark (Frampton, Packt, 1/e, 2020)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apache Spark** is a **distributed, in-memory, fault-tolerant cluster computing framework** designed for **fast, large-scale data processing**. It extends the **MapReduce model** with an in-memory execution engine that supports **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** scheduling, **lazy evaluation**, and **lineage-based fault recovery** through the abstraction of **Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDDs)**.

According to the reference text — *Mike Frampton, "Mastering Apache Spark", Packt Publishing, 1st Edition, 2020* — Spark provides **four primary unified libraries**:

| # | Library | Purpose |
|---|---------|---------|
| 1 | **Spark Core** | Task scheduling, memory management, fault recovery, RDD API |
| 2 | **Spark SQL** | Structured data processing via DataFrames and Datasets |
| 3 | **Spark Streaming** | Live, micro-batched stream processing (DStreams / Structured Streaming) |
| 4 | **MLlib** | Scalable machine learning algorithms |
| 5 | **GraphX** | Graph-parallel computation (graph analytics) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Course Outcome Mapping**
> This topic satisfies **CO2**: *Apply Spark programming model to solve large-scale data analytics problems using RDDs, DataFrames, and Datasets.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"Spark is like a massive kitchen with hundreds of chefs" 🍳👨‍🍳**
>
> - Imagine a giant restaurant (the **Cluster**) with hundreds of chefs (the **Executors / Workers**). 
> - The head chef (the **Driver Program**) prepares the **recipe** (the **DAG of stages and tasks**) but does **not** cook — he only *plans*.
> - Each chef gets a small **cutting board** (a **partition** of data) and works **independently in memory (RAM)**.
> - When a chef finishes, results are **merged** at the serving counter (**Shuffle / Reduce**).
> - If a chef burns the dish (**task failure**), Spark doesn't redo everything — it just **recomputes the lost portion** using the **recipe lineage** (RDD lineage graph). 
> - Unlike traditional **MapReduce** (which writes intermediate data to **disk** between every job), Spark keeps data **in memory** → **up to 100× faster** for iterative algorithms (ML, graph).

### 1.3 Key Architectural Terms (Bolded for Recall)

- **Driver Program**: The process running the `main()` function of the Spark application and creating the `SparkContext`.
- **Cluster Manager**: External service for resource allocation — *Standalone*, *YARN*, *Mesos*, *Kubernetes*.
- **Executor**: Worker process that runs **tasks** and keeps data **in memory or disk** across the application's lifetime.
- **Task**: A unit of work sent to **one executor**, operating on **one partition** of data.
- **Job**: A parallel computation consisting of multiple **stages**, triggered by an **action**.
- **Stage**: A set of **tasks** that can be executed in parallel without shuffling data.
- **RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset)**: Immutable, distributed collection of objects, **lineage-tracked**, **partitioned**, and operable via **transformations** and **actions**.

# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Spark's Five-Pillar Architecture (Frampton, Ch. 1–3)

### Pillar 1 — The Spark Driver & SparkContext

The **SparkContext** (`sc`) is the **entry point** of any Spark application. It coordinates the application with the **Cluster Manager** and creates **RDDs**, **accumulators**, and **broadcast variables**.

$$\text{SparkContext} \rightarrow \text{Cluster Manager} \rightarrow \text{Executors (Workers)}$$

### Pillar 2 — RDD: The Heart of Spark

RDDs have **five main properties** (per Frampton, Ch. 2, p. 36):

1. **Partitions** — atomic chunks of dataset.
2. **Dependencies** — lineage to parent RDDs (narrow vs wide).
3. **Compute function** — `compute(partition: Partition): Iterator[T]`.
4. **Partitioner** (optional) — `HashPartitioner`, `RangePartitioner`.
5. **Preferred Locations** (optional) — HDFS block locations for **data locality**.

### Pillar 3 — Lazy Evaluation & Lineage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Spark uses **lazy evaluation**: **transformations** are *not executed* until an **action** is triggered. This allows the engine to **optimize the entire DAG** (Catalyst optimizer for Spark SQL).

**Transformations** (lazy): `map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, `union`, `groupByKey`, `reduceByKey`, `join`, `distinct` 
**Actions** (eager, trigger jobs): `collect`, `count`, `first`, `take(n)`, `reduce`, `saveAsTextFile`, `foreach`

### Pillar 4 — Narrow vs Wide Dependencies

| Type | Dependency | Shuffle? | Example |
|------|------------|----------|---------|
| **Narrow** | Each parent partition used by **≤ 1 child partition** | ❌ No | `map`, `filter`, `union` |
| **Wide** | Each parent partition used by **≥ 1 child partition** | ✅ Yes | `groupByKey`, `reduceByKey`, `join` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Wide dependencies trigger SHUFFLES** — the **most expensive operation** in Spark. Always prefer **`reduceByKey` over `groupByKey`** because it performs **map-side (combiner) aggregation** first.

### Pillar 5 — Fault Tolerance via Lineage

If a partition is lost, Spark **recomputes it from the original data + transformation chain**:

$$RDD_{\text{recomputed}} = f_n(f_{n-1}(\dots f_1(\text{source data})\dots))$$

This is **faster than replicating data** (as Hadoop HDFS does) for **lineage chains of reasonable depth**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Rule | Unit | Notes |
|----|-----------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------|----------------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Parallelism** | $P = \text{sc.defaultParallelism}$ or $\sum \text{executor cores}$ | cores | Default = 2 in local mode |
| 2 | **Number of partitions (recommended)** | $N_p = 2 \text{ to } 3 \times N_{\text{cores}}$ | partitions | For CPU-bound tasks |
| 3 | **Block size for HDFS** | Default = **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x) | MB | Affects initial RDD partitions |
| 4 | **Task execution time model** | $T_{\text{task}} = T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{shuffle}} + T_{\text{GC}}$ | seconds | Optimize GC for memory-bound jobs |
| 5 | **RDD partition size** | $\text{Size}_{partition} = \dfrac{\text{Total Data Size}}{N_p}$ | bytes | Aim: **128 MB – 200 MB** per partition |
| 6 | **Speedup (Amdahl's Law)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ | ratio | $f$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = workers |
| 7 | **DataFrame cache size** | $\vert \text{cache} \vert \le \text{spark.memory.fraction} \times \text{Heap}$ | bytes | Default `spark.memory.fraction` = **0.6** |
| 8 | **Shuffle spill threshold** | $S_{\text{spill}} = \text{spark.shuffle.file.buffer} \times N_{\text{partitions}}$ | bytes | Increase to reduce spills |
| 9 | **Spark vs MapReduce speedup** | $\text{Speedup} \approx 10\times \text{ to } 100\times$ | factor | For iterative ML workloads (Frampton, Ch. 1) |
| 10 | **Lineage recomputation cost** | $C_{\text{recomp}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C(f_i)$ | time units | $n$ = depth of lineage DAG |

> [!NOTE]
> In markdown table, the absolute value notation uses `\vert` (not `\|`) to prevent markdown table breaking.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Iterative ML**: Logistic regression, K-means, ALS — each iteration = a **job**, but data stays in memory.
- **ETL Pipelines**: Spark SQL replaces legacy **Informatica / Talend** workflows.
- **Stream Analytics**: Kafka → Spark Streaming → dashboard (e.g., Uber, Netflix).
- **Genomics & Bioinformatics**: Genome sequence alignment with **ADAM** (built on Spark).
- **Graph Analytics**: Social network analysis using **GraphX** (PageRank, Connected Components).

# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Number of RDD Partitions

**Problem Statement:** Given a dataset of size $D$ bytes and $N_c$ available CPU cores, derive the optimal number of partitions $P^*$ to maximize parallelism without excessive task overhead.

### Step 1 — Define the Total Execution Time Model

Each partition becomes one task. Total wall-clock time of a job (ignoring shuffle) is:

$$T_{total} = T_{task} + T_{overhead} \times P$$

Where:
- $T_{task}$ = time to process one partition ≈ $\dfrac{D / P}{B}$ (data size per task / bandwidth $B$).
- $T_{overhead}$ = fixed cost per task (JVM startup, serialization, scheduler).

### Step 2 — Substitute into the Model

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total}(P) &= \frac{D}{P \cdot B} + T_{overhead} \cdot P
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate and Set to Zero (Minimize)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dT_{total}}{dP} &= -\frac{D}{B P^2} + T_{overhead} = 0 \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow P^2 &= \frac{D}{B \cdot T_{overhead}} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow P^{*} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{B \cdot T_{overhead}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Practical Engineering Approximation (Frampton, Ch. 7)

Since $T_{overhead}$ is small and hard to measure, the **industry rule of thumb** is:

$$
P_{\text{recommended}} = 2 \times N_c \quad \text{to} \quad 3 \times N_c
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion logic:** The analytical optimum is rarely computable in practice because $T_{overhead}$ varies by JVM and cluster. Hence Spark's documentation and Frampton recommend the **2×–3× core count heuristic**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Amdahl's Law Applied to Spark Parallelism

**Problem:** A Spark job has parallel fraction $f = 0.95$ and is run on $N = 8$ worker nodes. What is the maximum achievable speedup?

### Step 1 — State Amdahl's Law

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(8) &= \frac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \dfrac{0.95}{8}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.11875} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{0.16875} \\[4pt]
&\approx 5.93
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

Even with **8 nodes**, the speedup is capped at **~5.93×** because the **5% serial portion** (driver coordination, final result aggregation) bounds the maximum speedup.

### Step 4 — Compute Theoretical Max (as $N \to \infty$)

$$
S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}} = \frac{1}{1-f} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** No matter how many nodes you add, the **serial fraction** limits the maximum theoretical speedup. Always minimize the **driver-side** computation in Spark applications.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Spark Transformation Cost Comparison

| Transformation | Type | Shuffle? | Complexity | Memory Cost |
|----------------|------|----------|------------|-------------|
| `map(f)` | Narrow | ❌ | $O(N)$ | Low (one record at a time) |
| `filter(f)` | Narrow | ❌ | $O(N)$ | Low |
| `flatMap(f)` | Narrow | ❌ | $O(N)$ | Low |
| `union(rdd)` | Narrow | ❌ | $O(N + M)$ | Low |
| `groupByKey()` | **Wide** | ✅ | $O(N \log P)$ shuffle | **HIGH** (no combiner) |
| `reduceByKey(f)` | **Wide** | ✅ (with map-side combine) | $O(N \log P)$ | Moderate |
| `aggregateByKey(zero, seq, comb)` | **Wide** | ✅ (with combiner) | $O(N \log P)$ | Moderate |
| `join(rdd)` (with co-partitioned) | Narrow | ❌ | $O(N + M)$ | Moderate |
| `join(rdd)` (non co-partitioned) | **Wide** | ✅ | $O(N + M + \text{shuffle})$ | **HIGH** |
| `sortByKey()` | **Wide** | ✅ (range partitioning) | $O(N \log N)$ | **HIGH** |
| `coalesce(n)` | Narrow | ❌ (avoids shuffle) | $O(N)$ | Low |
| `repartition(n)` | **Wide** | ✅ (uniform) | $O(N \log P)$ | Moderate |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** `coalesce(n)` and `repartition(n)` are **not the same**. `coalesce` reduces partitions *without* shuffle (only merges). `repartition` triggers a **full shuffle** to evenly distribute data. Use `coalesce` to *decrease* partitions efficiently; use `repartition` to *increase* or *balance*.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Shuffle Write Amplification

**Problem:** A `reduceByKey` operation processes $N$ key-value pairs distributed across $P$ partitions. Estimate the shuffle write volume $V_{\text{shuffle}}$.

### Step 1 — Define Variables

- $S_{\text{avg}}$ = average serialized size of one KV pair (bytes).
- $N$ = total number of KV pairs.
- $C$ = combiner reduction ratio (e.g., $C = 100$ means 100 records per key are combined into 1 local record).

### Step 2 — Volume Without Combiner (`groupByKey`)

$$
V_{\text{no-combine}} = N \times S_{avg} + \text{metadata overhead}
$$

### Step 3 — Volume With Combiner (`reduceByKey`)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{combine}} &= \frac{N}{C} \times S_{avg} + \text{metadata overhead} \\[4pt]
\text{Savings} &= \frac{V_{\text{no-combine}} - V_{\text{combine}}}{V_{\text{no-combine}}} = 1 - \frac{1}{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example

For $C = 100$ (typical for word-count-like jobs):

$$
\text{Savings} = 1 - \frac{1}{100} = 0.99 = 99\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion logic:** A 100× combiner reduction means 99% reduction in shuffle volume. This is why `reduceByKey` is **strongly preferred** over `groupByKey` in production Spark jobs (Frampton, Ch. 4, "Key-Value Pair Operations").

# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Spark Cluster Architecture (Frampton, Ch. 1)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    driver[Driver Program and SparkContext] --> cm[Cluster Manager]
    cm --> e1[Executor 1 on Worker 1]
    cm --> e2[Executor 2 on Worker 2]
    cm --> e3[Executor 3 on Worker 3]
    e1 --> c1a[Core 1 Task A]
    e1 --> c1b[Core 2 Task B]
    e2 --> c2a[Core 3 Task C]
    e2 --> c2b[Core 4 Task D]
    e3 --> c3a[Core 5 Task E]
    e3 --> c3b[Core 6 Task F]
    driver --> dag[DAG Scheduler]
    driver --> ts[Task Scheduler]
```

> **Read this as:** The Driver receives your code, builds a DAG, asks the Cluster Manager for resources, and the Cluster Manager launches Executors on Worker nodes. Each Executor's cores process Tasks in parallel.

---

## 4.2 RDD Lineage DAG (Lazy Evaluation Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input HDFS File] -->|textFile| B[RDD1 lines]
    B -->|flatMap split| C[RDD2 words]
    C -->|filter lowercase| D[RDD3 valid words]
    D -->|map pair| E[RDD4 word pairs]
    E -->|reduceByKey sum| F[RDD5 word counts]
    F -.->|collect action triggers execution| G[Driver Result]
```

> **Read this as:** All blue boxes are **transformations** (lazy). The **dashed arrow** represents the **action** (`collect`) that finally materializes the computation. The full chain is the **lineage** — Spark uses it for **fault recovery** if any partition is lost.

---

## 4.3 Stage & Task Decomposition (Wide Dependency = Stage Boundary)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stage0["Stage 0 - No Shuffle"]
        S0T1[Task partition 1]
        S0T2[Task partition 2]
        S0T3[Task partition 3]
    end
    Stage0 -->|shuffle write| ShuffleBarrier((Shuffle Barrier))
    ShuffleBarrier --> Stage1
    subgraph Stage1["Stage 1 - After GroupByKey"]
        S1T1[Task reduce 1]
        S1T2[Task reduce 2]
        S1T3[Task reduce 3]
    end
```

> **Read this as:** Spark splits the DAG into **stages** at every **wide dependency boundary**. Each stage contains **tasks that can run in parallel without shuffling**. The shuffle barrier between Stage 0 and Stage 1 represents the expensive network I/O.

---

## 4.4 Spark Library Stack (Frampton, Ch. 1, Figure 1.2)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AppLayer["Application Layer"]
        SPARKML[MLlib Machine Learning]
        SPARKSTR[Spark Streaming]
        SPARKGRAPH[GraphX]
        SPARKSQL[Spark SQL]
    end
    subgraph CoreLayer["Core Execution Engine"]
        SPARKCORE[Spark Core RDD API and DAG Scheduler]
    end
    subgraph ResLayer["Resource Manager Layer"]
        YARN[YARN]
        MESOS[Mesos]
        K8S[Kubernetes]
        STAND[Standalone]
    end
    SPARKML --> SPARKCORE
    SPARKSTR --> SPARKCORE
    SPARKGRAPH --> SPARKCORE
    SPARKSQL --> SPARKCORE
    SPARKCORE --> YARN
    SPARKCORE --> MESOS
    SPARKCORE --> K8S
    SPARKCORE --> STAND
```

> **Read this as:** All four high-level libraries sit **on top of** Spark Core. Spark Core abstracts away the underlying **Cluster Manager** (YARN, Mesos, K8s, or Standalone). This unified stack is Spark's biggest advantage over Hadoop's fragmented ecosystem.

---

## 4.5 DataFrame vs RDD Execution Pipeline (Catalyst Optimizer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User DataFrame Code] --> B[Unresolved Logical Plan]
    B --> C[Catalyst Analyzer]
    C --> D[Optimized Logical Plan]
    D --> E[Physical Plan with Cost Model]
    E --> F[RDD DAG and Tasks]
    F --> G[Executors Run in Parallel]
```

> **Read this as:** When you use **DataFrames/Datasets** (Spark SQL), your code goes through the **Catalyst optimizer**, which applies **rule-based** and **cost-based** optimizations (predicate pushdown, constant folding, join reordering) **before** generating the final RDD DAG. With raw RDDs, **no such optimization** is applied — your code runs as-is.

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### **Q1. Define Resilient Distributed Dataset (RDD). List its five main properties.**

**Model Answer:**

An RDD is the **fundamental data structure of Apache Spark** — an immutable, partitioned collection of records that can be operated in parallel with **automatic fault tolerance via lineage tracking** (Frampton, Ch. 2).

The **five main properties** are:

1. **Partitions** — the dataset is divided into atomic chunks.
2. **Dependencies** — lineage links to parent RDDs (narrow or wide).
3. **Compute function** — `compute(Partition): Iterator[T]`.
4. **Partitioner** (optional) — e.g., `HashPartitioner`.
5. **Preferred Locations** (optional) — HDFS block locations for **data locality**.

**Model Answer:**

| **Optimization** | Allows DAG optimization | Forces DAG materialization |

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Spark Cluster Architecture** with a neat diagram. Describe the role of **Driver, Cluster Manager, Executor, and Task**.

**b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain the concept of **Lazy Evaluation** and **Lineage** in Spark. How does it enable **fault tolerance**?

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Spark Cluster Architecture** consists of the following components (Frampton, Ch. 1):

1. **Driver Program**: Runs the `main()` function. Contains the `SparkContext` which is the entry point. It builds the **DAG** of computation and submits it to the **Cluster Manager**.

2. **Cluster Manager**: Allocates cluster resources. Supported managers include **Standalone**, **YARN**, **Mesos**, and **Kubernetes**. It launches **Executors** on **Worker nodes**.

3. **Executors**: Worker processes launched on each node. They run **Tasks** in parallel, store data **in memory or on local disk**, and report results back to the Driver.

4. **Tasks**: The **smallest unit of work** — one task operates on **one partition** of an RDD using **one core** of an executor.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1):**

```
Driver Program → SparkContext → Cluster Manager → Executors → Tasks
```

**Working Flow:**

$$\text{Driver} \xrightarrow{\text{creates DAG}} \text{Click DAGScheduler} \xrightarrow{\text{requests resources}} \text{Cluster Manager} \xrightarrow{\text{launches}} \text{Executors} \xrightarrow{\text{run}} \text{Tasks}$$

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Lazy Evaluation** means Spark does **not execute transformations immediately** when they are called. Instead, it **records the transformations as a DAG of dependencies**. Execution happens only when an **action** is invoked (Frampton, Ch. 2, "RDD Operations").

**Example:**

```scala
val lines = sc.textFile("hdfs:///data.txt")      // TRANSFORMATION (lazy)
val words = lines.flatMap(_.split(" "))           // TRANSFORMATION (lazy)
val pairs = words.map(w => (w, 1))                // TRANSFORMATION (lazy)
val counts = pairs.reduceByKey(_ + _)             // TRANSFORMATION (lazy)
counts.saveAsTextFile("hdfs:///output")           // ACTION (triggers execution)
```

**Lineage:** Spark maintains a **graph of parent-child RDD relationships** — this graph is the **lineage**.

$$RDD_{counts} \rightarrow RDD_{pairs} \rightarrow RDD_{words} \rightarrow RDD_{lines} \rightarrow \text{HDFS}$$

**Fault Tolerance via Lineage:**

If any **partition** of an intermediate RDD is **lost** (e.g., executor crash), Spark **recomputes only that partition** by walking backward through the lineage graph, **without needing to replicate data** (unlike HDFS). This is **faster and storage-efficient** for **lineage chains of reasonable depth**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. ⚠️ **Don't confuse `persist()` with `cache()`** — `cache()` is shorthand for `persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_ONLY)`. Forgetting this loses a mark.
> 2. ⚠️ **Lineage ≠ Data replication**. State clearly: "Lineage enables recomputation, not duplicate storage."
> 3. ⚠️ **Wide vs Narrow dependencies** — if the student does not classify `reduceByKey` as a **wide dependency**, the answer is incomplete.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**a) [7 Marks]** Compare **RDDs and DataFrames** in Spark. When would you prefer one over the other?

**b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Narrow and Wide Dependencies** with examples. Why is `reduceByKey` preferred over `groupByKey`?

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

| Feature | RDD | DataFrame |
|---------|-----|-----------|
| **Data type** | Untyped / typed JVM objects (any type) | Named columns, schema-aware (tabular) |
| **API style** | Functional (lambda operations) | Domain-specific (SQL-like) |
| **Optimization** | **None** (no optimizer) | **Catalyst optimizer** + **Tungsten** execution |
| **Compile-time safety** | ✅ Yes (with Datasets / typed RDDs) | ❌ No (schema checked at runtime) |
| **Use case** | Unstructured data, custom UDFs, low-level control | Structured data, SQL queries, BI/ETL |
| **Performance** | Slower (no optimization) | Faster (10–100× due to Catalyst + Tungsten) |

**When to prefer RDDs:** Unstructured text, image processing, custom iterative algorithms needing low-level control, when **schema is unknown**.

**When to prefer DataFrames:** Structured/semi-structured (Parquet, JSON, CSV), SQL-based analytics, when **performance and optimization** matter.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Narrow Dependency:** Each parent partition contributes to **at most one** child partition. **No shuffle** is needed.

- **Examples:** `map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, `union`, `coalesce`.

**Wide Dependency:** Each parent partition contributes to **multiple** child partitions. **Shuffle** (network I/O) is required.

- **Examples:** `groupByKey`, `reduceByKey`, `sortByKey`, `join` (non co-partitioned).

**Why `reduceByKey` is preferred over `groupByKey` (Frampton, Ch. 4):**

1. **Map-side Combiner:** `reduceByKey` first applies the reducer **locally on each partition** before the shuffle. This **drastically reduces shuffle volume**.
2. **Network Efficiency:** `groupByKey` shuffles **all key-value pairs** to reducers, causing OOM errors and high network I/O.
3. **Mathematical Evidence (from Section 3.4):** With combiner ratio $C = 100$, shuffle volume reduces by **99%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. ⚠️ Students often write "`reduceByKey` is faster" **without explaining WHY** — always mention **map-side combiner** and **shuffle volume reduction**.
> 2. ⚠️ Don't say "DataFrame is always better than RDD" — state the **trade-off** (untyped safety vs optimization).
> 3. ⚠️ **Spark SQL ≠ Spark SQL on RDDs** — DataFrames go through **Catalyst optimizer**, raw RDDs do not.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-Second Revision**

- ✅ **Spark = in-memory distributed computing** with **DAG scheduler** and **RDD abstraction** (Frampton, 2020).
- ✅ **5 RDD properties**: Partitions, Dependencies, Compute, Partitioner, Preferred Locations.
- ✅ **Lazy evaluation**: Transformations build DAG; **actions trigger** execution.
- ✅ **Lineage** enables **fault tolerance** by recomputing lost partitions.
- ✅ **Narrow dependency** → no shuffle; **Wide dependency** → shuffle (expensive).
- ✅ **Always prefer `reduceByKey` over `groupByKey`** (combiner reduces shuffle by ~99%).
- ✅ **2×–3× cores = recommended partition count** (Frampton, Ch. 7).
- ✅ **DataFrames use Catalyst optimizer** → much faster than raw RDDs for structured data.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law** caps speedup: $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$ — minimize serial driver code.
- ✅ **Cluster Manager** options: Standalone, YARN, Mesos, Kubernetes.
- ✅ **Coalesce** = decrease partitions **without** shuffle; **Repartition** = full shuffle, equal balance.
- ✅ **Cache vs Persist**: `cache()` = `persist(MEMORY_ONLY)`; `persist()` allows MEMORY_AND_DISK, DISK_ONLY, etc.
- ✅ **Spark vs Hadoop MapReduce**: **100× faster** for iterative ML due to in-memory execution.
- ✅ **Shuffle write amplification** = 99% reduction possible with combiners (numerical derivation done above).
- ✅ **Five Spark libraries**: Core, SQL, Streaming, MLlib, GraphX — all unified under one engine.

---

> 🎯 **KTU 2024 Exam Strategy Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always combine a **diagram (4 marks)** + **definition (3 marks)** + **working steps (4 marks)** + **real-world example (3 marks)**. Examiners reward **complete, well-structured answers** with diagrams.
