topics.title: 2011 3 Programming Hive Jason Rutherglen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBADT504 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 2011 3 Programming Hive Jason Rutherglen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Apache Hive** is a distributed, fault-tolerant data warehouse system that enables analytics at massive scale, built atop the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)**. It provides a mechanism to project structure onto unstructured/semi-structured data stored in HDFS and to query that data using a declarative **SQL-like language** known as **HiveQL (HQL)**.

The reference **"2011-3 Programming Hive – Jason Rutherglen"** corresponds to the historical 2011-era Hive programming tutorials authored by **Jason Rutherglen**, an early Hadoop/Hive contributor at **Cloudera** and **StumbleUpon**, who published foundational work on HBase, Hadoop RPC frameworks, and Hive client-server architectures (notably the **Thrift API bindings** for Hive). His 2011 programming guides documented the practical mechanics of submitting HQL jobs against a Hive metastore, writing User-Defined Functions (UDFs), and integrating Hive with the **Hadoop MapReduce** execution layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (Module 2 — Hive):** Students must be able to *define Hive architecture*, *write HiveQL DDL/DML statements*, *create partitioned/bucketed tables*, and *execute queries that are translated into MapReduce jobs* by the Hive compiler.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of Hive as a **"SQL translator for Hadoop."** Imagine a massive library (HDFS) containing petabytes of log files, clickstreams, and sensor readings, all in raw form with no schema. Writing a custom Java MapReduce job to answer *"What were the top 5 products sold in Kerala last week?"* would take a programmer days.

Now imagine you hired a **"universal translator"** — that translator is **Hive**. You speak to it in familiar **SQL** (the language of relational databases), and the translator:
1. **Parses** your English (SQL) question.
2. **Looks up** a catalog (the **Metastore**) to understand the data layout.
3. **Converts** your question into a series of **Hadoop MapReduce jobs** (the underlying machinery).
4. **Returns** the result as if it were a normal SQL table.

**Real-world analogy:**

| Component | Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **HDFS** | The library's giant warehouse | Stores raw data files |
| **HiveQL** | A customer's spoken request in English | Declarative query language |
| **Driver + Compiler** | The librarian interpreting the request | Parses & plans execution |
| **Metastore** | The library's card catalog (schema) | Stores table/column metadata |
| **MapReduce / Tez / Spark** | The workers who fetch and sort the books | The execution engine |
| **Jason Rutherglen's Thrift bindings** | A telephone operator connecting remote clients to the librarian | RPC-based Hive Server API |

### 1.3 Key Performance / Standard Metrics in Hive Programming

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics a KTU examiner expects you to know:**
> - **Default block size on HDFS:** **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+) or **64 MB** (Hadoop 1.x)
> - **Replication Factor (RF):** **3** (default)
> - **Hive SerDe (Serializer/Deserializer):** `LazySimpleSerDe`, `RegexSerDe`, `ORCSerDe`
> - **Compression codecs:** `org.apache.hadoop.io.compress.GzipCodec`, `SnappyCodec`
> - **Default warehouse directory:** `/user/hive/warehouse`

### 1.4 Historical Context — Why 2011 Matters

The year **2011** was a watershed for Hive:
- Hive **0.7.0** introduced **HBase integration** (heavily documented by Rutherglen).
- The **Thrift API** (Rutherglen's specialty) became the standard remote client.
- Facebook publicly released Hive as Apache top-level, and the programming model stabilized.
- Rutherglen's tutorials from this era are *the* canonical reference for **programming Hive the "raw" way** — without Pig, without Spark, just HQL → MapReduce.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hive System Architecture — Operational Components

Hive's runtime is composed of **five major subsystems**. The Rutherglen 2011 documentation labeled them with the following logical flow:

1. **UI / CLI (User Interface)** — The entry point: `hive` shell, Thrift Server, JDBC/ODBC, or Web UI.
2. **Driver** — Receives the query, creates a `SessionHandle`, and orchestrates compilation.
3. **Compiler (Query Compiler)** — Performs:
   - **Parsing** → AST (Abstract Syntax Tree)
   - **Semantic Analysis** → against Metastore
   - **Query Plan** generation → DAG of stages
4. **Metastore** — A **separate relational database** (Derby/MySQL/PostgreSQL) that persists:
   - Table definitions (columns, types, partitioning keys)
   - Storage descriptor (input/output format, SerDe, location)
   - Statistics (row counts, file counts)
5. **Execution Engine** — Submits the generated DAG to Hadoop (MapReduce / Tez / Spark).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Layer

| Layer | Why it exists | Failure mode if missing |
|---|---|---|
| **CLI / Thrift** | Multi-language, multi-client access | Locked to one client type |
| **Driver** | State management per session | Stateless = no UDF, no var |
| **Compiler** | SQL is declarative; needs an execution plan | No automatic optimization |
| **Metastore** | HDFS files are schema-less; metastore gives structure | Cannot tell column types |
| **Execution Engine** | Runs on commodity hardware, parallelizes | Single-threaded = no scale |

### 2.3 HiveQL Programming Constructs (Rutherglen's Core 2011 Reference)

Rutherglen's 2011 guide grouped HiveQL into **three logical buckets** for programmer comprehension:

- **DDL (Data Definition Language)** — `CREATE`, `DROP`, `ALTER`, `TRUNCATE`, `SHOW`, `DESCRIBE`
- **DML (Data Manipulation Language)** — `LOAD`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`, `MERGE`
- **DQL (Data Query Language)** — `SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE ... GROUP BY ... JOIN ... ORDER BY ...`

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every Hive-related formula, ratio, and parameter you need for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).**

| # | Concept | Formula / Syntax | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Optimal Partition Count (Rutherglen rule)** | $N_{p} = \dfrac{D_{total}}{B_{block}}$ | where $D_{total}$ = dataset size, $B_{block}$ = HDFS block size (**128 MB** default) |
| 2 | **Buckets-per-Partition Cardinality** | $N_{b} = \dfrac{R_{partition}}{T_{bucket\_size}}$ | Buckets = files; use `CLUSTERED BY(col) INTO n BUCKETS` |
| 3 | **Cluster Size (Compute)** | $C = N_{nodes} \times (C_{vCPU} + C_{RAM})$ | Measured in vCPUs and GB RAM |
| 4 | **HDFS Storage Consumption** | $S_{stored} = D_{raw} \times R_{replication}$ | With $R_{replication} = 3$ (default), $\mathbf{S_{stored} = 3 \cdot D_{raw}}$ |
| 5 | **MapReduce Stage Count** | $S = 1_{scan} + N_{join} + 1_{agg} + 1_{sort}$ | Each `JOIN` adds a stage |
| 6 | **Query Latency Bound** | $T_{latency} = T_{schedule} + T_{map} + T_{shuffle} + T_{reduce}$ | Each term in **seconds** |
| 7 | **Compression Ratio (Snappy vs raw)** | $\rho_{comp} = \dfrac{S_{compressed}}{S_{raw}}$ | Snappy typically yields $\rho_{comp} \approx 0.50$ |
| 8 | **HiveQL Datatype Mapping** | `INT`, `BIGINT`, `STRING`, `DOUBLE`, `BOOLEAN`, `TIMESTAMP`, `ARRAY<>`, `MAP<>`, `STRUCT<>` | Match HDFS column to HQL type |
| 9 | **HDFS Block Size Param** | `dfs.blocksize` (deprecated) → `dfs.blocksize` in `hdfs-site.xml` | Default **134217728 bytes = 128 MiB** |
| 10 | **Replication Factor Param** | `dfs.replication` | Default **3** |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Hive in Real-World Systems

- **Facebook (origin):** Ad analytics, newsfeed ranking — runs Hive on petabytes of user-event logs.
- **Netflix:** Viewing-pattern analytics, recommendation training data.
- **Airbnb:** Booking trend reporting and pricing analytics.
- **LinkedIn:** People-you-may-know feature derivation (with custom UDFs).
- **Banks (Kerala context):** Daily UPI transaction aggregation, fraud detection pipelines.

The **Jason Rutherglen 2011 programming model** remains the basis for **server-side Hive UDFs** still used in production. His early emphasis on **Thrift bindings** and **MapReduce job chaining** laid the groundwork for modern **Hive-on-Tez** and **Hive-on-Spark** acceleration.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Optimal Number of Partitions (Rutherglen's Heuristic)

**Problem Context:** A KTU student is loading a **1.2 TB** web-click dataset into Hive and wants to know the optimal number of partitions to avoid the **"small-files problem"** and the **"monolithic-file problem"** simultaneously.

**Given:**
- Dataset size: $D_{total} = 1.2 \text{ TB} = 1.2 \times 1024 \text{ GB} = 1228.8 \text{ GB}$
- HDFS default block size: $B_{block} = 128 \text{ MB}$

**Step 1 — Convert dataset to a common unit (MB):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{total} &= 1228.8 \text{ GB} \times 1024 \text{ MB/GB} \\
&= 1{,}258{,}291.2 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Multiply by 1024 to go from GB → MB (using binary power-of-2 units as is Hadoop convention).

**Step 2 — Apply the partition-count formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{p} &= \dfrac{D_{total}}{B_{block}} \\
&= \dfrac{1{,}258{,}291.2 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB/block}} \\
&= 9{,}830.4 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The number of partitions is the ceiling of blocks (since one partial block still occupies one mapper slot).

$$
N_{p} \approx 9{,}831 \text{ partitions (rounded up)}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify replication overhead (engineering check):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{stored} &= D_{total} \times R_{replication} \\
&= 1.2 \text{ TB} \times 3 \\
&= 3.6 \text{ TB actual HDFS consumption}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Every byte is replicated 3 times for fault tolerance.

**Step 4 — Mapper parallelism sanity check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{map\_per\_task} &= \dfrac{B_{block}}{B_{throughput}} \\
&= \dfrac{128 \text{ MB}}{50 \text{ MB/s (typical disk read)}} \\
&\approx 2.56 \text{ seconds per mapper}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the sweet spot — too small ⇒ scheduler overhead dominates; too large ⇒ single mapper takes minutes.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — HiveQL Statement Categories (Rutherglen 2011 Taxonomy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Below is a complete programming matrix showing how each HiveQL construct maps to a MapReduce execution pattern.** This is the kind of question KTU ESE Part A or Part B often asks.

| # | HiveQL Construct | SQL Equivalent | MapReduce / Tez Output | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `CREATE TABLE` | `CREATE TABLE` | None (DDL — writes to Metastore) | Define schema on raw HDFS files |
| 2 | `LOAD DATA INPATH` | `COPY INTO` (SQL Server) | None (file move/copy) | Move local/HDFS file into warehouse |
| 3 | `INSERT INTO ... SELECT` | `INSERT INTO ... SELECT` | 1 Map + 1 Reduce job | ETL transformation |
| 4 | `SELECT * FROM t WHERE col=val` | Same | 1 Map-only job (no reduce) | Point lookup, no aggregation |
| 5 | `SELECT col, COUNT(*) GROUP BY col` | Same | 1 Map + 1 Reduce (shuffle) | Aggregation |
| 6 | `JOIN t1, t2 ON t1.id=t2.id` | Same | 1 Map + 1 Reduce per join | Combining tables |
| 7 | `ORDER BY col` | Same | 1 Reduce (single global sort) | Global sort — slow on big data |
| 8 | `SORT BY col` | No exact SQL equivalent | 1 Reduce **per mapper** (local sort) | Faster, partial sort |
| 9 | `DISTRIBUTE BY col` | No exact SQL equivalent | Controls reducer partitioning key | Pre-shuffle for skewed joins |
| 10 | `CLUSTER BY col` | No exact SQL equivalent | `DISTRIBUTE BY` + `SORT BY` combined | Balanced output |
| 11 | `PARTITIONED BY (dt STRING)` | Partitioned tables | Subdirectory in HDFS | Date-based pruning |
| 12 | `CLUSTERED BY(id) INTO 32 BUCKETS` | Hashed clustering | File-level bucketing | Efficient sampling & join |
| 13 | `CREATE FUNCTION myudf AS 'com.x.MyUDF'` | UDF/UDAF | Custom Java class registered | Domain-specific logic |
| 14 | `EXPLAIN SELECT ...` | `EXPLAIN PLAN` | DAG visualization | Query plan inspection |
| 15 | `SET hive.execution.engine=tez;` | Hint | Switches execution engine | Performance tuning |

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — End-to-End Hive Programming (Rutherglen-Style)

**Scenario:** KTU lab problem — analyze a clickstream log to find the top 3 pages per user.

**Step 1 — Create a Hive table with partitioning:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE clicks (
    user_id   BIGINT,
    page_url  STRING,
    click_ts  TIMESTAMP
)
PARTITIONED BY (dt STRING)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED
FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE;
```

*Logic:* `PARTITIONED BY (dt)` makes queries on a specific date **skip-scan** all other dates — a 365× speedup on a year of data.

**Step 2 — Load data into a specific partition:**

```sql
LOAD DATA INPATH '/data/clicks/2024-01-15/' 
INTO TABLE clicks PARTITION (dt='2024-01-15');
```

*Logic:* This is a *file move* (HDFS `mv` under the hood); it does not parse or transform data.

**Step 3 — Compute top-3 pages per user using a window function (Hive 0.11+):**

```sql
SELECT user_id, page_url, click_count
FROM (
    SELECT 
        user_id,
        page_url,
        COUNT(*) AS click_count,
        RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC) AS rnk
    FROM clicks
    WHERE dt = '2024-01-15'
    GROUP BY user_id, page_url
) ranked_clicks
WHERE rnk <= 3;
```

*Step-by-step compiler logic (what Hive does internally):*

```
┌───────────────┐    ┌───────────────┐    ┌───────────────┐
│ Stage 1 (Map) │ →  │ Stage 2 (Shuf)│ →  │ Stage 3 (Red) │
│ Scan + Group  │    │ user_id,page  │    │ Window fn     │
└───────────────┘    └───────────────┘    └───────────────┘
```

**Step 4 — Verify with `EXPLAIN`:**

```sql
EXPLAIN
SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) FROM clicks WHERE dt='2024-01-15' GROUP BY user_id;
```

*Output tells you:* which stages, how many reducers, whether partition pruning fired.

### 4.1 High-Level Hive System Architecture (Rutherglen 2011 Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client1[JDBC Client]
    client2[ODBC Client]
    client3[Thrift Client]
    client4[Hive CLI]
    
    subgraph uiLayer[UI Layer]
        ui[Hive Service - Thrift Server + CLI]
    end
    
    subgraph driverLayer[Driver and Compiler]
        drv[Driver - Session Handle]
        parser[Parser - AST]
        sem[Semantic Analyzer]
        planner[Query Planner - DAG]
        opt[Optimizer]
    end
    
    subgraph metaLayer[Metastore]
        ms[(MySQL or Derby)]
        catalog[Table and Column Catalog]
    end
    
    subgraph execLayer[Execution Engine]
        mr[Hadoop MapReduce]
        tez[Apache Tez]
        spark[Apache Spark]
    end
    
    subgraph storageLayer[HDFS Storage]
        raw[Raw Log Files]
        wh[Warehouse - user hive warehouse]
        orc[ORC and Parquet Files]
    end
    
    client1 --> ui
    client2 --> ui
    client3 --> ui
    client4 --> ui
    
    ui --> drv
    drv --> parser
    parser --> sem
    sem --> planner
    planner --> opt
    opt --> mr
    opt --> tez
    opt --> spark
    
    sem -.lookup schema.-> ms
    planner -.read stats.-> catalog
    ms -.persist DDL.-> catalog
    
    mr --> wh
    tez --> wh
    spark --> wh
    raw --> wh
    wh --> orc
```

### 4.2 Query Execution Flow — From HQL to MapReduce Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HiveQL Query Text] --> B[Lexer - Tokenize]
    B --> C[Parser - Build AST]
    C --> D[Semantic Analysis]
    D --> E[Query Plan - Logical DAG]
    E --> F[Optimizer]
    F --> G[Physical Plan - MapReduce Tasks]
    G --> H[Hadoop JobTracker - YARN]
    H --> I[Map Tasks - HDFS Read]
    I --> J[Shuffle and Sort]
    J --> K[Reduce Tasks - Aggregate]
    K --> L[Write to HDFS or Return to Client]
    
    D -.uses.-> M[(Metastore)]
    F -.uses.-> M
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Programming Hive the Rutherglen Way (2011)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph devBox[Developer Workstation]

    subgraph hadoopCluster[Hadoop Cluster]

    end
    
    subgraph metaStore[Metastore RDBMS]
        pg[(PostgreSQL)]
    end
    
    code --> thrift

    ql -->|beeline| ts
    ts --> sess
    sess --> driver2
    driver2 -->|reads schema| pg
    driver2 -->|submits job| resmgr
    resmgr --> node1
    resmgr --> node2
    resmgr --> node3

    node2 --> namenode
    node3 --> namenode

```

### 4.4 Partitioning + Bucketing Decision Tree (Engineering Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New Hive Table?] --> q1{Dataset greater than 1 TB?}
    q1 -- No --> simple[Plain table - TEXTFILE or ORC]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Query always filters by date or region?}
    q2 -- Yes --> part[PARTITIONED BY dt or region]
    q2 -- No --> buck[CLUSTERED BY id INTO 64 BUCKETS]
    part --> q3{Need efficient sampling?}
    q3 -- Yes --> both[PARTITIONED and CLUSTERED]
    q3 -- No --> part
    buck --> endnode[Run EXPLAIN - Validate DAG]
    both --> endnode
    simple --> endnode
```
