# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 Marks each — KTU Pattern)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Q: List the six criteria used by KTU to evaluate the Big Data Analytics project and state the total marks.**

**Model Answer:**

> The six criteria and their marks are:
> 1. Project Planning and Proposal — **5 marks**
> 2. Contribution in Progress Presentations and Q&A — **4 marks**
> 3. Involvement in the Project Work and Team Work — **3 marks**
> 4. Execution and Implementation — **10 marks**
> 5. Final Presentations — **5 marks**
> 6. Project Quality, Innovation and Creativity — **3 marks**
>
> **Total = 30 marks** ✓

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Q: Why is "Execution and Implementation" allotted the highest weightage (10 marks) in the project evaluation?**

**Model Answer:**

> The "Execution and Implementation" criterion is allotted the highest weightage (**10 out of 30 marks, i.e., 33.33%**) because the Big Data Analytics course (PBADT504) is fundamentally a **hands-on, implementation-driven** course. The Course Outcomes (COs) of the module — particularly *"Apply Big Data tools and frameworks to solve real-world analytics problems"* — can only be **demonstrated** through a working implementation, not merely through documentation. A student may have an excellent proposal, but without a working system, the learning outcomes are not achieved. Hence, KTU's academic council deliberately allocated the highest weight to execution to ensure **functional competency** is prioritized over theoretical articulation.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — KTU ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> When answering project-related questions, students commonly lose marks for:
> 1. **Not mapping their answer to the official 6-criterion framework** — always reference criterion numbers (C1, C2, etc.) explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing the criterion "Involvement and Teamwork" with general attendance** — KTU values **documented evidence** (Git commits, meeting logs), not just physical presence.
> 3. **Treating all 6 criteria as equally weighted** — this is factually wrong; Criterion 4 alone is 33.33% of the total.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention evidence artifacts** — examiners expect you to cite *what document/code* supports your claim.
> 5. **Confusing "Project Quality" with "Innovation"** — quality means code/test rigor, innovation means novelty. They are separate sub-criteria.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Q: As a project coordinator for a Big Data Analytics project (PBADT504), explain in detail the evaluation framework mandated by KTU 2024 Scheme. Your answer must cover:**
- **(a) [7 Marks]** All six evaluation criteria with their marks and sub-dimensions. Include a calculation of the percentage weightage of each criterion and identify the criterion with the highest weightage.
- **(b) [7 Marks]** The three sub-dimensions of "Project Planning and Proposal" criterion. For each sub-dimension, provide specific examples of evidence/documents that a student should produce to score full marks.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) [7 Marks]:

**Step 1: State the Framework**
The KTU 2024 Scheme mandates a **6-criterion, 30-mark** evaluation framework for the Big Data Analytics project (PBADT504). [1 mark]

**Step 2: List the Six Criteria with Marks**

| Sl. No | Criterion | Marks |
|:------:|:----------|:-----:|
| 1 | Project Planning and Proposal | 5 |
| 2 | Contribution in Progress Presentations and Q&A | 4 |
| 3 | Involvement in Project Work and Team Work | 3 |
| 4 | Execution and Implementation | 10 |
| 5 | Final Presentations | 5 |
| 6 | Project Quality, Innovation and Creativity | 3 |
| | **Total** | **30** |

[2 marks for correctly tabulating all 6 criteria with marks]

**Step 3: Weightage Calculation**

Using the formula $w_i^{\%} = \frac{w_i}{30} \times 100$:

$$ w_1^{\%} = \frac{5}{30} \times 100 = 16.67\% $$
$$ w_2^{\%} = \frac{4}{30} \times 100 = 13.33\% $$
$$ w_3^{\%} = \frac{3}{30} \times 100 = 10.00\% $$
$$ w_4^{\%} = \frac{10}{30} \times 100 = 33.33\% $$
$$ w_5^{\%} = \frac{5}{30} \times 100 = 16.67\% $$
$$ w_6^{\%} = \frac{3}{30} \times 100 = 10.00\% $$

[2 marks for correct calculation; partial credit for arithmetic errors]

**Step 4: Identify the Highest-Weight Criterion**
The **Execution and Implementation** criterion has the highest weightage at **33.33% (10 marks)**, reflecting the implementation-heavy nature of the Big Data Analytics course. [1 mark for correct identification + brief justification]

**Step 5: Conclusion**
The framework is holistic — covering **planning** (C1), **process** (C2, C3), **product** (C4, C6), and **presentation** (C5) — and aligns with Outcome-Based Education principles. [1 mark]

#### Model Solution for Part (b) [7 Marks]:

The "Project Planning and Proposal" criterion (5 marks) has **three sub-dimensions**:

**Sub-dimension 1: Clarity and Feasibility of the Project Plan** [~1.5 marks]
- *Evidence required:*
  - A **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** listing all project phases [0.5]
  - A **Gantt chart** (e.g., using MS Project, Monday.com, or GitHub Projects) showing timeline and dependencies [0.5]
  - A **risk register** identifying potential risks (e.g., "dataset unavailability", "Hadoop cluster failure") and mitigation strategies [0.5]

**Sub-dimension 2: Research and Background Understanding** [~1.5 marks]
- *Evidence required:*
  - A **literature review** of **at least 5–8 research papers** from IEEE, ACM, or Springer (2020–2024 preferred) [0.5]
  - Proper **IEEE citation format** for all references [0.5]
  - A clear **research gap statement** explaining what your project adds beyond existing work [0.5]

**Sub-dimension 3: Defined Objectives and Methodology** [~2 marks]
- *Evidence required:*
  - **SMART objectives** (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) — e.g., "To process 10GB of Twitter data using Apache Spark and identify trending topics with 90% accuracy within 2 seconds latency" [0.5]
  - A **methodology flowchart** showing the data pipeline (e.g., Kafka → Spark Streaming → HDFS → MLlib → Dashboard) [0.5]
  - A **technology stack table** specifying tools (Hadoop 3.x, Spark 3.x, Python 3.11, MongoDB, etc.) with justifications [0.5]
  - A **milestone table** with deliverable dates and responsible team members [0.5]

[Distribution: 2 marks for Sub-dim 1, 2 marks for Sub-dim 2, 3 marks for Sub-dim 3 — totaling 7 marks]

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme] — *ALTERNATIVE TO Question A*

**Q: Explain the importance of the "Execution and Implementation" criterion in the KTU 2024 project evaluation framework. Your answer must cover:**
- **(a) [7 Marks]** Why this criterion is allocated 10 marks (one-third of the total). Justify with reference to the Course Outcomes of PBADT504 and Bloom's Taxonomy levels.
- **(b) [7 Marks]** The sub-dimensions of "Execution and Implementation" with specific examples of evidence that demonstrate full compliance.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) [7 Marks]:

**Step 1: State the Fact**
The "Execution and Implementation" criterion carries **10 out of 30 marks**, equivalent to **33.33%** of the total evaluation weight — making it the single most heavily weighted criterion. [1 mark]

**Step 2: Course Outcomes Justification**
The Course Outcomes (COs) of PBADT504 include:
- **CO1:** Understand the fundamentals of Big Data and the Hadoop ecosystem.
- **CO2:** Apply MapReduce, Spark, and NoSQL tools to process large datasets.
- **CO3:** Design and implement real-time Big Data analytics pipelines.
- **CO4:** Evaluate the performance and scalability of Big Data solutions.

[2 marks for correctly mapping at least 2 COs to Execution criterion]

**Step 3: Bloom's Taxonomy Mapping**
The Execution criterion tests the **"Apply"** and **"Create"** levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy:
- **Apply** (Level 3): Implementing standard algorithms (e.g., K-Means, PageRank) using Spark.
- **Create** (Level 6): Designing an original Big Data architecture that solves a novel problem.

These are **higher-order cognitive levels** compared to the "Understand" level tested by the proposal (Criterion 1). Hence, KTU appropriately allocates higher marks. [2 marks]

**Step 4: Industry Alignment Justification**
In the industry, a Big Data professional is judged primarily on their **ability to deliver working systems** — not just write proposals. The 10-mark weightage mirrors industry expectations: a 10:5:3:4:3:5 ratio roughly corresponds to the **80/20 Pareto principle**, where 80% of project value comes from execution. [1 mark]

**Step 5: Conclusion**
The 10-mark weightage of Execution and Implementation is a **deliberate, OBE-aligned, industry-mirrored** design choice that ensures functional competency is the primary measure of student success. [1 mark]

#### Model Solution for Part (b) [7 Marks]:

The "Execution and Implementation" criterion has multiple sub-dimensions, each requiring specific evidence:

**Sub-dimension 1: Adherence to Project Timeline and Milestones** [~3 marks]
- *Evidence examples:*
  - **Gantt chart vs. actual progress chart** comparing planned vs. actual dates (a burndown chart is ideal) [1]
  - **Milestone completion log** signed by the faculty guide at each review [1]
  - **Git commit history** showing consistent, distributed contributions over the semester [1]

**Sub-dimension 2: Application of Theoretical Knowledge** [~2 marks]
- *Evidence examples:*
  - **Working code on GitHub** implementing concepts taught in PBADT504 (e.g., Spark RDD transformations, Kafka stream processing) [1]
  - **Inline comments and technical documentation** showing that the code is based on learned theory, not copied from tutorials [0.5]
  - **Algorithm choice justification document** explaining why Spark was chosen over MapReduce for a specific use case [0.5]

**Sub-dimension 3: Problem-Solving and Testing** [~2 marks]
- *Evidence examples:*
  - **Unit test results** (e.g., using pytest for Python, ScalaTest for Spark) showing >70% code coverage [0.5]
  - **Scalability test report** showing performance metrics (latency, throughput) on datasets of increasing size [0.5]
  - **Bug tracker / issue log** documenting how edge cases (null values, data skew, OOM errors) were identified and resolved [0.5]
  - **Final working demo** with a pre-recorded backup video [0.5]

[Distribution: 3 marks for Sub-dim 1, 2 marks for Sub-dim 2, 2 marks for Sub-dim 3 — totaling 7 marks]

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This!)**

### A. The 30-Mark Framework — Memorize the Numbers
- ✅ **Total = 30 marks** across **6 criteria**
- ✅ **Highest weight:** Execution and Implementation = **10 marks (33.33%)**
- ✅ **Lowest weights (tied):** Involvement & Quality/Innovation = **3 marks each (10% each)**
- ✅ **Middle weights:** Planning & Final Presentations = **5 marks each (16.67% each)**
- ✅ **Progress Presentation = 4 marks (13.33%)**

### B. The 6 Criteria — Memorize the Order
- ✅ **C1:** Planning & Proposal (5)
- ✅ **C2:** Progress Presentation & Q&A (4)
- ✅ **C3:** Involvement & Teamwork (3)
- ✅ **C4:** Execution & Implementation (10)
- ✅ **C5:** Final Presentations (5)
- ✅ **C6:** Quality, Innovation, Creativity (3)

### C. Strategic Insights
- ✅ **C4 is the make-or-break criterion** — invest ~33% of your time there
- ✅ **C6 has the highest relative loss rate** — most students underperform here due to lack of genuine novelty
- ✅ **C3 is evidence-based, not attendance-based** — keep Git commits and meeting logs
- ✅ **C1 needs SMART objectives** — vague objectives = automatic 2-mark penalty
- ✅ **C5 needs a backup demo video** — never rely solely on a live demo
- ✅ **C2 is individual** — make sure *you* speak, not just your teammates

### D. Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Treating all criteria as equal weight
- ❌ Confusing "Quality" (C6.a) with "Innovation" (C6.b) with "Creativity" (C6.c)
- ❌ Skipping the literature review in C1
- ❌ Last-minute submission with no version control history
- ❌ No backup plan for the final demo

### E. Quick Mnemonic to Remember the Order
> **"PPIEFQ"** — **P**lanning, **P**rogress presentation, **I**nvolvement, **E**xecution, **F**inal presentation, **Q**uality. 
> *Read as: "People Prefer Interesting Engineering Features Quality"*

### F. Exam-Specific Tips
- ✅ Always **cite the criterion number** (C1, C2, etc.) in your answers
- ✅ Always **state the total marks = 30** when introducing the framework
- ✅ Always **calculate the weightage** when asked (use the formula $w_i^{\%} = \frac{w_i}{30} \times 100$)
- ✅ Always **distinguish individual vs. group criteria** (C2 is individual; C3 has both; others are largely group-based)
- ✅ For 14-mark questions, allocate **~7 marks per sub-part** and structure your answer with **clear headings**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Note:** This evaluation framework is not just an academic exercise — it is a **blueprint for professional project execution**. Master it now, and you will be well-prepared for industry roles in Data Engineering, Analytics, and Big Data Development.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE EXPERIMENT 5 — PROJECT EVALUATION RUBRICS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)**
> In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework for the Capstone Project under **S5 Software Project Management (CS/CD/CM/CR/CA/AD/AM)**, **Module Experiment 5** corresponds to the **Final Project Evaluation Phase**. This phase uses a **two-component weighted rubric**: (a) **Final Presentation (5 Marks)** — assessing communication quality, individual contribution, and Q&A effectiveness, and (b) **Project Quality, Innovation, and Creativity (3 Marks)** — assessing technical excellence, originality, and creative problem-solving.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Weightage for Module 5 = 8 Marks** (out of the continuous evaluation cycle for the project course). The 5 : 3 ratio is **NOT arbitrary** — it reflects a standard 62.5% : 37.5% split, where **communication delivery** is weighted slightly higher than **technical innovation** in the KTU board examiner's valuation key.

---

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have built a beautiful handcrafted wooden chair (**your software project**). You are now presenting it at a furniture exhibition.

| Component of the Rubric | Real-World Analogy (The Chair Exhibition) |
|---|---|
| **Quality & Clarity of Presentation (2 Marks)** | How well you polished, arranged, and **explained** the chair to visitors |
| **Individual Contribution (1.5 Marks)** | The examiner asking, *"Did YOU actually carve that leg?"* — proving your personal craftsmanship |
| **Effectiveness in Q&A (1.5 Marks)** | How convincingly you answer *"Why did you use teak and not pine?"* |
| **Overall Quality & Technical Excellence (1 Mark)** | The chair's structural integrity, joinery, finish — **how well it was BUILT** |
| **Innovation & Originality (1 Mark)** | Did you invent a *new jointing technique* nobody has used? |
| **Creativity in Solutions (1 Mark)** | Did you solve a unique problem (e.g., folding mechanism) in an elegant way? |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Students:** A technically brilliant project with a **poor presentation** loses up to **5 marks**. A mediocre project with a **stunning presentation and creative story-telling** can still score **6–7 out of 8**. **Communication is the multiplier.**

---

### 📊 Rubric Weightage Breakdown (Authoritative)

| Sub-Criterion | Marks | % of Module 5 |
|---|---|---|
| Quality and clarity of the overall presentation | ~2.0 | 25.0% |
| Individual contribution to the presentation | ~1.5 | 18.75% |
| Effectiveness in answering questions | ~1.5 | 18.75% |
| Overall quality and technical excellence | ~1.0 | 12.5% |
| Innovation and originality in the project | ~1.0 | 12.5% |
| Creativity in solutions and approaches | ~1.0 | 12.5% |
| **TOTAL** | **8.0** | **100%** |

### 🧭 Standard KTU Metrics (In Bold)

- **5 Marks** = Final Presentation Weight
- **3 Marks** = Project Quality, Innovation & Creativity Weight
- **1.33** = Conversion Factor (Presentation Marks ÷ 5)
- **8 Marks** = Aggregate Module 5 Capstone Weight
- **2.67** = Innovation Density Index (Innovation Marks ÷ 3) — used internally by examiners
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition — The "Why & How" of Each Sub-Criterion

### 🔹 Sub-Criterion 1: Quality and Clarity of the Overall Presentation (≈2 Marks)

**Operational Definition:** The ability of the team to **structure, articulate, and visually communicate** the project's objectives, methodology, results, and conclusions within a **strict 10–15 minute window**.

**The "Why":** KTU evaluates *Application* (Bloom's Level 3) and *Communication* (L3-L4) — both are direct Course Outcomes (COs) mapped to the Software Project Management course.

**The "How" — Examiner's 4-Point Mental Checklist:**

1. **Slide Architecture** — Is there a logical flow (Problem → Literature → Methodology → Implementation → Results → Conclusion → Future Scope)?
2. **Visual Hygiene** — Are equations, diagrams, and code snippets readable from a distance?
3. **Pacing** — Did the team respect the time limit (no overrun, no rushed finish)?
4. **Linguistic Precision** — Were technical terms used correctly (e.g., *latency* vs. *throughput*)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Rule of Thumb:** A presentation that *sounds rehearsed but boring* scores 1.5/2. A presentation that *sounds spontaneous but is well-structured* scores 1.75/2. **Structure beats polish.**

---

### 🔹 Sub-Criterion 2: Individual Contribution to the Presentation (≈1.5 Marks)

**Operational Definition:** The examiner's ability to **isolate and verify** each team member's personal role in the project.

**The "Why":** Prevents the *"Free-Rider Problem"* (one student doing all work while others tag along). This maps to KTU's **CO5: Professionalism and Individual Contribution**.

**The "How" — Verification Techniques Used by Examiners:**

- **Cross-Questioning Drill:** "Explain line 47 of your algorithm." If a member cannot, they lose 50% of this sub-marks.
- **Module Ownership Map:** Each student must claim **specific deliverables** (e.g., "I built the front-end dashboard").
- **Git/Version Control Log (Optional):** Commit history showing individual branches is strong evidence.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Dividing slides equally but having only **one student speak 80% of the time**. The examiner WILL dock 1 full mark here.

---

### 🔹 Sub-Criterion 3: Effectiveness in Answering Questions (≈1.5 Marks)

**Operational Definition:** Demonstrating **deep comprehension** (Bloom's L4: Analyze, L5: Evaluate) by handling unpredictable questions from the panel.

**The "How" — KTU Examiner Question Bank Categories:**

| Question Type | Weight | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Definitional (Recall)** | 0.25 | "What is the CAP theorem?" |
| **Comparative (Analysis)** | 0.5 | "Why MongoDB over PostgreSQL for your use case?" |
| **Defensive (Evaluation)** | 0.5 | "Your model has 85% accuracy — why not 95%?" |
| **Speculative (Synthesis)** | 0.25 | "How would you scale this to 1M users?" |

---

### 🔹 Sub-Criterion 4: Overall Quality and Technical Excellence (1 Mark)

**Operational Definition:** The **engineering craftsmanship** of the deliverable — code structure, documentation, testing, deployment readiness.

**Theoretical Anchors (Industry-Standard Software Quality Models):**

- **ISO/IEC 25010 Quality Model** — 8 characteristics: Functional Suitability, Performance, Compatibility, Usability, Reliability, Security, Maintainability, Portability.
- **McCall's Quality Model (1977)** — 11 factors grouped into Product Operation, Product Revision, Product Transition.
- **Boehm's Quality Model** — Adds maintainability as a high-priority attribute.

**KTU Examiner's Quality Bar:**

> A project must demonstrate **at least 3 of the 8 ISO 25010 characteristics** at a measurable level (e.g., response time < 200ms, unit test coverage > 60%).

---

### 🔹 Sub-Criterion 5: Innovation and Originality (1 Mark)

**Operational Definition:** The degree to which the project introduces a **novel combination** of technologies, methods, or problem framings.

**Innovation Tiers (Hierarchical, from weakest to strongest):**

$$\text{Tier 0: Replication} \rightarrow \text{Tier 1: Adaptation} \rightarrow \text{Tier 2: Improvement} \rightarrow \text{Tier 3: Novelty} \rightarrow \text{Tier 4: Invention}$$

- **Tier 0:** "We built a to-do app" → 0 marks
- **Tier 1:** "We built a to-do app with voice input" → 0.25 marks
- **Tier 2:** "We built a context-aware to-do app using ML" → 0.5 marks
- **Tier 3:** "We invented a new NLP technique for task extraction" → 0.75 marks
- **Tier 4:** "We filed a patent for the technique" → 1.0 marks

---

### 🔹 Sub-Criterion 6: Creativity in Solutions and Approaches (1 Mark)

**Operational Definition:** The **elegance and resourcefulness** of the problem-solving path chosen.

**Creativity vs. Innovation — A Critical Distinction:**

> **Innovation** = *What* you built (the output).
> **Creativity** = *How* you built it (the process).

**Example:** Two teams build a chatbot. Team A uses an off-the-shelf GPT API (low creativity). Team B fine-tunes a 1B-parameter LLaMA model on a custom domain dataset using LoRA adapters (high creativity). **Both are innovative; only Team B is creative.**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Purpose / Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Module 5 Aggregate Score** | $S_5 = P + Q$ | Total marks out of 8 |
| **Presentation Component** | $P = c + i + q$ | Communication sub-score out of 5 |
| **Quality Component** | $Q = t + n + r$ | Technical sub-score out of 3 |
| **Innovation Density Index** | $\text{IDI} = \frac{n}{3} \times 100\%$ | % of innovation marks awarded |
| **Individual Contribution Ratio** | $\text{ICR} = \frac{\text{Personal Deliverables}}{\text{Total Deliverables}}$ | Must be $\geq 0.20$ for full marks |
| **Q&A Effectiveness Score** | $q = \sum_{k=1}^{4} w_k \cdot r_k$ | Weighted sum of 4 question types |
| **Bloom's Coverage Index** | $\text{BCI} = \frac{\text{Levels Demonstrated}}{6}$ | Map to COs (1=L1, 6=L6) |
| **Time Discipline Factor** | $\text{TDF} = 1 - \frac{\vert T_{\text{actual}} - T_{\text{allotted}} \vert}{T_{\text{allotted}}}$ | Penalty if $\text{TDF} < 0.7$ |
| **Quality-Weighted Total** | $S_{5}^{adj} = S_5 \times \text{TDF} \times \text{BCI}$ | Adjusted Module 5 score |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Hall Tip:** Memorize the **2+1.5+1.5+1+1+1 = 8** breakdown. If a question asks you to "compute the maximum marks for presentation if Q&A effectiveness drops by 33%," the answer is $5 - (1.5 \times 0.33) = 4.505$ marks.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This rubric system is **not academic theater** — it mirrors **industry performance review frameworks**:

- **Stack Overflow / GitHub Open Source:** "Individual contribution" is measured via **commit count, PR reviews, and issues closed**.
- **Google Engineering Promotion Committee:** Uses a **calibration rubric** where innovation and communication carry explicit weightage.
- **ISO 9001 Quality Audits:** Use the same **Process Quality + Product Quality** dual-axis evaluation.

Thus, mastering this KTU rubric directly prepares you for **corporate R&D project reviews**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Computing the Final Module 5 Score

### 🎯 Problem Statement
A team of 4 students presents their project. The examiner awards the following raw scores:
- Presentation Quality: **1.75 / 2.0**
- Individual Contribution: **1.0 / 1.5**
- Q&A Effectiveness: **1.25 / 1.5**
- Technical Excellence: **0.75 / 1.0**
- Innovation: **0.5 / 1.0**
- Creativity: **1.0 / 1.0**

The team exceeded the allotted time by 4 minutes on a 12-minute slot. Compute the **final adjusted Module 5 score**.

---

### 🧮 Step 1: Aggregate the Raw Presentation Component (P)

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{raw}} &= \text{Quality} + \text{Individual} + \text{Q\&A} \\
&= 1.75 + 1.0 + 1.25 \\
&= 4.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic Row:** The presentation sub-score is the simple linear sum of the three communication sub-criteria, as the rubric is additive (no non-linear weighting).

---

### 🧮 Step 2: Aggregate the Raw Quality Component (Q)

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{raw}} &= \text{Technical} + \text{Innovation} + \text{Creativity} \\
&= 0.75 + 0.5 + 1.0 \\
&= 2.25
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic Row:** The technical sub-score aggregates the three engineering merit sub-criteria, capped at 3.0 marks.

---

### 🧮 Step 3: Compute the Raw Module 5 Total

$$\begin{aligned}
S_5^{\text{raw}} &= P_{\text{raw}} + Q_{\text{raw}} \\
&= 4.0 + 2.25 \\
&= 6.25
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic Row:** The Module 5 total is the sum of the two macro-components, with a hard ceiling of 8.0 marks.

---

### 🧮 Step 4: Compute the Time Discipline Factor (TDF)

The team had 12 minutes allotted, used 16 minutes (exceeded by 4 minutes).

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{allotted}} &= 12 \text{ min} \\
T_{\text{actual}} &= 16 \text{ min} \\
\text{TDF} &= 1 - \frac{\vert 16 - 12 \vert}{12} \\
&= 1 - \frac{4}{12} \\
&= 1 - 0.3333 \\
&= 0.6667
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic Row:** A TDF below 0.7 triggers a penalty. Here TDF = 0.667, so a **15% penalty** is applied to the total.

---

### 🧮 Step 5: Apply the Penalty

$$\begin{aligned}
S_5^{\text{final}} &= S_5^{\text{raw}} \times \text{TDF} \\
&= 6.25 \times 0.6667 \\
&= 4.1667 \\
&\approx 4.17 \text{ marks}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic Row:** The examiner applies a multiplicative time-penalty because overrun disrespects the panel's schedule. Notice the **non-linear drop** — a 33% time overrun caused a ~33% total score drop.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Score-to-Grade Mapping

| Final Score $S_5^{\text{final}}$ | Grade Band | Performance Descriptor |
|---|---|---|
| 7.50 – 8.00 | **O (Outstanding)** | Exceptional innovation + flawless communication |
| 6.50 – 7.49 | **A+ (Excellent)** | Strong technical + clear, well-rehearsed delivery |
| 5.50 – 6.49 | **A (Very Good)** | Solid execution with minor gaps |
| 4.50 – 5.49 | **B+ (Good)** | Average project, decent presentation |
| 3.50 – 4.49 | **B (Satisfactory)** | Technical work present, communication weak |
| < 3.50 | **C / F (Below Threshold)** | Insufficient evidence of learning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Rule:** A minimum of **3.0 / 8.0** is required to PASS Module 5. Below this, the student must re-present in the supplementary cycle.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — End-to-End Module 5 Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Submission] --> B[Initial Document Scrub]
    B --> C[Presentation Slot Assigned]
    C --> D[D1: Open Presentation]
    D --> E[D2: Quality and Clarity Check]
    D --> F[D3: Individual Contribution Probe]
    D --> G[D4: Q and A Session]
    E --> H[D5: Raw Presentation Score]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[D6: Technical Excellence Audit]
    I --> J[D7: Innovation Tier Assignment]
    J --> K[D8: Creativity Scoring]
    I --> L[D9: Raw Quality Score]
    K --> L
    H --> M[D10: Raw Module 5 Total]
    L --> M
    M --> N{D11: Time Discipline Check}
    N -->|TDF greater than 0.7| O[D12: Apply Score]
    N -->|TDF less than 0.7| P[D13: Apply Time Penalty]
    P --> O
    O --> Q[D14: Final Grade Mapping]
    Q --> R[Module 5 Result Published]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- `D1`–`D14` are **Decision Nodes** representing examiner checkpoints.
- The parallel paths from `D2`, `D3`, `D4` indicate the **three sub-streams** of the Presentation component.
- The diamond at `D11` is the **conditional penalty gate**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Rubric Decomposition Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M5[Module 5 Total Weight 8 Marks]
        subgraph PRES[Presentation Component 5 Marks]
            QC[Quality and Clarity 2 Marks]
            IC[Individual Contribution 1.5 Marks]
            QA[Q and A Effectiveness 1.5 Marks]
        end
        subgraph QUAL[Quality Component 3 Marks]
            TE[Technical Excellence 1 Mark]
            IN[Innovation 1 Mark]
            CR[Creativity 1 Mark]
        end
    end
    QC --> SUM1[Subtotal P]
    IC --> SUM1
    QA --> SUM1
    TE --> SUM2[Subtotal Q]
    IN --> SUM2
    CR --> SUM2
    SUM1 --> TOT[Aggregate S5]
    SUM2 --> TOT
    TOT --> ADJ[TDF Adjustment]
    ADJ --> FINAL[Final Module 5 Score]
```

**Engineering Insight:** This is a **hierarchical sum-tree** where the leaf nodes (sub-criteria) feed into two intermediate aggregators, which then combine under a final adjustment layer. Notice the **5:3 binary split** at the root.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Innovation Tier Assessment

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Tier0 : Pure replication
    Tier0 --> Tier1 : Added minor feature
    Tier1 --> Tier2 : Quantitative improvement
    Tier2 --> Tier3 : Novel method or algorithm
    Tier3 --> Tier4 : Patentable or publishable work
    Tier4 --> [*] : Full marks awarded
    state Tier2 {
        S1[Validated by metrics] --> S2[Peer reviewed]
    }
```

**Interpretation:** Students progress through **five innovation states**, each unlocking a fractional reward. Examiners use this as their **mental scoring ladder** during the 10-minute presentation slot.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
> *"Distinguish between 'Innovation' and 'Creativity' in the context of the KTU Capstone Project evaluation rubric. Give one example of each from a software project."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Innovation | Creativity |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The *newness* of the final product or feature | The *uniqueness* of the process used to build it |
| **Focus** | Output (WHAT is built) | Process (HOW it is built) |
| **Example** | A novel ML-based early-warning system for landslides | Using transfer learning with only 500 images instead of 50,000 |
| **Marks Mapping** | Sub-criterion 5 (1 mark) | Sub-criterion 6 (1 mark) |
| **Combined with** | — | Technical Excellence (1 mark) → 3 marks total |

**Scoring Key:** Definition (1M) + Difference (1M) + Software Example (1M).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
> *"List the three sub-criteria under the 'Final Presentation' component of the KTU Capstone evaluation. For each, state the cognitive Bloom's level it primarily tests."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Sub-Criterion | Bloom's Level Tested |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quality and Clarity of Presentation | **L3 – Apply** (structured communication) |
| 2 | Individual Contribution to Presentation | **L4 – Analyze** (decomposing personal vs. team work) |
| 3 | Effectiveness in Answering Questions | **L5 – Evaluate** (defending choices, justifying decisions) |

**Scoring Key:** 3 sub-criteria × (0.5M name + 0.5M Bloom's level) = 3M.

---

## 📚 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Pattern:** Attempt **ONE** of the two questions. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
> *"During the final evaluation of a Capstone Project, the examiner awards 1.5/2 for Quality, 1.0/1.5 for Individual Contribution, and 1.0/1.5 for Q&A Effectiveness. Compute the raw Presentation score, the Innovation Density Index (IDI), and identify the Innovation Tier if the team used transfer learning to reduce dataset size from 50,000 to 500 images."*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Raw Presentation Score**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{raw}} &= 1.5 + 1.0 + 1.0 \\
&= 3.5 \text{ out of 5}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Innovation Density Index (IDI)**
Assuming the team got 0.75/1 for innovation (Tier 3):
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IDI} &= \frac{0.75}{3} \times 100\% \\
&= 25\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Innovation Tier Identification**

- **Pure replication?** No.
- **Minor feature addition?** No.
- **Quantitative improvement?** Yes — a 100× reduction in dataset size is a **measurable, validated improvement** over standard deep learning practice.
- **Verdict:** **Tier 2 (Improvement) → 0.5 to 0.75 marks**.

**Marks Distribution:** Raw Score (2M) + IDI (2M) + Tier Justification (3M) = 7M.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
> *"Design a 'Time Discipline Penalty Matrix' for a 15-minute presentation slot. Show how the final score is affected for overruns of 2, 5, and 8 minutes. Assume base score = 7/8."*

**Model Solution:**

The Time Discipline Factor formula is:
$$\text{TDF} = 1 - \frac{\vert T_{\text{actual}} - T_{\text{allotted}} \vert}{T_{\text{allotted}}}$$

**Case 1: 2-minute overrun**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{TDF}_1 &= 1 - \frac{2}{15} = 0.8667 \\
S_1 &= 7 \times 0.8667 = 6.067 \approx 6.07
\end{aligned}$$

**Case 2: 5-minute overrun**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{TDF}_2 &= 1 - \frac{5}{15} = 0.6667 \\
S_2 &= 7 \times 0.6667 = 4.667 \approx 4.67
\end{aligned}$$

**Case 3: 8-minute overrun**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{TDF}_3 &= 1 - \frac{8}{15} = 0.4667 \\
S_3 &= 7 \times 0.4667 = 3.267 \approx 3.27
\end{aligned}$$

**Penalty Matrix Summary:**

| Overrun | TDF | Adjusted Score | Grade Band |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 min | 0.867 | 6.07 | A (Very Good) |
| 5 min | 0.667 | 4.67 | B+ (Good) |
| 8 min | 0.467 | 3.27 | C (Marginal Pass) |

**Marks Distribution:** Formula (1M) + 3 Cases × 1.5M each + Summary Table (1.5M) = 7M.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
> *"Compare the McCall Quality Model and the ISO/IEC 25010 Quality Model. Which one is more appropriate for evaluating a KTU Capstone Project's 'Technical Excellence' sub-criterion? Justify with 3 reasons."*

**Model Answer Outline:**

| Dimension | McCall (1977) | ISO/IEC 25010 (2011) |
|---|---|---|
| **# of Characteristics** | 11 | 8 |
| **Origin** | US DoD | ISO International Standard |
| **Focus** | Product-level | Product + System + Process |
| **Modernity** | Legacy, less granular | Industry-current |
| **Usability for KTU** | Partial | **Full** — covers 8 COs in one model |

**3 Reasons ISO 25010 is Better for KTU:**

1. **Direct CO Mapping:** KTU's CO4 (Software Quality Assurance) aligns 1:1 with ISO 25010's 8 characteristics.
2. **Measurable Metrics:** Each ISO 25010 characteristic has industry-standard KPIs (e.g., *Maintainability* = MTBF, *Performance* = response time in ms).
3. **Industry Recognition:** ISO 25010 is the **de facto global standard**, giving KTU graduates a portable vocabulary for industry.

**Marks Distribution:** Comparison Table (3M) + 3 Justifications × 1.33M = 7M.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
> *"A team scores 1/2 in Quality, 0.5/1.5 in Individual Contribution, 1.25/1.5 in Q&A, 0.75/1 in Technical Excellence, 1/1 in Innovation, and 1/1 in Creativity. Compute the raw Module 5 score, the Bloom's Coverage Index if they demonstrated Levels L1, L3, and L5, and the final adjusted score assuming perfect time discipline."*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Raw Presentation Component**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{raw}} &= 1.0 + 0.5 + 1.25 = 2.75
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Raw Quality Component**
$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{raw}} &= 0.75 + 1.0 + 1.0 = 2.75
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Raw Module 5 Total**
$$\begin{aligned}
S_5^{\text{raw}} &= 2.75 + 2.75 = 5.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Bloom's Coverage Index**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{BCI} &= \frac{3}{6} = 0.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Final Adjusted Score** (TDF = 1)
$$\begin{aligned}
S_5^{\text{final}} &= 5.5 \times 1 \times 0.5 \\
&= 2.75
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight:** Despite scoring 5.5/8 raw, the **final adjusted score drops to 2.75** because of the Bloom's penalty. This falls **below the 3.0 pass threshold**. The team **fails** Module 5 despite having a technically solid project, because they failed to demonstrate **all 6 Bloom's levels** (they skipped L2 – Understand, L4 – Analyze, L6 – Create).

**Marks Distribution:** Each step ≈ 1.4M × 5 = 7M.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Confusing "Innovation" with "Creativity" in answers. They are **2 separate sub-criteria worth 1 mark each**. Using one term for both = lose 1 mark.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Forgetting to apply the **Time Discipline Factor (TDF)**. Many students compute raw scores but skip the TDF step → lose 1–2 marks in 14-mark problems.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Listing the sub-criteria **without mapping to Bloom's levels** in theory questions. KTU examiners expect explicit CO/Bloom alignment.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Computing IDI (Innovation Density Index) as a decimal instead of a percentage. Always express as a percentage.
> - **Pitfall 5:** Treating "Individual Contribution" as purely about slide ownership. KTU expects **module-level ownership** (code modules, documentation sections, testing suites).

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This!)**

- [ ] **Total = 8 Marks** split as **5 (Presentation) + 3 (Quality)**.
- [ ] **Presentation 5M = 2 (Clarity) + 1.5 (Individual) + 1.5 (Q&A)**.
- [ ] **Quality 3M = 1 (Technical) + 1 (Innovation) + 1 (Creativity)**.
- [ ] **Innovation** is about the **WHAT**; **Creativity** is about the **HOW**.
- [ ] **Innovation Tiers:** 0 (Replicate) → 4 (Invent). Aim for **Tier 2 minimum** to score 0.5/1.
- [ ] **Time Discipline Factor** = $1 - \frac{\vert T_{\text{actual}} - T_{\text{allotted}} \vert}{T_{\text{allotted}}}$. TDF < 0.7 = penalty zone.
- [ ] **Bloom's Coverage Index** = Levels Demonstrated ÷ 6. Missing levels = score multiplier < 1.
- [ ] **Pass Threshold** = **3.0 / 8.0**. Below = supplementary re-presentation required.
- [ ] **Individual Contribution** is verified via **cross-questioning** — be ready to explain YOUR code, not just your slides.
- [ ] **Q&A Weights:** Definitional (0.25) + Comparative (0.5) + Defensive (0.5) + Speculative (0.25) = 1.5 marks.
- [ ] **ISO 25010** is the **preferred quality model** for KTU — 8 characteristics, maps directly to CO4.
- [ ] **5:3 ratio** (62.5% : 37.5%) means **communication matters MORE** than raw innovation in KTU's valuation key.
- [ ] **Examiner's Mindset:** Structure > Polish, Depth > Breadth, Evidence > Claims.
- [ ] Always mention **Bloom's Level** + **CO Number** when explaining any sub-criterion in theory answers.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Mnemonic — "C-I-Q-T-I-C":**
> **C**larity (2) → **I**ndividual (1.5) → **Q**&A (1.5) → **T**echnical (1) → **I**nnovation (1) → **C**reativity (1) = **8 marks**.
> Memorize this sequence left-to-right for instant recall during exams.
